entry.title: stop being weird
entry.summary:
Title: Stop Being Weird: CET, Call Stack Spoofing Detections, and “Normal” Execution Flows (MassDriver)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - The post analyzes modern Windows call stack spoofing (gadget-based synthetic frames) and why it increasingly fails against Elastic’s behavioral detections and Intel CET shadow stack enforcement. - It explains Elastic rules that flag “image_rop” behavior (returns not preceded by a `call`) and signatureable call stack summaries caused by rare “call-preceded” ROP gadgets. - With CET, classic spoofing breaks because modifying return addresses desynchronizes the normal stack from the shadow stack, causing faults. - The proposed workaround is to stop spoofing and instead proxy sensitive/monitored API calls from shellcode to a “normal” on-disk artifact thread via `PostThreadMessage`, yielding clean, legitimate call stacks. - For sleep masking, it uses a CET-friendly fiber-based sleep routine (switching to a fiber that calls `Sleep` and returns), avoiding the “sleeping beacon” wait-stack pattern. - Useful for red teams/pentesters and implant developers adapting to CET + call-stack-based EDR detections; also useful for defenders to understand emerging evasion patterns and where to add telemetry.
Technical Focus: - Elastic call stack spoofing detections (“image_rop”, gadget patterns, call stack summary signatures) - Intel CET / shadow stack implications for ROP and stack tampering - API-call proxying from shellcode to a benign thread (message-based RPC) - Windows fibers for CET-compatible stack switching and sleep obfuscation - Gadget hunting constraints (call-preceded gadgets) and signatureability
Use Cases: - Implement CET-compatible alternatives to gadget-based call stack spoofing - Reduce EDR call-stack anomalies by moving monitored API calls into “normal” module execution - Build loaders/implants that proxy high-risk APIs (e.g., `LoadLibraryA`, memory APIs) through a broker thread - Sleep masking using fiber switching to avoid suspicious wait/sleep call stacks - Defensive research: create detections for message-based API brokers and fiber-based sleep patterns
Keywords: CET, shadow stack, Control-flow Enforcement Technology, call stack spoofing, stack spoofing detection, Elastic EDR, image_rop, ROP gadget, jmp reg gadget, synthetic frames, RtlUserThreadStart, BaseThreadInitThunk, PostThreadMessageA, WM_USER, worker thread, fibers, ConvertThreadToFiber, CreateFiber, SwitchToFiber, LoadLibraryA, Sleep, Beacon, BOF, GadgetHunter

entry.title: MassDriver
entry.summary:
Title: MassDriver — CET-Compatible API Proxying for Clean Call Stacks
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): MassDriver is a proof-of-concept framework that proxies “sensitive” Windows API calls from shellcode into an external artifact to maintain CET (Control-flow Enforcement Technology) compatibility and preserve clean/legitimate-looking call stacks. It targets the post–call stack spoofing landscape where CET/Shadow Stack makes traditional stack manipulation and spoofing brittle or detectable. The repo provides a loader/artifact + payload model (default payload calls `LoadLibraryA` and `Sleep`) to demonstrate how to offload API execution while keeping the originating shellcode’s stack behavior benign. It’s positioned as a companion to the author’s blog post on operating “after call stack spoofing under CET.” It includes build guidance requiring llvm-mingw to set CET compatibility flags and optional integration for Cobalt Strike via an aggressor script. Useful primarily for red teamers and malware developers researching modern usermode tradecraft under CET constraints, and for defenders studying emerging evasion patterns.
Technical Focus: - Intel CET / Shadow Stack constraints on usermode tradecraft - Proxying/forwarding Windows API calls from shellcode to a helper module (“artifact”) - Call stack cleanliness / stack-based detection evasion considerations - Payload/loader architecture for staged execution - Toolchain/build specifics for CET-compatible binaries (llvm-mingw)
Use Cases: - Building CET-compatible shellcode execution flows without classic call stack spoofing - Researching alternative evasive execution patterns that reduce suspicious stack artifacts - Generating PoC payloads for call stack inspection and EDR telemetry comparison - Integrating a CET-aware payload workflow into Cobalt Strike for testing
Keywords: CET, Shadow Stack, call stack spoofing, stack walking, Windows API, shellcode, loader, artifact, proxy calls, usermode evasion, EDR telemetry, llvm-mingw, MinGW, LoadLibraryA, Sleep, Cobalt Strike, Aggressor Script, PoC, Windows internals, clean call stack

entry.title: shellcode loaders the art of execution
entry.summary:
Title: Shellcode Loaders: Advanced Execution & Evasion Tradecraft
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - A comprehensive red-team guide to modern in-memory execution via shellcode, reflective DLL, .NET assembly, and PIC-based loaders, with emphasis on evasion against Defender/EDR telemetry (AMSI/ETW/callbacks/memory scanning). - It explains why naive “VirtualAlloc + RWX + CreateThread” loaders are heavily detected and breaks down loader design choices (staged vs stageless, stomped vs prepended models). - The core deep dive is Crystal Palace (Raphael Mudge) and the Tradecraft Garden: a PIC linker + modular “tradecraft” composition approach using COFF/PICO objects and spec files to separate capability from evasion. - Advanced techniques covered include IAT-hook-based sleep masking, module overloading with SEC_IMAGE sections, NtContinue-based entry transfer to avoid suspicious return addresses, and call stack spoofing (Draugr). - It also discusses static signature resistance via Crystal Palace transforms (+mutate/+disco/+optimize) and highlights real implementations (Eden, KaplaStrike, StealthPalace) and OPSEC/detection considerations. - Useful primarily for red team operators, implant/loader developers, and defenders wanting to understand high-fidelity loader tradecraft and detection points.
Technical Focus: - Reflective DLL loading vs PIC/prepended loaders (DoublePulsar-style) - Crystal Palace / Tradecraft Garden architecture (COFF, PICO, spec files, DFR API resolution) - IAT hooking at import resolution time (GetProcAddress interception) for sleep obfuscation - Module overloading via NtCreateSection + NtMapViewOfSection (SEC_IMAGE) and .pdata registration (RtlAddFunctionTable) - NtContinue context-based execution transfer and synthetic stack construction - Call stack spoofing (Draugr) and Ekko-style sleep masking (timer queue + NtContinue + SystemFunction032)
Use Cases: - Build modular, C2-agnostic in-memory loaders for DLL/COFF payloads - Replace default Cobalt Strike reflective loader via UDRL (Aggressor/CNA integration) - Implement sleep masking and memory permission choreography via IAT hooks - Reduce memory-scan and stack-walk detections using image-backed mapping and unwind registration - Generate per-build polymorphic PIC blobs to resist YARA/static signatures - Inform defensive detections around SEC_IMAGE mapping abuse, NtContinue patterns, and suspicious stack/unwind anomalies
Keywords: shellcode loader, reflective DLL injection, Stephen Fewer, Crystal Palace, Tradecraft Garden, PICO, COFF, spec file, UDRL, Cobalt Strike, IAT hooking, GetProcAddress, NtCreateSection, NtMapViewOfSection, SEC_IMAGE, module overloading, RtlAddFunctionTable, .pdata, unwind info, NtContinue, call stack spoofing, Draugr, Ekko sleep obfuscation, SystemFunction032, ETW, AMSI, YARA, +mutate, +disco, +optimize, Eden, KaplaStrike, StealthPalace

entry.title: sleepy beacons retrospective
entry.summary:
Title: Sleepy Beacons: A retrospective on how implants take naps
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post surveys the evolution of “sleep masking” techniques used by modern implants/beacons to reduce in-memory and thread-state exposure while idle. It starts from naive `Sleep()` loops and moves through Gargoyle-style protection cycling, then the Ekko/Foliage/Cronos family that chains `NtContinue` with crafted `CONTEXT` structures to flip `.text` protections and encrypt/decrypt code during sleep. It explains how these approaches are implemented (timers, thread-pool callbacks, APC chains, helper threads) and why defenders can still detect them via stack artifacts, timer/APC telemetry, and `CONTEXT` carving. The author highlights the arms race around passive call stack spoofing (AceLdr/VulcanRaven/SilentMoonwalk/Moonwalk++) and notes how Intel CET Shadow Stack breaks common `SetThreadContext`-based spoofing. The key takeaway is that kernel visibility via ETW Threat Intelligence (notably `NtProtectVirtualMemory` “ProtectVM” events) makes periodic IMAGE-VAD protection cycling a high-confidence detection signal, pushing attackers toward approaches that avoid `NtProtectVirtualMemory` entirely. Useful for red team/malware developers designing implants and for blue teams building detections around sleep obfuscation primitives.
Technical Focus: - Sleep masking / sleep obfuscation design patterns - `NtContinue` + crafted `CONTEXT` chains (timers/APCs/thread-pool) - Memory protection cycling (`NtProtectVirtualMemory` / `VirtualProtect`) - In-place code encryption (RC4 `SystemFunction032`, KsecDD IOCTL encrypt/decrypt) - Call stack spoofing & unwinding tricks (`RtlVirtualUnwind`, `.pdata`/UNWIND_INFO) - Detection via ETW-TI ProtectVM and userland stack/timer/APC artifacts
Use Cases: - Implementing stealthier beacon sleep loops in custom implants - Evaluating trade-offs between timer-queue, APC, and thread-pool based sleep masks - Building detections for sleeping beacons (stack frames, timer queues, `CONTEXT` scans) - Assessing CET impact on thread-context/stack spoofing techniques - Hunting for ETW-TI `NtProtectVirtualMemory` fluctuation patterns in EDR telemetry
Keywords: sleep mask, beacon, implant, Gargoyle, Ekko, Foliage, Cronos, DeathSleep, NtContinue, CONTEXT, RtlCaptureContext, APC, NtTestAlert, timer queue, thread pool, VirtualProtect, NtProtectVirtualMemory, SystemFunction032, KsecDD, IOCTL_KSEC_ENCRYPT_MEMORY, ETW Threat Intelligence, Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence, ProtectVM, IMAGE VAD, call stack spoofing, RtlVirtualUnwind, UNWIND_INFO, CET, Shadow Stack, NtSetContextThread, KiUserApcDispatcher, RtlpTpTimerCallback, Patriot, Hunt-Sleeping-Beacons, Moneta

entry.title: 0x33
entry.summary:
Title: 0x33 – Full Windows Offense Pipeline: PEB Walking to Kernel Rootkit (DKOM + Callback Abuse)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post is a code-heavy walkthrough that starts from a simple WinAPI call (MessageBoxA) and builds up a full offensive chain through user-mode evasion and injection techniques into kernel-mode rootkit capabilities. It covers dynamic API resolution (including manual PEB walking and PE export parsing) to avoid static imports and reduce visibility to analysis/hooking. It then demonstrates multiple injection primitives: IAT hooking, process hollowing, classic remote-thread DLL/shellcode injection, ntdll/NTAPI-based injection, and Early Bird APC injection with encrypted payloads (XOR/AES via CryptoAPI). The latter sections move into driver development basics (DriverEntry, IRPs, device objects/symlinks) and rootkit techniques including kernel image-load callbacks for injection, DKOM process/driver hiding, and token stealing for privilege escalation. It also discusses practical constraints like version-dependent EPROCESS offsets and PatchGuard/KPP detection risk. Useful primarily for red teamers, malware developers, and defenders wanting concrete examples of how these primitives chain together.
Technical Focus: - Manual API resolution via PEB walking and PE export table parsing - Import Address Table (IAT) hooking - Process hollowing (CREATE_SUSPENDED, WriteProcessMemory, SetThreadContext/ResumeThread) - Remote injection variants (CreateRemoteThread, NtCreateThreadEx, QueueUserAPC / Early Bird) - Payload/string obfuscation and runtime decryption (XOR, AES-256 via CryptoAPI) - Kernel driver/rootkit primitives (PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine, DKOM, ObRegisterCallbacks, token stealing)
Use Cases: - Build loaders that avoid static imports and common user-mode hooks - Implement and compare process injection techniques (thread creation vs APC-based) - Prototype process hollowing for masquerading under legitimate binaries - Develop a basic Windows kernel driver with user-mode control via IOCTL - Experiment with kernel-mode persistence/stealth (DKOM unlinking, module hiding) - Create detections/hunts for PEB-walking, APC injection, image-load callbacks, and DKOM artifacts
Keywords: Windows internals, PEB, TEB, __readfsdword, GS:[0x60], PE export parsing, GetProcAddress evasion, dynamic API resolution, IAT hooking, IMAGE_IMPORT_DESCRIPTOR, process hollowing, CREATE_SUSPENDED, SetThreadContext, WriteProcessMemory, VirtualAllocEx, CreateRemoteThread, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, NtWriteVirtualMemory, NtCreateThreadEx, QueueUserAPC, Early Bird injection, CryptoAPI, CryptAcquireContextW, CryptDeriveKey, AES-256, kernel driver, DriverEntry, IRP, IoCreateDevice, DeviceIoControl, PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine, KeInitializeApc, DKOM, EPROCESS, ActiveProcessLinks, PatchGuard, ObRegisterCallbacks, token stealing, PsInitialSystemProcess

entry.title: HallWatch
entry.summary:
Title: HallWatch — Usermode detector for indirect syscalls (Hell’s Hall / Tartarus’ Gate / RecycledGate / VEH syscalls)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - HallWatch is a Windows user-mode DLL/POC that detects and disrupts indirect-syscall techniques used to bypass user-mode API hooks (e.g., jumping directly to `ntdll`’s `syscall` instruction). - It patches the `syscall` instruction itself inside selected `ntdll!Nt*` stubs by overwriting the first byte (`0F 05` → `CC 05`), forcing an `INT3` breakpoint regardless of how execution reaches the syscall site. - A VEH handler catches the breakpoint, identifies the targeted stub, runs checks (return address trust, SSN mismatch detection, and stack unwind validation), then redirects execution to a private trampoline that performs the intended syscall safely/loggably. - To address Hell’s Gate and “shadow ntdll” cases (syscalls from attacker-owned executable memory or remapped modules), it periodically scans executable memory regions and module `.text` for syscall-stub patterns (`0F 05` followed near by `C3`). - It also includes self-healing (re-patching bytes if restored) and periodic VEH re-registration to resist handler removal. - Useful primarily for Blue Team/EDR research, sandboxing, and defenders studying syscall-evasion tradeoffs; also valuable to Red Teamers as a reference for what can be detected in user mode.
Technical Focus: - Indirect syscalls and syscall-stub evasion families (Hell’s Hall, Tartarus’ Gate, RecycledGate, VEH syscalls, Hell’s Gate) - `ntdll` export enumeration and syscall number (SSN) extraction from stub prologues - Byte-level patching of `ntdll` syscall instruction (`0F 05` → `INT3`) and VEH-based interception - Context manipulation (`CONTEXT->Rip`) and trampoline-based syscall execution - Stack validation via `RtlVirtualUnwind` and return-address/module trust heuristics - Executable-memory scanning with `VirtualQuery` + pattern matching for syscall stubs / shadow mappings
Use Cases: - Embed in sandboxes to flag/neutralize indirect-syscall loaders at runtime - Research/POC for user-mode detection strategies against syscall-hook bypasses - Telemetry generation: log suspicious syscall origins, SSN swaps, and unwind anomalies - Defensive hardening experiments (self-repairing hooks, VEH persistence) - Adversary emulation validation: test whether a syscall technique is detectable under these heuristics
Keywords: Windows, ntdll, Nt* syscalls, indirect syscall, Hell’s Hall, Hell’s Gate, Tartarus’ Gate, RecycledGate, VEH syscall, Vectored Exception Handler, INT3, breakpoint exception, syscall stub, SSN, RtlVirtualUnwind, VirtualQuery, RWX memory, shadow ntdll, trampoline, user-mode detection

entry.title: LoadReload
entry.summary:
Title: LoadReload
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): LoadReload is a Windows shellcode loader created for a master’s thesis, intended to run Sliver-generated shellcode (notably large payloads). It focuses on evasion and operational stability by decrypting an embedded/loaded payload (multibyte XOR) and resolving APIs dynamically via hashing to reduce static indicators. The project includes anti-emulation timing tricks (BusySleep using KUSER_SHARED_DATA) and a RW→RX memory permission loop aimed at “deconditioning” EDR/memory scanners by wasting scanning resources. It also preloads network-related DLLs to avoid behavioral detections tied to late/odd module loads and supports “module overloading” using an operator-chosen decoy DLL (e.g., edgehtml.dll). Shellcode execution is performed via LdrCallEnclave, an uncommon execution path compared to typical CreateThread/QueueUserAPC patterns. Useful primarily for red teamers, malware researchers, and defenders studying modern loader tradecraft and detection opportunities around memory protection churn, module load patterns, and unusual execution APIs.
Technical Focus: - Shellcode loading/execution on Windows (LdrCallEnclave) - API hashing and dynamic API resolution (IAT obfuscation) - Payload encryption/decryption (multibyte XOR) - Anti-emulation / timing checks (KUSER_SHARED_DATA-based sleep) - Memory protection manipulation (RW→RX loops) - DLL/module load strategy (preloading, decoy module overloading)
Use Cases: - Researching and benchmarking EDR behavioral detections against custom loaders - Building/understanding Sliver shellcode execution chains in Windows userland - Developing detections for suspicious memory-protection churn and atypical execution APIs - Studying anti-emulation timing techniques leveraging shared user/kernel data - Testing module-load heuristics (network DLL preloading, decoy/stomp/overload patterns)
Keywords: LoadReload, shellcode loader, Sliver, Windows, C++, multibyte XOR, API hashing, dynamic API resolution, IAT obfuscation, KUSER_SHARED_DATA, anti-emulation, BusySleep, RW-RX loop, VirtualProtect, memory scanners, EDR evasion, DLL preloading, edgehtml.dll, module overloading, LdrCallEnclave

entry.title: LACUNA Chain Ghost Frames defeats All EDR layers of call stack based detection
entry.summary:
Title: LACUNA Chain: Ghost Frames — defeats all EDR layers of call-stack-based detection
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post presents “LACUNA Chain,” a set of Windows x64 call-stack evasion techniques designed to defeat modern EDRs that collect stacks at the kernel boundary (e.g., ETW-Ti STACKWALK, Sysmon stack walking) rather than relying on userland NTDLL hooks. - It focuses on making kernel-collected call stacks “lie” by abusing gaps in `.pdata` unwind metadata: addresses not covered by `RUNTIME_FUNCTION` entries are treated as leaf functions by `RtlVirtualUnwind`, advancing RSP by 8 without errors. - The author introduces BYOUD-Gap (no `.pdata` modification), an ETW-Ti USER_APC timing window to control when stack snapshots occur, and “ghost functions/gadgets” (executable code in signed DLL regions lacking unwind metadata) to build semantically plausible frames near high-value APIs (e.g., kernelbase!VirtualProtect). - Additional contributions include BYOUD-RT (runtime stack-distance calculation via TEB), BYOUD-MF (RSP “teleport” using `UWOP_PUSH_MACHFRAME` in KiUser* dispatchers), and syscall parameter encryption/decryption via HW-breakpoint + VEH at the `syscall` instruction. - It’s primarily useful for red teams/malware developers researching EDR evasion and for defenders building next-gen detections (e.g., flagging stack frames that land in `.pdata` gaps, monitoring ETW-Ti APC queue depth). - The key takeaway is that stack-based detections can be systematically bypassed without modifying unwind metadata; remaining reliable signals shift toward kernel callback correlation and behavior-based analytics.
Technical Focus: - Windows x64 stack unwinding (`RtlVirtualUnwind`, `.pdata`, `RUNTIME_FUNCTION`, `UNWIND_CODE`) - Call-stack spoofing via `.pdata` gaps / “ghost frames” (BYOUD-Gap) - ETW Threat Intelligence (ETW-Ti) stack collection via USER_APC timing window - CET shadow stack implications and CET-compatible spoofing approaches - KiUser* dispatcher unwind behavior (`UWOP_PUSH_MACHFRAME`) and RSP manipulation (BYOUD-MF) - Syscall argument obfuscation using HW breakpoints + VEH (parameter encryption)
Use Cases: - Building CET-compatible call-stack spoofing for syscall-based injection chains - Evading ETW-Ti STACKWALK and Sysmon-style user-mode stack capture during sensitive syscalls - Constructing “semantically plausible” stacks using frames adjacent to common Win32 APIs (VirtualProtect/VirtualAlloc/WriteProcessMemory) - Defensive research: implementing detection for call-trace addresses that fall into `.pdata` uncovered ranges - Evaluating EDR reliance on stack-based rules vs kernel callback/behavior correlation
Keywords: Windows x64, EDR evasion, call stack spoofing, ETW-Ti, Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence, STACKWALK, USER_APC, RtlVirtualUnwind, RtlLookupFunctionEntry, .pdata, RUNTIME_FUNCTION, UNWIND_INFO, BYOUD, BYOUD-Gap, BYOUD-RT, BYOUD-MF, UWOP_PUSH_MACHFRAME, KiUserExceptionDispatcher, CET shadow stack, win32u.dll, ntdll.dll, kernelbase.dll, wow64.dll, syscall, VEH, hardware breakpoints, VirtualProtect, NtProtectVirtualMemory

entry.title: rover
entry.summary:
Title: Project ROVER – A Different Approach to Red Team Tool Design
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post describes the design philosophy and early results of “ROVER,” a stealth-first red team C2/implant framework built by working backward from common EDR detection stages (static, emulation, dynamic). - The goal is to reduce “signaturable surface area” so the operator can focus on functionality rather than constant reactive evasion patches. - Key ideas include per-build polymorphism (binary-level variation each compile), minimizing strings/API footprints, and executing BOF-style modules without allocating RWX/executable memory to avoid a major dynamic detection hotspot. - The framework also “cryptographically binds” code, data, modules, and server components so artifacts from different builds can’t be mixed and single-file captures are harder to analyze. - A limited test against Microsoft Defender for Endpoint showed no alerts/timeline entries for basic operation and multiple BOF executions, with only a behavioral timeline entry during local account enumeration. - Useful for red team tool builders, malware researchers, and defenders interested in how modern implants attempt to evade static/emulation/dynamic detection pipelines.
Technical Focus: - EDR detection pipeline decomposition (static vs emulation vs dynamic) - Per-compile polymorphism / build-time randomization - Non-executable memory BOF/module execution (avoiding RWX allocations) - Artifact coupling / compile-time cryptographic binding of components - Minimizing static indicators (strings, metadata, API call surface) - Position-independent, platform-agnostic implant architecture
Use Cases: - Designing implants/C2 agents with reduced static and memory-signature exposure - Building BOF/module execution systems that avoid RWX memory patterns - Researching which EDR stages trigger detections and how to isolate them - Hardening analysis resistance via component binding and per-build variance - Adversary emulation tooling that aims for longer-lived undetected deployments
Keywords: EDR, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, MDE timeline, static signatures, emulation sandbox, dynamic detection, ETW, API hooking, RWX memory, BOF, Beacon Object Files, in-memory encryption, polymorphic builds, position-independent code, cryptographic binding, artifact coupling, signature surface reduction, adversary emulation, C2 framework, malware analysis resistance

entry.title: Mega Malware Analysis Tutorial Featuring Donut.pdf
entry.summary:
Title: Mega Malware Analysis Tutorial Featuring Donut
Type: Documentation (PDF Tutorial / Threat Intel Write-up)
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This PDF is a step-by-step malware analysis tutorial from Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 that uses “Donut” (a .NET/PE shellcode generator/loader) as a central example. - It focuses on practical reverse engineering and triage workflows for modern Windows malware that leverages in-memory execution and staged payload delivery. - The material walks through identifying the loader/payload chain, extracting and decoding embedded or downloaded components, and analyzing execution behavior with both static and dynamic techniques. - It highlights common obfuscation/packing patterns and how analysts can recover configuration, IOCs, and behavioral indicators despite shellcode-based loading. - It is most useful for malware analysts and blue team detection engineers, but also informs red teamers about what artifacts and behaviors defenders can reliably observe. - The key value is connecting Donut-style tradecraft to concrete analysis steps and detection-relevant findings rather than treating it as a black box.
Technical Focus: - Donut-generated shellcode and in-memory loading of .NET assemblies - Windows process injection / memory allocation and execution patterns - Static analysis of droppers/loaders (strings, imports, embedded blobs) - Dynamic analysis and behavioral tracing (process, network, file, registry) - Deobfuscation/unpacking and payload extraction workflows - IOC generation and detection engineering considerations
Use Cases: - Malware triage playbooks for shellcode-based loaders - Extracting and analyzing embedded .NET payloads delivered via Donut - Building detections for in-memory execution and suspicious allocation/execution chains - Training analysts on end-to-end reverse engineering workflow (loader → payload → C2/IOCs) - Mapping observed behaviors to ATT&CK-style techniques for reporting
Keywords: Donut, shellcode, .NET assembly, in-memory execution, loader, dropper, unpacking, deobfuscation, process injection, VirtualAlloc, VirtualProtect, CreateThread, reflective loading, dynamic analysis, static analysis, IOCs, Windows internals, C2, Unit 42, malware analysis tutorial

entry.title: signed to kill reverse engineering a 0 day used to disable crowdstrike edr
entry.summary:
Title: Signed to Kill: Reverse Engineering a 0-Day Used to Disable CrowdStrike EDR
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This write-up reverse engineers a Microsoft-signed Windows kernel driver abused in a BYOVD scenario to disable security products, including CrowdStrike Falcon. - The driver exposes an unsafe IOCTL interface that allows arbitrary process termination from user mode by passing a PID (as an ASCII string) to the driver. - The analysis walks through identifying the relevant IOCTL (0x22E010), understanding the dispatch handler, and tracing the kill path via ZwOpenProcess → ZwTerminateProcess. - It highlights why Protected Process Light (PPL) protections don’t help when termination is performed from kernel context. - The author also demonstrates how to discover the device symbolic link dynamically (WinObj) and provides a PoC that opens the device and issues DeviceIoControl to kill the target process. - Useful for red teamers, malware developers, and defenders researching BYOVD/driver abuse and EDR tampering tradecraft.
Technical Focus: - BYOVD (Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver) abuse - Windows kernel driver reverse engineering (IDA Pro) - IOCTL interface analysis (IRP_MJ_DEVICE_CONTROL) - Process termination from kernel (ZwOpenProcess, ZwTerminateProcess) - PPL/EDR tamper bypass via kernel-mode primitives - Device object / symbolic link discovery (WinObj)
Use Cases: - Disabling EDR/AV agents prior to payload execution (tamper step) - Hunting and analyzing malicious/abused signed drivers in incident response - Building detections around suspicious driver loads and IOCTL usage - Validating defensive controls (WDAC, driver blocklists, vulnerable driver mitigation)
Keywords: BYOVD, Microsoft-signed driver, kernel-mode, Windows driver, IOCTL, IRP_MJ_DEVICE_CONTROL, DeviceIoControl, CreateFileW, symbolic link, WinObj, IDA Pro, ZwOpenProcess, ZwTerminateProcess, process killing, PPL, CrowdStrike Falcon, EDR bypass, vulnerable driver, sc.exe, OSRLOADER

entry.title: riscy business.html
entry.summary:
Title: RISC‑Y Business: Raging against the reduced machine
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post describes the engineering of a small, embeddable RISC‑V (rv64) virtual machine interpreter intended for low-footprint payload execution and practical obfuscation. - It avoids building a custom VM ISA by leveraging LLVM’s existing RISC‑V backend, then runs the guest bytecode inside a host process with a shared memory model (guest pointers are valid host pointers). - The authors build a freestanding toolchain (custom linker script, minimal CRT, relocation packing) to produce a raw binary blob that can be copied into memory and executed by the interpreter without RWX pages. - Hardening features include opcode enum shuffling, per-instruction XOR “encryption” keyed by bytecode offset, and a threaded interpreter using tail calls to increase indirect branching and complicate static analysis. - It also covers “whole-program LLVM” on Windows: embedding/extracting LTO bitcode from PE files, fixing alignment/padding issues, and retargeting x64 Windows LLVM IR to RISC‑V by generating import-resolving stubs that call back into the host. - Useful for red teamers, malware developers, and reverse engineers interested in VM-based obfuscation, payload staging, and LLVM-based transformation pipelines on Windows.
Technical Focus: - RISC‑V rv64 interpreter design (register model, instruction decoding, dispatch) - Freestanding binaries: custom linker scripts, CRT init arrays, relocation handling - VM hardening: opcode shuffling, bytecode XOR transform, threaded interpreters (tail-call dispatch) - Windows host integration: PEB walking, export resolution, host-call syscall bridge - LLVM LTO bitcode embedding/extraction in PE and IR retargeting (x64 → riscv64) - Build pipeline automation (CMake/clang-cl, lld, objcopy, Python post-processing)
Use Cases: - Embed a compact VM to execute staged “business logic”/shellcode-like payloads without RWX memory - Increase reverse-engineering cost via VM-based obfuscation (shuffled opcodes + encrypted bytecode + threaded dispatch) - Generate polymorphic payload variants by recompiling/shuffling/encrypting bytecode - Retarget and run simple Windows API-using C/C++ code inside a RISC‑V VM via LLVM IR rewriting - Instrument/obfuscate/fuzz Windows programs by extracting embedded LLVM bitcode and post-processing it
Keywords: RISC-V, rv64im, virtual machine interpreter, threaded interpreter, tail calls, musttail, opcode shuffling, bytecode encryption, XOR transform, LLVM, LTO, embedded bitcode, .llvmbc, clang-cl, lld, llvm-objcopy, PE, Windows PEB, export table walking, ntdll, syscall bridge, freestanding CRT, linker script, relocations, R_RISCV_64, IR retargeting, dllimport stubs, LoadLibraryA, MessageBoxA

entry.title: Charon
entry.summary:
Title: Charon (RedTeamGrimoire) — Standalone Artifact Builder / Shellcode Loader with Evasion
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Charon is a Windows-focused, self-compiling artifact builder that generates an executable “vessel” to load and execute operator-supplied raw shellcode with multiple evasion layers. It targets common EDR detections by avoiding typical RWX/Private memory allocation patterns via module stomping into a signed Microsoft DLL’s `.text` section, and by using indirect syscalls with dynamic SSN resolution to bypass userland hooks. It implements adaptive stack spoofing by parsing runtime unwind metadata (`.pdata`) to synthesize realistic call stacks, then wipes stack artifacts before transferring execution. The project includes a legacy monolithic variant (payload embedded/encrypted in resources) and a newer external-payload variant that loads a UUID-encoded encrypted payload file to reduce binary entropy and static signatures. It’s primarily useful for red teams/pentesters building custom loaders and for defenders studying modern loader tradecraft and detection opportunities. The interesting aspects are the combination of module-stomp execution, polymorphic syscall stubs, runtime key reconstruction, and stack forensics countermeasures in a single builder workflow.
Technical Focus: - Module stomping into file-backed `.text` sections (LoadLibraryExA + Chakra.dll) - Indirect syscalls and dynamic SSN discovery (Tartarus Gate-style) - Stack spoofing via unwind info parsing (`.pdata`) and synthetic call chain construction - Polymorphic syscall stub generation with junk instruction padding - Payload encryption/decryption (RC4) with runtime key reconstruction (“KeyGuard”) - External payload staging via UUID-encoded encrypted file
Use Cases: - Generate custom Windows shellcode loaders intended to evade userland EDR hooks and memory scanners - Stage payloads externally to reduce static entropy/signature hits on the main executable - Research/demonstrate module-stomping + indirect-syscall tradecraft in controlled assessments - Blue-team reverse engineering and building detections for stack spoofing, syscall patterns, and module integrity anomalies
Keywords: Windows shellcode loader, artifact builder, module stomping, Chakra.dll, LoadLibraryExA, `.text` section injection, indirect syscalls, SSN resolution, Tartarus Gate, HellHall, EDR hook bypass, stack spoofing, unwind info, `.pdata`, RtlUserThreadStart, BaseThreadInitThunk, VirtualProtectEx, RC4, UUID encoding, polymorphic stubs

entry.title: poisonx terminating protected windows processes via byovd
entry.summary:
Title: PoisonX: Terminating Protected Windows Processes via BYOVD
Type: Blog Post (with linked GitHub Tool)
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This write-up presents PoisonX, a research tool that demonstrates Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver (BYOVD) to terminate arbitrary Windows processes, including Protected Process (PP) and Protected Process Light (PPL) targets such as EDR/AV services. - It abuses a Microsoft-signed kernel driver that exposes a vulnerable IOCTL interface, allowing the driver to be loaded under Windows Code Integrity and then used as a kernel-level “kill” primitive. - The user-mode controller installs/uninstalls the driver via the Service Control Manager, communicates with it using DeviceIoControl, and can enumerate processes along with their PP/PPL protection level and signer info. - The tool auto-deploys by extracting one of multiple embedded driver variants and issuing an IOCTL (0x22E010) to terminate a specified PID. - It’s useful for red teams and security researchers to validate endpoint resilience against BYOVD-based defense evasion, and for blue teams to understand detection points around driver loading and suspicious IOCTL usage. - The key takeaway is that trusted, signed-but-vulnerable drivers can bypass user-mode protections and undermine PPL-based security controls.
Technical Focus: - BYOVD (Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver) abuse - Windows PP/PPL process protection bypass via kernel primitives - Vulnerable IOCTL interfaces and DeviceIoControl user↔kernel communication - Microsoft-signed driver trust chain / Windows Code Integrity implications - Driver installation and lifecycle via Service Control Manager (SCM) - Process enumeration with protection level and signer attribution
Use Cases: - Terminate EDR/AV PPL services during controlled red-team testing - Validate defensive controls: driver blocklists, driver-load telemetry, IOCTL monitoring - Research PP/PPL behavior and signer/protection-level mapping across processes - Build detections for suspicious kernel driver service creation and device GUID access - Reproduce and study real-world BYOVD tradecraft used by ransomware/malware
Keywords: BYOVD, vulnerable driver, Microsoft-signed driver, Windows Hardware Compatibility Publisher, IOCTL, DeviceIoControl, kernel-mode, SCM, Service Control Manager, PP, Protected Process, PPL, Protected Process Light, SeDebugPrivilege, EDR bypass, process termination, driver blocklist, Code Integrity, device interface GUID, CrowdStrike Falcon

entry.title: Apc Process Injection and DLL injection
entry.summary:
Title: Apc Process & DLL injection
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository contains Windows process injection samples that execute shellcode or load a DLL in a remote process using Asynchronous Procedure Calls (APC). - It demonstrates three variants: a simple remote shellcode APC injector, a DLL payload that runs inside the host process (with loader-lock considerations), and a DLL injector that queues `LoadLibraryA` via APC. - The code covers typical injection steps (process/thread discovery, remote memory allocation, writing payload/path, changing protections) and offers two APC queuing strategies: first-thread vs “shotgun” queuing to all threads. - Payloads are stored encrypted and decrypted at runtime (with a referenced simple crypter), and the README discusses AV/EDR detection outcomes and basic obfuscation attempts (e.g., Resource Hacker). - Useful for red teamers/pentesters and malware researchers studying APC-based execution and common detection points; also relevant for defenders building telemetry/detections around APC queuing and remote memory operations.
Technical Focus: - APC injection via `QueueUserAPC` and thread enumeration - Remote process discovery with Toolhelp snapshots (`CreateToolhelp32Snapshot`, `Process32First/Next`, `Thread32First/Next`) - Remote memory operations (`VirtualAllocEx`, `WriteProcessMemory`, `VirtualProtectEx`) - DLL injection by queuing `LoadLibraryA` (kernel32 resolution) - Loader lock / deadlock avoidance in DLL entry (spawning a new thread) - Simple shellcode encryption/decryption staging
Use Cases: - Build/modify a basic APC-based shellcode loader for lab testing - Demonstrate “shotgun APC” vs single-thread APC queuing behavior - Test defensive detections for APC queuing + RWX transitions in remote processes - Prototype DLL injection via APC-triggered `LoadLibraryA` - Study practical constraints (thread alertable state, DLLMain/loader-lock issues)
Keywords: APC injection, QueueUserAPC, asynchronous procedure call, process injection, shellcode loader, DLL injection, LoadLibraryA, kernel32.dll, CreateToolhelp32Snapshot, Process32First, Thread32First, OpenProcess, VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, VirtualProtectEx, remote thread enumeration, loader lock, DLLMain, resource extraction, AV detection, Bitdefender ATD, Windows Defender

entry.title: comouflage stealthy dll surrogate injection for process tree evasion
entry.summary:
Title: COMouflage: Stealthy DLL Surrogate Injection for Process Tree Evasion
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This write-up describes “COMouflage,” a Windows code-execution/injection technique that abuses COM’s DLL Surrogate feature to run an attacker-controlled DLL inside `dllhost.exe`. - The attacker creates per-user COM registration under `HKCU\Software\Classes\AppID\{GUID}` and `HKCU\Software\Classes\CLSID\{GUID}`, avoiding admin rights. - Setting `AppID\DllSurrogate` to an empty string forces Windows to use the default surrogate host (`dllhost.exe`), and `CLSID\...\InprocServer32` points to the malicious DLL path. - Triggering `CoCreateInstance` with `CLSCTX_LOCAL_SERVER` causes COM SCM to spawn the surrogate via COM services (`svchost.exe` → `dllhost.exe`), making the attacker process disappear from the visible process lineage. - It’s primarily useful for red teams and malware developers focused on parent/process-tree evasion and “living off the land” execution via legitimate COM plumbing. - The post also includes a C++ PoC and a basic YARA rule aimed at detecting the registry/API patterns associated with this technique.
Technical Focus: - COM registration hijacking via HKCU (`Software\Classes\AppID` / `Software\Classes\CLSID`) - DLL Surrogate mechanism (`DllSurrogate` → default `dllhost.exe`) - COM activation and instantiation (`CoInitializeEx`, `CoCreateInstance`, `CLSCTX_LOCAL_SERVER`) - Process tree / parent masquerading (`svchost.exe` spawning `dllhost.exe`) - WinAPI registry manipulation (`RegCreateKeyExW`, `RegSetValueExW`) - Detection ideas (static indicators + YARA)
Use Cases: - Execute payload DLLs inside `dllhost.exe` without classic remote-thread injection - Evade simplistic EDR detections relying on suspicious parent-child process chains - User-level (non-admin) COM-based execution and potential persistence via registry - Build detections for anomalous per-user COM surrogate registrations and activations
Keywords: COM, DllSurrogate, dllhost.exe, svchost.exe, COM SCM, CoCreateInstance, CLSCTX_LOCAL_SERVER, CLSID, AppID, HKCU, Software\Classes, InprocServer32, ThreadingModel, RegCreateKeyExW, RegSetValueExW, process tree evasion, parent PID masquerading, COM hijacking, T1546.015, YARA

entry.title: sleeping beauty ii
entry.summary:
Title: Sleeping Beauty II: CFG, CET, and Stack Spoofing
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post details updates to the StealthPalace/Adaptix RDLL loader and sleep-obfuscation pipeline to survive modern Windows exploit mitigations. - It explains why ROP-based sleep chains (Ekko/Kraken Mask style) crash in CFG-enabled processes due to indirect-call target validation, and fixes it by registering gadgets and call targets at runtime. - The author uses `NtSetInformationVirtualMemory` with `VmCfgCallTargetInformation` to mark mid-function gadgets, PIC regions, and executable sections as valid CFG call targets. - It then addresses stack spoofing detection during sleep (EDR stack walking) and why full `CONTEXT` swapping breaks on CET Shadow Stacks, which validate `RIP` consistency. - The CET-compatible approach swaps TIB/TEB stack bounds (StackBase/StackLimit) and spoofs `RSP` while preserving the real `RIP`, keeping CET satisfied while misleading stack walkers. - Finally, it compares Ekko v2 (timer-queue `NtContinue` dispatch) vs Kraken Mask (APC-queued chain on a helper thread), highlighting tradeoffs in state storage, restore safety, and gadget selection. - Useful primarily for red teams/implant developers working on Windows user-mode evasion against CFG/CET and call-stack-based detections.
Technical Focus: - Control Flow Guard (CFG) bitmap enforcement and runtime call-target registration - `NtSetInformationVirtualMemory(VmCfgCallTargetInformation)` and `CFG_CALL_TARGET_INFO` - CET / Shadow Stack constraints on `SetThreadContext` and `RIP` manipulation - TIB/TEB stack-bound spoofing (StackBase/StackLimit) to defeat stack walking - ROP-based sleep obfuscation (Ekko timer callbacks vs APC-based Kraken Mask) - Windows thread context operations (`GetThreadContext`, `SetThreadContext`, `NtContinue`, APCs)
Use Cases: - Making ROP-driven sleep masks work inside CFG-enabled processes (e.g., explorer.exe, svchost.exe, Edge) - Hardening implant sleep/idle behavior against EDR call-stack inspection - Building CET-compatible stack spoofing without breaking shadow stack validation - Comparing/choosing timer-queue vs APC dispatch for staged ROP execution - Researching detection opportunities around CFG target marking and TEB/TIB tampering
Keywords: Control Flow Guard, CFG bitmap, /guard:cf, NtSetInformationVirtualMemory, VmCfgCallTargetInformation, CFG_CALL_TARGET_INFO, RtlFailFast2, STATUS_STACK_BUFFER_OVERRUN, CET, Shadow Stack, Hardware-enforced Stack Protection, SetThreadContext, GetThreadContext, NtContinue, TEB, TIB, StackBase, StackLimit, stack spoofing, Ekko, CreateTimerQueueTimer, APC, NtQueueApcThread, NtTestAlert, NtSignalAndWaitForSingleObject, SystemFunction032, RC4, VirtualProtect, ntdll, kernel32, advapi32

entry.title: on target llm malware 1
entry.summary:
Title: On-Target LLM Malware Research Part 1 - Building a Self-Vibe-Coding DLL Harness
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post presents a proof-of-concept “on-target vibe-coding” malware harness: a precompiled Windows EXE that uses an LLM at runtime to generate task-specific C++ DLL payloads, compile them on the victim, and execute them. The problem explored is how malware could land with minimal/static capability and dynamically author missing functionality later in the execution chain, reducing prebuilt payload footprint and enabling flexible post-delivery behavior. The harness implements a generate/compile/run loop with automatic retries by feeding compiler/runtime errors and prior code back into subsequent LLM prompts. It supports Gemini and AWS Bedrock, strips markdown fences, enforces a strict DLL contract (exported `Run()`), compiles via MSVC `cl.exe`, then loads via `LoadLibraryA`/`GetProcAddress`. Example tasks include environment variable collection + HTTP POST exfil, downloading/saving/executing a file with basic source obfuscation instructions, and reading a local file then exfiltrating contents. It’s useful for red teamers and malware researchers studying LLM-assisted modular payload generation, and for defenders modeling emerging tradecraft and telemetry opportunities around on-host compilation and dynamic module creation.
Technical Focus: - LLM-driven runtime code generation (“vibe-coding”) and prompt contracts - On-host compilation pipeline using MSVC `cl.exe` (Visual Studio Build Tools) - DLL loading/execution via `LoadLibraryA` and `GetProcAddress` with exported `Run()` - Error-feedback repair loop (compiler/runtime errors → next prompt) - Provider integration/parsing (Gemini, AWS Bedrock) and response sanitization - Task templating and conditional requirements (e.g., WinHTTP for web tasks)
Use Cases: - Rapid prototyping of modular, task-driven Windows payload components generated at runtime - Researching feasibility/reliability of LLM-assisted code generation under real execution constraints - Simulating adversary tradecraft where capabilities are authored post-delivery - Defensive testing: detecting on-host compilation, transient source/DLL artifacts, and unusual DLL load patterns - Evaluating model/provider differences in code quality and failure modes under iterative repair
Keywords: LLM malware, on-target code generation, runtime compilation, C++, Windows DLL, MSVC, cl.exe, Visual Studio Build Tools, AWS Bedrock, Gemini, prompt engineering, error feedback loop, WinHTTP, HTTP POST exfiltration, LoadLibraryA, GetProcAddress, exported function Run, dynamic payload modules, file collection, source obfuscation

entry.title: queue early bird injection
entry.summary:
Title: Queue Early Bird Injection
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post explains Windows user-mode Asynchronous Procedure Calls (APCs) and how they can be abused for “Early Bird” process injection. It contrasts regular APC injection (unreliable because the target thread must enter an alertable wait) with Early Bird, which creates a process suspended and queues an APC to its main thread before any user code executes. The technique uses `CreateProcess(CREATE_SUSPENDED)`, `VirtualAllocEx`, `WriteProcessMemory`, `QueueUserAPC`, and `ResumeThread` so the queued APC is delivered immediately on resume (via `NtTestAlert`/`KiUserApcDispatcher`) before the process entry point runs. The author discusses basic OPSEC improvements like RW→RX protection flips and XOR-encrypted shellcode to reduce static/heuristic detections. It includes WinDbg walkthroughs showing the thread at `RtlUserThreadStart`, breaking on `ntdll!KiUserApcDispatcher`, and interpreting the call stack through `LdrInitializeThunk`. Useful primarily for red teamers, pentesters, and malware developers learning Windows injection internals; also relevant to defenders for understanding behavioral detection points.
Technical Focus: - User-mode APC mechanics and delivery (`KTHREAD`, `KAPC`, APC queues) - Early Bird APC injection workflow (suspended process + queued APC) - Windows injection primitives (`VirtualAllocEx`, `WriteProcessMemory`, `VirtualProtectEx`) - Loader/initialization path (`LdrInitializeThunk`, `NtTestAlert`, `KiUserApcDispatcher`) - Basic evasion tradeoffs (RWX vs RW→RX, shellcode XOR)
Use Cases: - Execute shellcode inside a newly spawned “legitimate” process before its entry point runs - Build and test process-injection PoCs for training/lab environments - Debug and validate APC delivery timing with WinDbg breakpoints - Develop detections around suspicious API sequences and memory-protection transitions
Keywords: APC injection, Early Bird, QueueUserAPC, CreateProcessA, CREATE_SUSPENDED, ResumeThread, VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, VirtualProtectEx, PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE, RWX, RW-to-RX, KTHREAD, KAPC, ApcListHead, NtTestAlert, KiUserApcDispatcher, LdrInitializeThunk, RtlUserThreadStart, WinDbg, process injection, shellcode, XOR encryption, Windows internals, EDR evasion, Defender detection

entry.title: gargoyle a decade later
entry.summary:
Title: Gargoyle, a decade later
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This retrospective revisits “Gargoyle,” a Windows proof-of-concept demonstrating that memory protection state is temporal: code can remain non-executable most of the time, briefly flip to executable to do work, then revert, defeating point-in-time “executable private pages” scans. The 2026 refresh reframes the work as a measurement/validation problem rather than “memory scanning evasion,” and adds clearer claim boundaries, documentation, and an acceptance harness. It expands beyond the original 32-bit x86 design with sibling demos for x64, ARM64, and ARM64EC, emphasizing architecture-specific semantics rather than pretending portability. A key technical correction is tightening the timer/APC model: APC completion requires an alertable wait (e.g., `SleepEx(..., TRUE)`), not merely observing a signaled timer handle. It’s useful for defenders and researchers building detection/forensics around protection transitions, timers/APCs, and state history, and for red teamers as a compact educational artifact (not an operational loader). The interesting contribution is the focus on “evidence language” and validation modes that distinguish what is directly observed vs inferred.
Technical Focus: - Temporal memory state / protection cycling (RX↔RW/NX transitions) - Waitable timers, APC queuing, and alertable waits (`SetWaitableTimer`, `SleepEx`) - Windows memory APIs and page protection changes (`VirtualProtectEx`) - Architecture-specific execution/ABI constraints (x86, x64, ARM64, ARM64EC dynamic code) - Validation methodology: harness modes, artifact checks, runtime identity reporting - Defensive evidence surfaces: allocation/protection history, stacks, PTE/CFG residue, telemetry correlation
Use Cases: - Build test corpora to evaluate memory scanners/EDR heuristics against short “execute windows” - Develop detections for suspicious protection transitions correlated with timers/APCs/alertable waits - Teach Windows internals concepts (APCs, waitable timers, page protections, calling conventions) with a small PoC - Validate cross-architecture assumptions (x64 vs ARM64 vs ARM64EC dynamic code rules) in lab/CI - Forensics research: compare snapshot-based memory views vs timeline/stateful evidence sources
Keywords: Gargoyle, temporal memory state, memory scanning, page protection flipping, VirtualProtectEx, SetWaitableTimer, APC, Asynchronous Procedure Call, SleepEx, alertable wait, waitable timer, Windows internals, x86, x64, ARM64, ARM64EC, PIC, ROP, stack pivot, memory forensics, CFG bitmap, PTE, VAD, Volatility plugin, validation harness, Windows-on-Arm, ETW telemetry

entry.title: shellcode loaders the art of execution
entry.summary:
Title: Shellcode Loaders: Advanced Execution & Evasion Tradecraft
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - A deep technical guide to modern in-memory execution via shellcode/DLL/.NET loaders, focusing on how loaders bridge payload capability to reliable execution under EDR/AV scrutiny. - It surveys classic shellcode loaders, Stephen Fewer-style reflective DLL loading, CLR-hosted .NET assembly loading, and staged vs. stageless delivery tradeoffs. - The core of the post is a detailed walkthrough of Raphael Mudge’s Crystal Palace/Tradecraft Garden ecosystem for building modular position-independent loaders (PIC/PICO) via spec-driven linking and binary transformations. - It covers advanced evasion tradecraft including IAT-hook-based sleep masking, module overloading with SEC_IMAGE sections, NtContinue-based entry transfer to avoid suspicious return addresses, and call stack spoofing (Draugr). - Real-world implementations (Eden, KaplaStrike, StealthPalace) are used to show how these techniques combine in production-grade loaders and how they integrate with Cobalt Strike UDRL and other C2s. - Useful primarily for red team operators, implant/loader developers, and defenders wanting to understand loader telemetry and detection points (memory, call stacks, ETW/behavioral signals, and static YARA).
Technical Focus: - Reflective DLL loading vs. PIC/prepended loaders (PICO architecture, spec-driven linking) - PE manual mapping (relocations, imports, TLS, IAT processing) and API resolution (hashing/DFR) - Module overloading using NtCreateSection/NtMapViewOfSection (SEC_IMAGE) and .pdata unwind registration (RtlAddFunctionTable) - Execution transfer and stack hygiene (NtContinue, synthetic stacks, BaseThreadInitThunk/RtlUserThreadStart frames) - Sleep masking/obfuscation (Ekko-style timer queue + NtContinue, RC4 via SystemFunction032) and IAT-hook triggers - Static signature reduction (Crystal Palace +mutate/+disco/+optimize, YARA considerations)
Use Cases: - Build custom reflective/PIC loaders for in-memory execution of beacon DLLs or COFF capabilities - Replace default Cobalt Strike reflective loader via UDRL for improved OPSEC - Implement sleep masking and call stack spoofing to reduce memory-scan and stack-walk detections - Use module overloading to shift from private-commit executable memory to image-backed regions - Develop/assess detections for loader behaviors (SEC_IMAGE sections, NtContinue timers, RtlAddFunctionTable, RX↔RW flips)
Keywords: shellcode loader, reflective DLL injection, Stephen Fewer, Crystal Palace, Tradecraft Garden, PICO, COFF, BOF, UDRL, Cobalt Strike, PE manual mapping, IAT hooking, GetProcAddress hook, NtCreateSection, NtMapViewOfSection, SEC_IMAGE, module overloading, NtContinue, RtlAddFunctionTable, .pdata, unwind info, call stack spoofing, Draugr, Ekko sleep masking, SystemFunction032, RC4, YARA, ETW, AMSI

entry.title: vmkit
entry.summary:
Title: vmkit — Header-only C++20 IR-bytecode VM loader template
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - vmkit is a freestanding-friendly, header-only C++20 template for building small IR/bytecode virtual machines used as shellcode loaders. - It solves the repetitive work of implementing dispatch/decode/decrypt plumbing by providing a compile-time generated 256-entry dispatch table and a `Handler<>` specialization model per opcode. - The design emphasizes loader practicality (allocate/write/decrypt/protect/execute) over ISA fidelity, with optional obfuscation hooks: opcode randomization, bytecode XOR encryption-at-rest, and per-operation context encryption. - An included example demonstrates a real Windows loader pipeline that embeds encrypted bytecode + payload, decrypts in place, changes memory protections, and transfers execution. - It’s useful for red team operators, implant/loader developers, and malware researchers who want a reusable VM-based execution layer that can be hardened without changing the VM core. - Interesting aspects include compile-time validation (concepts/static_assert) to prevent missing opcode handlers, and `if constexpr` feature gating to avoid leaving unused crypto/obfuscation code in the binary.
Technical Focus: - Compile-time dispatch table generation (`constexpr std::array`, fold expressions) - Template specialization-based opcode handlers + concepts/static_assert validation - Opcode randomization via forward/reverse permutation maps - Bytecode decryption-at-rest (in-place) and per-op context encryption hooks - Freestanding C++20 loader constraints (no RTTI/exceptions/CRT) - Fixed-size IR op layout, alignment, and side-table operand referencing
Use Cases: - Build custom VM-based Windows shellcode loaders with minimal boilerplate - Add per-build opcode shuffling to break static opcode-sequence signatures - Embed and decrypt encrypted bytecode/payload blobs at runtime - Implement anti-analysis or syscall/API-resolution logic inside opcode handlers - Rapidly prototype new loader pipelines by adding opcodes/handlers
Keywords: C++20, freestanding, header-only, VM loader, bytecode interpreter, IR, dispatch table, constexpr, template specialization, concepts, static_assert, opcode randomization, XOR encryption, in-place decryption, context encryption, Windows shellcode loader, VirtualAlloc, VirtualProtect, execution transfer, API hashing (Jenkins OAAT)

entry.title: a technical deep dive comparing anti cheat bypass and edr bypass
entry.summary:
Title: A Technical Deep Dive: Comparing Anti-Cheat Bypass and EDR Bypass
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post compares anti-cheat bypass techniques used to evade game integrity controls with EDR bypass techniques used to evade endpoint detection in enterprise environments. - It frames the different goals, motivations, impacts, and legal/ethical boundaries between cheating ecosystems and malware/attacker tradecraft, while noting overlap in underlying methods. - The article highlights common Windows user-mode primitives used in both domains (e.g., remote thread injection and memory allocation/writes) and contrasts them with EDR-centric tactics like persistence, privilege manipulation, and anti-analysis. - It provides illustrative code snippets for DLL injection, function hooking (Detours-style), packet manipulation, and obfuscation, then discusses EDR evasion concepts such as polymorphism, rootkit-style hiding, encoded shellcode, string obfuscation, and anti-debugging. - A small demo section shows how XOR-based API name obfuscation and runtime resolution (GetModuleHandle/GetProcAddress) can reduce obvious static indicators in a malicious DLL (referencing a Notepad++ DLL sideloading scenario). - Useful for red teamers and malware analysts as a conceptual mapping between anti-cheat and EDR evasion tradecraft, and for blue teamers to understand shared primitives and detection opportunities.
Technical Focus: - Windows process injection (CreateRemoteThread, VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, LoadLibrary) - API hooking / detouring and user-mode manipulation - Obfuscation & polymorphism (junk code, XOR string/API obfuscation, runtime API resolution) - Anti-analysis / anti-debugging (IsDebuggerPresent, conditional logic) - Kernel/rootkit concepts (NtQuerySystemInformation tampering) and hiding artifacts - Persistence and privilege/token manipulation (registry/services, AdjustTokenPrivileges)
Use Cases: - Map anti-cheat bypass primitives to EDR evasion patterns for threat modeling - Build detection logic around common injection and runtime API-resolution behaviors - Improve static analysis by recognizing XORed strings and delayed API imports - Red team tradecraft education: comparing gaming vs enterprise defensive stacks - Defensive research: identify telemetry points for injection, hooking, and obfuscation
Keywords: anti-cheat, EDR bypass, DLL injection, CreateRemoteThread, VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, LoadLibraryA, GetProcAddress, GetModuleHandle, API hooking, Detours, packet manipulation, polymorphic malware, string obfuscation, XOR, shellcode encoding, anti-debugging, IsDebuggerPresent, NtQuerySystemInformation, rootkit, DLL sideloading, Notepad++ v8.5.4

entry.title: morphkatz
entry.summary:
Title: MorphKatz — Windows x64 polymorphic machine-code rewriter
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - MorphKatz is a Windows x64 polymorphic rewriter that mutates x86-64 machine code inside PE executables and raw shellcode into semantically equivalent but byte-different variants. - It targets the problem of brittle byte-pattern detections (e.g., YARA/Defender signature atoms) by applying CFG-aware instruction rewrites while preserving control flow and EFLAGS behavior. - The tool uses YAML rule packs for equivalence-preserving transformations, supports seeded/reproducible polymorphism, and can optionally verify correctness via re-disassembly and Unicorn basic-block emulation. - It includes detection feedback loops such as YARA-targeted prioritization and a Microsoft Defender (MpCmdRun) scan+bisect workflow to isolate signature anchors and drive rewrite selection. - It also supports “data-section morphing” that XOR-encodes signature-bearing bytes in .rdata/.data and adds a runtime decoder stub (with an anti-emulation gate) to defeat static signature hits. - Useful for both red teams (authorized evasion testing with reproducible diffs/reports) and blue teams (measuring rule durability, triaging signature anchors, generating polymorphic corpora for research/ML).
Technical Focus: - PE (Portable Executable) rewriting and hygiene (checksum, Authenticode stripping, Rich header handling) - CFG-aware recursive disassembly and jump-table recovery (Zydis) - Semantics-preserving instruction substitution with EFLAGS liveness/dataflow analysis - Rule-driven rewriting via YAML packs + targeted raw-byte patch pairs - Verification via re-disassembly and Unicorn Engine emulation - Defender feedback loop (MpCmdRun scanning, multi-anchor bisection) and YARA-aware targeting
Use Cases: - Generate polymorphic variants of a known binary to test AV/EDR signature robustness - Quantify YARA rule durability across many semantically identical mutations - Bisect and identify the exact byte windows responsible for Defender detections - Produce reproducible “before/after” diff reports for engagement documentation - Create controlled corpora for binary-similarity evaluation or ML classifier training
Keywords: Windows x64, PE rewriting, polymorphic engine, x86-64, semantic equivalence, EFLAGS liveness, CFG recovery, Zydis, Unicorn Engine, LIEF, libyara, YAML rule packs, YARA targeting, Microsoft Defender, MpCmdRun.exe, signature bisection, data-section morphing, TLS callback, entry-point thunk, shellcode rewriting

entry.title: k noir
entry.summary:
Title: Havoc Professional 0.7: K-Noir
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post announces Havoc Professional v0.7 “K-Noir” and summarizes major new offensive C2/implant capabilities. - The release adds a new Linux implant supporting x86_64 and AArch64 with operator-focused features like SOCKS proxying, reverse port forwarding, ELF object execution via a BOF-like API, guardrails, and cross-platform P2P linking. - On Windows, the callstack spoofing system is reworked with per-API/module “callstack rules” and updated to be compatible with Intel CET Shadow Stack, addressing modern anti-spoofing detections. - It introduces a native Registry Manipulation extension (local/remote operations, timestomping/anti-forensics) and demonstrates bootkey extraction via a Python API. - Developer/operator QoL improvements include an in-client Python debug server and better console visibility for UI-driven actions. - The “Inject-Kit” expands modular injection by allowing custom explicit/spawn injection tasks and composable primitives (allocation/write/thread creation), plus new comms channels (direct + P2P TCP) and additional evasion features (e.g., EDR event handler blinding/unregistering). - Useful primarily for red teams and pentesters building stealthier, more modular operations across Windows and Linux; also relevant to defenders tracking emerging tradecraft (CET-aware stack spoofing, injection modularity, registry anti-forensics).
Technical Focus: - Linux implant development (x86_64/AArch64), tunneling, and ELF object execution (BOF-like) - Callstack/stack spoofing with rule-based per-API contexts - Intel CET Shadow Stack compatibility for spoofing/evasion - Modular process injection pipeline (allocation/write/thread creation primitives; BOF integration) - Windows Registry manipulation, remote registry access, and timestomping/anti-forensics - C2 communications: direct TCP and P2P TCP channels; sleep obfuscation integration
Use Cases: - Deploying cross-platform implants (Windows + Linux) with consistent operator workflows - Building more believable API call telemetry via context-specific callstack spoofing rules - Operating on CET-enabled Windows targets while maintaining stack-spoofing-based evasion - Implementing/plugging in custom injection techniques for different target/EDR conditions - Performing remote registry operations with reduced forensic footprint (timestomp/rollback) - Establishing SOCKS/reverse port forwards and P2P links over new TCP channels
Keywords: Havoc Professional, Kaine-kit, Linux implant, x86_64, AArch64, BOF API, ELF object execution, SOCKS5, reverse port forwarding, P2P C2, direct TCP channel, callstack spoofing, stack spoofing rules, Intel CET, Shadow Stack, Win32 API, NtCreateThreadEx, process injection, BeaconInjectProcess, Windows Registry, remote registry, timestomping, bootkey extraction, sleep obfuscation, EDR event handler blinding

entry.title: advanced evasion tradecraft precision module stomping b51feb0978fe
entry.summary:
Title: Advanced Evasion Tradecraft: Precision Module Stomping
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post refines “module stomping” into a repeatable workflow for stable Windows process injection by carefully selecting which already-loaded DLL code region to overwrite. - It addresses common operational failures (crashes from missing modules or insufficient `.text` space) and the detection risk of “sacrificial DLL” loading, which is noisy and often flagged by EDR. - The author proposes a data-driven pipeline: enumerate loaded modules in a target process, find DLLs whose in-memory `.text` capacity can fit the payload, choose a specific exported function as an overwrite start point, and calculate collateral overwrite (“blast radius”) across adjacent exports. - Tooling is provided (in the author’s `windows-process-injection` repo) to automate module inventory, PE parsing, export enumeration, and blast-radius analysis. - A PoC targets Microsoft Edge and demonstrates overwriting an export in `ffmpeg.dll` and executing a large stageless C2 payload via a new thread. - It’s most useful for red teamers, implant developers, and malware researchers focused on stealthier in-process execution and reliability tradeoffs.
Technical Focus: - Module stomping (overwriting file-backed DLL code in memory) - Remote process module enumeration (PEB/module list) - PE parsing and `.text` section capacity analysis (`Misc_VirtualSize`) - Export Address Table (EAT) mapping and export selection - “Blast radius” / adjacent function overwrite impact analysis - Thread-based execution from a stomped export entry point
Use Cases: - Selecting low-noise, already-loaded DLL targets for code injection in high-value processes (e.g., browsers) - Pre-flight validation to prevent crashes due to insufficient code space or bad module assumptions - Automating candidate DLL discovery based on payload size constraints - Assessing functional collateral damage before overwriting code to preserve process stability - Building more reliable module-stomping tradecraft for long-lived implants
Keywords: module stomping, process injection, Windows internals, PEB, loaded modules, DLL, PE format, .text section, Misc_VirtualSize, pefile, Export Address Table, EAT, RVA, remote thread, memory overwrite, Edge msedge.exe, ffmpeg.dll, EDR evasion, sacrificial DLL, Cobalt Strike beacon

entry.title: bof cocktails in cobalt strike
entry.summary:
Title: BOF Cocktails in Cobalt Strike
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post explains how to integrate the “BOF Cocktails” execution pattern into Cobalt Strike using the new Aggressor hook `BEACON_INLINE_EXECUTE` (CS 4.13). - The goal is to intercept Beacon Object File (BOF) execution and transform/augment the BOF payload before it is sent to Beacon, enabling embedded tradecraft without relying on an external agent/loader for evasion. - It demonstrates using Crystal Palace to process raw BOF bytes through a spec file that converts the BOF into a COFF-exporter object, merges additional hook objects and libraries, and re-exports the final BOF. - A concrete example instruments (hooks) `ADVAPI32!OpenProcessToken` by attaching a wrapper function that logs parameters and then calls the original API. - This is useful for red team operators and tool developers who want consistent pre-execution BOF modification, API instrumentation, or tradecraft composition directly in Cobalt Strike’s workflow. - It’s interesting because it replaces prior hacky interception methods (e.g., `alias_clear` overrides) with a first-class, reliable hook point.
Technical Focus: - Cobalt Strike Aggressor scripting hook: `BEACON_INLINE_EXECUTE` - BOF (Beacon Object File) byte-level interception and transformation - Crystal Palace spec-driven BOF/COFF manipulation (merge/attach/export) - API hooking via import redirection/wrapper functions (e.g., `OpenProcessToken`) - x86/x64 build pipelines and merged object/library composition
Use Cases: - Pre-processing BOFs to embed evasion/tradecraft before execution - Instrumenting sensitive WinAPI calls for debugging/telemetry during ops - Automatically merging common helper libraries into every executed BOF - Enforcing standardized BOF hardening/obfuscation steps at execution time - Rapid prototyping of inline hooks across a BOF fleet without changing each BOF
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, Aggressor Script, BEACON_INLINE_EXECUTE, Beacon, BOF, Beacon Object File, COFF, Crystal Palace, spec file, inline execute hook, API hooking, ADVAPI32, OpenProcessToken, dprintf, x86, x64, merge, attach, export, post-exploitation

entry.title: wasmforge sliver webassembly
entry.summary:
Title: Enter the WasmForge: Compiling Sliver into WebAssembly
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post introduces “WasmForge,” a build-wrapper/loader pipeline that compiles unmodified Go offensive tools (e.g., Sliver, Chisel) into WebAssembly and then runs them inside a custom Wazero-based host binary for Windows and macOS. - It targets the problem of heavy static signature coverage on well-known red team tools by changing the binary’s observable structure without requiring source-code forks or rewrites. - The approach patches Go’s stdlib for the `wasip1` target and inserts `//go:wasmimport` shims so common APIs (net/http, os/exec, pipes, etc.) transparently delegate to host-implemented functions. - The host runtime provides ~80 shimmed functions spanning sockets, OS helpers, Win32 API access (LoadLibrary/GetProcAddress/SyscallN/registry/process creation), and macOS framework loading/calling. - It details a key engineering challenge: translating WASM linear-memory “pointers” into valid host pointers, using heuristics plus Win32 metadata-derived per-API pointer masks, plus special handling for COM/vtables and mirrored pointers. - For evasion, it describes per-build runtime/payload polymorphism (randomized opcode tables, section IDs, and magic bytes), “ghost” gopclntab symbol-table splicing, and randomized imports/PE metadata/signing identity to reduce static/ML detections. - Useful primarily for red teams/offensive tool developers who need repeatable, cross-platform repackaging of commodity tooling for hardened endpoints, and for defenders as a case study in emerging WASM-based loader tradecraft.
Technical Focus: - Go → WASI (`wasip1`) compilation with patched stdlib and `//go:wasmimport` shims - Embedded WASM execution via a forked Wazero runtime (opcode/format randomization) - Host-function bridging for sockets, Win32 APIs, and macOS frameworks - WASM linear-memory pointer translation, shadow/mirror memory, COM/vtable handling - Win32 API pointer semantics via win32metadata/win32json-generated argument masks - Static-evasion knobs: gopclntab “ghost profiles,” PE import/metadata randomization, Authenticode signing variation
Use Cases: - Repackaging Sliver/Chisel/Mythic-style implants into less-signatured Windows/macOS droppers without modifying tool source - Running Go tooling through a WASM-hosted loader to evade static YARA/ML rules tied to Go PE artifacts (e.g., gopclntab patterns) - Cross-platform loader development where the payload is portable WASM and only shims differ per OS - Researching/detecting WASM-in-native-container tradecraft (nonstandard WASM headers, runtime opcode-table randomization) - Building a measurement-driven evasion pipeline using VT/EDR feedback to tune binary “appearance” (imports, symbols, metadata)
Keywords: WasmForge, WebAssembly, WASI Preview 1, wasip1, Go compiler, patched GOROOT, gopclntab, Wazero, host functions, wasmimport, linear memory, pointer translation, Win32 API, syscalln, LoadLibrary, GetProcAddress, COM vtable, win32metadata, win32json, PE sections, Authenticode signing, opcode table randomization, Sliver, Chisel, execute-assembly, BOF, goffloader, EDR evasion, YARA signatures

entry.title: bring your own rwx region dll byorwxdll 0283951d34e9
entry.summary:
Title: Bring Your Own RWX Region DLL (BYORWXDLL)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post describes BYORWXDLL, a Windows process-injection approach inspired by BYOVD that abuses legitimate signed DLLs containing PE sections mapped as RWX (or effectively RWX) at load time. The goal is to execute shellcode in a remote process while avoiding high-signal APIs commonly monitored by EDRs, specifically `VirtualAllocEx` and `VirtualProtectEx`. The methodology covers (1) static discovery of DLLs on disk with RWX section flags using Python `pefile`, (2) dynamic validation by loading the DLL and enumerating memory protections via `VirtualQuery`, (3) identifying target processes that already have the DLL loaded, and (4) writing shellcode into the pre-existing RWX region with `WriteProcessMemory` and executing it via `CreateRemoteThread`. It highlights `PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE` and `PAGE_EXECUTE_WRITECOPY` as viable target protections. This is primarily useful for red team/offensive tooling developers and malware researchers looking to reduce injection telemetry by reusing existing executable+writable mappings.
Technical Focus: - PE section characteristics and RWX mapping behavior - Static DLL scanning with Python `pefile` - Runtime memory enumeration with `VirtualQuery` / `MEMORY_BASIC_INFORMATION` - Remote process memory write and thread creation (`WriteProcessMemory`, `CreateRemoteThread`) - EDR evasion via avoiding `VirtualAllocEx` / `VirtualProtectEx` - `PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE` vs `PAGE_EXECUTE_WRITECOPY` (copy-on-write executable pages)
Use Cases: - Quieter shellcode injection by reusing existing RWX pages in a target process - Host-based discovery of “RWX-capable” signed DLLs for tradecraft planning - Building PoCs to evaluate EDR telemetry around memory allocation/protection changes - Defensive hunting: inventorying DLLs with RWX sections and processes that map them
Keywords: BYORWXDLL, BYOVD, RWX, PE sections, IMAGE_SCN_MEM_EXECUTE, IMAGE_SCN_MEM_READ, IMAGE_SCN_MEM_WRITE, pefile, LoadLibrary, VirtualQuery, PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE, PAGE_EXECUTE_WRITECOPY, WriteProcessMemory, CreateRemoteThread, VirtualAllocEx, VirtualProtectEx, process injection, Windows internals, EDR telemetry

entry.title: StackSentry
entry.summary:
Title: StackSentry
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): StackSentry is a Windows x64 user-mode memory triage and loader-analysis tool focused on attributing “sensitive” DLL loads and network API usage to their real in-memory origin, even when call stacks are spoofed or laundered. It launches a target process, injects a lightweight monitor DLL, hooks loader/network-relevant paths, and correlates events with stack state, memory mappings, and integrity signals to answer who actually triggered a DLL load or network call. The project targets modern evasion patterns such as callback/proxy/gadget-based LoadLibrary flows, threadpool chains, APC/VEH dispatch, execution from unbacked private memory, and modified image-backed code caves. It also includes research modes for unwind-metadata tampering (BYOUD) and optional CET shadow-stack comparison, plus an opt-in Moneta/PE-sieve-inspired memory audit and optional ETW timeline capture. Output is geared for lab analysis: JSONL event streams, summaries, memory/module dumps, modified-byte maps, and origin-region captures. Useful for malware analysts, threat hunters, and red teamers validating loader tradecraft and defensive hypotheses without requiring a kernel driver.
Technical Focus: - User-mode instrumentation via injected monitor DLL and API hooking - Call stack analysis, callsite validation, and stack spoofing detection - Memory classification (MEM_PRIVATE/MEM_MAPPED/MEM_IMAGE) and “unbacked” execution detection - Modified image detection (BackedModified), module integrity and LDR entrypoint hijack checks - Unwind metadata integrity/BYOUD detection (.pdata/.xdata/RUNTIME_FUNCTION, dynamic unwind) - Network API use attribution (Winsock/WinINet/WinHTTP/DNS) and correlation to origin
Use Cases: - Triage suspicious loaders/fileless implants by attributing network-module loads to hidden origins - Validate and reproduce call stack spoofing techniques (e.g., SilentMoonwalk, gadget/proxy chains) - Detect module stomping/code caves and preserve modified-byte diff maps for reverse engineering - Hunt for unwind-metadata spoofing (BYOUD) and correlate temporal divergence to sensitive loads - Attribute real network activity (connect/send/WinHTTP/WinINet/DNS) to private/modified code regions - Follow lab samples that pivot into child/remote processes and collect per-PID artifacts
Keywords: Windows x64, user-mode hooking, DLL injection, LoadLibrary, LdrLoadDll, call stack spoofing, callsite validation, MEM_PRIVATE, MEM_IMAGE, module stomping, code cave, PE integrity, PEB LDR_DATA_TABLE_ENTRY, entrypoint hijacking, unwind metadata, RUNTIME_FUNCTION, BYOUD, CET shadow stack, ETW krabsetw, Winsock ws2_32, WinINet, WinHTTP, dnsapi, APC, VEH, threadpool callbacks, memory forensics, Moneta, PE-sieve

entry.title: cet spoofing detection
entry.summary:
Title: CET Spoofing Detection
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This repository provides a proof-of-concept Windows tool to detect call stack/stack spoofing in Intel CET-enabled processes. It snapshots threads, identifies CET processes, and compares the user-mode stack against the CET shadow stack to find missing or inconsistent frames indicative of spoofed call stacks. The goal is to catch techniques used to evade EDR/userland stack-walking by manipulating return addresses or unwinding metadata while CET maintains a protected shadow stack. The author notes false positives with some .NET scenarios due to different stack/unwind behaviors. It’s primarily useful for Blue Teams/SOC and EDR engineers validating detections, and for Red Teams to understand CET-based detection surfaces. It’s interesting because it leverages CET’s shadow stack as a ground-truth signal against user stack tampering.
