Technical Focus: - Intel CET shadow stack vs user stack comparison - Thread snapshotting and per-thread stack inspection - Stack unwinding / frame validation (missing frames detection) - Detection of stack spoofing / call stack manipulation - Windows process/thread introspection in C++
Use Cases: - Detect stack spoofing in CET-protected processes during incident response - Validate/benchmark EDR detections against call stack spoofing techniques - Red team tradecraft testing to see whether spoofing survives CET-based scrutiny - Research on CET’s defensive value and limitations (e.g., .NET false positives)
Keywords: Intel CET, shadow stack, stack spoofing, call stack spoofing, stack unwinding, missing frames, Windows threads, thread snapshot, user stack, CET processes, EDR evasion, detection engineering, BYOUD, process introspection, C++, .NET false positives, unwind metadata, SOC tooling

entry.title: TinyLoad
entry.summary:
Title: TinyLoad v7.1
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): TinyLoad is a Windows x64 PE packer/crypter that wraps an input executable into a self-extracting stub and runs the decrypted payload directly in memory (no original payload written back to disk). It aims to hinder reverse engineering and static analysis by layering optional protections: custom LZ77 compression, a custom virtual-machine (VM) based encryption scheme, and on-demand page decryption via vectored exception handling (VEH). Each build mutates by randomizing VM opcode placement into multiple independently keyed subtables, making packed outputs non-identical across runs. It also includes anti-dump measures by redirecting key WinAPI calls through stub wrappers and zeroing the payload’s import directory after load to complicate import reconstruction from memory dumps. The project is a single C++ file with no external dependencies and is built with MinGW. It’s primarily useful for red team tooling developers, malware analysts/researchers (for understanding packer tradecraft), and defenders building unpacking/detection logic.
Technical Focus: - PE packing/crypter stub design (append payload, in-memory execution) - Custom LZ77 compression (hash-chain matching, 64KB window) - VM-based obfuscation/encryption (opcode virtualization, subtable randomization) - Vectored Exception Handler (VEH) page-fault driven on-demand decryption (PAGE_NOACCESS) - Anti-dump techniques (IAT/import directory destruction, API wrapper indirection) - Windows memory protections and page-level re-encryption watchdog logic
Use Cases: - Packing internal red-team payloads/tools to test detection and unpacking pipelines - Studying VM-based packer design and mutation strategies for RE practice - Developing defensive heuristics/signatures for packers using VEH page-fault decryption - Evaluating memory-dump resistance techniques (import wiping, partial in-memory plaintext)
Keywords: TinyLoad, PE packer, crypter, Windows x64, in-memory execution, stub loader, LZ77, hash chain, virtual machine obfuscation, VM interpreter, opcode randomization, stream cipher, ROTL, ROTR, VEH, VectoredExceptionHandler, PAGE_NOACCESS, page-fault decryption, VirtualAlloc, GetProcAddress, import directory wiping, anti-dump, memory dumping, PE sections

entry.title: Wyrm
entry.summary:
Title: Wyrm – v0.7.2 “Hatchling”
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Wyrm is an open-source post-exploitation Command-and-Control (C2) framework written in Rust, aimed at red team and purple team security testing. - It provides an HTTPS-based implant with an additional custom encryption layer beneath TLS and a bespoke packet format intended to resist decryption even under TLS inspection. - The framework emphasizes artifact/IOC obfuscation, configurable implant “profiles” (including evasion/anti-sandbox options), and dynamic payload generation. - It includes a fully reflective DLL (rDLL) model with a loader that stores the encrypted payload in the loader’s `.text` section, plus support for DLL search-order hijacking/sideloading workflows. - Operators interact via a terminal client, with Docker-based C2 deployment, resource staging for delivery (e.g., phishing support), and a database-backed, timestamped activity model for reporting. - Useful for red team operators, adversary emulation, and defenders researching modern C2 tradecraft and detection opportunities; the repository is archived (read-only) as of Mar 27, 2026.
Technical Focus: - Rust-based C2/implant architecture (client, server, loader, shared crates) - HTTPS communications with custom encryption below TLS + custom packet design - Reflective DLL loading and process injection/loader tradecraft - Configurable implant profiles (evasion, anti-sandbox, obfuscation) - In-memory execution (e.g., .NET assemblies) and payload staging - Dockerized infrastructure + database-backed operator telemetry
Use Cases: - Adversary emulation during authorized red team engagements - Testing perimeter inspection controls (TLS inspection, proxy environments) against layered encryption - Evaluating detection coverage for reflective loading, injection, and DLL sideloading behaviors - Building customizable post-exploitation workflows via statically linked “WOFs” object files - Rapid C2 deployment for lab environments using Docker Compose
Keywords: Wyrm, C2, command-and-control, post-exploitation, Rust, HTTPS, TLS inspection evasion, custom encryption, packet format, reflective DLL, rDLL, loader, process injection, DLL search order hijacking, DLL sideloading, in-memory execution, .NET assembly execution, anti-sandbox, obfuscation, Docker Compose

entry.title: mythic c2 early bird defender evasion
entry.summary:
Title: Mythic C2 with EarlyBird Injection and Defender Evasion
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - The post walks through building a Mythic C2 deployment that hides the real backend server behind HTTPS redirectors and uses a custom Windows loader to execute an Apollo agent payload in-memory. - It focuses on practical C2 OPSEC: separating a decoy web presence (e.g., WordPress) from C2 and payload delivery subdomains, using Nginx reverse proxying and legitimate TLS certificates to reduce infrastructure attribution and casual inspection. - For execution, it demonstrates EarlyBird APC injection by creating a suspended process, writing shellcode into it, queueing an APC with `QueueUserAPC`, and resuming the thread so code runs during early process initialization. - The loader includes basic evasion tradecraft such as compile-time string obfuscation, jittered sleeps, browser-like WinHTTP headers/User-Agent rotation, and a multi-tier download/fallback flow. - It also calls out OPSEC pitfalls (notably WinHTTP certificate-validation bypass flags) and provides defender-oriented detection ideas around suspended process creation, APC usage, and RWX memory allocations. - Useful primarily for red teamers and malware/tooling developers building Mythic-based operations, and secondarily for blue teams modeling detections for redirector-backed C2 and APC-based injection.
Technical Focus: - Mythic C2 architecture (Apollo agent + HTTP profile) - HTTPS redirectors / Nginx reverse proxy design with decoy content - EarlyBird APC injection (`CREATE_SUSPENDED`, `VirtualAllocEx`, `WriteProcessMemory`, `QueueUserAPC`, `ResumeThread`) - In-memory payload delivery (shellcode staging via “legit-looking” URLs) - WinHTTP-based payload retrieval + traffic shaping (headers, UA rotation, jitter) - Basic obfuscation (compile-time XOR string obfuscation, XOR payload fallback)
Use Cases: - Stand up a Mythic backend protected by redirectors and decoy web infrastructure - Stage and deliver shellcode via benign-looking paths (e.g., font assets) over HTTPS - Execute an agent via EarlyBird APC injection into a chosen target process (e.g., WerFault.exe) - Prototype OPSEC improvements/detections for redirector-based C2 and APC injection tradecraft - Build lab exercises for testing EDR/Defender visibility into suspended-process + APC patterns
Keywords: Mythic, Apollo agent, HTTP C2 profile, Nginx redirector, reverse proxy, HTTPS, Let’s Encrypt, Certbot, decoy site, WordPress, payload staging, shellcode, EarlyBird injection, APC injection, QueueUserAPC, CreateProcessW CREATE_SUSPENDED, VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, ResumeThread, WinHTTP, User-Agent rotation, jitter sleep, string obfuscation, Windows Defender evasion, WerFault.exe, RWX memory allocation, process injection detection

entry.title: reflectra
entry.summary:
Title: Reflectra — Modular User-Defined Reflective Loader (UDRL) on Crystal Palace
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Reflectra is a Windows x64 user-defined reflective loader that manually maps DLL payloads (PE sections, relocations, imports) and executes a chosen exported function without relying on the native Windows loader. It targets controlled in-memory execution for DLL-based C2 payloads, aiming to be reusable across frameworks (explicit examples include AdaptixC2) while keeping execution flow and memory layout under operator control. The project layers multiple evasion primitives into the loading pipeline, including indirect syscalls (LibGate), stack call spoofing (Draugr), ETW user-mode patching, and XOR masking of the DLL. It integrates with the Crystal Palace ecosystem and includes a build pipeline that outputs a transformed payload blob, a stub executable, and auto-generated YARA rules while addressing “islands of invariance.” Useful primarily for red teams, implant developers, and security researchers experimenting with custom loaders and EDR/YARA evasion tradeoffs. Notable limitations include incompatibility with some Go-based DLL payloads and that only IAT-resolved APIs are affected by its hooking approach.
Technical Focus: - Manual PE/DLL mapping (imports, relocations, section permissions) - User-defined reflective loader architecture (UDRL) and custom entrypoint targeting - Indirect syscalls (LibGate) and syscall-based evasion - Stack call spoofing (Draugr) - ETW user-mode patching / byte patching - YARA rule generation and mutation handling (“islands of invariance”)
Use Cases: - Execute DLL-based beacons/implants from memory with custom control over mapping and execution - Integrate a reusable reflective loader into multiple C2 frameworks that deliver DLL payloads - Research and test ETW patching, syscall indirection, and call-stack spoofing in a loader context - Generate and validate YARA signatures for transformed payload artifacts during development - Build standalone “stub.exe” runners for lab testing of in-memory DLL execution
Keywords: UDRL, reflective loader, manual mapping, PE loader, Windows x64, DLL beacon, Crystal Palace, Tradecraft Garden, LibGate, indirect syscalls, Draugr, stack spoofing, ETW patching, OutputDebugStringA, IAT hooking, XOR masking, YARA generation, islands of invariance, MinGW, in-memory execution

entry.title: small pic energy
entry.summary:
Title: Small PIC Energy
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post explores “small PIC” (position-independent code) design for offensive agents, framed as a challenge: how much beacon/agent functionality can fit into ~4KB PIC without devolving into shellcode-golf. - It introduces updates around the Crystal Palace linker/composition workflow and Tradecraft Garden, including a Simple BOF runner, improved library merge behavior, and a new JSON-over-HTTP “Linker Sidecar” service for language-agnostic integration. - The core technical idea is separating “capability assembly” (building COFF modules, patching config, selecting features) from “tradecraft pairing” (turning COFF into PIC/shellcode and applying transformations like optimize/mutate). - It proposes “BOF inversions”: moving Beacon API implementation responsibility out of the C2 agent and into the BOF itself by merging a BOF API implementation during linking, then executing as a PICO with a minimal runner in the agent. - This enables “BOF cocktails” style pre-processing/evasion to be applied to BOFs independently of the C2, benefiting both research/detection experiments and operators who want self-protecting BOFs. - Useful for red team toolsmiths, C2 engineers, and tradecraft researchers building modular PIC agents, BOF pipelines, and low-friction integration into non-Java stacks.
Technical Focus: - Position-independent code (PIC) minimization and modular agent design - COFF/BOF to PIC/PICO transformation pipelines via spec-driven linking - BOF inversions (embedding Beacon API implementations into BOFs) - Tradecraft separation: capability build vs time-of-use transformations (+optimize/+mutate/ised) - JSON-over-HTTP localhost sidecar API for linker automation/integration
Use Cases: - Build ultra-small Windows PIC stagers/agents with modular features (e.g., WinINet loop + command kernel + BOF runner) - Pre-process BOFs into self-contained PICOs that don’t require full Beacon API support in the agent - Integrate Crystal Palace linking into custom C2 frameworks (Python/Go/Rust/etc.) via the sidecar service - Run controlled experiments comparing “naked BOFs” vs tradecraft-paired BOFs for detection research - Reduce agent complexity by delegating BOF API surface into link-time merged code
Keywords: PIC, position-independent code, Crystal Palace, Tradecraft Garden, COFF, Beacon Object File, BOF, Beacon API, BOF cocktails, PICO, shellcode, WinINet, spec file, linker pipeline, +optimize, +mutate, ised, JSON-over-HTTP, sidecar service, localhost API

entry.title: CrystalForge
entry.summary:
Title: CrystalForge (AdaptixC2 agent extender with Crystal Palace UDRL pipeline)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): CrystalForge is a custom AdaptixC2 agent/extender that modifies the default beacon build pipeline to integrate Crystal Palace’s UDRL (User-Defined Reflective Loader) workflow. It aims to make it easier to generate Adaptix payloads that are post-processed through Crystal Palace using selectable `.spec` files, enabling custom loader techniques for evasion and experimentation. The tool supports generating DLL and shellcode outputs, where shellcode is produced by taking an intermediate DLL and linking/transforming it via Crystal Palace according to the chosen spec. It preserves the standard Adaptix beacon feature set and multi-transport support while adding a flexible loader integration layer. Limitations noted include lack of EXE/service outputs and incomplete coverage for dynamically resolved APIs (focus is on IAT-resolved functions). It’s primarily useful for red teams, offensive security researchers, and malware tradecraft developers working on loader customization and C2 payload generation workflows.
Technical Focus: - AdaptixC2 extender/agent plugin architecture - Crystal Palace UDRL pipeline and `.spec`-driven loader selection - DLL-to-shellcode transformation via intermediate DLL processing - Windows reflective loading / PE loader concepts - API hooking constraints (IAT vs dynamically resolved APIs) - Loader development workflow (precompiled external loader components)
Use Cases: - Integrate custom Crystal Palace loaders into AdaptixC2 payload generation - Produce shellcode variants from a standard beacon DLL using different UDRL specs - Rapidly test/iterate loader techniques (evasion-focused) within a C2 workflow - Standardize operator workflow for selecting loader specs during build time - Research and prototype reflective loader/PE loading tradecraft
Keywords: AdaptixC2, CrystalForge, Crystal Palace, UDRL, reflective loader, PE loader, shellcode generation, DLL payload, beacon, C2 agent, spec file, loader pipeline, IAT hooking, Windows internals, MinGW, C, C++, Go, stack spoofing, Crystal-Kit

entry.title: CrystalSliver
entry.summary:
Title: CrystalSliver (crystal-kit-sliver) — Crystal Palace evasion kit ported to Sliver C2
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - CrystalSliver is a Sliver C2 port of rasta-mouse’s Crystal-Kit, replacing Sliver’s default reflective loader and post-execution DLL loading with the Crystal Palace PICO (position-independent code) execution model. - It aims to reduce static and behavioral detections by avoiding common loader indicators (plain LoadLibrary/GetProcAddress usage, RWX mappings, obvious strings) and by adding obfuscation/sleep-masking and call stack spoofing. - The repo provides two main workflows: (A) wrapping a Sliver implant DLL into a Crystal Palace PICO, encrypting it (AES-256-CBC), and executing it via a small custom stager that decrypts and runs in-memory; (B) running post-ex DLLs through a Sliver Extension (“crystal-loader”) that loads a PICO blob and executes it with RW→RX transitions. - It also includes a “crystal-exec” extension command to run arbitrary shell commands through Crystal Palace without uploading a separate PICO file (embedded PICO inside the extension DLL). - Useful primarily for red teams/pentesters operating Sliver in Windows environments who want a Crystal-Kit-like evasion path outside of Cobalt Strike/Mythic. - Interesting because it documents a full cross-C2 port with build glue, operator runbooks, and specific evasion-driven implementation changes (export renaming, string stripping, RW→RX, encryption, and packaging).
Technical Focus: - Crystal Palace PICO generation/linking and in-memory execution - Reflective loading replacement for Sliver implants and post-ex DLLs - API hashing (ror13) and avoidance of LoadLibrary/GetProcAddress patterns - Memory permission staging (VirtualAlloc RW → VirtualProtect RX; no RWX) - Evasion features: IAT hooks, call stack spoofing (Draugr), sleep mask, runtime obfuscation (libtcg) - Sliver Extensions (in-memory extension DLL loading, command plumbing, argument passing)
Use Cases: - Wrap and deliver Sliver implant DLLs as encrypted PICO payloads executed fully in-memory via a custom stager - Execute sensitive post-ex tooling (e.g., credential dumping/recon DLLs) through a Sliver extension loader with reduced loader artifacts - Run one-off shell commands via “crystal-exec” without uploading additional payload files - Research/compare detection surfaces between default Sliver loaders and Crystal Palace-based execution chains - Build a repeatable Kali→Windows toolchain for PICO-based payload packaging and deployment
Keywords: Sliver C2, Crystal-Kit, Crystal Palace, PICO, reflective loader, post-execution, Windows x64, ror13 API hashing, IAT hooking, VirtualAlloc, VirtualProtect, RW-to-RX, sleep mask, Draugr call stack spoofing, libtcg obfuscation, AES-256-CBC, BCryptDecrypt, in-memory execution, Sliver Extension, MinGW-w64, NASM

entry.title: modules and monoliths
entry.summary:
Title: Modules and Monoliths
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post responds to memN0ps’ write-up on building a monolithic user-defined reflective loader (UDRL) in Rust and the implied tradeoffs versus modular loader frameworks like Crystal Palace and Crystal Kit. - The author argues for “componentization” of post-exploitation tradecraft: separating loader/evasion primitives into swappable modules rather than bundling everything into a bespoke monolith. - It frames Tradecraft Garden’s goal as defining clean dividing lines between capabilities and tradecraft components so researchers can study, replace, and publish improvements to individual primitives (e.g., module resolution, API invocation, callstack/telemetry evasion). - The post highlights ecosystem synergy where independent research (e.g., LibTP threadpool-based NTAPI proxying, call-gadget approaches to evade callstack signatures) can be dropped into shared libraries/modules without requiring a full C2 “weaponization” context. - It emphasizes research value over turnkey “beat EDR X” outcomes, advocating de-escalated releases and demonstrations grounded in system properties/events rather than product-specific bypasses. - Useful for red team tradecraft developers and defenders who want clearer, testable primitives and more reproducible research artifacts.
Technical Focus: - Modular vs monolithic loader architecture (UDRL design tradeoffs) - Componentized post-exploitation tradecraft (swap-in primitives/shared libraries) - PEB walking and alternative module lookup/resolution techniques - NTAPI call proxying via Windows Threadpool (LibTP pattern) - Callstack signature evasion via call gadgets / stack manipulation - Research release strategy: de-escalated release, non-weaponized demonstrations
Use Cases: - Building/maintaining modular Windows loaders where evasion primitives can be swapped independently - Rapidly testing new module resolution, syscall/NTAPI invocation, or telemetry-evasion techniques in isolation - Creating reproducible research artifacts that can be evaluated without coupling to a specific C2 - Developing defensive detections by analyzing discrete primitives (threadpool abuse, callstack anomalies, PEB-walk patterns) - Teaching/learning loader tradecraft via a modular foundation (e.g., Crystal Kit as a lab base)
Keywords: Tradecraft Garden, Crystal Palace, Crystal Kit, LibTCG, UDRL, reflective loader, modular architecture, monolithic loader, Rust nightly, PEB walk, module resolution, NTAPI, Windows Threadpool, LibTP, ETW/ETWTI, callstack signatures, call gadgets, post-exploitation tradecraft, Cobalt Strike, Mythic Xenon

entry.title: doublepulsar a user defined reflective loader in the crystal palace and tradecraft garden era
entry.summary:
Title: DoublePulsar: A User-Defined Reflective Loader in the Crystal Palace and Tradecraft Garden Era
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post is a deep technical walkthrough of “DoublePulsar,” an open-source Cobalt Strike User-Defined Reflective Loader (UDRL) written in Rust that implements a prepended reflective loader architecture. It explains how the loader decrypts and manually maps an encrypted Beacon payload, then applies multiple evasion layers including module stomping into a legitimate DLL’s `.text`, IAT hooking for runtime control, heap isolation, sleep obfuscation, and synthetic call stack/return address spoofing. The article contrasts monolithic UDRLs (TitanLdr/AceLdr/BokuLoader style) with the newer Crystal Palace/Tradecraft Garden modular PICO approach, outlining tradeoffs in development effort, flexibility, and detection response. It details implementation specifics (no_std Rust PIC, custom linker script, objcopy extraction, ACE thread context hijack, `.pdata` unwind parsing for stack spoofing, NtContinue-based sleep chains, optional direct syscalls). For defenders, it provides detection angles (ETWTI, call stack validation, module content mismatch, IAT integrity checks), plus YARA rules and a MITRE ATT&CK mapping focused on the loader’s artifacts rather than the encrypted payload. It’s most useful to red team tool builders and detection engineers who need concrete loader internals, IoCs, and behavioral detection opportunities for modern in-memory tradecraft.
Technical Focus: - Cobalt Strike UDRL (prepended reflective loader) and manual PE mapping - Module stomping / MEM_IMAGE vs MEM_PRIVATE evasion and CoW side effects - Synthetic call stack & return address spoofing via `.pdata` unwind parsing (SilentMoonwalk/uwd-style) - Sleep obfuscation chains using `NtContinue` + timers/APCs/waits (Ekko/FOLIAGE/Zilean) and fiber stacks - IAT hooking for API interception (sleep, heap, network) and heap isolation/encryption - Optional direct syscall dispatch (Hell’s Gate / Halo’s Gate / Tartarus Gate) and CFG bypass (`SetProcessValidCallTargets`)
Use Cases: - Build or customize a Cobalt Strike UDRL to control allocation, mapping, permissions, and runtime hooks - Implement module-stomped in-memory implants to reduce “unbacked RX” detections - Add sleep masking/encryption and heap isolation to reduce memory-scanner signature windows - Develop or test call-stack spoofing/return-address spoofing against ETW/call-stack-based detections - Create defensive detections for reflective loaders (IAT integrity, module mismatch scanning, ETWTI patterns) - Write YARA signatures targeting loader artifacts and validate against memory-resident samples
Keywords: DoublePulsar, UDRL, Cobalt Strike, reflective loader, sRDI, manual PE mapping, Rust no_std, position-independent code, module stomping, LoadLibraryExA, DONT_RESOLVE_DLL_REFERENCES, NtProtectVirtualMemory, IAT hooking, heap isolation, RtlCreateHeap, RtlWalkHeap, sleep obfuscation, Ekko, FOLIAGE, Zilean, NtContinue, fibers, call stack spoofing, .pdata, UNWIND_INFO, SilentMoonwalk, Hell’s Gate, Halo’s Gate, Tartarus Gate, SetProcessValidCallTargets, ETWTI, PE-sieve, Moneta, YARA, MITRE ATT&CK

entry.title: EtwTiViewer
entry.summary:
Title: EtwTiViewer — Live ETW-TI (Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence) Event Viewer
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - EtwTiViewer is a Windows research tool to live-consume and visualize events from the secure ETW provider **Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence (ETW-TI)**, a telemetry source commonly used by commercial EDRs to detect in-memory attacks and process injection. - It solves the problem of exploring ETW-TI coverage and event semantics in real time (keywords, fields, process/image context) with filtering and JSONL logging. - The repo documents ETW-TI’s access restriction (subscriber must be **PS_PROTECTED_ANTIMALWARE_LIGHT**) and demonstrates a lab-only workaround by patching the viewer process’s **EPROCESS->Protection** via kernel debugging so `EnableTraceEx2` succeeds. - Architecture combines a kernel driver (process/image callbacks + named-pipe forwarding + IOCTL control) with a C++17 ImGui/DirectX11 GUI ETW consumer using StartTrace/ProcessTrace and TDH decoding. - Useful for detection engineers, EDR researchers, and red teamers validating what ETW-TI emits for injection primitives; it’s interesting because it operationalizes a normally vendor-only telemetry source for controlled research.
Technical Focus: - Secure ETW provider consumption (Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence / ETW-TI) - Protected Process Light (PPL) / PS_PROTECTED_ANTIMALWARE_LIGHT and EPROCESS->Protection - Kernel debugging–assisted security boundary patching (WinDbg) - ETW real-time sessions (StartTraceW, EnableTraceEx2, ProcessTrace) + TDH decoding - WDM driver callbacks (PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutineEx, PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine) - Named-pipe event forwarding + IOCTL control plane
Use Cases: - Validate which ETW-TI events fire for specific injection techniques (APC, SetThreadContext, RWX, MapViewOfSection, Read/WriteProcessMemory) - Build/verify detection logic and telemetry mappings for EDR-like analytics in a lab - Compare ETW-TI keyword/task coverage across Windows builds - Generate labeled datasets (JSONL) for threat research and rule testing - Troubleshoot ETW subscription/access issues related to PPL requirements
Keywords: ETW-TI, Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence, ETW, Secure ETW provider, EnableTraceEx2, StartTraceW, ProcessTrace, TDH, PEVENT_RECORD, PPL, Protected Process Light, PS_PROTECTED_ANTIMALWARE_LIGHT, EPROCESS, _PS_PROTECTION, WinDbg, kernel debugging, WDM driver, PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutineEx, PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine, IOCTL, named pipe, process injection, APC injection, SetThreadContext, VirtualProtectEx, MapViewOfSection, ReadProcessMemory, WriteProcessMemory, ImGui, DirectX 11

entry.title: EtwSuite
entry.summary:
Title: EtwSuite
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): EtwSuite is a Windows desktop ETW (Event Tracing for Windows) inspection suite for browsing registered providers, inspecting provider metadata/schema, and consuming live ETW events. It aims to consolidate provider discovery, session configuration, live viewing, recording to native `.etl`, and offline inspection of `.etl`/exported `.json`/`.csv` in one GUI tool (inspired by EtwExplorer). It includes event filtering via simple wildcard search or a safe SQL-like expression parser over event fields (provider, event_id, process_name, payload fields, etc.). The tool supports exporting captured events to JSON/CSV and saving reusable “session templates” to a local SQLite database. It highlights practical limitations of ETW metadata availability and notes that some providers require elevation or special permissions (e.g., Threat-Intelligence requiring an allowed PPL signer). Useful for defenders, threat hunters, and red teamers who need to quickly explore ETW telemetry, validate detections, or understand what specific providers emit during activity.
Technical Focus: - ETW provider enumeration and metadata/schema inspection - Live ETW session consumption (levels/keywords masks) - ETL trace recording and offline parsing (TraceEvent) - Event filtering (wildcards and SQL-like expression parsing) - Export pipelines (JSON/CSV) and session persistence (SQLite) - Windows permissions constraints for sensitive providers (e.g., PPL-gated)
Use Cases: - Rapidly identify which ETW providers/events fire for a given technique or tool execution - Build and validate detection logic by filtering on process/event/payload fields - Record `.etl` traces during tests and review them offline on another machine - Create reusable ETW collection “templates” for common investigations (PowerShell, process creation, security auditing) - Troubleshoot ETW provider access/metadata issues across Windows versions
Keywords: ETW, Event Tracing for Windows, provider GUID, event schema, TraceEvent, ETL, Microsoft-Windows-Security-Auditing, Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence, PPL, Protected Process Light, keyword mask, event level, WinUI, Windows App SDK, .NET 8, C#, SQLite, JSON export, CSV export, telemetry filtering

entry.title: no agent no problem discovering remote edr 8ca60596559f
entry.summary:
Title: No Agent, No Problem: Discovering Remote EDR
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post shows how to build “agentless” endpoint monitoring by remotely enumerating and creating ETW trace sessions using Windows Performance Logs and Alerts (PLA) DCOM/COM interfaces (pla.dll), rather than deploying an EDR agent or using WMI. - It explains PLA concepts (data collectors, data collector sets, namespaces) and how tools like `logman` use `IDataCollectorSetCollection::GetDataCollectorSets` and related interfaces to query across namespaces, including less-visible ones (e.g., Service namespace vs Session). - The author demonstrates remote creation/modification of collectors via `IDataCollectorSet` + `Commit()` (with server parameter for remote targets) and highlights XML-templated collector creation (`CreateDataCollectorFromXml`) as a practical approach for trace collectors. - A PoC tool, JonMon-Lite, is presented to create remote trace sessions from XML/JSON config, write ETL output to a chosen location (including “remote host collects, local host stores”), and parse/forward events into local Event Viewer for analysis. - Defensive examples include detecting .NET assembly loads, Permanent WMI Event Subscriptions, and DCSync activity via relevant ETW providers (e.g., DotNETRuntime, WMI-Activity, RPC). - Offensive implications include stealthy remote recon/telemetry collection, hiding sessions in non-default namespaces, and remotely stopping/modifying security trace sessions (e.g., changing providers or security descriptors). - Useful for detection engineers and defenders seeking visibility without endpoint agents, and for red teams researching stealthy monitoring and ETW/PLA abuse paths.
Technical Focus: - ETW remote collection via PLA (pla.dll) DCOM interfaces - IDataCollectorSet / IDataCollectorSetCollection / IDataCollector COM API usage - Data collector namespaces (Session, Service, AutoLogger, etc.) and enumeration gaps - Remote creation/modification of trace sessions using `Commit()` and `CommitMode` - XML-templated trace collector provisioning (`CreateDataCollectorFromXml`) - Authentication/credentialing for remote collection and remote/local ETL storage
Use Cases: - Agentless remote telemetry collection on servers/endpoints where EDR agents are undesirable - Distributed ETW-based detection pipeline (ETL collection + near-real-time parsing/forwarding) - Remote hunting for suspicious activity using targeted ETW providers (RPC, WMI-Activity, .NET runtime) - Red-team stealth monitoring/recon without dropping binaries to disk - Auditing and hardening: detect/alert on unexpected PLA collector creation or trace session tampering
Keywords: ETW, Performance Logs and Alerts, PLA, pla.dll, DCOM, COM, IDataCollectorSet, IDataCollectorSetCollection, IDataCollector, ITraceDataCollector, IConfigurationDataCollector, logman, PerfMon, Data Collector Set, namespace, AutoLogger, ETL, CommitMode, CreateDataCollectorFromXml, Microsoft-Windows-DotNETRuntime, Microsoft-Windows-WMI-Activity, Microsoft-Windows-RPC, DCSync, Mimikatz, Rubeus

entry.title: doing what edrs wont
entry.summary:
Title: Blackbird: Doing What EDRs Won’t (Kernel Hook Engine)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post explains the design and hardening of Blackbird’s Windows kernel hook engine, built to provide syscall-level telemetry and control that most commercial EDRs avoid due to stability risk. It focuses on reliably inline-hooking selected `ntoskrnl.exe` `Nt*` routines (memory, section, and process inspection APIs) while preserving exact execution semantics to prevent crashes. The author details real failure modes encountered (truncated prologues, register-clobbering jump stubs, unsafe rollback) and the engineering changes that made hooks safe across SMP systems. Key implementation points include descriptor-driven hook definitions, trampoline construction in `NonPagedPoolExecute`, MDL-based remapping to write read-only kernel text, and cross-core synchronization via IPIs plus serializing instructions for self-modifying code correctness. Hook execution is gated (armed state, monitored PIDs, IRQL checks) and emits ETW telemetry with careful unload/rundown handling. It’s most useful for malware analysts, kernel developers, and red team/maldev researchers who need deep visibility or controlled kernel instrumentation, and for defenders studying how kernel-level monitoring can be implemented (or attacked) safely.
Technical Focus: - Windows kernel inline hooking of `ntoskrnl.exe` `Nt*` routines - Trampoline building and prologue “stolen bytes” correctness - MDL-based writable mappings of read-only kernel code (`IoAllocateMdl`, `MmMapLockedPagesSpecifyCache`, `MmProtectMdlSystemAddress`) - SMP-safe patching with IPIs (`KeIpiGenericCall`) and serialization (`KeMemoryBarrier`, `CPUID`) - Hook lifecycle safety: verification, rollback, rundown protection, in-flight tracking - ETW-based telemetry emission from kernel hooks
Use Cases: - Kernel-level telemetry for memory allocation/protection, section mapping, and process inspection syscalls - Building robust research instrumentation that avoids BSODs during inline patching - Studying EDR/anti-cheat style kernel monitoring tradeoffs and implementation pitfalls - Developing or evaluating syscall monitoring/anti-evasion techniques in controlled environments - Creating targeted per-process syscall tracing with minimal system-wide overhead
Keywords: Blackbird, Windows kernel, ntoskrnl.exe, kernel hooking, inline hook, trampoline, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, NtWriteVirtualMemory, NtProtectVirtualMemory, NtCreateSection, NtMapViewOfSection, NtQuerySystemInformation, ETW, MDL, IoAllocateMdl, MmMapLockedPagesSpecifyCache, KeIpiGenericCall, SMP, instruction cache coherency, PatchGuard (KPP), NonPagedPoolExecute, WinDbg, IRQL

entry.title: EDRChoker
entry.summary:
Title: EDRChoker
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): EDRChoker is a Windows tool that abuses Policy-based Quality of Service (QoS) to apply hard bandwidth caps to specific EDR/AV agent processes, forcing their telemetry/network communications to time out. It leverages Windows’ built-in QoS infrastructure backed by the `pacer.sys` driver, so the throttling rules take effect immediately and persist across reboots. The operator supplies a list of target process names to create QoS policies, or runs the tool without arguments to remove installed policies. The project is positioned as a defense-bypass technique by “choking” the telemetry stream rather than killing or tampering with the agent. It’s primarily useful for red teams/pentesters evaluating EDR resilience and for blue teams to understand and detect policy-based network interference against security tooling. It’s interesting because it uses legitimate OS networking policy controls to degrade EDR visibility with minimal direct interaction with the EDR process.
Technical Focus: - Windows Policy-based QoS (QoS Policy) manipulation - Bandwidth throttling to induce telemetry timeouts - `pacer.sys` (QoS Packet Scheduler) dependency - Persistence via system policy rules across reboot - Process-name–based targeting of network restrictions
Use Cases: - Degrade/disable EDR cloud connectivity without terminating the agent - Test SOC/EDR detection of QoS policy tampering and telemetry loss scenarios - Simulate adversary tradecraft that relies on “living off the land” network controls - Incident response validation: identify and remove malicious QoS policies
Keywords: Windows QoS, Policy-based QoS, pacer.sys, QoS Packet Scheduler, bandwidth throttling, telemetry disruption, EDR bypass, network timeout, persistent policy, process name targeting, Windows networking, endpoint agent connectivity, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, Elastic Defend, Cortex XDR, Tanium, Trend Micro Deep Security, HarfangLab, defensive evasion, living-off-the-land (LOTL)

entry.title: EDRUnChoker
entry.summary:
Title: EDRUnChoker — Fileless WMI remediation for EDRChoker QoS throttling
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): EDRUnChoker is a defensive PowerShell toolkit that mitigates EDRChoker-style abuse of Windows QoS (via `pacer.sys`) used to throttle EDR agent network telemetry to near-zero bandwidth. It deploys a fileless WMI permanent event subscription in `root\subscription` that runs on a 5-second timer and executes embedded VBScript to enumerate and remove malicious QoS policies. The key implementation detail is using WMI with `WbemContext` `PolicyStore` to correctly enumerate policies in both `ActiveStore` and `GPO:localhost`, since plain WMI `ExecQuery` can miss ActiveStore policies created by `New-NetQosPolicy`/EDRChoker. It targets throttles based on known security product app paths and/or aggressive bandwidth limits (≤ 1 Mbps). The tool also emits Application log events under provider `EDRChokerDefense` for SOC visibility and recommends Sysmon monitoring for tampering with the WMI subscription objects. Useful primarily for Blue Teams/IR and endpoint engineering teams responding to or hardening against telemetry-throttling attacks.
Technical Focus: - Windows QoS policy abuse and remediation (`New-NetQosPolicy`, `pacer.sys`) - WMI permanent event subscriptions (`root\subscription`, timers, consumers) - WMI enumeration with `WbemContext` and `PolicyStore` (ActiveStore vs GPO stores) - Fileless persistence/automation via `ActiveScriptEventConsumer` (VBScript) - Windows Event Log instrumentation for remediation evidence - Sysmon detections for WMI subscription tampering (Event IDs 19/20/21)
Use Cases: - Detect and remove malicious QoS throttling policies targeting EDR/AV agents - Deploy host-based self-healing remediation against EDRChoker-like techniques - Provide SOC telemetry (Application log) for incident correlation and proof of cleanup - Baseline and monitor WMI subscription objects to detect attacker defense evasion - Validate QoS policy state across ActiveStore and local GPO policy stores
Keywords: EDRChoker, EDRUnChoker, QoS, pacer.sys, New-NetQosPolicy, Get-NetQosPolicy, Windows Filtering Platform, WMI, root\\subscription, permanent event subscription, __EventFilter, ActiveScriptEventConsumer, VBScript, WbemContext, PolicyStore, ActiveStore, GPO:localhost, Application event log, Sysmon 19, Sysmon 20, Sysmon 21

entry.title: msmpeng svchost without flags
entry.summary:
Title: Why Does MsMpEng Spawn svchost.exe Without Flags?
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This write-up reverse engineers why Microsoft Defender’s MsMpEng.exe sometimes spawns `svchost.exe` with no typical service-host flags (i.e., not a normal SCM-launched service host). - It traces execution from `MsMpEng.exe` (largely a bootstrapper) into `mpsvc.dll`, and ultimately into `mpengine.dll`, where the anti-rootkit scanning path creates a suspended `svchost.exe` via `CreateProcessW` with a minimal command line (`"<SystemDirectory>\svchost.exe"`). - The spawned `svchost.exe` is registered with Defender’s KSL driver (`ksld.sys`) using an IOCTL dispatcher (`0x222044`, operation `8`) that stores a referenced process object for later use. - When the engine requests physical memory reads (operation `1`), `ksld.sys` attaches to the helper process context and maps `\Device\PhysicalMemory` to copy bytes back to the engine for analysis. - The key point is that this “weird” `svchost.exe` is a helper container for kernel-assisted physical memory mapping, not a service host instance. - Useful for DFIR/Blue Teams interpreting telemetry, and for red teamers/malware analysts who need to avoid misattributing this behavior to process injection or service abuse.
Technical Focus: - Microsoft Defender architecture (MsMpEng.exe, mpsvc.dll, mpengine.dll) - Anti-rootkit scanning workflow and helper process creation - KSL driver (`ksld.sys`) IOCTL interface (`0x222044`) and operation dispatch - Suspended process creation (`CreateProcessW` + `CREATE_SUSPENDED`) - Kernel process attachment and physical memory mapping (`KeStackAttachProcess`, `\Device\PhysicalMemory`) - WDF driver request handling and process object referencing (`ZwOpenProcess`, `ObReferenceObjectByHandle`)
Use Cases: - Triage/explain “svchost without flags” telemetry where parent is `MsMpEng.exe` - Build detections that distinguish Defender anti-rootkit helper behavior from suspicious svchost spawning - Validate EDR process-tree heuristics against legitimate Defender internals - Reverse engineer Defender/KSL interactions for research and threat emulation baselining
Keywords: Microsoft Defender, MsMpEng.exe, svchost.exe, mpsvc.dll, mpengine.dll, anti-rootkit, KSL, ksld.sys, DeviceIoControl, IOCTL 0x222044, CREATE_SUSPENDED, GetSystemDirectoryW, ZwOpenProcess, ObReferenceObjectByHandle, KeStackAttachProcess, \Device\PhysicalMemory, ZwMapViewOfSection, WDF, Windows internals, reverse engineering

entry.title: KernelToUserInjector
entry.summary:
Title: EVENSTAR – KernelToUserInjector (v3.0.0)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository contains sample Windows kernel-mode driver code that injects code into a user-mode process using Asynchronous Procedure Calls (APCs). - It focuses on performing kernel-to-user injection by locating a target process/thread (including a thread in an alertable wait state), mapping/providing a user virtual address (UVA) payload buffer, and queueing both a special kernel APC and a regular user-mode APC. - The project explicitly targets modern Windows (tested on Windows 11 25H2) and is updated to evade additional ETW “Threat Intelligence” sensors related to kernel APC injection telemetry. - It is primarily useful for red team operators, malware developers, and security researchers studying kernel-assisted injection and EDR/ETW detection surfaces. - It’s interesting because it demonstrates a kernel-originated injection path and references known work on bypassing Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence APC injection sensors.
Technical Focus: - Windows kernel-mode driver development (C, CPL0) - APC internals (special kernel APC vs user-mode APC) - Kernel-to-user code injection workflow (PID/TID targeting, alertable threads) - ETW / Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence sensor evasion considerations - Windows 11 injection/detection behavior validation
Use Cases: - Implementing and testing kernel-assisted user-mode payload execution via APCs - Researching/replicating ETW Threat Intelligence detections for APC injection - Building lab PoCs to evaluate EDR visibility into kernel-originated injection - Studying thread state requirements (alertable waits) for user APC delivery
Keywords: Windows kernel driver, kernel-to-user injection, APC injection, Asynchronous Procedure Call, special kernel APC, user-mode APC, KeInsertQueueApc, alertable wait, PID, TID, user virtual address, ETW, Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence, EDR evasion, Windows 11, Defender for Endpoint, telemetry bypass, Blackbone, KernelInjector

entry.title: striga.html
entry.summary:
Title: Striga: Lifting x86 to LLVM IR with Python
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post introduces Striga, a small Python-based x86_64 lifter that translates machine instructions into LLVM IR to make analysis and transformation easier than working directly with assembly. - It models CPU state as a `State` struct in memory (GPRs, flags, etc.) plus an opaque `memory` pointer for RAM, relying on LLVM optimizations (e.g., mem2reg, DSE) to recover SSA-like behavior. - The author explains the lifter architecture (one LLVM basic block per instruction), semantic handlers per mnemonic, and how operands/registers/subregisters and flags are implemented, including undefined-flag modeling via custom intrinsics. - Control-flow recovery is demonstrated with a BFS worklist over successor edges, handling direct branches cleanly while deferring indirect control flow to user-provided hooks (`__striga_jmp/call/ret`). - A key section covers “brightening”: wrapping lifted code into a conventional function signature, inlining/optimizing, and rewriting RAM-rooted GEPs into `inttoptr` to regain normal pointer semantics. - It’s useful for reverse engineers and research-focused red teamers/malware analysts building deobfuscation/devirtualization pipelines or experimenting with LLVM-based program analysis without heavy C++ build friction.
Technical Focus: - x86_64 instruction lifting to LLVM IR - CPU state modeling (State struct), flags semantics, subregister handling - LLVM IR construction (basic blocks per instruction, terminators, verification) - Custom intrinsics for indirect control flow and undefined behavior - CFG recovery via BFS over lifted successors - Brightening + LLVM optimization pipeline (inlining, mem2reg, GEP-to-inttoptr rewrite, TBAA)
Use Cases: - Rapid prototyping of LLVM-based deobfuscation/devirtualization workflows - Building analysis tooling (slicing, dataflow, symbolic-ish reasoning) on lifted IR - Experimenting with instruction semantics and flag modeling for emulation-resistant code - Generating recompilable/optimizable IR from small x86_64 code regions - Research/education: understanding lifter design tradeoffs vs Remill/angr/VEX/Sleigh
Keywords: Striga, LLVM IR, x86_64, lifter, binary lifting, Python, Capstone, SSA, mem2reg, TBAA metadata, getelementptr, inttoptr, intrinsic, __striga_jmp, __striga_call, __striga_ret, control-flow graph, BFS worklist, flag semantics, subregisters, devirtualization, deobfuscation, Remill

entry.title: MogVMP
entry.summary:
Title: MogVMP — Static devirtualizer for VMProtect 3.0–3.5 (32-bit) using Remill/LLVM
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): MogVMP is a proof-of-concept static devirtualizer targeting 32-bit binaries protected with VMProtect 3.0–3.5. It lifts the x86 handler code to LLVM IR via Remill, then relies on LLVM optimization passes plus a custom aliasing-aware constant propagation/store-forwarding pass to eliminate junk code and the VM virtualization layer. CFG recovery is designed to be fully static (no opcode traces required): it incrementally lifts from VMENTER, and when the next handler address doesn’t constant-fold, it treats it as a branch and forks the lifting process. It operates on unpacked binaries and focuses only on VMProtect’s virtualization (not unpacking or full deobfuscation). Multi-VM functions (e.g., external API calls) require supplying additional VMENTER addresses via the CLI, and output is intended to be readable LLVM IR rather than a turnkey recompilation pipeline. Useful primarily for reverse engineers/malware analysts and researchers working on devirtualization and VMProtect internals.
Technical Focus: - VMProtect 3.x virtualization devirtualization (x86 32-bit) - Remill-based instruction lifting to LLVM IR - LLVM optimization-driven removal of VM/junk layers - Static CFG recovery via incremental handler lifting and branch forking - Aliasing-aware constant propagation and store forwarding over allocas - VMENTER discovery (optional Intel PIN tracer)
Use Cases: - Recover higher-level semantics/IR from VMProtect-virtualized functions for analysis - Build and test static devirtualization workflows without dynamic opcode tracing - Generate readable LLVM IR to aid manual reversing and patching - Research/benchmark devirtualization techniques against VMProtect 3.0–3.5 - Assist malware triage where VMProtect virtualization blocks static analysis
Keywords: VMProtect, devirtualization, VMENTER, opcode handlers, x86 32-bit, PE, Remill, LLVM IR, lifting, CFG recovery, static analysis, constant propagation, store forwarding, alias analysis, junk code elimination, Intel PIN, tracer, virtualization layer, reverse engineering, obfuscation

entry.title: 0x48
entry.summary:
Title: Writing Self‑Mutating Malware: Building Polymorphic (Veil64) and Metamorphic (Morpheus) Engines on Linux x86_64
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post explains how self-mutating malware defeats signature-based detection by generating functionally equivalent but bytewise-different code across executions/infections. - It contrasts oligomorphic, polymorphic (mutating decryptor stubs/engines), and metamorphic (rewriting the whole body without relying on encryption) approaches, with historical context and practical implementation notes. - The author presents two concrete implementations: **Veil64**, a polymorphic x64 Linux code generator that emits many variants of a decryptor + stub (mmap/syscall setup, RIP-relative addressing, runtime keying), and **Morpheus**, an ELF infector that mutates its own code by scanning/replacing predefined “junk” patterns and rewriting targets on disk. - Techniques covered include register randomization, multiple equivalent decrypt algorithms, structured garbage insertion, control-flow disruption via random jumps, entropy collection (RDTSC/RSP/RIP), and on-the-fly machine-code emission (ModRM encoding). - It’s most useful for malware researchers, red teamers studying AV/EDR evasion mechanics, and defenders wanting to understand mutation-driven signature breakage and analysis friction. - Interesting because it provides low-level assembly examples showing how polymorphic engines are built byte-by-byte and how a simple metamorphic loop can be implemented without full disassembly.
Technical Focus: - Polymorphic decryptor generation (register renaming, algorithm variants, stub/engine layering) - Runtime entropy & key derivation (RDTSC, stack pointer, RIP/ASLR effects) - Dynamic machine-code emission (opcode/ModRM construction, RIP-relative addressing) - Garbage/junk code injection and control-flow obfuscation (random jumps, dummy blocks) - Linux x86_64 syscall-only loaders (mmap/munmap, getdents64, execve, fork/wait) - ELF infection mechanics and on-disk rewriting with backup markers (.morph8)
Use Cases: - Build/test polymorphic loaders to evaluate signature resilience of static scanners - Create mutation-heavy samples for reverse-engineering training and tooling validation - Study detection opportunities around runtime decryption, entropy sources, and syscall patterns - Prototype metamorphic “rewrite-on-execution” concepts for research (pattern-based mutation) - Develop YARA/heuristics focusing on behavioral invariants vs. byte signatures
Keywords: polymorphic engine, metamorphic malware, oligomorphic, self-mutating code, decryptor stub, runtime decryption, x86_64 assembly, Linux syscalls, mmap, munmap, getdents64, execve, fork, RDTSC, ASLR, RIP-relative addressing, ModRM encoding, register renaming, junk code injection, control-flow obfuscation, ELF infector, signature evasion, XorShift PRNG, XOR string encryption

entry.title: obfuscation vs the optimizer an llvm middle end arms race.html
entry.summary:
Title: Obfuscation vs the Optimizer: An LLVM Middle-End Arms Race
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post examines the inherent conflict between code obfuscation (making semantics hard to recover) and compiler optimization (making code simpler/faster), focusing on LLVM’s middle-end. - Using a small “mystery” function, it demonstrates how LLVM optimization passes quickly collapse naive arithmetic/bitwise obfuscation into a constant return value via constant folding and CSE. - It then upgrades the obfuscation into a Mixed Boolean-Arithmetic (MBA) style expression by mixing in an input variable, initially resisting LLVM 18’s pipeline. - A version change to LLVM 19 breaks the obfuscation: a small InstCombine commit enabling De Morgan–style inversion propagation allows the optimizer to normalize the expression and fold it to `x - x = 0`. - The post highlights how obfuscation techniques can be brittle against evolving compiler algebraic reasoning, and why understanding optimization passes benefits both obfuscator authors and reverse engineers/deobfuscator builders.
Technical Focus: - LLVM compilation stages (front-end / middle-end / back-end) and LLVM IR - Optimization passes: InstCombine, EarlyCSE, Reassociate, GVN, BDCE, SimplifyCFG - Constant propagation/folding and common subexpression elimination - Boolean algebra canonicalization (De Morgan’s law, NOT-pushing/inversion) - Mixed Boolean-Arithmetic (MBA) constant obfuscation - Tooling workflow: `clang`, `opt`, `llc`, Compiler Explorer pipelines
Use Cases: - Evaluate whether an LLVM-based obfuscation survives real optimization pipelines - Build more robust obfuscation passes by anticipating optimizer canonical forms - Create deobfuscation strategies by reusing compiler-style simplification logic - Diff LLVM versions/commits to understand why protected code suddenly deobfuscates - Improve reverse-engineering workflows by leveraging “const-ness”/value-range reasoning
Keywords: LLVM, Clang, LLVM IR, middle-end, optimizer, opt, InstCombine, EarlyCSE, Reassociate, GVN, BDCE, constant folding, constant propagation, common subexpression elimination, De Morgan’s law, boolean algebra, MBA, mixed boolean arithmetic, obfuscation, deobfuscation, reverse engineering, Compiler Explorer

entry.title: Beatrice.py
entry.summary:
Title: Beatrice.py — x64 opcode patching for YARA/AV signature evasion
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Beatrice.py is a Python tool that rewrites x64 machine code in existing binaries by swapping instructions with same-size alternative opcode encodings to change byte patterns without changing functionality. Its goal is to bypass static detections such as YARA rules, byte-signature scanners, and some memory scanners by altering the exact sequences those rules match. It focuses on strictly matching machine-code regions (not arbitrary data) to reduce the chance of corrupting complex PE files that contain embedded strings/data. The README notes it does not change imports/API usage or fully defeat behavior-based detection, and some binaries may still break (a “safe mode” exists). It’s primarily useful for red teamers/pentesters and malware/tooling developers who need to mutate payload/tool binaries to test or evade signature-based detections, and for defenders to understand how brittle byte-pattern signatures can be.
Technical Focus: - x86-64 instruction substitution and alternative opcode encodings - Static signature evasion (YARA byte patterns) - Binary patching while preserving semantics and instruction size - Handling immediates/edge-case instructions for pattern generation - PE/executable mutation constraints and breakage modes (e.g., Go/garble)
Use Cases: - Mutate known offensive tooling binaries to evade YARA/AV byte signatures - Generate variant samples for testing detection robustness and rule quality - Pre-processing stage before loaders/packers/shellcode embedding - Researching which instruction sequences are commonly signatured and how to alter them safely
Keywords: Beatrice.py, YARA, signature evasion, static detection, x64, x86-64, opcode substitution, instruction encoding, binary patching, machine code, PE executable, shellcode, Windows Defender, Elastic YARA rules, DefenderCheck, Import Address Table, IAT obfuscation, Metasploit, Mimikatz, Sliver, Havoc, Cobalt Strike, Donut

entry.title: DSCourier
entry.summary:
Title: DSCourier: Abusing WinGet Configuration COM API for Winget-less DSC/PowerShell Execution
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post describes a technique and tool (“DSCourier”) that abuses WinGet’s configuration (DSC) capability to achieve arbitrary code execution without launching `winget.exe`, `powershell.exe`, or `cmd.exe` in the initiating process tree. Instead of using `winget configure`, it directly invokes the WinGet Configuration engine via the `Microsoft.Management.Configuration` WinRT/COM API, causing Microsoft-signed components (`WindowsPackageManagerServer.exe` and `ConfigurationRemotingServer.exe`) to execute DSC resources (including `PSDscResources/Script`). The approach reduces common telemetry artifacts (no `winget.exe` command line with YAML path/URL, no obvious shell parentage) while still executing attacker-controlled PowerShell embedded in a DSC YAML. It includes requirements (WinGet full package with configuration support, WinGet v1.6.2631+, modern Windows builds), outlines the COM call chain, and provides detection ideas focusing on child processes spawned by `ConfigurationRemotingServer.exe`. It’s useful for red teams/pentesters exploring LOLBIN-style execution paths and for defenders updating detections beyond `winget.exe` process creation.
Technical Focus: - WinGet Configuration / DSC YAML abuse (`PSDscResources/Script`) - WinRT/COM activation of `Microsoft.Management.Configuration` (ConfigurationStaticFunctions) - Process chain shifting to Microsoft-signed hosts (`WindowsPackageManagerServer.exe`, `ConfigurationRemotingServer.exe`) - .NET interop via CsWinRT projections and `.winmd` metadata - Detection engineering for anomalous child processes from configuration hosts
Use Cases: - Execute PowerShell payloads via DSC without spawning `winget.exe` or PowerShell host binaries - Build stealthier execution chains that blend into legitimate WinGet COM consumer behavior - Purple-team validation of detections around `WindowsPackageManagerServer.exe` / `ConfigurationRemotingServer.exe` - Hardening guidance validation (GPO disabling App Installer or WinGet Configuration)
Keywords: WinGet, Windows Package Manager, winget configure, DSC, Desired State Configuration, PSDscResources/Script, YAML, Microsoft.Management.Configuration, WinRT, COM, CoCreateInstance, CsWinRT, .winmd, WindowsPackageManagerServer.exe, ConfigurationRemotingServer.exe, LOLBIN, process tree evasion, GPO Desktop App Installer, WDAC, AppLocker

entry.title: DSCourier
entry.summary:
Title: DSCourier — Applying DSC via WinGet Configuration COM API using Microsoft-signed binaries
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): DSCourier is a proof-of-concept that leverages the WinGet Configuration COM API to apply PowerShell Desired State Configuration (DSC) on a target through Microsoft-signed components. The goal is to execute configuration-driven actions (via `winget configure`/Microsoft.Management.Configuration) in a way that can reduce scrutiny compared to custom tooling, effectively “living off the land” with trusted binaries/APIs. It focuses on operationalizing DSC configuration application by calling into WinGet via COM from a .NET (C#) wrapper and supporting PowerShell assets. The author positions it as research-grade code intended for operators to modify and extend with custom configuration files. The technique is notable because it reportedly bypassed multiple EDR products (CrowdStrike Falcon, MDE, Elastic) in testing. It’s primarily useful for red teams/pentesters researching signed-binary execution paths and defenders looking to understand and detect WinGet/DSC abuse.
Technical Focus: - WinGet Configuration feature (`winget configure`) and feature-flag enablement - COM automation via Microsoft.Management.Configuration (WinGet Configuration COM API) - PowerShell Desired State Configuration (DSC) execution flow and dependencies (PSDscResources) - Living-off-the-land via Microsoft-signed binaries/components - .NET 8 / Windows SDK interop for COM-based invocation
Use Cases: - Execute/abuse DSC configurations on endpoints using WinGet’s configuration pipeline - Prototype EDR evasion research around trusted/signed configuration engines - Build custom operator workflows by authoring/modifying DSC configuration files - Defensive testing: create detections for WinGet Configuration COM usage and DSC resource execution - Validate enterprise hardening controls around WinGet installation and configuration enablement
Keywords: DSCourier, WinGet, winget configure, WinGet Configuration, COM API, Microsoft.Management.Configuration, DSC, Desired State Configuration, PowerShell, PSDscResources, LOLBIN, signed binaries, .NET 8, Windows SDK 10.0.22621, EDR bypass, CrowdStrike Falcon, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, Elastic Security, configuration-as-code, Windows 10, Windows 11, Server 2025

entry.title: LainAmsiOpenSession
entry.summary:
Title: LainAmsiOpenSession — Custom AMSI bypass by patching AmsiOpenSession in amsi.dll
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository provides a PowerShell-based AMSI bypass that patches the `AmsiOpenSession` function inside `amsi.dll` in-memory. - The goal is to prevent or disrupt AMSI session initialization so subsequent script/content scanning is impaired, enabling execution of PowerShell payloads that would otherwise be inspected. - It is derived from/related to prior public techniques (Sam Rothlisberger’s AMSI bypass) and takes inspiration from “Nuke-Amsi,” indicating a focus on direct function patching rather than higher-level flag toggles. - The technique centers on runtime modification of a Windows security component (AMSI) via API resolution and memory patching. - It is primarily useful for red team operators and pentesters performing Windows post-exploitation where AMSI blocks offensive PowerShell. - It’s interesting because it targets a less commonly patched AMSI function (`AmsiOpenSession`) and demonstrates a practical in-process patching approach.
Technical Focus: - AMSI internals (`amsi.dll`) and session lifecycle (`AmsiOpenSession`) - In-memory function patching / byte patching - PowerShell-based native API invocation (P/Invoke / reflection patterns) - Runtime DLL function resolution (e.g., `GetProcAddress`-style logic) - Memory protection changes (e.g., `VirtualProtect`) for patching
Use Cases: - Bypassing AMSI scanning during PowerShell-based post-exploitation - Running known-signature PowerShell tooling in constrained/monitored environments - Red team tradecraft experimentation with alternative AMSI patch points - Testing blue-team detections for in-memory tampering of `amsi.dll`
Keywords: AMSI, amsi.dll, AmsiOpenSession, PowerShell, in-memory patching, function patch, VirtualProtect, GetProcAddress, runtime hooking, EDR evasion, script scanning, Windows internals, post-exploitation, offensive PowerShell, Nuke-Amsi, Sam Rothlisberger, memory protection, API patching

entry.title: Invoke NetLoader
entry.summary:

entry.title: PositiveIntent
entry.summary:
Title: PositiveIntent
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - PositiveIntent is an evasive loader/builder for .NET Framework assemblies (e.g., Rubeus) that produces an obfuscated loader EXE embedding an encrypted target assembly as resources. - It aims to reduce EDR/AV visibility by avoiding common patching patterns and signatured console/CLI artifacts while still executing managed tooling. - The loader implements “patchless” AMSI and ETW bypass using hardware breakpoints with VEH² (Vectored Exception Handling) rather than SetThreadContext or in-memory patching. - It uses a Mono.Cecil-based obfuscation pipeline on both the payload assembly and the loader, RC4 encrypts the payload, reconstructs it at runtime, and stomps PE headers after CLR load to hinder memory scanners. - It supports optional encrypted output-to-file and hardcoded arguments to avoid signatured strings on the command line/console, plus hostname keying and entropy shaping via benign embedded resources. - Primarily useful for red teams and pentesters running .NET post-exploitation tooling in monitored Windows environments; also relevant to blue teams for understanding modern loader tradecraft and detection gaps.
Technical Focus: - Patchless AMSI bypass via hardware breakpoints + VEH² - ETW bypass without memory patching - .NET assembly obfuscation with Mono.Cecil - Encrypted resource-embedded payload delivery (RC4, chunked resources) - In-memory execution and PE header stomping - D/Invoke usage (no P/Invoke), entropy management
Use Cases: - Build an evasive wrapper/loader for known .NET offensive tools (e.g., Kerberos/AD tooling) - Reduce detection from AMSI/ETW telemetry during managed code execution - Avoid signatured console output by redirecting to encrypted logs for later offline decryption - Execute .NET tooling with hardcoded arguments to minimize suspicious CLI artifacts - Research/detection engineering around VEH-based bypasses, resource-staged payloads, and PE header stomping
Keywords: AMSI, ETW, VEH, VEH², hardware breakpoints, patchless bypass, .NET Framework 4.5.1, CLR, Mono.Cecil, obfuscation, D/Invoke, P/Invoke avoidance, RC4, resource embedding, in-memory execution, PE header stomping, entropy shaping, hostname keying, reflective loading, Rubeus

entry.title: patchless amsi bypass hwbp
entry.summary:
Title: Patchless AMSI Bypass via Hardware Breakpoints — Implementation Notes
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Describes a “patchless” AMSI bypass that avoids modifying `amsi.dll` by using x64 hardware breakpoints (DR0) on `amsi!AmsiScanBuffer` and a vectored exception handler (VEH) to intercept execution. - The VEH fires on `STATUS_SINGLE_STEP`, rewrites the caller-provided `AMSI_RESULT*` (6th argument at `[RSP+0x30]`) to `AMSI_RESULT_CLEAN`, sets `RAX = S_OK`, and redirects `RIP` to the caller’s return address to skip the function body entirely. - It focuses on implementation details that matter in practice: MS x64 calling convention stack layout, clearing `DR6`, setting the Resume Flag (RF) to avoid re-trigger loops, and probing pointers with `VirtualQuery` to prevent crashes. - The post reports lab validation against a commercial EDR where classic inline AMSI patch detections did not trigger because no `.text` bytes were changed. - It also outlines defender detection ideas: enumerating VEH chains, polling debug registers per thread, monitoring `NtSetContextThread`/context writes, and stack-walk inconsistencies. - Useful for red teams/pentesters building in-memory execution pipelines, and for blue teams designing telemetry to catch HWBP/VEH-based tampering.
Technical Focus: - AMSI internals (`AmsiScanBuffer`, `AMSI_RESULT`, provider model) - x64 hardware debug registers (DR0–DR7), execute breakpoints, `STATUS_SINGLE_STEP` - Vectored Exception Handling (VEH) and `CONTEXT` manipulation (`RIP/RSP/RAX/EFLAGS`) - MS x64 calling convention and stack argument offsets (`[RSP+0x30]`) - EDR evasion tradeoffs and detection strategies (VEH chain, DR polling, context-write monitoring)
Use Cases: - Bypass AMSI scanning for in-memory PowerShell/.NET payload execution without patching `amsi.dll` - Build “patchless” user-mode tamper primitives that evade simple memory-integrity signatures - Combine with ETW suppression via similar HWBP/VEH patterns (e.g., `EtwEventWrite`/`NtTraceControl`) - Defensive engineering: implement detections for VEH abuse and suspicious debug-register targeting
Keywords: AMSI, amsi.dll, AmsiScanBuffer, AMSI_RESULT_CLEAN, hardware breakpoints, HWBP, DR0, DR6, DR7, STATUS_SINGLE_STEP, vectored exception handler, AddVectoredExceptionHandler, CONTEXT, SetThreadContext, GetThreadContext, EFLAGS Resume Flag, MS x64 calling convention, NtSetContextThread, ETW, EtwEventWrite

entry.title: ghosting amsi cutting rpc to disarm av 04c26d67bb80
entry.summary:
Title: Ghosting AMSI: Cutting RPC to disarm AV
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post describes an AMSI bypass that targets the RPC layer AMSI uses to communicate with antimalware providers, rather than patching `amsi.dll` directly. - The technique hooks/patches `rpcrt4.dll!NdrClientCall3` (the RPC marshaling stub used by auto-generated AMSI RPC calls) to short-circuit scan requests before they are serialized and sent to the AV engine. - By forcing controlled return values (e.g., `S_OK` plus an error like `0x80070002`), it triggers AMSI’s fallback path (`ScanStatus = 2`), which results in `AMSI_RESULT_NOT_DETECTED` without a real scan. - A PowerShell PoC demonstrates in-memory patching with a trampoline (`mov rax, addr; jmp rax`) and discusses why this can be stealthier than common `AmsiScanBuffer`/`amsiInitFailed` bypasses that are heavily monitored. - It’s primarily useful for red teamers and offensive researchers studying AMSI internals and bypass surfaces, and for blue teamers looking to detect RPC-layer tampering and anomalous AMSI telemetry. - The interesting angle is exploiting AMSI’s “provider unavailable” resilience logic by breaking the communication bridge (RPC) while keeping the apparent AMSI call flow intact.
Technical Focus: - AMSI architecture and provider communication model (COM/RPC) - RPC runtime internals: `rpcrt4.dll!NdrClientCall3` and stub-based calls - In-memory patching/trampoline hooks (x64 `mov rax; jmp rax`) - AMSI fallback behavior and telemetry (`ScanStatus`, HRESULTs) - PowerShell + P/Invoke for `VirtualProtect`, `VirtualAlloc`, `FlushInstructionCache` - Evasion tradeoffs vs direct `amsi.dll` patching and integrity monitoring
Use Cases: - Bypassing AMSI content scanning for PowerShell payload execution in controlled red team ops - Researching AMSI’s RPC dependency and identifying additional choke points for interception - Building detections for RPC function patching and AMSI fallback-path anomalies (e.g., `ScanStatus=2`) - Validating EDR/AV resilience against user-mode memory patching of `rpcrt4.dll`
Keywords: AMSI, AmsiScanBuffer, rpcrt4.dll, NdrClientCall3, RPC, COM, trampoline hook, in-memory patching, VirtualProtect, VirtualAlloc, FlushInstructionCache, PowerShell, P/Invoke, HRESULT, ERROR_FILE_NOT_FOUND, 0x80070002, ERROR_NOT_READY, 0x80070015, AMSI ScanStatus, ETW telemetry, Windows Defender

entry.title: NEBULA
entry.summary:
Title: NEBULA (Nefarious Execution & Behavioral Unit for LOLBAS Attacks)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): NEBULA is an interactive, menu-driven PowerShell TUI framework for atomically testing Windows execution, persistence, WMI, COM-object, and LOLBAS techniques in a controlled environment. It helps security teams quickly exercise common “living off the land” and native Windows tradecraft to validate detections, telemetry coverage, and response playbooks. It tracks each executed test with timestamps, technique metadata, status (SUCCESS/FAILED/ERROR/DRY-RUN), and output for later review/export. It ships with benign example payloads (often calc.exe) derived from Atomic Red Team and mapped to MITRE ATT&CK technique IDs. Use Cases: - Validate EDR/SIEM detections against common LOLBAS and native execution chains - Reproduce and document Windows tradecraft behaviors for purple-team exercises - Baseline host telemetry (process, command line, WMI, COM) for detection engineering - Regression-test security controls after policy/agent updates (AppLocker/WDAC/EDR) - Train analysts on technique-to-telemetry mapping using safe, benign payloads
Keywords: PowerShell, Windows, TUI, atomic testing, LOLBAS, WMI, COM, persistence, MITRE ATT&CK, Atomic Red Team, regsvr32, mshta, rundll32, msbuild, certutil, bitsadmin, installutil, ExecutionPolicy Bypass, Windows Server 2016+

entry.title: Hecate
entry.summary:
Title: Hecate — Custom WhisperGate-themed Web UI for Mythic C2 (v3)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Hecate is an alternative operator web interface for Mythic C2 v3 that replaces Mythic’s default React UI without requiring any Mythic server modifications. - It connects directly to Mythic’s existing GraphQL and WebSocket subscription APIs and is deployed as a Dockerized HTTPS-only nginx-served frontend. - The UI focuses on operator workflow improvements: live callback management, multi-select tasking with tab completion, snippet library, interactive output rendering (file/process browsers), and fleet-wide beacon health. - It adds visualization and reporting features such as pivot graphs (SOCKS/P2P edges), timeline views, session replay, and MITRE ATT&CK coverage overlays with Navigator JSON export. - Useful primarily for red teams and penetration testers running Mythic who want better operational visibility, tasking ergonomics, and reporting artifacts. - Interesting because it demonstrates a full-featured third-party Mythic UI built purely on the public API surface (GraphQL/subscriptions) and packaged for reproducible deployment.
