Use Cases: - Deploy a custom operator UI for Mythic C2 operations (lab or team server) - Improve multi-callback tasking efficiency (snippets, command palette, batching) - Visualize pivots/tunnels and track lateral movement paths during ops - Generate ATT&CK-mapped activity summaries and export Navigator layers - Review operations via timeline/session replay for debriefs and reporting
Keywords: Mythic C2, GraphQL, WebSocket subscriptions, graphql-ws, Apollo Client, React 19, Vite 6, TypeScript, Zustand, Docker Compose, nginx, HTTPS, self-signed certificate, callback management, tasking UI, SOCKS pivoting, P2P routing, MITRE ATT&CK, ATT&CK Navigator JSON, session replay, timeline visualization

entry.title: CobaltStrike Linux Beacon
entry.summary:
Title: Cobalt Strike Linux Beacon (PoC Custom Implant)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This repository is a proof-of-concept custom “Beacon”-style implant written in C for Linux that communicates with a Cobalt Strike teamserver over HTTP/HTTPS using the default C2 profile. It demonstrates that third-party implants can interoperate with Cobalt Strike as long as they implement the expected protocol/metadata handling, enabling non-Windows platforms and custom behaviors. The implant includes a small command set (filesystem navigation, shell execution, upload/download, sleep/exit), SOCKS proxying, and in-memory execution of Linux BOFs via TrustedSec’s ELFLoader (supporting both TrustedSec and Outflank BOF formats). An Aggressor Script is included to generate payloads and integrate the custom beacon into Cobalt Strike workflows. This is primarily useful for red teams and implant developers exploring custom C2/implant engineering and extending CS beyond its stock payloads. It’s interesting because it provides a practical reference implementation for CS protocol compatibility and Linux BOF execution tradecraft.
Technical Focus: - Cobalt Strike teamserver protocol interoperability (HTTP/HTTPS, default profile) - Custom implant development in C for Linux - Aggressor Script (CNA) integration and payload generation - Linux BOF execution via TrustedSec ELFLoader (and Outflank BOFs) - SOCKS proxy / pivoting capabilities - Basic tasking/command execution and file transfer
Use Cases: - Build and test custom Cobalt Strike-compatible implants for Linux environments - Prototype alternative beacon behaviors (custom commands, obfuscation, platform support) - Execute Linux BOFs in-memory for post-exploitation without dropping full tooling - Establish SOCKS-based pivots through a Linux foothold - Research/detect CS-like traffic patterns by studying a reference implementation
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, Beacon, custom implant, Linux, C, HTTP, HTTPS, teamserver, default C2 profile, Aggressor Script, CNA, payload generation, BOF, Beacon Object File, ELFLoader, TrustedSec, Outflank, nix_bof_template, SOCKS proxy, post-exploitation, file upload, file download

entry.title: sharpcom
entry.summary:
Title: SharpCOM
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): SharpCOM is a C# “weaponized” port of Invoke-DCOM, implementing DCOM-based lateral movement for remote command execution on Windows systems. It builds on the SharpSploit DCOM module work and packages it as a standalone executable suitable for in-memory execution (e.g., Cobalt Strike `execute-assembly`). The tool supports multiple DCOM execution methods (example shown: `ShellWindows`) and targets a specified remote computer name to launch an arbitrary command. It’s primarily useful for red teams and pentesters operating in Windows/AD environments where DCOM is available and remote execution is needed without traditional service creation techniques. The project is interesting because it operationalizes a well-known lateral movement primitive into a practical C# implant-friendly utility.
Technical Focus: - DCOM-based remote code execution (lateral movement) - COM object instantiation and method invocation over the network - Windows RPC/DCOM communication paths - Tradecraft for in-memory .NET execution (e.g., Beacon execute-assembly) - Porting PowerShell tradecraft to C# tooling
Use Cases: - Remote command execution on Windows hosts via DCOM - Lateral movement in AD environments where DCOM is permitted - Post-exploitation execution through Cobalt Strike Beacon `execute-assembly` - Testing and validating DCOM-related detections and hardening controls
Keywords: SharpCOM, Invoke-DCOM, DCOM, COM, lateral movement, remote command execution, Windows RPC, ShellWindows, Cobalt Strike, Beacon, execute-assembly, SharpSploit, .NET, C#, post-exploitation, Windows internals, remote activation, OPSEC, red team tooling

entry.title: Cobalt Strike CheatSheet
entry.summary:
Title: Cobalt Strike CheatSheet
Type: Documentation
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - A curated cheat sheet of operational notes and command references for the Cobalt Strike C2 framework (Beacon). - It helps operators quickly configure infrastructure (listeners, pivot listeners) and execute common post-exploitation workflows without hunting through official docs. - Covers core Beacon commands and modules for execution, in-memory .NET execution, process injection, token manipulation, Kerberos ticket operations, and credential access (e.g., DCSync/Mimikatz integration). - Includes practical guidance for pivoting (SOCKS, CovertVPN), lateral movement (PsExec/WinRM/WMI “jump” and remote-exec), and basic exfil/collection actions (screenshots, keylogging, hashdump). - Emphasizes OPSEC: spawnto/ppid spoofing, avoiding default Malleable C2 behavior, and reducing disk/IOC footprint. - Useful primarily for red teamers and pentesters running Cobalt Strike, and secondarily for blue teams to understand common operator tradecraft and likely telemetry points.
Technical Focus: - Cobalt Strike Beacon command set and workflows - Listener types (HTTP/HTTPS, DNS, SMB, TCP) and pivoting - Malleable C2 profiles (traffic shaping/behavior customization) - Process injection & in-memory execution (execute-assembly, dllinject, shinject) - Credential access & AD abuse (DCSync, token theft, pass-the-hash) - OPSEC controls (spawnto, PPID spoofing, artifact reduction)
Use Cases: - Quick reference during engagements for Beacon commands and syntax - Setting up egress and internal pivot listeners (SMB/TCP/DNS) for segmented networks - Performing lateral movement via jump/remote-exec (PsExec/WinRM/WMI) using stolen/forged tokens - Tuning stealth via Malleable C2, spawnto, and PPID spoofing before post-exploitation - Mapping common CS tradecraft to defensive detections and hunting hypotheses

entry.title: UnCanny
entry.summary:
Title: UnCanny Coerce (UNCanny) — Windows Store InstallService plugin resolution NTLM coercion + LPE experiment
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repo documents a coercion primitive in the Windows Store Install Service (`InstallService.exe` / `InstallService.dll`) where a low-priv user can influence plugin resolution so the SYSTEM service attempts `LoadLibraryW` from a UNC path. - By registering an AppX package “in place” from a UNC share (loose-file registration), the package’s `InstalledLocation.Path` becomes the UNC, and `FulfillmentPluginId` can be set to that package family name to trigger outbound SMB authentication as the machine account (NTLM coercion). - The same path can become an LPE vector if `InstallServicePlugin.dll` is actually present and loadable from the UNC share, resulting in DLL load/execution in a SYSTEM process (with practical caveats). - The write-up focuses on reversing the InstallService work creation flow (`CreateInstallServiceWork`) and the plugin activation logic (`PluginHelpers::ActivatePlugin`) rather than generic coercion. - It’s mainly useful for red teamers/pentesters researching new coercion surfaces and defenders building detections around unusual AppX registration + InstallService activity. - A key limitation is that Developer Mode must be enabled to allow UNC loose-file AppX registration, making it less reliable for real-world ops.
Technical Focus: - Windows Store InstallService internals (`InstallService.dll`, `InstallService.exe`) - WinRT/COM activation path and plugin resolution (`FulfillmentPluginId`, `FindPackagesForUser`) - UNC path influence leading to `LoadLibraryW` and outbound SMB/NTLM authentication - AppX loose-file registration (`Add-AppxPackage -Register`) and Developer Mode gating - Machine-account NTLM coercion over SMB - Practical PoC tooling (PowerShell + Impacket/Samba behavior differences)
Use Cases: - Coerce machine-account NTLM authentication to an attacker-controlled SMB server for relay/capture scenarios - Explore/validate a potential SYSTEM DLL-load path via InstallService plugin loading (LPE research) - Build detections for suspicious AppX loose registration from UNC paths and subsequent InstallService work creation - Reproduce and study InstallService plugin activation behavior for further attack surface research
Keywords: Windows Store, InstallService.exe, InstallService.dll, Windows.Internal.InstallService.Control.InstallServiceControl, CreateInstallServiceWork, PluginHelpers::ActivatePlugin, FulfillmentPluginId, FindPackagesForUser, InstalledLocation, LoadLibraryW, UNC path, SMB, NTLM coercion, machine account, AppX, Add-AppxPackage -Register, Developer Mode, Impacket smbserver, Samba, LPE

entry.title: edr xdr bypass and detection evasion techniques an investigation of advanced evasion strategies from a red team perspective
entry.summary:
Title: EDR/XDR Bypass and Detection Evasion Techniques: An Investigation of Advanced Evasion Strategies from a Red Team Perspective
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This article surveys modern EDR/XDR detection architecture and common red-team evasion approaches, focusing on how endpoint products combine user-mode hooks, ETW telemetry, and kernel callbacks to build “closed-loop” detection. - It explains several evasion primitives—API unhooking, BOF/COFF in-memory execution, direct/indirect syscalls, ETW suppression, and kernel callback tampering—along with practical implementation notes, tradeoffs, and stability/detection risks. - The write-up emphasizes that single techniques are increasingly insufficient and highlights layered combinations (e.g., indirect syscalls + ETW patching, in-memory execution + sleep obfuscation) and “time-difference” (latency) attacks that exploit asynchronous EDR processing windows. - It includes case-study style discussion referencing Bitdefender, CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, and Defender for Endpoint, with qualitative/estimated detection-rate comparisons and operational constraints (Windows build variance, privilege requirements, integrity checks). - Useful for red teams and security researchers designing adversary simulations, and for blue teams seeking to understand telemetry gaps and hardening priorities (memory integrity, callback protections, behavioral correlation). - Interesting because it ties low-level Windows internals (ntdll hooks, ETW providers, kernel notify routines) to practical evasion/detection dynamics and highlights where modern EDRs are shifting (kernel + cloud correlation, stack tracing, integrity validation).
Technical Focus: - User-mode API hooking and ntdll.dll unhooking (inline/IAT hooks, code-section restoration) - BOF/COFF in-memory execution and custom loaders (fileless, no new process) - Direct vs indirect system calls and syscall pattern/call-stack evasion - ETW telemetry suppression (EtwEventWrite patching, provider/logging disruption) - Kernel callback monitoring and evasion (PsSet*NotifyRoutine lists, driver-assisted tampering) - Timing/latency exploitation (“time-difference” attacks) and delayed execution primitives
Use Cases: - Build/redesign post-exploitation tradecraft to reduce user-mode hook visibility (authorized testing) - Evaluate EDR coverage gaps around ETW, syscall telemetry, and stack backtracing - Develop detection engineering hypotheses (correlating memory perms, ETW gaps, anomalous syscall sequences) - Create adversary emulation plans that test layered evasion chains and EDR response latency - Inform hardening guidance (HVCI/Credential Guard, integrity checks, callback protections, scripting controls)
Keywords: EDR, XDR, Windows internals, ntdll.dll, API hooking, inline hook, IAT hooking, API unhooking, BOF, Beacon Object File, COFF, in-memory execution, fileless, direct syscalls, indirect syscalls, syscall stubs, ETW, EtwEventWrite, AMSI, kernel callbacks, PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutine, PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine, stack tracing, memory integrity, VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, CreateRemoteThread, sleep obfuscation, timer-based execution, SetTimer, CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Bitdefender, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint

entry.title: addressing initial access
entry.summary:
Title: Addressing Initial Access
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This Huntress post describes practical hardening steps to reduce common Windows initial access paths used in phishing campaigns after Microsoft began blocking Internet-downloaded Office macros by default. It focuses on adversary shifts to disk image delivery (ISO/IMG/VHD/VHDX) and malicious OneNote notebooks used to launch embedded payloads (e.g., Qakbot-era tradecraft). The authors provide copy/paste PowerShell one-liners that directly set Windows Registry values (instead of importing ADMX templates) to: restrict OneNote embedded file execution, block specific embedded file extensions, prevent double-click auto-mounting of disk images, and enforce “block content execution from the Internet” for Office apps. The approach loops through all user hives under HKU to apply settings broadly without hardcoding Office versions. It’s primarily useful for Blue Teams/IT admins implementing attack surface reduction, but also valuable for red teams to understand which default behaviors defenders can easily remove. The key value is showing low-effort, high-impact configuration changes that break prevalent phishing-to-execution chains.
Technical Focus: - Windows Registry-based security hardening (HKLM/HKU, per-user hive iteration) - OneNote malware mitigation (disable embedded files, blocked extensions) - Mark-of-the-Web (MOTW) / “blockcontentexecutionfrominternet” for Office - Preventing Explorer auto-mount behavior for ISO/VHD via ProgrammaticAccessOnly - PowerShell automation for enterprise-wide configuration (wildcards, loops) - Initial Access tradecraft shifts (macros → disk images/OneNote)
Use Cases: - Deploy registry-based controls to reduce phishing-driven initial access - Harden endpoints against OneNote-delivered payload execution - Disable user-initiated auto-mounting of ISO/VHD to disrupt disk-image lures - Enforce Office Internet-origin macro/content execution blocking across users - Validate red-team delivery assumptions against common hardening baselines
Keywords: Huntress, initial access, TA0001, phishing, OneNote malware, Qakbot, ISO, IMG, VHD, VHDX, auto-mount, ProgrammaticAccessOnly, Windows Registry, HKLM, HKEY_USERS, HKU, Group Policy, ADMX templates, PowerShell one-liner, Mark-of-the-Web, MOTW, blockcontentexecutionfrominternet, Office macros, attack surface reduction

entry.title: winget desired state initial access established
entry.summary:
Title: WinGet Desired State: Initial Access Established
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - The post describes an initial access technique that abuses Windows Package Manager (winget) “configure” (DSC-based) to execute attacker-controlled configuration steps, including PowerShell script execution, without typical Mark-of-the-Web/SmartScreen friction. - It highlights that `.winget` files are associated with `winget.exe configure "%1" --wait`, enabling double-click execution and often bypassing common attachment blocklists. - To reduce user interaction (avoiding the interactive “Y” prompt and noisy console output), it chains winget with a self-referencing LNK that extracts an appended YAML configuration from itself (via `more`), opens a decoy, enables winget configure features, and runs the configuration silently. - The technique leverages legitimate DSC resources (Script/Archive/WindowsProcess), with execution occurring under `ConfigurationRemotingServer.exe`, and modules auto-downloaded from PowerShell Gallery into the user profile. - It includes practical tradecraft notes for evasion (LNK size/command length constraints, aliasing `winget dsc`, minimizing spawned `powershell.exe`, avoiding common LNK YARA hits) and notes AMSI still applies because `System.Management.Automation` is used. - Defensive guidance includes KQL hunting for winget configure usage, reviewing winget logs/config history, and hardening via Store/AppInstaller/winget configuration policy controls (or WDAC).
Technical Focus: - WinGet configuration / Desired State Configuration (DSC) abuse (`winget configure`, `winget dsc`) - Self-referencing LNK payloads with appended data extraction (`more +N`) - Living-off-the-land execution chain (cmd.exe → winget → DSC resources → PowerShell/WindowsProcess) - EDR considerations (MDE sensitivity to LNK size/characteristics, YARA-based LNK detections) - Telemetry and hunting (MDE KQL, winget logs, `winget configure list`) - Hardening via MDM/Group Policy (EnableAppInstaller, EnableWindowsPackageManagerConfiguration, WDAC)
Use Cases: - Phishing-based initial access in Windows environments where Microsoft Store/AppInstaller is enabled - Covert execution of configuration/persistence steps via DSC resources (registry/env/process/script) - Red team tradecraft for reducing prompts and disguising execution with decoy content - Blue team detection engineering for winget/DSC execution chains and LNK-based delivery - Security hardening assessments for Store/winget feature exposure and policy enforcement
Keywords: winget, Windows Package Manager, AppInstaller, Desired State Configuration, DSC, PSDscResources, ConfigurationRemotingServer.exe, PowerShell Gallery, LNK, self-referencing shortcut, Mark of the Web, SmartScreen, AMSI, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, KQL, WDAC, EnableAppInstaller, EnableWindowsPackageManagerConfiguration, cmd.exe, initial access, phishing, LOLBIN

entry.title: LOLGlobs
entry.summary:
Title: LOLGlobs — Process execution through wildcard pattern evasion
Type: Documentation / Reference (Web Catalog)
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): LOLGlobs is a curated catalog of “living off the land” commands across Linux, macOS, Windows CMD, and PowerShell that can be invoked using wildcard/globbing patterns to evade simplistic detections and allowlists. It focuses on abusing wildcard characters (e.g., `*`, `?`, character classes like `[a-z]`) to match and execute binaries/cmdlets without typing the exact command string defenders may be filtering on. Entries are organized by platform and operational category (discovery, download, execution, exfiltration, lateral movement, etc.) and mapped to MITRE ATT&CK technique IDs. The resource highlights how common utilities (e.g., `certutil`, `bitsadmin`, `rundll32`, `mshta`, `bash`, `curl`, `ssh`, PowerShell cmdlets) can be leveraged when command-line monitoring relies on naive substring or exact-match rules. It’s primarily useful for red teams and pentesters for tradecraft and for blue teams to harden detections against wildcard/regex bypasses. Its value is in turning “string-based detection gaps” into concrete, testable examples across multiple OS ecosystems.
Technical Focus: - Wildcard/globbing-based command evasion (`*`, `?`, `[]`, brace patterns) - LOLBins/LOLBAS and native utility abuse across OSes - Command-line detection bypass (naive allowlists/blacklists, brittle regex) - MITRE ATT&CK mapping for execution, download, discovery, exfiltration, lateral movement - PowerShell cmdlet abuse patterns (e.g., `Invoke-*`, `Add-Type`, `New-Object`) - Windows signed binary proxy execution (e.g., `mshta`, `regsvr32`, `rundll32`)
Use Cases: - Build and validate detection rules resilient to wildcard/glob obfuscation - Generate adversary emulation test cases for command-line telemetry pipelines - Identify alternative native tooling for download/execution when common strings are blocked - Train analysts on how “benign” utilities become execution and staging primitives - Create allowlisting policies based on behavior/parent-child/process lineage rather than exact command strings
Keywords: LOLGlobs, globbing, wildcard evasion, command-line obfuscation, LOLBins, LOLBAS, PowerShell, Windows CMD, Linux, macOS, MITRE ATT&CK, T1059, T1105, T1218, mshta, rundll32, regsvr32, certutil, bitsadmin, WMI, wmic, Invoke-WebRequest, Invoke-RestMethod

entry.title: inlineExecute
entry.summary:
Title: inlineExecute (Cobalt Strike BOF for in-process .NET execution with ETW/AMSI patching)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository provides a Cobalt Strike Beacon Object File (BOF) that loads and executes a .NET assembly inside the current Beacon process instead of using `execute-assembly` with a sacrificial process. - It focuses on reducing telemetry by implementing ETW bypasses in `clr.dll` via Provider Handle Patching and Subscriber Bit Patching, based on the author’s “A Stealthier Reflective Loading” write-up. - The ETW approach aims to avoid common suspicious primitives (e.g., `VirtualProtect`, `WriteProcessMemory`) by not changing memory protections. - It also includes an AMSI bypass targeting `clr.dll` by modifying `g_amsiContext` and `AmsiScanBuffer`-related globals located in read/write memory, again avoiding memory-protection flips. - A `-scan` mode identifies patchable values/addresses using x64 instruction signatures to help validate offsets per `clr.dll` build. - Useful for red team operators/pentesters experimenting with stealthier in-process .NET tradecraft and researchers evaluating detection opportunities around CLR/ETW/AMSI manipulation.
Technical Focus: - Cobalt Strike BOF in-process .NET assembly execution - CLR hosting (`clr.dll`, `ICorRuntimeHost`) and AppDomain execution - ETW bypass: Provider Handle Patching - ETW bypass: Subscriber Bit Patching - AMSI bypass via `g_amsiContext` / `AmsiScanBuffer` global manipulation - Signature-based offset discovery for patching across `clr.dll` versions
Use Cases: - Execute tools like Rubeus/SharpHound-style .NET assemblies without spawning a child process - Reduce/alter ETW telemetry emitted by CLR during managed code execution - Bypass AMSI scanning for managed execution paths in-process - Research and prototype alternative patching methods that avoid `VirtualProtect`/WPM patterns - Validate detection engineering ideas by observing CLR global/ETW structure tampering
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, BOF, Beacon, inline execution, .NET assembly, CLR hosting, clr.dll, ICorRuntimeHost, AppDomain, ETW, Event Tracing for Windows, Provider Handle Patching, Subscriber Bit Patching, AMSI, AmsiScanBuffer, g_amsiContext, userland telemetry, signature scanning, x64 assembly, in-process execution

entry.title: SilentNimvest
entry.summary:
Title: SilentNimvest
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): SilentNimvest is a Nim-based parser/dumper for Windows SAM and SECURITY registry hives to extract local credential material and secrets. It implements the “Silent Harvest” technique to read sensitive registry keys without requiring SYSTEM, leveraging an Administrator token with `SeBackupPrivilege` and specific `NtOpenKeyEx` access/flags. The tool focuses on using a less commonly alerted registry API (`RegQueryMultipleValuesW`) to reduce EDR noise compared to typical hive dumping approaches. It can recover local user NTLM hashes, cached domain logon data, DPAPI-related keys, NL$KM, and LSA Secrets (including service account plaintext where present). It is primarily useful for red teams and pentesters performing post-exploitation credential access on Windows endpoints, and for defenders researching telemetry around stealthier registry access patterns. It is interesting because it operationalizes a lower-friction, lower-privilege path to hive-based secret extraction based on published research.
Technical Focus: - Silent Harvest registry access technique (SAM/SECURITY without SYSTEM) - `SeBackupPrivilege` abuse for registry key access - Native/Win32 registry APIs: `NtOpenKeyEx`, `RegQueryMultipleValuesW` - SAM parsing and NTLM hash extraction - SECURITY hive parsing: cached logons, LSA Secrets, NL$KM, DPAPI keys - Nim implementation details (winim, nimcrypto, checksums/des)
Use Cases: - Dump local account NTLM hashes from SAM during post-exploitation - Extract cached domain credentials (DCC) and machine account-related secrets from SECURITY - Recover LSA Secrets (e.g., service credentials) for lateral movement - Red-team tradecraft testing for EDR detection gaps around registry read APIs - Blue-team validation of detections for `SeBackupPrivilege` + unusual registry query patterns
Keywords: Silent Harvest, SilentNimvest, Nim, Windows, SAM, SECURITY hive, registry, credential dumping, SeBackupPrivilege, NtOpenKeyEx, RegQueryMultipleValuesW, LSA Secrets, cached domain logons, NL$KM, DPAPI machinekey, DPAPI userkey, NTLM, post-exploitation, EDR evasion, winim

entry.title: Adrenaline
entry.summary:
Title: Adrenaline BOF Kit
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Adrenaline is a C2-agnostic collection of Windows Beacon Object Files (BOFs) organized by attack-chain phase (MITRE-style) for reconnaissance, enumeration, credential access, and post-exploitation. It targets the problem of running common situational-awareness and collection tasks without spawning noisy child processes, enabling small, modular, automation-friendly execution inside a beacon/agent. The kit includes BOFs for artifact collection (clipboard, PowerShell history, window handle abuse), credential-oriented checks (cert store exportability, cloud instance metadata probing, process token enumeration), and host/security posture discovery (AMSI/ETW presence, AppLocker, Defender ASR, BitLocker, WEF, proxy state, MDM artifacts). Execution-focused modules interact with Windows subsystems via APIs/COM (firewall rules via INetFwPolicy2, service control via SCM, COM instantiation probing, Security event log queries via wevtapi). It’s primarily useful for red teams and pentesters operating on Windows endpoints who need low-footprint telemetry and targeted collection at scale. Interesting due to its breadth of modern enterprise posture checks (MDM/Intune, ASR, WEF) and cloud metadata credential exposure probing from within compromised hosts.
Technical Focus: - Beacon Object Files (BOF) development in C for Windows - Win32 API and COM-based enumeration/interaction (Task Scheduler, INetFwPolicy2, IDesktopWallpaper) - Credential and artifact collection (clipboard, PSReadLine/transcripts, certificate stores, tokens) - Security control and posture discovery (AMSI/ETW, AppLocker, Defender ASR, BitLocker, WSC, WEF) - Cloud instance metadata service probing (AWS/Azure/GCP) - Windows event log querying via wevtapi (4624/4625/4672)
Use Cases: - Rapid situational awareness on Windows endpoints with minimal process creation - Identify defensive controls and logging centralization (ASR, AppLocker, WEF, AMSI/ETW) to guide tradecraft - Loot operator-relevant artifacts (clipboard contents, PowerShell history, Notepad in-memory text) - Enumerate and assess credential opportunities (exportable cert keys, accessible process tokens, cloud metadata creds) - Modify/query local firewall rules and manage services for lateral movement/pivot prep - Enumerate scheduled tasks and terminal services sessions for persistence and user activity insights
Keywords: BOF, Beacon Object File, Windows, Win32 API, COM, IDesktopWallpaper, INetFwPolicy2, SCM, wevtapi, AMSI, ETW, AppLocker, Defender ASR, BitLocker, Windows Security Center, Windows Event Forwarding, PSReadLine, clipboard, process tokens, SeDebugPrivilege, certificate store, cloud metadata, AWS IMDS, Azure IMDS, GCP metadata, Task Scheduler COM, scheduled tasks, Terminal Services sessions

entry.title: gopacket
entry.summary:
Title: mandiant/gopacket — Go reimplementation of Impacket (63 tools + 24 protocol packages)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository is a native Go framework that reimplements Impacket’s Windows/AD network protocol tooling, providing 63 operator-facing tools and 24 reusable protocol libraries. - It targets the common problem of Python/Impacket operational friction (dependencies, portability) by enabling “compile once, run anywhere” binaries for Linux/macOS/Windows. - It covers core offensive protocol interactions and tradecraft: SMB/DCERPC/WMI/DCOM remote exec, secrets dumping (SAM/LSA/NTDS/DCSync), Kerberos (roasting, S4U, ticket forging), LDAP enumeration/abuse, and a full NTLM relay framework (ntlmrelayx) with multi-protocol support. - A notable focus is proxy safety/OPSEC: built-in SOCKS5 (`-proxy`) and proxychains compatibility, with explicit prevention of UDP leaks (disables UDP-dependent features under proxy). - Useful for red teams/pentesters wanting compiled equivalents of Impacket, and for defenders/purple teams studying how Go-based post-exploitation tooling differs on the wire from Python implementations. - Interesting because it anticipates the shift toward compiled attacker tooling and provides a readable reference implementation for protocol behavior, relay workflows, and AD attack primitives.
Technical Focus: - SMB2/3 client/server, NTLM authentication, pass-the-hash - Kerberos (TGT/TGS, S4U2Self/S4U2Proxy, ticket forging, PAC parsing) - DCE/RPC services (SAMR, LSARPC, SVCCTL, WINREG, DRSUAPI, TSCH, EPMAPPER) and remote execution (WMI/DCOM/SMB) - NTLM relay framework (multi-protocol capture/relay, SOCKS pivoting, attack modules) - LDAP/LDAPS enumeration and AD object security manipulation (DACL/owner/RBCD/shadow creds) - Proxying/transport controls (SOCKS5/socks5h, proxychains via libc linking, UDP leak avoidance)
Use Cases: - Compile and deploy single-binary equivalents of Impacket tools in restricted environments - AD enumeration and attack execution (Kerberoast/AS-REP roast, RBCD, DACL/owner edits, LAPS/GPP password retrieval) - Remote command execution over SMB/WMI/DCOM/Task Scheduler - Credential dumping and replication attacks (DCSync, NTDS.dit/SAM/LSA offline/remote extraction) - NTLM relay operations across SMB/LDAP(S)/HTTP/MSSQL/WinRM with SOCKS pivoting - Defensive testing: validate detections against Go-based protocol stacks and relay behavior
Keywords: Go, Impacket, Active Directory, SMB2, SMB3, NTLM, Kerberos, LDAP, LDAPS, DCE/RPC, DRSUAPI, SAMR, LSARPC, SVCCTL, WINREG, WMI, DCOM, psexec, secretsdump, DCSync, ntlmrelayx, RBCD, S4U2Proxy, Kerberoasting, AS-REP roasting, SOCKS5, proxychains, PAC, DPAPI, NTDS.dit, CVE-2021-42278, CVE-2021-42287, ADCS ESC8

entry.title: SilentHarvest BOF
entry.summary:
Title: SilentHarvest BOF
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): SilentHarvest_BOF is a Cobalt Strike Beacon Object File (BOF) that implements the “SilentHarvest” registry-only credential dumping technique (via SilentNimvest), aiming to extract Windows secrets with reduced noise compared to classic LSASS-centric dumping. It replicates hashdump-style extraction and also retrieves LSA secrets from `HKLM\SECURITY\Policy\Secrets`, relying on offline/registry-based decryption paths. A key operational benefit is that it only requires `SeBackupPrivilege` (often available to local Administrators) rather than full SYSTEM, by leveraging backup-style access to protected registry hives. The project targets newer Windows versions (Win10/Server 2016+) where secrets are protected with AES; legacy RC4 handling is not implemented. It also documents a BOF build approach that avoids standard BOF API declaration headers by post-processing symbols with `objcopy` (and optionally a separate “BOFPatcher” workflow). Useful primarily for red team operators and pentesters who want credential material from the registry under constrained privilege and OPSEC requirements.
Technical Focus: - SilentHarvest / registry-only credential dumping - `SeBackupPrivilege`-based access to protected registry hives - SAM/SECURITY/SYSTEM hive parsing and decryption for hashdump-equivalent output - LSA Secrets extraction from `HKLM\SECURITY\Policy\Secrets` - Windows AES-protected secret formats (Win10/Server 2016+) - BOF development tradecraft: `objcopy` import symbol patching (`DLL$API`)
Use Cases: - Dump local account password hashes without LSASS dumping - Extract LSA secrets (service account creds, DPAPI-related secrets depending on host state) - Recover cached domain credential material for offline cracking (e.g., Hashcat) - Run credential collection from an admin context where SYSTEM is not available - Integrate low-footprint credential harvesting into Cobalt Strike workflows
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, BOF, Beacon Object File, SilentHarvest, SilentNimvest, Windows registry, SAM, SECURITY hive, SYSTEM hive, LSA Secrets, HKLM\\SECURITY\\Policy\\Secrets, hashdump, SeBackupPrivilege, AES, cached domain credentials, Hashcat, objcopy, DLL$API imports, Windows Server 2016, Windows 10

entry.title: GodPotatoBOF
entry.summary:
Title: GodPotatoBOF
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): GodPotatoBOF is a Cobalt Strike Beacon Object File (BOF) port of the GodPotato privilege escalation technique. It targets Windows contexts where the current process has the SeImpersonate privilege, triggering the GodPotato flow to obtain a SYSTEM token. The BOF supports either spawning a new process to run an arbitrary command as SYSTEM or applying the stolen SYSTEM token to the current Beacon via `BeaconUseToken()`. It includes a CNA script (`godpotato.cna`) to expose a simple `godpotato` alias with options for command execution and custom named pipe selection. This is primarily useful for red teams and pentesters needing in-memory local privilege escalation within Cobalt Strike tradecraft.
Technical Focus: - SeImpersonate-based local privilege escalation - Token theft/impersonation and SYSTEM token acquisition - Cobalt Strike BOF development and in-memory execution - Named pipe usage/customization in exploitation flow - CNA (Cobalt Strike scripting) integration and operator UX
Use Cases: - Elevate a Beacon session to NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM on hosts where SeImpersonate is available - Run post-exploitation commands as SYSTEM without dropping an EXE - Apply a SYSTEM token to the existing Beacon for follow-on actions (e.g., service/LSASS access) - Operator-friendly execution via CNA alias with optional custom pipe naming
Keywords: GodPotato, GodPotatoBOF, Cobalt Strike, BOF, Beacon Object File, CNA, SeImpersonatePrivilege, privilege escalation, local privesc, token impersonation, SYSTEM token, BeaconUseToken, named pipe, Windows security, in-memory execution, post-exploitation, cmd execution, red team tooling

entry.title: LDAP Bof Collection
entry.summary:
Title: LDAP BOF Collection
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - A collection of 40+ LDAP-focused Beacon Object Files (BOFs) intended to run inside C2 agents (built for Adaptix C2, but usable anywhere BOFs and argument passing are supported). - It addresses the need to perform Active Directory enumeration and privilege escalation actions without relying on SOCKS/proxying external tooling (e.g., ldapsearch), by executing LDAP operations directly from the compromised host context. - The repo implements both standard LDAP (389) with signing/sealing and LDAPS (636) with permissive certificate validation (accept-all callback). - It provides commands for AD object enumeration, creation, modification, deletion, ACL/DACL manipulation, delegation configuration (including RBCD), and convenience “attack macros” that chain common abuse steps. - Useful primarily for red teams and pentesters operating in AD environments who want low-friction, in-agent LDAP tradecraft and automation. - Interesting because it packages a broad set of bloodyAD-like capabilities into BOFs, including DN/username auto-detection and Adaptix AxScript command registration.
Technical Focus: - LDAP/LDAPS binds, signing & sealing, certificate validation bypass (accept-all) - Active Directory object enumeration and attribute operations (read/add/set/remove) - AD security descriptors: DACL/ACE parsing and WriteDACL-style modifications - Delegation abuse: constrained/unconstrained delegation and Resource-Based Constrained Delegation (msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity) - UserAccountControl (UAC) flag manipulation and SPN management - BOF development/integration (beacon.h-style APIs, Adaptix AxScript mappings)
Use Cases: - Enumerate users/computers/groups, SPNs, delegation settings, and domain info via rootDSE from an implanted host - Identify writable/modifiable AD objects and inspect ACLs to plan privilege escalation - Add/modify ACEs to grant GenericAll/GenericWrite or DCSync-equivalent rights (where permissions allow) - Configure RBCD or delegation settings to enable lateral movement/impersonation paths - Create users/computers/groups and adjust attributes (SPNs, UAC flags) for roasting or persistence - Perform sensitive operations over LDAPS when LDAP is blocked or when changing passwords/attributes
Keywords: LDAP, LDAPS, Active Directory, BOF, Beacon Object File, Adaptix C2, AxScript, rootDSE, Distinguished Name, security descriptor, ntSecurityDescriptor, DACL, ACE, WriteDACL, GenericAll, GenericWrite, DCSync, Resource-Based Constrained Delegation, msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity, ServicePrincipalName, UserAccountControl, Kerberoasting, AS-REP roasting, machineAccountQuota, MinGW-w64

entry.title: lolol.farm
entry.summary:
Title: lolol.farm — “Living Off the Land” ecosystem index/search
Type: Other (Directory / Link hub)
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - lolol.farm is a searchable directory that aggregates “Living Off the Land” style projects and adjacent references across Windows, Linux, macOS, cloud, AD, CI/CD, and hardware. - It solves the discovery problem by centralizing many specialized catalogs (e.g., LOLBAS, GTFOBins, LOLDrivers, HijackLibs, LOLC2, LOLEXFIL) into one place with tags and platform filters. - The linked resources cover abuse of legitimate binaries, drivers, APIs, SaaS, webhooks, tunnels, and trusted infrastructure for execution, persistence, C2, phishing, and exfiltration, often mapped to MITRE ATT&CK. - It’s useful for red teams/pentesters to quickly find native/legitimate tradecraft options, and for blue teams/detection engineers to identify common abuse patterns and build detections/allowlisting strategies. - It’s interesting because it highlights how modern intrusions increasingly rely on “legitimate” components (signed drivers, built-in tools, popular domains/services) that are harder to block outright.
Technical Focus: - Living-off-the-land binaries/scripts (LOLBins/GTFOBins/LOOBins/LOLESXi) - Signed driver abuse / BYOVD (LOLDrivers) - DLL search order abuse and DLL hijacking candidates (HijackLibs) - Legitimate-service C2 and exfiltration channels (LOLC2, LOT Webhooks, LOT Tunnels, LOLEXFIL, LOLFSaaS) - Active Directory native tradecraft (LOLAD, WADComs) - Detection engineering and false positives (LoFP), ATT&CK mapping
Use Cases: - Rapidly enumerate LOL techniques for a given OS/platform during an engagement - Build detection content around common LOLBin/driver/API abuse patterns - Identify plausible C2/exfil channels using legitimate SaaS, webhooks, or tunnels - Research DLL hijack opportunities and defensive hardening targets - Threat intel enrichment: map observed tooling/behavior to known catalogs
Keywords: LOLBAS, GTFOBins, LOLDrivers, HijackLibs, LOLC2, LOLEXFIL, LOLAD, LOOBins, LOLESXi, BYOVD, DLL hijacking, Windows APIs, persistence, C2, exfiltration, webhooks, tunnels, RMM abuse, CI/CD workflow injection, MITRE ATT&CK

entry.title: dllhost
entry.summary:
Title: Windows Process Internals - Dllhost Explained
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This write-up reverse engineers `dllhost.exe` to explain what it actually does when launched and how it participates in COM surrogate activation. It shows that `dllhost.exe` mainly parses a GUID from the command line (`{GUID}` or `/ProcessID:{GUID}`), converts it via `IIDFromString`, initializes COM (`CoInitializeEx`), and registers itself as a surrogate (`CoRegisterSurrogateEx`). The post highlights a special GUID-specific branch where, if a Windows feature flag is enabled and the GUID matches a specific value, an elevated token triggers `SetProcessMitigationPolicy` before surrogate registration. It then connects runtime behavior to registry configuration (`AppID\...\DllSurrogate`) that determines whether COM uses the default system surrogate (`DllHost.exe /ProcessID:{AppID}`) or a custom surrogate executable built as `<path> {CLSID} -Embedding`. This is useful for defenders and red teamers to correctly interpret `dllhost.exe` executions as evidence of COM surrogate activity rather than a standalone LOLBin. It’s interesting because it ties together command-line parsing, COM internals, and registry-driven surrogate selection with concrete code-level observations.
Technical Focus: - `dllhost.exe` command-line parsing (`/ProcessID:{GUID}` vs `{GUID}`) - COM initialization and surrogate registration (`CoInitializeEx`, `CoRegisterSurrogateEx`) - GUID parsing (`IIDFromString`) - Registry-driven COM surrogate selection (`HKCR/HKLM ... AppID\...\DllSurrogate`) - `-Embedding` activation pattern and COM command construction (via `combase.dll`) - Token elevation checks and process mitigation policy (`GetTokenInformation`, `SetProcessMitigationPolicy`)
Use Cases: - Triage and interpret `dllhost.exe` process executions and command lines during incident response - Hunt for suspicious/custom COM surrogate configurations via `AppID\DllSurrogate` registry values - Validate whether observed `dllhost.exe /ProcessID:{GUID}` corresponds to an AppID-based surrogate activation - Build detections around anomalous surrogate paths or unexpected `-Embedding` COM activation chains
Keywords: dllhost.exe, COM surrogate, CoRegisterSurrogateEx, CoInitializeEx, IIDFromString, AppID, CLSID, DllSurrogate, HKCR, HKLM, combase.dll, -Embedding, COM activation, registry persistence, GetTokenInformation, TokenElevation, SetProcessMitigationPolicy, COMSysAppRedirectionTrustPolicy, Windows internals, reverse engineering

entry.title: bring your own trusted binary byotb bsides edition
entry.summary:
Title: Bring Your Own Trusted Binary (BYOTB) – BSides Edition
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post summarizes a BSides London 2024 talk on abusing “trusted” third-party binaries (primarily Cloudflare’s `cloudflared` and Win32-OpenSSH) for stealthy tunneling and access in compromised environments. - It focuses on using `cloudflared` as an SSH `ProxyCommand` to egress over TCP/443 as HTTPS, enabling reverse port forwards and SOCKS proxies without relying on typical tunneling tools (e.g., Ligolo) and with reduced EDR friction. - The author shows how to bring your own `ssh.exe` (plus required DLLs) and optionally deploy `sshd` for persistent command-line access, as well as port-forwarding for scenarios like coercion/NTLM relay workflows. - A key section explores Cloudflare WARP + `cloudflared` for tool-native access (e.g., NetExec) and introduces a “double tunnel” technique leveraging an undocumented `edge` feature to avoid outbound port 7844 restrictions by chaining through an Access listener over 443. - Defensive guidance is included: process/command-line telemetry, DNS monitoring for `argotunnel.com`, firewall logging/blocks for 7844, and file/download monitoring for `cloudflared` artifacts. - Useful for red teams and pentesters building firewall-friendly egress and pivoting paths, and for blue teams looking to detect Cloudflare-tunnel-based tradecraft.
Technical Focus: - BYOTB / living-off-trusted-binaries (Cloudflare `cloudflared`, Win32-OpenSSH) - SSH `ProxyCommand`, reverse (`-R`) and local (`-L`) port forwarding, SOCKS proxying - Cloudflare Tunnel / Access SSH and TCP forwarding over HTTPS (443) - WARP client as VPN-like access path for operator tooling (e.g., NetExec) - “Double tunnel” chaining to bypass port 7844 egress constraints (TCP/http2) - Detection opportunities: process args, DNS (`argotunnel.com`), firewall telemetry, file integrity
Use Cases: - Establish stealthy egress over 443 using `cloudflared` to carry SSH traffic as HTTPS - Create SOCKS proxies and port forwards for internal pivoting without custom implants - Deploy portable OpenSSH (`ssh.exe`/`sshd`) on targets lacking an SSH client - Forward SMB/HTTP to support relay/coercion workflows (e.g., NTLM relay/RBCD-style paths) - Operate in restrictive networks by avoiding direct outbound 7844 via chained tunneling - Build detections for Cloudflare-tunnel abuse via DNS/process/firewall/file monitoring
Keywords: cloudflared, Cloudflare Tunnel, Cloudflare Access, WARP, BYOTB, trusted binary abuse, SSH ProxyCommand, reverse port forward, local port forward, SOCKS proxy, Proxychains, NetExec, argotunnel.com, port 7844, TCP 443, HTTP/2, Win32-OpenSSH, sshd, NTLM relay, RBCD

entry.title: SoaPy
entry.summary:
Title: SoaPy — Offensive ADWS (Active Directory Web Services) interaction from Linux via custom .NET SOAP protocol implementations
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): SoaPy is a proof-of-concept Python tool that enables offensive enumeration and targeted attribute modification of Active Directory objects by talking to Active Directory Web Services (ADWS) from Linux. It implements several Microsoft/.NET transport and serialization protocols (e.g., NegotiateStream, Message Framing, and .NET Binary SOAP extensions) to communicate with ADWS without relying on Windows tooling. The main problem it addresses is performing stealthier AD enumeration and select write operations over ADWS (often reachable where LDAP may be monitored/blocked), including use through proxies into internal networks. It supports enumeration presets (users, computers, groups, delegation flags, SPNs, ASREP-roastable accounts, admins, RBCD-enabled objects) and raw query/attribute filtering. It also supports exploitation-oriented writes: setting `servicePrincipalName` (targeted Kerberoasting), enabling `DONT_REQ_PREAUTH` (targeted ASREP roasting), and writing `msDs-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity` (RBCD). Useful primarily for red teams and pentesters operating from Linux who want ADWS-based tradecraft and for defenders researching ADWS abuse paths.
Technical Focus: - Active Directory Web Services (ADWS) SOAP interaction - Custom implementations of NNS (NegotiateStream), NMF (Message Framing), NBFSE (.NET Binary Format: SOAP Extension) - AD object enumeration via queries and attribute selection - AD attribute modification for SPN, `userAccountControl` (DONT_REQ_PREAUTH), and RBCD - Kerberoasting / ASREP roasting enablement primitives - Resource-Based Constrained Delegation (RBCD) manipulation
Use Cases: - Enumerate AD users/computers/groups over ADWS from a Linux foothold - Proxy-based, lower-friction AD reconnaissance when LDAP tooling is constrained - Identify delegation misconfigurations (constrained/unconstrained, RBCD) - Perform targeted Kerberoasting by writing controlled SPNs to chosen accounts - Make specific accounts ASREP-roastable by setting `DONT_REQ_PREAUTH` - Configure/remove `msDs-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity` for RBCD attack setup
Keywords: ADWS, Active Directory Web Services, SOAP, LDAP query, Windows domain, NegotiateStream, NNS, .NET Message Framing, NMF, NBFSE, NTLM, Kerberos, servicePrincipalName, SPN, Kerberoasting, ASREP roasting, DONT_REQ_PREAUTH, userAccountControl, msDs-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity, RBCD, constrained delegation, unconstrained delegation, proxy enumeration, Python, offensive AD enumeration

entry.title: byovd process termination understanding the pattern by reversing tcvinprocterm driver 5c431d9d578b
entry.summary:
Title: BYOVD & Process Termination: Understanding The Pattern By Reversing TCVINProcTerm Driver
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Walkthrough of the BYOVD (Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver) pattern specifically for kernel-assisted process termination via a driver-exposed IOCTL. - Uses an educational sample driver (TCVINProcTerm.sys) that accepts a PID from user mode and terminates the target process in kernel mode using ZwOpenProcess + ZwTerminateProcess. - Demonstrates how to reverse a Windows kernel driver to recover the device name/symlink, IOCTL value, and input buffer contract by following DriverEntry → MajorFunction[IRP_MJ_DEVICE_CONTROL] → DeviceControl. - Covers the user↔kernel boundary (CreateFile on `\\.\DeviceName`, DeviceIoControl, IRPs, METHOD_BUFFERED) and how the I/O manager routes requests to dispatch routines. - Validates findings in a test-signed VM by loading the driver via SCM (`sc create/start`) and sending the recovered IOCTL with a 4-byte PID buffer to kill a process. - Useful for red teamers/malware analysts to understand common “EDR-killer” mechanics, and for defenders to recognize telemetry points (driver load + device handle + IOCTL-driven termination).
Technical Focus: - BYOVD mechanics (signed driver abuse via IOCTL) - Windows driver internals: DriverEntry, device objects, symbolic links, MajorFunction dispatch table - IRP handling and IOCTL parsing (IRP_MJ_DEVICE_CONTROL, METHOD_BUFFERED, SystemBuffer) - Kernel process termination primitives (ZwOpenProcess, ZwTerminateProcess, ZwClose, ACCESS_MASK/PROCESS_TERMINATE) - Reverse engineering workflow (Ghidra/IDA strings, xrefs, constant IOCTL recovery) - Lab validation (test signing, SCM driver loading, user-mode IOCTL client)
Use Cases: - Reverse unknown/suspicious drivers to extract device names and IOCTL “contracts” - Build minimal user-mode clients to reproduce driver behavior in a controlled lab - Understand and emulate BYOVD-style process termination tradecraft for testing detections - Create detection hypotheses around driver load + `\\.\` device access + DeviceIoControl patterns - Validate whether kernel termination attempts fail against protected targets (e.g., PPL scenarios)
Keywords: BYOVD, Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver, Windows kernel driver, IOCTL, DeviceIoControl, IRP_MJ_DEVICE_CONTROL, IRP, METHOD_BUFFERED, IoCreateDevice, IoCreateSymbolicLink, DriverEntry, MajorFunction, \\Device\\, \\DosDevices\\, \\\\.\\ device path, ZwOpenProcess, ZwTerminateProcess, PROCESS_TERMINATE, ACCESS_MASK, OBJECT_ATTRIBUTES, CLIENT_ID, test signing, bcdedit testsigning, sc create, sc start, Ghidra, IDA Pro, driver reverse engineering, EDR kill, process termination, PPL, Microsoft vulnerable driver blocklist, WDAC

entry.title: win11 kernel execution syscall hijack
entry.summary:
Title: Executing Kernel Routines via Syscall Table Hijack (Windows 11 KiServiceTable)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository demonstrates a Windows 11 kernel-mode technique to execute arbitrary existing kernel routines by temporarily hijacking a syscall dispatch entry in `nt!KiServiceTable`. - It assumes an initial kernel read/write primitive (demo uses the BYOVD RTCore64 driver) to flip page permissions at the PDE level, overwrite the `NtSetQuotaInformationFile` service-table entry, trigger the syscall, then restore the original entry and protections. - The write targets the packed 32-bit SSDT entry format (offset<<4 plus `stackArgs` nibble) and uses a generic user-mode `syscall` stub to call redirected routines with varying arity. - Included demos show kernel-assisted process introspection (`PsLookupProcessByProcessId`), token stealing for SYSTEM (`/privesc` via `memcpy`), and toggling PPL by patching `_EPROCESS.Protection` (`/ppl`). - The write-up discusses why this can evade or reduce friction with modern mitigations (KCFG/KCET/W^X/HVCI/VBS) by only calling code already in `ntoskrnl.exe` and performing “data-only” page-table flag changes. - It’s primarily useful for kernel exploitation researchers, red teamers with post-exploitation kernel R/W, and defenders studying SSDT tampering and BYOVD tradecraft.
Technical Focus: - SSDT/KiServiceTable syscall dispatch internals and entry encoding (offset<<4, stackArgs) - PDE/PTE paging manipulation to bypass read-only mappings (large pages, R/W bit flip) - Kernel R/W primitives via BYOVD (RTCore64) and reducing IOCTL telemetry - Redirecting syscalls to exported kernel routines (e.g., `PsLookupProcessByProcessId`, `memcpy`) - Token manipulation via `_EPROCESS.Token` (EX_FAST_REF) and PPL via `_EPROCESS.Protection` - Offset resolution strategies (WinDbg manual offsets vs runtime RVA resolution)
Use Cases: - Execute chosen `ntoskrnl` routines from user mode by repointing a syscall slot - Privilege escalation by swapping a process token to SYSTEM - Modify process protection state (PPL) for tampering or testing protections - Build a quieter kernel read/write channel after an initial BYOVD bootstrap - Research/detection engineering for SSDT entry changes and page-table permission flips
Keywords: Windows 11, kernel exploitation, KiServiceTable, SSDT, syscall hijacking, NtSetQuotaInformationFile, KeServiceDescriptorTableShadow, MiGetPteAddress, PDE, PTE, large page, page table flags, RTCore64, BYOVD, PsLookupProcessByProcessId, EPROCESS, EX_FAST_REF, token stealing, memcpy, PPL, _PS_PROTECTION, WinDbg, IDA Pro, KCFG, KCET, HVCI, VBS, PatchGuard

entry.title: PPLShade
entry.summary:
Title: PPLShade
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): PPLShade is a BYOVD (Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver) tool designed to manipulate Windows Protected Process Light (PPL) protections from kernel mode. It targets the problem of accessing, dumping, or tampering with security-sensitive processes (e.g., LSASS or AV/EDR components) that are protected by PPL and normally resist user-mode access. The repository includes a Visual Studio solution and bundled kernel drivers (e.g., LECOMAx64.sys, ipctype.sys) used as the vulnerable primitives to achieve kernel-level memory/process manipulation. The core concept is leveraging a signed-but-vulnerable driver to perform privileged operations that can downgrade/alter process protection state. It is primarily useful for red teams and pentesters validating defensive controls around driver loading, PPL enforcement, and credential theft protections, and for blue teams to build detections around BYOVD and PPL tampering. It’s interesting because it operationalizes a common real-world bypass path: kernel-assisted defeat of PPL without needing a bespoke kernel exploit.
Technical Focus: - BYOVD tradecraft (signed vulnerable driver abuse) - Windows Protected Process Light (PPL) internals and tampering - Kernel-mode process/memory manipulation primitives - Driver loading and abuse paths on Windows x64 - EDR/AV and LSASS access bypass patterns
Use Cases: - Disabling/downgrading PPL to enable LSASS dumping in controlled assessments - Testing EDR/AV resilience against BYOVD-based kernel tampering - Validating enterprise controls: driver blocklists (WDAC), HVCI/Memory Integrity, vulnerable driver mitigation - Building detections for suspicious driver loads and PPL state changes - Reproducing and studying PPL bypass techniques in a lab
Keywords: PPL, Protected Process Light, BYOVD, vulnerable driver, Windows kernel, kernel-mode, driver exploitation, LSASS, credential dumping, EDR bypass, AV tampering, signed driver abuse, WDAC, HVCI, Memory Integrity, process protection, SeDebugPrivilege, Nt/Zw APIs, Windows internals

entry.title: PhantomKiller
entry.summary:
Title: PhantomKiller
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): PhantomKiller is a Windows “BYOVD” (Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver) style process-killer tool packaged with a signed kernel driver (`PhantomKiller.sys`) and a C++ user-mode controller (`PhantomKiller.cpp`). Its goal is to terminate protected/security processes (e.g., EDR/AV agents) by leveraging kernel-mode capabilities rather than user-mode termination APIs that are commonly blocked. The repository appears to focus on reliably killing processes across multiple EDR products by using a driver interface to perform privileged operations. This is primarily useful for red teams and malware developers testing defensive resilience and driver-based tamper techniques, and for blue teams to understand/prepare detections around malicious driver loading and kernel-assisted process tampering. The interesting aspect is the operational packaging (signed driver + userland loader/controller) and the emphasis on EDR-agnostic process termination via kernel primitives.
Technical Focus: - BYOVD tradecraft (abusing/bringing a driver to gain kernel capabilities) - Kernel-mode process termination / process object manipulation - Signed driver loading and driver-service installation workflow - User-mode to kernel-mode IOCTL communication - EDR self-protection/tamper resistance bypass patterns
Use Cases: - Disabling EDR/AV agents during an engagement to validate defensive controls - Testing detections for malicious driver load + process-kill behavior - Lab research on kernel-assisted tampering and endpoint hardening - Building/validating incident response playbooks for BYOVD scenarios
Keywords: BYOVD, vulnerable driver, signed driver, Windows kernel, kernel-mode, IOCTL, device driver, process termination, EDR bypass, tamper protection, PsLookupProcessByProcessId, EPROCESS, driver service, SCM, NtLoadDriver, Windows internals, anti-malware, endpoint security, red team tooling

entry.title: goLoL
entry.summary:
Title: goLoL — Windows LOLBAS + LOLDrivers Host Scanner
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): goLoL is a Windows host enumeration tool that pulls live LOLBAS and LOLDrivers catalogs and correlates them with what exists on a target machine. In LOLBAS mode, it resolves documented binary paths to local locations (e.g., %WINDIR%, %ProgramFiles%, WindowsApps), checks presence on disk, and lists techniques that are runnable at the current privilege tier (user/admin/SYSTEM) with MITRE ATT&CK mappings. In LOLDrivers mode, it hashes local `.sys` files and matches them against the LOLDrivers JSON catalog to identify known vulnerable or malicious drivers. It supports sorting/grouping by binary, privilege tier, or ATT&CK technique and includes a plain ASCII output mode for unstable terminals. It’s useful for red teams/pentesters doing rapid on-host tradecraft discovery and for defenders validating exposure to LOLBins and risky drivers. The project explicitly notes it is not OPSEC-safe and requires network access to fetch catalogs each run.
Technical Focus: - Live ingestion/parsing of LOLBAS and LOLDrivers JSON catalogs - Privilege-context detection (standard user vs local admin group vs SYSTEM token) - Local path resolution and on-disk binary presence checks - MITRE ATT&CK technique ID to name mapping and reporting - Driver inventory hashing and hash-based matching against known-bad/vulnerable sets - Terminal output modes (interactive vs ASCII/plain)
Use Cases: - Identify available LOLBAS binaries and applicable techniques on a compromised/assessed Windows host - Quickly scope what actions are feasible at current privilege level (user/admin/SYSTEM) - Detect presence of known vulnerable/malicious drivers that could enable kernel exploitation or EDR bypass chains - Generate host-specific “living off the land” execution options mapped to ATT&CK for reporting - Triage endpoints for LOLBin exposure and risky driver footprint during incident response or hardening
Keywords: goLoL, LOLBAS, LOLDrivers, Windows, living-off-the-land, LOLBins, .sys drivers, driver hashing, JSON catalog, MITRE ATT&CK, privilege detection, SYSTEM token, local Administrators group, on-disk enumeration, path resolution, WindowsApps, Go, endpoint triage, technique mapping, host scanner

entry.title: PhantomKiller
entry.summary:
Title: PhantomKiller — Weaponizing Lenovo BootRepair.sys to Terminate Protected Processes (BYOVD)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): PhantomKiller is a Windows BYOVD proof-of-concept that abuses a legitimately signed Lenovo kernel driver (BootRepair.sys) to terminate arbitrary processes, including PPL-protected AV/EDR. The driver exposes an unrestricted device object (`\\.\BootRepair`) and an IOCTL (`0x222014`) that accepts a PID and calls `ZwTerminateProcess` without access checks or caller validation. This enables low-privileged users to kill high-privilege/protected security processes if the driver is already loaded, or attackers can load it themselves via `sc.exe` and then disable defenses. The repo includes a compiled driver/PoC and references a full reverse-engineering write-up. It’s primarily useful for red teams and offensive security researchers studying driver abuse and EDR tampering, and for defenders to understand and detect BYOVD-based process termination.
Technical Focus: - BYOVD (Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver) using a signed vendor driver - Windows kernel driver IOCTL abuse (`IRP_MJ_DEVICE_CONTROL`) - Insecure device object / missing DACL on `\\.\Device` interface - Kernel process termination chain: `PsLookupProcessByProcessId` → `ObOpenObjectByPointer` → `ZwTerminateProcess` - Killing PPL-protected processes (EDR/AV tampering) - Driver loading via Service Control Manager (`sc.exe`, type=kernel)
Use Cases: - Terminate EDR/AV processes prior to post-exploitation tooling deployment - Validate/benchmark EDR self-protection against kernel-assisted termination - Research and reproduce insecure IOCTL / device ACL patterns in third-party drivers - Develop detections for suspicious driver loads and IOCTL-based process killing
Keywords: BYOVD, BootRepair.sys, Lenovo PC Manager, signed driver abuse, kernel driver, IOCTL 0x222014, \\.\BootRepair, IRP_MJ_DEVICE_CONTROL, IRP_MJ_CREATE, DACL, ZwTerminateProcess, PsLookupProcessByProcessId, ObOpenObjectByPointer, PPL, EDR tampering, AV bypass, sc.exe, Windows x64, process termination, driver exploitation

entry.title: DrvEye
entry.summary:
Title: DrvEye (drivertool) — Static analysis & exploitation-triage toolkit for Windows kernel drivers
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): DrvEye is a Python-based static analysis toolkit that rapidly assesses Windows kernel driver (.sys) binaries for both loadability and exploitability. It models real Windows enforcement scenarios (Authenticode validation, WDAC policy, Secure Boot/DSE, HVCI/Memory Integrity, S Mode) to produce a per-configuration “will it load” verdict. On the exploitation side, it discovers IOCTL surfaces via multiple recovery methods, performs taint/slicing-based analysis to classify exploit primitives and bug classes, and recovers device names/symlinks (including via optional Unicorn emulation of DriverEntry). The tool can generate artifacts such as compilable DeviceIoControl PoCs, fuzzing harnesses, runtime IOCTL tracers, PowerShell load/check scripts, IDAPython annotation scripts, and JSON reports. It’s primarily useful for BYOVD hunters, kernel reverse engineers, and security researchers triaging large driver sets quickly, and for defenders validating whether a driver is practically usable in modern Windows configurations.
Technical Focus: - Authenticode/PKCS#7 verification and Windows kernel trust chain modeling - WDAC and vulnerable-driver blocklist ingestion (authroot/disallowedcert/SiPolicy) - IOCTL discovery (dispatch table, WDF emulation, brute-force scanning) and hash-dispatch reversal - Static taint analysis, backward slicing, constant propagation, and gate detection - Exploit primitive and bug-class classification for driver IOCTL handlers - Device object / symbolic link name recovery (static heuristics + Unicorn emulation)
Use Cases: - Triage suspected BYOVD drivers to see if they load under HVCI/WDAC/Secure Boot - Enumerate and prioritize risky IOCTL handlers and reachable privileged operations - Generate quick PoCs/fuzzers to validate suspected arbitrary R/W, token theft, callback tampering, etc. - Produce IDA Pro annotations to accelerate kernel driver reverse engineering - Build structured JSON reports for pipeline/batch analysis of driver corpora
Keywords: Windows kernel driver, BYOVD, Authenticode, PKCS#7, WDAC, HVCI, Memory Integrity, Secure Boot, DSE, S Mode, IOCTL, IRP_MJ_DEVICE_CONTROL, DeviceIoControl, taint analysis, backward slicing, WDF, Unicorn emulation, IDAPython, LOLDrivers, SiPolicy_Enforced.p7b

entry.title: byovd kernel driver hardware primitives.html
entry.summary:
Title: BYOVD: Reverse Engineering a Signed Kernel Driver with 13 Hardware Access Primitives
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This write-up reverse engineers Gigabyte APP Center’s signed Windows kernel driver `gdrv3.sys` and documents 13 IOCTL handlers that expose powerful hardware/kernel primitives. - It shows how BYOVD (Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver) enables post-exploitation kernel access to bypass user-mode protections like Protected Process Light (PPL) used by EDRs. - The driver provides unauthenticated arbitrary physical memory read/write and physical memory mapping, plus “authenticated” primitives (I/O ports, MSR R/W, kernel memcpy, VA→PA translation) guarded by an AES-128-CBC + checksum scheme. - The crypto gate is effectively broken because the AES key is hardcoded in the binary (`GIGABYTEPASSWORD`), making protected IOCTLs accessible to any attacker who can open the device. - The post details a repeatable methodology for hunting such drivers (import triage, locating IRP_MJ_DEVICE_CONTROL dispatch, enumerating IOCTLs, reversing buffer formats) and demonstrates chaining primitives for kernel takeover (e.g., WRMSR to LSTAR) and stealthy credential access. - Useful for red teamers, pentesters, malware developers, and defenders building driver allowlisting/detections around dangerous IOCTL surfaces.
Technical Focus: - BYOVD and PPL bypass via signed vulnerable drivers - IOCTL attack surface analysis (IRP_MJ_DEVICE_CONTROL dispatch reversing) - Arbitrary physical memory read/write and `\Device\PhysicalMemory` mapping - MSR read/write abuse (e.g., LSTAR/SYSENTER redirection) - Kernel arbitrary memcpy / write-what-where primitives - Weak “authentication” design: AES-128-CBC + checksum with hardcoded key
Use Cases: - Identify and reverse engineer signed drivers exposing dangerous IOCTLs - Build BYOVD chains to disable/blind EDR components from kernel mode - Achieve kernel execution by redirecting syscall entry (LSTAR) via WRMSR - Read/modify protected process memory (e.g., LSASS) via physical memory primitives - Defensive auditing: detect/block vulnerable drivers and monitor suspicious DeviceIoControl usage
Keywords: BYOVD, gdrv3.sys, Gigabyte APP Center, Windows kernel driver, IOCTL, IRP_MJ_DEVICE_CONTROL, DeviceIoControl, Protected Process Light, PPL, EDR bypass, MmMapIoSpace, ZwMapViewOfSection, \Device\PhysicalMemory, MmGetPhysicalAddress, RDMSR, WRMSR, LSTAR, SYSENTER, kernel memcpy, ObRegisterCallbacks

entry.title: WerWolf
entry.summary:
Title: WerWolf — In-memory BOF implementation of Silent Process Exit LSASS dumping
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): WerWolf is a Windows credential-dumping tool that triggers LSASS minidump creation using the Silent Process Exit technique via `RtlReportSilentProcessExit` (popularized at DEF CON 30). Its main contribution is delivery: the technique is executed as an in-memory Beacon Object File (BOF) loaded by a custom Python COFF loader, avoiding a dropped EXE and avoiding PowerShell-based artifacts (script block logging/AMSI). The dump is written by WerFault/WER to `C:\Windows\Temp\` and can be parsed offline (e.g., with pypykatz). The repo includes a COFF parser/loader in Python and a precompiled BOF (`wer_execute.o`) plus source. It’s primarily useful for red teams and pentesters evaluating EDR visibility around WER-based dumping and in-memory tradecraft.
Technical Focus: - Silent Process Exit / Windows Error Reporting (WER) LSASS dumping - `RtlReportSilentProcessExit` invocation and WerFault dump generation - In-memory BOF execution via custom COFF loader (Python ctypes) - Registry modifications required for Silent Process Exit behavior - Operational considerations around async dump creation and file polling
Use Cases: - Dump LSASS via WER/Silent Process Exit without dropping a standalone executable - Avoid PowerShell-based implementations to reduce script logging/AMSI exposure - Red-team tradecraft testing of EDR detections around WerFault/WER dump activity - Offline credential extraction from generated minidumps (e.g., pypykatz) - Researching COFF/BOF loading and in-memory execution patterns on Windows
Keywords: WerWolf, Silent Process Exit, LSASS dump, RtlReportSilentProcessExit, Windows Error Reporting, WER, WerFault.exe, minidump, BOF, Beacon Object File, COFF loader, ctypes, in-memory execution, registry modification, T1003.001, T1112, T1218, pypykatz, Windows 10, Windows Server 2016+

entry.title: fixing mimikatz sekurlsa logonpasswords on windows 11 24h2 25h2 253e82866197
entry.summary:
Title: Fixing Mimikatz sekurlsa::logonpasswords on Windows 11 24H2/25H2
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post explains why stock Mimikatz `sekurlsa::logonpasswords` fails on Windows 11 24H2/25H2 (builds 26100/26200) with “Logon list” and/or blank output, and how to patch it. - The root causes are (1) missing lsasrv.dll byte signatures for the new build, (2) a compiler change from RIP-relative to base-relative addressing when referencing `LogonSessionList`, and (3) internal structure layout changes that shift session and credential field offsets. - It walks through deriving new patterns, validating symbol addresses in WinDbg, and debugging Mimikatz by adding diagnostic prints to pinpoint where `ReadProcessMemory` fails due to an out-of-module resolved pointer. - Two fixes are proposed: adjust the address resolution formula to handle base-relative displacement (`module_base + disp`) or switch to an alternate signature in a function that still uses RIP-relative addressing. - Finally, it updates the MSV logon session and primary credential struct helpers to correct username/domain parsing and NTLM hash extraction. - Useful for red teamers and tool developers maintaining LSASS credential extraction tooling across Windows build changes; also relevant to defenders for understanding how signature-based LSASS scraping adapts to OS/compiler shifts.
Technical Focus: - Mimikatz lsasrv.dll signature/pattern scanning (LsaSrvReferences[]) - RIP-relative vs base-relative addressing in x64 (`lea [rip+disp]` vs `lea [reg+disp]`) - LSASS memory reading and pointer resolution (`ReadProcessMemory`, module base/size) - Windows logon session enumeration (`LogonSessionList`, `LogonSessionListCount`) - Internal struct/offset drift (KIWI_MSV1_0_LIST_* and MSV1_0_PRIMARY_CREDENTIAL_*) - WinDbg-assisted reverse engineering and validation
Use Cases: - Updating Mimikatz forks to support Windows 11 24H2/25H2 credential extraction - Building resilient signature strategies for locating LSASS globals across builds - Troubleshooting “Logon list” / empty output issues in LSASS scrapers - Researching how compiler/codegen changes break offensive tooling assumptions - Creating detections around lsasrv.dll pattern scanning and LSASS enumeration behavior
Keywords: Mimikatz, sekurlsa::logonpasswords, Windows 11 24H2, Windows 11 25H2, build 26100, build 26200, lsass.exe, lsasrv.dll, LogonSessionList, LogonSessionListCount, pattern scanning, byte signatures, RIP-relative addressing, base-relative addressing, LEA instruction, ReadProcessMemory, SeDebugPrivilege, WinDbg, MSV1_0_PRIMARY_CREDENTIAL, NTLM hash, KIWI_MSV1_0_LIST, struct offsets, Protected Process Light, RunAsPPL, Credential Guard, LsaIso.exe

entry.title: BYOVD and Looting LSASS in the Modern EDR Era
entry.summary:
Title: BYOVD and Looting LSASS in the Modern EDR Era
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post walks through a modern Windows credential-theft chain that uses BYOVD (Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver) to gain kernel read/write primitives and disable LSASS Protected Process Light (PPL). - It focuses on selecting a signed-but-not-blocklisted vulnerable driver (PDFWKRNL.sys / PdFwKrnl) and reversing its IOCTL handling to reach an arbitrary memmove-based read/write primitive (IOCTL 0x80002014). - With kernel R/W, the author locates LSASS’s `_EPROCESS` and clears the `Protection` byte to remove PPL, enabling subsequent dumping. - The dumping stage uses process cloning via `NtCreateProcessEx`, in-memory interception of `MiniDumpWriteDump` output via callbacks (writing to a `NUL` handle), and XOR obfuscation before writing the dump to disk to reduce common EDR detections. - It includes code snippets for the exploit harness, the dump tool, and a simple XOR decryptor, plus a defensive note recommending WDAC/CI policies to block the driver hash/publisher. - Useful primarily for red teams/pentesters and malware developers; secondarily for defenders validating BYOVD + LSASS dump detection/mitigation gaps.
Technical Focus: - BYOVD driver selection vs Microsoft Vulnerable Driver Blocklist / HVCI considerations - Reversing Windows driver IOCTL dispatch and CTL_CODE structure - Arbitrary kernel memory read/write via unsafe IOCTL (memmove primitive) - PPL bypass by modifying LSASS `_EPROCESS` `Protection` field - LSASS dumping evasion: `NtCreateProcessEx` cloning + `MiniDumpWriteDump` callback interception + `NUL` handle trick - Simple dump obfuscation (XOR) to evade signature-based scanning
Use Cases: - Disable LSASS PPL on modern Windows using a vulnerable signed driver (BYOVD) - Build a kernel R/W exploit harness around a known-bad IOCTL interface - Dump LSASS with reduced file/handle telemetry using cloning + in-memory minidump callbacks - Test/validate defensive controls: WDAC driver deny rules, blocklist coverage, EDR detections for cloning/minidump callbacks - Create detection engineering scenarios around vulnerable driver loading and LSASS protection tampering
Keywords: BYOVD, PDFWKRNL.sys, PdFwKrnl, IOCTL 0x80002014, CTL_CODE, arbitrary kernel read write, memmove primitive, DeviceIoControl, ntoskrnl.exe, PsInitialSystemProcess, _EPROCESS, ActiveProcessLinks, UniqueProcessId, Protection byte, PPL, LSASS, NtCreateProcessEx, process cloning, MiniDumpWriteDump, MINIDUMP_CALLBACK_ROUTINE, NUL device, XOR obfuscation, WDAC, Code Integrity, Microsoft Vulnerable Driver Blocklist, HVCI, KDU, loldrivers.io
