entry.title: CredsHunter
entry.summary:
Title: CredsHunter — CVE-2026-3609 XIGNCODE3 xhunter1.sys PPL-bypass LSASS credential dump PoC
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - CredsHunter is a Rust proof-of-concept that dumps LSASS credentials by abusing a handle-leak vulnerability in Wellbia’s XIGNCODE3 anti-cheat driver `xhunter1.sys` (CVE-2026-3609). - The driver’s `IRP_MJ_WRITE` command interface (cmd 785) calls `ObOpenObjectByPointer` with `AccessMode=KernelMode` and without `OBJ_KERNEL_HANDLE`, resulting in a kernel-minted `PROCESS_ALL_ACCESS` handle being placed into the attacker’s user-mode handle table, bypassing PPL protections. - With that handle, the tool reads LSASS memory (via `ReadProcessMemory`, with a fallback to a driver-assisted attach+memcpy primitive) and extracts LSA cryptographic material from `lsasrv.dll`. - It pattern-scans `lsasrv.dll` to locate AES/3DES key material and IV, then decrypts entries from `LogonSessionList` to recover NTLM and SHA1 hashes for active logon sessions; WDigest plaintext recovery is included as best-effort. - The repo bundles a vulnerable signed `xhunter1.sys`, making it a reproducible BYOVD-style primitive where driver loading is possible. - Useful for red teams/pentesters validating kernel-assisted PPL bypass and for defenders researching detection opportunities around vulnerable driver loading and anomalous handle creation.
Technical Focus: - BYOVD via signed vulnerable driver (XIGNCODE3 `xhunter1.sys`) - PPL bypass through kernel-mode process handle acquisition (`ObOpenObjectByPointer`, `AccessMode=KernelMode`) - Windows driver IOCTL/IRP write command interface abuse (cmd 785/787) - LSASS memory parsing: `lsasrv.dll` pattern scanning and key extraction - LSA protected memory / BCrypt key structures and 3DES decryption - `LogonSessionList` walking and MSV1_0 credential blob offsets (per-build)
Use Cases: - Reproducing CVE-2026-3609 to obtain LSASS access on PPL-protected systems - Testing EDR/blue-team detections for vulnerable driver load + suspicious handle grants - Researching kernel-to-user handle leakage and access-check bypass patterns - Building alternative memory readers by reusing the `MemReader` abstraction (e.g., BOF/agent integration) - Validating credential extraction logic (LSA keys, logon session parsing) across Windows builds
Keywords: CVE-2026-3609, XIGNCODE3, Wellbia, xhunter1.sys, BYOVD, LSASS, PPL bypass, ObOpenObjectByPointer, AccessMode KernelMode, OBJ_KERNEL_HANDLE, IRP_MJ_WRITE, PROCESS_ALL_ACCESS, ReadProcessMemory, KeStackAttachProcess, lsasrv.dll, LogonSessionList, MSV1_0, BCrypt, 3DES, WDigest

entry.title: catching credential guard off guard
entry.summary:
Title: Catching Credential Guard Off Guard
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post presents new credential extraction techniques that remain viable on fully patched Windows 11 and Windows Server 2025 systems with modern protections like Credential Guard (VBS/IUM) enabled. It focuses on bypassing the traditional “dump LSASS” approach by interacting with Credential Guard indirectly via Remote Credential Guard (RCG) and the Terminal Services/CredSSP authentication stack. The author reverse engineers and reimplements parts of the Terminal Services SSP (TsPkg.dll), and later the Negotiate SSP and Kerberos SSP, to gain the necessary security context for encrypting/decrypting CredSSP/RCG payloads and invoking KerbCredIsoRemote/NtlmCredIsoRemote interfaces. A key outcome is the ability to obtain chosen-challenge NTLMv1 responses (notably for the static challenge 1122334455667788) even when Microsoft documentation suggests Credential Guard should prevent this, enabling fast NT-hash recovery via rainbow tables and subsequent PtH/Kerberos key abuse. The technique can work unprivileged for the current user given an SPN-bearing account (e.g., a machine account created via Powermad), and can be extended with SYSTEM to target other logged-on sessions via token impersonation or via MSV1_0 package calls on RDP servers hosting RCG sessions. It includes defensive notes (limited visibility; suggests monitoring LsaCallAuthenticationPackage and NTLMv1 challenge patterns) and links to a PoC tool (“DumpGuard”).
Technical Focus: - Windows Credential Guard internals (VBS, Secure Kernel, IUM/lsaiso.exe, CredIso interfaces) - SSPI/LSA interaction (AcquireCredentialsHandle, InitializeSecurityContext, AcceptSecurityContext, LsaCallAuthenticationPackage) - Remote Credential Guard (MS-RDPEAR) and CredSSP (MS-CSSP) protocol mechanics - Reverse engineering/reimplementation of TsPkg (Terminal Services SSP), Negotiate SSP, Kerberos SSP - NTLMv1 chosen-challenge response generation and NT-hash recovery (rainbow tables) - Kerberos ticket handling under Credential Guard (TGT protection vs usable TGS)
Use Cases: - Extract crackable credential material on Credential Guard–protected endpoints without LSASS dumping - Generate NTLMv1 responses for targeted cracking/NT-hash recovery (e.g., static challenge lookups) - Post-exploitation lateral movement via recovered NT hash (PtH) and Kerberos RC4 key usage - Red team tradecraft for operating in environments with PPL + EDR LSASS protections - Defensive research: build detections around LSA package calls and anomalous NTLMv1 challenge usage
Keywords: Credential Guard, VBS, Secure Kernel, Isolated User Mode, lsaiso.exe, lsass.exe, SSPI, LSA, LsaCallAuthenticationPackage, TsPkg.dll, CredSSP, MS-CSSP, Remote Credential Guard, MS-RDPEAR, KerbCredIsoRemote, NtlmCredIsoRemote, Kerberos, NTLMv1, MSV1_0, AcquireCredentialsHandle, InitializeSecurityContext, AcceptSecurityContext, EncryptMessage, DecryptMessage, ALPC, RPC type serialization, SPN, machine account, Powermad, Pass-the-Hash

entry.title: HashDump BypassEDR
entry.summary:
Title: HashDump-BypassEDR
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository documents a Windows hash-dumping workflow designed to bypass common EDR interception points by leveraging the signed/“living off the land” utility `reg.exe` to export sensitive registry hives. - The approach avoids typical LSASS dumping telemetry by exfiltrating `SAM`, `SYSTEM`, and `SECURITY` data as `.reg` exports, then reconstructing the binary hive files offline using a provided PowerShell script (`RegReduction.ps1`). - It includes a small C tool (`BootKey.c` → `BootKey.exe`) to extract the system BootKey, which is then used with Impacket `secretsdump.py` to recover local account hashes. - The README highlights privilege nuances across Windows versions (e.g., `reg.exe export` requiring SYSTEM on Win10/11 and Server 2025, but often only admin on Server 2022 and below) and notes BootKey extraction may not require admin. - Useful primarily for red teams/pentesters performing credential access in environments where direct LSASS dumping is heavily monitored, and for defenders to understand an alternate credential theft path.
Technical Focus: - Registry hive export via `reg.exe` (SAM/SYSTEM/SECURITY) - Offline reconstruction of hive binaries from `.reg` exports (PowerShell) - BootKey (SysKey) extraction logic from SYSTEM hive/registry - Offline secrets extraction with Impacket `secretsdump` - EDR bypass via LOLBin/task decorrelation (avoiding LSASS dump hooks)
Use Cases: - Dump local Windows password hashes without creating LSASS dump artifacts - Perform offline credential extraction from exported registry data - Validate EDR detection gaps around `reg.exe export` and hive access - Red team tradecraft for constrained/monitored endpoints - Blue team threat modeling and detection engineering for hive export + exfil chains

entry.title: wsl com hooking rtti 3abbf873d61f
entry.summary:
Title: WSL, COM Hooking, & RTTI
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - The post explains how to obtain reliable telemetry for WSL process creation by hooking the WSL COM server (wslservice.exe), specifically the `ILxssUserSession::CreateLxProcess` method. - It addresses the problem that wslservice.exe ships without symbols and that ETW/TraceLogging providers don’t reliably expose WSL2 activity, making traditional monitoring difficult. - The author uses MSVC Run-Time Type Information (RTTI) metadata embedded in the binary to locate the COM interface vtable at runtime (TypeDescriptor → Complete Object Locator → vtable), then patches the target vtable slot. - It highlights that hooking `CreateLxProcess` captures both WSL1 and WSL2 flows because the COM interface is shared, even though the backend differs (Pico provider vs Hyper-V). - A POC DLL (`WslHook.dll`) logs client PID/process/SID, command line args, distro/instance IDs, and handles, demonstrating visibility into WSL execution without relying on `wsl.exe` telemetry. - Useful for red teams and defenders/researchers needing WSL execution visibility, and for reverse engineers dealing with symbol-less COM servers.
Technical Focus: - WSL COM architecture (client → wslservice.exe → backend dispatch) - COM interface vtable hooking (`ILxssUserSession::CreateLxProcess`) - MSVC RTTI structures (`_RTTITypeDescriptor`, `_RTTICompleteObjectLocator`) - Binary scanning using RVAs to resolve vtables without symbols - Vtable slot/offset validation across IDL versions and builds - Telemetry/logging via `OutputDebugStringW` (with ETW suggested for production)
Use Cases: - Detect/monitor WSL command execution that bypasses launching `wsl.exe` - Build EDR-style user-mode sensors for WSL activity in wslservice.exe - Reverse engineer and hook COM methods in symbol-less Windows binaries - Validate/triage offensive tradecraft leveraging `CreateLxProcess` for stealthy WSL execution - Research WSL1 vs WSL2 execution paths and parameter marshaling
Keywords: WSL, WSL2, WSL1, wslservice.exe, wsl.exe, COM, ILxssUserSession, CreateLxProcess, vtable hooking, RTTI, MSVC, _RTTITypeDescriptor, _RTTICompleteObjectLocator, RVA, IDA Pro, reverse engineering, HvSocket, Hyper-V, TraceLogging, ETW, OutputDebugStringW, Detours

entry.title: reflective dll
entry.summary:
Title: Reflective DLL Injection: Theory & Practice
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post explains reflective DLL loading/injection: manually mapping a PE image into memory without writing it to disk or using `LoadLibrary`, thereby bypassing common module-load telemetry (image load callbacks, PEB loader lists, file-backed scanning). It walks through a full reflective loader implementation: locating the in-memory base, resolving required APIs via PEB/Ldr export parsing and hashing, allocating `SizeOfImage`, copying headers/sections, applying relocations, resolving the IAT, setting final section protections, flushing the instruction cache, and calling `DllMain`. It highlights what simplistic loaders often skip (TLS callbacks, x64 exception handling via `RtlAddFunctionTable`, delay-load imports) and why those omissions break real-world payloads. The author also covers modern OPSEC improvements such as RW→RX memory transitions, PE header erasure, indirect syscalls to evade user-mode hooks, and module stomping/Mockingjay-style approaches to avoid private executable memory. A detection section maps these behaviors to practical signals defenders can hunt (thread start addresses in private memory, RW→RX flips after `WriteProcessMemory`, mismatches between module lists and memory maps, ETW TI events). Useful for red team tool authors/malware developers building custom loaders and for blue teams designing detections for in-memory PE execution.
Technical Focus: - Manual PE mapping (headers/sections, `SizeOfImage`, `SizeOfHeaders`) - Base relocations (`IMAGE_BASE_RELOCATION`, DIR64/HIGHLOW) - Import resolution (IAT/Thunk walking, `LoadLibraryA`/`GetProcAddress`) - PEB/Ldr module enumeration and export hashing (e.g., ROR13) - Memory protection strategy (RW→RX, per-section `VirtualProtect`, `NtFlushInstructionCache`) - Evasion/detection tradeoffs (indirect syscalls, header stomping, module stomping, ETW/EDR telemetry)
Use Cases: - Implementing a reflective loader for in-memory DLL execution in implants/C2 agents - Hardening loaders to reduce EDR visibility (avoid RWX, scrub headers, indirect syscalls) - Building detections for reflective injection (thread start in private RX, RW→RX flips, module list vs VAD discrepancies) - Malware analysis/reverse engineering of common reflective loader patterns used by frameworks
Keywords: reflective DLL injection, reflective loading, manual mapping, PE loader, PEB, Ldr, InMemoryOrderModuleList, export hashing, ROR13, IMAGE_BASE_RELOCATION, IAT, OriginalFirstThunk, VirtualAlloc, VirtualProtect, NtFlushInstructionCache, NtCreateThreadEx, CreateRemoteThread, WriteProcessMemory, indirect syscalls, SysWhispers3, Hell’s Hall, TartarusGate, module stomping, Process Mockingjay, TLS callbacks, RtlAddFunctionTable, ETW Threat Intelligence, PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine, Sysmon Event ID 7, unbacked executable memory

entry.title: userland edr bypass stack
entry.summary:
Title: The Userland EDR Bypass Stack: Unhooking, Syscalls, ETW/AMSI, and Kernel Callbacks
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post lays out a layered “stack” for evading modern Windows EDR visibility from user mode, explaining why single techniques (e.g., direct syscalls) are insufficient. It covers restoring a clean `ntdll.dll` to remove inline hooks, then dynamically resolving syscall numbers at runtime (Hell’s Gate / Halo’s Gate / Tartarus’ Gate) to stay portable across Windows builds. It explains indirect syscalls (jumping to an `ntdll` `syscall; ret` gadget) to avoid kernel-side detections that flag syscalls originating outside `ntdll`. It then describes patching AMSI (`AmsiScanBuffer`) and ETW (`EtwEventWrite`) to suppress script/content scanning and telemetry, including ordering/OPSEC considerations and mention of HWBP-based alternatives when patches are re-checked. Finally, it discusses the “nuclear option” of removing kernel callbacks via BYOVD-provided kernel R/W to blind process/thread/image/handle telemetry, and closes with defender detection angles for each layer. Useful primarily for red teams, implant/tooling developers, and detection engineers validating coverage.
Technical Focus: - Userland `ntdll.dll` unhooking (.text restoration) via disk mapping, KnownDlls, or suspended donor process - Runtime syscall number (SSN) discovery: Hell’s Gate, Halo’s Gate, Tartarus’ Gate - Indirect syscalls using `ntdll` `syscall; ret` gadgets to blend return addresses - AMSI bypass by patching `AmsiScanBuffer` and related OPSEC/ordering - ETW suppression by patching `EtwEventWrite` and telemetry implications - Kernel callback removal via BYOVD and callback array manipulation (process/thread/image/Ob callbacks)
Use Cases: - Building a Windows loader/implant initialization routine that reduces userland EDR telemetry - Making syscall-based tooling resilient across Windows versions without hardcoded SSNs - Avoiding detections based on syscall return address / “syscall from non-ntdll” heuristics - Silencing AMSI/ETW prior to in-process PowerShell/.NET execution - Evaluating blue-team detections for KnownDlls mapping, patch integrity, ETW dropouts, and driver-load/BYOVD activity
Keywords: EDR evasion, userland hooks, ntdll unhooking, inline hooking, KnownDlls, NtOpenSection, NtMapViewOfSection, Hell’s Gate, Halo’s Gate, Tartarus’ Gate, syscall stub, SSN resolution, indirect syscalls, syscall gadget, AMSI, AmsiScanBuffer, ETW, EtwEventWrite, BYOVD, vulnerable signed driver, ObRegisterCallbacks, PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutineEx, PatchGuard, HVCI, VBS, Windows 11 24H2, kernel callbacks

entry.title: c2 stack build guide
entry.summary:
Title: Building a C2 Stack: Implants, BOF Loaders, Redirectors, and DoH Channels
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post is an end-to-end build guide for a modular offensive C2 “stack” composed of a minimal Rust implant, a custom COFF/BOF loader, resilient redirector infrastructure, and a DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH) fallback channel. It focuses on understanding and re-implementing the core components so operators can adapt when commercial frameworks get signatured or blocked. Techniques covered include beacon loop design with jitter, implant OPSEC hardening (string encryption, PPID spoofing, TLS pinning), COFF parsing and relocation handling for BOF execution, and redirector patterns using Nginx plus optional domain fronting. The DoH channel section explains tasking/exfil via TXT/A queries tunneled inside HTTPS to public resolvers and discusses throughput limits and detection considerations. It’s primarily useful for red teamers and C2/implant developers, with some value to blue teams for understanding infrastructure and DoH-based C2 tradecraft. The key takeaway is treating C2 as replaceable layers (agent, execution extension, infra, transport) to improve resilience under active defense.
Technical Focus: - Rust beacon/implant architecture (state machine, jitter, HTTP tasking) - Implant OPSEC: compile-time string obfuscation, PPID spoofing, TLS pinning, UA rotation - BOF execution via COFF loading: section mapping, symbol resolution, relocations, `go` entrypoint - Beacon API emulation/dispatch (`BeaconPrintf`, `BeaconDataParse`, etc.) - Redirector design with Nginx reverse proxy, URI/UA filtering, VPN-isolated teamserver - DNS-over-HTTPS C2: TXT tasking, label-chunked exfil, resolver behavior, detection signals
Use Cases: - Build a minimal custom beacon for controlled red team operations - Add post-exploitation capabilities without recompiling by executing BOFs from a non-CS framework - Deploy disposable redirectors to protect teamserver origin and reduce burn impact - Implement a DoH-based fallback channel when direct HTTPS C2 is blocked or too noisy - Create infrastructure filtering rules to prevent scanning/fingerprinting from reaching the origin
Keywords: C2, command-and-control, Rust implant, beacon, jitter, OPSEC, TLS pinning, PPID spoofing, string encryption, BOF, Beacon Object File, COFF, relocations, VirtualAlloc, GetProcAddress, LoadLibraryA, BeaconPrintf, Nginx redirector, reverse proxy, domain fronting, CDN, DNS-over-HTTPS, DoH, TXT record, authoritative nameserver, exfiltration, Cloudflare DNS, teamserver, VPN-only origin

entry.title: hardware breakpoint hooking
entry.summary:
Title: Hardware Breakpoint Hooking: Bypassing Inline EDR Hooks Without Touching Memory
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post explains how to implement user-mode API “hooking” on Windows using x86-64 hardware breakpoints (DR0–DR7) instead of inline patching in `ntdll.dll`/`kernelbase.dll`. It targets the problem of EDR inline-hook integrity checks and telemetry triggered by memory modifications (e.g., `VirtualProtect`, `WriteProcessMemory`) by redirecting execution via `EXCEPTION_SINGLE_STEP` without changing code bytes. The guide walks through the debug register model, installing per-thread breakpoints via `GetThreadContext`/`SetThreadContext`, and handling breakpoints with a vectored exception handler (VEH) that edits the thread context and uses the Resume Flag (RF) to avoid re-trigger loops. It includes a minimal PoC that blocks remote thread injection by intercepting `NtCreateThreadEx` and forcing `STATUS_ACCESS_DENIED` or skipping the function body. The author also discusses detection vectors (DR register inspection, ETW single-step visibility, and anomalous `SetThreadContext`/`NtContinue` usage) and mitigations like self-context installation and APC-based self-install on other threads. Useful for red teams, implant developers, and researchers evaluating stealthy user-mode interception primitives and their defensive telemetry.
Technical Focus: - x86-64 debug registers (DR0–DR7) and DR7 configuration (RW/LEN/L bits) - Vectored Exception Handling (VEH) and `EXCEPTION_SINGLE_STEP` control flow - Thread context manipulation (`GetThreadContext`/`SetThreadContext`, `CONTEXT_DEBUG_REGISTERS`) - EFLAGS Resume Flag (RF) to prevent breakpoint re-entry - EDR detection/telemetry considerations (ETW single-step, DR snooping, context-call frequency) - Hooking/argument tampering for NT syscalls (e.g., `NtOpenProcess`, `NtCreateThreadEx`)
Use Cases: - Bypass or avoid inline-hook integrity checks without modifying module code bytes - Intercept and sanitize sensitive syscall arguments (e.g., strip `PROCESS_VM_WRITE` / `PROCESS_CREATE_THREAD`) - Block or disrupt injection primitives by trapping `NtCreateThreadEx`/`CreateRemoteThread`-style flows - Build a minimal HWBP-based hook engine for research and tradecraft prototyping - Evaluate defensive detections around DR register usage and single-step ETW telemetry
Keywords: hardware breakpoints, DR0, DR1, DR2, DR3, DR6, DR7, CONTEXT_DEBUG_REGISTERS, GetThreadContext, SetThreadContext, AddVectoredExceptionHandler, VEH, EXCEPTION_SINGLE_STEP, EFLAGS RF, NtOpenProcess, NtCreateThreadEx, ETW, Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence, inline hooks, ntdll.dll

entry.title: beacon object files guide
entry.summary:
Title: Beacon Object Files from Scratch: COFF Loading, Dynamic Resolution, and Battle-Tested Tradecraft
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post is a practical guide to writing Cobalt Strike Beacon Object Files (BOFs) from first principles, focusing on how COFF objects are loaded and executed in-process. It explains the BOF loader’s model (section mapping, symbol resolution via naming conventions, relocation patching, calling `go()`, and cleanup) and the constraints that shape BOF development (no CRT, no persistent globals, minimal scope). It walks through a “whoami” BOF using Windows token APIs, then covers argument handling with the BeaconData API and output via Beacon’s callback channels. A key section details dynamic function resolution beyond the `MODULE$Function` pattern using `GetModuleHandleA`/`GetProcAddress`, with OPSEC guidance to avoid plaintext strings in `.rdata`. It also provides “battle-tested” tradecraft around thread safety, handle/memory hygiene, and string obfuscation, culminating in a production-style token duplication/impersonation BOF example plus an Aggressor wrapper. Useful for red teamers and C2/tooling developers building stealthier in-process post-exploitation capabilities and wanting reliable, crash-resistant BOFs.
Technical Focus: - COFF object format basics (sections, symbols, relocations) and BOF loader behavior - Cobalt Strike BOF runtime APIs (BeaconPrintf, BeaconOutput, BeaconDataParse/Extract) - Dynamic import resolution (`MODULE$Function`, `LoadLibrary`/`GetProcAddress`) - Windows access token operations (OpenProcessToken, DuplicateTokenEx, ImpersonateLoggedOnUser) - OPSEC tradecraft: string placement avoidance, obfuscation, memory/handle hygiene, thread lifecycle - BOF debugging workflow (PDBs, bof_runner, local harnessing)
Use Cases: - Build custom in-process post-exploitation actions without spawning new processes or using PowerShell - Implement token theft/impersonation primitives as small composable BOFs - Add dynamic API resolution for ntdll/undocumented calls while reducing static IoCs - Create reliable BOFs that avoid crashes via proper cleanup and thread synchronization - Debug BOFs locally before deployment using bof_runner and instrumented logging

entry.title: implant architecture guide
entry.summary:
Title: Designing a Modern C2 Implant: Architecture and OPSEC
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post outlines key design decisions for building a modern command-and-control (C2) implant with an emphasis on operational security and detection resistance. It compares communication models (HTTPS polling, DNS, WebSockets, and named pipes) and recommends a layered architecture where an external “edge” host beacons out while internal hosts relay via SMB/named pipes. It breaks down the beacon loop (sleep → check-in → tasking → results) and explains why jitter is critical to avoid timing-based detection. The author contrasts in-process execution (BOFs, inline .NET via CLR hosting, reflective DLL loading, direct syscalls) with legacy fork-and-run approaches that generate high telemetry. Anti-forensics topics include compile-time string encryption, API hashing with PEB/export walking, ETW/AMSI patching, and reducing memory indicators commonly used to signature popular beacons. Network OPSEC guidance covers domain fronting, malleable HTTP profiles, certificate/domain hygiene, and safer process injection target selection, plus practical testing against Defender, Falcon, Elastic, and public YARA rules. Useful primarily for red teamers and C2/implant developers designing custom implants and tradecraft.
Technical Focus: - C2 communication models (HTTPS, DNS, WebSockets, named pipes/SMB relays) - Beacon loop design and sleep/jitter evasion - In-process task execution (BOFs, CLR hosting, reflective loading, direct syscalls) - Anti-forensics: string encryption, API hashing, PEB/export resolution - Telemetry suppression: ETW and AMSI patching, memory indicator reduction - Network OPSEC: domain fronting, malleable traffic profiles, certificate management
Use Cases: - Designing a custom implant architecture for constrained/internal networks (egress-less hosts) - Implementing beacon timing/jitter to reduce network periodicity detection - Building in-process execution pipelines to avoid child-process telemetry - Hardening implants against static signatures and memory scanning - Shaping C2 traffic to blend with legitimate SaaS/API patterns - Pre-op testing of payloads against AV/EDR and YARA rule sets
Keywords: C2 implant, beacon loop, jitter, HTTPS polling, DNS C2, WebSockets, named pipes, SMB relay, in-process execution, Beacon Object Files, COFF, CLR hosting, reflective DLL loading, direct syscalls, string encryption, API hashing, PEB walking, ETW patching, AMSI bypass, domain fronting, malleable C2 profile, TLS SNI, certificate rotation, process injection OPSEC, Sysmon Event ID 1, Windows Event 4688, YARA, CrowdStrike Falcon, Windows Defender, Elastic Security

entry.title: anti analysis techniques
entry.summary:
Title: Anti-Analysis Techniques: How Malware Detects Your Sandbox
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post catalogs common anti-analysis techniques used by modern malware/loaders to detect VMs, sandboxes, and debuggers, and to evade automated detonation pipelines. It explains the “signal” each check relies on (registry/WMI artifacts, hardware identifiers, resource baselines, timing anomalies, debugger state) and provides concrete code snippets for implementation. The article also outlines practical countermeasures for analysts, such as scrubbing VM artifacts, hardening VM profiles (cores/RAM/disk/resolution), and using anti-anti-debug tooling. It highlights timing attacks (Sleep acceleration detection, RDTSC/QPC divergence) as a key way to detect sandbox instrumentation and emulation. A realistic “anti-analysis chain” demonstrates how loaders stack many cheap checks and exit early to produce clean sandbox verdicts. Useful for malware analysts and blue teams hardening sandboxes, and for red teams/implant developers selecting evasion tradeoffs.
Technical Focus: - VM detection via registry keys, device IDs, BIOS strings, and guest tools artifacts - WMI-based environment fingerprinting (Win32_ComputerSystem/BIOS/DiskDrive/NetworkAdapter/Process) - Hardware/CPU hypervisor detection (CPUID hypervisor bit, vendor leaf 0x40000000, Red Pill variants) - Sandbox evasion via resource/behavior heuristics (uptime, files, windows, mouse movement, domain join) - Timing attacks and clock-source cross-checks (Sleep acceleration, RDTSC, QPC vs GetTickCount) - Anti-debugging techniques (PEB fields, NtQueryInformationProcess, debug registers DR0–DR3, exception tricks)
Use Cases: - Hardening malware analysis VMs to reduce sandbox fingerprinting - Building detection logic for sandbox/VM-aware malware families - Implementing or testing anti-analysis gates in loaders/implants (controlled research) - Creating lab baselines (user artifacts, uptime, software footprint) to defeat behavioral checks - Developing counter-instrumentation strategies for timing and anti-debug checks
Keywords: anti-analysis, sandbox evasion, VM detection, debugger detection, Windows PEB, BeingDebugged, NtGlobalFlag, NtQueryInformationProcess, ProcessDebugPort, ProcessDebugObjectHandle, ProcessDebugFlags, CPUID, hypervisor bit, RDTSC, QueryPerformanceCounter, GetTickCount64, SIDT, WMI, Win32_ComputerSystem, registry artifacts, hardware breakpoints, DR0-DR3, ScyllaHide, TitanHide

entry.title: reflective dll loader.html
entry.summary:
Title: Reflective DLL Loader (CustomRDI) — rewriting Stephen Fewer’s ReflectiveLoader with modern implementation details
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post walks through a from-scratch implementation of a reflective DLL loader (RDI) that maps and runs a DLL from memory without using `LoadLibrary`. It explains the full inner-loader workflow: locating the DLL base in a raw blob, allocating memory, copying headers/sections, resolving imports (including forwarded exports), applying relocations, setting per-section protections, flushing the instruction cache, and calling `DllMain`. The author highlights design changes versus Fewer’s original loader, including PEB-walking with djb2 API hashing, avoiding `GetProcAddress`/`LoadLibraryA` by parsing exports directly and using `LdrLoadDll` as a fallback, and using indirect syscalls by extracting SSNs and jumping to ntdll’s `syscall` instruction. It also documents practical debugging pitfalls (e.g., `.data` RIP-relative globals and MSVC `/GS` stack cookies breaking when executing from disk-layout bytes) and how they were fixed. The post closes with detection surface considerations and a clear limitations list (no TLS callbacks, no API set schema, no unwind metadata registration, etc.). Useful for red teamers and malware dev learners implementing custom in-memory loaders, and for defenders understanding common RDI artifacts.
Technical Focus: - Reflective DLL injection / in-memory PE loading - PE parsing: sections, imports, relocations, export resolution, forwarded exports - PEB module enumeration (InMemoryOrderModuleList) and API hashing (djb2) - Indirect syscalls (SSN extraction from ntdll stubs, syscall trampolines) - Memory protections via `NtProtectVirtualMemory` (RW → per-section RX/R) - WinDbg-based debugging of position-independence issues in raw PE blobs
Use Cases: - Building a custom reflective loader for payload DLL execution without `LoadLibrary` - Studying/teaching PE loader internals and common implementation pitfalls - Prototyping EDR-evasion-adjacent techniques (hash-based API resolution, indirect syscalls) - Creating test artifacts for defensive detection of in-memory PE mapping - Debugging and validating IAT patching, forwarders, and relocation handling in loaders
Keywords: Reflective DLL injection, ReflectiveLoader, PE format, in-memory loader, PEB walking, InMemoryOrderModuleList, export table parsing, import resolution, IAT patching, forwarded exports, base relocations, IMAGE_REL_BASED_DIR64, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, NtProtectVirtualMemory, NtFlushInstructionCache, LdrLoadDll, indirect syscalls, syscall number (SSN), djb2 hashing, WinDbg, /GS- stack cookies, API set schema

entry.title: edr internals research and bypass
entry.summary:
Title: EDR Tradecraft: Internals, Detection, Evasion & Advanced Research
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - A deep technical walkthrough of modern Windows EDR architecture, focusing on what telemetry sources EDRs use (user-mode hooks, kernel callbacks, minifilters, ETW—including ETW Threat Intelligence—and WFP) and how detections are built from those signals. - It explains how common detection engines (static, dynamic, heuristic, ML) map to concrete Windows primitives like Ps/Ob/Cm callbacks, IRPs, VAD/memory scanning, and ETW provider subscriptions. - The post then catalogs evasion/bypass tradecraft by layer: static/IAT evasion, injection technique selection, direct vs indirect syscalls (FreshyCalls/RecycledGate/SysWhispers4/Acheron/Sysplant), sleep-masking (Ekko/FOLIAGE/Zilean/DreamWalkers), call-stack spoofing (SilentMoonwalk/VulcanRaven), and patchless AMSI/ETW-TI bypass using hardware breakpoints + VEH/NtContinue. - It covers anti-tamper and PPL (Protected Process Light) constraints and the BYOVD landscape under Microsoft’s vulnerable-driver blocklist, including why HVCI/VBS changes feasibility. - Finally, it provides an iterative methodology for EDR reverse engineering (lab setup → lead gathering → instrumentation → IOCTL/IPC mapping → experiments → detection validation), making it useful for both offensive research and defensive detection engineering. - Most useful for red team operators, malware/tooling developers, and defenders doing EDR evaluation or writing high-fidelity detections.
Technical Focus: - Windows EDR component model (service, injected DLL, kernel drivers, minifilter, ETW consumer, WFP callouts) - Kernel telemetry: PsSetCreate* callbacks, ObRegisterCallbacks, CmRegisterCallbackEx, image-load callbacks - ETW internals (sessions/providers/consumers) and ETW-TI constraints (PPL-Antimalware) - Syscall bypass tradecraft (direct/indirect syscalls, SSN resolution: Hell’s/Halo’s/Tartarus’ Gate, FreshyCalls/RecycledGate) - Memory scanning heuristics (VADs, MEM_PRIVATE vs MEM_IMAGE, PEB.Ldr inconsistencies, module stomping) - Evasion primitives: sleep masks, call-stack spoofing, patchless AMSI via HW breakpoints + VEH, BYOVD/PPL bypass
Use Cases: - Reverse engineer an EDR’s drivers/services to identify telemetry sources, IPC endpoints, and IOCTL interfaces - Design red-team payload tradecraft to reduce user-mode hook visibility and behavioral signatures - Build detections for syscall-gate usage, sleep-mask patterns, stack spoofing artifacts, and AMSI/ETW-TI tampering - Evaluate feasibility of BYOVD/PPL bypass given blocklists, HVCI/VBS, and anti-tamper controls - Map EDR detections and bypasses to MITRE ATT&CK for reporting and coverage analysis
Keywords: EDR, Windows internals, kernel driver, minifilter, fltmgr.sys, IRP, IOCTL, ETW, Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence, ETW-TI, AMSI, AmsiScanBuffer, Vectored Exception Handler, hardware breakpoints, NtContinue, PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutineEx, PsSetCreateThreadNotifyRoutine, PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine, ObRegisterCallbacks, CmRegisterCallbackEx, WFP, PPL, Protected Process Light, BYOVD, HVCI, VBS, direct syscalls, indirect syscalls, SysWhispers4, FreshyCalls, RecycledGate, Ekko, SilentMoonwalk, VulcanRaven, DreamWalkers, module stomping, reflective DLL loading, process hollowing, doppelgänging, ghosting, VAD, PEB.Ldr

entry.title: sleep obfuscation techniques
entry.summary:
Title: Sleep Obfuscation Deep Dive: Ekko, Zilean, and Foliage
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post explains “sleep obfuscation” for Windows implants: encrypting an implant’s in-memory code/data during sleep to evade periodic EDR memory scanning and signature/YARA sweeps. It breaks down three implementations—Ekko (timer queue callbacks), Zilean (thread pool timers + memory protection cycling), and Foliage (APC-based execution on the same thread during alertable sleep). The article highlights the core challenge (encryption code must remain runnable while the main region is encrypted) and how each technique solves it via alternate execution contexts. It also covers operational improvements like avoiding RWX by toggling RX↔RW, choosing less-flagged crypto primitives than `SystemFunction032` (RC4), and encrypting relevant stack pages to prevent plaintext artifacts. Detection ideas are included, focusing on suspicious protection flips, timer/APC patterns, and sleeping threads adjacent to encrypted regions. Useful primarily for red teamers/implant developers and blue teams building detections for in-memory beacons.
Technical Focus: - EDR periodic vs event-triggered memory scanning threat model - Timer queue callbacks (`RtlCreateTimer`) and thread pool timers (`CreateThreadpoolTimer`) - APC-based execution with alertable sleep (`QueueUserAPC`, `SleepEx`) - In-place memory encryption and key handling (RC4 `SystemFunction032`, alternatives) - Memory protection transitions (RX→RW→RX via `VirtualProtect`) - Stack encryption considerations and detection heuristics
Use Cases: - Implementing sleep-masking in custom implants/loaders to reduce memory-scan exposure - Hardening beacon-like payloads against periodic YARA/signature sweeps - Designing EDR detections around protection-flip telemetry and timer/APC crypto patterns - Evaluating tradeoffs between timer-based vs APC-based sleep obfuscation in OPSEC reviews
Keywords: sleep obfuscation, sleep masking, EDR evasion, memory scanning, YARA, Ekko, Zilean, Foliage, RtlCreateTimer, CreateThreadpoolTimer, QueueUserAPC, SleepEx, VirtualProtect, PAGE_EXECUTE_READ, PAGE_READWRITE, SystemFunction032, RC4, ntdll, call stack cleanliness, RX RWX detection

entry.title: stack spoofing techniques
entry.summary:
Title: Call Stack Spoofing: Defeating EDR Stack Telemetry
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post explains how modern EDRs use kernel/ETW-based stack walking to detect suspicious API/syscall activity even when userland hooks are bypassed (e.g., via indirect syscalls). It focuses on defeating “unbacked memory” indicators in thread call stacks by making malicious execution appear to originate from legitimate, file-backed modules like kernel32/kernelbase/ntdll. It covers four approaches: simple return address spoofing, full frame spoofing with stack desynchronization/trampolines, synthetic call stacks built from ROP-style stack-adjust gadgets (e.g., SilentMoonwalk), and sleep-time thread stack rewriting (e.g., CallStackMasker) often paired with memory encryption sleep obfuscation (Ekko/Foliage). It also introduces a hardware-breakpoint + VEH method to modify stack context at the moment of sensitive API calls without patching code. The write-up includes common EDR validation checks (RBP chain integrity, “return address preceded by call”, stack gap heuristics, module backing) and countermeasures to keep spoofed stacks plausible. Useful primarily for red teams/implant developers and also for blue teams to understand stack-telemetry evasion patterns and detection opportunities.
Technical Focus: - EDR call stack telemetry (ETW/kernel callbacks) and stack walking - Return address spoofing and trampoline-based control-flow repair - Synthetic stack frame construction using ROP gadgets (add rsp; ret) - Thread stack spoofing during sleep + sleep obfuscation (memory encryption) - Hardware breakpoints (DR0–DR7) with Vectored Exception Handling (VEH) - Anti-detection: RBP chain consistency, call-site validation, frame sizing
Use Cases: - Making indirect syscalls/API usage appear to originate from legitimate modules - Evading “unbacked memory” / suspicious caller heuristics in EDR telemetry - Hardening in-memory implants against hunt-sleeping-beacon style scans - Building selective per-API stack spoofing without inline patching (HW BP + VEH) - Creating defensive detections around inconsistent frame pointers/call-site validation failures
Keywords: call stack spoofing, stack telemetry, EDR evasion, ETW stack walking, kernel callbacks, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, VirtualAlloc, indirect syscalls, unbacked memory, return address spoofing, stack frame spoofing, trampoline, ROP gadgets, SilentMoonwalk, CallStackMasker, sleep obfuscation, Ekko, Foliage, hardware breakpoints, DR0-DR7, VEH, RBP chain validation

entry.title: Puzzle
entry.summary:
Title: Puzzle — PoCs to Abuse Windows Minifilters for Stealthy Malware Drop/Execution
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Puzzle is a collection of proof-of-concepts and utilities that abuse Windows filesystem minifilters to drop and execute payloads in monitored environments with reduced scanning/visibility. It focuses on leveraging minifilter architecture and altitude ordering to bypass or degrade static and some dynamic inspection performed by endpoint security products. The repo includes a malicious Cloud Filter sync engine (CldFlt/Cloud Filter API) to materialize files without typical static analysis, a WOF-based approach (Wof/WOF API) using WIM-backed external data sources, and bindlink tooling (bindflt) to conceal payload content at runtime. An additional component (IdMapper) helps execute binaries hidden behind bindlinks via File Reference Numbers (FRN). It’s primarily useful for red teams and offensive security researchers exploring Windows-native evasion primitives, and for blue teams to understand detection gaps around minifilter-backed file virtualization and bindlinks. The projects are implemented mainly in Rust with supporting Python utilities and are tested on Windows 10/11 and Windows Server.
Technical Focus: - Windows minifilter architecture and filter altitudes - Cloud Filter API (CldFlt) file hydration/sync-provider abuse - Windows Overlay Filter / WOF API (Wof) with WIM external backing - bindflt bindlinks for runtime concealment - FRN (File Reference Number) / NTFS file identity-based access - EDR evasion via reduced static scanning and altered file content visibility
Use Cases: - Dropping payloads to disk while minimizing static AV/EDR scanning opportunities - Hiding executable content behind bindlinks to complicate runtime inspection - Executing concealed binaries by resolving targets via FRN rather than path-based access - Researching and validating detections for Cloud Filter and WOF-backed file behaviors - Building red-team tradecraft around Windows-native file virtualization features
Keywords: Windows minifilter, filter altitude, CldFlt, Cloud Filter API, sync provider, file hydration, Wof, WOF API, wofapi, WIM, external backing store, bindflt, bindlink, NTFS, FRN, File Reference Number, fltmc, static analysis evasion, runtime concealment, Rust tooling

entry.title: the defensive stack is exposed
entry.summary:
Title: The Defensive Stack is Exposed: LLMs, Reverse Engineering, and the End of Opaque Defense
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post argues that LLMs materially reduce the time and expertise needed to reverse engineer endpoint security products (AV/EDR) and other defensive tooling, shifting the advantage toward attackers who can now rapidly map detection logic and blind spots. It highlights that many products share common architectures (local rules, scripted engines, ML classifiers, cloud lookups, exclusions), making “universal” analysis workflows transferable across vendors. The core problem is the collapse of “security through obscurity” assumptions: if decision logic exists on/near the host (rules, policies, models, thresholds), it can be extracted or inferred, and LLMs accelerate that process. The author outlines practical attacker outcomes (rule/model/policy mining, version diffing, gap analysis, and even vulnerability discovery in the defensive product itself). For defenders, the recommendation is to treat EDR as one signal, and invest in layers less dependent on opaque endpoint logic: host hardening (WDAC/AppLocker, ASR, Credential Guard/RunAsPPL), SIEM correlation on raw telemetry, and identity-centric detections. It’s important because it reframes AI impact from “LLMs find 0-days” to “LLMs make defensive stacks inspectable and bypass development cheaper and faster.”
Technical Focus: - LLM-assisted reverse engineering workflows for EDR/AV tooling - Extraction/inference of local detection logic (rules, thresholds, scripted engines) - Policy/exclusion mining and identification of least-monitored execution paths - Cross-version diffing of agent/rule/model updates to infer vendor changes - Defensive product attack surface: parsers, IPC, services, kernel callbacks, update mechanisms, tamper protection - Defense-in-depth guidance: application control, ASR, SIEM correlation, identity telemetry
Use Cases: - Rapidly triage and understand EDR agent artifacts (configs, rule packs, scripts, models) during red team prep - Identify detection gaps/exclusions and plan quieter tradecraft paths before execution - Compare EDR updates to infer newly-detected behaviors and adjust tooling - Hunt for vulnerabilities in security agents (local SYSTEM services, IPC endpoints, update channels) - Build defensive architecture that remains effective even when endpoint logic is known to attackers - Create SIEM detections from raw endpoint/Windows telemetry independent of EDR alerting
Keywords: LLM, reverse engineering, EDR, antivirus, YARA, behavioral rules, Lua scripted engine, local ML model, feature extraction, scoring thresholds, policy extraction, exclusions, allowlists, version diffing, tamper protection, IPC interfaces, kernel callbacks, WDAC, AppLocker, ASR rules, Credential Guard, RunAsPPL, SIEM correlation, Sysmon, PowerShell script block logging, Entra ID, Conditional Access, token theft, OAuth abuse, Active Directory detection

entry.title: obfus.h
entry.summary:
Title: obfus.h — Macro-only compile-time C obfuscation library for TinyCC on Windows
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - obfus.h is a header-only, macro-based library that applies compile-time obfuscation to C programs compiled with Tiny C Compiler (tcc), targeting Windows x86/x64. - It aims to make reverse engineering harder by transforming control flow, obscuring function calls, hiding strings, and degrading decompiler output. - The project includes anti-debugging hooks (including an `ANTI_DEBUG;` construct), optional “v2” modes for heavier control-flow and anti-debug logic, and a “fake signatures” feature to mislead tooling/signature-based analysis. - It also provides an optional arithmetic “virtualization” layer where math/logic operations are expressed via VM-like macros (e.g., `VM_ADD`, `VM_IF`) to complicate static understanding. - It is primarily useful for red team tooling authors, malware developers, and CTF/payload developers who want lightweight source-level obfuscation without external packers. - Notably, it is tcc-specific (MSVC/GCC/Clang not supported), and the repo references third-party audits/deobfuscation research and a scanner (ObfusHunter).
Technical Focus: - Macro-based compile-time obfuscation (tcc-specific) - Control-flow flattening/mutation (“spaghetti” CFG) - Anti-debugging triggers and inline anti-debug constructs - String hiding via runtime stack construction (mov-based assembly) - Anti-decompilation output degradation techniques - Arithmetic/logic virtualization via embedded VM-like macro ops
Use Cases: - Obfuscating Windows-native C payloads compiled with TinyCC - Hindering static analysis and decompiler readability of small tools/loaders - Hiding high-signal strings (URLs, commands, mutex names) from basic string scanning - Adding anti-debug checks around sensitive code paths - Creating CTF crackme-style binaries with intentionally hostile reversing properties
Keywords: obfus.h, TinyCC, tcc, Windows x86, Windows x64, C obfuscation, macro obfuscation, control-flow obfuscation, anti-debugging, anti-decompilation, string hiding, stack string construction, decompiler breaking, fake signatures, virtualization, arithmetic VM, VM_ADD, VM_IF, MSVCRT, reverse engineering resistance

entry.title: PE XRay EDR
entry.summary:
Title: PE-XRay (PE-XRay-EDR)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): PE-XRay is a lightweight static analyzer for Windows PE files (.exe/.dll) that uses heuristic scoring rather than signature matching to highlight suspicious traits. It targets common malware/packer/protector indicators by inspecting PE structure, section properties, imports, entropy, and embedded strings. The tool flags anomalies such as malformed headers, unusual entry points, RWX (write+execute) sections, high entropy regions suggestive of packing/encryption, and risky WinAPI imports (e.g., CreateRemoteThread, SetWindowsHookExW). It provides both a GUI (IUP) and a CLI build, aimed at quickly triaging binaries and surfacing “evidence” for analyst decision-making. Useful for malware analysts, incident responders, and red teamers validating payload OPSEC against static heuristics.
Technical Focus: - PE header/section parsing and structural anomaly detection - Heuristic-based risk scoring engine - Entropy analysis (global + sliding-window) - Import table inspection for suspicious WinAPI usage - String extraction/intelligence (commands, URLs, tooling artifacts)
Use Cases: - Rapid triage of unknown executables and DLLs during investigations - Identifying packed/encrypted binaries via entropy and section traits - Spotting injection/persistence-related API imports in samples - Pre-deployment OPSEC checks of red team payloads against static heuristics - Hunting for malformed/weaponized PE structures in malware collections
Keywords: Portable Executable, PE parsing, static analysis, heuristic detection, entropy scanning, sliding-window entropy, packer detection, import table analysis, WinAPI, CreateRemoteThread, SetWindowsHookExW, RWX sections, section flags, entry point anomalies, malformed headers, string extraction, malware triage, Windows executables, MinGW-w64, IUP GUI

entry.title: dll sideloading
entry.summary:
Title: DLL Sideloading & Proxy DLL Loader in Rust (VLC case study)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post explains modern Windows DLL sideloading, focusing on how SafeDllSearchMode changes DLL search order but still allows hijacking DLLs loaded from an application’s own directory. It highlights constraints like KnownDlls (e.g., ntdll/kernel32) that cannot be sideloaded via normal search order, and shows how to hunt viable targets using ProcMon “NAME NOT FOUND” DLL load events. Using VLC as an example, it walks through identifying a target DLL (libvlc.dll), reverse engineering required exports/signatures with x64dbg and IDA, and building a malicious replacement DLL in Rust. It then improves stealth and usability by implementing a Proxy DLL: exporting/forwarding all original functions (hundreds of exports) to the legitimate DLL while executing a payload in a separate thread. The post also discusses OPSEC tradeoffs (prefer third-party apps over Microsoft binaries due to EDR scrutiny) and demonstrates running an implant inside the trusted signed process context.
Technical Focus: - Windows DLL search order (SafeDllSearchMode) and application-directory precedence - KnownDlls and limitations of DLL hijacking - Sideload target discovery with Sysinternals ProcMon - Export table analysis and function signature recovery (x64dbg, IDA) - Proxy DLL / export forwarding via .def files and ordinal preservation - Payload execution via threaded loader/self-injection inside host process
Use Cases: - Establishing execution/persistence by planting a malicious DLL next to a legitimate signed executable - Building proxy DLL loaders that preserve application functionality while running an implant - Red team tradecraft for selecting low-noise third-party sideload targets - Reverse engineering DLL exports to create compatible stubs/forwarders - Testing EDR visibility of signed-binary execution with in-process payloads
Keywords: DLL sideloading, SafeDllSearchMode, DLL search order, KnownDlls, System32, ProcMon, Sysinternals, x64dbg, IDA Pro, dumpbin, export table, ordinals, .def file, proxy DLL, DLL forwarding, Rust, libvlc.dll, VLC, shellcode loader, self-injection

entry.title: post3
entry.summary:
Title: Abusing a Lenovo “BootRepair” Driver IOCTL to Terminate Arbitrary Processes (PhantomKiller for dummies)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post walks through reversing a signed Lenovo kernel driver to find an exposed IOCTL that enables terminating arbitrary processes from user mode. - The author identifies the vulnerable code path by hunting for sensitive ntoskrnl imports (notably `ZwTerminateProcess`) and tracing cross-references in IDA to the `IRP_MJ_DEVICE_CONTROL` handler. - The driver’s IOCTL handler takes a 4-byte PID, uses `PsLookupProcessByProcessId` to get an `EPROCESS`, converts it to a process handle via `ObOpenObjectByPointer`, then calls `ZwTerminateProcess`, effectively bypassing normal user-mode access checks. - The write-up shows how to recover the device name/symbolic link (`\\.\BootRepair`), the IOCTL code, and the required input buffer size, then build a minimal C/C++ console app using `CreateFile` + `DeviceIoControl`. - It demonstrates a BYOVD-style primitive useful for killing protected/high-integrity processes (e.g., security tools) without needing Test Mode or an unsigned driver. - Useful for red teamers/pentesters and defenders analyzing driver attack surface and IOCTL abuse patterns.
Technical Focus: - Windows driver IOCTL reversing (IDA, xrefs, `IRP_MJ_DEVICE_CONTROL`) - BYOVD (Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver) process-kill primitive - Kernel APIs: `ZwTerminateProcess`, `PsLookupProcessByProcessId`, `ObOpenObjectByPointer` - Device object namespaces and symbolic links (`\DosDevices\`, `\\.\` paths) - User-mode exploitation via `CreateFile` / `DeviceIoControl`
Use Cases: - Terminate EDR/AV or other high-integrity processes using a signed vulnerable driver - Validate and reproduce a driver IOCTL vulnerability during research - Build detections around suspicious IOCTL usage and known-abusable device names - Driver attack-surface auditing by scanning for dangerous kernel imports and weak IOCTL validation
Keywords: BYOVD, Lenovo driver, BootRepair, PhantomKiller, IOCTL, IRP_MJ_DEVICE_CONTROL, DeviceIoControl, CreateFile, \DosDevices, symbolic link, ZwTerminateProcess, PsLookupProcessByProcessId, ObOpenObjectByPointer, EPROCESS, process termination, kernel driver exploitation, DriverEntry, ntoskrnl imports, Driver Signature Enforcement (DSE)

entry.title: malleable rust loader
entry.summary:
Title: Malleable Rust Loader
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This repository provides a configurable Rust-based loader/packer designed to execute multiple payload types (notably DLLs mapped and executed from memory) while reducing static AV analysis signal. Behavior is driven by a signed, encrypted JSON configuration that can be updated at runtime from multiple “Link” sources (HTTP, file, embedded memory; DNS/WebSocket planned). Retrieved configs/payloads can be transformed through chained “DataOperation” stages (e.g., AES-GCM-SIV, Base64, ROT13, zlib compression, HTML delimiter wrapping, PNG steganography) before verification and execution. It includes “defuse” gating checks (internet reachability, hostname, domain-join) to control when config reloads and payload execution occur. The project supports cross-compilation, optional OLLVM-based obfuscation and string encryption, and can build as an EXE or a DLL (with a helper C “overlord” loader for testing). Useful primarily for red teams/pentesters and malware/implant developers experimenting with malleable staging, in-memory module loading, and resilient config distribution.
Technical Focus: - Config-driven loader architecture with runtime config rotation - Ed25519 signing/verification for config trust and anti-tamper - In-memory DLL loading/execution (MemoryModule / memorymodule-rs) - Multi-stage data deobfuscation/decryption pipelines (AES-GCM-SIV, stegano, zlib) - Cross-compilation and binary obfuscation (OLLVM, string encryption) - Execution gating/“defuse” checks (hostname, domain join, internet checks)
Use Cases: - Stage and execute an encrypted DLL from memory (e.g., C2 implant DLL) with configurable retrieval paths - Rotate loader behavior by pushing signed configs to multiple fallback URLs/files - Hide configs/payloads inside benign-looking web pages or PNG images (stegano) for staging - Build a Windows EXE/DLL loader from Linux and deploy/execute via SMB + Impacket tooling - Prototype malleable staging logic (pooling, fallback links, parallel/linear fetch strategies)
Keywords: Rust, loader, packer, in-memory execution, DllFromMemory, MemoryModule, memorymodule-rs, Ed25519, signed configuration, JSON config, AES-GCM-SIV, AEAD, steganography, PNG stegano, zlib, Base64, ROT13, OLLVM, LLVM obfuscation, cross-compilation, x86_64-pc-windows-gnu, UPX, HTTP GET, SMB upload, Impacket, PsExec, domain join check, hostname check

entry.title: Release Stoffels Escape
entry.summary:
Title: Brute Ratel v1.1 “Stoffel’s Escape” Release (Badger core rewrite & evasion upgrades)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This release post describes Brute Ratel C4 v1.1 updates, centered on a full rewrite of the “Badger” implant core to reduce memory artifacts and known IOCs highlighted by public reverse-engineering/detection writeups. - It replaces prior reflective-DLL-centric design elements with a new staged/wrapper approach, adds per-build randomized crypto keys, and introduces polymorphic shellcode generation (e.g., random register usage) to resist signature/YARA detections. - Major in-memory evasion changes include on-demand DLL loading, PEB-based module discovery to avoid LoadLibrary/LdrLoadDll, proxying DLL-load calls via legitimate Windows functions to evade EDR stack-tracing/EtwTI-based heuristics, and expanded sleep masking with full-region encryption plus thread stack masquerading/start-address spoofing. - It expands indirect syscall support to x86 and WOW64, adds “phantom_thread” (alertable-thread context hijack with ROP gadget redirection), and introduces “memhook” for runtime patching/hooking of arbitrary memory regions without BOFs. - Operational features include built-in SOCKS with nested pivoting, “burnable” SOCKS profiles, SOCKS over DoH, improved staging (small ~7–8KB stages) and exit behaviors for APC/QAPC scenarios, plus various Commander UX/tasking improvements. - Useful primarily for red teams/pentesters and adversary emulation operators; also relevant to blue teams for understanding modern C2 implant evasion patterns and telemetry gaps.
Technical Focus: - Indirect syscalls (x64/x86/WOW64) and syscall-based evasion - PEB module enumeration and DLL-load proxying (LoadLibrary/LdrLoadDll avoidance) - Sleep masking, full-region in-memory encryption, custom heap/zeroization - Thread stack masquerading, start-address spoofing, APC/QAPC execution nuances - Alertable-thread injection via context hijacking + ROP gadgets (“phantom_thread”) - Pivoting via built-in SOCKS, SOCKS over DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH)
Use Cases: - EDR-evasion-focused implant deployment and post-exploitation on Windows - Stealthier process injection and thread-based execution (alertable thread targeting) - Nested pivoting inside segmented networks (SMB/TCP pivots, SOCKS/DoH routing) - Runtime patching of security controls or program behavior via in-memory hooks - Blue-team detection engineering: hunting for PEB-walking, proxied DLL loads, stack-masqueraded threads, and indirect-syscall patterns
Keywords: Brute Ratel, BRc4, Badger, C2, reflective DLL, shellcode loader, polymorphic shellcode, YARA evasion, RC4, randomized keys, PEB, LoadLibrary, LdrLoadDll, EtwTI, stack tracing, sleep masking, thread stack masquerading, start address spoofing, indirect syscalls, WOW64, APC, QAPC, ROP gadgets, context hijacking, SOCKS proxy, DNS over HTTPS, SMB pivoting, TCP pivoting, BOF, Coffexec, memhook, AMSI strings

entry.title: Release Scandinavian Defense
entry.summary:
Title: Brute Ratel v1.2 “Scandinavian Defense” Release – Memory Evasion and Bring-Your-Own Injection via COFF
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This release post describes Brute Ratel C4 v1.2 (“Scandinavian Defense”) with a focus on in-memory evasion against userland EDR hooking and telemetry. - It targets common EDR “traps” such as PEB/IAT/EAT manipulation, page-guarded decoy mappings, VEH-based exception interception, and origin checks on API calls from unbacked RX/RWX regions. - The post outlines techniques to locate legitimate ntdll metadata without trusting the PEB, unhook EDR DLLs before resolving NTAPI pointers, and adjust PIC/PE staging to avoid detections tied to unbacked allocations (notably discussed with Elastic). - It also adds stronger sleep-masking: heap + stack encryption, thread stack frame duplication, and anti-debugging behaviors to hinder sandboxing and reverse engineering. - A major operator feature is “bring your own injection” by extending COFF/BOF execution to accept file buffers (shellcode/.NET/reflective DLL) via `set_coffargs`, plus a buffer-size API for allocation workflows. - Useful primarily for red teams/pentesters and malware tradecraft researchers; it’s interesting for its detailed discussion of modern EDR memory traps and practical countermeasures (including hardware-breakpoint “patchless” AMSI/ETW evasion).
Technical Focus: - Userland EDR hooking/traps (PEB/IAT/EAT hooks, page guards, VEH interception) - Legitimate ntdll discovery without PEB trust (kernel structure/EPROCESS-derived metadata) - Unhooking system DLLs / restoring clean syscall/NTAPI resolution - Sleep masking: heap & stack encryption, thread stack spoofing/duplication - COFF/BOF extensibility and file-buffer argument passing for custom injection - Hardware breakpoints (DR0–DR3/DR7) + VEH for “patchless” AMSI/ETW evasion
Use Cases: - Building more resilient Windows implants/loaders that survive common EDR userland traps - Customizing injection tradecraft by shipping operator-supplied COFF modules and payload buffers - Reducing in-memory artifacts during sleep (stack/heap encryption, stack frame spoofing) - Evading AMSI/ETW detections without inline patching (hardware-breakpoint redirection) - Hardening payloads against debugging/sandbox automation (anti-debug crash-on-detect)
Keywords: Brute Ratel, Badger, EDR evasion, userland hooks, ntdll.dll, kernel32.dll, kernelbase.dll, PEB hooking, IAT hooking, EAT hooking, Page Guard, Vectored Exception Handler (VEH), ETWTI, LdrLoadDll, LoadLibrary, reflective DLL, PIC, module stomping, COFF, BOF, set_coffargs, AMSI, AmsiScanBuffer, ETW, EtwEventWrite, hardware breakpoints, DR0-DR3, DR7, GetThreadContext, sleep masking, stack encryption, heap encryption, thread stack spoofing

entry.title: Release Resurgence
entry.summary:
Title: Brute Ratel v1.3 “Resurgence” Release – No Strings Attached
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This release post describes Brute Ratel C4 v1.3 (“Resurgence”), focusing on payload/core changes aimed at reducing static and in-memory detections and improving C2 malleability. - It explains common EDR detection surfaces (YARA on disk/memory, injection telemetry, LOLBin/sideload tracking, network signatures/certs, and behavioral/ML scoring) and maps v1.3 changes to those weaknesses. - Key updates include removing embedded strings and the prior ror13 hashing artifacts, rewriting/optimizing shellcode, changing how configuration is stored (binary vs base64), and expanding malleable C2 to include response bodies and headers (plus distinct “command/no-command” responses). - Operational features include reverse port forwarding (`rportfwd`), improved DoH chunking controls via “DNS interval,” and multiple server/commander QoL improvements (autosave/restore profiles, faster SOCKS, UI enhancements). - The post also covers licensing/ethics, discusses prior leaks leading to vendor detections, and points defenders to community YARA rules for older versions. - Useful primarily for red teams/pentesters using BRc4 and for blue teams seeking detection context around BRc4 tradecraft and artifacts.
Technical Focus: - EDR detection surfaces: YARA (disk/memory), thread/injection telemetry, kernel callbacks (e.g., PsSetCreateThreadNotifyRoutine) - Payload artifact reduction: string removal, hashing algorithm changes, shellcode rewrite/size optimization - C2 malleability: customizable HTTP(S)/DoH request+response bodies and headers, Burp-compatible profiles - DoH transport constraints and chunking behavior (64-byte limit) with timing controls (“DNS interval”) - Reverse port forwarding and SOCKS performance/crypto changes - Operational security considerations: certificates, network signatures, behavioral scoring
Use Cases: - Tuning BRc4 network profiles to evade signature-based network detections (custom responses/headers, valid certs) - Reducing static/memory signature hits by altering payload artifacts (strings/hashes/decryption stubs) - Pivoting/lateral movement support via reverse port forwarding (`rportfwd`) and improved SOCKS - DoH-based C2 operations with controllable chunk timing to manage traffic patterns - Defensive detection engineering using published YARA rules for leaked/older BRc4 payloads
Keywords: Brute Ratel, BRc4, Badger, Ratel Server, C2 malleable profile, EDR evasion, YARA, memory scanning, code injection, CreateRemoteThread, QueueUserAPC, ETW, PsSetCreateThreadNotifyRoutine, NtProtectVirtualMemory, ntdll.dll, ror13 hashing, RC4, DNS over HTTPS (DoH), reverse port forwarding, SOCKS proxy, behavioral detection, Microsoft Defender/ATP, CrowdStrike, Elastic EDR, Burp Suite

entry.title: Release Nightmare
entry.summary:
Title: Brute Ratel v1.5 (Nightmare) Release – “Ghosts From The Past”
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This release post describes Brute Ratel C4 v1.5 “Nightmare,” focusing on new evasion and operator quality-of-life updates. - The main technical addition is a revamped module stomping implementation that aims to avoid common detections seen in older/public approaches (e.g., Cobalt Strike-style stomping) by addressing loader/PEB anomalies and Control Flow Guard (CFG) constraints. - It explains how `LoadLibraryEx(DONT_RESOLVE_DLL_REFERENCES)` affects PEB/LDR flags and entrypoint handling, why those artifacts are detectable, and how BR attempts to “fully link” the module and patch entrypoint-related metadata. - The post also covers CFG-related failures (e.g., `ntdll!LdrpDispatchUserCallTarget` leading to `RtlFailFast2`) and introduces operator commands to disable/enable CFG for custom workflows. - Additional features include an undocumented Remote Procedure Call-based process injection method (requires alertable target threads) and built-in Pass-the-Hash with improved OpSec compared to classic Mimikatz PTH. - Useful primarily for red team operators, adversary simulation tool developers, and defenders studying modern in-memory tradecraft and detection points.
Technical Focus: - Module stomping implementation details and detection artifacts - Windows loader internals (PEB/LDR, `_LDR_DATA_TABLE_ENTRY` flags, DllMain/loader lock) - Control Flow Guard (CFG) evasion/handling and indirect call validation - Undocumented RPC-based process injection (alertable state requirement) - Pass-the-Hash (NTLM) process creation/token impersonation - ETW evasion and in-memory artifact reduction during sleep (restore-on-sleep behavior)
Use Cases: - Implementing stealthier in-memory payload hosting backed by on-disk modules - Building detections for module stomping via PEB/LDR inconsistencies and memory-vs-disk `.text` comparisons - Avoiding CFG-related crashes when executing from stomped `.text` regions - Performing alternative remote process injection via RPC in suitable targets - Conducting lateral movement/authentication abuse using NTLM hashes (PTH) with better operational controls
Keywords: Brute Ratel, BRc4, Badger, module stomping, LoadLibraryEx, DONT_RESOLVE_DLL_REFERENCES, PEB, LDR_DATA_TABLE_ENTRY, DllMain, loader lock, IAT resolution, `.text` section, memory-vs-disk comparison, Control Flow Guard, CFG, ntdll, LdrpDispatchUserCallTarget, RtlFailFast2, STATUS_STACK_BUFFER_OVERRUN, ETW evasion, stack spoofing, heap encryption, RPC injection, alertable thread, Pass-the-Hash, NTLM, token impersonation, Mimikatz PTH

entry.title: Closing%20the%20Execution%20Gap.pdf
entry.summary:
Title: Closing the Execution Gap: Hardware-Backed Telemetry for Detecting Out-Of-Context Execution
Type: Research Paper
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This paper proposes an endpoint detection approach that shifts the “chokepoint” from OS-level telemetry (hooks, syscalls, ETW providers) to CPU-level execution evidence to detect code running from private (non-image-backed) memory. It targets modern fileless/in-memory tradecraft and common EDR evasion (direct syscalls, hook bypass, missing/dropped telemetry) by using hardware-assisted tracing that attackers can’t easily suppress. The system correlates Windows scheduler/context-switch events and PMU-driven Performance Monitor Interrupts (PMIs) with Intel Last Branch Record (LBR) and Intel Processor Trace (IPT) data, collected from user mode via ETW, to observe branch targets and instruction pointers that land in private executable regions. It maintains a memory tracker for private allocations and state transitions (alloc/write/protect/free) and, upon detecting execution in private memory, builds a provenance graph linking processes, threads, memory regions, and contextual artifacts (e.g., network/registry/image loads) from a local context database. Case studies show detection of both canonical remote injection (VirtualAllocEx/WriteProcessMemory/CreateRemoteThread) and “un-instrumentable” local indirect-call execution that lacks OS telemetry. It’s most useful for EDR/defensive engineers and threat hunters building durable detections against in-memory implants and injection, and it’s interesting because it operationalizes LBR/IPT via ETW as a practical, execution-centric detection primitive rather than behavior-rule chasing.
Technical Focus: - ETW-based collection of context switches, call stacks, and (Windows 24H2) context registers - Hardware execution telemetry: Intel LBR and Intel Processor Trace (IPT), branch/TIP decoding - PMU interval sampling and Performance Monitor Interrupt (PMI) as inflection points - Correlation of execution events to private memory allocations (VAD/private vs image-backed) - Provenance/graph modeling of process-thread-memory relationships and enrichment via local context DB - Injection/execution primitives coverage (APC, remote thread, SetThreadContext, indirect calls, section mapping)
Use Cases: - Detect execution of shellcode/implants from private executable memory (local or remote injection) - Catch execution primitives that evade OS instrumentation (e.g., indirect function-call into shellcode) - Attribute in-memory execution to the correct actor process/thread via correlated scheduler + trace data - Generate investigation-ready provenance graphs for triage (who allocated/wrote/protected/executed) - Build anomaly/embedding baselines over execution+provenance graphs to reduce false positives (e.g., JIT)
Keywords: ETW, Windows scheduler, context switch, CONTEXT registers, Windows 24H2, Performance Monitor Interrupt, PMU, Intel LBR, Last Branch Record, Intel Processor Trace, IPT, MSR, TraceSetInformation, NtSetSystemInformation, ipt.sys, branch tracing, TIP packets, private memory, VAD, VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, CreateRemoteThread, QueueUserAPC, SetThreadContext, NtCreateSection, code injection, in-memory execution, EDR evasion, direct syscalls, SysWhispers, Hell’s Gate, graph provenance, Threat Intelligence ETW provider (Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence)

entry.title: berlin
entry.summary:
Title: Berlin — Cross-platform CPU-based Virtual Machine Detection Framework
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Berlin is a C library/PoC that detects whether it is running inside a virtual machine by leveraging CPU “vmexit” instructions and timing-based side channels. It targets the problem of avoiding OS/artifact-based VM checks (e.g., registry keys) that create IOCs and are easy to spoof, instead focusing on CPU behavior differences under virtualization. The core API is a single function `int isVM(int threshold);` that returns true when measured execution latency exceeds a configurable threshold (and also returns true on error). The project emphasizes portability by abstracting OS-specific dependencies and being freestanding (no C/C++ runtime requirement), intended for embedding into offensive tooling. It’s primarily useful for red teams, pentesters, and malware developers who want sandbox/VM awareness with fewer host artifacts. It’s interesting because it frames VM detection as “CPU feature emulation best-effort,” using vmexit-induced latency as a cross-platform signal.
Technical Focus: - VM detection via vmexit-triggering CPU instructions - Timing attacks / latency measurement for virtualization inference - Freestanding C implementation (no CRT), portability abstractions - Threshold tuning and performance/optimization considerations (-O3) - Anti-analysis / sandbox evasion design tradeoffs
Use Cases: - Embed VM/sandbox checks into loaders, implants, or droppers - Reduce reliance on OS-level VM artifacts (registry/files/drivers) to lower IOCs - Research and benchmarking of vmexit timing characteristics across hypervisors - Defensive testing: validate whether sandboxes/VMs leak detectable timing signals
Keywords: virtual machine detection, vmexit, timing attack, side-channel, hypervisor, KVM, QEMU, CPU emulation, anti-analysis, sandbox evasion, freestanding C, no CRT, clang, -O3, threshold calibration, bare metal vs VM, offensive tooling, PoC, cross-platform

entry.title: zero loader
entry.summary:
Title: zero-loader — Polymorphic x64 shellcode loader (CRT-free) with patchless AMSI/ETW bypass and call stack spoofing
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): zero-loader is a Windows 10/11 x64 shellcode loader designed to produce polymorphic builds where each compilation regenerates keys, nonces, string encodings, and PE metadata to avoid static signatures. It focuses on modern userland evasion: indirect syscalls with randomized syscall;ret gadget selection, patchless AMSI/ETW bypass using VEH + hardware breakpoints, and call stack spoofing via ntdll gadgets and thread pool execution. Payloads are staged over HTTPS (WinINet), compressed with LZNT1, and encrypted with Chaskey-12 CTR, with runtime key recovery and post-exec cleanup (clearing debug registers, removing VEH, wiping secrets). For in-memory placement it supports “phantom DLL hollowing” using NTFS transactions + SEC_IMAGE rollback and module stomping of signed DLL .text to attribute memory to legitimate modules. It also includes a DLL sideloading mode with auto-generated export forwarding and version-info cloning, plus an exit hook to keep the host process from terminating. Useful primarily for red teams, pentesters, and malware researchers evaluating EDR detection gaps and telemetry around thread pool execution, syscall indirection, and memory attribution tricks.
Technical Focus: - Indirect syscalls (SSN extraction, hooked-stub fallback, syscall gadget pooling/randomization) - Patchless AMSI/ETW bypass (VEH + hardware breakpoints via NtContinue) - Phantom DLL hollowing (TxF/CreateFileTransacted, NtCreateSection, NtMapViewOfSection, SEC_IMAGE rollback) - Module stomping of signed DLL sections and memory attribution - Call stack spoofing + thread pool execution (TpAllocWork/TpPostWork, ntdll gadgets) - Polymorphic build pipeline (string obfuscation, PE metadata mutation, API hashing/PEB walking)
Use Cases: - Generate per-build-unique Windows loaders for controlled adversary simulation - Test EDR visibility/detections for AMSI/ETW bypass without code patching - Evaluate detections around thread pool execution and call stack spoofing - Research memory attribution techniques (SEC_IMAGE mapping, module stomping) - Build DLL sideloading proxies with export forwarding and version cloning for lab scenarios
Keywords: Windows x64, shellcode loader, polymorphic build, CRT-free, indirect syscalls, SSN extraction, ntdll, syscall;ret gadgets, RDTSC, AMSI, ETW, VEH, hardware breakpoints, DR0 DR1 DR7, NtContinue, call stack spoofing, thread pool, TpAllocWork, module stomping, phantom DLL hollowing, TxF, CreateFileTransactedA, NtCreateSection, NtMapViewOfSection, SEC_IMAGE, LZNT1, RtlDecompressBuffer, Chaskey-12, WinINet, InternetCrackUrlA, DLL sideloading, export forwarding, LdrRegisterDllNotification, RtlExitUserProcess, LdrAddRefDll, UAC runas

entry.title: crimes against ntdll implementing early cascade injection
entry.summary:
Title: Crimes against NTDLL – Implementing Early Cascade Injection (Rust)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post explains how to implement Outflank’s “Early Cascade Injection” technique in Rust to execute code at the earliest stage of Windows user-mode process initialization. - It abuses NTDLL’s Application Compatibility Shim Engine by enabling `g_ShimsEnabled` and redirecting the shim DLL-load callback pointer (`g_pfnSE_DllLoaded` / `g_fnSE_DllLoaded`) to attacker-controlled code. - The target process is created suspended, stage-1 bootstrap code is injected, and the shim callback pointer is patched (with required pointer encoding) so execution occurs during normal loader activity (around `LdrInitializeThunk`). - The stage-1 stub (written as `no_std` Rust “shellcode-like” code) disables shims immediately and queues a same-thread APC via `NtQueueApcThread` to run a stage-2 payload (e.g., reflective DLL loader). - It avoids common injection artifacts like `CreateRemoteThread`, cross-process APC injection, and thread context hijacking, and can run before many user-mode EDR hooks/telemetry components initialize. - The write-up also covers resolving undocumented NTDLL globals via byte-pattern scanning and handling encoded callback pointers using `SharedUserData!Cookie` at `0x7FFE0330`. - Useful for red teamers/implant developers building stealthy spawn-and-inject primitives; also relevant to defenders studying early-process injection and loader/shim abuse.
Technical Focus: - NTDLL loader internals (`LdrInitializeThunk`) and early user-mode execution - Application Compatibility Shim Engine abuse (`g_ShimsEnabled`, `g_pfnSE_DllLoaded`) - Pointer encoding/decoding using `SharedUserData!Cookie` (system pointer encoding) - Byte-pattern scanning to locate undocumented globals in NTDLL - `no_std` Rust stubs as position-independent bootstrap code - Same-thread APC execution via `NtQueueApcThread` / dispatch via `NtTestAlert`
Use Cases: - Stealthy spawn-and-inject for C2 implants prior to user-mode EDR initialization - Fileless payload execution without remote thread creation - Building early-stage loaders that transition into reflective DLL injection - Researching/detecting shim-engine variable tampering and early loader callback abuse
Keywords: Early Cascade Injection, ntdll.dll, Shim Engine, Application Compatibility, g_ShimsEnabled, g_pfnSE_DllLoaded, g_fnSE_DllLoaded, LdrInitializeThunk, CREATE_SUSPENDED, process injection, EDR preload, user-mode hooks, SharedUserData Cookie, 0x7FFE0330, EncodeSystemPointer, NtQueueApcThread, NtTestAlert, APC, PEB walking, reflective DLL injection, Rust no_std, byte pattern scanning, undocumented globals

entry.title: release
entry.summary:
Title: Havoc Professional Release (Kaine-kit)
Type: Blog Post
