Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post announces the initial release of Havoc Professional and its Kaine-kit implant ecosystem, describing operator-focused features aimed at working in hardened environments. - It highlights new C2 transport options (including encrypted DNS via DoH/DoT), plus runtime listener/profile and even protocol switching (e.g., HTTP↔SMB, DNS↔HTTP) without respawning an agent. - The release adds support for Async BOFs (event-driven/background BOF jobs using the standardized Beacon APIs) and “Adaptive BOFs” where the loader proxies Win32 API calls so BOFs inherit the agent’s evasion features without embedding spoofing logic. - Kaine-kit emphasizes modular, runtime-loadable extensions (e.g., SOCKS, process/filesystem tooling) and introduces “PowerSafe” for native PowerShell execution with bypasses for AMSI and multiple PowerShell logging controls. - It previews “Firebeam,” a VM/bytecode execution pipeline intended to run existing tooling via transpilation, with optional runtime Win32 proxying/stack spoofing via extensions. - Evasion R&D includes a callstack spoofing extension (desync and synthetic modes) and “ALLURE,” a custom bin2bin obfuscator designed to raise static detection cost while preserving semantics. - Useful primarily for professional red teams/pentesters and security researchers evaluating modern C2/implant tradecraft and evasion modularity.
Technical Focus: - Encrypted DNS C2 (DNS, DoH, DoT) and layered channel strategies - Runtime C2 profile/protocol switching (listener reconfiguration, P2P vs direct) - Async BOFs and Beacon API compatibility (BeaconWakeup, stop-job events) - BOF loader API proxying / “adaptive” execution with evasion inheritance - Modular implant extensions (static embed vs dynamic load) and operator tooling (SOCKS, process mgmt) - Evasion: callstack spoofing (desync/synthetic), native PowerShell bypasses, bin2bin obfuscation, VM/bytecode execution (Firebeam)
Use Cases: - Establish low-signature initial access/persistence via DNS and later upgrade to higher-bandwidth channels - Rotate burned infrastructure and change C2 profiles without losing an active session - Run long-lived host monitoring (process/logon notifications) using Async BOFs - Reuse existing Cobalt Strike/Outflank/TrustedSec BOFs with minimal porting (argument packing scripts) - Execute PowerShell natively while suppressing AMSI and common PowerShell telemetry controls - Apply runtime evasion (stack spoofing, API proxying) to third-party tooling/BOFs without modifying their source
Keywords: Havoc Professional, Kaine-kit, C2 framework, DNS over HTTPS, DoH, DNS over TLS, DoT, runtime channel switching, listener reconfiguration, SMB C2, HTTP C2, P2P, Beacon Object Files, BOF, Async BOF, Beacon API, BeaconWakeup, callstack spoofing, AMSI bypass, Script Block Logging, Constrained Language Mode, PowerShell, Firebeam VM, bytecode transpilation, Win32 API proxying, bin2bin obfuscation, ALLURE, SOCKS proxy, modular extensions

entry.title: SysWhispers4
entry.summary:
Title: SysWhispers4 — Advanced Windows Syscall Stub Generator (Direct/Indirect Syscalls + Evasion)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - SysWhispers4 is a Python generator that outputs C/ASM syscall stubs to invoke NT kernel functions directly, aiming to bypass user-mode AV/EDR hooks on `ntdll.dll`. - It focuses on robust syscall number (SSN) resolution across many Windows versions/builds and multiple architectures (x64, x86, WoW64, ARM64), with both direct and indirect invocation options. - The project implements and extends common SSN discovery techniques (Hell’s Gate/Halo’s Gate/Tartarus’ Gate) and adds newer strategies like FreshyCalls (VA-sorting), mapping clean `ntdll` from `\KnownDlls` (“from_disk”), RecycledGate (VA-sort + opcode validation), and a hardware-breakpoint/VEH method to extract SSNs without reading hooked bytes. - It also bundles optional evasion helpers such as ETW patching (`EtwEventWrite`), AMSI patching (`AmsiScanBuffer`), `ntdll` unhooking via remapping clean `.text`, call-stack spoofing, junk instruction insertion, and Ekko-style sleep encryption of `.text`. - Useful primarily for red teams, pentesters, and offensive security researchers building custom loaders/injectors; secondarily for blue teams to understand syscall-based tradecraft and build detections. - Interesting because it combines multiple resilient SSN resolution paths with configurable invocation and on-disk signature reduction (egg-hunt syscall opcode patching), targeting modern EDR heuristics.
Technical Focus: - Windows NT syscalls and SSN resolution (Hell’s Gate, Halo’s Gate, Tartarus’ Gate, FreshyCalls, RecycledGate) - Direct vs indirect syscalls (`syscall; ret` gadgets, randomized gadget selection) - `\KnownDlls\ntdll.dll` mapping and `ntdll` unhooking (clean `.text` remap) - VEH + hardware breakpoints (DR0–DR3) for SSN extraction - User-mode telemetry bypasses (ETW patching) and AMSI patching - Obfuscation techniques (egg-hunt syscall opcode, junk instructions, XOR SSN encryption, stack spoofing, sleep encryption)
Use Cases: - Generate syscall stubs for custom process injection (e.g., `NtAllocateVirtualMemory`, `NtWriteVirtualMemory`, `NtCreateThreadEx`) - Build tooling that avoids user-mode API hooks by calling NT syscalls directly/indirectly - Test EDR resilience against VA-sorted SSN resolution and KnownDlls-based clean mapping - Reduce static signatures by removing `0F 05` from disk (egg-hunt) and encrypting SSN tables - Research/detect syscall-based tradecraft and common user-mode patching behaviors (ETW/AMSI/ntdll)
Keywords: SysWhispers4, Windows syscalls, SSN resolution, ntdll hooks, AV evasion, EDR evasion, Hell’s Gate, Halo’s Gate, Tartarus’ Gate, FreshyCalls, RecycledGate, KnownDlls, NtMapViewOfSection, indirect syscall, syscall gadget, RDTSC entropy, VEH, hardware breakpoints, ETW patch, EtwEventWrite, AMSI bypass, AmsiScanBuffer, ntdll unhooking, call stack spoofing, egg hunt, sleep encryption, WoW64, ARM64, sysenter, SVC #0

entry.title: analyzing the evolution and execution of syswhispers 1 3 74cbbcdaf397
entry.summary:
Title: Analyzing the Evolution and Execution of SysWhispers (1–3)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post breaks down how the SysWhispers project evolved from SysWhispers1 to SysWhispers3 to bypass user-mode AV/EDR hooks by invoking Windows syscalls more stealthily. - It explains the core problem: EDRs commonly hook NTDLL/Native API functions (e.g., Nt* / Zw*) in userland to monitor and block suspicious behavior before it reaches kernel mode. - SysWhispers1 relies on syscall number tables and OS version detection (initially via RtlGetVersion; later via PEB-based checks) to generate syscall stubs across Windows versions. - SysWhispers2 moves to dynamic syscall number resolution by parsing ntdll.dll exports and inferring syscall numbers by sorting Zw* function addresses, avoiding static tables and explicit OS version checks. - SysWhispers3 further reduces static signatures by avoiding custom syscall stubs and instead performing indirect syscalls by jumping into legitimate syscall stubs already present in ntdll, using a dispatcher that maps hashed names to resolved pointers. - It’s primarily useful for red teamers, malware developers, and offensive security researchers; it’s also relevant to blue teams for understanding detection gaps around indirect syscalls and NTDLL-based telemetry blind spots.
Technical Focus: - User-mode API hooking and bypass (NTDLL Nt*/Zw* hooks) - Direct vs indirect syscalls on Windows - Syscall number (SSN) resolution: static tables vs dynamic inference - PEB-based environment/OS discovery and anti-detection tradeoffs - Parsing PE structures (EAT/export directory) in ntdll.dll - Dispatcher patterns and hashed API name resolution
Use Cases: - Generating syscall-capable stubs/headers for offensive tooling to evade userland hooks - Building loaders/injectors that avoid high-level Win32 APIs commonly monitored by EDR - Researching and reproducing syscall-resolution techniques for detection engineering - Developing detections around anomalous syscall invocation patterns and NTDLL export-walking behavior
Keywords: SysWhispers, SysWhispers2, SysWhispers3, Windows syscalls, SSN, system service number, NTDLL, NtCreateFile, Zw* exports, Export Address Table, EAT parsing, PEB, RtlGetVersion, user-mode hooks, EDR bypass, direct syscall, indirect syscall, syscall stub, dispatcher, API hashing, ntoskrnl.exe, WoW64

entry.title: bof vs
entry.summary:
Title: Beacon Object File Visual Studio Template (bof-vs)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository provides a Visual Studio 2022 project template for developing Cobalt Strike Beacon Object Files (BOFs), including support for Sleepmask BOFs. - It streamlines BOF development by offering Debug builds that compile BOFs into executables for source-level debugging without needing a live Beacon instance. - The template includes mocked Beacon APIs (data parsing, output/format) plus an argument packer that emulates Aggressor’s `bof_pack`, enabling realistic local testing. - It adds helper macros for Dynamic Function Resolution (DFR) to simplify Win32 API import declarations and common BOF coding patterns. - A UnitTest configuration integrates GoogleTest and provides helpers to run BOFs and capture output for assertions. - A Python-based linter (`boflint`) runs during Release builds to catch common BOF incompatibilities (relocations, missing entrypoints, bad imports, stack probing, exception handling). - Useful primarily for red team operators and tool developers writing BOFs, and for defenders/researchers who want reproducible BOF test harnesses.
Technical Focus: - Beacon Object File (BOF) development workflow in Visual Studio - Mocked Beacon API + argument packing/parsing emulation - Dynamic Function Resolution (Win32 API import patterns) - Sleepmask BOF development and “mock Beacon” memory layout - Unit testing BOFs with GoogleTest - Static analysis/linting for BOF constraints (relocations, EH, stack probing)
Use Cases: - Rapidly scaffold and build new BOFs for Cobalt Strike - Debug BOF logic locally as a Windows executable before deploying to Beacon - Write regression tests for BOFs and validate output deterministically - Develop and test Sleepmask BOFs with configurable malleable C2-like settings - Catch BOF-breaking compiler/linker patterns early via `boflint` - Maintain multiple related BOFs in a single VS project (one BOF per .cpp)
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, BOF, Beacon Object File, Visual Studio 2022, Windows x64, Win32 API, Dynamic Function Resolution, DFR macro, beacon.h, mocked Beacon API, bof_pack, argument packer, GoogleTest, unit tests, Sleepmask, malleable C2, relocations, import resolution, stack probing, exception handling, boflint, Python 3

entry.title: stealth injection with rop thread hijacking without executable memory
entry.summary:
Title: Stealth Injection with ROP: Thread Hijacking Without Executable Memory (T(ROP)H)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post describes T(ROP)H, a Windows x64 DLL injection technique that avoids allocating executable memory in a remote process by using thread hijacking plus a minimal ROP chain. - It targets a common EDR/IOC signal—`VirtualAllocEx` with executable permissions—by only allocating RW memory for the DLL path and reusing existing code in loaded modules. - The core idea is to suspend a remote thread, adjust its `RIP`/`RSP`, and place a small stack buffer so execution hits a `pop rcx; ret` gadget and then returns into `LoadLibraryA` with the DLL path passed via `RCX` (x64 calling convention). - It highlights a practical constraint: `SetThreadContext` can’t directly set certain volatile registers like `RCX`, motivating the ROP gadget approach. - The write-up also notes detectability: despite avoiding RX allocations, the method is still visible due to `SetThreadContext` generating ETW events (Kernel-Audit-API-Calls and Threat-Intelligence). - Useful for red teamers and malware developers exploring stealthier injection tradeoffs, and for blue teams building detections around thread context tampering and ETW telemetry.
Technical Focus: - Windows x64 calling convention (RCX argument passing) - Thread hijacking via `SuspendThread` / `GetThreadContext` / `SetThreadContext` / `ResumeThread` - ROP gadget discovery and runtime address resolution (e.g., `pop rcx; ret` in `ntdll.dll`) - DLL injection via `LoadLibraryA` without executable memory allocation - Remote memory operations (`VirtualAllocEx`, `WriteProcessMemory`) for data-only payload staging - ETW-based detection surface for `SetThreadContext`
Use Cases: - Stealthier DLL injection PoCs that avoid RWX/RX remote allocations - Evaluating EDR detection gaps between memory-permission IOCs vs. thread-context telemetry - Building/validating detections for thread hijacking and ROP-style control-flow manipulation - Red team tradecraft research on “living-off-the-module” execution using existing code pages
Keywords: T(ROP)H, thread hijacking, ROP chain, pop rcx ret, Windows x64, calling convention, RCX, RIP, RSP, SetThreadContext, GetThreadContext, SuspendThread, ResumeThread, VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, LoadLibraryA, kernel32.dll, ntdll.dll, ETW, Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence, Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-Audit-API-Calls, DLL injection, EDR detection

entry.title: xwin starterpack
entry.summary:
Title: xwin-starterpack
Type: GitHub Tool (template/dev environment)
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This repository is a starter template for developing and debugging Windows C/C++ software from a Linux host using LLVM/Clang and the xwin toolchain. It solves the friction of setting up Windows SDK/CRT headers/libs for cross-compilation and provides a repeatable VS Code + devcontainer workflow. The setup uses a Windows VM running OpenSSH and `lldb-server`, with SSH port forwarding to enable remote debugging from Linux via LLDB/DAP in VS Code. The repo includes container/devcontainer configuration plus a Makefile and project skeleton, and adds support for x86 and ARM64 targets. It’s useful for red teamers, exploit devs, and malware researchers who want fast iteration on Windows-native code without living on Windows, and for defenders building Windows tooling in a controlled environment. Interesting because it combines cross-compilation (xwin) with a practical remote-debug pipeline that mirrors real-world Windows debugging workflows.
Technical Focus: - Cross-compiling Windows binaries on Linux (Clang/LLVM + xwin) - Windows CRT/SDK provisioning (Universal CRT concepts) - Remote debugging with `lldb-server` and LLDB platform mode - VS Code devcontainers (Docker/Podman) and reproducible builds - SSH port forwarding for debugger transport (LLDB DAP)
Use Cases: - Build Windows-native payloads/tools from a Linux workstation - Remote-debug Windows binaries inside a VM from VS Code on Linux - Rapid prototyping for exploit development across x64/x86/ARM64 - Reproducible CI-like local builds using containerized toolchains
Keywords: xwin, LLVM, Clang, lldb, lldb-server, LLDB DAP, VS Code, devcontainer, Docker, Podman, cross-compilation, Windows SDK, Universal CRT, OpenSSH Server, SSH port forwarding, Windows VM, Makefile, x86, ARM64, remote debugging

entry.title: unwind data cant sleep introducing insomniacunwinding
entry.summary:
Title: Unwind Data Can’t Sleep – Introducing InsomniacUnwinding
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post analyzes why common sleep-masking approaches break x64 stack walking and therefore produce suspicious/garbled call stacks when EDRs unwind threads during sleep. It explains Windows x64 unwinding (.pdata RUNTIME_FUNCTION entries and .rdata UNWIND_INFO) and shows that encrypting PE headers/.pdata/.rdata prevents RtlVirtualUnwind from reconstructing frames. The author introduces “InsomniacUnwinding”: preserve only the minimum unwind-critical bytes (PE headers, .pdata, and surgically extracted UNWIND_INFO structures) while encrypting the rest of the image to reduce signature exposure. A cross-process PoC encrypts/decrypts the beacon from an external helper via named pipes, avoiding the need for call stack spoofing when both components execute from backed (legitimate) modules. The technique is then applied in-process by modifying Ekko’s timer/NtContinue-based sleepmask to patch preserved regions via timer-queued RtlCopyMemory calls. It’s useful for red teamers and implant developers trying to combine memory encryption during sleep with clean, unwindable call stacks and reduced YARA-detectable plaintext.
Technical Focus: - Windows x64 stack unwinding (.pdata, RUNTIME_FUNCTION, UNWIND_INFO, RtlVirtualUnwind) - Sleep masking / in-memory encryption tradeoffs vs call stack integrity - Surgical extraction/preservation of UNWIND_INFO referenced by .pdata - Cross-process memory modification (OpenProcess, ReadProcessMemory, WriteProcessMemory) + named pipes IPC - Timer-queue/NtContinue-based execution chains (Ekko) and context-driven API invocation
Use Cases: - Implement sleep masking that keeps call stacks unwindable during sleep - Reduce memory-scanner/YARA hits by encrypting most of .rdata while preserving only unwind metadata - Build cross-process “encryptor” helpers to avoid self-encryption pitfalls and spoofing requirements - Enhance Ekko-style timer sleepmasks to maintain valid unwind data for main-thread stack walking - Research/detection testing of stack-unwinding failure modes as a signal for encrypted implants
Keywords: Windows x64 unwinding, RtlVirtualUnwind, UNWIND_INFO, RUNTIME_FUNCTION, .pdata, .rdata, IMAGE_DIRECTORY_ENTRY_EXCEPTION, PE headers, sleep masking, memory encryption, call stack walking, YARA scanning, cross-process injection, ReadProcessMemory, WriteProcessMemory, named pipes, Ekko, CreateTimerQueueTimer, NtContinue, CONTEXT structures, RtlCopyMemory, backed vs unbacked memory, module stomping, Cobalt Strike Beacon

entry.title: InsomniacUnwinding
entry.summary:
Title: InsomniacUnwinding — Surgical UNWIND_INFO preservation for sleep masking
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): InsomniacUnwinding is a Windows sleep-masking PoC based on the Ekko technique that encrypts a payload image during sleep while keeping stack unwinding functional. Instead of preserving large portions of `.rdata`, it “surgically” restores only the PE headers, `.pdata`, and the minimal `UNWIND_INFO` structures required for correct stack walking (~250 bytes). It extends the Ekko timer chain to temporarily decrypt/restore these regions before and after sleeping, maintaining a normal-looking call stack without relying on call stack spoofing. The repo includes YARA testing to demonstrate that embedded signatures disappear while sleeping because most of the image is encrypted. It’s primarily useful for red teamers and malware developers experimenting with stealthy in-memory sleep behavior and for defenders studying how sleep masking can preserve unwind metadata. The key takeaway is that call stack spoofing is often a side-effect of unbacked/invalid unwind data during sleep masking, not a strict requirement.
Technical Focus: - Windows x64 exception handling and stack unwinding (`UNWIND_INFO`, `.pdata`) - Sleep masking via timed callback chains (Ekko-style) - In-memory image encryption/decryption (`SystemFunction032`) - Memory protection transitions (`VirtualProtect` RW/RX) - PE structure preservation (headers, section data) - YARA-in-memory scanning validation
Use Cases: - Implementing sleep masking that keeps debuggers/stack walkers resolving frames correctly - Reducing forensic/detection artifacts while a payload is dormant (encrypted image) - Researching EDR telemetry around unwind metadata integrity and sleep-masked code - Building test cases for defensive detections on `.pdata`/unwind tampering and timed sleep chains
Keywords: sleep masking, Ekko, UNWIND_INFO, .pdata, stack unwinding, x64 SEH, PE headers, VirtualProtect, SystemFunction032, RtlCopyMemory, WaitForSingleObject, SetEvent, timer chain, in-memory encryption, call stack spoofing, backed memory, YARA, BaseThreadInitThunk, RtlUserThreadStart

entry.title: rpc proxy injection part ii breaking elastic edr telemetry 7eac298508c2
entry.summary:
Title: RPC Proxy Injection Part II: Breaking Elastic EDR Telemetry
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post is a continuation of an RPC-based “proxy injection” PoC that aims to mask the origin of process injection by splitting responsibilities between an RPC server (injector) and a DLL (helper). It focuses on evading Elastic EDR telemetry that flags the classic `VirtualAllocEx` + `WriteProcessMemory` pattern by replacing `WriteProcessMemory` with a “data smuggling” approach using Windows thread descriptions. The technique sets a target thread’s description to hold shellcode bytes, forces the target to allocate/return the description buffer via an APC calling `GetThreadDescription`, then copies from that buffer into the final RWX region using an APC to `RtlMoveMemory`. It also replaces `CreateRemoteThread` execution with `NtQueueApcThreadEx2` (special user APC) to trigger shellcode execution. The implementation includes chunking to bypass thread-description size limits and uses an unused PEB offset as a remote scratch location for pointer handoff. It’s mainly useful for malware developers and red team operators exploring EDR telemetry gaps, and for blue teams looking to detect unconventional injection primitives beyond the usual Win32 APIs.
Technical Focus: - Windows RPC (ncalrpc) used as an execution/proxy orchestration channel - Thread Description abuse (`SetThreadDescription` / `GetThreadDescription`) for payload staging - APC-based injection/execution via `NtQueueApcThreadEx2` (special user APC) - Avoiding `WriteProcessMemory` / `CreateRemoteThread` behavioral detections - PEB-based remote scratch storage (unused PEB offset) and remote pointer retrieval - Encrypted shellcode staging/decryption (Speck CBC) and chunked copying
Use Cases: - Bypassing EDR detections tied to `WriteProcessMemory` and `CreateRemoteThread` - Researching alternative cross-process memory staging primitives (thread metadata abuse) - Building PoCs to test telemetry coverage for undocumented/native syscalls (APC paths) - Developing detections for suspicious thread-description usage and special-user APC queuing - Demonstrating “responsibility splitting” across components (DLL + RPC server) to reduce single-process suspicious sequences
Keywords: Windows RPC, ncalrpc, process injection, Elastic EDR, telemetry evasion, WriteProcessMemory, VirtualAllocEx, CreateRemoteThread, APC injection, NtQueueApcThreadEx2, QUEUE_USER_APC_FLAGS_SPECIAL_USER_APC, GetThreadDescription, SetThreadDescription, RtlMoveMemory, NtSetInformationThread, ThreadNameInformation, PEB, NtQueryInformationProcess, ReadProcessMemory, Speck cipher, shellcode staging, T1055

entry.title: import patching
entry.summary:
Title: Injecting Code using Imported Functions into Native PE Files (Import Patching)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post explains how to inject new code into an existing native Windows PE without source code, where the injected code needs to call external APIs not currently imported by the binary. - The core problem is that extending or relocating the existing Import Lookup Table (ILT) / Import Address Table (IAT) can overwrite adjacent data or require rewriting code/data references—often infeasible without full disassembly/reassembly. - The proposed solution rebuilds the import directory in a new section and uses per-import trampoline stubs so the original code can keep referencing the original IAT entries unchanged. - Each original IAT entry is patched to point to a trampoline, which then jumps through the corresponding entry in the new IAT, effectively “rewiring” imports without touching `.text`. - The post includes a C# implementation using AsmResolver to add new sections, generate thunk stubs, rebuild import buffers, and update PE data directories. - Useful for red teamers, reverse engineers, and tool authors building binary patchers/obfuscators/loaders; interesting because it’s a generic import-extension method that avoids complex code relocation.
Technical Focus: - PE import internals: ILT, IAT, Import Directory, Data Directories - IAT patching and thunk/trampoline generation (x64) - Adding new PE sections and updating headers/offsets - AsmResolver PEFile/PEImage manipulation and ImportDirectoryBuffer - Address fixups/relocations considerations for patched binaries
Use Cases: - Injecting functionality into closed-source executables while preserving original code layout - Adding new imported APIs (e.g., CRT/Win32) to support injected payload logic - Building binary-level obfuscators/deobfuscators that need controlled import rewriting - Instrumentation/introspection by rerouting imported calls through custom stubs - Creating patched “loader-like” behavior by inserting a new entry stub then jumping to original entry point
Keywords: Portable Executable, PE, Import Directory, Import Lookup Table, ILT, Import Address Table, IAT, thunk, trampoline, x86-64, code injection, binary patching, section injection, data directories, AsmResolver, C#, ucrtbase.dll, puts, entry point patching, relocations

entry.title: bypassing image load kernel callbacks
entry.summary:
Title: Bypassing Image Load Kernel Callbacks
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post analyzes Windows “image load” kernel callbacks (registered via `PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine`) that security products use to observe EXE/DLL/driver loads, and why they’re hard to evade from low-privilege userland. - By tracing `LoadLibrary` behavior, it shows the callback is triggered when the loader maps an image section (`SEC_IMAGE`) and calls `NtMapViewOfSection`, meaning standard module loading inherently generates these events. - The author demonstrates “spoofing” image-load telemetry by manually opening a PE on disk, creating an image section, and mapping it—generating large volumes of image-load events without actually loading the module into the process in the normal sense. - To truly avoid image-load callbacks, the post describes building a custom userland PE loader that maps/copies sections without `SEC_IMAGE`, performs relocations/import resolution/TLS/exception registration, and then links the module into loader structures so `GetProcAddress`/`GetModuleHandle` work. - Key work includes locating and updating undocumented loader internals like the loader hash table and the module base-address red-black tree index. - It introduces **DarkLoadLibrary**, a `LoadLibrary`-like implementation designed not to trigger image-load events, plus a concealment routine to remove PEB traces and encrypt/de-exec mapped regions; useful primarily for red teams and malware developers focused on telemetry evasion and OPSEC.
Technical Focus: - `PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine` image-load notify callbacks - Windows loader internals (`Ldrp*` routines, PEB/LDR lists) - `NtOpenFile` / `NtCreateSection(SEC_IMAGE)` / `NtMapViewOfSection` call chain - Custom in-memory PE loading (relocations, imports, TLS, SEH) - Loader hash table (x65599 / `RtlHashUnicodeString`) and module indexing (RB-tree)
Use Cases: - Implementing userland module loading that avoids kernel image-load telemetry - Generating decoy/noise image-load events to degrade or mislead monitoring pipelines - Building stealthier implants that still support `GetProcAddress`/`GetModuleHandle` - Concealing in-memory modules from scanners by unlinking from PEB and encrypting pages
Keywords: Windows loader, image load callbacks, PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine, NtMapViewOfSection, NtCreateSection, SEC_IMAGE, NtOpenFile, PEB, LDR_DATA_TABLE_ENTRY, InLoadOrderModuleList, LdrpMapDllWithSectionHandle, LdrpMinimalMapModule, GetProcAddress, GetModuleHandle, RtlHashUnicodeString, x65599, LdrpHashTable, red-black tree, RtlRbInsertNodeEx, DarkLoadLibrary, reflective loading, Sysmon image load events, telemetry evasion

entry.title: DarkLoadLibrary
entry.summary:
Title: DarkLoadLibrary
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - DarkLoadLibrary is a custom Windows DLL loader intended for offensive tradecraft, providing a “LoadLibrary-like” capability without relying on the standard loader path. - It supports loading a DLL either from disk (via a path usable by `CreateFileW`) or directly from an in-memory buffer, enabling fileless-style module loading. - An optional mode (`NO_LINK`) avoids linking the loaded module into the PEB module lists, reducing visibility to user-mode enumeration and some monitoring. - The project is associated with research on bypassing image-load kernel callbacks (commonly used by EDR for DLL load telemetry), indicating a focus on evasion of load-notification-based detections. - It is primarily useful for red teams, penetration testers, and implant/tooling developers who need controlled module mapping and execution behavior. - The author notes compatibility gaps versus the full Windows loader due to edge cases (imports, relocations, loader quirks), so some DLLs may not load correctly.
Technical Focus: - Custom PE/DLL loading (manual mapping-style loader) - Loading modules from memory buffers vs filesystem - PEB module list linking/unlinking (stealth loading) - Evasion of image-load monitoring / kernel callback telemetry - Windows loader behavior and edge-case handling
Use Cases: - In-memory loading of post-exploitation capability DLLs without dropping to disk - Reducing module visibility by avoiding PEB linkage (`NO_LINK`) - Testing/validating defensive detections around image-load events and module enumeration - Building custom loaders for implants where standard `LoadLibrary` is too observable
Keywords: Windows, DLL loader, LoadLibrary, manual mapping, PE loading, in-memory DLL, reflective loading, PEB, module list, CreateFileW, image load callbacks, PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine, EDR evasion, user-mode telemetry, offensive tooling, C, Windows internals

entry.title: t rop h thread hijacking without executable memory allocation d746c102a9ca
entry.summary:
Title: T(ROP)H: Thread Hijacking with ROP (Thread Hijacking without Executable Memory Allocation)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post demonstrates a Windows x64 thread-hijacking DLL injection technique that avoids allocating RWX (or otherwise executable) memory in a remote process, reducing a common injection IOC. Instead of writing shellcode and redirecting RIP to it, the author builds a minimal ROP chain to call `LoadLibraryA` inside an existing remote thread. The key challenge addressed is that `SetThreadContext` can’t directly set volatile registers like `RCX` (needed for the first argument in the Windows x64 calling convention), so a `pop rcx; ret` gadget is used to load the DLL path pointer from the hijacked thread’s stack. The walkthrough covers finding a suitable gadget (e.g., in `ntdll.dll`), writing the DLL path into remote memory, suspending the thread, adjusting `RIP/RSP`, writing the ROP stack frame, and resuming execution. It’s useful for red teamers/pentesters and malware developers exploring stealthier injection tradeoffs, and for defenders understanding how “no RWX” injection can still occur. The post also notes detectability remains high due to `SetThreadContext` generating ETW telemetry (Kernel-Audit-API-Calls and Threat-Intelligence providers).
Technical Focus: - Windows x64 calling convention (RCX argument passing) and volatile registers - Thread hijacking via `SuspendThread` / `GetThreadContext` / `SetThreadContext` / `ResumeThread` - ROP gadget discovery and runtime address resolution (e.g., `pop rcx; ret` in `ntdll.dll`) - DLL injection via `LoadLibraryA` with remote `VirtualAllocEx` + `WriteProcessMemory` (RW only) - Stack pivoting/stack frame setup for ROP (RSP adjustment, return chaining to `ExitThread`) - ETW-based detection of `SetThreadContext` usage
Use Cases: - DLL injection without remote executable memory allocation (avoid RWX heuristics) - Proof-of-concept process injection research and tradeoff evaluation vs classic shellcode hijacking - Building/validating detections for thread-context tampering and ROP-based execution - Adversary emulation scenarios focusing on “living-off-existing-code” execution patterns
Keywords: Windows x64, thread hijacking, ROP, return-oriented programming, SetThreadContext, GetThreadContext, SuspendThread, ResumeThread, RIP, RSP, RCX, pop rcx; ret, LoadLibraryA, ExitThread, VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, ntdll.dll, kernel32.dll, ETW, Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence

entry.title: AnneFrankInjector
entry.summary:
Title: AnneFrankInjector
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): AnneFrankInjector is a Windows shellcode loader/builder aimed at AV/EDR evasion for CTFs, red team ops, and certification labs. It generates EXE or DLL loaders that either embed shellcode (stageless) or retrieve it over HTTP (staged), with optional on-the-fly encryption and obfuscation. The project combines multiple stealth techniques including indirect syscalls (SysWhispers-style), API hashing (Djb2), and NTDLL unhooking via KnownDLLs to reduce user-mode monitoring. Execution options include EarlyBird APC injection, spawn-and-inject into a newly created benign process, and configurable sleep delays to evade sandbox timeouts. It also includes optional persistence mechanisms (Run key, scheduled task, startup folder) plus “reflective/fileless” and LNK stager modes, wrapped in both a CLI and a tkinter GUI. Useful primarily for red teamers/pentesters and malware researchers evaluating loader tradecraft and defensive coverage.
Technical Focus: - Indirect syscalls (SysWhispers3-style) and syscall-based evasion - API hashing (Djb2) and dynamic API resolution - NTDLL unhooking via KnownDLLs mapping - EarlyBird APC injection and process spawn injection - Payload encryption (AES-128-CBC) and string/name scrambling - Staged vs stageless shellcode delivery (HTTP stager)
Use Cases: - Generate evasive EXE/DLL shellcode loaders for controlled red team engagements - Test EDR/AV detection of common loader primitives (APC, unhooking, indirect syscalls) - Build staged payload delivery over HTTP with encrypted shellcode - Create LNK-based initial access stagers (PowerShell) for lab simulations - Evaluate persistence detections (Run keys, scheduled tasks, startup folder)
Keywords: shellcode loader, AV evasion, EDR bypass, indirect syscalls, SysWhispers3, API hashing, Djb2, NTDLL unhooking, KnownDLLs, EarlyBird APC injection, process injection, spawn injection, AES-128-CBC, staged payload, HTTP stager, reflective loader, fileless execution, persistence, Run key, scheduled task, startup folder, rundll32, LNK stager, PowerShell EncodedCommand, MinGW-w64, NASM

entry.title: Reflective DLL Injection
entry.summary:
Title: Reflective DLL Injection via Manual Mapping (YetAnotherReflectiveLoader)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post walks through implementing reflective DLL injection by manually mapping a PE image into a remote process instead of using `LoadLibraryA`. - It contrasts classic DLL injection (`VirtualAllocEx` → `WriteProcessMemory` → `CreateRemoteThread`) with a custom PE loader that performs allocation, header/section copying, relocations, TLS callback execution, import resolution (IAT patching), and entrypoint invocation. - The approach is designed to reduce common EDR/AV telemetry by avoiding high-signal APIs where possible (e.g., using direct/indirect syscalls like `ZwAllocateVirtualMemory`/`NtWriteVirtualMemory`) and by doing environment-dependent steps inside the target via shellcode. - It includes stealth/cleanup considerations: zeroing PE headers, converting RWX mappings into per-section protections (`RX`/`RW`) via `VirtualProtect`, and removing the loader shellcode region (“suicide by micro-stub”). - The write-up is most useful for red teamers, malware developers, and researchers studying PE loading internals and injection tradeoffs on modern Windows/Defender configurations. - It’s interesting because it ties together PE format parsing, loader re-implementation details, and practical OPSEC improvements (PEB heap usage, permission hardening) in a single end-to-end flow.
Technical Focus: - Manual PE mapping (custom PE loader) in a remote process - Base relocations (`.reloc`, `IMAGE_BASE_RELOCATION`, delta patching) - TLS callback discovery/execution (`IMAGE_DIRECTORY_ENTRY_TLS`) - Import resolution and IAT patching (`IMAGE_IMPORT_DESCRIPTOR`, thunks, ordinal vs name) - Syscalls / native APIs (`ZwAllocateVirtualMemory`, `NtWriteVirtualMemory`) and API-hook evasion concepts - Post-injection stealth: header wiping, section-based `VirtualProtect`, shellcode self-deallocation
Use Cases: - Building a reflective loader/manual mapper for in-memory DLL execution - Studying Windows loader behavior (relocations, TLS, imports) for offensive tooling - Reducing injection artifacts (RWX regions, obvious PE headers) during adversary emulation - Developing/validating detections for manual mapping indicators (IAT patching, anomalous protections, header tampering) - Creating shellcode that finalizes mapping steps from within the target process context
Keywords: reflective DLL injection, manual mapping, PE loader, Windows internals, Portable Executable, IMAGE_NT_HEADERS, SizeOfImage, ImageBase, NtWriteVirtualMemory, ZwAllocateVirtualMemory, relocations, IMAGE_BASE_RELOCATION, IMAGE_REL_BASED_DIR64, TLS callbacks, IMAGE_TLS_DIRECTORY, import resolution, IAT patching, IMAGE_IMPORT_DESCRIPTOR, CreateRemoteThread, VirtualProtect, PEB, RtlAllocateHeap, RWX memory, header stomping

entry.title: Release Reboot
entry.summary:
Title: Brute Ratel v1.6 “Reboot” Release Notes (Ratel Server/Badger/Commander)
Type: Documentation
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This release post summarizes Brute Ratel v1.6 updates across the C2 server, the Badger implant, and the Commander UI, with emphasis on evasion, stability, and operator QoL. - It adds LDAP Sentinel sleep/jitter plus SASL (encrypted bind) with Kerberos fallback and attribute filtering to reduce noisy LDAP enumeration and evade network detections. - New post-ex features include ACL enumeration via `GetSecurityDescriptorDacl`, process environment variable modification (`setenv`), a lightweight HTTP(S) “curl” command, and optional secure file deletion (multi-pass overwrite) via `rm rf`. - Evasion-focused changes include disabling userland DLL load telemetry (to counter EDR userland LoadLibrary-style hooks) and lowering network traffic entropy for staged/stageless payloads using malleability and transport changes. - SMB Badger named-pipe access is relaxed (Everyone DACL) to support scenarios like IIS exploitation where credentials/tokens aren’t available. - Commander gains Windows 10 support, Hi-DPI scaling, theming via stylesheets, and multiple UI/UX improvements for managing sessions, logs, downloads, and connectivity state.
Technical Focus: - LDAP enumeration evasion (sleep/jitter, SASL bind encryption, attribute filtering) - Windows security descriptors and DACL parsing (`GetSecurityDescriptorDacl`) - Anti-forensics (secure deletion / overwrite-on-delete) - EDR evasion (userland DLL load notification suppression, entropy reduction, sleep obfuscation + module stomping stability) - C2 transport/malleability and DNS comms backend updates - SMB named pipe access control changes for payload connectivity
Use Cases: - Stealthier AD reconnaissance and custom BloodHound-compatible data collection (with BofHound) - On-host permission auditing of files/folders during post-exploitation - Operating reliably inside constrained service contexts (IIS/service processes) by setting missing environment variables - Quick internal web app reachability checks without standing up SOCKS - Reducing forensic recoverability of dropped tooling/artifacts - Improving C2 survivability against network inspection/EDR telemetry and operator workflow via Commander enhancements
Keywords: Brute Ratel, BRc4, Badger, Ratel Server, Commander, LDAP Sentinel, SASL, Kerberos, LDAP bind, sleep jitter, BloodHound, BofHound, DACL, GetSecurityDescriptorDacl, named pipe, SMB, PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutineEx, LoadLibrary hooking, module stomping, sleep obfuscation, network entropy, DNS C2, malleable C2, secure deletion, EnCase, Autopsy, CrowdStrike, Defender ATP, Elastic EDR, FortiEDR

entry.title: Release Pandemonium
entry.summary:
Title: Brute Ratel v1.7 “Pandemonium” Release (Fallback C2, Module Stomping Extensions, Apple Silicon Support)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This release post describes Brute Ratel v1.7 (“Pandemonium”), a major overhaul of the Badger implant, Ratel Server, and Commander, with breaking compatibility from v1.6. - The key operational improvement is a “Fallback C2 Strategy” that lets operators chain multiple malleable profiles (HTTP/DoH) and autonomously rotate profiles/domains when blocks occur, reducing long-term reuse of a single profile across many hosts. - It extends the platform’s “Advanced Module Stomping” to in-memory PE execution (memexec) and BOF execution (coffexec), including PEB patching and restoring original DLL bytes on sleep/completion to reduce memory-vs-disk detection (e.g., Pe-sieve style checks). - Additional capabilities include screen recording for operator activity monitoring, full Unicode support across many file/identity commands, and a “memexec profiler” for registering in-memory executables as built-in commands via JSON profiles. - Commander gains official Apple Silicon support, runtime theme/font changes, JSON profile upload for listeners/commands/autoruns/clickscripts, and several UX/reporting changes. - Useful primarily for red teams and adversary emulation operators focused on resilient C2 and post-execution evasion under modern EDR scrutiny.
Technical Focus: - C2 resilience via chained fallback malleable profiles (HTTP/DoH) and autonomous rotation - Module stomping for in-memory execution (PE/BOF), PEB patching, and byte restoration - In-memory execution workflows: memexec and coffexec (BOF) - Operator tooling enhancements: screen recording and Unicode-safe file/identity operations - Commander cross-platform support (Apple Silicon) and JSON-driven profiling/configuration
Use Cases: - Maintain C2 connectivity by automatically failing over to alternate domains/profiles when primary infrastructure is blocked - Execute BOFs and PEs in memory while reducing suspicious memory artifacts via module stomping and restoration - Capture user workflows on sensitive systems (e.g., SWIFT operator activity) through screen recording for tradecraft/understanding - Standardize and rapidly deploy listener/command configurations using JSON profiles across engagements - Operate Brute Ratel Commander natively on macOS Apple Silicon and Windows 11 environments
Keywords: Brute Ratel, Badger, Ratel Server, Commander, C2 fallback strategy, malleable profiles, HTTP C2, DoH, domain rotation, redirectors, module stomping, PEB patching, memexec, coffexec, BOF, in-memory execution, IAT, Pe-sieve, screen recording, Apple Silicon ARM64, Windows 11, Unicode support, JSON profiles

entry.title: Release Mirage
entry.summary:
Title: Brute Ratel v1.8 “Mirage” Release – Evading Every EDR On The Planet Part 2
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post announces Brute Ratel C4 v1.8 (codename “Mirage”) and highlights major internal rewrites focused on operational security and EDR evasion. Key additions include full stack frame spoofing even during active execution (“sleep zero”), exception-handler unhooking that targets both vectored exception handling and hardware breakpoint (SINGLE_STEP) telemetry, and a “Raw DNS Badger” option alongside existing DoH/DNS listener support. The stack spoofing changes aim to prevent EDRs from tracing execution back to in-memory shellcode regions via stack walking/ETW and then performing memory scanning/signature checks. The release also improves operator workflow and tradecraft features (custom PsExec service binaries, net use share mounting, staged payload support in the profiler, large file downloads, and API-based bulk badger removal). It’s primarily useful for red teams and adversary simulation operators focused on stealthy post-exploitation and resilient C2 in monitored Windows environments. It’s notable because it explicitly targets modern EDR stack-tracing and exception-based telemetry collection techniques rather than only relying on indirect syscalls/return-address spoofing.
Technical Focus: - Stack frame spoofing / synthetic call stacks (sleep-zero execution) - EDR telemetry evasion via ETW/stack walking countermeasures - VEH and hardware breakpoint (SINGLE_STEP) exception unhooking - DNS/DoH C2 transport (“Raw DNS Badger”) - Post-exploitation tradecraft (PsExec service execs, SMB share mounting) - AD/BloodHound data handling (LDAP security descriptors, BOFHound compatibility)
Use Cases: - Running post-exploitation tasks while reducing stack-trace attribution to shellcode memory - Bypassing EDR user-mode telemetry that leverages VEH and hardware breakpoint exceptions - Operating C2 over DNS/DoH or raw DNS in restricted egress environments - Lateral movement customization via PsExec-compatible service payloads - Automating agent (“badger”) lifecycle management via server API scripts - Feeding improved LDAP/SD data into BloodHound workflows via BOFHound
Keywords: Brute Ratel, BRC4, Mirage, badger, C2, EDR evasion, stack spoofing, sleep zero, call stack, ETW, Kernel ETW, Vectored Exception Handling, VEH unhooking, hardware breakpoints, SINGLE_STEP, shellcode, module stomping, DNS C2, DoH, LDAP security descriptor, BOFHound, BloodHound, PsExec, SMB net use, SOCKS proxy, port forwarding

entry.title: Release Metamorphosis
entry.summary:
Title: Brute Ratel v2.0 “Metamorphosis” Release – Everything Everywhere All At Once
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This release post describes major architectural and operational changes in Brute Ratel v2.0, including a heavily rewritten “Badger” implant, server optimizations, and Commander QoL updates. - It addresses two main problems: improving evasion/OPSEC capabilities in the implant (sleep masking, stack-chain control, loader/entry-point options) and tightening licensing enforcement to prevent multi-user/large-scale misuse of single-user licenses. - The Badger gains configurable sleep masking that preserves a valid stack-chain, operator-defined/custom stack frames for execution and library loading, improved SOCKS proxy performance, and new WinRM/COM-based pivoting commands. - The server adds mechanisms to “resurrect” removed/deauthed agents by importing agent profiles (JSON) and tracking a badger_counter, plus faster/more stable DoH comms and shellcode changes aimed at reducing static detections. - Commander improvements focus on safer profile/listener editing, better help/escaping guidance, and trimming/formatting to reduce operator errors. - Useful primarily for experienced red team operators and toolsmiths who need malleable C2/implant behavior and understand OPSEC tradeoffs; it’s notable for explicit stack-chain/sleep-masking configurability and operational handling of deauthed agents.
Technical Focus: - Sleep masking with stack-chain validity - Operator-defined stack frames / call stack spoofing controls - Implant execution configuration (entry point, offset, core-thread execution method) - WinRM + COM object lateral movement/pivoting - DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH) C2 performance/stability - Agent lifecycle management (deauth handling, profile import/restore)
Use Cases: - Configure stealthier implant runtime behavior (sleep masking + believable stack traces) for EDR-heavy environments - Run stable SOCKS pivoting for internal network access through compromised hosts - Lateral movement/pivoting via WinRM using COM automation - Recover/re-authenticate “removed” agents that later beacon back (deauth.log → JSON profile import) - Operate DoH-based comms for environments where DNS/HTTPS blending is advantageous
Keywords: Brute Ratel, Badger, Commander, Ratel Server, sleep masking, stack chain, call stack spoofing, custom stack frames, OPSEC, evasion, SOCKS4a, SOCKS5, WinRM, COM objects, lateral movement, pivoting, DNS over HTTPS, DoH C2, shellcode, deauth.log, agent resurrection, JSON profile import, licensing enforcement, hardware-bound licensing

entry.title: Release Rinnegan
entry.summary:
Title: Brute Ratel v2.2 “Rinnegan” Release Notes
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This release post summarizes public-facing changes in Brute Ratel v2.2 (Rinnegan), covering updates to the Badger implant, Commander UI, and the Ratel server/API ecosystem. It focuses on operational and evasion-related adjustments (e.g., changes to memexec hooking strategy, removal of certain signature-prone artifacts, and tweaks intended to reduce YARA/EDR detection opportunities). A major addition is a new Python library, `bruteratel.py`, plus 32 asyncio/WebSocket-based API wrapper scripts to automate common C2 tasks (listener/badger/profile management, command queueing, file hosting, backups, activity queries). The Commander UI core moves to Qt 6.9 for better compatibility with modern Linux distros (e.g., Ubuntu 24.04). The post also notes .NET tradecraft improvements via `sharpinline` changes and new commands for encrypted in-memory storage/execution of multiple .NET assemblies, plus “working hours” callback scheduling. Useful primarily for red teams/operators who automate Brute Ratel workflows and want updated OPSEC/evasion behavior; also relevant to defenders tracking evolving C2/implant tradecraft.
Technical Focus: - C2 framework release changes (Badger/Commander/Ratel server) - Evasion/OPSEC hardening (YARA/EDR signature avoidance, artifact reduction) - AsyncIO WebSocket API automation (`bruteratel.py` + wrapper scripts) - In-memory execution and command execution plumbing (memexec hooking changes) - .NET in-memory execution pipeline (`sharpinline`, AMSI-related behavior) - LDAP query tuning to reduce detection surface
Use Cases: - Automate Brute Ratel server operations (list/add/remove listeners, badgers, profiles) - Script command dispatching and queue management at scale via WebSocket API - Manage hosted files/downloads and server backups programmatically - Execute .NET assemblies in-memory with reduced repeated upload needs (encrypted “dotnet store”) - Configure callback “working hours” to match target time zones and reduce noise - Adjust operational profiles to minimize signature-based detections (YARA/telemetry-aware changes)
Keywords: Brute Ratel, BRc4, Badger, Commander, Ratel server, Qt 6.9, WebSocket API, asyncio, Python automation, bruteratel.py, memexec, indirect syscalls, YARA signatures, EDR evasion, sharpinline, CLR invocation, AMSI, LDAP Sentinel queries, BOF APIs, token manipulation

entry.title: Release Flux
entry.summary:
Title: Brute Ratel v2.3 “Flux” Release Notes (Badger implant redevelopment, Safe HTTP, Advanced Async BOFs)
Type: Documentation
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This release describes Brute Ratel v2.3 (Flux), focused on a major redevelopment of the Badger implant using a custom-built compiler to improve OpSec and reduce memory footprint (~30% smaller, with more planned). - It addresses limitations in common toolchains (MinGW/Clang) around memory regions/stack layouts that conflicted with stealth requirements. - Key additions include “safe_http,” which uses a tiny PIC stub to perform HTTP transactions while keeping Badger code/heap encrypted so the implant is effectively absent from memory during network activity, reducing ETW/EDR memory-scan exposure tied to WinINet/WinHTTP. - It introduces an “Advanced Async BOF” framework via `coffexec_async`, enabling BOFs to run while Badger remains sleep-masked/hidden, with new BOF APIs for forced sleep, wake/send, stop-task checks, buffer upload, and token handling. - Several built-in capabilities (e.g., portscan, dcsync, pth) are ported to async BOFs to keep the main implant masked during longer operations. - Useful primarily for red teams/pentesters and implant developers focused on stealthy post-exploitation tradecraft and BOF-based extensibility under modern EDR scrutiny.
Technical Focus: - Custom compiler/toolchain tailoring for implant stealth (stack layout, memory region handling, size reduction) - Sleep masking and in-memory encryption of code/heap - PIC stubs for “safe” HTTP transactions and reduced network-triggered memory scanning - Asynchronous COFF/BOF execution model (`coffexec_async`) and new BOF APIs - ETW/EDR detection pressure around WinINet/WinHTTP network activity - Task control, token handling, and buffered exfil/upload via main HTTP thread
Use Cases: - Running long or noisy post-exploitation actions while keeping the primary implant sleep-masked - Building BOFs that can force masking, wake to send buffered output, and exit cleanly - Reducing memory-resident indicators during HTTP C2 check-ins/transactions - Porting internal commands (portscan, samdump, pth, dcsync) into async BOF workflows for better OpSec - Improving operational stability/performance for socks and remote port forwarding
Keywords: Brute Ratel, Badger implant, v2.3 Flux, custom compiler, MinGW, Clang, OpSec, memory footprint reduction, sleep masking, in-memory encryption, PIC stub, safe_http, WinINet, WinHTTP, ETW, EDR evasion, COFF, BOF, coffexec_async, BadgerForceSleep, BadgerWakeupAndExit, BadgerWakeupAndSend, BadgerDispatch, BadgerStopTask, BadgerDownloadBuffer, BadgerSetToken, dcsync, pass-the-hash, samdump, portscan, socks, rportfwd

entry.title: process preluding
entry.summary:
Title: Process Preluding: Child Process Injection Before The Story Begins
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post analyzes a telemetry gap in Windows process creation where some kernel ETW Threat Intelligence (Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence / EtwTI) events are only enabled after security products receive process creation callbacks. It shows that by using legacy process creation syscalls (NtCreateProcess / NtCreateProcessEx) to create an “address-space-only” child process (no threads yet), an attacker can allocate memory, write shellcode, and set an execution trigger before the kernel notifies EDR via process creation callbacks. The PoC overwrites a low-artifact PEB field (PostProcessInitRoutine) to execute shellcode when the first thread is finally created, potentially avoiding EtwTI events like WriteProcessMemory/VirtualProtectEx that would normally be enabled per-process by EDR drivers. The technique is positioned as a timing/order-of-callbacks issue rather than a privilege escalation, and it parallels earlier “early bird / preload” concepts but applied to kernel telemetry enablement. It’s useful for red teams and malware developers exploring injection stealth, and for blue teams/EDR engineers to harden callback handling and detect legacy process creation. Defensive notes include detecting legacy creation via missing GUID_ECP_CREATE_USER_PROCESS and that WDAC policies may block these legacy APIs.
Technical Focus: - Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence (EtwTI) per-process logging flags (PROCESS_LOGGING_INFORMATION) - Kernel process creation callbacks (PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutineEx) timing assumptions - Legacy process creation APIs (NtCreateProcess, NtCreateSection, section-backed SEC_IMAGE) - Pre-thread “address space only” processes and PEB manipulation (PostProcessInitRoutine) - Remote memory operations (VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, VirtualProtectEx) and ETW visibility gaps - Detection opportunities: GUID_ECP_CREATE_USER_PROCESS, handle-creation callbacks, WDAC blocking behavior
Use Cases: - Pre-callback child-process injection to reduce ETW/EDR visibility during early process lifetime - Researching EDR telemetry dependencies on process creation notifications and per-process ETW enablement - Building detections for legacy process creation and “no-thread-yet” process states - Hardening EDR drivers to enable TI logging earlier or treat legacy-created processes as suspicious/blockable
Keywords: Windows ETW, Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence, EtwTI, PROCESS_LOGGING_INFORMATION, PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutineEx, process creation callback, NtCreateProcess, NtCreateProcessEx, NtCreateUserProcess, NtCreateSection, SEC_IMAGE, PEB, PostProcessInitRoutine, VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, VirtualProtectEx, CreateRemoteThread, GUID_ECP_CREATE_USER_PROCESS, WDAC, process injection, telemetry gap, early bird injection

entry.title: enumerating windows process creation callbacks 98e09153e2d7
entry.summary:
Title: Enumerating Windows Process Creation Callbacks
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post explains how Windows tracks kernel-mode process creation notification callbacks via the `PspCreateProcessNotifyRoutine` array and demonstrates enumerating those callbacks from user mode. - It solves the problem of identifying which security/EDR drivers are registered to receive process creation events (i.e., who “sees” new processes immediately). - The technique uses `NtQuerySystemInformation(SystemModuleInformation)` to map loaded kernel modules, then leverages a BYOVD vulnerable driver exposing an arbitrary kernel read primitive to read and walk the 64-entry callback array. - It covers decoding the stored callback pointers (masking flag bits), dereferencing the `EX_CALLBACK` structure to recover the real function pointer, and attributing each callback to a driver by address range matching. - A Windows 11 PoC output shows callbacks attributed to drivers like `WdFilter.sys` (Defender) and other core components. - The post also discusses detection realities (often the vulnerable driver is detected, not the enumeration logic) and provides a sample YARA rule to flag binaries implementing this enumeration pattern. - Useful for red teams/malware devs doing EDR recon/evasion research and for blue teams building detections around BYOVD + kernel callback enumeration behavior.
Technical Focus: - `PspCreateProcessNotifyRoutine` process creation notify callbacks - BYOVD kernel read primitives via `DeviceIoControl` IOCTLs - `NtQuerySystemInformation` / `SystemModuleInformation` kernel module enumeration - Pointer decoding / flag-bit masking and `EX_CALLBACK` dereference - Callback-to-driver attribution via module base/size range checks - YARA-based static detection patterns for callback enumeration tooling
Use Cases: - Enumerate which EDR/security drivers monitor process creation on a host - Kernel telemetry reconnaissance prior to process injection/spawn-heavy tradecraft - Validate whether a driver successfully registered process notify callbacks - Build detections/hunts for BYOVD-assisted kernel memory inspection activity - Research Windows kernel callback internals and common EDR monitoring points
Keywords: Windows kernel, PspCreateProcessNotifyRoutine, process creation callbacks, PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutine, EX_CALLBACK, ntoskrnl.exe, NtQuerySystemInformation, SystemModuleInformation, BYOVD, vulnerable driver, arbitrary kernel read, DeviceIoControl, IOCTL, GIO, GDRV, WdFilter.sys, EDR telemetry, kernel memory enumeration, YARA rule, Windows 11

entry.title: windows early boot configuration the cmcontrolvector and pspsystemmitigationoptions
entry.summary:
Title: Windows Early Boot Configuration: The CmControlVector and PspSystemMitigationOptions
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post reverse-engineers how Windows initializes system-wide process mitigation settings during early kernel boot, focusing on the global `PspSystemMitigationOptions`. - It shows that these mitigation options are not read during normal runtime/process creation, but are populated in kernel init Phase 0 via the Configuration Manager using the `CmControlVector`. - The author traces execution to `nt!CmpGetSystemControlValues` and explains why tools like Procmon miss the registry reads (they occur before userland/most drivers are available). - It breaks down the `CM_CONTROL_VECTOR` entry format (registry key/value, target buffer, size/type/flags) and demonstrates that Windows 11 24H2 contains hundreds of such early-boot configuration entries. - Supporting artifacts include an IDA script to extract the vector, a JSON dump of entries, and basic stats/cross-reference mapping to identify which kernel subsystems consume which boot-time values. - Useful for Windows internals researchers, red teamers doing mitigation/IFEO analysis, and defenders validating when/where exploit-protection settings actually take effect (reboot-required semantics).
Technical Focus: - Windows kernel initialization (Phase 0 vs Phase 1) and early-boot registry processing - Configuration Manager internals (`CmControlVector`, `CmpGetSystemControlValues`) - Process mitigation initialization (`PspSystemMitigationOptions`, `_EPROCESS` MitigationOptions*) - Registry-based mitigation configuration (system-wide vs IFEO per-image) - Kernel debugging/reversing workflows (data breakpoints, IDA automation, xrefs)
Use Cases: - Determine why system-wide Exploit Protection changes require reboot and where they are latched - Map boot-time registry values to kernel globals and the subsystems that consume them - Support research into mitigation bypasses/behavior differences across Windows versions - Build detection/forensics logic around expected mitigation state derivation (system-wide + IFEO) - Automate extraction of early-boot configuration surfaces for auditing/hardening
Keywords: Windows kernel, Configuration Manager, CmControlVector, CM_CONTROL_VECTOR, CmpGetSystemControlValues, CmInitSystem0, Phase 0 initialization, early boot, registry, Session Manager\\Kernel, MitigationOptions, PspSystemMitigationOptions, IFEO, Image File Execution Options, _EPROCESS, MitigationFlags, MitigationOptions2, MitigationOptions3, kernel debugging, IDA Pro, reversing, Procmon boot trace

entry.title: edr xdr bypass and detection evasion techniques an investigation of advanced evasion strategies 9594946ad102
entry.summary:
Title: EDR/XDR Bypass and Detection Evasion Techniques: An Investigation of Advanced Evasion Strategies from a Red Team Perspective
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This article surveys modern EDR/XDR telemetry and enforcement mechanisms (user-mode hooks, ETW, kernel callbacks, memory integrity, cloud correlation) and why classic post-exploitation tradecraft increasingly fails. - It breaks down several evasion primitives—API unhooking, BOF/COFF in-memory execution, indirect/direct syscalls, ETW suppression, and kernel callback tampering—explaining how each targets a specific visibility layer. - The post emphasizes “layered evasion” (combining techniques like indirect syscalls + ETW patching, or in-memory execution + sleep obfuscation) rather than relying on single bypasses. - It introduces “time-difference/latency” attacks that exploit delays between kernel notifications and user-mode/cloud analysis to execute actions before enforcement. - Case-study style sections discuss practical constraints and detection differences across products (e.g., Bitdefender user-mode hook focus; CrowdStrike/SentinelOne behavioral/stack-tracing sensitivity) and highlight residual detection vectors (memory permission flips, syscall patterns, integrity checks). - Useful for red teams/malware developers to understand where telemetry originates and for blue teams to derive hardening and detection ideas (integrity validation, syscall/stack anomaly detection, timer/APC monitoring, HVCI/LSA protections).
Technical Focus: - User-mode API hooking and ntdll.dll unhooking (inline/IAT hooks) - BOF/COFF in-memory execution and custom loaders (C2 “inline-execute” model) - Direct/indirect system calls and syscall pattern/call-stack evasion - ETW provider telemetry suppression (EtwEventWrite patching) and logging blind spots - Kernel callbacks (PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutine / thread / image load) and tampering risks - Timing/latency exploitation (timer-based delayed execution, suspended threads)
Use Cases: - Evaluate how a given EDR collects telemetry (hooks vs ETW vs kernel callbacks) and map likely detection points - Design controlled red-team tradecraft tests for fileless execution and reduced process-creation artifacts (BOF workflows) - Build detection engineering hypotheses: monitor ntdll .text integrity changes, ETW write patching, anomalous syscall density, timer/APC-based delayed execution - Develop defensive hardening plans: enable HVCI/Credential Guard, protect driver/registry keys, correlate memory-permission transitions with network/credential access - Create lab benchmarks comparing tool variants (standalone PE vs BOF) and measure alerting under different EDRs/policies
Keywords: EDR, XDR, API hooking, inline hook, IAT hooking, ntdll.dll, API unhooking, BOF, COFF, Cobalt Strike, PostExpKit, in-memory execution, direct syscall, indirect syscall, ETW, EtwEventWrite, kernel callbacks, PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutine, stack tracing, sleep obfuscation, SetTimer, CreateRemoteThread, VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, HVCI, Credential Guard, behavioral correlation, cloud telemetry

entry.title: Kernel Callback Integrity Bypass
entry.summary:
Title: Windows Kernel Callback Integrity Bypass (PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutine from Manually Mapped Drivers)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This write-up explains why modern Windows blocks callback registration (e.g., `PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutine`, `ObRegisterCallbacks`) from manually mapped/unbacked kernel drivers, typically returning `STATUS_ACCESS_DENIED`. - It reverses the internal validation path (`MmVerifyCallbackFunctionCheckFlags` → `MiLockLoadedDataTableEntry` → `MmFindDataTableEntryByAddress`) and shows the core issue: the callback address must resolve to a legitimate loader entry in the kernel’s module tracking structures. - The author demonstrates a bypass by crafting a fake `_KLDR_DATA_TABLE_ENTRY`-like structure with an embedded `_RTL_BALANCED_NODE` and inserting it into `MmLoadedModuleTree` (a red-black tree keyed by module base/size). - The technique relies on correct structure layout/offsets (notably Win11 padding), safe initialization of list entries and persistent `UNICODE_STRING` buffers, and synchronized tree modification using `PsLoadedModuleResource`. - Useful for red teamers, kernel researchers, and malware developers working on manual mapping, stealth drivers, or post-exploitation kernel tooling that needs kernel callbacks. - Interesting because it documents a practical method to “re-back” an unbacked driver in kernel bookkeeping to satisfy callback integrity checks on current Windows/Defender configurations.
Technical Focus: - Kernel callback integrity checks (`MmVerifyCallbackFunctionCheckFlags`) - Loader/module tracking internals (`_KLDR_DATA_TABLE_ENTRY`, `PsLoadedModuleResource`) - `MmLoadedModuleTree` red-black tree traversal and insertion (`_RTL_BALANCED_NODE`) - Manual mapping / unbacked driver constraints and evasion - Kernel pool allocation and lifetime-safe `UNICODE_STRING` handling - Race-condition avoidance via kernel resource locks
Use Cases: - Register process/thread/image/OB callbacks from a manually mapped driver - Support stealthy kernel telemetry/interception without standard driver loading - Restore stability for kernel memory manipulation techniques needing process lifecycle hooks (e.g., VAD-related work) - Research/detection engineering: understand artifacts and invariants around module trees and callback registration
Keywords: Windows kernel, PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutine, PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutineEx, STATUS_ACCESS_DENIED, MmVerifyCallbackFunctionCheckFlags, MiLockLoadedDataTableEntry, MmFindDataTableEntryByAddress, MmLoadedModuleTree, PsLoadedModuleResource, _KLDR_DATA_TABLE_ENTRY, _RTL_BALANCED_NODE, red-black tree, intrusive data structures, manual mapping, unbacked driver, ExAllocatePool2, POOL_FLAG_NON_PAGED, UNICODE_STRING, RtlInitUnicodeString, NodeModuleLink, InLoadOrderLinks, IDA Pro, WinDbg

entry.title: bypassing static analysis deep dive
entry.summary:
Title: Bypassing static analysis – Deep Dive
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post breaks down how AV/EDR static scanning evaluates files when they land on disk, focusing on hashes, byte signatures, suspicious strings/IAT imports, and PE section entropy. - It demonstrates a practical workflow for signature evasion: use ThreatCheck to identify flagged byte sequences, then reverse in Ghidra to locate the responsible code/constant and modify implementation details without changing behavior (example: altering AES routines in a Havoc payload). - It covers packing as an alternative (PEzor + donut), including tradeoffs like packer templates being signatured and environment-bound decryption (e.g., using GetComputerNameExA/FQDN). - The author then builds custom shellcode loaders (stageless and staged), showing how encrypting embedded shellcode (AES) defeats simple signature matching but can raise entropy-based flags. - It also highlights static detection via suspicious imports and shows API hashing to remove obvious WinAPI names from the IAT/strings while still resolving functions at runtime. - Overall, it’s most useful for red teamers/pentesters and malware developers who need to understand and iterate on disk-based evasion, and for defenders who want to recognize these evasion patterns and their artifacts.
Technical Focus: - AV/EDR static detection primitives (hashes, signatures, strings/IAT, entropy) - Signature breaking via reverse engineering (ThreatCheck + Ghidra) - Packing and PIC shellcode conversion (PEzor, donut) - Windows shellcode loader patterns (VirtualAlloc/memcpy/VirtualProtect/CreateThread) - Payload encryption (XOR vs AES) and entropy side-effects - API hashing / runtime API resolution from exports (kernel32.dll)
Use Cases: - Iteratively modifying payloads to evade Defender/AV static signatures on disk - Identifying which code/bytes/constants trigger detections and patching them safely - Building custom staged/stageless loaders with encrypted payload handling - Reducing static indicators by removing suspicious imports via API hashing - Evaluating packed/encrypted binaries for entropy-based detection risk
Keywords: static analysis, antivirus, EDR, Windows Defender, signature-based detection, MD5, ThreatCheck, Ghidra, PE file, Import Address Table, IAT, entropy analysis, Detect It Easy, PE-bear, Havoc C2, PEzor, donut, shellcode loader, VirtualAlloc, VirtualProtect, CreateThread, WaitForSingleObject, API hashing, kernel32.dll exports, GetComputerNameExA, AES encryption, XOR, staged payload, stageless payload, msfvenom, Meterpreter

entry.title: bypass amsi on windows 11
entry.summary:
Title: Bypass AMSI On Windows 11
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post reverse-engineers AMSI behavior on Windows 11 and explains why a common OSEP-taught AMSI bypass (patching the first DWORD of `amsiContext` checked for “AMSI”) no longer works. - It walks through AMSI’s PowerShell call flow (`AmsiInitialize` → `AmsiOpenSession` → `AmsiScanBuffer`) using WinDBG breakpoints, then details multiple bypass strategies by forcing AMSI APIs to fail with `E_INVALIDARG (0x80070057)` or by toggling PowerShell’s internal `AmsiUtils` state. - The author shows updated Windows 11 logic in `AmsiOpenSession` (additional NULL checks on registers/structure fields) and adapts the reflection-based context patch to target the 2nd QWORD instead. - It also demonstrates in-memory patching of `AmsiOpenSession`/`AmsiScanBuffer` via `VirtualProtect` + byte overwrites, including light opcode obfuscation to evade signatures. - A key section covers why some bypasses fail for `.NET Assembly.Load()` scenarios: CLR invokes `clr.dll!AmsiScan`, changing which AMSI functions are exercised, making `AmsiScanBuffer` patching the reliable approach. - Useful for red teamers/pentesters developing PowerShell tradecraft and for defenders wanting to understand common AMSI tampering patterns on Windows 11.
Technical Focus: - AMSI internals and API call sequence (`AmsiInitialize`, `AmsiOpenSession`, `AmsiScanBuffer`) - Windows 11 behavioral changes in `amsi.dll` validation logic - PowerShell reflection abuse (`System.Management.Automation.AmsiUtils`, `amsiInitFailed`, `amsiContext`) - In-memory patching with `VirtualProtect` and direct opcode writes - CLR assembly scanning path (`clr.dll!AmsiScan`) during `Assembly.Load()` - WinDBG breakpoint-driven analysis of runtime execution
Use Cases: - Update legacy AMSI bypasses to work on Windows 11 by patching different `amsiContext` fields - Disable/impair AMSI scanning for PowerShell script execution via reflection-based state manipulation - Patch AMSI functions in-memory to force error returns and allow execution of flagged content - Enable in-memory loading of offensive .NET tooling (e.g., Rubeus) by targeting the CLR scanning path - Build detections around `VirtualProtect` + writes to `amsi.dll`/`clr.dll` code regions and suspicious reflection access to `AmsiUtils`
Keywords: AMSI, amsi.dll, PowerShell, Windows 11, WinDBG, AmsiInitialize, AmsiOpenSession, AmsiScanBuffer, HAMSICONTEXT, HAMSISESSION, E_INVALIDARG, 0x80070057, reflection, System.Management.Automation.AmsiUtils, amsiInitFailed, VirtualProtect, memory patching, opcode patch, clr.dll, AmsiScan, Assembly.Load, in-memory execution, Rubeus

entry.title: mutationgate
entry.summary:
Title: MutationGate
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This write-up introduces “MutationGate,” a technique to bypass EDR user-mode inline hooks on Windows NTAPI functions (e.g., NtAllocateVirtualMemory, NtWriteVirtualMemory) without patching ntdll or using custom syscall stubs. - It leverages hardware breakpoints plus a vectored exception handler (VEH) to intercept execution inside an unhooked “benign” NTAPI syscall stub (e.g., NtDrawText) and swap the syscall number (SSN) in RAX to that of a “suspicious” hooked NTAPI (e.g., NtQueryInformationProcess). - The result is that the syscall instruction executes from within ntdll (more “legitimate” call stack location) while avoiding the hooked entrypoint and avoiding common YARA-detectable direct/indirect syscall stub byte patterns. - The post compares MutationGate to direct syscalls, indirect syscalls (SysWhispers-style), and ntdll unhooking/overwrite approaches, highlighting typical IOCs and detection opportunities. - It also notes potential detections such as suspicious AddVectoredExceptionHandler usage and mismatches between the ntdll function name on the stack and the actual kernel syscall reached. - Useful primarily for red teams, pentesters, and malware developers researching syscall-based evasion; also valuable for blue teams building detections around VEH/hardware-breakpoint syscall tampering.
Technical Focus: - EDR inline hooking of ntdll NTAPI stubs - Hardware breakpoints (DR registers) + Vectored Exception Handling (VEH) - Syscall stubs, syscall numbers (SSN), and RAX/R10 calling convention details - Indirect syscall evasion and call stack / return address artifacts - YARA-style detection patterns for syscall stubs (direct/ SysWhispers2/3) - Detection ideas: syscall/stack inconsistency and suspicious exception-handler setup
Use Cases: - Bypassing user-mode inline hooks on targeted NTAPIs without patching ntdll - Building stealthier syscall invocation primitives for loaders/injectors - Researching and testing EDR detections for VEH/hardware-breakpoint tampering - Developing defensive heuristics for syscall provenance and stack consistency checks
Keywords: MutationGate, EDR bypass, inline hook, ntdll.dll, NTAPI, syscall stub, syscall number, SSN, RAX, R10, hardware breakpoint, DR0-DR7, VEH, AddVectoredExceptionHandler, indirect syscall, SysWhispers2, SysWhispers3, YARA, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, NtWriteVirtualMemory, NtQueryInformationProcess, NtDelayExecution, call stack spoofing, user-mode hooking detection

entry.title: misconceptions about dotnet
entry.summary:
Title: Addressing Common Misconceptions about .NET in the InfoSec World
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post debunks frequent misinformation about .NET that shows up in security commentary, malware analysis, and reverse engineering discussions. - It clarifies platform/runtime realities (cross-platform .NET vs .NET Framework vs Mono), execution model details (JIT vs “interpreted”), and why “easy decompilation” is not the same as “easy analysis” under modern obfuscation/VM protections. - The author highlights that .NET assemblies are real PE files and can include native code (mixed-mode, native stubs, obfuscator-emitted native), which impacts tooling and analysis approach. - It argues against common bad practices: relying on dnSpy’s C# editor for patching (prefer IL/CIL editing), over-focusing on ECMA-335 low-level parsing, and overvaluing “reflection-based packers” as novel tradecraft. - It also critiques low-fidelity YARA rules for .NET malware/packers that match generic framework/obfuscator artifacts and cause false positives. - Useful for malware analysts, reverse engineers, and detection engineers who handle .NET samples and want more accurate mental models and better tooling choices.
Technical Focus: - .NET runtime ecosystem: .NET Framework vs .NET (Core) vs Mono, CLI/ECMA-335 context - CIL/MSIL and JIT compilation vs interpretation; runtime execution modes - .NET obfuscation/anti-RE: control-flow, arithmetic, virtualization (KoiVM/VMProtect), JIT hooks (DNGuard) - Mixed-mode assemblies and native code inside managed PE files (C++/CLI, native stubs) - IL-level patching/editing vs decompiler-based source editing (dnSpy) - Detection pitfalls for .NET: YARA false positives on packer/obfuscator artifacts
Use Cases: - Improve analyst tradecraft when reversing obfuscated .NET malware beyond “just decompile it” - Choose appropriate libraries for .NET binary processing (dnlib/Mono.Cecil/AsmResolver vs raw-table Python parsers) - Safely patch/modify .NET samples using IL editing rather than recompiling decompiled C# - Dynamically invoke .NET methods via Reflection for quick string/config deobfuscation - Write higher-signal detection content (YARA) that avoids matching generic obfuscator/framework indicators
Keywords: .NET, .NET Framework, .NET Core, Mono, CLI, ECMA-335, CIL, MSIL, JIT, ILSpy, dnSpy, dnlib, Mono.Cecil, AsmResolver, mixed-mode assembly, C++/CLI, Reflection, Assembly.Load, obfuscation, KoiVM, VMProtect, DNGuard, Native AOT, single-file AppHost, YARA, .NET Reactor, ConfuserEx, de4dot, pythonnet

entry.title: awaitfuscator
entry.summary:
Title: Awaiting the Awaitables – Building the AwaitFuscator
Type: Blog Post
