Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post explains how C# `async`/`await` works under the hood and abuses the awaitable pattern to build an obfuscator (“AwaitFuscator”) that turns normal .NET methods into decompiled code that appears to be only long chains of `await`. - It leverages custom awaiters (via `GetAwaiter` extension methods) where `GetResult()` can execute arbitrary logic and return types that enable chaining across multiple awaiters. - The author details how the C# compiler generates async state machines (`IAsyncStateMachine.MoveNext`, `Async*MethodBuilder`, state fields, lifted locals) and how to replicate that structure so decompilers recognize it as legitimate async code. - To preserve locals/parameters across awaits, variables are lifted into a shared “Frame” object passed through awaiters, effectively moving the original method body into awaiter `GetResult()` implementations. - It also discusses hiding the generated awaiter types from common decompilers by marking them `[CompilerGenerated]` and using anonymous-type-like names, plus limitations (binary bloat, incomplete exception handling, decompiler options can reveal compiler-generated code). - Useful for reverse engineers (understanding async lowering), red teamers/malware devs (anti-decompiler tricks), and CTF authors (creating crackmes with unusual decompilation artifacts).
Technical Focus: - C# awaitable pattern (`GetAwaiter`, `INotifyCompletion`, `IsCompleted`, `OnCompleted`, `GetResult`) - Async state machine lowering (`IAsyncStateMachine`, `MoveNext`, state fields, lifted locals) - `AsyncTaskMethodBuilder` / `AsyncVoidMethodBuilder` bootstrapping and continuation wiring - Obfuscation via code relocation into awaiters and await-chaining across types - Decompiler evasion using `[CompilerGenerated]` and anonymous-type naming conventions - IL/.NET decompilation behavior (pattern matching in ILSpy/dnSpy)
Use Cases: - Build .NET crackmes/CTF challenges that decompile into misleading “only await” code - Prototype anti-decompiler/anti-RE transformations for managed binaries - Teach/learn how async/await is compiled into state machines and continuations - Stress-test decompilers and develop deobfuscation tooling for compiler-generated async patterns
Keywords: C#, .NET, async, await, awaitable, GetAwaiter, INotifyCompletion, GetResult, IsCompleted, OnCompleted, IAsyncStateMachine, MoveNext, AsyncTaskMethodBuilder, AsyncVoidMethodBuilder, state machine, lifted locals, Frame object, obfuscation, anti-decompiler, ILSpy, dnSpy, CompilerGenerated attribute, extension methods, decompilation, reverse engineering

entry.title: trustme
entry.summary:
Title: trustme — BOF to impersonate TrustedInstaller via DISM API trigger and thread impersonation
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): `trustme` is a Cobalt Strike Beacon Object File (BOF) that elevates an already-admin Beacon to a token that includes the `NT SERVICE\TrustedInstaller` group, enabling modification of TrustedInstaller-owned resources (files/registry) that SYSTEM alone may not access. Instead of starting the TrustedInstaller service via the Service Control Manager (a commonly monitored/logged path), it triggers `TrustedInstaller.exe` indirectly by loading `dismapi.dll` and running a DISM health check (`DismCheckImageHealth`) while keeping the DISM session open. It enumerates processes and threads using native NT APIs (`NtGetNextProcess`/`NtGetNextThread`) and identifies TrustedInstaller by querying process image names (`NtQueryInformationProcess`). It then impersonates a TrustedInstaller thread using `NtImpersonateThread` and applies the resulting token to the Beacon session via `BeaconUseToken` until `rev2self`. This is primarily useful for red teams/pentesters operating post-exploitation on Windows who need TrustedInstaller-level access with reduced SCM telemetry.
Technical Focus: - TrustedInstaller token/group impersonation - DISM API abuse to spawn TrustedInstaller (`dismapi.dll`, `DismCheckImageHealth`) - Native NT process/thread enumeration (`NtGetNextProcess`, `NtGetNextThread`) - Thread impersonation (`NtImpersonateThread`) and token handling in Beacon (`BeaconUseToken`) - Privilege requirements and enablement (`SeDebugPrivilege`) - Evasion/telemetry reduction by avoiding SCM interactions
Use Cases: - Modify/replace TrustedInstaller-protected system binaries and components - Write to protected registry hives/keys owned by TrustedInstaller - Post-exploitation privilege escalation from admin to TrustedInstaller-equivalent access - Red-team tradecraft to avoid direct `sc start TrustedInstaller` / `StartServiceW` artifacts - Operationalizing TrustedInstaller impersonation as a Cobalt Strike BOF + CNA command
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, BOF, Beacon, TrustedInstaller, NT SERVICE\\TrustedInstaller, SYSTEM, token impersonation, thread impersonation, NtImpersonateThread, NtGetNextProcess, NtGetNextThread, NtQueryInformationProcess, ProcessImageFileName, DISM, dismapi.dll, DismCheckImageHealth, SeDebugPrivilege, Windows internals, SCM evasion, rev2self

entry.title: slack udc2
entry.summary:
Title: Slack UDC2 BOF (Cobalt Strike UDC2 over Slack API)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This repository implements a Cobalt Strike Beacon Object File (BOF) that provides a UDC2 transport channel by encapsulating Beacon frames inside Slack API requests. It solves the problem of operating C2 traffic over a common SaaS platform to blend with legitimate outbound HTTPS traffic and traverse restrictive egress controls. The project includes a Windows in-memory BOF client (no MSVCRT, WinAPI-only) and a Python server component that relays extracted UDC2 frames to a Team Server UDC2 listener. It documents core UDC2 proxy functions (`udc2Proxy`, `udc2Close`) and uses WinINet for HTTP(S) communication to Slack. The implementation is intentionally simplified for learning and lacks fragmentation and multi-beacon routing, making it more suitable for lab/education than full-scale operations. Useful primarily for red teamers/pentesters learning UDC2/BOF transport development and for defenders studying Slack-based C2 tradecraft.
Technical Focus: - Cobalt Strike UDC2 transport implementation - Beacon Object File (BOF) development constraints (no CRT, in-memory) - Slack Web API as a C2 carrier (message size limits, channel IDs, bot tokens) - HTTP(S) communications via WinINet (`InternetOpenA`, `HttpSendRequestA`, etc.) - Relay/proxy architecture (Python server forwarding to Team Server listener) - Error handling and transport-layer limitations (timeouts, fragmentation)
Use Cases: - Build a custom UDC2 transport that tunnels Beacon traffic through Slack - Prototype/learn BOF-based network transports and UDC2 proxy mechanics - Egress-restricted C2 experiments using SaaS endpoints over HTTPS - Defensive testing: create detections for anomalous Slack bot/API usage patterns - Lab simulation of “living-in-SaaS” C2 channels for threat emulation
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, Beacon, UDC2, BOF, Beacon Object File, Slack API, Slack bot token, channel ID, WinINet, HTTP over TLS, proxy transport, C2 tunneling, Team Server, Python relay, Windows API, in-memory execution, fragmentation limit, SaaS C2, egress bypass, traffic encapsulation

entry.title: icmp udc2
entry.summary:
Title: ICMP UDC2 BOF (Cobalt Strike) — ICMP Echo Request/Reply UDC2 Channel
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This repository provides a Cobalt Strike Beacon Object File (BOF) that implements a UDC2 communication channel over ICMP echo request/reply traffic. It encapsulates Beacon frames inside ICMP payloads and includes fragmentation/reassembly logic to handle larger messages across multiple packets. The project also includes a Python “UDC2 server” proxy that receives ICMP, extracts/assembles Beacon data, forwards it to the Team Server’s UDC2 listener, and returns tasking via ICMP replies. Reliability features include configurable retries and exponential backoff, while BOF constraints are met by avoiding MSVCRT and using Windows APIs for in-memory execution. It’s primarily useful for red teams/pentesters needing an alternate C2 transport in environments where TCP/HTTP egress is constrained but ICMP is permitted, and for defenders studying ICMP-based tunneling tradecraft. It’s interesting because it operationalizes a full request/ack/task protocol over ICMP with explicit packet types and fragmentation flags, making the traffic more structured than simple ICMP tunnels.
Technical Focus: - Cobalt Strike UDC2 channel implementation (BOF-based) - ICMP echo request/reply encapsulation of C2 frames - Fragmentation/reassembly and sequencing flags - ACK/task polling protocol flow over a connectionless transport - Windows ICMP APIs (IPHLPAPI/IcmpSendEcho) and BOF constraints (no MSVCRT) - Python proxy server bridging ICMP to Team Server UDC2 listener
Use Cases: - Establishing C2 over ICMP in restricted egress networks - Testing detection/monitoring for ICMP tunneling and anomalous echo payloads - Building/customizing alternative Beacon transports via BOFs - Lab research on reliability/latency tradeoffs of ICMP-based C2 - Developing network signatures around ICMP payload structure, fragmentation patterns, and polling behavior
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, Beacon, BOF, UDC2, ICMP, echo request, echo reply, ICMP tunneling, C2 channel, IPHLPAPI.dll, IcmpCreateFile, IcmpSendEcho, IcmpCloseHandle, packet fragmentation, reassembly, ACK, tasking, exponential backoff, Windows API, Python proxy, Wireshark, RFC 792

entry.title: ClickOnceBlobber
entry.summary:
Title: ClickOnceBlobber (ClickOnce AppDomainManager Injection Toolkit)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): ClickOnceBlobber is a toolkit to weaponize legitimate, signed .NET ClickOnce applications by hijacking a dependency DLL and forcing the CLR to load it via AppDomainManager injection using an added `.exe.config`. It automates rebuilding a ClickOnce deployment by replacing a target DLL, inserting the config, and patching ClickOnce manifests (hashes, sizes, deployment provider URL, and related metadata) so the modified app still installs and runs. The primary payload is a C# port of Quarkslab’s ProxyBlob agent, providing a SOCKS5 tunnel whose C2 traffic is tunneled through Azure Blob Storage over HTTPS to blend into environments where `*.blob.core.windows.net` is allowed. The repo also includes alternative payload examples (message box PoC and a shellcode loader) and a built-in web server with correct ClickOnce MIME types for hosting. It’s mainly useful for red teams/pentesters focusing on initial access and stealthy egress, and it’s interesting because it combines trusted ClickOnce execution paths with cloud-based tunneling to reduce obvious C2 indicators.
Technical Focus: - ClickOnce deployment internals (.application/.manifest/.deploy) and hash patching - .NET CLR AppDomainManager injection via `.exe.config` - DLL dependency hijacking within ClickOnce app bundles - ProxyBlob-style C2 over Azure Blob Storage (raw REST over HTTPS) - Cryptography/protocol details: X25519, HKDF-SHA3-256, XChaCha20-Poly1305 (HChaCha20 derivation), GUID/UUID byte-order pitfalls - Build/packaging automation: csc.exe, NuGet, ILMerge, BouncyCastle
Use Cases: - Backdooring a discovered ClickOnce `.application` URL for initial access in an engagement - Establishing a SOCKS5 pivot channel that blends into Azure Blob Storage traffic - Rapid PoC validation of AppDomainManager injection in a target ClickOnce app - Delivering alternate payloads (custom DLL or shellcode loader) while keeping the signed host EXE unchanged - Testing/defending ClickOnce supply-chain and deployment integrity controls
Keywords: ClickOnce, AppDomainManager, .exe.config, CLR, .NET Framework, DLL hijacking, manifest patching, SHA256 hashes, SmartScreen reputation, signed binary abuse, ProxyBlob, SOCKS5, Azure Blob Storage, blob.core.windows.net, HTTPS tunneling, X25519, HKDF-SHA3-256, XChaCha20-Poly1305, HChaCha20, BouncyCastle, ILMerge, NuGet, dfshim CleanOnlineAppCache

entry.title: trust me im a shortcut
entry.summary:
Title: Trust me, I’m a shortcut — deceptive Windows LNK targets and Explorer Properties spoofing
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post analyzes how Windows Explorer’s shortcut (LNK) Properties dialog can misrepresent what a shortcut will actually execute, enabling social-engineering-friendly payload delivery. - It breaks down the LNK binary format (ShellLinkHeader, LinkTargetIDList, LinkInfo, StringData, ExtraData) and shows how multiple “target” representations can conflict. - It covers CVE-2025-9491 (Explorer truncating displayed command-line arguments to 260 chars) and several additional variants where arguments are hidden or the displayed target is spoofed via EnvironmentVariableDataBlock and fallback parsing behavior. - Key techniques include padding/whitespace abuse, disabling the Target field, and crafting invalid paths so Explorer displays one target but executes another (sometimes after “repairing” the LNK). - The post also introduces the open-source “lnk-it-up” toolkit: a generator for crafting these deceptive LNKs and a tester that uses native Windows APIs to predict displayed vs executed behavior. - Useful for red teams/pentesters building initial access lures and for blue teams/DFIR to understand and detect LNK deception beyond simple static parsing.
Technical Focus: - Windows Shell Link (LNK) file format internals and flags (LinkFlags, IsUnicode, HasExpString, HasLinkInfo, HasLinkTargetIDList) - Explorer Properties UI inconsistencies (display vs execution) and truncation behavior (260-char display limit) - EnvironmentVariableDataBlock (TargetAnsi/TargetUnicode) manipulation and Unicode/ANSI edge cases - Target resolution precedence and fallback between LinkTargetIDList, LinkInfo, and ExtraData - LNK-based social engineering and LOLBIN/LOLBAS execution chains - Detection approaches: native API-based evaluation vs strict parsers vs YARA keywording
Use Cases: - Crafting phishing lures where the shortcut’s displayed target/arguments look benign but execution is malicious - Building and testing LNK payloads that hide command-line arguments (e.g., PowerShell/mshta/rundll32 chains) - Defensive triage: identify LNKs where Explorer-displayed target differs from actual executed target - DFIR enrichment: extract and interpret LinkInfo/IDList artifacts (original paths, volumes, UNC hints) - Creating detection rules for suspicious LNK structures/flags and malformed target fields
Keywords: Windows LNK, Shell Link, Explorer Properties, target spoofing, command-line argument hiding, CVE-2025-9491, LinkTargetIDList, LinkInfo, StringData, ExtraData, EnvironmentVariableDataBlock, HasExpString, IsUnicode, ANSI vs UTF-16LE, path validation, reserved characters, UNC paths, LOLBAS, mshta.exe, PowerShell, YARA, lnk-it-up, native Windows APIs

entry.title: Limelighter
entry.summary:
Title: LimeLighter
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): LimeLighter is a Go-based utility for Windows Authenticode code signing that can generate a spoofed (self-created) code-signing certificate for a chosen domain/FQDN and use it to sign EXE/DLL files, or alternatively sign with a real PFX certificate. It targets operational needs where signed binaries may receive less scrutiny from some security controls and reputation-based checks. The tool wraps common signing tooling (OpenSSL and osslsigncode) to create certificates, produce signed outputs, and verify signatures. It includes options for fake cert generation via a `-Domain` parameter, real cert signing via `-Real`/`-Password`, and signature verification via `-Verify`. It’s primarily useful for red teams/pentesters and malware developers testing trust/signing-related detection paths, and for blue teams to understand and detect suspicious/self-signed Authenticode usage.
Technical Focus: - Authenticode/PE code signing for EXE/DLL - Spoofed/self-generated code-signing certificates (domain/FQDN-based) - PFX certificate handling and password-protected private keys - OpenSSL certificate generation workflows - osslsigncode signing/verification pipeline - Signature verification and inspection
Use Cases: - Sign payloads/tools to evaluate EDR and application control responses to signed binaries - Generate “lookalike” certificates for lab testing of trust and reputation controls - Sign binaries with legitimate PFX certs in controlled engagements - Verify and triage Authenticode signatures during analysis or QA of build artifacts
Keywords: Authenticode, code signing, PE, EXE, DLL, X.509, certificate, self-signed certificate, spoofed certificate, PFX, PKCS#12, private key, OpenSSL, osslsigncode, signature verification, Windows trust, EDR evasion testing, domain FQDN, Go (Golang)

entry.title: persistence the art of staying in
entry.summary:
Title: Persistence: Advanced Red Team Persistence Techniques (The Art of Staying In)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - A long-form, phase-based guide to persistence techniques across Windows, Active Directory, Linux, macOS, and cloud/Kubernetes environments, mapped to MITRE ATT&CK TA0003. - It focuses on maintaining access through reboots, credential rotation, endpoint reimaging, and incident response by layering redundant footholds with OPSEC tradecraft. - Windows coverage includes registry autoruns (Winlogon, IFEO/VerifierDlls, SSP/auth packages, time providers, netsh helpers), stealth scheduled tasks (including “invisible” tasks via security descriptor deletion), services (including SDDL hiding and svchost-hosted DLL services), WMI event subscriptions, and DLL/COM hijacking. - AD persistence sections cover Kerberos ticket forgery (Golden/Silver plus newer Diamond/Sapphire), DCSync rights backdooring, AdminSDHolder, SIDHistory, DCShadow, Skeleton Key, DSRM abuse, and GPO-based domain-wide execution. - Linux/macOS/cloud sections extend persistence into systemd/cron/SSH/PAM/eBPF rootkits, launchd/login items/dylib hijacking, and identity-plane backdoors in Entra/AWS/GCP plus Kubernetes RBAC/DaemonSets. - Useful primarily for red teams and pentesters designing post-exploitation persistence, and for blue teams translating these mechanisms into hunt/detection coverage; it’s notable for emphasizing stealth variants and real-world APT case studies.
Technical Focus: - Windows persistence primitives (Registry autoruns, IFEO/GlobalFlag/VerifierDlls, services, scheduled tasks, WMI subscriptions) - Execution-flow hijacking (DLL search order hijacking, COM hijacking, .NET AppDomainManager injection) - Active Directory persistence (Kerberos ticket forgery, DCSync, AdminSDHolder, DCShadow, GPO abuse) - Pre-OS / kernel persistence (UEFI bootkits, kernel drivers, Linux eBPF/LKM rootkits) - Cloud & identity persistence (Entra ID app backdoors, Golden SAML, AWS IAM backdoors, GCP service accounts, Kubernetes RBAC/DaemonSets) - OPSEC and persistence layering strategies (decoys vs stealth footholds, naming, timing, decoupled C2)
Use Cases: - Designing multi-layer persistence plans for red team operations across endpoint + AD + cloud - Building detection/hunting checklists for stealth persistence (e.g., WMI subscriptions, hidden services/tasks, COM hijacks) - Training content for post-exploitation tradecraft and MITRE ATT&CK TA0003 mapping - Purple-team validation using atomic tests and targeted telemetry (Sysmon/WMI/task/service/AD audit) - Incident response scoping: identifying persistence that survives reimage/password resets (AD/cloud identity plane)
Keywords: MITRE ATT&CK TA0003, Windows Registry Run keys, Winlogon Userinit, IFEO Debugger, GlobalFlag, VerifierDlls, SilentProcessExit, Scheduled Tasks, TaskCache Security subkey, service SDDL, svchost ServiceDll, WMI Event Subscription, __EventFilter, CommandLineEventConsumer, COM hijacking, DLL search order hijacking, AppDomainManager injection, UEFI bootkit, BlackLotus, CVE-2022-21894, Active Directory persistence, Golden Ticket, Silver Ticket, Diamond Ticket, Sapphire Ticket, DCSync, AdminSDHolder, SIDHistory, DCShadow, Skeleton Key, DSRM, GPO persistence, systemd, PAM backdoor, LD_PRELOAD, eBPF rootkit, LaunchAgents, LaunchDaemons, macOS Login Items, Golden SAML, Entra ID app registration, AWS IAM access keys, EventBridge, GCP service account keys, Kubernetes ClusterRoleBinding, DaemonSet

entry.title: red team infrastructure the full picture
entry.summary:
Title: Red Team Infrastructure The Full Picture: From Domain to Beacon
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post is an end-to-end blueprint for building resilient red team infrastructure that keeps C2 reachable while minimizing exposure and burn risk. - It covers a layered architecture where the team server is never internet-facing, traffic is funneled through redirectors and optionally CDN/serverless relays, and non-matching traffic is silently diverted to decoys. - Key tradecraft includes domain selection/aging and categorization, strict infrastructure segmentation by engagement phase (phish → long-term C2 → interactive ops → exfil), and Infrastructure-as-Code deployment with Terraform. - It details HTTPS redirector hardening using multi-layer filtering (UA, secret headers, URI allowlists, IP blocklists), TLS certificate OPSEC choices, and CDN relay setups (Azure CDN/Front Door, CloudFront, GCP) to blend into trusted cloud IP space. - It also includes phishing stack guidance (mail server + SPF/DKIM/DMARC, GoPhish + Evilginx) and C2 traffic shaping via Malleable C2 profiles (headers, URIs, caching controls, sleep/jitter). - Useful primarily for Red Teamers and pentesters running adversary simulations; also relevant to Blue Teams for understanding common infrastructure patterns and choke points for detection.
Technical Focus: - Redirector design and multi-layer HTTP(S) traffic filtering (mod_rewrite/Nginx rules) - Domain reputation, aging, and web categorization evasion - CDN relays and cloud edge routing (Azure CDN/Front Door, AWS CloudFront, GCP Cloud CDN) - Infrastructure-as-Code for offensive infra (Terraform, VPC/VPN segmentation) - C2 malleability and traffic shaping (Cobalt Strike Malleable C2, SourcePoint, caching controls) - Phishing infrastructure and deliverability (SPF/DKIM/DMARC, GoPhish, Evilginx)
Use Cases: - Build a segmented red team infra stack where only redirectors are exposed and team servers stay private - Deploy CDN-fronted C2 to leverage trusted cloud IP ranges and managed certificates - Implement hardened redirectors that selectively proxy only valid beacon traffic and decoy everything else - Stand up phishing infrastructure with high deliverability and AitM session capture workflows - Generate and validate Malleable C2 profiles aligned to redirector/CDN constraints (no-cache, header gates, URI allowlists)
Keywords: red team infrastructure, C2, Cobalt Strike, Sliver, Havoc, Mythic, redirector, Apache mod_rewrite, Nginx reverse proxy, TLS certificates, Let’s Encrypt, Cloudflare Origin Certificate, Microsoft-managed certificate, domain aging, domain categorization, Bluecoat/Symantec SiteReview, SPF, DKIM, DMARC, GoPhish, Evilginx2, Azure CDN, Azure Front Door, AWS CloudFront, GCP Cloud CDN, Cloudflare Workers, cloudflared tunnel, Terraform, Malleable C2 profile, SourcePoint, OPSEC, Geo-IP filtering, header-based allowlisting, URI allowlisting, IP blocklists

entry.title: ligolo iwa

Title: Ligolo-IWA
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Ligolo-IWA is a JavaScript-based Ligolo-ng agent designed to run inside Chrome/Chromium by leveraging the Isolated Web Applications (IWA) platform. It enables tunneling/pivoting capabilities from within a browser context, using a signed Isolated Web App bundle rather than a traditional native agent binary. The repo focuses on building and installing a signed web bundle (`.swbn`) and using Chrome’s IWA dev/testing flow (`chrome://web-app-internals`) to deploy it. It’s interesting because it applies the emerging IWA standard to post-exploitation-style networking (Ligolo-ng) and highlights current ecosystem limitations (e.g., Edge not supported).

entry.title: 3LayersPersistence
entry.summary:
Title: 3LayersPersistence
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This repository demonstrates a Windows persistence framework where a single EXE installs three different persistence mechanisms and can convert itself into functional proxy DLLs at runtime. It covers (1) permanent WMI event subscription persistence using an `ActiveScriptEventConsumer` triggered by Windows Defender signature update registry changes, (2) user-level COM hijacking via an HKCU CLSID that causes a proxy `MsComHost.dll` to be loaded by common processes, and (3) DLL search-order hijacking against Spotify by planting a proxy `dsound.dll` in `%APPDATA%\Spotify\`. The core technique is EXE-to-DLL patching: modifying PE headers, redirecting entrypoint to `DllMain`, and generating a forwarded export table that passes calls to a renamed legitimate DLL. It also implements anti-noise controls like an installation flag and a machine-unique mutex to prevent payload execution storms when the DLL is loaded by multiple hosts. Useful for red teamers, malware developers, and defenders studying persistence tradecraft, proxy DLL construction, and artifact/timestamp manipulation.
Technical Focus: - Permanent WMI event subscriptions (WQL, `RegistryValueChangeEvent`, `ActiveScriptEventConsumer`) - COM hijacking via HKCU CLSID override and proxy DLL loading - DLL search order hijacking / sideloading (Spotify `dsound.dll`) - EXE-to-proxy-DLL conversion (PE header patching, `DllMain`, export forwarding) - Timestamp/metadata stomping and filesystem timestomping - Execution guards (install check flag, global mutex uniqueness)
Use Cases: - Build and test multi-layer Windows persistence in lab environments - Study/implement proxy DLL export forwarding and runtime PE patching - Validate detection logic for WMI persistence, COM hijacks, and DLL sideloading - Generate forensic/detection baselines using the included verification and cleanup scripts
Keywords: Windows persistence, WMI permanent event subscription, ROOT\\subscription, WQL, RegistryValueChangeEvent, ActiveScriptEventConsumer, VBScript, Win32_Process.Create, COM hijacking, CLSID, HKCU, svchost.exe, DLL proxying, forwarded exports, .edata section, DLL search order hijacking, dsound.dll, Spotify, PE header patching, timestomping

entry.title: emulat3
entry.summary:
Title: Emulat3
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Emulat3 is a Python tool that performs instruction-by-instruction emulation of x64 PE functions or raw shellcode using Vivisect (the same emulation engine used by FLARE FLOSS). It prints per-step state including disassembly, register/EFLAGS values, stack context around RSP, memory writes, and exceptions to help understand code behavior without executing it natively. The tool supports emulating from a PE entry point, targeting a specific function VA, listing discovered functions, and emulating shellcode from raw bytes or hex strings/files. It includes call-following logic that can manually step into subroutines (optionally only for a specific target VA) and rolls back state if a followed call crashes. This is useful for malware analysts, reverse engineers, and red teamers who need quick dynamic-style insight into small routines (e.g., decoders, unpacking stubs) in a controlled emulator. Its value is in providing FLOSS-like emulation visibility with a simple CLI and detailed state logging.
Technical Focus: - Vivisect-based x64 CPU emulation and stepping - PE function discovery and emulation by virtual address - Shellcode emulation (raw/hex, configurable base/entry) - Call instruction handling (manual stepping into calls, selective follow) - Execution state introspection (registers, stack, memory write logging, exceptions)
Use Cases: - Triage and understand shellcode behavior without running it on a host - Step through suspected decoder/decryptor routines inside a PE - Identify where and how memory is written during emulation (e.g., unpacking buffers) - Reproduce and inspect crash points (invalid instruction/segfault) during analysis - Quickly enumerate functions in a PE and emulate specific targets by VA
Keywords: Vivisect, FLOSS, emulation, x64, amd64, PE, Portable Executable, shellcode, disassembly, registers, RIP, EFLAGS, stack, RSP, memory writes, call-following, function VA, reverse engineering, malware analysis, Python 3.12

entry.title: windows persistence techniques 0e5735740c9c
entry.summary:
Title: Windows Persistence Techniques
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post is a lab-tested walkthrough of basic Windows user-level persistence methods to regain execution after reboot, logon, or user activity. It demonstrates several common autostart vectors using simple command-line actions (copying binaries, adding registry values, and modifying user configuration). Techniques covered include Startup folder placement, HKCU Run key persistence, user logon scripts via `UserInitMprLogonScript`, screensaver executable hijacking, and PowerShell profile abuse. The author notes tradeoffs between reliability and noise, and shows that stacking multiple methods can yield multiple callbacks after login. It’s primarily useful for red teamers/pentesters validating persistence options, and for blue teamers mapping common persistence locations to hunt and harden.
Technical Focus: - Startup folder autostart (`%APPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup`) - Registry persistence (HKCU `...\CurrentVersion\Run`) - Logon script persistence (HKCU `Environment\UserInitMprLogonScript`) - Screensaver persistence (HKCU `Control Panel\Desktop\SCRNSAVE.EXE`, `ScreenSaveTimeOut`) - PowerShell profile execution (`Documents\WindowsPowerShell\profile.ps1`) - Persistence chaining / multiple execution triggers
Use Cases: - Establish user-context persistence for a payload across logoff/reboot - Validate which autostart vectors work in a given Windows environment - Create detection/hunting checks for common HKCU persistence keys and profile scripts - Purple-team exercises to test alerting on registry modifications and autostart locations
Keywords: Windows persistence, Startup folder, HKCU Run key, CurrentVersion\\Run, reg add, UserInitMprLogonScript, logon script, Control Panel\\Desktop, SCRNSAVE.EXE, ScreenSaveTimeOut, screensaver hijack, PowerShell profile, profile.ps1, autostart extensibility, user context persistence, callback, registry modification, living-off-the-land, endpoint hardening

entry.title: PrivKit
entry.summary:
Title: PrivKit — Beacon Object Files for Windows Local Privilege Escalation Checks
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): PrivKit is a collection of Cobalt Strike Beacon Object Files (BOFs) that perform common Windows local privilege escalation (LPE) discovery checks from within an active Beacon session. It solves the problem of quickly enumerating misconfigurations and credential artifacts without dropping tools to disk, leveraging in-memory BOF execution. The repo includes checks for AlwaysInstallElevated, Autologon credentials, Windows Credential Manager, writable PATH directories, modifiable autoruns, modifiable service permissions (DACL), unquoted service paths, token privileges, PowerShell history, and UAC status/integrity level. It’s primarily useful for red team operators and penetration testers running Cobalt Strike who need fast, low-footprint local privesc triage. It’s interesting because it packages multiple high-signal LPE misconfiguration checks into BOFs with an Aggressor script interface (PrivCheck.cna) for streamlined operator workflow across x86/x64.
Technical Focus: - Cobalt Strike BOF development and in-memory execution - Windows registry-based misconfiguration checks (Winlogon, Installer, Run/RunOnce) - Windows service misconfigurations (unquoted paths, weak service DACLs) - Credential discovery (Autologon, Credential Manager) - Token/UAC enumeration (integrity levels, filtered tokens, privileges) - Local persistence/abuse primitives (PATH hijacking, autorun writability)
Use Cases: - Rapid post-compromise Windows LPE enumeration from an existing Beacon - Identifying misconfigurations enabling service binary replacement or path hijacking - Hunting for stored credentials (Autologon, Credential Manager) to pivot/escalate - Assessing UAC posture and whether UAC bypass paths may be viable - Operator-friendly “run all checks” triage via `PrivCheck` in Cobalt Strike

entry.title: comouflage surrogate injection cfb93e15afcd
entry.summary:
Title: COMouflage: Surrogate Injection
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post describes “COMouflage,” a COM-based DLL surrogate technique that results in code execution inside `dllhost.exe` while making the process tree appear as normal COM activity (with `svchost.exe` as the parent). - It solves the problem of achieving stealthy user-mode “injection”/execution without directly opening or writing into a target process and without requiring admin rights, by abusing COM registration in `HKCU`. - The core method forges `AppID` and `CLSID` registry entries, sets `DllSurrogate` to an empty string to force the default surrogate host, points `InprocServer32` to the attacker DLL, and triggers activation via `CoCreateInstance` with `CLSCTX_LOCAL_SERVER`. - The article includes a C++ proof-of-concept, notes on volatile vs non-volatile registry keys, and shows the resulting process lineage (`svchost.exe` → `dllhost.exe /Processid:{GUID}`) that omits the initiating process. - It also provides a basic YARA rule focused on registry strings and COM-related API imports to detect binaries implementing the pattern. - Useful for red teams, pentesters, and malware developers for stealth execution tradecraft, and for blue teams as a concrete COM-surrogate abuse case to hunt/detect.
Technical Focus: - COM out-of-proc activation and DLL Surrogate (`DllSurrogate`) abuse - Per-user COM registration hijacking via `HKCU\Software\Classes` - Registry-based execution triggers (`AppID`, `CLSID`, `InprocServer32`, `ThreadingModel`) - Process lineage/PPID masquerading via COM SCM (`svchost.exe` → `dllhost.exe`) - C++ implementation using WinAPI + COM (`RegCreateKeyExW`, `RegSetValueExW`, `CoCreateInstance`) - Detection engineering with YARA (string + import + constant heuristics)
Use Cases: - Stealthy user-level DLL execution inside `dllhost.exe` without remote-thread injection - Parent/lineage evasion against simplistic process-tree detections - Tradecraft component for loaders/implants that want “living-off-the-land” COM hosting - Defensive hunting for suspicious per-user COM registrations and surrogate activations - Building detections around COM surrogate registry artifacts and activation behavior
Keywords: COM, Component Object Model, DllSurrogate, dllhost.exe, svchost.exe, COM SCM, AppID, CLSID, InprocServer32, HKCU, Software\Classes, CoCreateInstance, CLSCTX_LOCAL_SERVER, RegCreateKeyExW, RegSetValueExW, ThreadingModel, Apartment, process lineage, PPID masquerading, T1546.015, YARA, Windows internals, DLL injection (surrogate)

entry.title: VEN0m Ransomware
entry.summary:
Title: VEN0m Ransomware (Rust BYOVD + UAC Bypass PoC)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): VEN0m-Ransomware is a ransomware proof-of-concept written in Rust that demonstrates disabling/impairing AV/EDR protections and then encrypting user files on Windows 11. Its core evasion uses BYOVD by loading a legitimate signed driver from IObit Malware Fighter (IMFForceDelete.sys) vulnerable to arbitrary file deletion (CVE-2025-26125) to delete/“shred” security product files on disk, including locked files, rather than relying on suspicious memory tampering. It also implements a UAC bypass by hijacking auto-elevated Slui.exe via HKCU `Software\Classes\Launcher.SystemSettings\Shell\Open\Command` with `DelegateExecute`, then triggering execution with `ShellExecuteW` + `runas`. After defenses are weakened, it establishes persistence via Winlogon `Userinit` registry modification and performs extension-based file encryption with a hardcoded 32-byte key, dropping a ransom note and scheduled task to relaunch the note UI. The repo includes a decryptor (“Antid0te.exe”) that reverses encryption using the same key. It’s primarily useful for red teams/malware researchers to study BYOVD-based defense disruption and common Windows persistence/UAC bypass patterns, and for blue teams to derive detections around driver/service creation, suspicious registry keys, and mass file operations.
Technical Focus: - BYOVD (Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver) using IMFForceDelete.sys (CVE-2025-26125) - Arbitrary file deletion via IOCTL + `DeviceIoControl` (0x8016E000) - UAC bypass via Slui.exe auto-elevation + `DelegateExecute` registry hijack - Windows service creation/loading of kernel drivers (SCM) - Winlogon `Userinit` persistence - Ransomware-style file enumeration and symmetric encryption + extension renaming
Use Cases: - Simulate ransomware tradecraft in controlled lab environments - Research/detect BYOVD-based AV/EDR disruption via on-disk deletion of protected components - Build detections for suspicious driver loading/service names and unexpected signed-driver usage - Hunt for UAC bypass artifacts involving Slui.exe and `Launcher.SystemSettings` class hijacks - Validate incident response playbooks for ransomware behaviors (encryption, wallpaper change, scheduled tasks)
Keywords: Rust, ransomware, BYOVD, IMFForceDelete.sys, IObit Malware Fighter, CVE-2025-26125, DeviceIoControl, IOCTL 0x8016E000, arbitrary file delete, signed driver abuse, Windows 11 24H2, UAC bypass, Slui.exe, DelegateExecute, ShellExecuteW, HKCU\Software\Classes, kernel driver service, Winlogon Userinit, scheduled task, file encryption, .vnm extension

entry.title: weaponizing byovd to kill and evade windows defender 535ad94652b0
entry.summary:
Title: Weaponizing BYOVD to Kill and Evade Windows Defender
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post demonstrates a Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver (BYOVD) technique to repeatedly terminate Windows Defender’s core process (MsMpEng.exe) using a legitimate but vulnerable kernel driver that exposes an arbitrary process-kill IOCTL. The approach loads the driver as a service, opens its device interface, enumerates processes to find the Defender PID, and issues DeviceIoControl calls to kill it in a loop every ~2 seconds to prevent automatic recovery. It provides C++ proof-of-concept code and shows practical impact (e.g., running Mimikatz after Defender is kept down) on Windows 11. The author also includes basic detection notes (multi-engine scan results, EDR observations) and a generic YARA rule matching the device name, IOCTL constant, and related APIs. Useful for red teams/malware developers studying kernel-assisted defense evasion and for blue teams building detections around suspicious driver usage and repeated AV service termination. It’s interesting because it highlights how signed drivers with dangerous IOCTLs can create persistent “blind spots” even when user-mode defenses restart.
Technical Focus: - BYOVD (Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver) for defense evasion - Vulnerable driver IOCTL abuse for arbitrary process termination - Windows kernel driver loading via Service Control Manager (sc.exe) - User-mode to kernel-mode interaction via CreateFile + DeviceIoControl - Process enumeration via Toolhelp32 APIs to target MsMpEng.exe - Detection engineering: YARA patterns for BYOVD tooling
Use Cases: - Disabling/neutralizing Microsoft Defender by continuously killing MsMpEng.exe - Post-exploitation evasion to run commodity tools (e.g., credential dumping) with reduced interference - Testing EDR/AV resilience against kernel-assisted tampering and service restart loops - Building detections for suspicious driver device access + kill-process IOCTL patterns - Purple-team validation of driver-blocking policies and kernel attack surface reduction
Keywords: BYOVD, vulnerable driver, Windows Defender, MsMpEng.exe, process termination, IOCTL, DeviceIoControl, CreateFileA, STProcessMonitorDriver, kernel driver, sc.exe, Service Control Manager, Toolhelp32Snapshot, Process32FirstW, Process32NextW, Windows 11, defense evasion, YARA, CVE-2026-0828, EDR bypass

entry.title: DataOnlyGadget
entry.summary:
Title: DOG: Data Only Gadgets (DataOnlyGadget)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): DOG is a Windows kernel post-exploitation framework that sits on top of an existing kernel read/write primitive and automates “data-only gadget” discovery and chaining. Instead of relying on kernel shellcode or classic ROP/JOP, it repurposes legitimate kernel code paths and kernel data structures as reusable building blocks to perform actions under modern mitigations (VBS, HVCI, CET/kCET). The tool dynamically resolves `ntoskrnl` symbols, walks kernel structures to recover offsets per-build, enumerates kernel objects, classifies gadget candidates, and builds runtime chains to execute operations like token stealing, callback tampering, PPL modification, and LSASS dumping. It provides a pluggable `KernelReadWrite` backend so different exploit classes (IOCTL-based R/W, win32k/GDI primitives, write-what-where, DMA/physmem access) can reuse the same discovery/chaining pipeline. Useful primarily for kernel exploit developers and red team operators who already have a primitive and want portability across Windows builds without hardcoded offsets. Interesting because it formalizes a data-oriented approach to kernel post-exploitation that aims to remain viable as code-execution-centric techniques are constrained by platform mitigations.
Technical Focus: - Data-oriented kernel exploitation (“data-only gadgets”) and gadget chaining - Runtime `ntoskrnl` symbol resolution (DbgHelp) and kernel ASLR handling - Dynamic kernel structure walking and offset recovery (NTKernelWalkerLib) - Pluggable kernel virtual/physical memory R/W backends (VA/PA, VA→PA translation) - Kernel object enumeration and gadget candidate classification - Post-exploitation actions: token swap, callback manipulation, PPL/LSASS operations
Use Cases: - Porting an existing kernel R/W primitive into a reusable post-exploitation toolkit - Automating per-build offset/structure discovery for Windows kernel exploitation - Privilege escalation via token stealing (target PID token swap) - Disabling/altering kernel callbacks and modifying PPL to access protected processes - LSASS credential access via WDigest patching, raw memory page dumping, or minidump generation - Hiding/unlinking processes and suspending protected processes using kernel data manipulation
Keywords: Windows kernel, ntoskrnl, data-only exploitation, kernel gadgets, gadget chaining, arbitrary read/write, kernel VA, physical memory, VA2PA, DbgHelp, symbol resolution, kernel ASLR, VBS, HVCI, CET, kCET, PatchGuard, SSDT hijack, token stealing, PPL, LSASS, WDigest, minidump, callback removal, kernel object enumeration, NTKernelWalkerLib

entry.title: reverse engineering a 0day used against crowdstrike edr a5ea1fbe3fd4
entry.summary:
Title: Reverse Engineering a 0day used Against CrowdStrike EDR
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post reverse engineers a set of Microsoft-signed kernel drivers (15+ variants) observed in BYOVD-style attacks targeting EDR products, including CrowdStrike. - The driver exposes an IOCTL interface that allows a user-mode client to request termination of an arbitrary process by PID, enabling killing of protected EDR processes (e.g., PPL-protected services) that are difficult to terminate from user mode. - The author focuses on reversing the dispatch/DeviceIoControl handler (skipping an obfuscated/non-standard DriverEntry) and identifies the “process killer” IOCTL (0x22E010). - The kill path is straightforward: parse PID from an ASCII string, then use ZwOpenProcess followed by ZwTerminateProcess from kernel context. - The post also covers finding the device symbolic link dynamically (WinObj) to enable user-mode access, then building a PoC that opens the device and sends the IOCTL to terminate CrowdStrike. - It’s most useful for red teamers, malware developers, and defenders analyzing BYOVD tradecraft and kernel-assisted EDR tampering.
Technical Focus: - BYOVD (Bring Your Own Vulnerable/Abusable Driver) for EDR tampering - Windows kernel driver reverse engineering (IDA Pro, dispatch routines) - IOCTL interface discovery and abuse (DeviceIoControl) - Kernel-mode process termination (ZwOpenProcess, ZwTerminateProcess) - PPL (Protected Process Light) bypass via kernel operations - Device object / symbolic link enumeration (WinObj)
Use Cases: - Terminate EDR/AV processes that resist user-mode termination (PPL) - Analyze suspicious signed drivers for exposed privileged IOCTLs - Build detections around driver loading + suspicious IOCTL usage - Reproduce and validate BYOVD-based EDR disruption in lab environments
Keywords: BYOVD, kernel driver, Windows kernel, CrowdStrike, EDR bypass, PPL, Protected Process Light, IOCTL, 0x22E010, DeviceIoControl, dispatch routine, DriverEntry obfuscation, IDA Pro, ZwOpenProcess, ZwTerminateProcess, process termination, symbolic link, WinObj, CreateFileW, Microsoft-signed driver

entry.title: qilin edr killer
entry.summary:
Title: Qilin EDR killer infection chain (malicious msimg32.dll analysis)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This Talos write-up reverse engineers a multi-stage loader delivered as a malicious, side-loaded `msimg32.dll` used in Qilin ransomware intrusions to disable endpoint defenses. - The chain focuses on evading user-mode telemetry and hooks (including ETW suppression and syscall-hook bypass) to decrypt, map, and execute an embedded EDR-killer payload fully in memory. - It uses SEH/VEH-driven control-flow obfuscation, IAT hooking (e.g., `ExitProcess`) for delayed execution, and “neighboring syscall stub” techniques (Halo’s Gate-style) to recover clean syscalls without patching hooked stubs. - The final stage loads abused/renamed helper drivers (notably a signed ThrottleStop-derived driver) to gain physical memory read/write primitives, unregister kernel callbacks used by EDRs, and terminate protected EDR processes via a second driver. - The malware targets and can disable/neutralize telemetry from 300+ EDR drivers across many vendors, highlighting how attackers increasingly attack the defensive layer early in execution. - It’s most useful for blue teams, EDR engineers, and malware analysts for understanding modern anti-EDR tradecraft and where to build detections/mitigations; red teams can also study it as a catalog of real-world evasion patterns.
Technical Focus: - DLL sideloading with export forwarding and `DllMain` execution - SEH/VEH-based control-flow obfuscation and hardware-breakpoint-triggered VEH - Syscall unhooking/bypass via neighboring stub scanning (Halo’s Gate-style) - ETW suppression and user-mode hook neutralization - In-memory PE loading (section mapping, dual-view mapping, relocations, module stomping) - Kernel callback removal and physical-memory manipulation via abused signed drivers (BYOVD-like)
Use Cases: - Build detections for SEH/VEH abuse, hardware breakpoints, and anomalous `KiUserExceptionDispatcher`/`.mrdata` tampering - Hunt for DLL sideloading of `msimg32.dll` and suspicious export-forwarding behavior - Detect/mitigate abuse of legitimate signed drivers providing physical memory primitives (e.g., ThrottleStop/TechPowerUp lineage) - Create telemetry and response playbooks for EDR blinding (callback unregistering) and protected-process termination attempts - Develop YARA/Sigma/EDR analytics around IAT hooking of `ExitProcess`, ETW tampering, and in-memory module stomping

entry.title: bypassing code integrity using byovd for kernel r w primitives 8135087e1c1e
entry.summary:
Title: Bypassing Code Integrity Using BYOVD for Kernel R/W Primitives
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post demonstrates a Windows Code Integrity (CI) / Driver Signature Enforcement (DSE) bypass by abusing a signed-but-vulnerable driver (BYOVD) to gain kernel read/write primitives. - The core idea is to locate `ci.dll` in kernel memory, compute the version-specific offset to `g_CiOptions`, and then overwrite that bitmask via an IOCTL-based kernel write primitive to relax/disable CI enforcement. - It walks through enabling `SeDebugPrivilege`, enumerating loaded kernel modules with `NtQuerySystemInformation(SystemModuleInformation)`, deriving offsets using WinDbg symbol queries, and using `DeviceIoControl` against a vulnerable driver device to patch kernel memory. - The example uses Gigabyte’s `gdrv.sys` (noted as widely exploited/blacklisted) and highlights the operational need for a non-blacklisted driver with equivalent R/W primitives. - It includes a basic PoC implementation in C++ plus a detection section with static scan results and a YARA rule aimed at identifying tools that patch `g_CiOptions`. - Useful for red teams, pentesters, and malware developers studying CI bypass tradecraft, and for blue teams building detections around BYOVD + CI tampering.
Technical Focus: - Windows Code Integrity internals (`ci.dll`, `g_CiOptions` bitmask) - BYOVD abuse to obtain kernel read/write primitives via IOCTL - Kernel module enumeration via `NtQuerySystemInformation` (SystemModuleInformation) - WinDbg symbol/offset derivation for kernel variables - DSE/CI policy manipulation (test signing, permissive/chill checks) - Detection engineering (static scanning, YARA patterns)
Use Cases: - Disable/relax DSE to load unsigned or custom kernel drivers - Build a CI bypass PoC for lab validation of BYOVD risk - Develop detections for kernel CI tampering and suspicious driver IOCTL usage - Assess exposure to known-vulnerable/LOLDrivers and driver blocklist effectiveness - Red team tradecraft research on kernel-level policy manipulation
Keywords: Windows Code Integrity, DSE, UMCI, ci.dll, g_CiOptions, BYOVD, kernel read/write primitive, DeviceIoControl, IOCTL, NtQuerySystemInformation, SystemModuleInformation, SeDebugPrivilege, WinDbg, symbol resolution, gdrv.sys, LOLDrivers, driver blocklist, kernel memory patching, test signing, YARA detection

entry.title: Fake DriverObject
entry.summary:
Title: Forging a Fake `DRIVER_OBJECT` for Manually Mapped Windows Kernel Drivers
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This write-up explains how to construct a “fake” Windows kernel `DRIVER_OBJECT` so a manually mapped (reflectively loaded) driver can interact with the standard I/O Manager subsystems instead of being “deaf and mute.” It reverse engineers `IoCreateDriver` to replicate how Windows allocates and initializes `DRIVER_OBJECT` + `DRIVER_EXTENSION` as a contiguous block (0x150 + 0x58 = 0x1A8 bytes), including key fields like `Type`, `Size`, dispatch table defaults, and the extension back-pointer. The post also covers how to obtain the required `IoDriverObjectType` without a static import by querying an always-present device (e.g., `\Device\Tcp`) and using `ObGetObjectType`-style logic. The end result is a kernel object that the OS can safely reference, enabling use of common driver APIs and IRP dispatching while reducing instability and obvious artifacts. It’s primarily useful for red teamers, kernel implant developers, and researchers working on manual mapping/reflective loading and stealthy kernel execution. It’s interesting because it bridges Windows Object Manager internals with practical loader design and highlights detection-relevant artifacts (imports, namespace visibility, ETW TI logging).
Technical Focus: - Windows kernel `DRIVER_OBJECT` / `DRIVER_EXTENSION` layout and initialization - Reverse engineering `IoCreateDriver` (IDA/WinDbg) and object allocation via `ObCreateObjectEx` - IRP dispatch table (`MajorFunction`) and default handlers (`IopInvalidDeviceRequest`) - Object Manager types (`IoDriverObjectType`, `_OBJECT_TYPE`) and dynamic resolution via existing devices - Kernel object attributes (`OBJECT_ATTRIBUTES`, `OBJ_KERNEL_HANDLE`) and namespace anonymity - ETW TI driver load logging (`EtwTiLogDriverObjectLoad`) implications
Use Cases: - Enabling IRP handling and `DeviceIoControl`-style communication for manually mapped kernel drivers - Building reflective/manual kernel loaders that mimic legitimate driver object creation - Researching Windows Object Manager behavior and kernel object construction patterns - Developing and testing stealth tradeoffs (imports, object naming, ETW telemetry) for kernel implants - Creating more stable “ghost” drivers that interact with I/O subsystems without immediate crashes
Keywords: Windows kernel, DRIVER_OBJECT, DRIVER_EXTENSION, IoCreateDriver, ObCreateObjectEx, IoDriverObjectType, OBJECT_TYPE, OBJECT_ATTRIBUTES, OBJ_KERNEL_HANDLE, IRP, MajorFunction, I/O Manager, DEVICE_OBJECT, IoGetDeviceObjectPointer, \Device\Tcp, ObReferenceObjectByHandle, ExAllocatePool2, RtlPcToFileHeader, EtwTiLogDriverObjectLoad, manual mapping, reflective loader, YetAnotherReflectiveLoader, IDA Pro, WinDbg

entry.title: KslKatz
entry.summary:
Title: KslKatz — BYOVD Credential Extractor using Microsoft Defender’s KslD.sys
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): KslKatz is a standalone Windows credential extraction tool that uses a Microsoft-signed Defender driver (KslD.sys) as a BYOVD primitive to read kernel/physical memory and bypass LSASS PPL protections. It abuses an older vulnerable KslD.sys left on disk to expose an unrestricted `MmCopyMemory`-backed IOCTL read (plus info leaks for KASLR defeat), enabling direct physical reads of LSASS memory. The tool locates LSASS and required secrets by leaking kernel pointers via `NtQuerySystemInformation(SystemHandleInformation)`, walking EPROCESS lists, and performing manual page-table translation from LSASS DTB/CR3. It avoids noisy remote memory scanning by signature-scanning `lsasrv.dll` and `wdigest.dll` from disk (no `LoadLibrary`), then reads only the needed structures from LSASS to extract MSV1_0 NT/LM/SHA1 hashes and WDigest cleartext (when caching is enabled). It is primarily useful for red teams and pentesters with local admin who want a “living-off-the-land” style LSASS credential theft path using only Microsoft-signed components. It’s notable because it highlights a practical gap in driver blocklisting (Microsoft drivers excluded) and a “no CVE / no fix” posture despite a powerful kernel read primitive.
Technical Focus: - BYOVD via Microsoft Defender KslD.sys (service ImagePath swap to vulnerable binary) - Arbitrary kernel/physical memory read via IOCTL (SubCmd 12) and KASLR leak (SubCmd 2) - PPL bypass by reading LSASS through physical memory + manual page table walks (CR3/DTB) - EPROCESS discovery via handle table kernel pointer leak (SystemHandleInformation) - Local on-disk signature scanning of lsasrv.dll/wdigest.dll to resolve internal globals - LSA secrets decryption (AES-CFB128 / 3DES-CBC) and BCRYPT key structure traversal
Use Cases: - Dump MSV1_0 credential material (NT/LM/SHA1) from PPL-protected LSASS with local admin - Extract WDigest cleartext passwords when caching is enabled or has been enabled in-memory - Post-exploitation credential access without third-party vulnerable drivers (Microsoft-signed only) - Research/validation of Defender driver exposure, blocklist limitations, and kernel read primitives - Building detections around suspicious KslD service reconfiguration and `\\.\KslD` access
Keywords: KslKatz, KslD.sys, Microsoft Defender, BYOVD, MmCopyMemory, IOCTL 0x222044, Protected Process Light, LSASS, credential dumping, MSV1_0, WDigest, LogonSessionList, l_LogSessList, NtQuerySystemInformation, SystemHandleInformation, EPROCESS, ActiveProcessLinks, CR3, DTB, page table walk, KASLR bypass, ChangeServiceConfigW, AllowedProcessName, Windows CNG, bcrypt.dll, AES-CFB128, 3DES-CBC, DriverSiPolicy.p7b, HVCI, vulnerable driver blocklist

entry.title: KslDump
entry.summary:
Title: KslDump – BMVD (Bring the Microsoft Vulnerable Driver)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): KslDump is a Python proof-of-concept/tool that dumps credentials from PPL-protected LSASS by abusing a Microsoft Defender kernel driver (KslD.sys) that exposes powerful IOCTLs to user mode. It leverages a leftover vulnerable, Microsoft-signed KslD.sys on disk and re-points the service ImagePath to that version, avoiding BYOVD-style third‑party driver loading. The driver’s IOCTL `0x222044` subcommands provide KASLR defeat (leaking CR3/IDTR) and an arbitrary kernel/physical memory read primitive via an attacker-controlled `MmCopyMemory()` call. With physical memory reads, the tool walks kernel structures to locate LSASS, translates LSASS virtual addresses via page tables, extracts LSA encryption material (AES/3DES/IV), and decrypts logon session credentials to recover NT hashes. It’s primarily useful for red teams, pentesters, and security researchers evaluating credential theft paths that rely only on built-in Microsoft-signed components. It’s notable because it highlights a “blocklist paradox” (Microsoft drivers excluded from the vulnerable driver blocklist) and a practical PPL bypass that depends mainly on local admin plus weak driver access control.
Technical Focus: - Microsoft Defender KslD.sys IOCTL abuse (`0x222044`, SubCmd 2/12) - Arbitrary physical/kernel memory read via `MmCopyMemory` - PPL/LSASS protection bypass through kernel primitives - KASLR defeat using CR3/IDTR leakage and IDT-based ntoskrnl base discovery - Kernel structure walking (EPROCESS, ActiveProcessLinks) and page table translation - LSA secrets/key extraction and credential decryption (MSV1_0)
Use Cases: - Dumping LSASS credentials/NT hashes on systems where LSASS runs as PPL - Post-exploitation credential access using only Microsoft-signed, on-box components - Researching/validating kernel-driver exposed attack surface in Defender components - Purple-team testing of detections around service/registry tampering and suspicious driver IOCTL usage
Keywords: KslDump, KslD.sys, Microsoft Defender, BMVD, BYOVD, IOCTL 0x222044, MmCopyMemory, physical memory read, kernel memory disclosure, PPL, LSASS, KASLR bypass, CR3, IDTR, IDT, ntoskrnl, EPROCESS, ActiveProcessLinks, LSA secrets, MSV1_0, NTLM hash, registry ImagePath, AllowedProcessName, DriverSiPolicy.p7b, HVCI

entry.title: credential dumping local security authority lsalsass exe
entry.summary:
Title: Credential Dumping: Local Security Authority (LSA | LSASS.EXE)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post is a practical walkthrough of dumping and extracting credentials from Windows LSASS (lsass.exe), a high-value process that holds authentication material for interactive sessions. - It covers both remote and local approaches using common offensive tooling (lsassy, NetExec/nxc modules, Impacket secretsdump) and built-in/signed utilities (Task Manager, Process Explorer, ProcDump) plus a “fileless” dump method via `rundll32.exe` + `comsvcs.dll` MiniDump. - The article explains what credential artifacts can be recovered (NTLM hashes, cleartext passwords in some cases, Kerberos tickets/keys, DPAPI material) and how to parse dumps offline with pypykatz. - It also introduces stealthier dumping via nanodump concepts (e.g., indirect syscalls and reduced artifacts) and operational steps like dumping, exfiltration, and cleanup. - Defensive sections highlight mitigations (Credential Guard, LSASS PPL, ASR rules) and network/host detection ideas (DRSUAPI/DCSync anomalies, admin share access, RemoteRegistry starts, large SMB transfers). - Useful primarily for red teams/pentesters validating post-compromise impact, and for blue teams building detections and hardening guidance around credential theft.
Technical Focus: - LSASS memory dumping (local vs remote) - SMB/WMI/RPC-based remote execution and retrieval workflows - LSA Secrets and registry-based credential material - DRSUAPI / DCSync via Impacket secretsdump - Dump parsing with pypykatz (Mimikatz sekurlsa-equivalent) - Mitigations: Credential Guard, RunAsPPL (LSASS PPL), ASR; detection via service/network telemetry
Use Cases: - Remote credential harvesting across Windows fleets using NetExec modules (lsassy/nanodump) - Extracting NTLM hashes and Kerberos tickets for lateral movement simulations (PtH/PTT) - Offline parsing of acquired LSASS dumps during incident response or purple-team exercises - Building detections for LSASS dumping tradecraft (admin share writes, RemoteRegistry start, large dump exfil) - Validating hardening controls (Credential Guard, PPL) against common dump techniques
Keywords: LSASS, LSA, credential dumping, lsassy, NetExec, nxc, CrackMapExec successor, Impacket, secretsdump, DRSUAPI, DCSync, SMB, RemoteRegistry, ProcDump, Process Explorer, Task Manager dump, comsvcs.dll, rundll32, MiniDump, pypykatz, Kerberos TGT, NTLM hash, DPAPI, WDigest, RunAsPPL, Protected Process Light, Credential Guard, ASR rules, Pass-the-Hash, Pass-the-Ticket, nanodump

entry.title: rust for malware development
entry.summary:
Title: Rust for Malware Development
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post compares Rust vs. C/C++ for Windows malware development, focusing on why Rust binaries can be harder to statically analyze and may evade some signature-based detections. - It references academic findings that Rust malware tends to be larger, produces more tooling misclassifications in automated analysis, and decompiles poorly in common RE tools (e.g., Ghidra/IDA), partly due to rustc optimizations and symbol mangling. - A hands-on example builds a simple shellcode loader (read bytes from disk, VirtualAlloc/VirtualProtect, CreateThread) and contrasts the resulting decompilation quality between Rust and C. - It then develops a Rust “dropper” that enumerates processes, injects payload via remote mapping injection (file mapping + MapViewOfFileNuma2), and executes via CreateRemoteThread. - Finally, it demonstrates staging a Sliver C2 implant over HTTPS and injecting the downloaded shellcode into a target process (e.g., notepad.exe). - Useful for red teamers and malware/tooling developers; also relevant to defenders/reverse engineers due to the discussion of analysis friction and OPSEC pitfalls (e.g., embedded build paths in Rust binaries).
Technical Focus: - Rust vs C/C++ malware tradeoffs (binary size, static linking, RE difficulty) - Windows shellcode loading/execution (VirtualAlloc, VirtualProtect, CreateThread) - Process enumeration (EnumProcesses, OpenProcess, GetModuleBaseNameW) - Remote mapping injection (CreateFileMapping, MapViewOfFile, MapViewOfFileNuma2) - Remote thread execution (CreateRemoteThread, WaitForSingleObject) - Rust compilation artifacts (optimizations/inlining, symbol mangling, path leakage)
Use Cases: - Build Windows shellcode loaders/droppers in Rust - Implement process discovery and target selection for injection - Prototype remote mapping injection as an alternative to VirtualAllocEx-based flows - Stage and execute Sliver implants over HTTPS in-memory - Evaluate reverse-engineering/detection gaps for Rust-compiled payloads
Keywords: Rust, rustc, C, C++, Windows API, shellcode loader, dropper, process injection, remote mapping injection, CreateFileMappingA, MapViewOfFile, MapViewOfFileNuma2, VirtualAlloc, VirtualProtect, CreateThread, CreateRemoteThread, EnumProcesses, OpenProcess, GetModuleBaseNameW, Ghidra, IDA, symbol mangling, static linking, Sliver C2, HTTPS stager, OPSEC, strings path leakage

entry.title: process injection part i.html
entry.summary:
Title: Process Injection – Part I
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post introduces Windows process injection fundamentals and frames injection as a way to execute code in the context of another process. - It focuses on the core building blocks needed to inject shellcode/DLLs, typically involving obtaining a handle to a target process, allocating memory inside it, writing payload bytes, and triggering execution. - The discussion centers on common Win32/NT APIs used for classic “remote thread” style injection and the permissions/privileges required to interact with other processes. - It’s primarily useful for red teamers, pentesters, and malware developers learning baseline injection tradecraft, and for blue teamers who need to understand what to hunt for. - It’s interesting as a foundational primer that sets up later, more advanced injection variants and the detection surface they create (API sequences, access rights, cross-process memory operations).
Technical Focus: - OpenProcess and process access rights (e.g., PROCESS_VM_WRITE/OPERATION, PROCESS_CREATE_THREAD) - VirtualAllocEx / WriteProcessMemory workflow - CreateRemoteThread (classic remote thread injection) - Shellcode staging in remote process memory - Basic detection surface of cross-process memory + thread creation
Use Cases: - Implementing a basic Windows process injection PoC for training/labs - Understanding common EDR telemetry points for injection (memory allocation/write + remote thread) - Building simple loaders that execute payloads inside a chosen target process - Creating detection rules for suspicious API call chains and handle access patterns
Keywords: process injection, Windows, Win32 API, OpenProcess, VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, CreateRemoteThread, remote thread injection, shellcode, DLL injection, PROCESS_VM_WRITE, PROCESS_VM_OPERATION, PROCESS_CREATE_THREAD, cross-process memory, code execution, EDR telemetry, detection surface, handle permissions
