entry.title: parent pid spoofing b0b17317168e
entry.summary:
Title: Parent PID Spoofing
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post explains Parent PID (PPID) spoofing on Windows as an evasion technique that lets an attacker create a process while specifying a different parent process to alter the process tree. It focuses on using `CreateProcess` with `STARTUPINFOEX` and process thread attributes (available since Windows Vista) to set `PROC_THREAD_ATTRIBUTE_PARENT_PROCESS`. The author integrated PPID spoofing into an existing C# process-injection project that already supports “vanilla” injection and DLL injection, and added process hollowing as well. A demo shows spawning a benign child (e.g., `notepad.exe`) under a chosen parent (e.g., `explorer.exe`) and then injecting MSFVenom-generated shellcode to obtain a Meterpreter session. It’s useful for red teamers and malware developers researching Windows process creation/injection tradecraft, and for defenders understanding what telemetry to monitor. The post also briefly notes detection ideas around extended startup info usage and suspicious child process behavior (e.g., system binaries making network connections).
Technical Focus: - PPID spoofing via `STARTUPINFOEX` and `PROC_THREAD_ATTRIBUTE_PARENT_PROCESS` - Windows process creation attributes (`InitializeProcThreadAttributeList`, `UpdateProcThreadAttribute`) - Handle operations (`OpenProcess`, `DuplicateHandle`) - Process injection workflows (shellcode injection, DLL injection, process hollowing) - Basic detection/telemetry considerations for EDR/AV
Use Cases: - Create “legitimate-looking” process trees during payload execution - Combine PPID spoofing with shellcode/DLL injection to reduce suspicion - Red team tradecraft development in C# using Win32 APIs - Defensive testing: build detections for suspicious `CreateProcess` + attribute updates - Lab demonstrations of process lineage manipulation and post-exploitation staging
Keywords: Parent PID spoofing, PPID spoofing, STARTUPINFOEX, EXTENDED_STARTUPINFO_PRESENT, PROC_THREAD_ATTRIBUTE_PARENT_PROCESS, CreateProcess, UpdateProcThreadAttribute, InitializeProcThreadAttributeList, OpenProcess, DuplicateHandle, Win32 API, C#, process injection, process hollowing, DLL injection, shellcode, MSFVenom, Meterpreter, EDR evasion, process tree spoofing

entry.title: sign expired
entry.summary:
Title: sign-expired — Signtool modification for expired certificates
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This repository provides a modified workflow to allow Microsoft’s `signtool.exe` to sign binaries using expired code-signing certificates. It does this by DLL hijacking: a custom-built `XmlLite.dll` is placed next to the official, Microsoft-signed `signtool.exe` so it is loaded at runtime. The injected DLL alters/patches behavior so the same signing command works even when the certificate is expired. The repo includes the C++ source (`dllmain.cpp`) and bundled binaries (`signtool.exe`, `XmlLite.dll`). It is primarily useful for red teamers, malware developers, and researchers exploring Authenticode trust edge-cases and signing tool abuse. It’s interesting because it leverages a trusted Microsoft-signed utility plus side-loading to bypass expected certificate validity enforcement.
Technical Focus: - DLL search order hijacking / DLL side-loading (XmlLite.dll) - Authenticode code signing and certificate validity checks - Runtime patching/hooking inside `signtool.exe` - Windows trust chain / signing tool behavior - C++ DLL entrypoint manipulation (`DllMain`)
Use Cases: - Testing how security products validate Authenticode signatures vs certificate expiration - Simulating attacker tradecraft that abuses trusted signed tooling (LOLBIN-adjacent) - Researching Windows signing pipeline and trust enforcement gaps - Building detections for DLL side-loading against developer tooling (e.g., signtool)
Keywords: signtool, Authenticode, code signing, expired certificate, DLL hijacking, DLL side-loading, XmlLite.dll, Windows, trust validation, certificate chain, signature verification, WinVerifyTrust, DllMain, C++, Microsoft-signed binary, LOLBIN, supply chain, EDR evasion

entry.title: process injection ctray
entry.summary:
Title: Windows Process Injection: Extra Window Bytes
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post describes a Windows GUI-based process injection technique that abuses “Extra Window Bytes” (index 0) on the `Shell_TrayWnd` window class to hijack Explorer’s tray window object. It targets the `CTray` class instance pointer stored via `SetWindowLongPtr/GetWindowLongPtr`, a method historically associated with PowerLoader-era malware. The technique reads the existing `CTray` object and its vtable from `explorer.exe`, allocates memory in the remote process, writes a modified `CTray` object whose `WndProc` points to attacker-controlled shellcode, and swaps the window’s extra bytes pointer to the new object. Execution is triggered by posting a window message (e.g., `WM_CLOSE`), then the original pointer is restored to reduce instability. It’s useful for red teams, pentesters, and malware researchers studying alternative injection primitives and “Shatter”-style UI attacks that still work despite UIPI-era mitigations.
Technical Focus: - Extra Window Bytes abuse (`GetWindowLongPtr/SetWindowLongPtr`, index 0) - `Shell_TrayWnd` / Explorer tray window internals (`CTray` object, vtable) - Remote memory operations (`OpenProcess`, `ReadProcessMemory`, `WriteProcessMemory`) - Remote allocation and execution (`VirtualAllocEx`, RWX/RW regions) - Message-based triggering (`PostMessage`, `WM_CLOSE`) - Shatter attacks and UIPI considerations
Use Cases: - Injecting code into `explorer.exe` via GUI object pointer manipulation - Researching non-thread-creation injection techniques (message-triggered execution) - Building/validating EDR detections for window object tampering and vtable-style hijacks - Reproducing historical malware tradecraft (PowerLoader-like injection)
Keywords: Windows process injection, Extra Window Bytes, SetWindowLongPtr, GetWindowLongPtr, Shell_TrayWnd, explorer.exe, CTray, vtable hijacking, WndProc, Shatter attack, UIPI, FindWindow, GetWindowThreadProcessId, OpenProcess, ReadProcessMemory, WriteProcessMemory, VirtualAllocEx, PostMessage, WM_CLOSE, PowerLoader

entry.title: bypassing edr in a crystal clear way
entry.summary:
Title: Bypassing EDR in a Crystal Clear Way
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post walks through building an evasive Windows reflective loader (UDRL-style) that loads a C2 implant (Cobalt Strike Beacon used as the example) while minimizing common EDR detection signals. - It explains why default rDLL/sRDI loaders get caught (MEM_PRIVATE/RWX regions, unbacked return addresses, broken stack unwinding, and in-memory signatures) and then incrementally fixes each issue. - Core techniques include module overloading (stomping a legitimate SEC_IMAGE-mapped DLL) plus .pdata registration via RtlAddFunctionTable to restore valid stack unwinding, and NtContinue-based execution transfer to avoid leaving loader return addresses on the stack. - It also covers load-time API call stack spoofing using synthetic frames (Draugr-style gadget-based spoofing), sleep masking by hooking Sleep via Crystal Palace import resolution, and removing Crystal Palace “islands of invariance” by rewriting instructions with ised to defeat YARA signatures. - The approach is built around Crystal Palace (PIC linker) to compose tradecraft components as COFF/PIC objects and integrate with C2 payload pipelines (including raw Beacon generation via CNA). - Useful primarily for red team operators, implant/loader developers, and defenders studying modern behavioral detections around call stacks, memory backing, and in-memory scanning.
Technical Focus: - Reflective loading internals (PE mapping, relocations, IAT resolution, section permissions) - Module overloading / DLL stomping with SEC_IMAGE (NtCreateSection/NtMapViewOfSection) - Call stack evasion: .pdata registration (RtlAddFunctionTable) and NtContinue execution transfer - API call stack spoofing with synthetic frames and gadget selection (Draugr-style) - Sleep masking via API hooking / import resolution interception (GetProcAddress hooks) - Signature evasion: XOR payload encryption + Crystal Palace YARA island removal with ised
Use Cases: - Building custom UDRLs for C2 frameworks (Cobalt Strike, Havoc-like loaders) with reduced behavioral IOCs - Avoiding “unbacked memory + suspicious stack” detections during both loader setup and beacon runtime - Researching/replicating EDR call-stack validation logic (unwind correctness, “preceding CALL” checks) - Implementing sleep masking and memory-scan resistance for long-lived implants - Hardening offensive artifacts against static YARA signatures generated from invariant byte sequences
Keywords: EDR evasion, reflective loader, UDRL, Cobalt Strike Beacon, Crystal Palace, PIC linker, COFF, PICO, sRDI, module overloading, DLL stomping, NtCreateSection, NtMapViewOfSection, SEC_IMAGE, RtlAddFunctionTable, .pdata, stack unwinding, NtContinue, CONTEXT, call stack spoofing, Draugr, gadget selection, LoadLibraryA, CFG, GetProcAddress hook, sleep masking, VirtualProtect, MEM_PRIVATE, RWX, ETW, YARA, ised, islands of invariance, ROR13 hashing, PEB walking, EAT parsing

entry.title: Byoud
entry.summary:
Title: Fantastic unwind information and where to find them (BYOUD – Bring Your Own Unwinding Data)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post introduces BYOUD, a CET-compliant call stack spoofing framework that avoids modifying return addresses (which Intel CET shadow stacks would detect) by instead manipulating Windows x64 unwind metadata. - It targets the separation between CET’s return-flow integrity (shadow stack) and Windows stack walking/unwinding (SEH + `.pdata`/`UNWIND_INFO`) to make stack traces appear legitimate and fully unwindable while hiding real caller frames. - Multiple variants are described: direct tampering of `UNWIND_INFO`, hijacking `RUNTIME_FUNCTION` unwind offsets, injecting/reusing runtime function entries, and a JIT-oriented approach using `RtlAddFunctionTable`. - The post also dives into practical constraints and artifacts, including interactions with `/GS` stack cookies and why `dbghelp`/`StackWalk64` can fail to consult dynamic function tables depending on address ownership. - It proposes a more complex “state-driven” architecture to better align visible stacks with shadow stack reality, and discusses detection strategies (integrity monitoring of exception metadata, shadow stack vs. normal stack comparison). - Useful for red teamers/malware developers researching post-CET evasion and for blue teamers building detections around unwind metadata tampering and shadow stack validation.
Technical Focus: - Intel CET / Hardware-enforced Shadow Stack (HSP) constraints on stack spoofing - Windows x64 unwinding internals: `.pdata`, `RUNTIME_FUNCTION`, `UNWIND_INFO`, unwind opcodes (`UWOP_ALLOC_SMALL/LARGE`) - Metadata tampering and runtime function table manipulation (offset hijacking, injection, reuse) - Dynamic function tables for JIT code: `RtlAddFunctionTable` / `RtlDeleteFunctionTable` and lookup paths - Stack walking discrepancies: `RtlVirtualUnwind`/`RtlLookupFunctionEntry` vs `dbghelp` (`StackWalk64`, `SymFunctionTableAccess64`) - Detection approaches: section integrity checks, memory protection monitoring, shadow stack correlation
Use Cases: - Hide in-memory execution/shellcode frames from user-mode stack walkers without tripping CET - Build CET-compatible proxy-call based stack spoofing for API invocation (e.g., via RPC proxy functions) - Research and validate how EDRs/tools reconstruct stacks (OS unwinder vs dbghelp pipeline) - Develop detections for `.pdata`/unwind metadata tampering and suspicious dynamic function tables - Create PoCs to test shadow stack vs normal stack inconsistency detection
Keywords: Intel CET, Hardware Stack Protection, shadow stack, Control Protection Exception, Windows x64 unwinding, SEH, .pdata, .xdata, RUNTIME_FUNCTION, UNWIND_INFO, UNWIND_CODE, UWOP_ALLOC_LARGE, BaseThreadInitThunk, RtlUserThreadStart, RtlVirtualUnwind, RtlLookupFunctionEntry, RtlAddFunctionTable, StackWalk64, dbghelp.dll, SymFunctionTableAccess64, stack spoofing, call stack evasion

entry.title: BYOUD
entry.summary:
Title: BYOUD — Bring Your Own Unwind Data
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): BYOUD is a Windows x64 stack spoofing framework that hides or alters call-chain visibility by manipulating unwind metadata rather than only crafting fake return addresses. It targets how debuggers and EDRs reconstruct stack traces by abusing PE exception handling structures (e.g., `.pdata`, `RUNTIME_FUNCTION`, `UNWIND_INFO`) and dynamic function tables. The repo implements multiple techniques ranging from in-place `UNWIND_INFO` tampering to injecting/hijacking `RUNTIME_FUNCTION` entries and registering JIT unwind info via `RtlAddFunctionTable`, including variants that account for visibility differences in tools like WinDbg and SystemInformer. It includes `byoud.dll` (core framework with `ShieldedExecution` and an assembly `CallGate` stub) and `udinject.exe` (a harness to test techniques and validate unwind/stack-trace behavior). It’s primarily useful for red teams, offensive security researchers, and malware developers studying call stack evasion and telemetry blind spots. It’s interesting because it flips the usual stack spoofing approach by attacking the unwind/exception metadata that many stack-walking implementations trust.
Technical Focus: - Windows x64 unwind metadata (`UNWIND_INFO`) manipulation - PE exception directory / `.pdata` and `RUNTIME_FUNCTION` abuse - Dynamic function tables via `RtlAddFunctionTable` - Stack trace evasion against debuggers/EDRs - `LdrpInvertedFunctionTable` manipulation (WinDbg-oriented variant) - `/GS` stack cookies interaction and constraints
Use Cases: - Evaluating EDR/user-mode stack-walking robustness against unwind-data tampering - Building PoCs for stealthy shellcode execution with obscured call chains - Researching debugger vs. EDR differences in unwind resolution (WinDbg/SystemInformer) - Developing and testing new stack spoofing variants based on exception metadata - Training/validation tool for understanding Windows x64 unwinding behavior
Keywords: Windows x64, stack spoofing, stack walking, unwind data, UNWIND_INFO, RUNTIME_FUNCTION, .pdata, PE exception directory, RtlAddFunctionTable, LdrpInvertedFunctionTable, VirtualAlloc, shellcode, call chain hiding, EDR evasion, WinDbg, SystemInformer, /GS, stack cookies, exception handling, user-mode telemetry

entry.title: doublepulsar rs
entry.summary:
Title: doublepulsar-rs — Rust Cobalt Strike User-Defined Reflective Loader (UDRL) implementing sRDI
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository provides a Rust-based, position-independent reflective loader for Cobalt Strike using the User-Defined Reflective Loader (UDRL) API and Shellcode Reflective DLL Injection (sRDI). - It focuses on executing an encrypted beacon payload in-memory while reducing common memory-scanning and behavioral detections via module stomping, call stack spoofing, sleep obfuscation, and memory/code encryption. - The loader uses a “prepended” architecture (ReflectiveLoader placed before the PE) to behave as PIC shellcode that decrypts and maps the payload at runtime. - It includes multiple sleep obfuscation implementations (Ekko default, FOLIAGE, Zilean, XOR masking) and optional syscall dispatch with Hell’s Gate / Halo’s Gate / Tartarus Gate style fallbacks. - Useful primarily for red teams and adversary emulation engineers building custom Cobalt Strike loaders, and for blue teams studying modern in-memory tradecraft and writing detections (YARA rule included). - Interesting due to the combination of modern evasive techniques (heap isolation, IAT hooking, synthetic stacks, RC4 via SystemFunction032) packaged into a relatively small (~65KB) Rust PIC loader.
Technical Focus: - Cobalt Strike UDRL + sRDI reflective loading (prepended PIC loader) - Module stomping and in-memory PE mapping/decryption - Sleep obfuscation (Ekko / FOLIAGE / Zilean / XOR masking) with NtContinue chains and fiber support - Synthetic call stack spoofing + return address spoofing - IAT hooking, heap isolation, dynamic memory encryption - Optional direct/indirect syscalls (Hell’s Gate / Halo’s Gate / Tartarus Gate)
Use Cases: - Custom Cobalt Strike stageless beacon loader development (x64) - Evasion research against EDR memory scanning and behavioral detections - Purple-team tradecraft emulation (sleep masking, stack spoofing, module stomping) - Detection engineering: build YARA/memory signatures and validate against loader behaviors - Training/education on reflective loading architectures and in-memory OPSEC
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, UDRL, User-Defined Reflective Loader, sRDI, reflective loader, Rust, position-independent code, shellcode, module stomping, synthetic call stack, call stack spoofing, return address spoofing, sleep obfuscation, Ekko, FOLIAGE, NtContinue, fibers, IAT hooking, heap isolation, SystemFunction032, RC4, Hell’s Gate, Halo’s Gate, Tartarus Gate, YARA, in-memory decryption, Windows x64

entry.title: venom rs
entry.summary:
Title: venom-rs — Shellcode Reflective DLL Injection (sRDI) in Rust (Codename: Venom)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Venom-rs is a Rust implementation of Shellcode Reflective DLL Injection (sRDI), converting a DLL into position-independent “shellcode” that can be injected and executed in a remote process. It includes a shellcode generator that packages a reflective loader + payload DLL + optional parameter data, and a simple PoC injector to allocate memory and run the resulting blob. The reflective loader performs in-memory PE loading tasks (mapping, relocations/rebasing, import resolution) and can execute either the payload’s DllMain or a specified exported function (e.g., “SayHello”) selected via flags and a function-hash mechanism. The project documents the bootstrap stub and the in-memory layout, emphasizing that OPSEC hardening is intentionally left to the user. It’s primarily useful for red teamers, pentesters, and malware researchers studying or prototyping Windows in-memory loading/injection tradecraft. Interesting due to its compact (~4KB) loader and clear, modular Rust codebase demonstrating end-to-end sRDI generation and execution.
Technical Focus: - Shellcode Reflective DLL Injection (sRDI) design and implementation - In-memory PE loading: relocations, import resolution, section protections - Position-independent code (PIC) packaging and bootstrap stub mechanics - Windows process injection primitives (VirtualAlloc, remote execution) - Function selection via hashing and parameter passing conventions (x64 calling)
Use Cases: - Generate PIC blobs from DLL payloads for custom injectors (BYOI) - Prototype/learn reflective loading and PE mapping behavior in Rust - Build and test process injection chains in controlled lab environments - Research detection opportunities around reflective loaders and RWX memory usage
Keywords: Rust, Windows, sRDI, reflective DLL injection, shellcode, position-independent code, PE reflection, in-memory loader, import resolution, relocations, rebasing, section protections, VirtualAlloc, process injection, DllMain, function hashing, bootstrap shellcode, payload DLL, injector, x64 calling convention

entry.title: process hollowing
entry.summary:
Title: Process Hollowing
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post walks through implementing classic Windows process hollowing by creating a benign process (example: `svchost.exe`) in a suspended state, locating its entry point in memory, overwriting it with shellcode, and resuming execution. It addresses the practical challenge of ASLR by using `NtQueryInformationProcess`/`ZwQueryInformationProcess` to retrieve the target’s PEB, then reading `PEB.ImageBaseAddress` to parse PE headers and compute the entry point VA (`ImageBase + AddressOfEntryPoint`). The technique uses `ReadProcessMemory` to extract remote PEB/PE structures and `WriteProcessMemory` + `ResumeThread` to redirect execution to the payload. A Rust PoC is referenced that prefers direct NTAPI (`ntdll.dll`) usage over higher-level Win32 APIs. It’s primarily useful for red teamers, pentesters, and malware developers studying Windows injection tradecraft and how EDRs may detect it (with a VirusTotal detection snapshot included).
Technical Focus: - Process hollowing (suspended process + entry point overwrite) - NTAPI process introspection (`NtQueryInformationProcess` / `ZwQueryInformationProcess`) - PEB parsing and `ImageBaseAddress` retrieval - PE header parsing (`IMAGE_DOS_HEADER`, `IMAGE_NT_HEADERS64`, `IMAGE_OPTIONAL_HEADER64`) - Remote memory operations (`ReadProcessMemory`, `WriteProcessMemory`) - Thread control (`ResumeThread`) and ASLR considerations
Use Cases: - Execute payloads under the guise of a legitimate process image - Build/understand injection PoCs in Rust using `ntdll.dll` syscalls/NTAPI - Research EDR detection points around hollowing (suspended create, remote writes, entry point tampering) - Training material for Windows internals: PEB and PE header layout in live processes
Keywords: process hollowing, svchost.exe, suspended process, CreateProcessA, NtQueryInformationProcess, ZwQueryInformationProcess, ProcessBasicInformation, PEB, ImageBaseAddress, ASLR, ReadProcessMemory, WriteProcessMemory, ResumeThread, entry point, AddressOfEntryPoint, IMAGE_DOS_HEADER, IMAGE_NT_HEADERS64, IMAGE_OPTIONAL_HEADER64, Rust, NTAPI, ntdll.dll, shellcode injection, WinDbg, PE parsing

entry.title: parallel syscalls
entry.summary:
Title: Parallel Syscalls (EDR Parallel-asis through Analysis)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post explains the “Parallel Syscalls / Parallel-asis” technique for obtaining reliable Windows syscall numbers at runtime without trusting potentially userland-hooked NTDLL stubs. It leverages Windows 10’s parallel DLL loading detour-detection logic, specifically the `ntdll!LdrpThunkSignature` bytes used to validate critical loader syscalls, to recover syscall IDs for `NtOpenFile`, `NtCreateSection`, and `NtMapViewOfSection`. With those syscalls, an operator can map a fresh on-disk copy of `ntdll.dll` into memory, parse its Export Address Table, and extract additional syscall numbers dynamically. The article provides foundational background on Windows rings, syscall flow (KERNEL32/KERNELBASE → NTDLL → ntoskrnl/SSDT), common EDR hooking methods, and PE/PEB parsing needed to implement the approach. It’s primarily useful for red teamers, malware developers, and offensive tool authors building syscall-based execution paths to reduce exposure to user-mode API hooks. It’s interesting because it repurposes a Windows loader anti-detour mechanism as a syscall-number oracle and a stepping stone to unhooked NTDLL mapping.
Technical Focus: - Windows 10 loader internals (parallel DLL loading, `LdrInitializeThunk`, `LdrpEnableParallelLoading`, `LdrpDetectDetour`) - Syscall stub structure and runtime syscall number extraction - Mapping a clean `ntdll.dll` via `NtOpenFile` + `NtCreateSection` + `NtMapViewOfSection` - PEB/TEB traversal to locate loaded modules without `GetModuleHandle` - PE parsing (DOS/NT headers, DataDirectory, Export Address Table) - Userland hooking and unhooking concepts (inline hooks, IAT hooks, Detours)
Use Cases: - Build direct/indirect syscall frameworks that avoid hooked Win32/NTDLL APIs - Load an unhooked NTDLL copy in-memory to resolve clean syscall stubs/exports - Implement stealthier process injection or memory operations using syscalls - Research/detect EDR hook strategies by understanding loader detour checks - Port syscall-resolution logic into Rust/C#/Nim offensive tooling
Keywords: Windows syscalls, Parallel-asis, Parallel Syscalls, EDR evasion, userland hooks, NTDLL, LdrpThunkSignature, LdrpDetectDetour, LdrInitializeThunk, NtOpenFile, NtCreateSection, NtMapViewOfSection, ZwMapViewOfSection, SSDT, ntoskrnl.exe, PEB, TEB, PE parsing, Export Address Table, inline hooking, IAT hooking, Microsoft Detours, WinDbg, IDA, Rust offensive security

entry.title: malware trick 39.html
entry.summary:
Title: Malware development trick 39: Run payload via EnumDesktopsA. Simple Nim example.
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - The post demonstrates executing in-memory shellcode by abusing the Windows GUI API `EnumDesktopsA`, implemented in the Nim programming language. - It allocates RWX memory with `VirtualAlloc`, copies a shellcode buffer into it via `RtlMoveMemory`, then casts the buffer to a `DESKTOPENUMPROCA` callback and triggers execution when `EnumDesktopsA` enumerates desktops. - The example uses a benign “meow-meow” MessageBox payload to validate the technique and shows compilation with Nim/MinGW for x64 Windows. - This is primarily useful for red teamers, pentesters, and malware developers exploring alternative shellcode execution paths via callback-based WinAPI. - It’s interesting as a minimal callback execution primitive in Nim that avoids creating a new thread directly and illustrates how common enumeration APIs can be repurposed for code execution.
Technical Focus: - WinAPI callback execution via `EnumDesktopsA` / `DESKTOPENUMPROCA` - In-memory shellcode staging (RWX allocation + copy) - `VirtualAlloc` and `RtlMoveMemory` usage - Nim + winim FFI and function pointer casting - Window stations/desktops enumeration (`GetProcessWindowStation`)
Use Cases: - Proof-of-concept shellcode execution using GUI enumeration callbacks - Porting callback-based execution techniques from C to Nim - Testing EDR/telemetry coverage for unusual callback execution flows - Building minimal loaders that trigger execution without `CreateThread`
Keywords: EnumDesktopsA, DESKTOPENUMPROCA, GetProcessWindowStation, VirtualAlloc, RtlMoveMemory, PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE, MEM_COMMIT, shellcode, callback execution, function pointer casting, Nim, winim, MinGW, Windows 11, window station, desktop enumeration, in-memory execution, MessageBox payload, loader, WinAPI abuse

entry.title: IronPE
entry.summary:
Title: IronPE – Minimal Windows PE manual loader written in Rust
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): IronPE is a minimal manual PE loader (“manual mapping”) for Windows written in Rust that can load and execute x86 and x64 PE files directly from memory. It reimplements core behaviors of the Windows loader: parsing PE headers, allocating memory, mapping sections, applying base relocations, resolving imports, and jumping to the Original Entry Point (OEP). The project is positioned as an educational/research implementation to understand PE structure and Windows loader internals, and is a Rust rewrite of the author’s earlier C# dotNetPELoader. It demonstrates architecture constraints (x86 loader can’t load x64 PE and vice versa) and includes a demo running tools like mimikatz via in-memory execution. Useful for red teamers, malware researchers, and reverse engineers studying loader tradecraft, and for blue teamers wanting to understand what manual mapping does at a low level.
Technical Focus: - Portable Executable (PE) parsing (headers, sections) - Manual mapping / in-memory execution - Base relocation processing - Import Address Table (IAT) resolution - WinAPI loader primitives (VirtualAlloc, LoadLibrary, GetProcAddress) - x86 vs x64 loader/PE compatibility
Use Cases: - Build a research-grade PE manual loader in Rust - Study Windows loader internals by reimplementing the load pipeline - Execute a PE from memory for controlled red team simulations - Create test artifacts to validate EDR detections around manual mapping behaviors - Compare Rust vs C#/C implementations of PE loading logic
Keywords: Windows, Rust, PE loader, manual mapping, Portable Executable, in-memory execution, section mapping, base relocations, import resolution, IAT, OEP, VirtualAlloc, LoadLibrary, GetProcAddress, x86, x64, WinAPI, fileless, mimikatz, reverse engineering

entry.title: initial access the art of getting in
entry.summary:
Title: Initial Access: The Art of Getting In
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - A long-form red team tradecraft guide focused on the “initial access” phase (MITRE ATT&CK TA0001), framed as a 9-phase playbook from payload delivery through social engineering, exploitation, and physical access. - It aims to help operators reliably obtain a first foothold despite modern controls like email gateways, SmartScreen/MOTW, AMSI, EDR, and MFA. - The post breaks down payload delivery chains (container/trigger/loader/beacon), MOTW bypass via containers (notably VHD/VHDX), HTML smuggling, code-signing/reputation considerations, DLL sideloading discovery with Procmon, and modern shellcode loader patterns (RW→RX, section mapping, direct/indirect syscalls). - It also covers phishing variants (attachments/links, QR “quishing”, Teams phishing), AitM/MFA bypass with Evilginx, OAuth device-code phishing, credential attacks (spraying/stuffing with IP rotation), and exploitation of public-facing apps with scanning workflows. - Later sections address vishing/helpdesk social engineering, watering holes/drive-bys, hardware implants/USB attacks, and supply-chain/trusted-relationship compromise, backed by recent APT/cybercrime case studies (APT29, Scattered Spider, Lazarus, Cl0p, etc.). - Most useful for red teams and pentesters designing initial access campaigns; secondarily useful for blue teams as a consolidated map of common ingress patterns and OPSEC considerations.
Technical Focus: - Mark-of-the-Web (Zone.Identifier ADS) bypass via ISO/VHD/IMG containers - HTML smuggling (client-side payload assembly) and attachment-based delivery chains - SmartScreen reputation, code signing, and metadata/signature cloning - DLL sideloading (search order abuse) and Procmon-based discovery - EDR evasion loader patterns: RW→RX, NtCreateSection/NtMapViewOfSection, direct/indirect syscalls (SysWhispers/HellsGate) - MFA bypass and token theft: Evilginx AitM, OAuth device-code phishing (Microsoft 365/Entra ID)
Use Cases: - Build and test initial-access delivery chains that survive gateway + SmartScreen + EDR controls - Design phishing campaigns (email/Teams/QR) with realistic pretexts and fallback vectors - Simulate MFA-bypass scenarios via session cookie theft or device-code OAuth abuse - Perform controlled password spraying/stuffing with enumeration and IP-rotation OPSEC - Triage external attack surface for exploitable public-facing apps (httpx/nuclei workflows) - Plan and scope physical-access and trusted-relationship simulations for engagements
Keywords: Initial Access, MITRE ATT&CK TA0001, T1566, phishing, spearphishing, HTML smuggling, Mark-of-the-Web, Zone.Identifier, SmartScreen, VHD/VHDX, ISO, DLL sideloading, Process Monitor, AMSI, ETW, direct syscalls, NtCreateSection, NtMapViewOfSection, Evilginx, adversary-in-the-middle, OAuth device code, Microsoft 365, Entra ID, password spraying, o365spray, CredMaster, fireprox, nuclei, httpx, vishing, supply chain compromise, CitrixBleed, CVE-2023-34362 MOVEit

entry.title: raii
entry.summary:
Title: RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) for Offensive Security Tooling in C++ (with a VirtualAlloc/VirtualFree Wrapper)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This write-up explains how to use the C++ RAII idiom to make Windows offensive/security tooling more reliable by automatically managing resources (heap memory, Win32 handles, locks, etc.). - It highlights common failure modes in red-team utilities—leaks, double-frees, and missed cleanup across early returns/exceptions—and shows how RAII ties acquisition to constructors and cleanup to destructors. - The post walks through ownership semantics (raw pointers are non-owning), exception safety via stack unwinding, and how to prevent unsafe copying by deleting copy operations. - It also covers lvalue/rvalue categories and how rvalue references enable move semantics for safe ownership transfer. - A concrete example implements a `VirtualMemory` RAII class wrapping `VirtualAlloc`/`VirtualFree`, applying the Rule of Five, `noexcept`, and typed accessors. - Useful for red teamers, pentesters, and Windows tooling developers who need stable long-running implants/utilities and fewer operational crashes.
Technical Focus: - RAII and exception-safe cleanup (stack unwinding, destructors) - Ownership semantics (raw pointers vs owning wrappers) - Rule of Five (delete copy, implement move ctor/assignment) - Move semantics and `std::move` (rvalue references) - Win32 memory management (`VirtualAlloc`, `VirtualFree`, protection flags) - Modern C++ practices (`noexcept`, `const` correctness, templates, `std::format`)
Use Cases: - Building leak-resistant Windows red-team tooling that uses Win32 resources heavily - Implementing safe wrappers for process/thread/token handles and synchronization primitives - Managing executable memory buffers for shellcode staging without cleanup bugs - Refactoring legacy C-style code with many error paths into exception-safe C++ - Enabling safe ownership transfer of allocated memory between components/modules
Keywords: RAII, C++, Windows internals, Win32 API, VirtualAlloc, VirtualFree, MEM_COMMIT, MEM_RESERVE, MEM_RELEASE, PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE, exception safety, stack unwinding, destructor, smart pointer, ownership, double free, memory leak, Rule of Five, move semantics, rvalue reference, std::move, noexcept, const correctness, templates, std::format

entry.title: LdrShuffle
entry.summary:
Title: LdrShuffle — Stealthy code execution via runtime modification of DLL EntryPoint in the PEB loader lists
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): LdrShuffle is a Windows code-execution/injection technique that redirects execution by overwriting the `EntryPoint` field of a loaded module’s `_LDR_DATA_TABLE_ENTRY` at runtime. It leverages legitimate Windows loader behavior (thread attach/detach and process attach/detach `DllMain` invocations) so payload execution occurs under a less suspicious, loader-originated call stack. The repo includes a local PoC for “API proxying” (running chosen Win32 APIs with controlled arguments/return value handling) and a cross-process injector (`LdrInject`) that performs remote PEB/LDR walking plus `WriteProcessMemory` to patch a target DLL’s entrypoint without `CreateRemoteThread`/APCs. It discusses practical constraints of executing under the Loader Lock and in `DllMain`-like contexts (deadlocks, race conditions), and mitigations such as spawning a new thread for problematic APIs (e.g., WinINet/WinHTTP). It also includes notes/demos for adapting the approach to run Cobalt Strike beacon shellcode more safely by triggering only on `DLL_THREAD_ATTACH` and creating a new thread. Useful for red teams, implant developers, and defenders studying loader-based execution and call stack evasion tradeoffs.
Technical Focus: - PEB / `PEB_LDR_DATA` / `_LDR_DATA_TABLE_ENTRY` traversal and manipulation - Overwriting DLL `EntryPoint` to hijack loader-driven execution paths - Thread attach/detach (`DLL_THREAD_ATTACH`/`DLL_THREAD_DETACH`) execution triggers - Loader Lock / `DllMain` constraints, deadlocks, and stability considerations - Cross-process memory R/W injection (`OpenProcess`, `WriteProcessMemory`) - Call stack shaping / API proxying via legitimate loader call chains
Use Cases: - Stealthy in-process code execution without classic thread/APC injection primitives - Remote “threadless” style execution trigger by patching a target module’s entrypoint - Running sensitive APIs with a loader-originated call stack (API proxying) - Researching/validating EDR detections around PEB/LDR tampering and entrypoint patching - Implant staging where execution is deferred until a thread event occurs
Keywords: Windows loader, PEB, PEB_LDR_DATA, _LDR_DATA_TABLE_ENTRY, EntryPoint hijack, DllMain, Ldrp, Loader Lock, DLL_THREAD_ATTACH, DLL_THREAD_DETACH, API proxying, call stack evasion, process injection, threadless injection, OpenProcess, WriteProcessMemory, VirtualAlloc, VirtualProtect, CreateThread, WinINet, WinHTTP, Cobalt Strike beacon, shellcode execution

entry.title: main
entry.summary:
Title: VectoredOverloading (Vectored Overloading PE Injection Technique)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): VectoredOverloading is a C++ proof-of-concept implementing “Vectored Overloading,” a local PE injection technique first seen in the KidKadi malware. It abuses Hardware Breakpoints (HWBP) and Vectored Exception Handling (VEH) to interfere with the Windows loader’s DLL mapping flow and transparently substitute a malicious PE image in place of a legitimate DLL’s section object. The technique creates a `SEC_IMAGE` mapping from a real DLL, overlays it with a payload PE, and then hooks loader syscalls by setting HWBPs on `NtOpenSection` and `NtMapViewOfSection`. When those syscalls are hit, a VEH handler skips/emulates the syscalls and rewrites OUT parameters so the loader believes it is mapping the legitimate DLL while actually mapping the payload. The Windows loader then performs normal image processing (imports, relocations, etc.) and eventually transfers execution to the payload entrypoint. This is mainly useful for red teamers and malware researchers studying loader-abuse tradecraft and for blue teams developing detections around VEH/HWBP-based syscall manipulation.
Technical Focus: - Vectored Exception Handling (VEH) abuse - Hardware breakpoints (debug registers) for control-flow interception - Windows loader internals and image mapping (`SEC_IMAGE`) - Syscall emulation/parameter spoofing for `NtOpenSection` and `NtMapViewOfSection` - PE header manipulation (DLL characteristics, entrypoint changes) - Section object/view substitution during DLL load
Use Cases: - Implementing/understanding stealthy local PE loading via loader-assisted execution - Researching malware techniques that avoid classic remote-thread injection patterns - Building detections for anomalous VEH registration and debug-register usage - Reproducing KidKadi-style loader manipulation in a controlled lab - Developing EDR telemetry/heuristics around syscall skipping and OUT-parameter tampering
Keywords: Vectored Overloading, KidKadi, PE injection, local injection, Windows loader, VEH, VectoredExceptionHandler, hardware breakpoints, debug registers, DR0-DR7, NtOpenSection, NtMapViewOfSection, SEC_IMAGE, section object, MapViewOfSection, syscall emulation, PE header manipulation, DLL characteristics, import resolution, image mapping

entry.title: stacktracer
entry.summary:
Title: StackTracer
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): StackTracer is a Rust utility that prints the stack trace (stack frames) of a specified thread in a target Windows process. Its primary goal is to help operators identify “legit-looking” call stacks from real processes that can be replicated in payloads/shellcode to improve evasiveness (e.g., when a C2 framework allows specifying spoofed stack frames). The tool takes a PID and optionally a TID, then outputs a comma-separated list of module!function+offset frames for that thread. This is useful for red teamers and malware developers experimenting with call stack spoofing / thread stack manipulation to blend into normal execution patterns. It’s interesting because it operationalizes collection of realistic stack frame chains, which are often needed when testing EDR detections based on anomalous call stacks.
Technical Focus: - Windows thread stack walking / stack trace collection - Per-process / per-thread inspection via PID/TID targeting - Call stack spoofing / “legitimate stack frame” replication for payloads - Symbol/module+offset resolution (module!function+0xoffset style output) - Rust-based Windows tooling (cross-compiling with x86_64-pc-windows-gnu)
Use Cases: - Collect real call stacks from benign GUI/system processes to model “normal” execution chains - Build or tune spoofed call stacks for shellcode loaders / C2 implants that support stack frame specification - Validate whether a payload’s execution stack resembles common Windows API call paths - Support EDR evasion research focused on call-stack-based detections
Keywords: Stack walking, stack trace, call stack spoofing, stack frames, Windows threads, PID, TID, process inspection, NtUserWaitMessage, USER32.dll, win32u.dll, KERNEL32.dll, shellcode evasion, C2 tradecraft, Rust, x86_64-pc-windows-gnu, module offset, function+offset, EDR telemetry

entry.title: Fritter
entry.summary:
Title: Fritter — Evasive Donut Fork Shellcode Generator
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Fritter is a heavily modified fork of Donut that generates position-independent shellcode for in-memory execution of VBScript, JScript, EXE, DLL, and .NET assemblies. It focuses on evasion and signature resistance by reworking Donut’s crypto, compression, and API resolution layers and removing unneeded features while targeting x64-only. Each build produces unique output via per-run randomization of the entry stub, encoding layer, and loader blob (different instructions/keys/layout). At runtime it reduces the executable footprint by managing memory permissions so only a small portion is executable at a time, optionally using a VEH-based “sliding window” mode. It also supports multiple output formats (raw, base64, C, Python, PowerShell, C#, hex) and includes options for .NET AppDomain naming and “module overloading” via a decoy module. Useful primarily for red teams/pentesters and malware researchers evaluating loader detection and shellcode staging tradeoffs.
Technical Focus: - Position-independent code (PIC) shellcode generation (x64) - Loader randomization / polymorphism for signature resistance - Reworked crypto + compression + API resolution layers - Memory permission choreography (RW→RX) and VEH-based sliding execution window - In-memory execution of .NET assemblies and unmanaged PE/DLL entrypoints - Optional HTTP staging and module overloading (decoy module)
Use Cases: - Generate evasive in-memory loaders for EXE/DLL/.NET payloads during red team ops - Produce per-build unique shellcode to test EDR signature robustness - Research/diff loader design changes vs Donut (API hashing, crypto/compression, PIC) - Build staged payload delivery workflows (HTTP staging, multiple output encodings) - Evaluate detection opportunities around RW/RX transitions and VEH usage
Keywords: Donut, shellcode generator, PIC, x64, in-memory execution, .NET assembly loading, VBScript, JScript, DLL entrypoint, API resolution, API hashing, encryption, compression, polymorphic loader, randomization, RWX avoidance, RW-to-RX, VEH, sliding window, module overloading, HTTP staging

entry.title: Cicada
entry.summary:
Title: Cicada PIC Stager
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Cicada is a position-independent Windows shellcode stager (PIC) that fetches a second-stage payload over HTTPS and executes it with timer-based context switching. It focuses on stealth by avoiding imports and string literals, resolving APIs via PEB walking, and using WinHTTP with a certificate validation bypass to allow self-signed C2 certificates. Its main defensive-evasion feature is “protected sleep” (inspired by Ekko): during sleep it RC4-encrypts the stager’s shellcode region, heap, and stack, and hides most of the timer/ROP chain by encrypting the chain body separately. Execution is driven by an encrypted timer chain and `NtContinue` to pivot contexts rather than a direct call/jump into the stage. After handing off execution, it self-destructs by freeing the stager memory. Useful primarily for red teamers, implant developers, and security researchers studying sleep obfuscation and PIC shellcode staging tradecraft.
Technical Focus: - Position-independent Windows shellcode (PIC) without relocations - PEB walking and dynamic API resolution (no `GetProcAddress` imports) - WinHTTP HTTPS staging with certificate validation bypass - Sleep obfuscation via RC4 encryption of code/heap/stack - Timer-chain / ROP-style execution flow using `NtContinue` CONTEXT switching - Self-deletion/self-freeing stager behavior (`VirtualFree`)
Use Cases: - Building a small Windows stager that retrieves and runs remote shellcode over HTTPS - Researching Ekko-style sleep obfuscation and encrypted timer-chain designs - Developing/benchmarking EDR detections for stack/heap/code encryption during sleep - Studying import-less API resolution and stringless shellcode techniques - Creating lab PoCs for staged payload delivery and post-execution self-erasure
Keywords: Windows shellcode, PIC, stager, WinHTTP, HTTPS staging, certificate validation bypass, self-signed certificate, PEB walk, dynamic API resolution, ntdll, kernel32, advapi32, NtContinue, CONTEXT, timer queue, sleep obfuscation, Ekko, RC4, SystemFunction032, VirtualProtect, VirtualAlloc, VirtualFree

entry.title: WaveGate
entry.summary:
Title: WaveGate
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - WaveGate is a Tauri/Rust-based remote access tool (RAT) with an operator UI and a Windows-focused client offering a broad post-compromise feature set. - It aims to provide a “feature-complete” offensive Rust RAT reference implementation rather than an evasive implant; the author notes it is early-stage and likely signatureable. - Core capabilities include remote shell/execution, file/registry/process/service/task management, WMI console, clipboard monitoring, and interactive remote desktop plus webcam/audio streaming. - It also includes network/pivoting features like a SOCKS5-style reverse proxy (including SMB/named pipe proxying), domain fronting via custom SNI, and proxy-aware outbound comms. - Post-exploitation modules cover credential/cookie recovery (Chromium variants), UAC bypass, multiple persistence methods, anti-VM/environment checks, and extensive system/AD/Kerberos enumeration plus lateral movement via WMI/WinRM/SMB/PsExec-like methods. - Useful for red teamers/pentesters and malware researchers studying Rust implant architecture, operator tooling, and Windows post-exploitation tradecraft in a single codebase.
Technical Focus: - Rust + Tauri operator/client architecture - Windows post-exploitation (WMI, registry, services, scheduled tasks, process control) - Remote desktop capture/control (DXGI tile capture, H.264; BitBlt fallback) - Lateral movement & pivoting (SMB/WMI/WinRM, token impersonation, reverse proxy) - AD & Kerberos enumeration/ticket operations - C2 communications (TLS, HTTP→WebSocket upgrade, binary framing, SNI override/domain fronting)
Use Cases: - Build/modify a Rust-based RAT for lab red teaming and feature prototyping - Study implementation patterns for Windows management modules (registry/services/tasks/WMI) - Prototype pivoting via reverse proxy and SMB/named-pipe transport concepts - Evaluate detection opportunities for common RAT behaviors (persistence, UAC bypass, credential access) - Training/demo platform for operator UI workflows (file manager, RD, chat, logs)

entry.title: PEBwalker
entry.summary:
Title: PEBwalker
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): PEBwalker is a Rust implementation of a Windows PEB (Process Environment Block) walker used to resolve module exports and function addresses at runtime via checksum/hash comparisons. It avoids static imports by traversing the loaded module list from the PEB, locating a target DLL by hashed name, parsing its export table, and matching exported function names by hash to return a callable function pointer. This is commonly used in shellcode/loaders to reduce obvious IAT-based indicators and enable dynamic API resolution (e.g., resolving NTDLL syscalls like NtAllocateVirtualMemory). The repo includes example usage showing UTF-16 hashing for module names and byte hashing for function names. It’s primarily useful for red team tooling, malware development research, and defenders studying common in-memory API resolution patterns.
Technical Focus: - PEB access and Ldr module list traversal - Runtime API resolution via hashed module/function names - PE export table parsing (IMAGE_EXPORT_DIRECTORY) - Avoiding static imports / IAT minimization - Rust-based Windows low-level interop (FFI/pointers)
Use Cases: - Building loaders/shellcode that dynamically resolves Windows APIs - Reducing static indicators from import tables in offensive tooling - Studying/detecting PEB-walking and export-hash resolution techniques - Implementing custom GetProcAddress/LoadLibrary-like logic without imports
Keywords: Windows, PEB, Process Environment Block, Ldr, InMemoryOrderModuleList, export table, PE parsing, IMAGE_EXPORT_DIRECTORY, API hashing, checksum, dynamic import resolution, IAT evasion, ntdll.dll, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, function pointer, Rust, FFI, shellcode, loader

entry.title: Doppel
entry.summary:
Title: Döppel (Doppel) — Advanced Evasive Persistent Shellcode Loader for Windows
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Döppel is a Windows C++ proof-of-concept shellcode loader designed to be evasive, VM-aware, and persistent. It focuses on defeating common analysis and telemetry by performing multiple VM/sandbox checks, unhooking `ntdll.dll`, and patching ETW. The loader retrieves an encrypted payload over TCP (RC4), then executes a staged workflow where shellcode is XOR-decrypted “just-in-time” and injected into `explorer.exe` in chunks. It includes stealthier injection details such as setting the remote thread context into a `MEM_IMAGE`-backed region to evade injected-thread heuristics, and mentions using the “gargoyle” technique to reduce in-memory scanning exposure. Persistence is implemented in an unusual way via a debugger global flag targeting `wuauclt.exe`. Useful primarily for red teamers, malware analysts, and defenders studying modern loader tradecraft and detection opportunities.
Technical Focus: - Anti-VM / anti-sandbox checks (mouse, hardware, activity heuristics) - `ntdll.dll` unhooking and ETW patching - Dynamic API resolution (Kernel32) for evasion - Staged payload retrieval (RC4 over TCP) and C2 comms (reverse-HTTPS) - Chunked shellcode decryption/injection into `explorer.exe` + thread context manipulation (`MEM_IMAGE`) - “Gargoyle” in-memory evasion technique + obfuscation/packing
Use Cases: - Build/test evasive shellcode loader tradecraft in a controlled lab - Study ETW/`ntdll` tampering and corresponding detection strategies - Evaluate EDR heuristics around remote thread creation and injected-thread enumeration - Reverse engineering practice on a packed/obfuscated loader with anti-analysis gates - Prototype alternative persistence mechanisms leveraging debugger-related flags
Keywords: Windows, C++, shellcode loader, process injection, explorer.exe, remote thread, thread context, MEM_IMAGE, ntdll unhooking, ETW patching, anti-VM, sandbox evasion, dynamic API resolution, Kernel32.dll, RC4, XOR decryption, reverse HTTPS C2, persistence, wuauclt.exe, gargoyle technique

entry.title: KaplaStrike
entry.summary:
Title: KaplaStrike (Crystal-Palace UDRL)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): KaplaStrike is a custom Cobalt Strike reflective loader (UDRL) built using the Crystal Palace framework, focused on stealthy in-memory loading and EDR evasion. It replaces typical `LoadLibrary`-based loading with module overloading using `NtCreateSection`/`NtMapViewOfSection`, and improves stack hygiene by registering unwind metadata (`.pdata`) via `RtlAddFunctionTable`. The loader transfers execution using `NtContinue` with synthetic thread-start frames (`BaseThreadInitThunk`/`RtlUserThreadStart`) and performs call stack spoofing during setup (via Draugr). It includes build-time XOR encryption of the Beacon DLL, sleep masking implemented through IAT hooking (`addhook`) with per-section XOR, and removes Crystal Palace YARA signatures using `ised`. It’s primarily useful for red team operators and malware/tooling developers building custom CS loaders and researching modern userland evasion tradecraft. The repo also provides a CNA script to strip Cobalt Strike’s default reflective loader so the raw DLL can be linked into a PIC blob for execution by an external shellcode runner.
Technical Focus: - Module overloading (section mapping) via `NtCreateSection` / `NtMapViewOfSection` - Unwind info / exception handling registration with `RtlAddFunctionTable` (`.pdata`) - Execution transfer with `NtContinue` and synthetic thread start frames - Call stack spoofing (Draugr-style) - Sleep masking via IAT hooking (`addhook`) and per-section encryption - Static signature reduction (YARA removal) and build-time payload encryption
Use Cases: - Building a stealthier Cobalt Strike UDRL for controlled red team operations - Researching userland EDR telemetry gaps around section mapping and `NtContinue` - Producing PIC “blob” payloads to be run by custom shellcode loaders - Experimenting with sleep masking implementations independent of Malleable C2 - Studying call stack spoofing + unwind registration for cleaner crash/telemetry behavior
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, UDRL, reflective loader, Crystal Palace, Crystal-Kit, module overloading, NtCreateSection, NtMapViewOfSection, RtlAddFunctionTable, .pdata, unwind info, NtContinue, BaseThreadInitThunk, RtlUserThreadStart, call stack spoofing, Draugr, sleep masking, IAT hooking, addhook, YARA signature removal, XOR encryption, PIC shellcode, CNA script, Malleable C2 profile

entry.title: MostShittyAV
entry.summary:
Title: AMSI Raccoon Lab (MostShittyAV) — The World’s Most Intentionally Terrible Antivirus Scanner
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This repository is an educational antimalware/AV simulator written in Nim that implements intentionally weak detection logic to teach how AV-style heuristics work and how they can be bypassed. It includes basic signature scanning (string matching) and simple heuristic checks (suspicious extensions, non-printable byte ratio, small-file checks, entropy-style indicators). A key focus is integrating the scanner as a Windows AMSI provider (IAntimalwareProvider) via a DLL wrapper, enabling experimentation with AMSI’s provider architecture. The project ships with PowerShell scripts and test corpora to generate “malicious,” “clean,” and “bypass” files (e.g., double extensions, uncommon extensions, no-extension payloads). It’s useful for red teamers, pentesters, and defenders who want a safe lab to understand detection gaps and evasion patterns without relying on real malware or commercial AV internals. It’s interesting because it demonstrates the practical weaknesses of naive static heuristics and how AMSI provider registration/behavior can be tested end-to-end.
Technical Focus: - Windows AMSI provider development (IAntimalwareProvider) and registration - Signature-based detection via ASCII/case-insensitive pattern matching - Heuristic detection: extension allow/deny logic, tiny-file checks - Content-based heuristics: non-printable byte ratio, entropy-like indicators - Evasion/bypass test case generation and validation in a lab workflow - Nim-based Windows tooling + PowerShell automation (build/test/register)
Use Cases: - Build a lab AMSI provider to understand AMSI scanning flow and integration points - Create training exercises on bypassing naive AV heuristics (extensions, casing, content shaping) - Generate deterministic test files for evaluating detection logic and logging/telemetry - Prototype and compare simple static detection strategies before implementing stronger approaches - Blue team education: demonstrate why simplistic signatures/heuristics produce false negatives/positives
Keywords: AMSI, Antimalware Scan Interface, IAntimalwareProvider, Windows, AMSI provider, DLL wrapper, Nim, PowerShell, signature scanning, heuristic detection, entropy, non-printable bytes, file extension heuristics, case-insensitive matching, evasion, bypass techniques, red team training, malware simulation, static analysis, provider registration

entry.title: maps scanner
entry.summary:
Title: MAPS Cloud Scanner (maps_scanner)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - A Python research tool that directly interacts with Microsoft Defender’s MAPS (Microsoft Active Protection Service) cloud backend to obtain file and URL reputation verdicts and study dynamic signature delivery behavior. - It solves the problem of treating MAPS as a black box by implementing the same on-the-wire protocol used by Defender clients: Microsoft Bond CompactBinaryV1. - The repo provides file submission scanning, SHA-256 reputation lookups, URL reputation checks, and a heartbeat/connectivity test to MAPS endpoints. - It also includes offline PE inspection (metadata/imports/sections/AuthentiCode), raw payload build/decode/replay for protocol analysis, and an API fuzzer to enumerate undocumented endpoints/fields and observe server behavior. - Useful primarily for security researchers, malware analysts, and red teamers studying Defender cloud detections and protocol behavior; also relevant to blue teams validating reputation workflows and telemetry assumptions. - Interesting because it operationalizes MAPS protocol research into a practical CLI with tooling for replay and fuzzing, enabling repeatable experimentation against live services.
Technical Focus: - Microsoft Defender MAPS cloud reputation and sample submission workflow - Microsoft Bond serialization (CompactBinaryV1) implementation and parsing - Cloud verdict querying (file hash, file upload, URL reputation) - Payload capture/decoding/replay for protocol reverse engineering - Endpoint/field fuzzing for undocumented MAPS behaviors - Offline PE and AuthentiCode signature analysis
Use Cases: - Validate how Defender MAPS classifies a file (clean/malware/PUA/unknown) via direct cloud submission - Query reputation for a SHA-256 without uploading the sample - Test URL reputation decisions made by Defender cloud services - Replay captured MAPS requests to reproduce verdicts and compare responses over time - Fuzz MAPS request types/fields to discover hidden capabilities or server-side validation rules - Perform quick local PE triage (imports/sections/signing) alongside cloud reputation checks
Keywords: Microsoft Defender, MAPS, Microsoft Active Protection Service, cloud reputation, sample submission, SHA-256, URL reputation, Bond, CompactBinaryV1, protocol reverse engineering, payload replay, API fuzzing, PE analysis, AuthentiCode, dynamic signatures, SDN, DSS, Python, requests, malware analysis

entry.title: process events.md
entry.summary:
Title: Sysmon Community Guide – Process Events
Type: Documentation (GitHub)
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This chapter explains how Sysmon records process-related telemetry: process creation, termination, and process access. It focuses on Sysmon’s deterministically generated ProcessGuid and LogonGuid fields, which uniquely identify a specific process instance and logon session and avoid PID/logon ID reuse issues. For Windows, it notes process events are captured at kernel level via ObjRegisterCallbacks and that ProcessGuid is maintained by Sysmon (not a native EPROCESS attribute). For Linux, it describes ProcessGuid/LogonGuid generation using host-unique data such as `/etc/machine-id`. It also clarifies which Sysmon event types do not reference ProcessGuid (e.g., WMI events and kernel driver load). The chapter highlights how LogonGuid helps map all processes/actions to a user session, including the dual logon IDs created with UAC for local admins and the importance of correlating with Security Event ID 4624 (linked logon ID). Useful for defenders building correlation rules and for red/purple teams understanding how activity is stitched together in Sysmon logs.
Technical Focus: - Sysmon process telemetry (create/terminate/access) - ProcessGuid generation and correlation vs PID reuse - LogonGuid generation and session correlation - Windows kernel callbacks (ObjRegisterCallbacks) used by Sysmon - Linux host identity influence (`/etc/machine-id`) - Cross-event field relationships (ProcessGuid-derived names, image relations)
Use Cases: - Correlate multi-step attack chains across Sysmon events using ProcessGuid - Attribute process activity to specific user sessions via LogonGuid - Improve SIEM detections by avoiding PID/logon ID reuse pitfalls - Investigate UAC split-token behavior by linking logon sessions (4624 Linked Logon ID) - Validate which Sysmon event categories can/can’t be tied to a process GUID
Keywords: Sysmon, ProcessGuid, LogonGuid, process creation, process termination, process access, ObjRegisterCallbacks, kernel driver, EPROCESS, Windows Security Event ID 4624, Linked Logon ID, UAC, split token, /etc/machine-id, Linux Sysmon, event correlation, process tree, LSA logon session, telemetry mapping

entry.title: driver loading.md
entry.summary:
Title: Sysmon Community Guide – Driver Loading (Event ID 6)
Type: Documentation (GitHub)
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This chapter explains how to use Sysmon Event ID 6 (DriverLoad) to monitor kernel driver loading on Windows as a high-signal detection source. It motivates driver-load monitoring by outlining how malicious or vulnerable drivers enable kernel-level persistence, rootkits, credential theft, and security control bypass (including BYOVD). The guide recommends a “log all, then baseline and exclude known-good” strategy due to the typically low daily volume of driver load events. It emphasizes validating both `Signature` and `SignatureStatus`, and enabling Sysmon certificate revocation checking (`<CheckRevocation/>`) to catch abused or revoked certificates. It also shows how to integrate LOLDrivers’ hash-based Sysmon rules to detect known-vulnerable signed drivers that signature validation alone won’t flag. The content is primarily useful for Blue Teams/DFIR and detection engineers, with red teams benefiting from understanding common detection pivots around driver abuse.
Technical Focus: - Sysmon Event ID 6 (DriverLoad) telemetry and fields - Driver signing validation (`Signature`, `SignatureStatus`) and revocation checks - Baselining and allowlisting/exclusion rule design for low-volume events - BYOVD detection using LOLDrivers (hash/signature metadata) - Investigation heuristics for suspicious driver loads (path, vendor, signature state) - MITRE ATT&CK mapping for driver/rootkit activity
Use Cases: - Detect unsigned or invalidly signed kernel drivers (potential rootkits/tool drivers) - Alert on known-vulnerable driver loads associated with BYOVD tradecraft - Build environment-specific allowlists for common vendor drivers after baselining - Triage suspicious driver loads from unusual directories (temp/user/removable media) - Hunt for tool-specific drivers (e.g., mimidrv.sys) by name/hash/signature
Keywords: Sysmon, Event ID 6, DriverLoad, Windows kernel drivers, code signing, SignatureStatus, certificate revocation, CheckRevocation, BYOVD, LOLDrivers, loldrivers.io, mimidrv.sys, rootkit, LSASS, kernel-mode, allowlisting, baselining, MITRE ATT&CK T1014, T1068, T1543.003, T1562, T1611

entry.title: image loading.md
entry.summary:
Title: Sysmon Community Guide – Image Loading (Event ID 7)
Type: Documentation (GitHub Repository Chapter)
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This chapter explains how Sysmon Event ID 7 (“Image loaded”) can be used to detect suspicious DLL/OCX/module loads, while emphasizing that it is one of Sysmon’s highest-volume event types and can severely impact performance if misconfigured. It argues against exclusion-based logging (log everything then filter) and recommends a strict “targeted include only” strategy focused on high-signal DLLs and specific process+DLL combinations. The write-up maps image-load detections to common attacker techniques such as DLL injection/process injection, script engine abuse (PowerShell/VBScript/JScript), COM/ActiveX abuse (e.g., regsvr32 + scrobj.dll “Squiblydoo”), and credential access tooling indicators. It provides concrete Sysmon XML filtering examples for script engines, unsigned DLLs in sensitive processes, suspicious load paths, and credential-access-related DLLs. It also covers investigation pivots, false-positive sources (enterprise tooling, dev environments), and validation/performance guidance. Useful primarily for Blue Teams/Detection Engineers, but also valuable for red teams to understand likely telemetry and detection choke points.
Technical Focus: - Sysmon Event ID 7 (ImageLoad) semantics and fields - High-volume telemetry management via include-only filtering - Script engine DLL load detection (PowerShell/VBScript/JScript/WSH) - COM/ActiveX and LOLBin indicators (regsvr32/mshta) - DLL injection/hijacking indicators (path, signature, metadata) - Credential access and dumping-related module loads (samlib/vaultcli/wdigest/dbghelp)
Use Cases: - Detect PowerShell engine usage when loaded by non-powershell processes (Office/LOLBins) - Alert on regsvr32 loading scrobj.dll (Squiblydoo) and mshta loading jscript9.dll - Identify unsigned DLLs loaded into sensitive processes (e.g., lsass.exe, explorer.exe) - Flag DLL loads from user-writable or temporary directories (Users/ProgramData/Windows\Temp) - Hunt for credential theft/dumping activity via suspicious library loads (wdigest, dbghelp, vaultcli) - Baseline and tune Sysmon ImageLoad rules to keep event volume and CPU overhead manageable
Keywords: Sysmon, Event ID 7, ImageLoad, DLL loading, OCX, module load, Sysmon XML, include-only filtering, signature validation, Signed false, ImageLoaded, Process injection, DLL injection, DLL hijacking, reflective DLL loading, LOLBins, regsvr32, scrobj.dll, mshta.exe, jscript9.dll, vbscript.dll, System.Management.Automation.dll, wshom.ocx, scrrun.dll, samlib.dll, vaultcli.dll, wdigest.dll, dbghelp.dll, LSASS, MITRE ATT&CK T1055, T1218.010, T1218.005

entry.title: create remote thread.md
entry.summary:
Title: Sysmon Community Guide – Create Remote Thread (Event ID 8)
Type: Documentation (GitHub Repository Chapter)
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This chapter explains Sysmon Event ID 8, which logs usage of the Win32 API `CreateRemoteThread`, a common primitive for Windows process injection. - It highlights why the event is “low-volume, high-value” for detecting malware and attacker tradecraft such as code injection, defense evasion via trusted processes, and potential privilege escalation. - The write-up outlines the typical injection chain (`OpenProcess` → `VirtualAllocEx` → `WriteProcessMemory` → `CreateRemoteThread`) and documents the key Sysmon fields (source/target process, thread start address/module/function). - It recommends a “log all, exclude known-good” strategy due to the generally low legitimate usage in enterprise environments, and provides an example exclusion-focused Sysmon configuration snippet. - It also notes a key limitation: Sysmon Event ID 8 only covers `CreateRemoteThread`, so attackers can evade it using alternatives like `NtCreateThreadEx`, `RtlCreateUserThread`, or APC-based injection, requiring correlation with other Sysmon events. - Useful primarily for Blue Teams/DFIR and detection engineers, but also valuable for Red Teams to understand detection surfaces and gaps.
Technical Focus: - Sysmon Event ID 8 telemetry and fields (StartAddress/StartModule/StartFunction) - Windows process injection via `CreateRemoteThread` - Memory allocation and remote write primitives (`VirtualAllocEx`, `WriteProcessMemory`) - Detection engineering strategy (baseline + exclusions) - Evasion via alternative thread creation/injection APIs - Correlation with complementary Sysmon events (e.g., Process Access, Image Load)
Use Cases: - Build/validate Sysmon rules to detect remote-thread-based process injection - Triage suspicious injections into high-value processes (e.g., `explorer.exe`, `lsass.exe`) - Hunt for anomalous thread start addresses/modules indicating shellcode or unbacked memory - Reduce false positives by baselining and excluding verified legitimate sources/targets - Identify detection gaps where attackers may switch to `NtCreateThreadEx`/APC injection
Keywords: Sysmon, Event ID 8, CreateRemoteThread, process injection, OpenProcess, VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, thread start address, StartModule, StartFunction, PEB module list, NtCreateThreadEx, RtlCreateUserThread, QueueUserAPC, defense evasion, MITRE ATT&CK T1055, DLL injection, PE injection, Windows internals, detection engineering

entry.title: Mangle
entry.summary:
Title: Mangle — PE executable manipulation for evasion (strings, size inflation, cert cloning)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Mangle is a Go-based tool for post-processing compiled Windows executables (EXE/DLL) to reduce common detection artifacts. It focuses on three main capabilities: removing/overwriting known IoC-related strings (currently Go-binary specific), inflating file size by appending null-byte padding to exceed EDR scanning thresholds, and cloning the Authenticode certificate chain/attributes from a legitimate signed binary onto another file. The goal is to help loaders and payloads evade on-disk and in-memory scanning that relies on static strings, file characteristics, and signature metadata. It’s primarily useful for red teams, pentesters, and malware developers doing tradecraft research, and secondarily for blue teams to understand and test detection gaps around large-file scanning and signature trust. It was featured at Source Zero Con 2022 and uses PE parsing via Binject’s `debug/pe`.
Technical Focus: - Windows PE (Portable Executable) parsing and modification - Go binary indicator/string stripping via in-place hex overwrites - File size inflation via null-byte padding to bypass scanning limits - Authenticode certificate/signature attribute cloning (certificate chain, timestamps) - EDR evasion tradecraft (static/on-disk and in-memory scanning constraints)
Use Cases: - Remove known suspicious strings from Go-compiled payloads without changing string lengths - Inflate droppers/loaders to test or exploit EDR large-file scanning blind spots - Clone signing metadata from legitimate binaries to study trust/signature-based heuristics - Build red-team test cases to validate defensive controls against PE tampering - Generate samples for detection engineering around PE anomalies (padding, signature inconsistencies)
Keywords: Mangle, Golang, Windows PE, EXE, DLL, Authenticode, code-signing certificate, certificate chain, timestamp countersignature, PE modification, string patching, IoC strings, Go indicators, null-byte padding, file inflation, EDR evasion, static detection, in-memory scanning, Binject debug/pe, loader tradecraft

entry.title: OffensiveDLR
entry.summary:
Title: OffensiveDLR
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): OffensiveDLR is a toolbox of research notes and PoC code focused on weaponizing the .NET Dynamic Language Runtime (DLR) by embedding scripting engines/compilers (IronPython, Boo, Lua, JScript/VBScript via ClearScript, SSharp) inside PowerShell and C# payloads. It targets the problem of executing complex post-exploitation logic without relying on `csc.exe`, dropping files, or exposing PowerShell script content to common telemetry like ScriptBlock Logging. The repo demonstrates in-memory compilation/execution, runtime assembly resolution tricks, and multiple shellcode execution/injection techniques implemented in “DLR languages.” It also includes Kukulkan, a slimmed-down SILENTTRINITY variant with encrypted comms and optional Cobalt Strike integration. This is primarily useful for red teams, pentesters, and offensive tool developers exploring alternative execution paths and telemetry/inspection blind spots created by DLR embedding. It’s interesting because it shows how “non-PowerShell” logic can still leverage full .NET APIs while reducing typical PowerShell-centric detections and operational friction.
Technical Focus: - .NET DLR embedding (IronPython, Boo.Lang, NeoLua, ClearScript, SSharp) - In-memory compilation/execution without `csc.exe` and minimal disk artifacts - PowerShell host wrappers for embedded engines/compilers - Runtime dependency loading via `AppDomain.CurrentDomain.AssemblyResolve` - Shellcode execution/injection patterns (QueueUserAPC, CreateThread, CreateRemoteThread, WriteProcessMemory) - Memory dumping via `MiniDumpWriteDump`
Use Cases: - Execute post-exploitation logic in IronPython/Boo/Lua/JScript from PowerShell without visible ScriptBlock content - Build C# loaders that embed scripting engines and resolve assemblies at runtime - Run shellcode in-memory using alternative language runtimes/compilers - Prototype payload “retooling” workflows where operators swap embedded scripts without recompiling full tooling - Experiment with DLR-based tradecraft and detection gaps for defensive validation
Keywords: .NET, DLR, CLR, PowerShell, C#, IronPython, Boo.Lang, Boolang, ClearScript, JScript, VBScript, NeoLua, SSharp, in-memory execution, embedded compiler, AssemblyResolve, reflection, AMSI bypass, ScriptBlock Logging evasion, shellcode injection, QueueUserAPC, CreateRemoteThread, WriteProcessMemory, MiniDumpWriteDump, SILENTTRINITY, Cobalt Strike

It focuses on using `BooCompiler` with the `CompileToMemory` pipeline to generate an in-memory assembly rather than writing output to disk. The examples demonstrate invoking compiled script code via Boo’s duck typing (from Boo) and via .NET reflection (from C#), including how module/type names are derived (e.g., `ScriptModule`). Use Cases: - Embed a scripting language (Boo) into a .NET application - Build plugin systems that compile user-provided code at runtime - Execute automation scripts without shipping a separate compiler executable (`booc.exe`) - Prototype dynamic features using in-memory compilation and reflection - Implement script-driven configuration/logic in internal tools

entry.title: Invoke Native Methods with DllImport
