entry.title: c sharp memory injection
entry.summary:
Title: C# Memory Injection Examples
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This repository provides C# code examples demonstrating multiple Windows process memory injection techniques. It aims to show relatively minimal/opsec-conscious implementations by avoiding unnecessary memory changes and extra process/file creation where possible. Included techniques cover APC-based shellcode execution (against existing and newly created processes), classic remote DLL injection via `CreateRemoteThread` + `LoadLibrary`, thread hijacking by suspending and redirecting an existing thread, and IAT (Import Address Table) pointer modification to redirect a specific imported function to shellcode. The examples use simple calc-spawning shellcode (linked externally) for demonstration. It’s primarily useful for red teamers, pentesters, and security researchers studying Windows injection tradecraft and detection opportunities.
Technical Focus: - APC injection via `QueueUserAPC` and alertable thread states - Remote process memory allocation/writing and execution primitives - Remote DLL injection (`CreateRemoteThread`, `LoadLibrary`) - Thread hijacking (suspend/resume, context/flow redirection) - IAT patching / import pointer redirection in a remote process
Use Cases: - Build/modify C# PoCs for common Windows injection patterns - Compare injection tradeoffs (reliability, noise, prerequisites like alertable threads) - Develop detections around API sequences and memory permissions/remote thread creation - Training material for Windows internals and offensive tradecraft labs
Keywords: C#, Windows, process injection, memory injection, shellcode, QueueUserAPC, APC injection, alertable state, CreateRemoteThread, LoadLibrary, DLL injection, thread hijacking, SuspendThread, IAT injection, Import Address Table, VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, remote process, Win32 API, opsec tradeoffs

entry.title: Dynamic Invoke
entry.summary:
Title: Emulating Covert Operations – Dynamic Invocation (Avoiding PInvoke & API Hooks)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post introduces D/Invoke (DInvoke), an API added to SharpSploit that dynamically resolves and invokes unmanaged Windows APIs from C# without static P/Invoke imports. It targets two common detection/visibility points: suspicious Import Address Table entries in .NET assemblies and user-mode API hooking on Win32/NTDLL functions. The article explains using .NET delegates created from function pointers (via `Marshal.GetDelegateForFunctionPointer`) to call exports resolved at runtime by parsing module export tables, walking the PEB loader lists, or loading modules on demand. It also covers advanced execution primitives: manual mapping of PE modules (including “module overloading” using file-backed decoys) and generating syscall stubs from a fresh `ntdll.dll` to bypass userland hooks. Defensive considerations are included (module-load telemetry, memory scanners like pe-sieve, ETW/.NET runtime provider, and userland instrumentation via Frida). It’s primarily useful for red team/offensive .NET tool developers, while also providing concrete detection angles for blue teams.
Technical Focus: - Dynamic API resolution/invocation in .NET using delegates and function pointers - PEB module enumeration and export table (EAT) parsing - Manual mapping of PE modules and module overloading (file-backed decoy mapping) - Syscall stub extraction from fresh `ntdll.dll` (userland hook bypass) - Detection via modload telemetry, memory scanning (pe-sieve), and ETW (.NET runtime provider)
Use Cases: - Build .NET post-exploitation tooling without suspicious P/Invoke imports - Evade user-mode API hooks by resolving exports dynamically or using fresh manually mapped modules - Execute DLLs/EXEs reflectively from memory (including calling exports/DllMain) - Invoke NT syscalls via dynamically generated stubs to bypass userland monitoring - Develop detections based on anomalous module loads, ETW CLR events, and in-memory PE artifacts
Keywords: DInvoke, SharpSploit, Dynamic Invocation, PInvoke, Import Address Table, IAT, API hooking, userland hooks, delegates, Marshal.GetDelegateForFunctionPointer, ntdll.dll, LdrLoadDll, PEB, export address table, EAT parsing, manual mapping, reflective loading, module overloading, syscall stubs, NtOpenProcess, NtCreateThreadEx, ETW, Sysmon, SilkETW, pe-sieve, Frida

entry.title: Introducing Donut
entry.summary:
Title: Introducing Donut – Injecting .NET Assemblies as Shellcode
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post introduces Donut, a shellcode generator that converts arbitrary .NET assemblies (EXE/DLL) into position-independent x86/x64 shellcode for injection into local or remote Windows processes. It addresses limitations of common in-memory .NET execution approaches (e.g., `Assembly.Load` being in-process only, and Cobalt Strike `execute-assembly` being predictable and constrained) by enabling flexible process injection and “migrate”-style tradecraft. Donut bootstraps the CLR inside the target using the Unmanaged CLR Hosting API, creates a new AppDomain, loads the assembly from memory or stages it from a URL, and invokes a specified method with parameters. Payload modules can be embedded (stageless) or downloaded (staged), are encrypted (Chaskey + random key), and the decrypted buffer is freed to reduce memory-scanning exposure. The article also discusses operational considerations (prefer injecting into already-managed processes) and provides a basic detection idea: alert when unmanaged processes load CLR-related DLLs (e.g., `mscoree.dll`, `mscorlib.dll`). Useful for red teams/pentesters building in-memory .NET execution and for defenders modeling/detecting CLR injection behavior.
Technical Focus: - Unmanaged CLR Hosting API (CLR bootstrap/hosting from native code) - .NET assembly in-memory loading and AppDomain isolation/unloading strategy - Position-independent shellcode generation (x86/x64) for process injection - Staged vs stageless payload delivery; encrypted module packaging (Chaskey) - Detection concept: module-load telemetry (WMI/Sysmon/ETW) for CLR injection
Use Cases: - Generate shellcode to execute .NET post-exploitation tooling inside arbitrary processes - Add “migrate”/process-hopping capability to C2 agents using .NET modules - Execute large or complex .NET assemblies in-memory without dropping to disk - Red-team emulation of CLR injection tradecraft and defender validation of detections - Build/modify custom CLR-loader shellcode by editing and recompiling `payload.c`
Keywords: Donut, CLR injection, .NET assembly, shellcode, position-independent code, Unmanaged CLR Hosting API, AppDomain, Assembly.Load, execute-assembly, process injection, remote process injection, in-memory execution, staging, stageless payload, Chaskey, mscoree.dll, mscorlib.dll, VirtualFree, WMI Win32_ModuleLoadTrace, Sysmon ImageLoad, ETW

entry.title: Cruller
entry.summary:
Title: Donut v1.0 “Cruller” – ETW Bypasses, Module Overloading, and Much More
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post announces and explains Donut v1.0 (“Cruller”), a shellcode generator that turns native PEs, .NET assemblies, and scripts into positionable shellcode with an embedded loader. The release adds a modular ETW bypass system (defaulting to patching `ntdll!EtwEventWrite` to return) alongside existing AMSI/WLDP bypass modules. A major new feature is “Module Overloading,” which maps a legitimate file-backed image via `NtCreateSection`/`NtMapViewOfSection` and overwrites it with the payload so execution appears to originate from disk-backed memory, reducing detections that flag private/RWX or non-file-backed code. It also introduces configurable native PE header handling (mutate vs preserve) to maintain compatibility with payloads needing resources, and can spoof decoy headers to evade tools like pe-sieve that compare in-memory vs on-disk headers. Additional improvements include better handling of missing relocation info, offset-based execution for chaining with hollowing/infection workflows, Docker-based builds, and loader/output enhancements (C#/Python/UUID formats). Useful primarily for red teams and offensive tool developers, with defensive value for understanding modern injection evasion patterns and what to detect.
Technical Focus: - ETW bypass via patching `EtwEventWrite` and modular bypass framework - Module Overloading (file-backed manual mapping using section objects) - PE header mutation/preservation and decoy header spoofing vs memory scanners (e.g., pe-sieve) - Native PE relocation handling (including absent relocation data) - Loader behavior changes (custom `LoadLibrary`/`GetProcAddress`, exit/block options) - Alternate shellcode encodings/output generators (C#, Python, UUID strings)
Use Cases: - Generate shellcode loaders for native PE, .NET, and script payloads - Reduce telemetry/detections by disabling ETW event emission in-process - Execute payloads from file-backed memory to evade “private memory execution” heuristics - Improve stealth against PE replacement scanners by mutating headers to match decoy modules - Support process hollowing/file infector workflows via base+offset execution handoff - Cross-platform shellcode generation using the provided Docker build environment
Keywords: Donut, shellcode generator, ETW, Event Tracing for Windows, EtwEventWrite, ntdll.dll, AMSI bypass, WLDP bypass, Module Overloading, NtCreateSection, NtMapViewOfSection, section objects, manual mapping, PE headers, header mutation, PE resources, relocations, process injection evasion, pe-sieve, Cobalt Strike, UUID shellcode encoding, LoadLibrary, GetProcAddress, Dockerfile

entry.title: Mixed Assemblies
entry.summary:
Title: Mixed Assemblies – Crafting Flexible C++ Reflective Stagers for .NET Assemblies
Type: Blog Post / GitHub Tool (example code library)
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Describes using C++/CLI “mixed-mode” assemblies to bridge native C++ and managed .NET, enabling loaders/stagers that can execute .NET payloads from an unmanaged entry point (EXE main or DLLMain). - Focuses on solving practical tradecraft issues with .NET payloads (easy decompilation, lack of DLLMain equivalent, and common in-memory loading patterns) by wrapping managed execution inside a mostly-native PE. - Explains why mixed assemblies can’t be loaded via `Assembly.Load(byte[])` (memory-only reflection load) and instead are typically loaded from disk (`Assembly.LoadFrom/LoadFile`) or via a custom PE loader. - Provides example implementations that avoid loader lock by spawning a new thread from `DllMain`, then transitioning into managed code to `Assembly.Load()` an embedded/downloaded .NET assembly and invoke a method via reflection. - Discusses detection angles (CLR module load monitoring via WMI/ETW) and notes AMSI implications in .NET 4.8, including the advantage of performing AMSI bypass/unhooking in native code before loading managed payloads. - Useful for red teamers/pentesters building Windows stagers, and for blue teamers looking to detect CLR injection and mixed-mode loader behavior.
Technical Focus: - C++/CLI mixed-mode assemblies (/clr) and native↔managed interop - Loader lock constraints in `DllMain` and thread-based initialization - In-memory .NET assembly loading via `System.Reflection.Assembly::Load` - PE resources as payload containers (embedded DLL/EXE, optional stego/encryption) - CLR load detection (WMI `Win32_ModuleLoadTrace`, ETW/module monitoring) - AMSI behavior in .NET 4.8 and native pre-unhook strategies
Use Cases: - Build on-disk DLL stagers that execute managed payloads when side-loaded (`DllMain` execution) - Wrap/obfuscate .NET tooling inside a less-reversible native-looking PE - Create hybrid implants leveraging Win32/NTAPI/COM plus .NET APIs in one artifact - Stage managed payloads from embedded resources or network download, then invoke entry methods via reflection - Defensive detection engineering for “non-.NET process loads CLR” heuristics
Keywords: C++/CLI, mixed-mode assembly, /clr, IJW, CLR hosting, mscoree.dll, clr.dll, System.Reflection.Assembly.Load, Assembly.LoadFrom, DLLMain, loader lock, CreateThread, PE resources, FindResource, LoadResource, reflective loading, WMI Win32_ModuleLoadTrace, ETW, CLR injection detection, AMSI, .NET 4.8, unmanaged AMSI bypass, SILENTTRINITY

entry.title: radar supplement july.pdf
entry.summary:
Title: Using CLR Hosting to Evade AMSI
Type: Technical Paper
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This paper analyzes how AMSI scans .NET assemblies and presents an evasion technique that avoids common in-memory patching approaches. - It reverse engineers .NET Framework assembly-loading paths in `clr.dll` and identifies that some loaders (notably `StreamImageLayout`) do not trigger AMSI scanning, unlike `RawImageLayout` used by typical `AppDomain.Load(byte[])`/buffer-based loads. - The author demonstrates using Microsoft-documented CLR Hosting APIs from a native (C/C++) host to influence assembly resolution/loading so the CLR consumes an `IStream` (memory-backed) and bypasses AMSI inspection. - The technique relies on implementing COM hosting interfaces (`IHostControl`, `IHostAssemblyManager`, `IHostAssemblyStore`) to supply the assembly image via `IStream` (e.g., `SHCreateMemStream`) when the CLR fails to resolve it normally (e.g., not found in GAC). - A practical example shows executing a known offensive .NET tool (Rubeus) without obfuscation and without AMSI blocking, contrasting with AMSI-blocked behavior when loading via direct memory buffer methods. - Useful for red teams and malware/tool developers; also valuable to defenders for understanding blind spots in AMSI coverage and where to instrument detections beyond classic AMSI patch signatures.
Technical Focus: - AMSI internals and call flow (`AmsiInitialize`, `AmsiOpenSession`, `AmsiScanBuffer`) - .NET Framework assembly loading internals (`clr.dll`, `AssemblyNative`, `PEImageLayout` subclasses) - CLR Hosting APIs and COM interfaces (`ICLRMetaHost`, `ICLRRuntimeInfo`, `ICLRRuntimeHost`, `ICorRuntimeHost`) - Custom assembly resolution via hosting (`IHostControl`, `IHostAssemblyManager`, `IHostAssemblyStore`) - `IStream`-based loading (`StreamImageLayout`, `SHCreateMemStream`) vs buffer-based loading (`RawImageLayout`) - Strong-name assembly binding and GAC resolution behavior
Use Cases: - Build a native CLR host to execute managed payloads while avoiding AMSI’s .NET assembly scan path - Execute/reflectively invoke .NET post-exploitation tooling (e.g., Rubeus-like assemblies) without classic AMSI patching - Research/detect alternative .NET loading paths that bypass AMSI and design compensating telemetry (ETW/CLR load events) - Develop PoCs to validate AMSI coverage gaps across .NET Framework versions and loader variants
Keywords: AMSI, AmsiScanBuffer, amsi.dll, MpOav.dll, .NET Framework, CLR, clr.dll, mscoree.dll, CLR Hosting, ICLRMetaHost, ICLRRuntimeInfo, ICLRRuntimeHost, ICorRuntimeHost, IHostControl, IHostAssemblyManager, IHostAssemblyStore, AppDomain, AssemblyNative, PEImageLayout, StreamImageLayout, RawImageLayout, IStream, SHCreateMemStream, strong name, GAC, reflection, dnSpy, IDA, ETW

entry.title: Obolos
entry.summary:
Title: Obolos — Windows Direct Syscall Evasion Engine (Dynamic SSN + Synthetic Stack + Polymorphic Stubs)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Obolos is a standalone Windows evasion “engine” intended to be embedded into other red team tooling to execute direct syscalls while reducing common EDR visibility points. It focuses on bypassing user-mode API hooks by dynamically resolving System Service Numbers (SSNs) at runtime via PEB/export parsing and neighboring-byte inspection when hooks (e.g., JMP trampolines) are detected. It also implements synthetic call stack construction by parsing the Exception Directory (.pdata) of legitimate system functions to build believable stack frames for stack-walk based detections. To resist static signatures, it generates many randomized syscall assembly stubs per build (junk instructions/NOP padding) via a Python generator. It’s primarily useful for red team operators, implant/tool developers, and security researchers experimenting with syscall-based evasion and stack-walk deception.
Technical Focus: - Direct syscalls on Windows (ntdll/SSN-based invocation) - Dynamic SSN resolution via PEB + export directory parsing - User-mode hook detection/bypass (JMP trampoline handling) - Synthetic call stack / stack spoofing using .pdata unwind metadata - Polymorphic syscall stub generation (build-time randomization) - x64 assembly + C integration (ml64/cl toolchain)
Use Cases: - Integrate into custom loaders/implants to reduce user-mode EDR hook visibility - Research and prototype stack-walk evasion via unwind-aware synthetic frames - Generate per-build polymorphic syscall stubs to hinder signature-based detections - Build a reusable syscall abstraction layer for offensive Windows tooling - Test defensive detections around syscall patterns and anomalous call stacks
Keywords: Windows, EDR evasion, direct syscall, ntdll, SSN, System Service Number, PEB parsing, export directory, user-mode hooks, JMP trampoline, syscall stubs, polymorphic code, junk instructions, stack spoofing, synthetic call stack, stack walking, .pdata, unwind info, ml64, cl.exe

entry.title: armory rs
entry.summary:
Title: Armory (armory-rs) — Rust Beacon Object Files (BOFs) Port of TrustedSec BOFs
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Armory-rs is a collection of Windows Beacon Object Files (BOFs) rewritten in Rust for adversary simulation, threat emulation, and security research. It ports the full set of 115 TrustedSec BOFs (situational awareness, remote operations, and injection) from C to Rust using the rustbof framework. The project provides a broad set of post-exploitation primitives (host/network enumeration, registry/service/task operations, credential/token artifact access, and multiple process injection techniques) mapped to MITRE ATT&CK. It targets COFF/BOF execution workflows and is intended to be run via compatible loaders (e.g., TrustedSec COFFLoader). Useful for red teams/pentesters building BOF-based tradecraft, and for blue teams/detection engineers validating telemetry and writing detections against common BOF behaviors. Notable for demonstrating a large-scale Rust port of a well-known BOF arsenal and for covering both SA and injection patterns in a consistent framework.
Technical Focus: - Rust-based BOF development via rustbof (COFF object payloads) - Windows situational awareness and host/network enumeration - Remote operations via SCM, registry, scheduled tasks, and user management - Credential/token artifact access (DPAPI, browser/app tokens) - Process injection techniques (CreateRemoteThread, NtCreateThreadEx, APC, context hijack, KCT hijack) - MITRE ATT&CK technique mapping for emulation/detection alignment
Use Cases: - Build and run BOF modules in Cobalt Strike/COFF-compatible execution chains - Threat emulation/purple team exercises aligned to ATT&CK techniques - Detection engineering: generate endpoint telemetry for services/registry/tasks/injection behaviors - Rapid situational awareness on compromised Windows hosts (processes, sessions, shares, routes, DNS cache) - Research and prototyping of Rust implementations of common post-exploitation actions

entry.title: Browser Dumping The Core TacticBehind Most Infostealers.html
entry.summary:
Title: Browser Dumping: The Core Tactic Behind Most Infostealers (Chrome App-Bound Encryption / ABE on Windows)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post explains how infostealers “dump” Chromium-based browser secrets on Windows by decrypting locally stored cookies/passwords, and how Chrome’s historical DPAPI-based protection enabled same-user malware to trivially decrypt data. - It then breaks down Google Chrome’s newer App-Bound Encryption (ABE, announced around Chrome 127) which gates decryption behind an Elevation Service COM interface that validates the caller’s “app identity” (initially via path validation). - The article details the ABE key hierarchy: an `app_bound_key` (32-byte AES-256) stored in `Local State` as `os_crypt.app_bound_encrypted_key` with an `APPB` prefix, wrapped with layered DPAPI (SYSTEM then user) plus embedded validation data. - It documents the blob formats for legacy `v10` (DPAPI) and newer `v20` (AES-256-GCM) encrypted values, including cookie plaintext quirks (cookie value offset after 32 bytes). - Alternative decryption vectors are discussed, including admin-level DPAPI unwrapping and the acknowledged bypass of code injection into Chrome to call the IPC/COM interface legitimately. - It also briefly covers Device Bound Session Credentials (DBSC) as a complementary defense that aims to make stolen cookies unusable off-device. - Useful for red teamers, malware analysts, and defenders reverse-engineering infostealers or building detections around browser secret access and ABE/IElevator usage.
Technical Focus: - Windows DPAPI (CryptProtectData / CryptUnprotectData) and user vs SYSTEM context - Chrome App-Bound Encryption (ABE) and Elevation Service COM (IElevator / IElevator2Chrome) - Caller validation via executable path normalization (ProtectionLevel path validation) - Chromium secret storage artifacts (Local State JSON, SQLite Login Data/Cookies DB) - AES-256-GCM encrypted blob formats (v10/v11 vs v20, IV/Tag layout) - Code injection as an ABE bypass vector; admin/offline decryption considerations
Use Cases: - Reverse engineer infostealer browser credential/cookie theft workflows - Build PoCs or internal tooling to recover Chrome/Edge secrets for IR or testing - Create detections for suspicious COM usage of Chrome Elevation Service / DPAPI patterns - Validate security impact of running malware in same-user context vs elevated/admin context - Assess how DBSC changes the value of stolen cookies for session hijacking
Keywords: Chrome, Chromium, Microsoft Edge, browser dumping, infostealer, DPAPI, CryptUnprotectData, CryptProtectData, Local State, os_crypt.app_bound_encrypted_key, APPB, App-Bound Encryption, ABE, IElevator, COM service, Elevation Service, path validation, OSCrypt, AES-256-GCM, v10, v20, SQLite Login Data, Cookies database, code injection, SYSTEM context, user context, Device Bound Session Credentials, DBSC, session cookie theft

entry.title: turnt
entry.summary:
Title: TURNt (TURN tunneler)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): TURNt is a red team tunneling toolkit that smuggles interactive C2 traffic (e.g., SOCKS5, hidden VNC/RDP access) through legitimate TURN infrastructure used by web conferencing providers like Microsoft Teams (and historically Zoom). It addresses the problem of slow, noisy, or easily fingerprinted covert channels by blending into high-volume, commonly allowlisted conferencing traffic and leveraging TURN over TLS on port 443 (TURNS). The toolset uses WebRTC data channels (SCTP) over TURN/TCP/TLS to multiplex multiple proxied connections while avoiding classic TCP-over-TCP tunneling pitfalls, though it still inherits head-of-line blocking from the single underlying TCP stream. TURNt uses a manual “pidgin mode” offer/answer exchange (base64 blobs) intended to be passed via an existing implant/C2 rather than a dedicated signaling server. It includes a controller that exposes a local SOCKS5 proxy and an admin console to manage local/remote TCP port-forwards over an active tunnel. It’s primarily useful for red teams and operators needing short-lived, interactive access paths that are harder to isolate at the network layer; defenders can also use the included IOC notes to validate detections around conferencing TURN traffic.
Technical Focus: - TURN/TURNS (TURN over TLS) tunneling via third-party conferencing providers - WebRTC data channels and SCTP multiplexing over TCP/TLS - SOCKS5 proxying with remote DNS resolution - Manual signaling (offer/answer) and out-of-band coordination via existing C2 - Local/remote TCP port-forwarding over a tunneled transport - Operational constraints: head-of-line blocking, connection fragility, platform limitations
Use Cases: - Establish a SOCKS5 pivot channel over Microsoft Teams TURN servers for internal web/SSH access - Local port-forward internal services (RDP/VNC/HTTP) to the operator machine - Remote port-forward to expose operator-hosted services into a target network (e.g., phishing pages, listeners) - Blend interactive operator traffic into conferencing-like egress on 443/TLS to reduce DPI/anomaly visibility - Lab validation of network detections/allowlisting risks around conferencing TURN endpoints
Keywords: TURN, TURNS, WebRTC, SCTP, DTLS, SOCKS5, port forwarding, pivoting, NAT traversal, relay servers, Microsoft Teams, Zoom, QUIC admin interface, DPI evasion, egress filtering, TLS over TCP, offer/answer signaling, base64 handshake, C2 tunneling, red team tooling

entry.title: lateral movement with elastic edr and asr enabled 9c3e5dcf38b0
entry.summary:
Title: Bypassing Elastic EDR to Perform Lateral Movement
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post walks through a lab lateral-movement chain in a Windows environment monitored by Elastic EDR, with ASR enabled on the target workstation. - The author focuses on evading specific Elastic detection rules triggered by SMB tool transfer, remote execution from file shares, WMI-based lateral movement, and service-based execution spawning system shells. - Techniques include altering PE “magic bytes”/loader characteristics via a Cobalt Strike malleable profile, using non-standard file extensions (e.g., staging as `.png`, executing as `.scr`), and switching from WMI execution to service-based remote command execution using SCShell (BOF). - The chain also leverages an Elastic rule exclusion by placing the payload in an excluded directory path (e.g., under `C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Search`) to reduce detection. - It’s primarily useful for red teamers/pentesters testing Elastic’s behavioral detections and for blue teamers validating rule coverage and risky exclusions. - The interesting part is the practical, rule-driven evasion workflow: reading Elastic rule logic and iterating execution methods until detections stop firing.
Technical Focus: - Elastic Security detection rules and query-based evasion - SMB-based payload staging and file-share execution detections - Cobalt Strike malleable C2 profile / reflective loader modifications - Windows services for remote execution (SCShell / BOF usage) - Extension and header-byte (MZ) based detection bypass - ASR/EDR operational constraints during lateral movement
Use Cases: - Validate and tune Elastic EDR detections for lateral movement - Red team tradecraft for moving laterally under Elastic agent monitoring - Identify dangerous detection exclusions (path-based allowlists) in enterprise rules - Build test cases for SOC detections around SMB transfer, WMI, and service execution
Keywords: Elastic EDR, Elastic Security, lateral movement, SMB share, file transfer detection, MZ header bytes, PE magic bytes, Cobalt Strike, malleable C2 profile, reflective loader, BOF, SCShell, Windows services, sc.exe, WMI, SharpWMI, ASR, cmd.exe detection, msiexec.exe, System.Management.Automation.dll

entry.title: understanding lsass the security brain of windows
entry.summary:
Title: Understanding LSASS — The Security Brain of Windows
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This article explains LSASS (Local Security Authority Subsystem Service) from an internal/architectural perspective rather than as a “credential dumping target.” - It breaks down LSASS as a session-based authentication authority that manages multiple concurrent logon sessions, each with isolated identity and authentication state. - The post covers how Kerberos and NTLM authentication material is maintained per-logon-session (LUID-scoped), including TGT/TGS tickets, session keys, and related metadata enabling single sign-on. - It discusses why LSASS access is privileged (SYSTEM + SeDebugPrivilege) and how mitigations like LSA Protection / Protected Process Light (PPL) restrict memory access. - A key focus is “in-memory Kerberos ticket transfer” and the conceptual basis of Pass-the-Ticket: reusing already-issued service tickets by moving them between sessions rather than re-authenticating. - It’s useful for red teamers and defenders who need to understand where tickets live, how session scoping works, and why modern tradecraft shifts from raw LSASS dumping to stealthier LSA/Kerberos interface abuse and behavioral detection.
Technical Focus: - LSASS architecture and logon session isolation (LUID scoping) - Kerberos ticket lifecycle in memory (TGT, TGS, session keys) - NTLM material within LSASS logon sessions - Access tokens and authentication state management - LSA Protection / Protected Process Light (PPL) and privilege requirements - Pass-the-Ticket concepts and detection implications
Use Cases: - Map where Kerberos tickets reside and how they’re bound to logon sessions during investigations - Design detections for Pass-the-Ticket via anomalous ticket usage/service access patterns - Improve red team tradecraft by understanding session boundaries and ticket reuse mechanics - Evaluate the defensive value/limitations of PPL and LSASS access restrictions - Train teams on Windows authentication internals beyond “dump LSASS” narratives
Keywords: LSASS, Local Security Authority, Windows internals, logon session, LUID, access token, Kerberos, NTLM, TGT, TGS, AS-REQ, AS-REP, TGS-REQ, TGS-REP, single sign-on, Pass-the-Ticket, SeDebugPrivilege, LSA Protection, Protected Process Light, Active Directory, KDC, EDR detection

entry.title: LetMeowIn
entry.summary:
Title: LetMeowIn
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): LetMeowIn is a Windows x64 LSASS credential dumping tool implemented in C++ with MASM x64, focused on stealth and EDR/AV evasion. It aims to reduce detection by using indirect system calls (manual NTAPI usage), breaking telemetry (notably ETW), and obfuscating API resolution via compile-time hashing and pointer/name hiding. Instead of opening new handles to LSASS (a common detection point), it attempts to duplicate already-existing LSASS handles on the system, then creates an offline copy of LSASS for dumping. It also tampers with the output minidump by corrupting the `MDMP` signature and includes a helper script (`restoresig.py`) to restore it later. The repo notes historical bypass success against several security products, but also highlights a key limitation: it depends on pre-existing LSASS handles and only supports x64. Useful primarily for red teams, pentesters, and malware researchers studying modern LSASS dumping tradecraft and defensive gaps.
Technical Focus: - LSASS credential dumping via offline process cloning/copying - Indirect syscalls / manual NTAPI invocation (syscall evasion) - ETW (Event Tracing for Windows) tampering to reduce telemetry - Handle duplication to LSASS (avoid OpenProcess patterns) - Compile-time hashing & API obfuscation - Minidump (`MDMP`) signature corruption and restoration
Use Cases: - Stealthy credential access on Windows endpoints where LSASS access is possible - Researching/replicating EDR detection bypass patterns (syscalls, ETW, handle ops) - Building detections around LSASS handle duplication, dump signature anomalies, and ETW disruption - Training and purple-team exercises focused on credential dumping tradecraft
Keywords: LSASS, credential dumping, minidump, MDMP, Windows x64, C++, MASM, NTAPI, indirect syscalls, syscall stub, ETW, telemetry bypass, API hashing, string obfuscation, handle duplication, OpenProcess avoidance, offline dump, EDR evasion, Windows Defender, CrowdStrike Falcon, Cortex XDR

entry.title: catdumper
entry.summary:
Title: catdumper — LSASS MiniDump + in-memory RC4/Base64 + HTTPS exfiltration
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): catdumper is a Windows LSASS credential dumping tool that snapshots the LSASS process and generates a MiniDump, then encrypts (RC4) and Base64-encodes the dump entirely in memory. It exfiltrates the encrypted dump and its randomly generated key over HTTPS to a companion Python Flask server (exfil.py), which decodes/decrypts and writes the dump to disk for offline parsing (e.g., Mimikatz). The project focuses on evasion tactics such as compile-time polymorphism for unique binaries, string obfuscation (e.g., “lsass.exe”), and randomized network timing/packet sizing. It also claims to unhook NtReadVirtualMemory to bypass userland EDR hooks and performs benign API call patterns to reduce heuristic suspicion. Useful primarily for red teams/pentesters researching LSASS dumping tradecraft and defenders studying common evasion patterns around credential access and exfiltration.
Technical Focus: - LSASS process snapshotting and MiniDump generation - In-memory encryption (RC4) and Base64 encoding - Userland API unhooking (NtReadVirtualMemory) - Compile-time polymorphism / signature randomization - HTTPS exfiltration to a Flask receiver - String obfuscation and heuristic evasion via benign API noise
Use Cases: - Dumping LSASS credentials for post-exploitation (offline analysis with Mimikatz) - Testing EDR/AV detections for in-memory dump handling and exfiltration - Researching/replicating userland unhooking and anti-hook techniques - Building detection logic for LSASS access + dump creation + outbound HTTPS staging patterns
Keywords: LSASS, MiniDump, process snapshot, credential dumping, Mimikatz, NtReadVirtualMemory, API unhooking, userland hooks, EDR evasion, RC4, Base64, in-memory encryption, string obfuscation, polymorphism, compile-time RNG, Windows API, HTTPS exfiltration, Flask, Python receiver, randomized beaconing

entry.title: offensive dpapi with nemesis
entry.summary:
Title: Offensive DPAPI With Nemesis
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post explains how Nemesis 2.2 automates end-to-end offline DPAPI decryption workflows, with a focus on decrypting modern Chromium cookies and saved logins on Windows. It addresses the operational pain of DPAPI’s multi-step dependency chain (SYSTEM/user masterkeys, LSA secrets, domain DPAPI backup keys, and now Chromium App-Bound Encryption and CNG “Chromekey1”). The authors detail how Nemesis ingests artifacts (registry hives, masterkey files, LSASS dumps, Chromium “Local State”/“Login Data”, and CNG SystemKeys files), correlates them by host “Source”, and performs forward/retroactive decryption regardless of submission order. It also notes Windows changes that altered decrypted masterkey structures (breaking some existing libraries) and how Nemesis accounts for them. The write-up is primarily useful for red teams/pentesters doing post-compromise credential/cookie recovery and for defenders/IR teams needing to understand the artifact chain and where to detect/contain it. It’s interesting because it operationalizes the newest Chromium 137+ ABE + CNG wrinkle into a largely automated, file-based pipeline.
Technical Focus: - Windows DPAPI masterkeys (SYSTEM vs user) and decryption prerequisites - LSA secrets extraction (DPAPI_SYSTEM) via SYSTEM/SECURITY hives and LSASS dumps - Domain DPAPI backup key and MS-BKRP concepts (backup key decryption path) - Chromium cookie/login protection evolution: DPAPI → Local State encrypted_key → App-Bound Encryption (ABE) - CNG/KSP integration in Chrome 137+ (Google “Chromekey1” in SystemKeys) and DPAPI-protected private key material - Retroactive decryption/correlation logic in Nemesis (artifact linking by host/source)
Use Cases: - Decrypt Chromium cookies and saved passwords from collected endpoint artifacts (offline analysis) - Automate DPAPI masterkey recovery using registry hives, LSASS dumps, or submitted key material - Post-exploitation triage at scale: carve DPAPI blobs from files and identify required masterkey GUIDs - Incident response validation: reproduce attacker decryption paths to assess exposure and scope - Research/testing of DPAPI parsing changes on newer Windows builds and tool compatibility
Keywords: DPAPI, CryptProtectData, CryptUnprotectData, DPAPI_SYSTEM, LSA secrets, LSASS dump, SYSTEM hive, SECURITY hive, masterkey GUID, domain DPAPI backup key, MS-BKRP, SharpDPAPI, mimikatz, pypykatz, Impacket, DPAPIck3, Chromium, Chrome Local State, Login Data, App-Bound Encryption, iElevator COM, CNG, KSP, Google Chromekey1, SystemKeys, Nemesis 2.2, retroactive decryption

entry.title: credential guard
entry.summary:
Title: Credential Guard
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post explains Microsoft Defender Credential Guard (VBS/VTL1 isolation via LsaIso.exe) and why it blocks classic LSASS credential theft, then maps publicly disclosed bypass/abuse techniques into a purple-team validation playbook. - It covers four attack vectors: WDigest variable patching to re-enable plaintext caching, “Pass-the-Challenge” abuse of LsaIso NTLM response functions to recover/crack NTLMv1 material, patch-downgrade to reintroduce a Credential Guard EoP (CVE-2022-34709), and SSP negotiation abuse via Remote Credential Guard/CredSSP to extract NTLMv1 responses without dumping LSASS. - The article references PoCs/tools (BypassCredGuard, PassTheChallenge, WindowsDowndate, DumpGuard) and notes which techniques Microsoft has mitigated (patching/downgrade) vs those still largely unresolved (pass-the-challenge, SSP negotiation). - It provides concrete detection guidance using Windows auditing and Sysmon (privilege adjustments, LSASS handle access, registry auditing for poqexec/CBS downgrade, Kerberos ticket events, suspicious DLL loads, and LSA API monitoring). - Useful for purple teams and SOC/detection engineers to test Credential Guard effectiveness beyond “LSASS dump” detections, and for red teams to understand realistic prerequisites/noise and alternative credential access paths.
Technical Focus: - Virtualization-Based Security (VBS), Virtual Trust Levels (VTL0/VTL1), LsaIso.exe vs LSASS.exe - WDigest internals and in-memory patching (g_IsCredGuardEnabled, g_fParameter_UseLogonCredential) - Remote Credential Guard / CredSSP (TSSSP) and NTLMv1 response extraction - Kerberos ticketing telemetry (TGT/TGS) and SPN/machine-account abuse - Patch downgrade / Windows servicing (poqexec.exe, CBS/SideBySide) and CVE-2022-34709 - Detection engineering with Windows Security logs, Sysmon, and API telemetry (WriteProcessMemory, LsaCallAuthenticationPackage)
Use Cases: - Validate Credential Guard posture and alert coverage in enterprise Windows fleets - Simulate Credential Guard bypass attempts for purple-team exercises (PoC-driven) - Build detections for non-LSASS-dump credential access (RCG/CredSSP/LSA package calls) - Hunt for downgrade attempts abusing Windows servicing/poqexec registry paths - Correlate AD events (machine account creation + Kerberos tickets) to detect SSP negotiation abuse
Keywords: Credential Guard, VBS, VTL1, LSASS, LsaIso.exe, WDigest, g_IsCredGuardEnabled, g_fParameter_UseLogonCredential, SeDebugPrivilege, WriteProcessMemory, Pass-the-Challenge, NtlmIumCalculateNtResponse, NtlmIumLm20GetNtlm3ChallengeResponse, NTLMv1, Remote Credential Guard, CredSSP, TSSSP, LsaCallAuthenticationPackage, SPN, machine account quota, Event ID 4703, 4741, 4768, 4769, Sysmon Event ID 7, Sysmon Event ID 10, WindowsDowndate, poqexec.exe, SideBySide Configuration, CVE-2022-34709, DumpGuard, Impacket, PsExec

entry.title: MemoryModule
entry.summary:
Title: MemoryModule — Load a DLL from Memory (In-Memory PE/DLL Loader)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - MemoryModule is a C/C++ library that implements loading a Windows DLL directly from an in-memory buffer rather than from a file path. - It solves the limitation of `LoadLibrary`/`LoadLibraryEx`, which require the DLL to exist on disk, avoiding “write-to-temp-then-load” workarounds. - The project effectively reimplements key parts of the Windows PE loader: mapping sections, applying relocations, resolving imports, and calling the module entry point, exposing an API similar to `LoadLibrary` plus extended hooks. - It’s useful for red teams, pentesters, malware developers, and also defenders/researchers who need to understand or emulate reflective/in-memory loading behavior. - It’s interesting because it provides a well-known reference implementation for in-memory PE loading and is commonly used as a building block for fileless execution and custom loaders.
Technical Focus: - PE (Portable Executable) parsing and manual mapping - Import Address Table (IAT) resolution and dependency loading - Base relocations and image rebasing - Section mapping, memory protections, and loader-like initialization - Custom alloc/free and API surface (`MemoryLoadLibraryEx`, `MemoryGetProcAddress`)
Use Cases: - Build custom in-memory DLL loaders for payload staging without touching disk - Implement reflective-loading style execution in implants or red team tooling - Embed plugins/resources as raw DLL bytes inside an executable - Research/detect manual-mapping behaviors by studying a canonical implementation - Unit-test PE loading edge cases (exports, high addresses, malformed input)
Keywords: MemoryModule, in-memory DLL loading, manual mapping, reflective loader, PE format, Portable Executable, LoadLibraryEx, MemoryLoadLibraryEx, MemoryGetProcAddress, IAT, import resolution, base relocations, section mapping, VirtualAlloc, VirtualProtect, DllMain, fileless execution, Windows loader internals, C/C++ loader library

entry.title: idealistic definitions
entry.summary:
Title: An Idealistic View of Malware Analysis Definitions (WTF is heuristics?)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post clarifies commonly-misunderstood malware analysis and detection terms: static analysis, dynamic analysis, heuristics, and “deterministic” (signature-like) detections. - It frames static analysis as non-executing inspection of file bytes/structure (e.g., PE parsing) and dynamic analysis as execution-based observation (sandbox/emulator/EDR) of in-memory artifacts and runtime actions. - Heuristics are described as “looks malicious” scoring/triage signals (entropy anomalies, suspicious imports/traits, generic patterns) rather than definitive proof, while deterministic detections are precise indicators with low false positives (e.g., unique YARA strings, known-bad hashes). - The article highlights that “signature” rules can be either heuristic or deterministic depending on how specific the matched indicator is and how it’s interpreted. - It also discusses behavioral detection via hooks/ETW (e.g., LSASS handle opens vs. actual credential-reading) and the ambiguous classification of emulation (“static emulation” vs dynamic). - Useful for both red and blue teams to reason about why detections fire, where false positives come from, and how context changes interpretation.
Technical Focus: - Static file analysis (PE parsing, imports/sections/headers) - Dynamic analysis (sandboxing, memory scanning, behavior observation) - Heuristic vs deterministic/signature-based detection models (scoring/thresholds) - YARA rule specificity and false-positive tradeoffs - Behavioral telemetry via API hooking and ETW - Emulation as a hybrid analysis technique
Use Cases: - Calibrating YARA/signature rules to reduce false positives while retaining coverage - Explaining AV/EDR alert rationale to analysts and stakeholders - Red team planning around heuristic triggers (e.g., LSASS access, injection-like behavior) - Designing detection logic that separates “suspicious” from “high-confidence malicious” - Choosing appropriate analysis methods (static vs sandbox vs endpoint telemetry) during triage
Keywords: static analysis, dynamic analysis, heuristics, deterministic detection, signature-based detection, YARA, PE (Portable Executable), imports, entropy, sandbox, emulator, memory scanning, behavior analysis, EDR, ETW, ntdll hooking, LSASS, OpenProcess, code injection, mimikatz, SHA256, MD5

entry.title: maldev myths
entry.summary:
Title: MalDev Myths
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post critiques common “malware development” loader/dropper advice that the author argues is outdated or misapplied against modern AV/EDR and sandboxing. - It reframes priorities around defeating automated detection (emulation, memory scanning, telemetry) rather than manual reverse engineering. - Key points include: PE section choice for embedded payloads is usually irrelevant (but avoid adding new sections), entropy is a weak automated signal, and the specific encryption algorithm matters less than avoiding signatured implementations and obvious patterns. - It challenges popular “EDR bypass” tropes like ntdll unhooking and ETW patching, emphasizing that ETW can’t truly be disabled from user mode and that syscall tricks are often detectable via ETW/callstack analysis. - The author discourages classic remote process injection primitives (OpenProcess/VirtualAllocEx/WriteProcessMemory/CreateRemoteThread) and suggests focusing on guardrails, anti-emulation, and “EDR deconditioning” before straightforward shellcode execution. - Useful for red teamers and offensive tool developers building Windows loaders who want a detection-driven approach rather than technique-stacking.
Technical Focus: - PE sections (.text/.data/.rsrc) and static heuristics (IAT/imports, entropy) - Payload encoding/encryption tradeoffs and signatured crypto/API usage (crypt32, base64, XOR patterns) - AV emulation cutoffs and anti-emulation instruction padding - ETW realities (user-mode patching limits) and AMSI considerations - Syscalls, ntdll hooking/unhooking, and callstack-based detections - Injection tradeoffs and alternative execution primitives / DLL sideloading
Use Cases: - Designing Windows shellcode loaders that survive AV emulation and sandboxing - Reducing static indicators (suspicious imports, abnormal PE structure) without overfitting to myths - Prioritizing guardrails to prevent execution in VT/sandboxes and other middleboxes - Avoiding high-signal injection chains and choosing lower-noise execution methods - Evaluating third-party “EDR bypass” repos/blogs for unnecessary or counterproductive techniques
Keywords: MalDev, shellcode loader, dropper, PE sections, .rsrc, IAT, imports, heuristic analysis, entropy, static analysis, AV emulation, sandbox evasion, execution guardrails, ETW, EtwWrite, AMSI, ntdll unhooking, direct syscalls, indirect syscalls, callstack analysis, CreateRemoteThread, VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, DLL sideloading, EDR deconditioning, memory scanning, VirusTotal bypass

entry.title: how edr works
entry.summary:
Title: How EDR Works — The (Anti-)EDR Compendium (EDR functionality and bypasses in 2024/2025)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post is a conceptual deep-dive into how modern Windows EDRs detect shellcode loaders, framed around three “bubbles of bane”: on-disk AV signatures/emulation, in-memory scanning, and telemetry/behavior correlation. It explains the main telemetry sources EDRs rely on (user-mode API hooks, AMSI, ETW/ETW-TI, and kernel callbacks) and how they’re enriched with process/memory introspection (PEB/EPROCESS, memory region classification, call stack analysis). The author maps common loader behaviors (RW→RX transitions, remote process operations, thread creation) to likely detection triggers and highlights why “fixing” one detection surface (e.g., memory encryption) often increases exposure on another (telemetry). It covers attacker tradecraft such as command-line and PPID spoofing, ETW/AMSI patching, direct/indirect syscalls, image/module stomping, and reflective loading, emphasizing TOCTOU and trust boundaries (e.g., PEB is mutable). The piece argues that sophisticated techniques like memory encryption and call stack spoofing aren’t always necessary if you minimize suspicious telemetry and understand what actually triggers scans. Useful primarily for red teamers and malware developers building loaders, but also valuable for defenders designing robust detections around call stacks, memory regions, and event correlation.
Technical Focus: - Windows EDR telemetry pipelines (ETW, ETW-TI, kernel callbacks, user-mode hooks, AMSI) - AV signature scanning vs AV emulation (pre-execution) and memory scanning triggers - Memory region trust model (IMAGE/backed vs PRIVATE/unbacked, COW/working set checks) - Call stack and thread-state analysis for syscall/callback evasion detection - Loader tradecraft: RW→RX patterns, thread creation, remote process injection surfaces - Spoofing/patching techniques (PEB command line, PPID, ETW/AMSI patching, direct/indirect syscalls)
Use Cases: - Build or harden custom shellcode loaders to reduce EDR telemetry and scanning triggers - Design detections around suspicious memory regions, RW→RX transitions, and thread start addresses - Evaluate bypass tradeoffs: memory encryption vs increased behavioral telemetry - Threat hunting: correlate process creation events with PEB/EPROCESS inconsistencies (args/PPID) - Assess injection techniques (hollowing, module stomping, reflective loading) and their observable artifacts
Keywords: EDR, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, Windows Defender, AMSI, ETW, ETW-TI, PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutine, ObRegisterCallbacks, ntdll.dll hooking, direct syscalls, indirect syscalls, SysWhispers, call stack analysis, memory scanning, YARA, AV emulation, VirtualAlloc, VirtualProtect, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, NtWriteVirtualMemory, CreateRemoteThread, process hollowing, module stomping, PEB spoofing, PPID spoofing, ETW patching, AmsiScanBuffer patching, PRIVATE vs IMAGE memory, Copy-on-Write, TOCTOU

entry.title: supermega
entry.summary:
Title: Supermega — A shellcode loader laboratory for Red Team EDR bypass experimentation (Cordyceps EXE/DLL injection)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Supermega is a framework/lab to rapidly prototype and test different Windows shellcode “carrier” (loader) implementations and injection strategies aimed at bypassing EDR. It supports combining multiple input payloads (shellcode/DLL) with multiple benign “injectable” EXEs, then producing an “infected” executable where a carrier stub in `.text` loads/decodes/executes a payload placed in `.text` or `.rdata`. The workflow leverages a C→ASM→EXE→shellcode pipeline (inspired by hasherezade’s paper) with templated C (jinja2) and logged intermediate artifacts to ease iteration and debugging. Techniques discussed include entrypoint modification vs backdooring/hijacking code paths (e.g., inside `main()`), IAT reuse/patching, memory permission transitions (RW→RX, RW→RWX, RWX→RX), and keeping payloads in IMAGE-backed sections to improve OPSEC against memory scanners. It also covers a DLL “in-memory form” loader that performs relocations and import resolution to avoid PRIVATE executable memory regions. Useful primarily for Red Teamers, pentesters, and offensive security researchers building/testing loaders and studying EDR detection boundaries.
Technical Focus: - PE file modification (entrypoint change, code backdooring/hijack inside `main`, section placement) - Shellcode loader patterns (VirtualAlloc/VirtualProtect, execute-from-memory) - IAT reuse and optional IAT patching - Payload encoding/decoding (e.g., XOR) and templated loader generation (C/jinja2 → ASM cleanup) - IMAGE vs PRIVATE memory tradeoffs; section permission changes (RWX→RX) / “module stomping”-adjacent considerations - DLL preloading/in-memory mapping (relocations, import resolution) from an injected IMAGE section
Use Cases: - Build and compare multiple loader variants against different EDR products/configurations - Generate “carrier” shellcode from C templates for quick iteration and customization - Experiment with payload placement in `.text` vs `.rdata` and different invocation methods (entrypoint vs hijack) - Test OPSEC tradeoffs: PRIVATE RX regions vs modified IMAGE sections and their detectability - Prototype DLL-loading approaches that keep execution in IMAGE-backed memory - Regression/integration testing of loader changes using automated execution checks
Keywords: Supermega, Cordyceps, shellcode loader, EDR bypass, PE injection, EXE infection, DLL injection, .text section, .rdata section, entrypoint patching, code cave, main hijack, VirtualAlloc, VirtualProtect, RWX to RX, RW to RX, IAT reuse, IAT patching, payload encryption, XOR decoder, IMAGE vs PRIVATE memory, relocations, import resolution, reflective loading, anti-emulation, guardrails

entry.title: exe injection
entry.summary:
Title: Cordyceps: Shellcode in EXE Injection (IAT/.rdata reuse via fixups)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post describes a PE backdooring technique (“Cordyceps”) that injects shellcode into an existing EXE/DLL while preserving the host binary’s normal structure and behavior. The goal is to reduce EDR detection by avoiding common shellcode patterns like PEB-walking, API hashing, and explicit `LoadLibrary`/`GetProcAddress` resolution. Instead, the injected code is patched (“fixups”) to call Windows APIs through the target executable’s existing Import Address Table (IAT) using RIP-relative `call [rip+off]` sequences that remain valid under ASLR. It also moves/places required strings and data into `.rdata` and patches RIP-relative `lea` references so the payload looks like typical compiled code rather than shellcode with embedded suspicious blobs. This is useful for red teams and malware developers building stealthy initial access payload carriers, and for defenders to understand a class of “looks-like-normal-PE” injection that defeats simplistic heuristics.
Technical Focus: - PE file modification / backdooring and section-aware injection - IAT reuse via RIP-relative call fixups (ASLR-safe) - Eliminating PEB-walk/API-resolver shellcode patterns - `.rdata` data placement and RIP-relative LEA fixups - EDR evasion via “IMAGE region” execution and reduced telemetry
Use Cases: - Embedding a loader into a legitimate EXE while keeping original imports and code layout largely intact - Building payloads that call WinAPI without `GetProcAddress`/`LoadLibrary` shellcode stubs - Reducing behavioral/telemetry triggers associated with classic shellcode API resolution - Creating more “benign-looking” call stacks/threads originating from mapped IMAGE memory - Researching/detecting PE tampering that reuses IAT and adds `.rdata` artifacts
Keywords: Windows PE, EXE injection, shellcode, IAT, Import Address Table, RIP-relative addressing, ASLR, `.text`, `.rdata`, fixups, PEB walk, GetProcAddress, LoadLibrary, VirtualAlloc, EDR evasion, IMAGE memory region, PE sections, backdooring, loader, SuperMega

entry.title: automating goad and live malware labs
entry.summary:
Title: Automating GOAD and Live Malware Labs (Cyber Ranges as Code with Ludus and Elastic)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post describes building a scalable, repeatable cyber range that combines the GOAD Active Directory lab with a live XZ backdoor (CVE-2024-3094) malware lab, fully instrumented into Elastic Security. - It solves the operational pain of manually provisioning complex multi-VM AD forests and malware environments by using Ludus (Proxmox/Ansible + YAML) to deploy everything from code and Elastic Agent/Fleet to standardize telemetry collection. - The guide covers automating Elastic Agent enrollment (Fleet policies + Elastic Defend in Detect mode), and streaming endpoint telemetry to Elastic Cloud/Serverless for SIEM/XDR analytics. - A key focus is safe handling of live malware: it reconciles “must be isolated” vs “needs cloud egress” by using Ludus Testing Mode to implement pinhole egress only to required Elastic endpoints, with WireGuard for out-of-band access. - It then walks through representative attack chains (credential stuffing, PrintNightmare, secrets dumping, Kerberoasting, MSSQL xp_cmdshell, scheduled-task persistence, and XZ backdoor SSH trigger) and maps them to detections and investigation workflows. - Useful for purple teams, detection engineers, and SOC analysts validating rules against realistic Windows+Linux adversary behavior; also relevant to red teams who want reproducible emulation infrastructure.
Technical Focus: - Ludus cyber range automation (Proxmox, Ansible, YAML “range as code”) - Elastic Agent + Fleet enrollment, Elastic Defend telemetry (EDR/XDR) - Safe live-malware lab isolation with controlled egress (Testing Mode pinholes) - GOAD AD attack emulation (Kerberoasting, NTLM relay/ADCS themes, PrintNightmare) - XZ backdoor / supply-chain simulation (CVE-2024-3094, xzbot SSH trigger) - Elastic investigation + correlation (ECS, Event Analyzer, Session Viewer, Attack Discovery/AI)
Use Cases: - Automate deployment of a multi-domain AD lab for continuous detection validation - Run repeatable ATT&CK-mapped attack chains to regression-test SIEM/EDR rules - Safely host and observe “live” malware behavior while preventing internet exposure - Validate endpoint telemetry quality (process trees, TTY/session reconstruction) across Windows and Linux - Build an IaC-driven purple-team pipeline integrating lab provisioning, instrumentation, and analytics
Keywords: Ludus, Proxmox, Ansible, YAML, GOAD, Active Directory, Kerberoasting, PrintNightmare, CVE-2021-34527, DCSync, secretsdump, Impacket, Rubeus, MSSQL xp_cmdshell, schtasks, Elastic Agent, Fleet, Elastic Defend, Elastic Security, Elastic Cloud, ECS, Event Analyzer, Session Viewer, WireGuard, CVE-2024-3094, XZ backdoor, xzbot, pinhole egress, MITRE ATT&CK, ES|QL, Attack Discovery, AI Assistant, Elastic Workflows

entry.title: tlsh.org
entry.summary:
Title: TLSH (Trend Locality Sensitive Hash) — A Locality Sensitive / Fuzzy Hash
Type: Documentation
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): TLSH is a fuzzy hashing library/tool that generates similarity digests for files (minimum ~50 bytes) to enable approximate matching rather than exact equality checks. It solves the problem of identifying related or near-duplicate files (e.g., malware variants, repacked binaries, modified documents) by comparing hash distances where similar inputs yield similar hashes. The site highlights TLSH’s focus on speed/scalability for large-scale search and clustering, and claims improved robustness against adversarial evasion compared to other fuzzy hashes like ssdeep and sdhash. TLSH is widely integrated into threat intel and malware ecosystems (VirusTotal, MalwareBazaar, MISP) and is standardized in STIX 2.1. It’s useful for SOC analysts, threat hunters, malware analysts, and defenders building similarity-based triage pipelines. The referenced papers/talks cover design choices, fast search, scalable clustering, and robustness considerations.
Technical Focus: - Fuzzy hashing / locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) for file similarity - Similarity distance scoring and nearest-neighbor style comparisons - Large-scale indexing, fast search, and scalable clustering of digests - Robustness/evasion resistance vs. ssdeep/sdhash - Threat intel ecosystem integration (VirusTotal, MISP, STIX 2.1)
Use Cases: - Cluster malware samples into families/variants using similarity digests - Hunt for modified/repacked binaries across large repositories - Enrich threat intel objects with TLSH and share via STIX/MISP - Triage incoming files by finding nearest known-bad/known-good neighbors - Detect near-duplicate documents or payloads in incident response datasets
Keywords: TLSH, fuzzy hashing, locality-sensitive hash, similarity digest, malware clustering, threat hunting, VirusTotal API, MalwareBazaar, MISP, STIX 2.1, ssdeep, sdhash, nearest neighbor search, scalable indexing, robustness to evasion, file similarity, SOC triage, high-dimensional clustering

entry.title: ADVobfuscator
entry.summary:
Title: ADVobfuscator (C++20 compile-time obfuscation library)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): ADVobfuscator is a C++20 header-based library that performs compile-time generation of obfuscated/encrypted constants and code constructs without external tooling or compiler modifications. It targets the common reverse-engineering problem of plaintext strings and static data being easily recoverable from binaries by transforming them at compile time and decoding at runtime. The repo includes mechanisms for obfuscated strings/byte blocks, AES-128 compile-time encryption (with some compiler limitations), compile-time random generation, and a compile-time finite state machine approach to obfuscate function calls. It is primarily useful for red team tool developers, malware developers, and software protectors who want to raise static analysis cost and reduce obvious indicators in binaries. It’s interesting because it leverages modern C++ metaprogramming/constexpr to push obfuscation into the compilation pipeline, making it portable across toolchains (GCC/Clang/MSVC) when built in Release mode.
Technical Focus: - C++20 constexpr/metaprogramming for compile-time transformations - Compile-time string and byte-array obfuscation - AES-128 compile-time encryption/decryption patterns - Compile-time PRNG / randomization for per-build variance - Finite State Machine (FSM) techniques to obfuscate control flow / calls - Toolchain/optimization constraints (Release-only behavior)
Use Cases: - Hide sensitive strings (URLs, mutex names, commands, registry paths) from static extraction - Obfuscate embedded configuration blobs or shellcode-like byte arrays in C++ implants/tools - Add per-build variability to reduce signature stability across compiled artifacts - Increase reverse-engineering effort for proprietary agents or red team payload components - Demonstrate/teach modern C++ compile-time obfuscation techniques via examples/tests
Keywords: C++20, constexpr, template metaprogramming, compile-time obfuscation, string obfuscation, data obfuscation, AES-128, AES string, std::array, compile-time random, PRNG, finite state machine, control-flow obfuscation, static analysis resistance, reverse engineering, GCC, Clang, MSVC, Release build, CMake

entry.title: avred update
entry.summary:
Title: Avred Update: Advances in Reversing Microsoft Defender Signature Format
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post summarizes new reverse-engineering findings about Microsoft Defender’s signature database format and explains how they clarify Avred’s earlier detection behaviors. - It highlights that Defender signatures are composed of multiple weighted sub-rules and a score threshold; a detection triggers when accumulated sub-rule weights exceed the threshold. - This model explains “non-dominant” matches (multiple sub-rules contributing) versus “dominant” matches (a single sub-rule weight meeting/exceeding the threshold). - The post also details wildcard/placeholder constructs in signatures, showing that rules are not purely byte-exact and can tolerate variations (e.g., NOPs/register changes) via wildcard types. - It references `WdFilter.sys` as the kernel minifilter responsible for file scanning and provides examples translating wildcard constructs into YARA-like patterns. - Useful for malware analysts, red teamers, and detection engineers trying to understand Defender signature robustness and why naive byte-tweaks may fail.
Technical Focus: - Microsoft Defender signature database reverse engineering - Weighted sub-rule scoring and threshold-based triggering - Wildcard/placeholder encoding (90 01–90 05 types) - YARA-style representation of proprietary signature logic - Windows Defender scanning path via `WdFilter.sys` minifilter
Use Cases: - Explaining/triaging why specific binaries trigger Defender detections - Designing more realistic signature-evasion experiments (and understanding their limits) - Building tooling to parse/approximate Defender signature logic - Creating defensive test cases to validate signature resilience to minor mutations

entry.title: go buena clr
entry.summary:
Title: go-buena-clr — Good CLR Host with Native Patchless AMSI Bypass
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): go-buena-clr is a Go implementation of a “good” .NET CLR host based on Joshua Magri’s “Being a Good CLR Host” technique, built on top of Ne0nd0g’s go-clr. It focuses on hosting the CLR via custom hosting interfaces (notably `IHostControl`) to implement `ProvideAssembly`, enabling assembly loading via `Load_2` instead of `Load_3`. This design is used to bypass AMSI scanning “natively” without patching AMSI in memory (avoiding common behavioral detections around AMSI patching). The repo includes an example (“BuenaVillage”) that embeds a .NET tool (e.g., Rubeus) and executes it with arguments through the custom host. It’s primarily useful for red teamers/pentesters and offensive tool developers who need in-process .NET execution with reduced AMSI friction. It’s interesting because it leverages CLR hosting semantics rather than direct AMSI tampering or breakpoint-based bypasses.
Technical Focus: - CLR hosting from Go via COM interfaces (`ICLRMetaHost`, `ICLRRuntimeHost`, `IHostControl`) - Custom `ProvideAssembly` implementation to control assembly resolution/loading - .NET in-memory execution (embedded assembly bytes, entrypoint invocation) - AMSI bypass via alternate assembly load path (`Load_2` vs `Load_3`) - Interop details: GUIDs, HRESULT handling, SAFEARRAY/VARIANT marshaling
Use Cases: - Execute embedded .NET tradecraft tools (e.g., Rubeus) from a Go loader - Build custom implants/loaders that run managed payloads without AMSI memory patching - Research/demonstrate CLR hosting-based AMSI evasion techniques - Integrate .NET execution into C2 agents written in Go
Keywords: Go, CLR hosting, .NET, IHostControl, ProvideAssembly, ICLRMetaHost, ICLRRuntimeHost, AppDomain, Assembly.Load, Load_2, Load_3, AMSI, AMSI bypass, patchless bypass, in-memory execution, COM interop, GUID, HRESULT, SAFEARRAY, Rubeus

entry.title: HolyGrail
entry.summary:
Title: HolyGrail — BYOVD Hunter for Prioritizing Windows Drivers
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): HolyGrail is a Rust-based static analysis tool that helps identify Windows kernel drivers (.sys) that are good candidates for Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver (BYOVD) abuse. It aims to reduce manual triage by scanning drivers for kernel imports and characteristics commonly used in BYOVD exploitation chains (e.g., memory/MDL primitives, section mapping, cross-process read/write, process control, and device I/O). The tool also checks candidates against Microsoft Windows 10/11 Driver Block Policies (version/hash/certificate) and flags drivers listed in LoLDrivers. It provides “comms hints” by looking for device interfaces that could enable a simple user-mode IOCTL bridge. This is primarily useful for red teams and security researchers hunting loadable, less-known drivers with useful primitives, and secondarily for defenders auditing fleets for BYOVD exposure. Its value is in combining import-based capability heuristics with real-world allow/block signals (Blocklist + LoLDrivers) to prioritize analysis.
Technical Focus: - BYOVD candidate discovery and driver triage - Static analysis of kernel imports and driver capabilities - Microsoft Driver Block Rules (Win10/Win11) matching (version/hash/cert) - LoLDrivers dataset correlation - Device objects / IOCTL interface discovery (“comms path” hints) - Windows kernel primitives (MDL, section/VM mapping, cross-process R/W)
Use Cases: - Rapidly triage large collections of .sys files to find BYOVD-relevant primitives - Identify drivers likely to load on Win10/Win11 without block policy issues - Filter out well-known/flagged drivers by checking LoLDrivers presence - Support manual reverse engineering by prioritizing high-signal driver candidates - Defensive auditing: detect risky third-party drivers deployed in an environment
Keywords: BYOVD, Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver, Windows kernel, driver.sys, kernel imports, IOCTL, device object, MDL, section mapping, virtual memory mapping, cross-process read write, process termination, Microsoft Driver Blocklist, Windows 10, Windows 11, LoLDrivers, Authenticode, certificate hash, Rust, static analysis

entry.title: 200 kernel bugs in 30 days
entry.summary:
Title: 100+ Kernel Bugs in 30 Days: High-Scale Driver Vulnerability Research with Agent Swarms
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - The post describes an automated, agent-based pipeline for large-scale vulnerability research on Windows third-party kernel drivers (.sys), focusing on unintentional memory corruption rather than “known-bad” backdoored drivers. - The authors scraped ~1.8k driver binaries, prioritized 202 “high-risk” targets via attack-surface heuristics, and used a multi-agent LLM workflow (decompile/triage/audit) to generate structured findings. - They built a VM + kernel-debugging validation loop (including QEMU virtual devices) to load drivers, exercise IOCTL interfaces, and iteratively produce PoCs that trigger crashes and confirm bugs. - Results: 521 potential issues across 158 unique binaries; they estimate a high false-positive rate (~60% even among high-confidence items) but still project 100+ likely exploitable user→kernel LPE-class bugs on Windows 11 x64. - The write-up highlights common bug classes in IOCTL handlers (arbitrary read/write, heap overflows, UAF) and discusses real-world impact including BYOVD risk and KASLR bypass via info leaks. - It’s most useful for kernel exploit researchers/red teams (methodology) and defenders/PSIRTs (scale implications, IOC hash list, blocklist gaps), and it’s notable for showing how cheap closed-loop agentic auditing can be.
Technical Focus: - Windows kernel driver attack surface analysis (IOCTL dispatch, METHOD_NEITHER, device ACLs) - LLM/agent-swarm assisted reverse engineering and decompilation-driven auditing - Memory corruption classes in kernel-mode code (heap/stack overflow, UAF, type confusion, integer overflow) - VM-based validation harness with kernel debugging + crash dump triage - QEMU device emulation to satisfy driver initialization and expose IOCTLs - BYOVD and vulnerable driver ecosystem / blocklist limitations
Use Cases: - Automating triage and auditing of large driver corpora for LPE candidates - Building closed-loop “audit → PoC → crash → refine” pipelines for kernel bug validation - Defensive hunting: checking enterprise fleets for vulnerable driver presence via published hashes - Prioritizing drivers for manual RE based on IOCTL complexity and unsafe handler patterns - Informing driver blocklist/certificate revocation discussions and PSIRT intake scaling
Keywords: Windows kernel drivers, .sys, IOCTL, DeviceIoControl, IRP_MJ_DEVICE_CONTROL, METHOD_NEITHER, KMDF, WDK, memory corruption, arbitrary read, arbitrary write, heap overflow, stack overflow, use-after-free, type confusion, integer overflow, local privilege escalation, KASLR bypass, BYOVD, Microsoft vulnerable driver blocklist, LOLDrivers, QEMU, kernel debugging, crash dump analysis, agent swarms, LLM-assisted reverse engineering, CVE-2025-65001

entry.title: lsawhisper bof
entry.summary:
Title: LSA Whisperer BOF (lsawhisper-bof)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): LSA Whisperer BOF is a Cobalt Strike Beacon Object File port of Evan McBroom’s LSA Whisperer that interacts with Windows authentication packages via the LSA untrusted/trusted client interface. It performs credential- and token-related operations by calling `LsaCallAuthenticationPackage` rather than opening/reading/injecting into the LSASS process, making it relevant even when LSASS runs as PPL and/or Credential Guard is enabled. The BOFs implement modules for MSV1_0 (DPAPI credential key recovery, strong credential keys, NTLMv1 response generation), Kerberos (ticket listing/dumping/purging), and CloudAP (Entra ID/AAD and AD FS SSO cookie extraction plus cloud status queries). Targeting other logon sessions is supported via LUID selection and typically requires SYSTEM/`SeTcbPrivilege`. It’s primarily useful for red teams and pentesters needing post-compromise credential material and SSO artifacts with reduced LSASS memory-touching telemetry. The project is interesting because it operationalizes “legitimate” LSA client APIs to access high-value auth data without classic LSASS dumping techniques.
Technical Focus: - LSA client APIs (`LsaConnectUntrusted`, `LsaRegisterLogonProcess`, `LsaCallAuthenticationPackage`) - MSV1_0 authentication package protocol messages (DPAPI cred keys, NTLM challenge/response) - Kerberos ticket cache interaction and `.kirbi` extraction (incl. session keys) - CloudAP/AAD plugin interfaces for Entra ID / device / AD FS SSO cookies - Logon session targeting via LUID and privilege requirements (`SeTcbPrivilege`) - Operating under PPL/Credential Guard constraints without LSASS memory access
Use Cases: - Recover DPAPI credential keys (including “strong” keys on Win10+) for offline DPAPI masterkey decryption (e.g., SharpDPAPI workflows) - Dump cached Kerberos tickets to base64 `.kirbi` blobs for lateral movement or service access - Selectively purge Kerberos tickets (optionally by server) to shape authentication behavior during ops - Extract Entra ID (Azure AD) SSO cookies / device SSO cookies / AD FS enterprise SSO cookies from cloud-joined endpoints - Generate NTLMv1 responses with operator-chosen challenges for downgrade/cracking scenarios (where not blocked)
Keywords: LSA, LsaCallAuthenticationPackage, LSASS, PPL, Credential Guard, MSV1_0, Kerberos, ticket cache, .kirbi, session keys, DPAPI, credkey, strong credential key, NTLMv1, LUID, SeTcbPrivilege, CloudAP, Entra ID, Azure AD, SSO cookie, AD FS, Cobalt Strike, BOF, Aggressor Script, logon sessions

entry.title: cloud protection microsoft defender antivirus

Title: Cloud protection and Microsoft Defender Antivirus
Type: Documentation
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This Microsoft Learn page explains how Microsoft Defender Antivirus “cloud protection” (MAPS) augments local real-time protection with cloud-backed intelligence and ML-driven detections. - The goal is to identify and block new or emerging threats faster than traditional signature/security intelligence update cycles, sometimes before widespread infection occurs. - It describes the high-level architecture: endpoints submit signals/metadata to Microsoft cloud services, which use the Microsoft Intelligent Security Graph and machine learning to return rapid protection decisions. - The doc notes cloud protection is enabled by default but may need re-enabling if disabled by policy, and it points to configuration guidance. - It also highlights “emergency dynamic intelligence updates” (near real-time fixes delivered via cloud) for eligible subscriptions (e.g., Windows 10 E5). - Useful primarily for Blue Teams, SecOps, and endpoint administrators planning Defender AV hardening and update strategy; it’s relevant to Red Teams for understanding detection latency and cloud-dependent detection paths.

entry.title: cloud protection microsoft antivirus sample submission
entry.summary:
Title: Cloud protection and sample submission at Microsoft Defender Antivirus

Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This document explains how Microsoft Defender Antivirus uses cloud-delivered protection and automatic sample submission to rapidly classify and block new or unknown threats. - When local detection can’t reach a verdict, Defender sends file metadata to Microsoft’s cloud; if still inconclusive, it may request the full file sample depending on the organization’s sample submission policy. - It outlines “block at first sight” (BAFS), including synchronous vs. asynchronous cloud verdicting and how disabling sample submission effectively disables BAFS (limiting analysis to metadata). - The article details configurable submission modes (send safe samples, prompt, send all, or do not send) and notes platform differences across Windows, macOS, and Linux. - It provides examples of metadata fields sent (process context, behavioral signals, hashes, signer info) and clarifies privacy/compliance handling of submitted samples as customer data. - Useful primarily for Blue Teams/SecOps and endpoint administrators tuning Defender for Endpoint protection vs. privacy/consent tradeoffs; also relevant to red teams for understanding cloud-verdict dependencies and telemetry surfaces.

Use Cases: - Configure Defender AV cloud protection and sample submission via Intune/GPO/ConfigMgr/PowerShell - Decide between “safe samples” vs. “all samples” to improve detection of macro/phishing payloads - Troubleshoot why BAFS/verdicting behavior changes when sample submission is disabled - Assess what endpoint metadata is transmitted to Microsoft for cloud classification - Set organizational policy balancing detection efficacy with consent/PII concerns
