Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This write-up demonstrates Windows APC (Asynchronous Procedure Call) queue injection implemented in Rust to execute shellcode inside a remote process without creating a new remote thread. - The goal is to reduce common EDR detections associated with classic injection patterns like `CreateRemoteThread`, while still using suspicious primitives such as remote memory allocation and `WriteProcessMemory`. - It explains the requirement for a target thread to enter an “alertable” state (e.g., via `SleepEx`, `WaitForSingleObjectEx`, etc.) for queued user-mode APCs to execute, and notes this as a practical limitation when choosing target processes. - The PoC allocates RWX memory in the target process, writes shellcode (example opens calc.exe), enumerates target threads via Toolhelp APIs, and queues the shellcode pointer to each thread using `QueueUserAPC`. - It highlights operational issues like multiple thread hits causing repeated payload execution and suggests adding a mutex in shellcode to enforce single execution. - Useful for red teamers/pentesters learning Windows injection tradecraft and defenders studying behavioral detection opportunities around APC queuing and remote memory operations.
Technical Focus: - User-mode APC mechanics and “alertable” thread states - Remote process memory allocation and shellcode staging (`VirtualAllocEx`, `WriteProcessMemory`) - Thread enumeration via Toolhelp snapshot APIs (`CreateToolhelp32Snapshot`, `Thread32First/Next`) - APC queuing with `QueueUserAPC` and function pointer casting (`PAPCFUNC`) - Rust + windows-rs FFI for Win32 API interaction
Use Cases: - Implementing alternative process injection that avoids `CreateRemoteThread` - Testing EDR detections for APC queuing + remote memory write patterns - Building/learning Rust-based Windows offensive tooling - Evaluating which processes/threads are likely to become alertable for reliable execution - Demonstrating tradeoffs/limitations of “classic” evasion techniques in modern environments
Keywords: APC injection, QueueUserAPC, Asynchronous Procedure Call, alertable state, SleepEx, WaitForSingleObjectEx, VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, OpenProcess, OpenThread, Toolhelp32Snapshot, THREADENTRY32, process injection, shellcode, RWX memory, windows-rs, Rust FFI, EDR evasion, thread enumeration, mutex (single-instance)

entry.title: hells hollow a new SSDT hooking technique with alt syscalls rootkit
entry.summary:
Title: Hells Hollow: A new SSDT Hooking technique (with Alt Syscalls / Rootkit)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This post describes a Windows kernel SSDT-hooking approach (“Hells Hollow”) that leverages Windows 11 “alt syscalls” to intercept system calls in a way that can evade or complicate common syscall-integrity checks. It focuses on how rootkits/EDR-like drivers can hook syscall dispatch by targeting alternate syscall paths rather than only the traditional SSDT flow defenders often validate. The write-up ties the technique to modern syscall evasion tradecraft (HellsGate-style direct syscalls) and the evolving Windows 11 syscall surface. It’s primarily useful for red team/offensive kernel developers and malware/rootkit authors exploring stealthy syscall interception, and for blue teams researching new kernel-level detection gaps. The key value is highlighting that “syscall integrity” assumptions can break when alternate syscall mechanisms exist and are not equivalently monitored.
Technical Focus: - Windows kernel SSDT hooking and syscall dispatch internals - Windows 11 alternate syscalls (“alt syscalls”) mechanics - Rootkit-style kernel interception and stealth considerations - Syscall integrity validation / anti-hooking assumptions - EDR evasion tradecraft around direct/alternative syscalls
Use Cases: - Developing or evaluating stealthy kernel syscall hooks for research - Testing EDR/syscall-integrity products against non-standard syscall paths - Building detections for anomalous syscall dispatch/alt-syscall usage - Reverse engineering Windows 11 syscall mechanisms and hook points
Keywords: Windows 11, kernel driver, SSDT, system service dispatch table, syscall hooking, alt syscalls, Nt* syscalls, rootkit, EDR evasion, syscall integrity, anti-hooking, kernel-mode interception, dispatch path, direct syscalls, HellsGate, ntdll, KiSystemCall, syscall table, Windows internals

entry.title: reflective dll injection in c
entry.summary:
Title: Reflective DLL injection and bootstrapping in C
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post walks through implementing reflective DLL injection in C, focusing on how a DLL can load itself from memory without using the normal Windows loader path. - It covers the “bootstrapping” steps needed to manually map a PE image: locating the module in memory, parsing PE headers, resolving imports, applying relocations, and invoking the DLL entry point. - The technique is commonly used to execute a DLL payload from a raw byte buffer (e.g., shellcode, network-delivered blob) and avoid touching disk. - It’s useful for red team/offensive tooling authors and malware developers building custom loaders, and for blue teams wanting to understand what artifacts and behaviors manual mapping produces. - The interesting part is the practical, low-level PE/loader logic that mirrors what `LdrLoadDll` does, enabling in-memory execution patterns that can bypass simplistic “LoadLibrary-only” detections.
Technical Focus: - Reflective DLL injection (in-memory DLL loading) - Manual PE mapping (sections, headers, memory protections) - Import Address Table (IAT) resolution and export parsing - Base relocations and ASLR handling - Calling `DllMain` / entry point bootstrapping
Use Cases: - Building a custom in-memory DLL loader for implants - Executing DLL payloads from encrypted/packed buffers without dropping to disk - Red team tradecraft research on injection/loader telemetry - Defensive analysis: detecting manual-mapped modules vs standard loader modules
Keywords: reflective DLL injection, manual mapping, PE format, DOS header, NT headers, section mapping, IAT, import resolution, export table, base relocations, ASLR, DllMain, loader stub, in-memory execution, Windows internals, process injection, shellcode loader, LdrLoadDll, LoadLibrary, GetProcAddress

entry.title: remote process dll injection

Title: Remote process DLL injection in Rust
Type: Blog Post

entry.title: vectored exception handling squared rust
entry.summary:
Title: Vectored Exception Handling Squared (VEH²) in Rust — Patchless AMSI bypass via hardware breakpoints
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post implements CrowdStrike’s “Vectored Exception Handling Squared” (VEH²) technique in Rust and explains the underlying Windows exception mechanics. - It targets the problem of “patchless” interception of sensitive routines (e.g., AMSI scanning) without modifying module code (avoiding common tamper detections like code hashing). - The core idea is to register a Vectored Exception Handler, trigger an initial exception (e.g., `int3`), and then modify the provided `CONTEXT` to set CPU debug registers (hardware breakpoints) without calling `SetThreadContext`/`NtSetContextThread` (and thus reducing ETW/API-call telemetry). - When the hardware breakpoint fires (`EXCEPTION_SINGLE_STEP`), the handler manipulates registers/stack (`RIP`, `RSP`, `RAX`) to spoof a return value and “return” from the target function without executing it. - It’s useful for red teamers, malware researchers, and defenders studying detection surface around VEH, debug registers, and AMSI/ETW bypass patterns. - It’s interesting because it demonstrates a stealthier breakpoint registration path by leveraging exception-delivered `CONTEXT` mutation rather than explicit thread-context syscalls.
Technical Focus: - Vectored Exception Handling (AddVectoredExceptionHandler) and exception dispatch flow - Hardware breakpoints via x86/x64 debug registers (DR0–DR7) and `EXCEPTION_SINGLE_STEP` - `CONTEXT` structure manipulation (RIP/RSP/RAX + debug register flags) - Patchless AMSI bypass concept (intercepting `AmsiScanBuffer`-like execution) - Telemetry/detection implications of `SetThreadContext` → `NtSetContextThread` and ETW audit providers - Threading caveats (per-thread breakpoints; COM/multithread considerations)
Use Cases: - Implementing a patchless user-mode interception primitive for sensitive functions (AMSI/ETW-like targets) - Researching EDR detection gaps around VEH handlers and debug-register usage - Building PoCs to understand exception-based control-flow manipulation and stealth tradeoffs - Developing defensive detections for anomalous VEH registration and debug register state changes
Keywords: VEH, VEH², Vectored Exception Handling, AddVectoredExceptionHandler, EXCEPTION_BREAKPOINT, int3, EXCEPTION_SINGLE_STEP, hardware breakpoint, debug registers, DR0, DR7, DR6, CONTEXT, GetThreadContext, SetThreadContext, NtSetContextThread, ETW, Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-Audit-API-Calls, AMSI, AmsiScanBuffer, Rust, windows-sys, RIP, RSP, RAX

entry.title: how a windows process is created part 1
entry.summary:
Title: Genesis – The Birth of a Windows Process (Part 1)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This article breaks down Windows process creation at a systems-internals level, focusing on the user-mode APIs, required privileges, and the core kernel/user data structures involved. - It surveys common creation paths (CreateProcess*, ShellExecute*) and explains how they converge into `CreateProcessInternal` and ultimately `NtCreateUserProcess` for the user→kernel transition. - The post highlights key creation flags and structures (`STARTUPINFO/STARTUPINFOEX`, `PROCESS_INFORMATION`, `UpdateProcThreadAttributes`) and touches on “modern” (UWP/Store) process activation via package attributes or `IApplicationActivationManager`. - It provides an overview of process representations and per-subsystem tracking (`EPROCESS/KPROCESS`, `ETHREAD`, `PEB`, `CSR_PROCESS`, `W32PROCESS`) and points to kernel callbacks like `PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutine(Ex*)`. - It also introduces special process classes—Protected Process / PPL, Minimal processes, Pico processes (WSL), and VBS trustlets (Isolated User Mode)—and what enforces their protection or creation constraints. - Useful for red teamers, malware developers, and defenders who need accurate mental models for process spawning, token usage, and why certain processes resist inspection/injection.
Technical Focus: - Win32 process creation APIs vs native syscalls (`CreateProcess*`, `CreateProcessInternal`, `NtCreateUserProcess`) - Token-based process creation and privilege requirements (SeImpersonate, SeAssignPrimaryToken, SeIncreaseQuota) - Process creation attributes (`STARTUPINFOEX`, `UpdateProcThreadAttributes`, package full name) - Windows process/kernel structures (`EPROCESS`, `KPROCESS`, `ETHREAD`, `PEB`, `CSR_PROCESS`, `W32PROCESS`) - Protected Process / Protected Process Light (PPL) signing/EKU enforcement and access-right restrictions - Minimal/Pico processes and Pico provider model (`PsRegisterPicoProvider`, `PspCreatePicoProcess`), VBS trustlets (IUM/VTL1)
Use Cases: - Implementing or instrumenting custom process creation (suspended start, extended attributes, token-based spawning) - Debugging/detecting process creation flows and correlating user-mode API calls to kernel syscalls - Understanding why PP/PPL targets block handle access, injection, or termination and what rights remain - Building kernel/user telemetry around process lifecycle (notify routines, EPROCESS/PEB inspection) - Researching WSL Pico processes and VBS trustlets for attack surface or defensive monitoring
Keywords: Windows process creation, CreateProcess, CreateProcessAsUser, CreateProcessWithTokenW, CreateProcessWithLogonW, ShellExecuteEx, CreateProcessInternal, NtCreateUserProcess, RtlCreateUserProcess, NtCreateProcessEx, STARTUPINFOEX, UpdateProcThreadAttributes, PROC_THREAD_ATTRIBUTE_PACKAGE_FULL_NAME, access token, SeImpersonatePrivilege, SeAssignPrimaryTokenPrivilege, SeIncreaseQuotaPrivilege, EPROCESS, KPROCESS, ETHREAD, PEB, CSRSS, CSR_PROCESS, W32PROCESS, PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutineEx, Protected Process, PPL, Code Integrity, EKU OID, Pico process, PsRegisterPicoProvider, WSL, Isolated User Mode, trustlet, VTL1, IApplicationActivationManager

entry.title: Living off the Process
entry.summary:
Title: Living off the Process (LOTP) – Indirect Shellcode Writing via In-Process ROP + Thread Hijacking
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post describes a Windows x64 injection technique the author calls “Living off the Process,” aiming to minimize common EDR-detected artifacts (e.g., heavy WriteProcessMemory usage, new RWX allocations). - The core idea is to reuse what already exists inside a target process: pre-existing RWX memory regions, existing ROP gadgets in loaded modules, and an existing thread to hijack. - Shellcode is written indirectly into the remote process in 8-byte chunks by chaining a gadget like `mov [rax], r8; ret`, with small per-chunk assembly stubs that load `R8` (data) and `RAX` (destination) rather than calling WriteProcessMemory for the payload itself. - The operator builds a ROP chain and “fake stack,” then uses `SuspendThread`/`GetThreadContext`/`SetThreadContext` to redirect `RIP` to the first stub and `RSP` to the ROP chain, finally pivoting with `pop rsp; ret` to execute the written shellcode. - It includes practical code for gadget discovery (module enumeration + `ReadProcessMemory` scanning), RWX region discovery (`VirtualQueryEx`), and thread selection (`CreateToolhelp32Snapshot`). - Useful for red teamers and offensive researchers exploring lower-noise process injection tradeoffs and how ROP-based memory writes can replace direct remote-memory writes.
Technical Focus: - ROP gadget discovery in remote processes (module enumeration + byte-pattern search) - Indirect remote memory writing via gadgets (`mov [reg], reg; ret`) and per-chunk stubs - Thread hijacking and context manipulation (`SuspendThread`, `GetThreadContext`, `SetThreadContext`) - RWX memory region discovery and reuse (`VirtualQueryEx`, PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE) - Stack pivoting / fake stack setup (`pop rsp; ret`) - Windows x64 assembly/machine-code stub generation
Use Cases: - Stealthier shellcode injection PoCs avoiding typical `VirtualAllocEx` + bulk `WriteProcessMemory` patterns - Researching EDR telemetry gaps around thread-context hijacking and ROP-based writes - Building tradecraft for “process-resident” execution using existing executable regions and gadgets - Developing detections around suspicious `SetThreadContext` usage, stack pivots, and anomalous RWX region reuse
Keywords: Windows 11, process injection, ROP chain, ROP gadgets, thread hijacking, SetThreadContext, GetThreadContext, SuspendThread, ResumeThread, VirtualQueryEx, ReadProcessMemory, WriteProcessMemory, EnumProcessModulesEx, PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE, RWX memory, stack pivot, pop rsp ret, x64dbg, shellcode, code stubs

entry.title: long live the shellcode
entry.summary:
Title: Long Live the Shellcode — Demystifying AV/EDR Evasion (sRDI, Reflective Loading, Meterpreter)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post explains why “shellcode” generated by many C2 frameworks (and Meterpreter) is often not traditional PIC shellcode, but a reflectively-loaded DLL packaged to behave like shellcode via sRDI. - It walks through debugging a common VirtualAlloc/RtlCopyMemory/CreateThread loader and highlights that additional memory allocations come from the payload itself because it performs reflective loading in-memory. - The author breaks down Reflective DLL Injection (ReflectiveLoader responsibilities: relocations, imports, section mapping, entrypoint) and how sRDI adds a small stub so execution can start at the buffer base without computing the loader offset externally. - Meterpreter is used as a concrete example: its “shellcode” contains a hotpatched DOS header stub that jumps to ReflectiveLoader at a baked-in RVA. - The key operational takeaway is that EDR detections often focus on memory allocation and “unbacked” executable regions, and these behaviors may be driven by the reflective loader inside the payload, not just the external loader. - Useful for red teamers/pentesters troubleshooting payload detections and for defenders understanding what telemetry to expect from reflective-loading implants.
Technical Focus: - Reflective DLL Injection (Stephen Fewer ReflectiveLoader) - Shellcode Reflective DLL Injection (sRDI) stubs and RVA-based jumps - Windows memory allocation/execution patterns (VirtualAlloc/NtAllocateVirtualMemory, RWX, unbacked memory) - Meterpreter payload internals (reflective DLL + hotpatched DOS header) - Debugging shellcode/implants to attribute memory behavior to loader vs payload - EDR detection concepts around module-backed vs non-module-backed memory
Use Cases: - Diagnose why a “simple” shellcode loader still triggers EDR due to payload self-allocation/reflective mapping - Modify/replace reflective loaders (e.g., Cobalt Strike UDRL, Havoc) to change allocation/protection patterns - Build better implant telemetry expectations during dynamic analysis (breakpoints on NtAllocateVirtualMemory, etc.) - Create detection logic focused on reflective loading artifacts (unbacked RX regions, reflective import resolution patterns)
Keywords: sRDI, Reflective DLL Injection, ReflectiveLoader, Meterpreter, msfvenom, CreateThread, VirtualAlloc, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, RtlCopyMemory, LoadLibraryA, VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, CreateRemoteThread, unbacked memory, module-backed memory, EDR evasion, Cobalt Strike UDRL, Havoc C2, PIC payload, DOS header hotpatching

entry.title: memory lane
entry.summary:
Title: Memory Lane
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post revisits older “simple shellcode loader” AV-evasion techniques and shows why they no longer reliably bypass modern Windows Defender. - The author reproduces classic msfvenom + VirtualAlloc/RtlMoveMemory/RWX execution loaders, then uses tooling (Ghidra, gocheck/MpCmdRun) to identify what Defender flags (often the embedded shellcode and later behavioral patterns). - It demonstrates incremental changes: XOR-encrypting shellcode to reduce static signatures, then shifting execution from direct function-pointer invocation to an EarlyBird APC injection flow (CreateProcess suspended → VirtualAllocEx/WriteProcessMemory → VirtualProtectEx → QueueUserAPC → ResumeThread) to evade common behavioral detections. - The post highlights pitfalls of relying on VirusTotal: cloud/sandbox verdicts can turn into hash-based detections that “nuke” the whole file rather than specific bytes. - It introduces guardrails (e.g., hostname checks) as a basic sandbox-evasion/scoping mechanism and discusses why memory scanners still catch unencrypted payloads during post-exploitation. - Useful for red teamers and malware/tooling developers who need a realistic view of Defender’s current detection surface (static, behavioral, cloud) and practical loader tradeoffs.
Technical Focus: - Windows Defender static vs behavioral vs cloud (hash) detections - Shellcode loader patterns (RWX allocation, memcpy, direct execution) - XOR shellcode encryption and string/signature avoidance - EarlyBird APC injection (suspended process + QueueUserAPC) - Sandbox/VT operational security and guardrails - Reverse engineering triage with Ghidra + MpCmdRun/gocheck
Use Cases: - Updating legacy shellcode loader tutorials to work against modern Defender - Building stageless loaders with reduced static indicators (encrypted payloads) - Switching to alternative execution primitives (APC) to avoid common heuristics - Testing and isolating Defender detections locally (MpCmdRun chunking) without leaking samples to VT - Adding environment guardrails to reduce sandbox detonation and scope execution
Keywords: Windows Defender, MpCmdRun.exe, gocheck, Ghidra, msfvenom, shellcode loader, VirtualAlloc, VirtualAllocEx, RtlMoveMemory, WriteProcessMemory, VirtualProtectEx, PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE, RWX, EarlyBird APC, QueueUserAPC, CreateProcessA, CREATE_SUSPENDED, process injection, behavioral detection, cloud protection, VirusTotal, YARA, Sigma, sandbox evasion, guardrails, hostname check, memory scanning, sleep mask, Ekko

entry.title: T 1
entry.summary:
Title: T-1: Intelligent VM Detection with DTC
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - T-1 is a Windows-focused C++ shellcode loader that gates execution using a simple machine-learning–derived virtual machine (VM) detection heuristic. - It trains a Decision Tree Classifier (DTC) in Python (scikit-learn) using system telemetry—primarily “process count per user”—then ports the learned decision logic into C++ conditionals (`VmDetection.cxx`). - At runtime, the program enumerates processes/users (via snapshot-based APIs) to compute the feature and decides whether it is likely bare metal or virtualized. - If a VM is detected, it can self-delete; otherwise it proceeds to execute shellcode (including an “inlined” execution path). - This is useful for red teamers and malware researchers exploring sandbox/VM evasion tradeoffs and how to operationalize ML-derived rules without shipping an ML runtime. - It’s interesting as a minimal example of converting a trained model into static branching logic suitable for small loaders.
Technical Focus: - VM/sandbox detection via behavioral host telemetry (processes-per-user) - Decision Tree Classifier training and rule extraction (scikit-learn) - Windows process/user enumeration via Toolhelp snapshots - Shellcode loading/execution techniques (inlined execution) - Self-deletion / on-disk artifact removal - C++/Python pipeline for model-to-code translation
Use Cases: - Add VM/sandbox gating to a loader before payload execution - Experiment with ML-derived heuristics for environment classification - Demonstrate “model export” into static C/C++ logic for OPSEC/size reasons - Research detection opportunities around process enumeration + self-delete behaviors - Build lab PoCs for comparing heuristic vs. ML-assisted evasion
Keywords: VM detection, sandbox evasion, shellcode loader, DecisionTreeClassifier, scikit-learn, rule extraction, model-to-code, Windows API, Toolhelp32Snapshot, process enumeration, user enumeration, process_count_per_user, VmDetection, in-memory execution, inlined shellcode execution, self-deletion, anti-analysis, C++, Python, MinGW Makefile

entry.title: rdll rs

Technical Focus: - Reflective DLL generation (stomp vs prepend/sRDI loader compatibility) - Rust Windows development (cdylib, exports, DllMain lifecycle) - Windows API integration via FFI (Rust ↔ C bridge) - pe_to_shellcode / PE-to-shellcode conversion pipeline - Cobalt Strike Aggressor scripting (bdllinject, CommandBuilder) and named pipes - Build automation with Cargo + `xtask` + CMake submodules
Use Cases: - Build a reflective DLL payload in Rust for in-memory injection workflows - Rapidly test DLL logic as an EXE before deploying as a DLL/reflective DLL - Integrate custom Rust capabilities (crates) into a DLL-based implant module - Send execution output from an injected reflective DLL back to Beacon via named pipes - Adapt artifacts for different reflective loaders (legacy stomp vs modern prepend)
Keywords: Rust, reflective DLL, Windows, DllMain, FFI, cdylib, Cargo, xtask, pe_to_shellcode, pe2shc, sRDI, stomp loader, in-memory loading, DLL injection, Cobalt Strike, Aggressor Script, Beacon, named pipe, bdllinject, CommandBuilder

entry.title: bypassEDR AV
entry.summary:
Title: ASP4RUX/bypassEDR-AV (Bypass EDR / AV)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository contains a Go-based Windows shellcode loader that embeds payloads in the PE resource section and executes them in-memory. - It extracts hex-encoded shellcode from a custom manifest/resource blob (delimited by `#!` and `$!`), decodes it, allocates RWX memory, writes the payload, and starts execution via a new thread. - The loader uses a mix of Win32 resource APIs (e.g., `FindResourceW`, `LoadResource`) and direct NT syscalls (`NtAllocateVirtualMemory`, `NtWriteVirtualMemory`, `NtCreateThreadEx`, `NtWaitForSingleObject`) to reduce reliance on higher-level APIs commonly monitored by EDR. - It includes a simple hash/XOR + SHA-1 based identifier scheme for syscall-related strings/IDs (basic obfuscation). - The workflow documents generating shellcode (e.g., msfvenom), embedding it using `rsrc` (manifest resource), and compiling a GUI-mode binary. - Useful primarily for red teamers/pentesters and malware researchers studying resource-based payload staging and syscall-based injection patterns.
Technical Focus: - PE resource section payload storage (manifest/resource type usage) - Win32 resource extraction APIs (FindResourceW/LoadResource) - Hex decoding and in-memory shellcode staging - Direct NT syscalls for memory allocation/write/thread creation - Basic string/hash obfuscation (XOR + SHA-1) - Thread-based shellcode execution and synchronization (NtWaitForSingleObject)
Use Cases: - Building a Windows in-memory shellcode loader with payloads stored in resources - Testing EDR detections around resource extraction + RWX allocation + thread start - Researching syscall-based injection tradeoffs vs. Win32 API usage - Creating PoCs for defensive validation (YARA/EDR rules on resource-backed loaders)
Keywords: Go, Windows, shellcode loader, PE resources, manifest, rsrc, FindResourceW, LoadResource, MAKEINTRESOURCE, in-memory execution, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, NtWriteVirtualMemory, NtCreateThreadEx, NtWaitForSingleObject, RWX, PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE, MEM_COMMIT, MEM_RESERVE, syscall, SHA-1, XOR obfuscation, msfvenom, Meterpreter reverse_tcp

entry.title: havoc obfuscator
entry.summary:
Title: havoc-obfuscator
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - A shell script that automates rebranding/obfuscation changes to the Havoc C2 framework to reduce static detections and make network/service artifacts look less “Havoc-like.” - It focuses on modifying HTTP behavior and UI/command naming to evade AV/EDR signatures and C2 hunting based on default indicators. - Techniques include IIS 8.5 impersonation (including removing the `X-Havoc: True` header), serving a fake IIS-style page instead of a 404/empty response, and refactoring/renaming built-in command strings (e.g., `Shell` → `MiniMice`, `DotRunner` → `miniMiceDot`) across teamserver, client, and payloads. - It also documents operational notes like using malleable profiles (e.g., windows-update), avoiding default ports, and using redirectors/proxies to mitigate fingerprinting (e.g., JARM). - Useful primarily for red teams/pentesters running Havoc infrastructure and for defenders to understand common “rebrand and blend-in” modifications applied to open-source C2s.
Technical Focus: - Havoc C2 codebase refactoring and string/command renaming - HTTP header manipulation and server fingerprint impersonation (IIS 8.5) - Handling redirect/404 behavior to mimic legitimate web servers - Malleable C2 profiles (YAOTL) and traffic shaping - Fingerprinting evasion considerations (JARM, certificates, redirectors)
Use Cases: - Rebrand Havoc deployments to reduce default IoCs and signature hits - Make Havoc teamserver responses resemble Microsoft IIS behavior - Patch/adjust Havoc’s redirect handling to avoid anomalous 404/zero-length responses - Standardize renamed operator commands across client/teamserver/payloads - Prepare C2 infrastructure for use behind redirectors/proxies
Keywords: Havoc C2, obfuscation, rebranding, AV evasion, EDR evasion, C2 hunting, IIS 8.5 impersonation, HTTP headers, X-Havoc header, 301 redirect, 404 handling, fake IIS page, YAOTL, malleable C2 profile, windows-update profile, JARM fingerprinting, redirector, proxy, Go teamserver, CMake client build

entry.title: sharem
entry.summary:
Title: SHAREM (Shellcode Analysis Framework)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - SHAREM is a Windows-focused shellcode analysis framework that combines emulation and a custom disassembler to analyze and deobfuscate shellcode without traditional debugging. - It emulates 20,000+ WinAPI functions and “virtually all” user-mode Windows syscalls, logging behavior and categorizing API/syscall activity to aid triage and reverse engineering. - A key capability is letting encoded shellcode deobfuscate itself during emulation, then presenting the decoded/discovered code directly in the disassembly view. - It supports “timeless debugging” (execution history) and “complete code coverage” to explore alternate paths and identify unreachable or hidden functionality. - The tool can enrich disassembly using emulation-derived context (e.g., discovered functions, structure decoding, registry extraction), and can optionally simulate or perform limited network actions (e.g., URLDownloadToFileA into memory). - Useful primarily for malware analysts and reverse engineers, but also valuable for red teams/pentesters validating payload behavior and for blue teams building detections from observed API/syscall traces. - Presented at DEFCON 31 and Black Hat Arsenal, with a companion Ghidra script (separate repo) that ingests SHAREM JSON output for Ghidra workflows.
Technical Focus: - Windows shellcode emulation (WinAPI + user-mode syscalls) - Automated deobfuscation via emulation (self-decoding shellcode) - Custom disassembly with emulation-assisted annotation - Timeless debugging / execution tracing (incl. optional stack snapshots) - Complete code coverage / path exploration - JSON output integration (e.g., Ghidra ingestion), structure/registry extraction
Use Cases: - Deobfuscate and analyze encoded Windows shellcode samples safely - Produce API/syscall traces for behavioral understanding and detection engineering - Identify hidden branches/unreachable logic via code-coverage-style exploration - Enrich reverse engineering in Ghidra using SHAREM-generated JSON artifacts - Validate syscall-based techniques across Windows versions/builds (e.g., SSN/OS-build dependent behavior)
Keywords: SHAREM, shellcode analysis, Windows emulation, WinAPI emulation, Windows syscalls, user-mode syscalls, deobfuscation, self-decoding shellcode, disassembler, timeless debugging, execution trace, stack tracing, complete code coverage, registry extraction, URLDownloadToFileA, DLL harvesting, syscall numbers (SSN), Windows 7, Windows 10, Windows 11, Ghidra script, JSON output, Python, ShellWasp

entry.title: ApexLdr
entry.summary:
Title: ApexLdr (DLL Payload Loader)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): ApexLdr is a Windows DLL-based shellcode loader written in C, designed to be executed via DLL sideloading (example target: `pythonw.exe` loading a fake `python310.dll`). It stages shellcode over HTTP/S and executes it using the Windows Threadpool API rather than creating a straightforward remote thread, aiming to reduce common behavioral detections. The project includes multiple evasion-oriented implementation details such as indirect syscalls (SysWhispers3 with randomized jumpers), DLL unhooking, API hashing, IAT camouflage, and delay via “API hammering.” It is CRT-independent to reduce dependencies and potentially simplify deployment/OPSEC. The repo documents basic build/usage and shows testing with Havoc C2 and Windows Defender. It’s primarily useful for red teamers, pentesters, and malware developers studying loader tradecraft and common EDR/AV friction points.
Technical Focus: - DLL sideloading and export proxying - Shellcode staging over HTTP/S - Indirect syscalls (SysWhispers3, jumper_randomized) - Threadpool-based execution (TP work items/timers) - User-mode unhooking of ntdll / API unhooking - API hashing, IAT camouflage, and execution delay techniques
Use Cases: - Build a sideloadable DLL loader for initial execution via trusted binaries - Stage and run C2 shellcode (e.g., Havoc) with less direct CreateThread-style behavior - Experiment with syscall-based evasion vs. user-mode API hooking - Research/benchmark defensive detections for threadpool execution and unhooking - Adapt the loader to other sideload targets by changing the exported entrypoint and proxying exports
Keywords: DLL sideloading, shellcode loader, HTTP staging, HTTPS, Threadpool API, TP_WORK, SysWhispers3, indirect syscalls, jumper_randomized, ntdll unhooking, user-mode hooks, IAT camouflage, API hashing, CRT-independent, execution delay, API hammering, Havoc C2, Windows Defender, SigThief, export proxying

entry.title: CallStackMasker
entry.summary:
Title: CallStackMasker — Dynamically spoofing/masking call stacks with timers (PoC)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - CallStackMasker is a C++ proof-of-concept that dynamically overwrites a thread’s call stack during “sleep” and restores it before execution resumes, aiming to defeat call-stack-based telemetry and analysis. - It uses queued timers to run stack-manipulation routines while the implant thread is in a waiting state, analogous to sleep obfuscation techniques that hide memory during idle periods. - The default mode mimics a static call stack pattern taken from `spoolsv.exe`; a `--dynamic` mode enumerates accessible threads, finds one in a target wait state (e.g., `WaitForSingleObjectEx`), and clones its call stack and start address. - The repo references prior art (Ekko sleep obfuscation, WithSecureLabs CallStackSpoofer/TickTock) and thread enumeration examples (Hunt-Sleeping-Beacons). - Useful for red teamers and malware developers researching evasion against EDR stack-walking and “sleeping beacon” hunting; also relevant to blue teams for understanding and detecting stack spoofing tradecraft. - Tested only on Windows 22H2 (19045.2486), with potential fragility across builds due to reliance on low-level behavior.
Technical Focus: - Call stack spoofing / stack frame manipulation - Timer-based execution (queued timers) during thread sleep - Thread enumeration and selecting target wait states - Mimicking benign process/thread call stacks (e.g., `spoolsv.exe`) - Sleep obfuscation tradecraft (Ekko-inspired) - EDR evasion against stack-walking telemetry
Use Cases: - Implementing or prototyping call-stack masking for sleeping implants/beacons - Evaluating how EDRs attribute suspicious activity via call stacks - Building detections for anomalous timer usage + stack inconsistencies - Researching robustness of stack spoofing across Windows versions/builds
Keywords: call stack spoofing, call stack masking, timers, queued timers, sleep obfuscation, Ekko, WaitForSingleObjectEx, thread enumeration, stack walking, EDR evasion, spoolsv.exe, start address spoofing, Windows internals, WithSecureLabs CallStackSpoofer, TickTock, Hunt-Sleeping-Beacons, C++, implant sleep, telemetry evasion

entry.title: Ekko
entry.summary:
Title: Ekko — Sleep Obfuscation via CreateTimerQueueTimer
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Ekko is a C proof-of-concept implementing a Windows “sleep obfuscation” technique using the Win32 `CreateTimerQueueTimer` API. - It aims to reduce detection during beacon/implant sleep by avoiding straightforward `Sleep()` patterns and leveraging timer-queue callbacks to manage delayed execution. - The repo is positioned as a minimal reference implementation inspired by techniques observed in MDSec’s Nighthawk and research credited to Peter Winter-Smith and Austin Hudson’s write-up. - The author explicitly notes the implementation has known flaws and warns against copy/pasting into real implants without understanding and fixing issues. - It’s primarily useful for red teamers, implant developers, and defenders studying modern evasion tradecraft and how timer-based sleeping can appear in telemetry. - Interesting because it demonstrates a commonly discussed but often poorly implemented evasion primitive in a small, auditable codebase.
Technical Focus: - Win32 Timer Queue Timers (`CreateTimerQueueTimer`) - Sleep obfuscation / delayed execution patterns - Callback-based execution flow - EDR/behavioral detection evasion primitives - Windows user-mode tradecraft (implant sleep strategy)
Use Cases: - Implementing or experimenting with alternative sleep mechanisms in Windows implants (research/PoC) - Building detections for timer-queue-based delayed execution and anomalous callback behavior - Training and code review exercises on common pitfalls in sleep obfuscation implementations - Comparing `Sleep()` vs timer/callback approaches in endpoint telemetry
Keywords: Ekko, sleep obfuscation, CreateTimerQueueTimer, timer queue, Win32 API, delayed execution, callback, Windows internals, user-mode evasion, EDR bypass, implant tradecraft, beacon sleep, MDSec Nighthawk, Peter Winter-Smith, Austin Hudson, suspicious.actor, proof of concept, C, behavioral detection

entry.title: LdrLibraryEx
entry.summary:
Title: LdrLibraryEx — Minimal x64 in-memory DLL loader (ntdll-only)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): LdrLibraryEx is a small x64 library that manually maps PE/DLL images either from disk or from an in-memory buffer, aiming to emulate key parts of the Windows loader with minimal dependencies (only ntdll). It supports common loader features such as delayed imports, TLS callbacks, SEH, and API set resolution (Win10+). A notable option maps images into private (unbacked) memory to bypass kernel image-load notification callbacks (e.g., PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine), which some EDRs use for telemetry. The project exposes a simple `LdrLibrary` API plus an extended `LdrLibraryEx` context that allows hooking/overriding loader-related routines to alter mapping behavior. It’s primarily useful for red teamers, malware developers, and researchers studying Windows loader internals and manual mapping tradeoffs. The author notes it’s optimized for x86_64 MinGW and may not compile under Visual Studio, and it’s archived/read-only.
Technical Focus: - Manual PE mapping / reflective DLL loading (x64) - NTDLL-based loader primitives and minimal WinAPI usage - API Set schema resolution (api-ms-win-*.dll) - Bypassing image-load callbacks via private/unbacked memory mapping - Handling PE features: delayed imports, TLS, SEH, entrypoint control - Loader behavior customization via hookable context (LdrLibraryEx)
Use Cases: - In-memory loading of tooling modules without standard `LoadLibrary` paths - Researching/replicating Windows loader behavior for PE mapping - Evasion experiments against telemetry based on image-load notifications - Building custom loaders for implants or post-exploitation frameworks - Testing defensive detections around manual mapping and private mappings
Keywords: manual mapping, reflective loader, PE loader, DLL in-memory load, ntdll, LdrLoadDll, API set, api-ms-win, PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine, image load callbacks, private memory mapping, unbacked memory, TLS callbacks, delayed imports, SEH, entrypoint suppression, x64, MinGW, Windows internals, EDR telemetry

entry.title: earlycascade injection
entry.summary:
Title: Early Cascade Injection PoC (Cracked5pider)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository contains a proof-of-concept implementation of “Early Cascade Injection,” a Windows process-creation-time injection technique described by Outflank. - The PoC focuses on achieving stealthy code execution early in a target process’ lifetime by leveraging internals involved in Windows shims/AppCompat-style loading behavior. - It hardcodes internal offsets/pointers (notably `g_ShimsEnabled` and `g_pfnSE_DllLoaded`) rather than dynamically resolving them, making it version-specific and fragile across builds. - The author notes testing on Windows 10 build `10.0.22631.4317` and credits Outflank’s write-up plus related EDR preload research (MalwareTech). - It’s primarily useful for red teamers, exploit devs, and security researchers studying early-process injection tradecraft and potential EDR blind spots during process initialization. - The key value is a minimal, readable codebase that operationalizes the blog-described technique for experimentation and detection engineering.
Technical Focus: - Windows process creation internals and early-stage injection timing - AppCompat/Shim engine internals (shim enablement and DLL-load callbacks) - Hardcoded symbol/offset usage across Windows builds - Stealthy user-mode injection tradeoffs vs. EDR visibility windows - PoC implementation in C++ (with supporting build artifacts)
Use Cases: - Reproducing and validating Outflank’s Early Cascade Injection technique in a lab - Testing EDR/telemetry coverage during process initialization and shim-related loading - Building detections around unusual shim-engine state changes or early DLL load callbacks - Comparing early injection approaches (e.g., “EDR preload” concepts) against modern defenses
Keywords: Early Cascade Injection, Outflank, Windows process creation, AppCompat, Shim Engine, shims, g_ShimsEnabled, g_pfnSE_DllLoaded, DLL injection, early-stage injection, user-mode injection, EDR evasion, telemetry gaps, Windows internals, Windows 10 22631.4317, MalwareTech, EDR preload, PoC, C++

entry.title: shellcode tester pro
entry.summary:
Title: Shellcode Tester Pro
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Shellcode Tester Pro is a Python/PyQt5 GUI for analyzing, emulating, and reverse engineering shellcode samples. - It addresses the need to safely inspect shellcode behavior without executing it on a live system by using Unicorn Engine for CPU emulation and Capstone for disassembly. - The tool supports importing shellcode in multiple formats (escaped `\x`, raw hex, and binary files like `.bin/.exe/.elf`) and can extract shellcode from binaries. - It includes heuristic checks and fingerprinting to identify common payload/tooling origins (e.g., msfvenom, Cobalt Strike, Sliver, Empire), plus detection of NOP sleds, encryption, and obfuscation patterns. - It provides basic deobfuscation (notably XOR), memory dumping, and report export (PDF), and can run simulated execution in an external terminal. - A plugin system allows extending analysis and automation, making it useful for malware analysts, reverse engineers, and red teamers validating payloads.
Technical Focus: - Shellcode emulation (Unicorn Engine) - Disassembly and instruction analysis (Capstone) - Shellcode extraction from PE/ELF/binary blobs - Heuristic detection and tool fingerprinting (msfvenom/C2 frameworks) - Obfuscation/encryption detection and XOR deobfuscation - Extensible plugin architecture (Python)
Use Cases: - Triage unknown shellcode safely via emulation and disassembly - Identify likely shellcode generator/C2 framework from fingerprints - Extract embedded shellcode from executables for deeper RE - Detect and peel simple XOR-obfuscated payloads; dump decoded memory - Generate quick analysis artifacts (disassembly/report) for case notes - Extend analysis workflows with custom plugins (e.g., YARA-like checks, IOC extraction)
Keywords: shellcode, Unicorn Engine, Capstone, emulation, disassembly, reverse engineering, malware analysis, PyQt5, msfvenom, Cobalt Strike, Sliver, Empire, NOP sled, XOR deobfuscation, obfuscation detection, memory dump, PE, ELF, plugin system, heuristic analysis

entry.title: Astral PE
entry.summary:
Title: Astral-PE — Low-level PE header & entry-point mutator for native Windows binaries
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Astral-PE is a post-build mutator for native Windows PE files (.exe/.dll/.sys) that rewrites PE structural metadata and lightly obfuscates the entry point without packing, encrypting, injecting, or performing runtime code obfuscation. It targets “static structure” indicators used by unpackers, YARA rules, and sandbox clustering by clearing or altering fields like timestamps, Rich header, section names, debug directories/PDB paths, data directories, and import/export naming. It can also remove unused TLS/load-config/relocations, patch DOS stub fields, and adjust flags/versions to reduce toolchain fingerprinting while keeping execution intact. The tool is positioned as a pipeline step to apply after (and optionally before/after) packers/protectors, including signed-binary considerations (e.g., overlay stripping only if signed). It’s useful for red teams, malware developers, and reverse-engineering challenge authors who want to disrupt static analysis heuristics and signature-based detection that relies on stable PE metadata. It’s interesting because it focuses on “surgical” PE compliance-safe mutations rather than traditional packing, aiming to break assumptions in automated tooling.
Technical Focus: - PE/COFF header and Optional Header mutation (TimeDateStamp, checksum, flags, subsystem versions) - Rich Header removal and toolchain fingerprint disruption - Debug Directory/PDB path stripping and metadata sanitization - Import table name mutation and export table fabrication - Entry point (AddressOfEntryPoint) patching/shuffling - Data directory cleanup and section permission/characteristics changes
Use Cases: - Post-process packed/protected payloads to break static unpacker logic and YARA signatures - Reduce toolchain and build-environment attribution (Rich header, timestamps, PDB paths) - Disrupt sandbox clustering and heuristic PE classifiers relying on stable metadata - Create “structurally weird” binaries for crackmes or RE training - Integrate into CI/CD build pipelines before signing and distribution
Keywords: Windows PE, PE/COFF, header obfuscation, metadata mutation, Rich Header, TimeDateStamp, Debug Directory, PDB path, Import Address Table, import name mutation, Export Table, AddressOfEntryPoint, DOS stub, e_lfanew, TLS directory, Load Config, relocations, section characteristics, static analysis evasion, YARA bypass

entry.title: amber
entry.summary:
Title: Amber – Reflective PE Packer / Position-Independent PE Loader
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Amber is a reflective (position-independent) PE loader/packer that converts native Windows PE files (EXE/DLL/SYS) into payloads intended for in-memory execution. It focuses on stealthy deployment by encoding the generated loader/payload (via the SGN encoder), resolving APIs in less obvious ways (CRC32_API or IAT_API), and wiping the reflective payload from memory after execution to reduce scanner visibility. The loader supports features like TLS callbacks and forwarded imports, and newer versions reduce reliance on high-signal Win32 APIs by switching to NTDLL functions; an experimental raw-syscall mode is available for x64. Payloads can be staged remotely or executed directly like shellcode. This is primarily useful for red teams, penetration testers, and malware researchers building custom in-memory loaders and studying PE reflective loading tradecraft. It’s interesting because it combines PE loading correctness (imports/TLS/forwarders) with multiple evasion-oriented implementation choices (encoding, API resolution strategy, memory wiping, syscall option).
Technical Focus: - Reflective PE loading (PIC loader for EXE/DLL/SYS) - In-memory execution and post-load payload wiping - API resolution via hashing (CRC32) and IAT-based resolving - SGN multi-pass encoding/obfuscation of payloads - NTDLL-based loading and experimental raw syscalls (x64) - Handling TLS callbacks and forwarded imports
Use Cases: - Generate reflective payloads for in-memory execution during red team ops - Build custom loaders for EXE/DLL deployment without dropping to disk - Experiment with API-resolution tradeoffs (hashed vs IAT-based) - Research/detect reflective loading behaviors (imports/TLS/forwarders, memory wiping) - Create staged payload delivery workflows (remote fetch + in-memory run)
Keywords: reflective loader, PE, EXE, DLL, SYS, in-memory execution, position-independent code, SGN encoder, payload encoding, CRC32 API hashing, IAT resolver, NTDLL, raw syscalls, TLS callbacks, forwarded imports, shellcode-like payload, memory wiping, packer, crypter, Windows internals

entry.title: iat api
entry.summary:
Title: IAT_API (Import Address Table API resolver/caller for shellcode)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository provides x86/x64 assembly “block” code that locates and calls Windows API functions by parsing the running module’s Import Address Table (IAT) rather than walking exports. - It resolves functions by iterating `_IMAGE_IMPORT_DESCRIPTOR` entries and matching a CRC32 hash of `(module name + function name)` against a caller-supplied value, then performs a direct `CALL` with provided arguments. - The approach is designed to evade certain exploit mitigations and detections that focus on Export Address Table (EAT) parsing (e.g., EMET EAF/EAF+) and “caller checks,” since it avoids reading KERNEL32/NTDLL exports and calls the resolved pointer directly. - It includes a Python helper to compute the CRC32 hashes and example MessageBox shellcode for both 32-bit and 64-bit. - It’s primarily useful for shellcode authors, exploit developers, and red team/malware tradecraft research where API resolution needs to be compact and mitigation-aware. - A key constraint is that the target API must already be imported by the host PE, otherwise resolution fails/crashes.
Technical Focus: - PE import parsing (`_IMAGE_IMPORT_DESCRIPTOR`, Import Table/IAT walking) - API hashing (CRC32 with polynomial 0x11EDC6F41; configurable rotation to avoid ROR13 signatures) - Shellcode-style position-independent assembly (x86/x64) - EMET mitigation bypass concepts (EAF/EAF+, caller checks) - Indirect API invocation via IAT function pointers
Use Cases: - Building shellcode that resolves APIs without EAT traversal - Researching/evading EAF/EAF+ style export-filtering mitigations - Creating hash-based API lookup schemes for payloads/loaders - Developing PoCs that must operate under exploit-mitigation constraints - Comparing IAT-based vs EAT-based API resolution tradeoffs in detection engineering
Keywords: IAT, Import Address Table, PE parsing, _IMAGE_IMPORT_DESCRIPTOR, shellcode, x86 assembly, x64 assembly, Windows API resolution, API hashing, CRC32, 0x11EDC6F41, EMET, EAF, EAF+, caller checks, KERNEL32, NTDLL, KERNELBASE, block_api

entry.title: LdrLockLiberator
entry.summary:
Title: LdrLockLiberator — Escaping Windows Loader Lock from DllMain
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): LdrLockLiberator is a C proof-of-concept/toolkit that provides techniques to execute meaningful code from `DllMain` despite Windows’ Loader Lock constraints. It targets the common problem where doing “real work” in `DllMain` deadlocks or breaks due to the loader holding internal locks and incomplete loader state. The repo includes approaches like forcibly fully unlocking the loader (setting loader events and toggling internal state such as `LdrpWorkInProgress`) and a safer “escape at exit” method using CRT `atexit` plus DLL pinning via `LdrAddRefDll(LDR_ADDREF_DLL_PIN)` to survive `FreeLibrary`. It was released alongside the “Perfect DLL Hijacking” article and includes sample DLL-hijack scenarios (e.g., hijacking `MpClient.dll` for `OfflineScannerShell.exe`) with a default `ShellExecute` payload. It’s primarily useful for red teamers, exploit developers, and malware researchers who need reliable execution when `DllMain` is the only available entrypoint, while also being relevant to defenders studying loader abuse patterns. The project emphasizes compatibility with modern mitigations by avoiding memory-protection changes and pointer patching where possible (x64-focused).
Technical Focus: - Windows Loader Lock / `DllMain` execution constraints and deadlock avoidance - NTDLL loader internals (loader events, `LdrpWorkInProgress`) - `LdrAddRefDll` and `LDR_ADDREF_DLL_PIN` (DLL pinning) - CRT `atexit` abuse for deferred execution outside loader lock - DLL hijacking / export forwarding considerations for static vs dynamic loads - x86-64 Windows native API linking (`ntdll.lib`), WDK build workflow
Use Cases: - Build DLL hijacks that safely run payload logic without `DllMain` deadlocks - Defer execution until process exit to bypass loader-lock restrictions - Research/validate loader-internal manipulation impacts and stability tradeoffs - Create PoCs for “perfect” DLL hijacking chains in real Windows binaries - Develop detection ideas around unusual loader-state changes or DLL pinning
Keywords: DllMain, Loader Lock, LdrLock, NTDLL, LdrAddRefDll, LDR_ADDREF_DLL_PIN, LdrpWorkInProgress, atexit, LoadLibrary, FreeLibrary, DLL hijacking, export forwarding, MpClient.dll, OfflineScannerShell.exe, ShellExecute, Windows loader internals, x64, WDK 7.1, ntdll.lib, CRT/MSVCRT

entry.title: operating system design review
entry.summary:
Title: Operating System Design Review: A systematic analysis of modern systems architecture
Type: Documentation (GitHub repository / long-form research write-up)
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository is a large research write-up comparing modern OS user-mode architecture, centered on loaders/linkers and concurrency, with deep reverse engineering of the Windows loader (NTDLL) and comparisons to glibc/ELF and some macOS behavior. - It documents Windows “parallel loader” internals (work queues, loader worker threads, events, locks) and module lifetime state transitions (LDR_DDAG_STATE), with traces, WinDbg workflows, and supporting code/data. - A major theme is how Windows’ DLL ecosystem (circular dependencies, delay/lazy loading, DLL search order) and loader-lock semantics create correctness, deadlock, and security liabilities (e.g., DllMain constraints, destructor ordering hazards, timing windows). - It also argues that Windows thread lifecycle practices (process exit via NtTerminateProcess, DLL thread notifications, TLS/FLS patterns) lead to unsafe teardown behavior (“process meltdown”) and hard-to-reason-about shutdown states. - Useful for reverse engineers, Windows internals researchers, exploit developers, and defenders who need to understand loader behavior, module initialization edge cases, and where architectural quirks create attack surface or reliability issues. - Interesting because it combines practical reversing artifacts (traces, pseudocode, experiments) with cross-OS design comparisons and concrete failure modes tied to real Windows primitives.
Technical Focus: - Windows loader internals (NTDLL Ldrp* functions, parallel loading, loader locks/events) - Module dependency graphs and state machines (LDR_DDAG_NODE / LDR_DDAG_STATE) - DLL initialization/finalization semantics (DllMain, constructors/destructors, TLS/FLS callbacks) - Delay loading / lazy loading mechanics and failure modes - Thread lifecycle and process teardown behavior (RtlExitUserProcess, NtTerminateProcess) - Cross-OS loader/linker comparison (PE vs ELF, LoadLibrary vs dlopen, symbol resolution)
Use Cases: - Reverse engineer Windows loader behavior for reliability/security research - Identify deadlock-prone patterns involving loader lock, COM apartments, and DllMain - Analyze DLL load/unload ordering bugs and lazy-load side effects in complex apps - Build or validate loader emulation/compat layers (Wine/ReactOS gaps, parallel loading) - Develop detections/hardening guidance around DLL search order and load-time injection risks - Support exploit research involving module loading timing, IAT patch windows, and teardown races
Keywords: Windows loader, NTDLL, LdrpQueueWork, LdrpWorkQueue, LdrpWorkQueueLock, LdrpLoadCompleteEvent, LdrpWorkCompleteEvent, LdrpProcessWork, LDR_DDAG_NODE, LDR_DDAG_STATE, PEB, TEB, loader lock, DllMain, DLL_PROCESS_ATTACH, DLL_PROCESS_DETACH, delay load, lazy loading, LoadLibrary, GetProcAddress, GetModuleHandle, dlopen, dlsym, glibc loader, ELF, TLS, FLS, COM, NtTerminateProcess, RtlExitUserProcess, DLL hijacking, WinSxS, activation contexts

entry.title: Detecting Indirect Syscalls
entry.summary:
Title: Detecting-Indirect-Syscalls (Indirect Syscall Detector)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This repository provides a Windows x64 user-mode detector for “indirect syscalls” used to bypass EDR hooks in ntdll by jumping directly to the `syscall` instruction. It sets hardware execution breakpoints (DR0–DR3) on selected `syscall` instructions inside ntdll and uses a Vectored Exception Handler (VEH) to intercept `EXCEPTION_SINGLE_STEP` when those instructions execute. On each hit, it inspects the return address on the stack (RSP) and checks whether it falls within a whitelist of trusted module address ranges (e.g., ntdll, optionally other system DLLs). If the return address points to untrusted memory (heap/stack/allocated regions), it flags the event as likely indirect-syscall activity. The tool instruments all threads by enumerating them and updating their debug registers via `NtGetContextThread`/`NtSetContextThread`, with a follow-up scan to catch new threads. It’s primarily useful for Blue Teams/EDR engineers and defenders researching detection of Hell’s Gate/SysWhispers-style syscall evasion, and for Red Teams to understand detection constraints and bypass conditions (e.g., executing within trusted module ranges/ROP).
Technical Focus: - Indirect syscall evasion (Hell’s Gate / SysWhispers patterns) - x64 hardware breakpoints via debug registers (DR0–DR3, DR7) - Vectored Exception Handling (VEH) and single-step exception flow - Return-address validation / call-site provenance checking - Thread enumeration and per-thread context modification (Nt* thread APIs) - Trusted module range construction via PE export parsing
Use Cases: - Detecting user-mode indirect syscall execution in a protected process - Building PoC telemetry for syscall-evasion research and validation - Evaluating EDR detection ideas based on call-site/return-address provenance - Red team testing of indirect-syscall tradecraft against breakpoint/VEH-based detectors - Extending trusted-module policies and measuring false positives/negatives
Keywords: indirect syscalls, ntdll.dll, syscall instruction, Hell’s Gate, SysWhispers, hardware breakpoints, DR0, DR1, DR2, DR3, DR7, Vectored Exception Handler, VEH, EXCEPTION_SINGLE_STEP, trap flag, EFLAGS.TF, return address validation, RSP, NtGetContextThread, NtSetContextThread, CreateToolhelp32Snapshot, NtOpenThread, NtSuspendThread, PE exports, trusted module ranges, Windows x64

entry.title: Ebyte Syscalls
entry.summary:
Title: Ebyte-Syscalls — VEH-Based Function Call Obfuscation
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository implements Windows function-call obfuscation and lightweight hooking by redirecting execution through Vectored Exception Handling (VEH) rather than explicit jumps/call patches. - It triggers exceptions intentionally (guard-page violations or INT3 breakpoints) and then rewrites the thread context (RIP/EIP) inside the VEH to transfer control to a redirect/hook target. - For guard-page hooks, it uses `PAGE_GUARD` via `NtProtectVirtualMemory` and relies on `STATUS_SINGLE_STEP` (trap flag) to reapply guard protections after Windows clears them. - For INT3 hooks, it byte-swaps the first instruction byte to `0xCC` and flushes the instruction cache with `NtFlushInstructionCache`. - The project emphasizes “byte switching” and in-place modification (no new executable memory allocations, no runtime assembly generation), and resolves Nt* exports via manual PE parsing instead of `GetProcAddress`. - Useful for red teams, malware developers, and researchers exploring exception-driven control-flow obfuscation and anti-analysis tradeoffs; defenders can also use it to understand detection opportunities (VEH enumeration, exception-rate anomalies).
Technical Focus: - Vectored Exception Handler (VEH) control-flow redirection - `PAGE_GUARD` / `STATUS_GUARD_PAGE_VIOLATION` + trap-flag `STATUS_SINGLE_STEP` re-arming - INT3 breakpoint hooking (`0xCC`) / `STATUS_BREAKPOINT` - Thread context manipulation (RIP/EIP, EFlags) - `NtProtectVirtualMemory` and `NtFlushInstructionCache` usage - Manual PE export parsing / dynamic API resolution
Use Cases: - Obfuscate sensitive function calls and indirect execution paths behind exception handling - Implement stealthier user-mode hooks without allocating trampolines/shellcode buffers - Prototype EDR-evasion concepts based on exception-driven control flow - Research/detect VEH-based hooking by monitoring exception patterns and handler registration
Keywords: Vectored Exception Handler, VEH, PAGE_GUARD, STATUS_GUARD_PAGE_VIOLATION, STATUS_SINGLE_STEP, trap flag, EFlags, STATUS_BREAKPOINT, INT3, 0xCC, thread context, RIP, EIP, NtProtectVirtualMemory, NtFlushInstructionCache, manual PE parsing, export resolution, user-mode hooking, control-flow obfuscation, byte patching

entry.title: Creation%20Of%20Process.md
entry.summary:
Title: Creation Of Process (Windows Internals)
Type: Documentation
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This write-up walks through how Windows creates a “normal” user-mode process, starting from a simple C++ `CreateProcessW` example and drilling down into the underlying call chain. - It explains key `CreateProcessW` parameters and shows how they appear in compiled code via static analysis (Cutter) and dynamic debugging (x64dbg). - The core technique is tracing the transition from `CreateProcessW` → `CreateProcessInternalW` (KernelBase) → `NtCreateUserProcess` (ntdll) → syscall into the kernel, clarifying where user-mode ends and kernel-mode begins. - It also introduces direct process creation using Native APIs (`NtCreateUserProcess`) and the required supporting structures (`RTL_USER_PROCESS_PARAMETERS`, `PS_CREATE_INFO`, `PS_ATTRIBUTE_LIST`). - Useful for red teamers/malware developers who need low-level process creation control (e.g., attributes/flags), and for defenders/researchers who want to understand telemetry points and API layering.
Technical Focus: - Win32 process creation (`CreateProcessW`) parameterization and behavior - API call chain: Kernel32/KernelBase → NTDLL → syscalls - Native process creation: `NtCreateUserProcess` - Process creation structures: `RTL_USER_PROCESS_PARAMETERS`, `PS_CREATE_INFO`, `PS_ATTRIBUTE_LIST`, `UNICODE_STRING` - Static and dynamic reverse engineering of process creation (Cutter, x64dbg) - Process/thread creation flags and attributes (PS attributes, access masks)
Use Cases: - Reverse engineer how a sample spawns child processes and what APIs it truly reaches - Build custom process creation code using NT APIs for finer-grained control - Understand where EDR/user-mode hooks may sit in the process creation stack - Implement/experiment with process creation attributes (e.g., parent process attribute concepts) - Debug and validate process creation flows at the assembly/syscall boundary
Keywords: Windows internals, process creation, CreateProcessW, CreateProcessInternalW, KernelBase.dll, kernel32.dll, ntdll.dll, NtCreateUserProcess, syscall, x64dbg, Cutter, static analysis, dynamic analysis, RTL_USER_PROCESS_PARAMETERS, RtlCreateProcessParametersEx, PS_CREATE_INFO, PS_ATTRIBUTE_LIST, UNICODE_STRING, ACCESS_MASK, PROCESS_ALL_ACCESS, THREAD_ALL_ACCESS, process creation flags, thread creation flags, PS_ATTRIBUTE_IMAGE_NAME

entry.title: Overview%20of%20process%20creation.md
entry.summary:
Title: Overview of Process Creation (Windows Internals)
Type: Documentation
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This write-up summarizes the Windows process creation flow from high-level Win32 APIs down to the native system call boundary. - It traces how `CreateProcess` (kernel32.dll) leads into the undocumented `CreateProcessInternal*` and then transitions via `NtCreateUserProcess` (ntdll.dll) into the kernel implementation. - It also covers alternate process creation APIs in advapi32 (`CreateProcessWithLogonW`, `CreateProcessWithTokenW`, `CreateProcessAsUser`) and explains how Secondary Logon (seclogon) and RPC are involved for “logon/token-based” creation paths. - The post highlights security relevance by mapping these APIs to common abuse patterns such as token manipulation and process injection, and notes required privileges (e.g., `SeAssignPrimaryToken`) for certain paths. - Useful for red teamers, malware analysts, and defenders who need to understand where telemetry can be collected (user-mode API, ntdll, kernel) and how different creation methods change artifacts and requirements.
Technical Focus: - Win32 process creation (`CreateProcess`) and call chain to native APIs - Undocumented internals (`CreateProcessInternal*`, kernel `NtCreateUserProcess`) - Native system call interface (`NtCreateUserProcess`, `PS_CREATE_INFO`, attribute lists) - Token/logon-based process creation (advapi32, seclogon, RPC) - Privileges and access tokens in process creation (`SeAssignPrimaryToken`) - MITRE ATT&CK mappings (T1055, T1134, T1543 references)
Use Cases: - Map process-creation telemetry points for EDR/detection engineering (API vs syscall vs service-mediated paths) - Choose appropriate process creation API for token-based execution (impersonation vs primary token scenarios) - Understand how Secondary Logon affects artifacts and required permissions - Support research into process injection/spawn techniques and their prerequisites - Build or audit tooling that uses `NtCreateUserProcess` directly for controlled process instantiation
Keywords: Windows internals, process creation, CreateProcess, CreateProcessInternal, NtCreateUserProcess, kernel32.dll, ntdll.dll, advapi32.dll, CreateProcessWithLogonW, CreateProcessWithTokenW, CreateProcessAsUser, seclogon, Secondary Logon service, RPC, access token, SeAssignPrimaryToken, svchost.exe, MITRE T1134, MITRE T1055, MITRE T1543

entry.title: RedirectThread
entry.summary:
Title: RedirectThread (CONTEXT-Only Attack Surface / Execution-Only Process Injection)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - RedirectThread is a Windows C++ research toolset exploring “execution-only” remote code execution primitives centered around manipulating a thread’s `CONTEXT` rather than the classic allocate→write→execute injection flow. - It demonstrates ways to trigger code execution in a remote process while minimizing or avoiding remote memory allocation and writes, including DLL loading via `LoadLibraryA` and calling arbitrary WinAPI with parameters using `SetThreadContext` (optionally without suspending the thread). - The repo includes multiple delivery methods (e.g., `CreateRemoteThread`, `NtCreateThread`, and several APC queueing variants) and context manipulation approaches (ROP-gadget based vs. a two-step hijack). - It also includes utilities to create alertable threads to support APC-based execution paths and test reliability. - This is primarily useful for red teamers, pentesters, and malware researchers evaluating alternative injection tradecraft and for blue teamers studying detection opportunities around thread-context tampering and APC usage.
Technical Focus: - Thread context manipulation (`CONTEXT`, `GetThreadContext`/`SetThreadContext`) - Execution-only injection primitives (skipping remote alloc/write where possible) - Remote thread creation via Win32 and NT APIs (`CreateRemoteThread`, `NtCreateThread`) - APC-based execution (`QueueUserAPC`, `NtQueueApcThread*`, alertable threads) - ROP gadget usage for controlled execution redirection - ETW-related thread creation “noise” experimentation (project folder indicates focus)
Use Cases: - Evaluate stealthier alternatives to common process injection patterns in Windows engagements - Prototype and compare thread-context hijacking variants (suspend vs. non-suspend, ROP vs. two-step) - Test APC injection reliability by generating/identifying alertable threads - Develop detections/hunts for `SetThreadContext` abuse, APC queueing, and unusual thread start patterns - Research tradeoffs between Win32 vs. NT-native thread creation telemetry
Keywords: Windows process injection, thread hijacking, CONTEXT struct, SetThreadContext, GetThreadContext, CreateRemoteThread, NtCreateThread, APC injection, QueueUserAPC, NtQueueApcThread, NtQueueApcThreadEx, alertable threads, ROP gadget, LoadLibraryA, remote code execution, ETW, shellcode injection, DLL injection, Windows internals, EDR evasion

entry.title: Syscalls Thread Hijacking
entry.summary:
Title: Syscalls-Thread-Hijacking (System calls Thread Hijacking Malwares)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This repository is a collection of Windows thread-hijacking and shellcode injection samples that use dynamically-resolved direct and indirect syscalls to reduce user-mode API hooking visibility. It focuses on bypassing user-mode AV/EDR hooks by avoiding standard Win32/NtAPI call paths and minimizing static IAT artifacts via manual export parsing of `ntdll.dll`. The code resolves syscall numbers (SSNs) at runtime, builds custom syscall stubs, and performs remote process injection by enumerating processes/threads, allocating RWX memory, writing/decrypting shellcode, and redirecting a thread’s instruction pointer. It includes both EXE-based remote injection variants and a DLL variant that runs inside the host process and spawns a worker thread to avoid loader-lock deadlocks. The author notes it is not “ntdll unhooking” and will not defeat kernel-level telemetry (ETW, callbacks, kernel trampolines) if the syscall instruction itself is instrumented. Useful for red teamers, malware researchers, and defenders studying syscall-based injection tradecraft and its detection boundaries.
Technical Focus: - Direct syscalls vs indirect syscalls (SSN extraction, syscall stub generation) - Manual PE export parsing / custom `GetProcAddress` (EAT walking, forwarded exports handling) - Thread hijacking (suspend, context/IP manipulation, resume) - Remote process injection workflow (process/thread enumeration, memory allocation, RWX protection) - Shellcode encryption/decryption and payload staging - DLL execution patterns (DllMain constraints, loader lock avoidance via new thread)
Use Cases: - Build and test syscall-based injection PoCs that bypass user-mode hooks - Study tradeoffs and stability risks of hijacking existing threads vs creating suspended threads - Develop detections for syscall-stub patterns, manual export walking, and thread-context tampering - Compare direct vs indirect syscall implementations and their failure modes under EDR instrumentation - Create lab samples for malware analysis training focused on low-level Windows injection
Keywords: Windows, ntdll.dll, Native API, direct syscalls, indirect syscalls, SSN, syscall stub, user-mode hooking bypass, EDR evasion, PE parsing, export address table, IMAGE_EXPORT_DIRECTORY, GetProcAddressManualEx, NtQuerySystemInformation, process injection, thread hijacking, SuspendThread, thread context, shellcode loader, DLL injection

entry.title: CallStackSpoofer
entry.summary:
Title: CallStackSpoofer (x64 Call Stack Spoofing)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository provides a C + x64 assembly implementation of call stack spoofing on Windows, intended to make sensitive API calls appear to originate from benign-looking call chains. - It targets detection mechanisms used by EDRs that rely on stack walking / call stack telemetry to attribute suspicious behavior (e.g., memory allocation, thread creation, syscalls) to untrusted modules. - The project includes an assembly “spoofer” stub and supporting headers/structs to craft a fake return-address chain and control the call flow while preserving correct calling conventions. - It serves as a practical companion to the author’s write-up on x64 call stack spoofing, focusing on implementation details rather than a full offensive framework. - Useful primarily for red team operators, implant/tooling developers, and security researchers evaluating stack-based detections and evasions. - Interesting because it demonstrates a common modern evasion primitive (stack spoofing) in a small, auditable codebase that can be integrated into custom loaders or post-exploitation tooling.
Technical Focus: - Windows x64 calling convention and stack frames - Call stack spoofing / return address chain fabrication - Assembly stubs for indirect calls and control-flow manipulation - Stack walking–based EDR telemetry evasion - Low-level API invocation patterns (user-mode)
Use Cases: - Integrate stack spoofing into a custom loader/implant to reduce suspicious call stack artifacts - Research and reproduce EDR detections based on stack traces and validate bypass hypotheses - Build test harnesses for defensive tooling that inspects call stacks (ETW/telemetry consumers) - Educational reference for implementing x64 spoofing stubs in C/ASM
Keywords: Windows x64, call stack spoofing, stack walking, EDR evasion, return address spoofing, ROP-like chain, assembly stub, indirect call, control-flow manipulation, calling convention, stack frame, telemetry, user-mode, C, MASM, red team tooling, implant development, loader integration

entry.title: windows x64 shellcode pipeline
entry.summary:
Title: windows-x64-shellcode-pipeline
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This repository provides a Dockerized, reproducible build pipeline for generating custom Windows x64 position-independent shellcode from C source code instead of hand-written assembly. It solves the common workflow pain of building PIC-compatible payloads, extracting the correct bytes from a compiled PE, and producing a final `.bin` shellcode blob (plus a C array output) in an automated way. The pipeline compiles a scaffolded PE, post-processes/cleans the generated assembly via Python tooling, and extracts the entrypoint-aligned `.text` bytes into raw shellcode. It includes a stack-alignment fix using Matt Graeber’s AlignRSP stub to avoid x64 calling convention/16-byte alignment issues. An example XOR encoder/decoder stub demonstrates how to ship encoded (polymorphic-style) payloads that decode at runtime, with guidance for implementing custom encoders. It’s primarily useful for red teamers, pentesters, and malware dev learners who want a consistent cross-compilation workflow and a practical template for API-resolving shellcode bootstraps (PEB walking, dynamic imports).
Technical Focus: - Windows x64 position-independent code (PIC) shellcode generation - Cross-compilation/build automation with Docker + Makefile (mingw-w64 toolchain) - PE-to-shellcode extraction (entrypoint RVA, `.text` section parsing) - PEB walking and dynamic API resolution (LoadLibraryA/GetProcAddress reconstruction) - x64 stack alignment (AlignRSP stub, 16-byte boundary) - Shellcode encoding/decoding stubs (XOR example, polymorphic pipeline hooks)
Use Cases: - Build custom Windows x64 shellcode payloads from modular C code - Create reproducible shellcode builds in CI/CD or isolated dev environments - Prototype loader-style shellcode that resolves APIs dynamically via the PEB - Generate encoded shellcode variants by swapping in custom encoder/decoder logic - Produce `.bin` blobs and C-formatted byte arrays for integration into droppers/loaders
Keywords: Windows x64, shellcode, position-independent code, PIC, Docker, Makefile, mingw-w64, x86_64-w64-mingw32-gcc, PE parsing, .text section, entrypoint RVA, PEB, API hashing, GetProcAddress, LoadLibraryA, AlignRSP, stack alignment, encoder stub, XOR, polymorphic shellcode

entry.title: NovaHypervisor
entry.summary:
Title: NovaHypervisor
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - NovaHypervisor is an early-stage defensive, host-based x64 Intel hypervisor for Windows 10+ intended to mitigate kernel-mode attacks, including BYOVD-style abuse. - It aims to protect security products (AV/EDR) and critical kernel memory structures by preventing unauthorized reads/writes/executes to specified kernel memory addresses. - The project provides a kernel driver (hypervisor) plus a user-mode client (NovaClient) to add/remove protected addresses and define access permissions (r/w/x). - It is designed to coexist with Hyper-V and relies on VT-x and virtualized IOMMU support. - Logging is implemented via WPP tracing to work from VMX root mode, with guidance for ETL collection and kernel debugging setup. - It explicitly avoids supporting execution-hook features (inline hook + EPT hook combos) to reduce offensive abuse potential, positioning it as an educational defensive reference.
Technical Focus: - Intel VT-x virtualization (VMX root/non-root) - Host-based hypervisor on Windows with Hyper-V compatibility - Kernel memory access control (read/write/execute restrictions) - Defensive mitigation of BYOVD and kernel tampering - Windows kernel driver development (WDK, service loading) - WPP software tracing / ETW-style logging from kernel/VMX context
Use Cases: - Protecting specific kernel memory regions from unauthorized access during incident response or hardening experiments - Researching defensive virtualization approaches against kernel-mode malware and BYOVD chains - Building/validating hypervisor-based memory access monitoring concepts in a lab - Developing detections/telemetry pipelines using WPP/ETL logs for hypervisor events - Educational reference for Windows hypervisor + driver architecture and debugging workflows
Keywords: NovaHypervisor, defensive hypervisor, Intel VT-x, VMX root, Hyper-V, Windows kernel driver, WDK, BYOVD, kernel tampering, kernel memory protection, EPT, memory access control, WPP tracing, ETL, logman, bcdedit, testsigning, kernel debugging, IOMMU, Windows 10, Windows 11

entry.title: PIClin
entry.summary:
Title: PIClin (Position Independent Code Linker)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - PIClin is a build tool that compiles C, Rust, or Zig source code into position-independent Windows shellcode (or optionally a PE), using MinGW GCC and custom linking. - It aims to let developers write “normal” code that calls Win32 APIs and standard C library functions without rewriting for shellcode constraints (e.g., no need for stack strings or manual API resolver stubs). - At link time it detects imported Win32/MSVCRT functions and generates a `winlib.c` shim that provides dynamic resolution for those APIs, currently targeting `kernel32.dll`, `ntdll.dll`, and `msvcrt.dll`. - The project relies on linker-script techniques to keep strings/data usable in PIC shellcode and provides a simple `loader.exe` for testing the produced `.bin`. - It’s useful for red teamers, exploit developers, and shellcode authors who want faster iteration from high-level source to injectable payloads, while still retaining the ability to customize OPSEC (e.g., swapping in indirect syscalls). - Notable limitations include assumptions about DLLs already being loaded, ad-hoc C header parsing, and Rust requiring `no_std`-style constraints to avoid standard library linking issues.
Technical Focus: - Position-independent code (PIC) generation for Windows shellcode - Linker scripts and custom linking with MinGW-w64 (gcc/ld) - Automatic API import detection and generated dynamic API resolver (`winlib.c`) - Win32 API usage from shellcode (kernel32/ntdll/msvcrt) - Multi-language payload compilation (C, Rust, Zig) - Shellcode vs PE output formats and test loader generation
Use Cases: - Compile PoC payloads into raw shellcode for injection (process injection, loaders, exploit chains) - Rapid prototyping of Win32 API–based shellcode without manual import tables - Generating a PE variant of the same payload for easier debugging and staging - Extending API coverage by adding DLL/function metadata via `win32-db` and `piclin/winlib.py` - Building custom shellcode frameworks that replace the default resolver with stealthier syscall techniques
