Keywords: PIClin, position-independent code, shellcode compiler, MinGW-w64, gcc, ld, linker script, Win32 API, kernel32.dll, ntdll.dll, msvcrt.dll, dynamic API resolving, LoadLibrary, VirtualAlloc, VirtualProtect, CreateProcessA, Rust no_std, Zig, winlib.c, BOF2Shellcode, win32-db

entry.title: gargoyle
entry.summary:
Title: Gargoyle — Memory Scanning Evasion Technique (x86 Windows)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Gargoyle is a 32-bit Windows proof-of-concept that evades memory scanners by ensuring its payload code is only executable for brief windows of time. It uses a ROP gadget plus a stack trampoline and position-independent code (PIC) to transition between non-executable and executable states, typically keeping the payload pages non-executable when idle. The included harness periodically triggers execution (e.g., a message box every 15 seconds) and then tail-calls logic to mark the code non-executable again while waiting on a timer. The project demonstrates how page permissions and control-flow tricks can reduce exposure to snapshot-based memory analysis and some scanning heuristics. It’s primarily useful for red teamers, exploit developers, and malware researchers studying in-memory evasion and ROP/PIC mechanics, and for blue teamers looking to understand and detect such behavior.
Technical Focus: - Windows memory protection changes (RW/RX toggling, DEP/NX semantics) - ROP gadget usage and stack trampolines - Position-independent code (PIC) on x86 - Runtime gadget discovery in mshtml.dll (searching executable sections) - Timer-based execution windows / “execute only when needed” design
Use Cases: - Prototype “sleep masking” style concepts using page-permission flipping - Research and education on x86 ROP chains, trampolines, and PIC loaders - Evaluate how memory scanners/EDR handle transient RX regions and ROP-based transitions - Develop detection ideas around frequent VirtualProtect-like transitions and suspicious ROP gadget sourcing
Keywords: Windows x86, memory scanning evasion, memory analysis, ROP, ROP gadget, stack trampoline, PIC, position-independent code, DEP, NX, page permissions, VirtualProtect, mshtml.dll, executable section scanning, timer-based execution, sleep masking, in-memory payload, NASM, Visual Studio, VMMap

entry.title: ImmoralFiber
entry.summary:
Title: ImmoralFiber (PoisonFiber & PhantomThread) — Offensive techniques using Windows Fibers
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository provides proof-of-concept implementations of two offensive techniques built around Windows Fibers: remote fiber injection (PoisonFiber) and improved fiber-based callstack masking (PhantomThread). - PoisonFiber focuses on remotely enumerating fiber-using threads/processes, locating dormant Fiber objects on remote heaps, and triggering execution by modifying fiber CONTEXT/stack state or Fiber Local Storage (FLS) callback tables. - It demonstrates multiple sub-techniques: overwriting existing fiber resumption code, redirecting execution by pushing a new return target, and hijacking default or user-defined FLS callbacks for one-shot execution. - PhantomThread evolves traditional “sleep masking” by removing common fiber indicators (e.g., `TEB->SameTebFlags` `HasFiberData`, `RtlUserFiberStart` artifacts) and by masking both the active and dormant fibers using a dummy fiber with a valid `XoredCookie`. - The project is useful for red teamers, malware developers, and security researchers studying stealthy execution/injection primitives and EDR detection gaps around fibers. - It’s interesting because it operationalizes lesser-used Windows internals (fiber objects, FLS internals, cookies) into injection and anti-scanning techniques, while explicitly noting expected telemetry and limitations (e.g., inline callstack collection).
Technical Focus: - Windows Fibers internals (Fiber objects, dormant fibers, `CONTEXT`, fiber stacks) - Remote enumeration of fiber usage via TEB (`SameTebFlags`, `HasFiberData`) - Remote heap enumeration and validation using `XoredCookie` / `FiberData` sanity checks - Fiber Local Storage (FLS) internals and callback table hijacking - Callstack masking / sleep masking and artifact removal (`RtlUserFiberStart`) - Memory scanning evasion tradeoffs and detection telemetry (ETW TI, RWX scanning, usermode hooks)
Use Cases: - Remote code execution in fiber-using processes without creating new remote threads - Researching/validating detections for fiber-based injection and FLS callback abuse - Building or testing callstack masking approaches against periodic memory scanners - Developing forensic tooling to enumerate/validate fiber objects and hidden stacks - Training material / lab POCs for Windows internals and EDR telemetry generation
Keywords: Windows Fibers, PoisonFiber, PhantomThread, fiber injection, dormant fiber, ConvertThreadToFiber, CreateFiber, SwitchToFiber, TEB, SameTebFlags, HasFiberData, CONTEXT, RIP, heap enumeration, Fiber Local Storage, FLS, FlsData, GLOBAL_FLS_DATA, callback table hijack, XoredCookie, callstack masking, sleep masking, ETW Threat Intelligence, KERNEL_THREATINT_KEYWORD_WRITEVM_REMOTE, CreateToolhelp32Snapshot, RWX memory, usermode hooks

entry.title: xorInject
entry.summary:
Title: XOR Encrypted Shellcode Execution via Desktop Enumeration (xorInject)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository contains a Boo (.NET) script that executes XOR-encrypted shellcode by abusing Windows desktop window enumeration callbacks. - It decrypts shellcode at runtime (XOR), allocates executable memory, copies the decrypted payload, and triggers execution via `EnumDesktopWindows` using a delegate callback. - The technique is positioned as an alternative to more common thread-creation execution patterns, aiming to blend execution into benign-looking GUI enumeration behavior. - Shellcode is represented as MAC-address formatted bytes, then converted back into raw bytes before execution. - It’s primarily useful for red teamers, pentesters, and malware developers exploring .NET-based loaders and callback execution primitives. - The interesting aspect is the combination of uncommon language choice (Boo), simple obfuscation, and callback-based execution through user32 APIs.
Technical Focus: - XOR runtime decryption / lightweight shellcode obfuscation - Callback-based code execution via `EnumDesktopWindows` - Win32 memory allocation/protection (`VirtualAlloc`, `PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE`) - Desktop context handling (`GetThreadDesktop`) - .NET interop / delegates for unmanaged callbacks (P/Invoke) - Shellcode encoding/decoding (MAC-address formatted byte strings)
Use Cases: - Building or testing alternative Windows shellcode execution chains using GUI callbacks - Prototyping .NET/Boo-based loaders for tradecraft experimentation - Evaluating EDR detections around `VirtualAlloc` + RWX + callback execution patterns - Training/education on Windows callback execution primitives and P/Invoke in .NET
Keywords: Boo, .NET Framework, P/Invoke, shellcode loader, XOR encryption, runtime decryption, EnumDesktopWindows, GetThreadDesktop, VirtualAlloc, PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE, delegate callback, user32.dll, kernel32.dll, memory allocation, RWX memory, desktop enumeration, Windows internals, callback execution, obfuscation, MAC-address shellcode encoding

entry.title: CelestialSpark
entry.summary:
Title: CelestialSpark V2 (Stardust-based Meterpreter/Sliver reverse_TCP staging shellcode)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): CelestialSpark V2 is a modern 64-bit position-independent Windows reverse_TCP staging shellcode intended to be compatible with Meterpreter and Sliver staging workflows, built on top of Cracked5pider’s Stardust v2 shellcode template. It targets the problem of legacy msfvenom Meterpreter reverse_tcp stagers being heavily signatured by AV/EDR by re-implementing core networking and staging logic in a smaller, custom PIC codebase. The project hardcodes byte-order helpers (htons/htonl), implements a custom inet_addr, and dynamically resolves/imports Winsock APIs from ws2_32.dll rather than relying on standard imports. It leverages Stardust’s compile-time hashing (FNV1a) and PEB-based module resolution to avoid cleartext API/module references and to remain position independent. It’s primarily useful for red teamers, pentesters, and implant developers experimenting with custom stagers and shellcode engineering, and secondarily for defenders wanting realistic samples for detection engineering. The repo includes build tooling (Make/CMake, mingw) and is designed to be used with external shellcode loaders/injectors.
Technical Focus: - Position-independent (PIC) Windows x64 shellcode design - PEB-based module enumeration and dynamic API resolution - Compile-time hashing (FNV1a) for module/function name resolution - Winsock (ws2_32.dll) reverse TCP socket creation and communication - Staged payload delivery/execution logic (Meterpreter/Sliver-style stager) - Shellcode build pipeline (mingw, Make/CMake) and loader compatibility
Use Cases: - Generate custom reverse_TCP stager shellcode with configurable IP/port - Replace/augment msfvenom stagers to reduce static IoCs in engagements - Integrate with shellcode loaders/injectors (process injection, in-memory execution) - Research/learn PIC shellcode patterns, PEB walking, and hashed API resolving - Create detection test cases for staged reverse TCP shellcode behavior
Keywords: Windows x64 shellcode, position-independent code, reverse TCP stager, Meterpreter, Sliver, Stardust, PEB walking, dynamic API resolution, FNV1a hashing, ws2_32.dll, Winsock, inet_addr, htons, htonl, staging payload, in-memory execution, shellcode loader, process injection, mingw-w64, C/C++20

entry.title: EarlyCascadeImprooved
entry.summary:
Title: EarlyCascade Improved (Early Cascade Injection PoC)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository is a proof-of-concept implementation of Outflank’s “Early Cascade Injection,” a process injection technique that executes code very early in process creation, before many user-mode EDR components are initialized. - It works by forcing the Windows Shim engine to be enabled and then hijacking a Shim engine callback to gain execution inside the newly created process. - The “improved” changes over the original 0xNinjaCyclone code include selecting an arbitrary target process, loading shellcode dynamically from a file, and applying basic XOR obfuscation to reduce static shellcode detections. - The PoC creates a suspended process, allocates and writes a stub + shellcode, enables the shim engine, hijacks the callback, and resumes the thread to trigger execution; the stub then disables the shim engine and uses APC queuing to run the payload later. - It’s primarily useful for red teamers, pentesters, and malware researchers studying early-process injection and user-mode EDR evasion tradeoffs. - It’s interesting because it leverages Windows application compatibility (shim) internals as an execution primitive during a timing window that can bypass common user-mode monitoring.
Technical Focus: - Early-process injection during process creation (suspended process workflow) - Windows Shim engine enablement and callback hijacking - Remote memory allocation/writes (stub + payload staging) - APC-based deferred execution - Dynamic API/address resolution for internal flags/callbacks - Basic shellcode obfuscation (XOR) and file-based payload loading
Use Cases: - EDR evasion research focused on “pre-user-mode sensor” execution windows - Developing/validating detections for shim-engine abuse and early injection chains - Red team tradecraft prototyping for stealthy initial code execution in a new process - Studying Windows application compatibility (shim) internals and callback surfaces
Keywords: Early Cascade Injection, Outflank, shim engine, AppCompat, process creation, CreateProcess suspended, callback hijack, remote process injection, VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, APC, QueueUserAPC, shellcode loader, XOR obfuscation, Windows internals, user-mode EDR bypass, stub assembly, meterpreter_reverse_tcp, Visual Studio 2022, proof-of-concept

entry.title: bof oxide
entry.summary:
Title: bof_oxide — Rust BOF (Beacon Object File) POC/Template for COFF loaders
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository is a proof-of-concept/template for writing Beacon Object Files (BOFs) in Rust that can be executed by common COFF loaders used in C2 frameworks like Sliver, Havoc, and Cobalt Strike. - It aims to reduce BOF “volatility,” improve debugging ergonomics, and provide better error handling compared to typical C BOF workflows. - The design keeps the standard C `go` entrypoint, but forwards Beacon API function pointers into a Rust `initialize()` function that constructs a Rust `Beacon` wrapper and calls user code (e.g., `rust_bof`). - It demonstrates argument parsing and output via Beacon APIs (e.g., `BeaconOutput`, `BeaconPrintf`) and shows example execution using the Coffee BOF runner and Sliver. - Useful for red teamers/pentesters and offensive tool developers who want Rust ergonomics while staying compatible with BOF/COFF execution constraints. - Interesting because it illustrates a practical C↔Rust FFI pattern for BOFs and highlights the realities/limitations of Rust in COFF-loaded, in-memory execution contexts.
Technical Focus: - Beacon Object Files (BOF) and COFF object execution - Cobalt Strike/Sliver/Havoc Beacon API interop - C↔Rust FFI with function-pointer passing - BOF entrypoint conventions (`go`) and loader compatibility - Debugging/error-handling patterns for BOFs - PE/COFF sections and symbol layout (objdump inspection)
Use Cases: - Build Rust-based BOFs compatible with Cobalt Strike/Sliver/Havoc COFF loaders - Prototype and test BOFs locally with Coffee or similar BOF runners - Create safer wrappers around Beacon APIs (printing, formatting, argument handling) - Study COFF object structure/symbols for loader troubleshooting and compatibility
Keywords: BOF, Beacon Object File, COFF, COFFLoader, Cobalt Strike, Sliver, Havoc, Rust, C, FFI, function pointers, BeaconPrintf, BeaconOutput, BeaconFormatAlloc, BeaconFormatFree, go entrypoint, Coffee BOF runner, PE-COFF, objdump, in-memory execution

entry.title: Proxll
entry.summary:
Title: Proxll — Proxy DLL Template Generator (with windows.h conflict workaround)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Proxll is a C++ tool that generates proxy DLL templates to facilitate DLL proxying/hijacking scenarios without breaking the host application. - It automates creating forwarded exports and a wrapper for a chosen target export (or `DllMain`) so custom code can run and then call through to the real function to preserve expected behavior. - The repo also documents a common implementation pitfall: `windows.h` symbol conflicts when defining exported functions, and solves it by splitting code into a “core” file that includes `windows.h` and an “exports” file that avoids it. - The workflow uses ProcMon/API Monitor to identify DLL load opportunities, then `proxllGen.exe` generates a buildable template (including a `.def` file) compiled via MinGW. - Useful primarily for red teamers/pentesters and malware developers building reliable proxy DLLs; also relevant to defenders for understanding how proxying is implemented and why apps don’t crash when hijacked.
Technical Focus: - DLL proxying / DLL search order hijacking mechanics - Forwarded exports and `.def`-based export mapping - LoadLibrary/GetProcAddress call-through wrappers - One-time execution / re-entrancy blocking in exported stubs - C++ build/ABI issues with `windows.h` symbol conflicts - MinGW (x86_64-w64-mingw32) build pipeline for Windows DLLs
Use Cases: - Generate a proxy DLL that executes a payload and then forwards to the legitimate DLL to avoid application crashes - Rapidly scaffold export stubs/forwarders for a target system DLL (e.g., `version.dll`) - Create reproducible templates for testing DLL hijack opportunities found with ProcMon - Build lab PoCs demonstrating proxy DLL behavior and debugging via DebugView
Keywords: proxy DLL, DLL proxying, DLL hijacking, DLL search order, forwarded exports, export forwarding, .def file, exported functions, LoadLibrary, GetProcAddress, DllMain, windows.h conflict, C++ ABI, MinGW-w64, x86_64-w64-mingw32-g++, ProcMon, API Monitor, DebugView, Windows internals

entry.title: ADPT
entry.summary:
Title: ADPT (Another DLL Proxying Tool)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - ADPT is a Rust-based toolchain that automates DLL proxying to exploit DLL hijacking/sideloading opportunities on Windows x64. - It reduces the manual work of identifying which exports are actually invoked, recreating function signatures, and building a compatible proxy DLL by using a tracing DLL and minimal assembly stubs. - The workflow generates an “ExportTracer” DLL to log called exports (to a file like `C:\Windows\Temp\result.log`), then generates a final proxy DLL that forwards all exports while executing a user payload in a chosen export. - It preserves original export ordinals and supports running the payload either on a new thread (Rust `std::thread` or `NtCreateThreadEx`) or on the calling thread to avoid process termination in sensitive binaries. - It’s primarily useful for red teamers/pentesters and malware developers doing tradecraft around DLL sideloading; defenders can use it to reproduce and validate hijack paths. - Limitations: you still need Procmon-like tooling to find the hijack point, payload is expected in Rust (though alternatives are mentioned), and only 64-bit DLLs are supported.
Technical Focus: - Windows DLL hijacking / sideloading and proxy DLL generation - Export tracing (runtime logging of invoked exported functions) - Export forwarding with ordinal preservation - Payload execution strategies (calling thread vs new thread) - Native thread creation (`NtCreateThreadEx`) vs Rust threading - Rust-based Windows offensive tooling build pipeline (release builds, linker quirks)
Use Cases: - Rapidly weaponize discovered DLL search-order hijacks/sideloading in third-party apps - Determine which DLL exports are actually used by a target binary without reversing - Build proxy DLLs that keep application functionality while executing a payload - Test “delayed import” sideloading scenarios (example: `TextShaping.dll` via `gdi32full.dll`) - Reproduce and validate defensive detections for proxying/sideloading behaviors
Keywords: DLL proxying, DLL hijacking, DLL sideloading, Windows x64, export forwarding, export ordinals, delayed import, TextShaping.dll, gdi32full.dll, Process Hacker, Procmon, Rust, DllMain avoidance, NtCreateThreadEx, std::thread, API monitoring alternative, PE exports, linker LNK2005, DInvoke_rs

entry.title: Split
entry.summary:
Title: Split — Divide-and-Conquer Remote Process Injection to Confuse EDRs
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Split is a Rust proof-of-concept that applies a “divide and conquer” strategy to EDR evasion by splitting a classic remote process injection chain across multiple processes. Instead of performing NtOpenProcess → NtAllocateVirtualMemory → NtWriteVirtualMemory → NtProtectVirtualMemory → NtCreateThreadEx in a single process, it forks the current process via NtCreateUserProcess after each step so each action occurs in a different process context. Because the forked process inherits memory/handles and continues execution at RIP+1, the injection can proceed while reducing the likelihood that an EDR correlates the full sequence as a single malicious behavior. The author reports that without forking, 2/3 tested EDRs (MDE, CrowdStrike, SentinelOne) alerted on remote injection, while with forking none alerted, despite telemetry still showing cross-process activity. The repo also includes string-literal obfuscation via LITCRYPT (used for Dinvoke_rs-related strings) and notes multiple OPSEC limitations (no syscalls, no unhooking, ETW not patched, plaintext shellcode). It’s primarily useful for red teamers and offensive tool developers exploring behavioral-correlation weaknesses in EDR detections.
Technical Focus: - Remote process injection via NTAPI (NtOpenProcess, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, NtWriteVirtualMemory, NtProtectVirtualMemory, NtCreateThreadEx) - Process forking/creation with NtCreateUserProcess and handle inheritance - Behavioral evasion by breaking attack chains into multiple processes (correlation disruption) - Rust implementation and dynamic invocation (DInvoke-style) - String literal obfuscation with LITCRYPT - EDR telemetry vs alerting gaps (MDE, CrowdStrike, SentinelOne)
Use Cases: - Testing whether an EDR correlates multi-step injection chains across process boundaries - Building/experimenting with “split execution” tradecraft for staged operations - Researching detection engineering improvements for cross-process correlation - Creating lab PoCs to compare alerting with/without process-splitting
Keywords: Windows, Rust, EDR evasion, divide and conquer, process injection, remote thread, NTAPI, NtCreateUserProcess, NtOpenProcess, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, NtWriteVirtualMemory, NtProtectVirtualMemory, NtCreateThreadEx, handle inheritance, fork, DInvoke, LITCRYPT, behavioral detection, correlation, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, CrowdStrike, SentinelOne

entry.title: Unwinder
entry.summary:
Title: Unwinder — Call stack spoofing for Rust (SilentMoonWalk weaponization + stack replacement)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Unwinder is a Rust crate that implements stable, unwindable call stack spoofing on Windows by weaponizing the SilentMoonWalk technique. - It aims to defeat call-stack-based detections (e.g., EDR/userland scanners inspecting thread stacks) by producing “legitimate-looking” frames while executing arbitrary functions or indirect syscalls. - The crate exposes macros to call arbitrary function pointers or perform indirect syscalls with up to 11 parameters, returning the callee’s return value as `*mut c_void`. - It supports chaining spoofed calls without growing the visible call stack and uses TLS for efficiency; Windows API resolution is handled via `dinvoke_rs`. - An additional experimental “stack replacement” mode maintains a clean call stack throughout execution by dynamically swapping return addresses with offsets into legitimate functions of matching frame size, restoring the original return address before `ret`. - This is primarily useful for red teams, pentesters, and malware developers building Rust implants/loaders who need stealthier execution traces.
Technical Focus: - SilentMoonWalk call stack spoofing (unwind-info-aware stack manipulation) - Indirect syscalls with spoofed/unwindable stacks - Return address spoofing, concealment frames, `jmp rbx`-style trampolines - TLS-assisted per-thread state for spoofing - Dynamic API resolution/invocation via `dinvoke_rs` - Experimental stack replacement with frame-size matching and runtime offset selection
Use Cases: - Building Rust-based loaders/implants with reduced call-stack anomalies - Executing Windows APIs (e.g., `Sleep`, `OpenProcess`, `LoadLibraryA`) under spoofed stacks - Performing NT syscalls (e.g., `NtDelayExecution`) via indirect syscall paths while preserving plausible stacks - Evading tools that flag suspicious thread call stacks (e.g., PE-sieve `/threads`) during in-memory execution - Reflective DLL injection scenarios where exported entrypoints wrap stack replacement start/end
Keywords: Rust, Windows, call stack spoofing, SilentMoonWalk, unwind info, RUNTIME_FUNCTION, UNWIND_INFO, indirect syscall, NTAPI, NtDelayExecution, dinvoke_rs, dynamic API resolution, TLS, return address spoofing, stack replacement, reflective injection, BaseThreadInitThunk, EDR evasion, PE-sieve, macros

entry.title: Christmas
entry.summary:
Title: Maldev-Academy Christmas (multi-process staged process injection PoC)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository is a Windows process-injection proof-of-concept inspired by a technique mentioned by Hexacorn, implemented as a multi-process “assembly line” where each child process performs one injection step. - The injector creates a target process, then uses subsequent child processes to allocate memory, change protections to RWX, write the payload in 1024-byte chunks (spawning a new process per chunk), and finally execute it. - Payload material is RC4-encrypted (example: Havoc Demon), with a companion tool (`ChristmasPayloadEnc.exe`) to encrypt and pad the payload to a 1024-byte multiple to fit the write logic. - The design emphasizes distributing suspicious actions across many short-lived processes and passing state via command-line arguments while inheriting the target process handle. - It’s primarily useful for red teamers, malware developers, and security researchers studying EDR evasion tradeoffs and process-creation telemetry (the README includes a demo claiming MDE bypass with Havoc Demon).
Technical Focus: - Multi-process staged injection workflow (process-per-step) - Handle inheritance across child processes - Remote memory allocation / protection changes / remote writes - Chunked payload writing (1024-byte segments) - RC4 payload encryption and padding - Command-line IPC/state passing between processes
Use Cases: - Researching how splitting injection primitives across processes affects EDR detection - Building/experimenting with alternative process injection chains for lab testing - Generating encrypted/padded payload blobs for controlled execution tests - Developing detections around anomalous process trees + inherited handles + remote memory ops
Keywords: Windows process injection, multi-process injection, handle inheritance, CreateProcess, command-line arguments, remote memory allocation, VirtualAllocEx, VirtualProtectEx, WriteProcessMemory, RWX memory, payload chunking, RC4, Havoc Demon, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, EDR evasion, process tree telemetry, maldev, proof of concept

entry.title: PhantomInjector
entry.summary:
Title: PhantomInjector – Advanced In-Memory PowerShell Process Injection Framework
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - PhantomInjector is a PowerShell-based framework for executing shellcode in remote processes using multiple in-memory injection techniques with built-in evasion options. - It targets common defensive controls by offering AMSI bypass (patching `AmsiScanBuffer`), ETW bypass (hooking/patching `EtwEventWrite`), and NTDLL unhooking by restoring clean bytes from disk. - Supported injection methods include APC injection (Early Bird/`QueueUserAPC`), thread hijacking (`Get/SetThreadContext`), classic remote thread injection, process ghosting (TxF + section/process creation), and module stomping v2 (tail-of-image overwrite with thread-context hijack). - The tool can optionally use direct syscalls via in-memory assembly stubs to reduce user-mode API telemetry and bypass hooks. - It includes OPSEC-oriented checks (anti-debug, sandbox detection) and supports local or remote payload retrieval. - Useful primarily for red teams and penetration testers to prototype and compare injection/evasion tradeoffs; also relevant to blue teams for understanding detection points and Sysmon rule ideas.
Technical Focus: - PowerShell in-memory shellcode loading and execution - Process injection: APC, thread hijack, remote thread, module stomping, process ghosting - AMSI bypass via `AmsiScanBuffer` patching - ETW bypass via `EtwEventWrite` patching/hooking - NTDLL unhooking (restoring clean `ntdll.dll` bytes) - Direct syscalls (NTAPI stubs: `NtCreateThreadEx`, `NtAllocateVirtualMemory`, `NtQueueApcThread`, etc.)
Use Cases: - Execute shellcode in a chosen target process (e.g., notepad) using different injection primitives - Run payloads with reduced PowerShell/ETW/AMSI visibility during an engagement - Compare WinAPI vs direct-syscall execution paths for telemetry differences - Simulate advanced tradecraft (process ghosting/module stomping) for detection engineering validation - Build/validate Sysmon/Sigma detections for memory protection changes, remote thread creation, and suspicious PowerShell command lines
Keywords: PowerShell, process injection, shellcode, APC injection, Early Bird, QueueUserAPC, thread hijacking, GetThreadContext, SetThreadContext, CreateRemoteThread, process ghosting, TxF, NtCreateSection, NtCreateProcessEx, module stomping, ntdll unhooking, direct syscalls, NtCreateThreadEx, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, AMSI bypass, AmsiScanBuffer, ETW bypass, EtwEventWrite, Sysmon, Sigma, RWX memory, Windows internals

entry.title: koneko
entry.summary:
Title: koneko — Robust Cobalt Strike shellcode loader with advanced evasion
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository contains a Windows C/C++/x64 assembly shellcode loader designed primarily for running Cobalt Strike Beacon shellcode with multiple evasion features. - It focuses on defeating user-mode detections by implementing a custom sleep routine (instead of standard Sleep/SleepEx) and spoofing thread call stacks during idle periods. - The loader uses extensive return-address spoofing and indirect syscalls to reduce visibility from API hooking and EDR telemetry. - It also includes anti-VM and anti-debug checks and obfuscates embedded shellcode by splitting it into pointer-like chunks using the Windows EncodePointer API. - The author notes limitations: the spoofing/obfuscation benefits apply to the current process and don’t automatically carry over after process injection, and the sleep-hooking logic is tailored to Cobalt Strike’s behavior. - Useful for red teamers and malware researchers studying modern user-mode evasion patterns and loader tradecraft.
Technical Focus: - Sleep obfuscation via NtCreateEvent / NtWaitForSingleObject - Thread call stack spoofing and return address spoofing - Indirect syscalls (NTAPI syscall invocation patterns) - Fiber switching to evade memory scanning/heuristics - Anti-VM and anti-debugging checks - Shellcode obfuscation using EncodePointer and address-splitting
Use Cases: - Building/experimenting with stealthy in-process shellcode loaders for lab red teaming - Researching EDR user-mode hook evasion (API hooking vs indirect syscalls) - Prototyping sleep-masking/callstack-masking techniques for long-lived implants - Developing detections for anomalous NTAPI sleep patterns, fibers, and spoofed stacks
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, Beacon, shellcode loader, Windows internals, sleep obfuscation, call stack spoofing, return address spoofing, indirect syscalls, NTAPI, NtCreateEvent, NtWaitForSingleObject, SleepEx hook, fibers, EncodePointer, anti-VM, anti-debug, EDR evasion, user-mode hooks, Cortex XDR, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint

entry.title: go shellcode
entry.summary:
Title: Ne0nd0g/go-shellcode
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - A Go-based collection of Windows shellcode runners plus utilities for transforming/encrypting shellcode blobs. - It demonstrates multiple local execution and remote injection techniques using a variety of Win32 and NT native/undocumented APIs, including thread creation, APC-based execution, and entrypoint patching in suspended processes. - Several implementations intentionally avoid `golang.org/x/sys/windows` by manually loading DLLs and resolving procedures, highlighting different tradeoffs in API usage and detection surface. - It includes “EarlyBird” APC injection (QueueUserAPC + ResumeThread/NtTestAlert), ETW-thread execution via `EtwpCreateEtwThread`, and “special user APC” via `NtQueueApcThreadEx`. - ShellcodeUtils supports base64 encoding and XOR/RC4/AES-256-GCM (Argon2id-derived key) encryption/decryption workflows for shellcode files. - Useful for red teamers, pentesters, and malware researchers to prototype loaders, compare injection primitives, and build test cases for EDR detections.
