entry.title: BOF Spawn
entry.summary:
Title: BOF Spawn – Process Injection (Cobalt Strike BOF)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - BOF Spawn is a Cobalt Strike Beacon Object File that spawns a new process (often suspended) and injects either a Beacon payload or arbitrary shellcode using a “fork & run” style workflow. - It focuses on evasion by combining indirect syscalls (to reduce userland hook interference) with Draugr synthetic stack frames (to spoof call stacks and hinder stack-based detections). - The tool supports PPID spoofing and process mitigation policy tweaks (e.g., BlockDLL, disabling CFG) to influence monitoring and execution constraints. - Multiple execution methods are implemented, ranging from direct RIP hijacking to gadget-based jumps (JMP RAX/RBX in ntdll) and callback-based execution (e.g., EnumResourceTypesW). - The README also documents realistic detection surfaces (ETW-TI for context changes, kernel callbacks for process creation and memory protections), making it useful for both offensive tradecraft and defensive validation. - Intended primarily for red teams/pentesters and malware developers working on Windows x64 post-exploitation and injection tradeoffs.
Technical Focus: - Cobalt Strike BOF + CNA integration (beacon_inline_execute) - NtCreateUserProcess-based spawning (NT path usage) - Indirect syscalls / syscall resolution (VxTable-style) - Draugr synthetic stack frames (stack spoofing) - Thread context manipulation (NtGetContextThread/NtSetContextThread) and gadget-based execution - Process mitigation policies (BlockDLL, CFG disable) and memory permission strategies (RW→RX vs RWX)
Use Cases: - Execute Beacon in a newly spawned process with reduced userland-hook visibility - Inject and run custom shellcode with selectable execution primitives (RIP hijack, gadgets, callbacks) - PPID spoofing to shape process tree telemetry during post-exploitation - Tradecraft testing against EDR detections (ETW-TI, kernel callbacks, memory scanning) - Lab research on stack spoofing + indirect syscall combinations in real tooling
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, BOF, Beacon Object File, CNA, process injection, fork and run, NtCreateUserProcess, NT path, indirect syscalls, syscall resolution, VxTable, Draugr, synthetic stack frames, stack spoofing, PPID spoofing, NtGetContextThread, NtSetContextThread, RIP hijacking, ntdll gadget, JMP RAX, JMP RBX, EnumResourceTypesW, callback execution, CFG, BlockDLL, RWX, RW to RX, ETW-TI, PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutine

entry.title: Draugr
entry.summary:
Title: Draugr — BOF with Synthetic Stackframe (Call Stack Spoofing for Indirect Syscalls)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Draugr is a Windows Beacon Object File (BOF) framework that performs indirect syscalls while fabricating a “legitimate-looking” user-mode call stack to evade EDR stack-walking detections. It targets detections that flag sensitive API/syscall usage originating from unbacked/executable memory regions typical of shellcode. The implementation combines return-address spoofing with construction of synthetic frames that mimic a normal thread start chain (e.g., `RtlUserThreadStart` → `BaseThreadInitThunk`) so stack inspection shows plausible return addresses. It dynamically resolves syscall numbers (including hook-resilient neighbor scanning in `ntdll`) and computes accurate stack frame sizes by parsing x64 unwind metadata (`RtlLookupFunctionEntry` / `UNWIND_INFO`). A `jmp [rbx]` gadget in `kernelbase.dll` is used to pivot control to a fixup routine that restores registers/stack and returns cleanly, preserving syscall return values (unlike some threadpool-based evasions). The repo also documents a hard mitigation: Intel CET shadow stacks will break this technique due to return-address mismatches.
Technical Focus: - Indirect syscalls (Hell’s Gate / Halo’s Gate-style SSN resolution) - Synthetic call stack / return address spoofing (stack-walking evasion) - x64 unwind parsing (.pdata, `RUNTIME_FUNCTION`, `UNWIND_INFO`, `UNWIND_CODE`) - Gadget discovery in PE .text (e.g., `FF 23` = `jmp [rbx]`) - Assembly stub for stack/register manipulation and fixup trampoline - CET shadow stack incompatibility / hardware-enforced control-flow integrity
Use Cases: - EDR evasion research for syscall-based memory operations (e.g., `NtAllocateVirtualMemory`) - Building stealthier BOFs that need syscall return values without spawning new threads - Studying stack-walk based detections and how synthetic frames affect telemetry - Defensive validation: testing CET/shadow-stack protections against stack spoofing
Keywords: BOF, Beacon Object File, indirect syscall, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, ntdll, kernelbase.dll, kernel32.dll, RtlUserThreadStart, BaseThreadInitThunk, call stack spoofing, return address spoofing, stack walking, UNWIND_INFO, RtlLookupFunctionEntry, .pdata, gadget scanning, jmp [rbx], fixup trampoline, Intel CET, shadow stack, #CP exception

entry.title: Huginn
entry.summary:
Title: Huginn Project (Position-Independent COFF Loader Shellcode)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Huginn is a Windows x64 position-independent COFF (.o) loader that executes COFF payloads fully in-memory by appending the object file to loader shellcode (no disk drop). - It implements a full COFF loading pipeline: section mapping, symbol resolution (including a custom import convention), relocation handling, per-section memory protections, and calling a `go(PCOFF_INFO)` entry point. - The loader emphasizes evasion with stack spoofing (synthetic frames mimicking `BaseThreadInitThunk` → `RtlUserThreadStart`), indirect syscalls via HalosGate SSN resolution, and an EAF-oriented export parsing bypass using gadget-based memory reads. - It avoids CRT/import tables by resolving APIs at runtime via PEB-walking and compile-time hashes, and can proxy `LoadLibraryA` through threadpool/timer callbacks. - After execution it performs aggressive cleanup: frees all allocated regions and destroys a dedicated heap to reduce artifacts and leaks. - Useful for red teams, pentesters, and malware developers building BOF/COFF-style in-memory modules and experimenting with syscall/stack-walk evasion tradecraft.
Technical Focus: - In-memory COFF object loading (sections, symbols, relocations, IAT/GOT) - Position-independent shellcode without CRT/imports (PEB walk + hashed API resolution) - Indirect syscalls (HalosGate SSN resolution + syscall gadget) - Stack spoofing / synthetic call stacks (unwind info parsing, frame sizing) - EAF-style export resolution bypass (gadget-based reads) - Memory protection hygiene and post-exec heap/region cleanup
Use Cases: - Execute custom COFF/BOF-like modules in-memory as shellcode - Build loaders that reduce user-mode API telemetry via indirect syscalls - Test/benchmark stack-walking detections against synthetic stack frames - Develop modular payloads with runtime-resolved imports (`DLL$Function` convention) - Create short-lived in-memory tooling with minimal residual allocations
Keywords: COFF loader, in-memory execution, position-independent code, shellcode, BOF, Windows x64, PEB walk, API hashing, ntdll, kernel32, kernelbase, HalosGate, indirect syscall, SSN resolution, syscall gadget, stack spoofing, unwind info parsing, EAF bypass, export table parsing, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, RtlCreateHeap, memory protections, IAT, relocations, threadpool callback, timer callback

entry.title: KrakenMask
entry.summary:
Title: KrakenMask (APC-based Sleep Mask with Gadget/Callstack Evasion)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - KrakenMask is a Windows “sleep mask” implementation that obfuscates beacon sleep by using APC execution and gadget-based return address manipulation to evade modern EDR detections. - It specifically targets detections that flag `VirtualProtect` calls made via APC when the call stack returns through `NtTestAlert`/`ZwTestAlert`. - The project demonstrates altering the return address of `VirtualProtect` to a `call NtTestAlert` gadget to change the observed stack trace and reduce detection fidelity. - Updates mention additional call stack masking during the delay phase to evade tools/detections like HuntBeaconSleep-NG, and a technique that sets `CONTEXT.RIP` to a `jmp rdi` gadget with `RDI` holding the target function address. - Useful for red team operators and malware developers building stealthy in-memory implants, and for blue teams studying how call stack–based detections can be bypassed. - Interesting because it operationalizes a concrete bypass against published detection logic (e.g., Elastic’s VirtualProtect-via-NtTestAlert rule) using low-level control-flow and context manipulation.
Technical Focus: - APC-based execution (`KiUserApcHandler`) for sleep/obfuscation - Call stack spoofing / return address “gadget” redirection - `VirtualProtect` / `NtProtectVirtualMemory` detection evasion - Thread context manipulation (`CONTEXT.RIP`, register staging like `RDI`) - Gadget selection (e.g., `call NtTestAlert`, `jmp rdi`) - EDR rule evasion against call-stack-summary heuristics
Use Cases: - Implementing stealth sleep in C2 beacons/implants to reduce behavioral detections - Testing and validating EDR detections that rely on `VirtualProtect` + APC call stack patterns - Researching practical call stack masking/spoofing techniques on Windows - Building PoCs to reproduce and evaluate Elastic-style behavioral rules
Keywords: KrakenMask, sleep mask, APC, KiUserApcHandler, VirtualProtect, NtProtectVirtualMemory, NtTestAlert, ZwTestAlert, call stack spoofing, call stack masking, return address spoofing, ROP gadget, jmp rdi gadget, CONTEXT.RIP, ntdll.dll, KERNELBASE.dll, EDR evasion, HuntBeaconSleep-NG, Elastic detection rule, in-memory implant

entry.title: OdinLdr
entry.summary:
Title: OdinLdr — Cobalt Strike Reflective Loader with Synthetic Stackframe
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): OdinLdr is a custom reflective loader for Cobalt Strike Beacon and post-exploitation DLLs focused on evasion during in-memory loading and execution. It implements indirect syscalls for memory allocation/protection (e.g., NtAllocateVirtualMemory / NtProtectVirtualMemory) while crafting synthetic stack frames to make call stacks appear legitimate to EDR/user-mode hooks. The project also uses synthetic stack framing around LoadLibraryA and employs gadgets to read module headers in a way intended to reduce exposure to EAF (Export Address Filtering) style monitoring. This is primarily useful for red teams and malware developers who need stealthier in-memory execution paths than default loaders. It’s interesting because it combines reflective loading with stack spoofing and syscall indirection—common detection pressure points for modern EDRs.
Technical Focus: - Reflective DLL loading for Cobalt Strike Beacon/postex - Indirect syscalls (user-mode syscall invocation without direct API calls) - Synthetic/spoofed stack frames (call stack manipulation) - Virtual memory operations (NtAllocateVirtualMemory, NtProtectVirtualMemory) - EAF-aware module header parsing via ROP/gadgets - EDR evasion tradecraft around API hooking/call stack telemetry
Use Cases: - Load and execute Beacon reflectively with reduced user-mode API visibility - Execute post-exploitation modules in-memory while spoofing call stacks - Research/testing of EDR detections for syscall patterns and stack integrity - Developing alternative loaders for OPSEC-focused Cobalt Strike operations
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, Beacon, reflective loader, UDRL, indirect syscall, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, NtProtectVirtualMemory, LoadLibraryA, synthetic stackframe, stack spoofing, call stack evasion, EDR bypass, user-mode hooks, ntdll, KERNEL32, KERNELBASE, EAF, Export Address Filtering, ROP gadget, in-memory execution

entry.title: DropSpawn BOF
entry.summary:
Title: DropSpawn_BOF (DropSpawn) — Cobalt Strike BOF for spawning Beacons via DLL Application Directory Hijacking
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): DropSpawn is a Cobalt Strike Beacon Object File (BOF) that spawns new Beacon sessions by abusing DLL search order hijacking, positioned as an alternative to classic remote process injection. The key idea is spoofing the “application directory” used by the Windows loader so even System32 executables (e.g., WerFault.exe) can be coerced into loading an attacker-controlled DLL from a user-writable directory. Operators generate a proxy/hijack DLL that preserves the target DLL’s exports and forwards calls to the real System32 DLL to avoid crashing, while executing the payload on load. The repo includes a Python helper (generate_dll.py) to extract exports and build a forwarding DLL using MinGW, plus guidance for finding candidates with Procmon. It supports cross-arch scenarios (x86↔x64) and optionally uses PPID spoofing for the spawned process. It’s primarily useful for red teams/pentesters seeking lower-IOC process creation-based execution paths compared to remote memory injection chains.
Technical Focus: - DLL search order / application directory hijacking - Undocumented NTDLL process creation APIs (RtlCreateUserProcess-related) - DLL proxying via export forwarding (.def generation) - Cross-architecture Beacon spawning (x86/x64) - PPID spoofing during process creation - Self-deleting/rename-delete technique for loaded DLL cleanup
Use Cases: - Spawn additional Cobalt Strike Beacons without remote process injection primitives - Execute payloads by hijacking DLL loads in trusted Windows binaries (e.g., System32 tools) - Build stable hijack DLLs that forward exports to legitimate System32 DLLs - Reduce on-host artifacts by self-deleting dropped DLL payloads after load - Experiment with detection differences between PPID-spoofed vs non-spoofed spawns
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, BOF, Beacon spawning, DLL hijacking, DLL search order, application directory spoofing, System32, WerFault.exe, Procmon, export forwarding, proxy DLL, .def file, MinGW, RtlCreateUserProcess, NTDLL, PPID spoofing, CreateProcess, sysnative, self-deletion, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint (MDE)

entry.title: ProcessInjectionTechniques
entry.summary:
Title: Process Injection Series (ProcessInjectionTechniques)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository is a curated “process injection series” that documents and implements many Windows process injection and execution techniques commonly used by malware and red teams. - It aims to centralize step-by-step explanations, source code, and demos for a wide range of TTPs under MITRE ATT&CK T1055. - Covered methods span classic remote thread injection and DLL injection through more evasive approaches like process hollowing/ghosting, module stomping, APC/EarlyBird, thread hijacking, and fiber-based execution. - It also includes lower-level tradecraft such as NTAPI-based injection, NTDLL unhooking (“Lagos Island”), PEB-walking for API resolution/obfuscation, and direct/indirect syscalls (SysWhispers-style) to reduce userland hooking visibility. - The examples use a consistent demo shellcode payload (MessageBox) to make technique comparison easier. - Useful primarily for red teamers, pentesters, and malware researchers; secondarily for blue teams building detections by understanding implementation details and API/behavioral footprints.
Technical Focus: - Windows process injection patterns (local/remote, thread creation, APC, hijacking) - Memory allocation/protection and RWX tradeoffs (VirtualAlloc/VirtualProtect, RWX hunting) - PE/loader manipulation (process hollowing, PE injection, entrypoint patching) - Section mapping injection (NtCreateSection/NtMapViewOfSection) - Evasion primitives (NTDLL unhooking, API obfuscation, PEB walking) - Direct and indirect syscalls (bypassing userland hooks)
Use Cases: - Build and test multiple injection variants for red team payload delivery - Compare detection surface across injection techniques (API calls, memory perms, thread behavior) - Develop/validate EDR bypass hypotheses (syscalls, unhooking, API resolution tricks) - Create lab exercises or workshops on Windows injection internals - Generate defensive analytics for suspicious memory mappings, thread starts, and module tampering
Keywords: process injection, T1055, Windows internals, CreateRemoteThread, WriteProcessMemory, VirtualAllocEx, VirtualProtect, NtCreateSection, NtMapViewOfSection, APC injection, EarlyBird, process hollowing, process ghosting, module stomping, reflective DLL injection, remote thread hijacking, PEB walk, API obfuscation, NTDLL unhooking, direct syscalls, indirect syscalls, SysWhispers2, SysWhispers3, Dirty Vanity, Mokingjay

entry.title: RWX MEMEORY HUNT AND INJECTION DV
entry.summary:
Title: RWX_MEMEORY_HUNT_AND_INJECTION_DV (RWX Memory Hunt and Injection)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository demonstrates a Windows shellcode injection technique that avoids creating new RWX memory by hunting for existing RWX pages inside a legitimate running process (OneDrive.exe). - It enumerates OneDrive.exe, opens a handle, queries its virtual memory layout, locates RWX regions, writes shellcode into an existing RWX region, and executes it. - Execution is performed by “forking” OneDrive.exe into a new process and setting the forked process start address to the injected shellcode, then terminating the clone after execution. - The approach aims to reduce common telemetry associated with VirtualAllocEx/VirtualProtect-based injection patterns. - It is primarily useful for red teamers and malware developers researching defense evasion and alternative injection primitives, and for blue teamers building detections around RWX page usage and process cloning/fork behaviors.
Technical Focus: - Remote process memory enumeration (VirtualQueryEx / memory region scanning) - Reuse of existing RWX memory regions (no new RWX allocation) - Shellcode writing into a remote process (WriteProcessMemory) - Windows “fork”/process cloning API usage for execution - Start-address manipulation / execution redirection in a cloned process - NTDLL-based position-independent shellcode constraints
Use Cases: - Proof-of-concept defense-evasion injection without VirtualAllocEx/VirtualProtect - Researching detection opportunities around RWX pages in signed/benign processes (e.g., OneDrive.exe) - Developing and testing NTDLL-based shellcode execution chains - Building EDR analytics for suspicious RWX region reuse + remote writes + process cloning
Keywords: Windows injection, RWX memory, VirtualQueryEx, WriteProcessMemory, OneDrive.exe, process handle, memory scanning, shellcode, NTDLL shellcode, defense evasion, EDR bypass, AV bypass, process cloning, fork API, start address, remote code execution, C++, malware development, FUD

entry.title: DllShimmer
entry.summary:
Title: DllShimmer
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - DllShimmer is a Go-based generator for creating proxy/backdoored Windows DLLs to “weaponize” DLL hijacking/sideloading. - It parses a target DLL’s export table to extract exported function names, ordinals, and forwarded exports, then generates C++ proxy boilerplate plus a matching `.def` file. - The goal is to preserve normal program behavior while allowing the operator to insert custom code into exported functions, without needing reverse engineering or function signature knowledge. - A key design point is producing a proxy whose Export Address Table (EAT) mirrors the original (including ordinals and forwarded exports), avoiding the suspicious “new EAT” patterns common in simpler proxy generators. - It supports dynamic forwarding via `LoadLibraryA()`/`GetProcAddress()` by default, and an optional static-linking mode that emits an import library (`.lib`) with tradeoffs in path control and debuggability. - Useful primarily for red teams, pentesters, and malware developers building DLL proxy payloads; also relevant to blue teams for understanding realistic proxy-DLL tradecraft and artifacts.
Technical Focus: - PE export parsing (EAT, ordinals, forwarded exports) - Proxy DLL generation (C++ stubs + `.def` export mapping) - DLL hijacking / DLL sideloading tradecraft - Dynamic API resolution (`LoadLibraryA`, `GetProcAddress`) - Static vs dynamic linking behaviors in Windows loader - Operational constraints (calling conventions, argument/register handling, current directory issues)
Use Cases: - Generate a proxy DLL that preserves all exports while executing custom code on function calls - Build DLL sideloading payloads for initial access or persistence in Windows applications - Rapidly create “drop-in replacement” DLLs for testing hijackable search-order scenarios - Reproduce and study export-table–matching techniques for detection engineering and forensics
Keywords: Windows, DLL hijacking, DLL sideloading, proxy DLL, PE parsing, Export Address Table, EAT, forwarded exports, ordinals, .def file, LoadLibraryA, GetProcAddress, import library, static linking, dynamic linking, MinGW-w64, x86-64, calling convention, SetCurrentDirectoryW, persistence

entry.title: epic
entry.summary:
Title: EPIC (Extensible Position Independent Code)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - EPIC is a single-binary toolkit that streamlines writing, compiling, and linking Windows x64 position-independent code (PIC) payloads/shellcode in C/C++. - It solves common shellcode development pain points: predictable compilation output, avoiding unintended dependencies/bloat from standard headers/libc, and producing small payloads via dead-code elimination. - The workflow separates compilation (`pic-compile`) from linking (`pic-link`) so developers can compile once and rapidly generate different payload variants by selecting modules at link time. - It provides a PIC-compatible minimal `libc` and Win32 headers, plus a loader template generator to quickly test payloads as a normal EXE. - A notable technique is its “global context” mechanism using a fixed preserved register (RBX) to emulate global RW state without changing memory permissions. - Useful primarily for malware developers and red team operators building modular implants and shellcode, and for researchers who want reproducible PIC builds and inspectable linker maps.
Technical Focus: - Windows x64 PIC/shellcode build pipeline (GCC/LD/objcopy with custom linker scripts) - Module-based linking and symbol availability checks (`MODULE`, `EXISTS`) - Dead-code elimination via function sections and linker garbage collection + linker map inspection - PIC-safe minimal libc and Win32 header strategy (avoiding MinGW header bloat) - Register-based global context/state management (RBX preservation) - Loader template generation for payload testing/debugging
Use Cases: - Build modular Windows x64 shellcode payloads with selectable capabilities at link time - Generate minimal `payload.bin` from a PE `.text` extraction for injection into custom loaders - Rapidly test PIC payloads by compiling an embedded loader EXE - Debug payload logic using “monolith” non-PIC builds with standard debugging aids - Audit exactly what code lands in the final payload using generated linker maps
Keywords: EPIC, position-independent code, PIC, shellcode, Windows x64, MinGW-w64, gcc, ld, objcopy, custom linker script, PE .text extraction, dead code elimination, -ffunction-sections, linker map, modular payload, RBX register, global context, WinAPI headers, minimal libc, loader template

entry.title: Venoma
entry.summary:
Title: Venoma — C++ Cobalt Strike Beacon Dropper with API Hashing and Indirect Syscalls
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Venoma is a Windows C++ “raw” Cobalt Strike beacon dropper focused on AV/EDR evasion during payload staging and execution. - It combines compile-time API hashing and runtime dynamic linking to reduce static indicators (imports/strings) and complicate analysis. - The project includes multiple execution and stealth techniques such as PPID spoofing, DLL unhooking (including “Fresh + Perun’s fart”), ETW patching, and indirect syscall execution to bypass user-mode hooks. - It supports several payload execution methods (EnumPageFilesW, local/remote APC) and includes self-deletion for post-execution cleanup. - It also documents integration with the Cobalt Strike Artifact Kit workflow (renaming to artifact64big.exe and using a custom CNA) to generate stageless Windows payloads. - Useful primarily for red teams, pentesters, and malware researchers evaluating defensive coverage and detection gaps around common loader tradecraft.
Technical Focus: - Compile-time API hashing / IAT obfuscation - Runtime dynamic API resolution (dynamic linking) - Indirect syscalls (hook evasion) - DLL unhooking (fresh mapping / “Perun’s fart” technique) - ETW patching (telemetry suppression) - APC-based and callback-based execution (EnumPageFilesW, local/remote APC)
Use Cases: - Building and testing custom Cobalt Strike beacon loaders with reduced static signatures - Evaluating EDR user-mode hooking resilience against indirect syscalls and unhooking - Comparing execution techniques (APC vs callback execution) for stability/detection - Generating Artifact Kit-compatible droppers for controlled red team operations - Researching defensive detections for ETW tampering, PPID spoofing, and loader behaviors
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, beacon dropper, Windows loader, C++, compile-time API hashing, API hashing, dynamic API resolution, IAT obfuscation, PPID spoofing, DLL unhooking, ETW patching, indirect syscalls, syscall stubs, APC injection, remote APC, EnumPageFilesW, Artifact Kit, stageless payload, self-deletion, EDR evasion

entry.title: Caro Kann
entry.summary:
Title: Caro-Kann — Encrypted Shellcode Injection to Avoid Kernel-Triggered Memory Scans
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Caro-Kann is a PoC injector that aims to evade EDR memory scanning triggered by common injection/execute primitives and kernel callbacks (e.g., ETW TI / thread creation notifications). - It splits payload delivery into two stages: an encrypted “known-malicious” payload stored in RW memory and a small “unknown” loader shellcode placed in RX memory. - A remote thread is created to run the loader, which sleeps to outlast immediate scan windows, decrypts the real payload, flips memory protections from RW→RX, and then jumps into the decrypted payload. - The project highlights how certain Windows API combinations (e.g., remote thread creation) can trigger scans, and how keeping recognizable payload bytes non-executable until just-in-time can reduce detections. - It’s primarily useful for red teamers, pentesters, and malware developers experimenting with injection tradecraft and EDR evasion assumptions; it also provides defenders insight into a common “staged decrypt + protect + execute” pattern.
Technical Focus: - Staged shellcode injection (RW encrypted payload + RX loader) - Just-in-time decryption and memory protection changes (RW→RX) - Remote thread creation as an execution primitive - EDR scan triggers from userland hooks vs kernel callbacks (ETW TI / notify routines) - API resolving / PIC shellcode building and extraction (mingw-w64, asm, ld)
Use Cases: - Building PoC loaders that reduce static in-memory signatures prior to execution - Testing EDR behavior around kernel-triggered memory scans on injection events - Developing/benchmarking staged payload execution (sleep → decrypt → VirtualProtect → jump) - Creating training material for detection engineering around late-decrypt execution chains
Keywords: shellcode injection, encrypted payload, XOR encryption, RW to RX, VirtualProtect, NtCreateThreadEx, remote thread, ETW TI, kernel callbacks, PsSetCreateThreadNotifyRoutine, memory scanning, EDR evasion, staged loader, PIC shellcode, API hashing/resolving, Nim, winim, mingw-w64, module stomping, indirect syscalls

entry.title: PoolParty
entry.summary:
Title: PoolParty — Process Injection via Windows Thread Pools (Black Hat EU 2023)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - PoolParty is a C++ proof-of-concept collection of process injection techniques that execute payloads by abusing Windows Thread Pool internals rather than creating new remote threads. - It targets EDR detection gaps around common injection primitives (e.g., CreateRemoteThread) by hijacking or planting thread-pool work items inside an existing target process. - The repo implements multiple variants, including overwriting a worker factory start routine and inserting crafted TP_* objects (TP_WORK, TP_WAIT, TP_IO, TP_ALPC, TP_JOB, TP_DIRECT, TP_TIMER) into the target’s thread pool structures. - Execution is triggered through legitimate thread pool mechanisms (e.g., timer queue expiration) after allocating/writing shellcode into the remote process. - Useful for red teams, pentesters, and malware researchers studying stealthy code execution and Windows internals; also relevant for blue teams building detections around thread pool manipulation. - Interesting because it operationalizes lesser-monitored kernel/user-mode primitives (worker factories, thread pool queues) into repeatable injection patterns presented at Black Hat EU 2023.
Technical Focus: - Windows Thread Pool internals (TP_POOL and TP_* work item structures) - Worker Factory hijacking / start routine overwrite - Remote process memory allocation and shellcode staging - Queue/list manipulation to enqueue crafted thread-pool objects - Triggering execution via timer queue / thread pool dispatch paths - EDR evasion via avoiding classic remote-thread creation
Use Cases: - Stealthy payload execution inside a remote process without CreateRemoteThread - Researching/demonstrating thread pool–based injection tradecraft - Developing and validating defensive detections for thread pool tampering - Comparing injection telemetry coverage across EDR products - Training material for Windows internals and offensive code execution techniques
Keywords: Windows thread pool, process injection, TP_WORK, TP_WAIT, TP_IO, TP_ALPC, TP_JOB, TP_DIRECT, TP_TIMER, worker factory, start routine overwrite, TP_POOL, timer queue, remote code execution, shellcode, WinExec, EDR evasion, Windows internals, Black Hat EU 2023, C++ PoC

entry.title: snapinject rs
entry.summary:
Title: snapinject_rs — Process Snapshotting Shellcode Injection (Rust)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository is a Rust proof-of-concept implementing remote process injection using Windows process snapshotting, derived from ORCA000’s “snaploader.” - It creates a sacrificial process, takes a snapshot of that process, and injects shellcode into the snapshot-based execution context. - The project is intentionally minimal and does not include common evasion features like dynamic API resolution. - It can be built as a standalone EXE (replace the `SHELL_CODE` in `main.rs`) or consumed as a Rust library via a single `inject_shellcode(process_name, shellcode)` call. - It’s primarily useful for red teamers and security researchers exploring alternative injection primitives and how snapshot-based approaches behave under EDR telemetry. - Its value is as a readable reference implementation for snapshot-based injection workflows in Rust.
Technical Focus: - Windows process injection (remote shellcode injection) - Process snapshotting-based execution (snaploader-style technique) - Sacrificial process creation and manipulation - Rust FFI / Windows API interop patterns - PoC tradeoffs (no dynamic API resolution / limited stealth)
Use Cases: - Build and test snapshot-based injection PoCs in Rust - Compare EDR detection/telemetry of snapshot injection vs classic techniques (e.g., WriteProcessMemory/CreateRemoteThread) - Embed an injection primitive into a larger Rust offensive toolchain - Research and prototype defensive detections for snapshotting/injection behavior
Keywords: Rust, Windows, process injection, shellcode, remote injection, process snapshotting, snaploader, sacrificial process, WinAPI, FFI, EDR telemetry, offensive tooling, PoC, MIT license, inject_shellcode, malware tradecraft, red team, detection research

entry.title: bof launcher
entry.summary:
Title: bof-launcher (Beacon Object File launcher)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - bof-launcher is a standalone library for loading and executing Cobalt Strike–style Beacon Object Files (BOFs) outside of Beacon, on Windows and Linux across multiple architectures (x86/x64, ARM/AArch64 on Linux). - It solves the problem of reliably running COFF (Windows) and ELF (Linux) BOF modules in custom tooling by handling object parsing, relocations, symbol resolution, argument passing, and output capture. - The project provides clean C and Zig APIs, supports integration from C/C++/Zig/Rust/Go, and aims for zero dependencies (including avoiding libc). - It includes features for operational flexibility such as asynchronous/long-running BOFs in separate threads, “sacrificial process” patterns for risky modules, and “BOF chaining” via a flexible API. - The repo also ships a growing BOF collection (many written in Zig) plus build tooling to cross-compile and test (including QEMU-assisted foreign-arch tests). - It’s useful for red teams, implant/C2 developers, and BOF authors who want portable post-ex modules and a non-Beacon execution environment.
Technical Focus: - COFF and ELF object loading (parsing, relocations, symbol resolution) - Cross-platform BOF execution runtime (Windows/Linux; x86/x64/ARM/AArch64) - Zero-dependency loader design (no libc) - Zig-based build/cross-compilation and QEMU test execution - BOF execution patterns: threading, sacrificial process, chaining - BOF documentation extraction to YAML for tooling (cli4bofs integration)
Use Cases: - Embed BOF execution into custom implants/C2 agents on Windows and Linux - Rapidly test/debug third-party BOFs without Cobalt Strike Beacon (via cli4bofs) - Build cross-platform post-ex modules in Zig (or C) and run them as BOFs - Execute higher-risk modules (e.g., privesc) in a sacrificial process to reduce blast radius - Chain multiple BOFs as modular tasking in an operator workflow
Keywords: Beacon Object File, BOF, Cobalt Strike, COFF, ELF, in-memory execution, relocations, symbol resolution, Win32 API, Linux, x86, x64, ARM, AArch64, Zig, C API, Rust FFI, Go integration, QEMU, post-exploitation

entry.title: FaceDancer
entry.summary:
Title: FaceDancer
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): FaceDancer is a Windows exploitation utility for generating hijackable, proxy-based DLLs that execute attacker-supplied shellcode while forwarding legitimate exported functions to the real DLL. It focuses on two workflows: recon (extracting exports and generating a .def file for proxying) and attack (building a proxy DLL and, for COM techniques, producing the required HKCU registry entries). The tool targets DLL search-order/weak-permission hijacks (e.g., writable AppData plugin directories) and COM-based system DLL image loading where processes query HKCU COM registrations before falling back to machine-wide entries. This enables low-privilege code execution/persistence by abusing trusted/native processes (including WindowsApps-packaged apps like new Outlook/Teams) without needing to write into protected install directories. FaceDancer explicitly does not implement EDR evasion; operators must provide their own “malicious DLL” payload that contains any desired stealth. It’s primarily useful for red teams and pentesters building reliable DLL proxy hijacks and COM hijack chains.
Technical Focus: - DLL proxying via export forwarding using .def files - DLL hijacking via weak directory permissions / search-order abuse - COM hijacking / COM-based DLL load control via HKCU registry precedence - Shellcode embedding by converting an input DLL payload - Targeting WindowsApps-packaged applications (e.g., olk.exe, ms-teams.exe)
Use Cases: - Generate a proxy DLL that preserves application stability while executing payload code - Identify/export all functions from a target DLL to build correct forwarding stubs - Achieve low-privilege persistence by planting writable-folder proxy DLLs (e.g., add-ins/plugins) - Achieve low-privilege execution by registering user-level COM entries to redirect system DLL loads - Build process-specific COM proxy triggers (only execute when a chosen process loads the COM DLL)
Keywords: DLL hijacking, DLL proxying, export forwarding, .def file, Windows loader, COM hijack, HKCU registry, CLSID, InprocServer32, image load, WindowsApps, Outlook (olk.exe), Microsoft Teams (ms-teams.exe), svchost, Explorer, persistence, initial access, shellcode, Rust, application whitelisting bypass

entry.title: SourcePoint
entry.summary:
Title: SourcePoint — Polymorphic Cobalt Strike Malleable C2 Profile Generator
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - SourcePoint is a Go-based generator that creates polymorphic Malleable C2 profiles for Cobalt Strike to reduce static indicators and improve C2 evasion. - It solves the operational problem of repeatedly hand-crafting and maintaining complex, environment-specific profiles by randomizing or parameterizing many profile features and producing ready-to-use output. - The project covers profile controls across global beacon behavior (sleep/jitter, user-agent, headers), HTTP(S) traffic shaping (multiple baked-in templates plus custom profiles), and in-memory tradecraft (staging options, syscall modes, sleep mask, smart inject, PE/reflective loader modifications). - It includes options for process injection behavior (allocator choice, RWX handling, minimum allocation, execution methods) and post-execution settings (spawnto, AMSI disable option, keylogger API choice, thread start spoofing). - Useful primarily for red teams and penetration testers running Cobalt Strike infrastructure; also relevant to blue teams for understanding how beacon profiles can be diversified to evade signature-based detections. - Interesting because it operationalizes many Malleable C2 and Beacon configuration knobs (including newer BeaconGate controls) into repeatable, on-the-fly profile generation.
Technical Focus: - Cobalt Strike Malleable C2 profile generation and traffic shaping (HTTP/HTTPS) - Beacon staging and reflective loader customization (stomppe, obfuscate, magic MZ/PE, transformations) - Process injection configuration (VirtualAllocEx vs NtMapViewOfSection, memory permissions) - Syscall modes (none/direct/indirect) and “smart inject”/sleep mask features - Post-execution behavior tuning (spawnto, thread start spoofing, keylogger API selection) - Redirector/edge compatibility (X-Forwarded-For, CloudFront/AzureEdge-oriented profiles)
Use Cases: - Generate unique Malleable C2 profiles per operation/teamserver to reduce repeatable IoCs - Rapidly prototype and test HTTP(S) beacon traffic patterns that blend with target environments - Standardize profile hardening (sleep/jitter, data jitter, headers, staging choices) via YAML configs - Tune injection and post-execution settings to match an engagement’s detection surface - Create CloudFront/AzureEdge-compatible profiles and configure redirector-aware headers
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, Malleable C2, C2 profile, beacon, polymorphic profiles, evasion, traffic shaping, HTTP GET/POST, User-Agent, X-Forwarded-For, CloudFront, AzureEdge, wininet, winhttp, VirtualAllocEx, NtMapViewOfSection, reflective DLL, stomppe, sleep mask, smart inject, direct syscalls, indirect syscalls, BeaconGate, AMSI, spawnto, SetWindowsHookEx, GetAsyncKeyState, PE header cloning, YAML configuration

entry.title: kernel callback removal
entry.summary:
Title: Kernel Callbacks Removal (Bypassing EDR Detections)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository contains Windows kernel-mode techniques and PoCs for disabling or bypassing common EDR telemetry by removing/neutralizing kernel callback mechanisms. - It targets multiple monitoring surfaces used by EDRs, including process/thread/image notify routines, file system minifilter callbacks, network callout callbacks, and ETW Threat Intelligence (ETW-TI). - The projects rely on version-specific offsets and opcode/pattern scanning to locate internal kernel structures, with a strong warning that incorrect values can cause BSODs. - A highlighted “new” technique describes evading kernel-level verification by overwriting the callback function pointer inside a callback entry with a KCFG-compliant stub that returns, avoiding detections that watch the callback array itself (notably for notify routines). - It is primarily useful for red teamers, exploit developers, and security researchers studying kernel telemetry suppression, and for defenders validating assumptions about kernel-based detections. - The codebase emphasizes hands-on reversing/debugging workflows (WinDbg/IDA) to adapt to specific Windows builds (tested on Windows 11 build 26100).
Technical Focus: - Kernel notify routines (PsSet*NotifyRoutine) callback discovery and tampering - File system minifilter callback bypass (FltMgr / minifilters) - Windows Filtering Platform (WFP) / network callout callback manipulation - ETW Threat Intelligence (ETW-TI) suppression/bypass - Pattern scanning, offsets, and build-specific kernel structure reversing - KCFG (Kernel Control Flow Guard) constraints and compliant callback stubbing
Use Cases: - Build a lab PoC to measure how an EDR depends on kernel callbacks for visibility - Develop/modify kernel telemetry suppression techniques for controlled red team engagements - Create detection engineering tests for callback integrity monitoring and tamper alerts - Reverse Windows kernel structures per-build to understand callback registration internals - Validate defensive assumptions around ETW-TI and kernel callback-based detections
Keywords: Windows kernel, EDR bypass, kernel callbacks, notify routines, PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutine, PsSetCreateThreadNotifyRoutine, PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine, minifilter, FltMgr, file callback, WFP, callout driver, network callout, ETW-TI, Event Tracing for Windows, pattern scanning, offsets, WinDbg, IDA, KCFG, Control Flow Guard, kernel tampering, BSOD risk

entry.title: LayeredSyscall
entry.summary:
Title: LayeredSyscall – Abusing VEH to Bypass EDRs (Legitimate Call Stack + Indirect Syscalls)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): LayeredSyscall is a Windows user-mode evasion technique/tool that combines indirect syscalls with call stack spoofing to bypass user-land EDR API hooks. It abuses Vectored Exception Handling (VEH) to generate a “legitimate-looking” call stack frame while executing sensitive NT syscalls, addressing the common detection gap where indirect syscalls produce anomalous stacks. The repo provides C/C++ wrappers (prefix `wrp` to syscall names) and hook initialization/teardown routines (`IntializeHooks()` / `DestroyHooks()`). Users can customize which benign Windows API call is used to shape the stack (default demo uses `MessageBox`), enabling per-API stack selection. It’s primarily useful for red teams, offensive security researchers, and malware developers evaluating or bypassing user-mode telemetry, and for blue teams studying detection opportunities around VEH registration and stack heuristics.
Technical Focus: - Vectored Exception Handling (VEH) abuse (AddVectoredExceptionHandler-style flow) - Indirect syscalls / direct NT syscall invocation - User-mode EDR hook bypass (ntdll/API hooking evasion) - Call stack spoofing / stack frame fabrication - Windows NT process creation via `NtCreateUserProcess` and RTL helpers
Use Cases: - Integrate syscall wrappers into implants/loaders to reduce user-mode hook visibility - Proof-of-concept research on stack-based detections vs. syscall-based evasion - Testing EDR resilience to VEH-based control-flow and stack anomalies - Customizing “legitimate” stack origins per operation (e.g., process/thread/memory syscalls)
Keywords: Windows, EDR bypass, user-mode hooks, ntdll, indirect syscall, direct syscall, Vectored Exception Handling, VEH, call stack spoofing, stack frames, NtCreateUserProcess, RtlCreateProcessParametersEx, UNICODE_STRING, PS_CREATE_INFO, PS_ATTRIBUTE_LIST, MessageBox, exception handler registration, telemetry evasion, C/C++ PoC

entry.title: StackMask
entry.summary:
Title: StackMask (Masking the Implant with Stack Encryption)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): StackMask is a proof-of-concept demonstrating how to encrypt a thread’s stack before entering a custom “sleep” routine to reduce in-memory artifacts during idle periods. It targets the common detection surface where EDRs snapshot memory (including stack contents) while implants are sleeping or waiting. The PoC retrieves the current stack pointer (RSP), uses `VirtualQuery` to determine the stack memory region boundaries, then encrypts that range. To avoid instability while modifying stack memory, the workflow includes suspending the thread prior to encryption. The “sleep” mechanism leverages CPU cycles (time-based evasion) rather than a straightforward `Sleep()` call, referencing prior work on sandbox/analysis evasion. It’s primarily useful for red team operators and malware developers exploring in-memory stealth and anti-analysis tradecraft.
Technical Focus: - Stack boundary discovery via RSP and `VirtualQuery` - In-place stack encryption/decryption around sleep - Thread suspension to safely modify stack memory - Custom sleep using CPU-cycle/time-based techniques - Windows virtual memory page querying and protections
Use Cases: - Reduce stack-based forensic artifacts during implant sleep/idle - Experiment with EDR evasion concepts around memory snapshotting - Research custom sleep implementations that avoid `Sleep()` telemetry - Prototype defensive detections for anomalous stack memory changes
Keywords: Windows, stack encryption, RSP, VirtualQuery, virtual memory, thread suspension, custom sleep, CPU cycles, time-based evasion, in-memory evasion, EDR bypass, implant masking, memory artifacts, C, PoC, sandbox evasion, runtime encryption, stack region discovery

entry.title: Rust for Malware Development
entry.summary:
Title: Rust for Malware Development
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - A large Rust codebase of Windows-focused malware development and red team PoCs covering execution, injection, evasion, persistence, and C2-style components. - It consolidates many “maldev” building blocks (shellcode loaders, process manipulation, syscall techniques, API hooking/unhooking, and anti-analysis) into runnable, documented snippets and mini-projects. - Techniques include process ghosting/herpaderping/hollowing, PPID spoofing, thread hijacking variants, direct/indirect syscalls (e.g., Tartarus/Recycled Gate), AMSI bypasses, ETW/stack-trace evasion concepts (e.g., proxy DLL loads), and sleep obfuscation (Ekko). - It also includes encryption/obfuscation utilities for payloads and strings plus helper tooling (PEB offset finding, dynamic export resolution, EDR checker, WinHTTP/NtSockets downloaders). - Useful primarily for red teamers, malware researchers, and offensive tool developers learning or prototyping Windows tradecraft in Rust; secondarily useful for blue teams to understand technique implementations for detection engineering. - Interesting because it maps many modern Windows offensive patterns into Rust using windows-rs/NTAPI patterns and emphasizes modular PoCs that can be recombined.
Technical Focus: - Windows process injection & execution (hollowing, ghosting, herpaderping, early-cascade, thread hijacking) - NTAPI usage and direct/indirect/parallel syscalls; unhooking gates (Tartarus/Recycled) - EDR/AV evasion primitives (AMSI bypass, anti-debugging, sleep obfuscation, proxy DLL load/ETW-related evasion) - Dynamic API resolution via PEB/export parsing and hashing - Payload encryption/obfuscation (AES/RC4/XOR, SystemFunction032/033, format-based shellcode obfuscation) - Windows networking/IO for staging (WinHTTP, NtCreateFile/NtDeviceIoControlFile “NtSockets”, named pipes)
Use Cases: - Build and test Rust-based shellcode loaders and injection chains on Windows - Prototype syscall-based execution paths to reduce userland API hooking exposure - Implement staging/downloading and IPC (WinHTTP, named pipes) in offensive implants - Experiment with anti-analysis (anti-debug/anti-VM) and timing/sleep obfuscation - Create lab PoCs for detection validation around process tampering and injection behaviors - Study/port common maldev techniques into custom Rust tooling
Keywords: Rust, Windows, windows-rs, WinAPI, NTAPI, ntdll, syscalls, direct syscalls, indirect syscalls, TartarusGate, RecycledGate, process injection, process hollowing, process ghosting, herpaderping, PPID spoofing, thread hijacking, AMSI bypass, API hooking, PEB parsing, ETW, sleep obfuscation, WinHTTP, named pipes, shellcode encryption

entry.title: CallStackSpoofer
entry.summary:
Title: CallStackSpoofer (WithSecureLabs)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository is a proof-of-concept for spoofing arbitrary user-mode call stacks when executing Windows system calls (e.g., calling `NtOpenProcess`). - The goal is to confuse or evade EDR detections that rely on call stack inspection/stack-walking to attribute suspicious syscalls (such as opening handles to LSASS) to malicious code paths. - It provides sample “legitimate-looking” stacks (selectable via `--wmi`, `--rpc`, `--svchost`) derived from Sysmon process access telemetry, and demonstrates how to reproduce those stack shapes during the syscall. - The PoC is Windows-build sensitive (tested on Windows 10 21H2 19044.1706) because stack/unwind details and offsets can vary across versions. - The README includes practical debugging guidance using WinDbg (`bp ntdll!NtOpenProcess`, `knf`) to validate stack layout, child-SP usage, and per-function stack consumption. - Useful primarily for red teamers, offensive tool developers, and security researchers studying stack-based EDR telemetry and syscall attribution.
Technical Focus: - User-mode call stack spoofing for syscalls (e.g., `ntdll!NtOpenProcess`) - Windows x64 stack walking, unwind metadata (UNWIND_CODE), and stack size calculation - EDR/Sysmon call-stack-based detection evasion and syscall attribution - WinDbg stack analysis (`knf`, breakpoints) and symbol resolution - Mimicking benign execution chains (WMI/RPC/svchost-like stacks)
Use Cases: - Building/validating syscall wrappers that present benign-looking call stacks - Researching how EDRs correlate syscalls with user-mode call stacks - Reproducing “known-good” stack traces from Sysmon/telemetry to test detections - Developing and debugging stack spoofing implementations across Windows builds
Keywords: call stack spoofing, syscall, NtOpenProcess, ntdll, Windows x64, stack walking, unwind info, UNWIND_CODE, Child-SP, WinDbg, knf, Sysmon, process access, LSASS, EDR evasion, RPC, WMI, svchost, KERNELBASE, RPCRT4

entry.title: ModuleStomping
entry.summary:
Title: WithSecureLabs ModuleStomping (Countercept) – Hiding Malicious Code with Module Stomping
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository contains proof-of-concept code and supporting materials for Countercept/WithSecure research on “module stomping,” a Windows code-injection/evasion technique. - The core idea is to load a legitimate module into a process and overwrite (“stomp”) parts of that module’s mapped image/sections with attacker-controlled payload code, then execute it, aiming to blend into normal module-backed memory rather than obvious anonymous RWX regions. - The repo includes multiple components (injectors, payloads/utilities, a driver, and a UI) corresponding to parts of the accompanying blog series, with documentation included in the `documentation` directory. - It focuses on practical implementation details of manipulating PE sections and process memory to host and run shellcode under the guise of a legitimate DLL/module mapping. - Useful primarily for red teams, pentesters, and malware researchers studying injection tradecraft and EDR detection gaps; also relevant for blue teams building detections around suspicious module section modifications and execution flow. - Interesting because it demonstrates an alternative to classic VirtualAlloc-based injection patterns by abusing the trust/structure of loaded modules.
Technical Focus: - Windows process injection and in-memory execution - PE/module mapping and section manipulation (“stomping”) - Memory protections and executable page transitions - User-mode injection tooling plus kernel driver support - Evasion tradeoffs vs. common RWX/anonymous memory heuristics
Use Cases: - Implementing and testing module-stomping style payload staging in controlled assessments - Evaluating EDR visibility/detections for modified module-backed memory regions - Building defensive analytics for module section integrity and anomalous execution - Researching alternative injection primitives beyond classic remote allocation + WriteProcessMemory
Keywords: module stomping, process injection, Windows internals, PE sections, DLL, shellcode, memory mapping, WriteProcessMemory, VirtualProtect, CreateRemoteThread, reflective loading, EDR evasion, module-backed memory, code cave, kernel driver, Countercept, WithSecureLabs, C++ PoC, in-memory execution

entry.title: NOPe
entry.summary:
Title: NOPe – Testing Alternate NOP Opcodes for Shellcode “NOP Sleds”
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - NOPe is a small Python tool that experiments with “NOP-like” instruction sequences as alternatives to traditional 0x90 NOP sleds for x86 and x64 shellcode. - It prepends various candidate NOP-equivalent opcodes to a supplied raw shellcode blob and executes the result in memory to validate whether the sequence is safe (i.e., doesn’t break execution). - The repo includes example Windows messagebox shellcode binaries (x86/x64) and notes that you must run the matching Python architecture (32-bit for x86, 64-bit for x64). - It also ships `bin2sc.py`, a helper to convert raw `.bin` shellcode into C array, C string (`\x..`), or Rust byte-array source formats. - This is mainly useful for red teamers, exploit devs, and shellcode authors testing polymorphic padding/landing techniques and exploring instruction-level constraints. - The interesting angle is practical validation of alternative padding bytes that may affect signatures, bad-byte constraints, or decoder/landing reliability.
Technical Focus: - x86/x64 “NOP-like” instruction variants and multi-byte NOPs - In-memory shellcode execution/testing harness (Python) - Shellcode preprocessing (prefixing/padding) and architecture handling - Raw shellcode generation with msfvenom (Windows messagebox payloads) - Shellcode formatting/export to C and Rust (bin-to-source conversion)
Use Cases: - Testing safe alternative NOP sled bytes under bad-character constraints - Evaluating whether specific “do-nothing” instruction sequences preserve shellcode execution - Rapidly validating x86 vs x64 shellcode execution environments (Python arch mismatch checks) - Converting `.bin` shellcode into embeddable C/Rust source for loaders/PoCs - Building/experimenting with polymorphic padding to vary byte patterns
Keywords: NOP sled, NOP-like instructions, x86, x64, shellcode, in-memory execution, opcode variants, multi-byte NOP, bad bytes, polymorphism, msfvenom, windows/messagebox, raw shellcode, Python, bin2sc, C array, C string shellcode, Rust byte array, payload formatting

entry.title: AlternativeShellcodeExec
entry.summary:
Title: Alternative Shellcode Execution Via Callbacks (AlternativeShellcodeExec)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository is a collection of Windows API “callback-based” execution techniques for running position-independent shellcode without relying on classic primitives like `CreateThread`. - It demonstrates many different APIs that accept function pointers (callbacks) and can be repurposed to transfer execution to attacker-controlled code after allocating/writing shellcode in memory. - The project is organized as many small C++ examples, each focusing on a specific callback-capable API (e.g., threadpool waits/timers, enumeration callbacks, fiber-related execution). - It’s primarily useful for red teamers, pentesters, and malware developers exploring alternative in-process execution flows and how they may evade simplistic detections keyed on common injection/execution APIs. - It’s also valuable to blue teams as a reference set for building behavioral detections around unusual callback registration + RWX/RX memory patterns and suspicious control-flow transfers. - The breadth of APIs covered highlights how large the Windows callback surface is and why “block CreateRemoteThread/CreateThread” is not a sufficient defensive strategy.
Technical Focus: - Windows callback function abuse for code execution - Threadpool-based execution (waits/timers) - Enumeration APIs as execution trampolines (GUI/GDI/locale/resource/cert callbacks) - Fiber execution and context manipulation - In-process shellcode staging (allocation, permissions, indirect invocation)
Use Cases: - Build/compare alternative shellcode execution PoCs for Windows - Develop EDR detection logic for callback registration followed by execution from non-module memory - Red team tradecraft research to diversify execution chains beyond `CreateThread` - Training material for Windows internals/control-flow and callback surfaces - Test defensive coverage against indirect execution techniques
Keywords: Windows API, callback execution, shellcode, position-independent code, CreateThreadPoolWait, CreateTimerQueueTimer, SetTimer, EnumWindows, EnumChildWindows, EnumDesktopWindows, EnumDisplayMonitors, CertEnumSystemStore, LdrEnumerateLoadedModules, LdrpCallInitRoutine, fibers, RtlUserFiberStart, FlsAlloc, InitOnceExecuteOnce, indirect code execution

entry.title: Mirage
entry.summary:
Title: Mirage — Abusing VBS Enclaves to Hide Shellcode in VTL1 (PoC)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Mirage is a proof-of-concept memory evasion technique that leverages a vulnerable Virtualization-Based Security (VBS) enclave DLL to store payload data in VTL1 and only materialize executable shellcode in VTL0 briefly. It demonstrates calling vulnerable enclave functions (`SealSettings`/`UnsealSettings`) to shuttle shellcode from the enclave into an RWX buffer, execute it, then overwrite the buffer with a cleanup buffer to reduce post-execution memory artifacts. The looped “unseal → execute → wipe” workflow is intended to complicate EDR memory scanning and forensic capture by minimizing dwell time of recognizable shellcode in normal user-mode memory. The repo includes a known vulnerable `prefs_enclave_x64.dll` and references prior work related to CVE-2023-36880. It’s primarily useful for red teamers, offensive security researchers, and defenders studying emerging VBS/VTL-based evasion tradecraft. The key interest is the abuse of enclave boundaries (VTL1) as a staging area for payloads rather than traditional in-process obfuscation alone.
Technical Focus: - Windows VBS enclaves and VTL0/VTL1 isolation - Enclave API abuse via vulnerable functions (`SealSettings`, `UnsealSettings`) - Shellcode staging, transient RWX allocation, and post-run memory wiping - Memory evasion against user-mode scanning/forensics - CVE-2023-36880-related research lineage (enclave exploitation)
Use Cases: - Prototype VTL1-backed shellcode staging to reduce in-memory exposure in VTL0 - Research and validation of EDR detection gaps around enclave-mediated payload delivery - Development of defensive detections for anomalous enclave loading/calls and RWX transitions - Reproducing and studying vulnerable enclave behavior for patch verification and threat modeling
Keywords: VBS, enclave, VTL1, VTL0, Virtualization-Based Security, Windows internals, shellcode, memory evasion, RWX memory, SealSettings, UnsealSettings, prefs_enclave_x64.dll, cleanup buffer, transient execution, EDR bypass research, CVE-2023-36880, user-mode telemetry, payload staging, Akamai research

entry.title: superdeye
entry.summary:
Title: SuperdEye — Indirect Syscalls (HellHall/TartarusGate) in Pure Go
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - SuperdEye is a Go + Go-assembler implementation of HellHall (a revised TartarusGate approach) to perform indirect Windows syscalls. - It targets the problem of user-mode AV/EDR hooks in `ntdll.dll` by scanning for hooked stubs and recovering the correct System Service Number (SSN) for a desired `Nt*` function. - If the target stub is hooked, it searches neighboring syscall stubs above/below to find a clean pattern and infer the SSN, then builds an indirect syscall invocation. - The library exposes a generic `SuperdSyscall` interface and also provides wrapper functions for some syscalls to resemble `golang.org/x/sys/windows` usage. - It’s primarily useful for red team operators, implant/tooling developers, and researchers experimenting with syscall-based evasion and Windows internals. - Interesting because it brings HellHall/TartarusGate-style SSN recovery and indirect syscalls into a relatively portable Go package, comparable to BananaPhone (more direct/limited neighbor search) and Acheron (address-order SSN deduction).
Technical Focus: - NTDLL user-mode hook detection and stub scanning - SSN (syscall number) recovery via neighbor scanning (TartarusGate/HellHall) - Indirect syscall invocation (syscall trampoline/stub) - Go + Go assembler low-level Windows interop - `Nt*` syscall wrappers aligned with `golang.org/x/sys/windows`
Use Cases: - Building Go-based loaders/implants that avoid calling hooked `ntdll` exports directly - Researching and prototyping syscall evasion techniques against user-mode EDR hooks - Implementing stealthier process injection primitives (e.g., `NtAllocateVirtualMemory`, `NtCreateThreadEx`) - Comparing HellGate/HellHall/TartarusGate-style approaches with other Go syscall projects
Keywords: Windows, ntdll.dll, NtCreateThreadEx, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, SSN, syscall number, indirect syscall, TartarusGate, HellHall, HellGate, EDR hooks, AV bypass, user-mode hooking, syscall stub scanning, Go assembler, golang, golang.org/x/sys/windows, process injection, NTSTATUS, PEB

entry.title: HellsGate
entry.summary:
Title: Hell’s Gate – Original C Implementation of the Hell’s Gate VX Technique
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository contains the original C (with some assembly) implementation accompanying the “Hell’s Gate” paper by Paul Laîné and smelly__vx. - It demonstrates a Windows evasion technique for performing direct/indirect system calls by dynamically resolving NT syscall numbers from ntdll at runtime rather than relying on imported WinAPI stubs. - The approach is commonly used to reduce user-mode API hooking visibility (e.g., by EDR) by invoking syscalls more directly. - The included code serves as a reference implementation for extracting syscall IDs and building syscall stubs, and the repo also ships the PDF paper. - It’s primarily useful for red team operators, malware developers, and security researchers studying Windows internals and user-mode monitoring bypass patterns. - It’s interesting because it captures a widely reused syscall-resolution pattern in a compact, reproducible form that can be adapted into loaders/implants.
Technical Focus: - Windows NT syscalls and syscall number resolution - ntdll parsing / export table inspection - Direct/indirect syscall stubs (syscall; ret) - User-mode API hooking evasion concepts - C/ASM interop for low-level Windows execution
Use Cases: - Building implants/loaders that avoid calling hooked WinAPI functions - Researching and reproducing syscall-based evasion tradecraft - Creating test samples to validate EDR/user-mode hook detections - Teaching/learning Windows syscall mechanics and ntdll internals
Keywords: Hell’s Gate, Windows, ntdll.dll, NTAPI, system calls, syscall numbers, direct syscall, indirect syscall, EDR evasion, user-mode hooks, SSDT, export address table, PE parsing, assembly stub, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, NtProtectVirtualMemory, NtCreateThreadEx, Windows internals

entry.title: Remote DLL Injection with Timer based Shellcode Execution
entry.summary:
Title: Remote DLL Injection with Timer-based Shellcode Execution
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This repository demonstrates a Windows remote DLL injection workflow where an injector loads a DLL into a target process and the injected DLL executes shellcode via the Windows thread pool timer mechanism. It combines classic `CreateRemoteThread` + `LoadLibraryW` DLL injection with in-memory execution triggered by `CreateThreadpoolTimer`, aiming to run payload code on “legitimate” system-managed thread pool threads rather than a newly created suspicious thread. The code includes process enumeration/targeting, remote injection plumbing, and a timer callback that runs shellcode after configuring `TP_CALLBACK_ENVIRON`. This is primarily useful for red teamers, pentesters, and malware researchers studying alternative execution primitives and potential EDR detection gaps around thread pool–based execution. It’s interesting because it shifts execution to thread pool infrastructure (timers/callbacks), which can change telemetry patterns compared to straightforward remote thread shellcode execution.
Technical Focus: - Remote DLL injection (`CreateRemoteThread`, `LoadLibraryW`) - Windows Thread Pool Timers (`CreateThreadpoolTimer`) - Timer callback–driven shellcode execution - `TP_CALLBACK_ENVIRON` configuration - Process enumeration and target selection - In-memory payload execution in a remote process context
Use Cases: - Evaluate EDR/AV visibility of thread pool timer–based execution vs. classic remote thread techniques - Build and test alternative post-exploitation execution chains in Windows userland - Research detection opportunities around thread pool objects, timer callbacks, and injected module behavior - Training/demo code for understanding DLL injection and thread pool internals
Keywords: Windows, C++, DLL injection, remote process injection, CreateRemoteThread, LoadLibraryW, thread pool, CreateThreadpoolTimer, TP_CALLBACK_ENVIRON, timer callback, shellcode execution, in-memory execution, process enumeration, code injection, userland, EDR evasion, Windows internals, callback execution, stealth execution

entry.title: Azalea
entry.summary:
Title: Azalea (GitHub repository) — content unavailable (404)

entry.title: al khaser
entry.summary:
Title: al-khaser (Anti-analysis / Anti-VM / Anti-debugging PoC)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - al-khaser is a Windows PoC “malware” application intended to stress-test anti-malware, sandbox, and analysis environments by executing many real-world anti-analysis techniques. - It helps defenders and researchers validate whether their sandbox/VM artifacts are detectable and whether their monitoring/debugging stack is exposed. - The repo implements broad categories of checks: anti-debugging (PEB flags, NtQueryInformationProcess, breakpoints, TLS callbacks), anti-VM/emulation (registry/files/WMI/SMBIOS/ACPI/CPUID artifacts across VMware/VirtualBox/Hyper-V/KVM/QEMU/Xen/Wine/Parallels), timing-based evasion, and analysis-tool detection. - It also includes anti-injection/module-hiding detection logic and showcases common code injection primitives (e.g., CreateRemoteThread, APC, NtCreateThreadEx, RunPE). - Useful for Blue Teams building stealthier sandboxes/EDR labs, malware analysts hardening detonation environments, and Red Teams/malware devs studying common anti-analysis patterns and their detection surface. - Interesting because it consolidates many “in-the-wild” checks into a single, configurable harness for repeatable testing and regression.
Technical Focus: - Windows anti-debugging (PEB, NtQueryInformationProcess, HideThreadFromDebugger, breakpoint/SEH tricks) - Anti-VM / anti-emulation artifact discovery (registry, filesystem, processes, devices, MAC OUIs) - Firmware/CPU virtualization detection (CPUID hypervisor leafs, SMBIOS/ACPI table inspection) - Timing/sleep evasion (RDTSC, NtDelayExecution, timer APIs, wait APIs) - Anti-injection / hidden module enumeration (Toolhelp, PSAPI, LdrEnumerateLoadedModules, memory walking) - Analysis tool and hook detection (process/DLL indicators, module-bounds hook checks)
Use Cases: - Validate whether a sandbox/VM build leaks common virtualization artifacts (VMware/VirtualBox/Hyper-V/KVM/QEMU/Xen/Wine/Parallels). - Regression-test EDR/sandbox hardening changes against known anti-analysis checks. - Build detections for anti-analysis behavior (API usage patterns, WMI queries, CPUID/RDTSC sequences). - Train malware analysts on common evasion techniques and how to mitigate them in lab environments. - Compare module enumeration approaches to spot injected/hidden modules in a process.
Keywords: Windows, anti-analysis, anti-debugging, anti-VM, sandbox evasion, IsDebuggerPresent, CheckRemoteDebuggerPresent, PEB BeingDebugged, NtQueryInformationProcess, ProcessDebugPort, HideThreadFromDebugger, TLS callbacks, RDTSC, CPUID hypervisor, SMBIOS, ACPI, WMI Win32_BIOS, VirtualBox, VMware, Hyper-V, KVM, QEMU, Xen, Wine, module enumeration, LdrEnumerateLoadedModules, CreateRemoteThread, QueueUserAPC, NtCreateThreadEx, RunPE

entry.title: DarkLoadLibrary
entry.summary:
Title: DarkLoadLibrary
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - DarkLoadLibrary is a custom Windows DLL loader intended for offensive tradecraft, providing a “LoadLibrary-like” capability without relying on the standard loader path. - It supports loading a DLL either from disk (via a path usable by `CreateFileW`) or directly from an in-memory buffer, enabling fileless-style module loading. - An optional mode (`NO_LINK`) avoids linking the loaded module into the PEB module lists, reducing visibility to user-mode enumeration and some monitoring. - The project is associated with research on bypassing image-load kernel callbacks (commonly used by EDR for DLL load telemetry), indicating a focus on evasion of load-notification-based detections. - It is primarily useful for red teams, penetration testers, and implant/tooling developers who need controlled module mapping and execution behavior. - The author notes compatibility gaps versus the full Windows loader due to edge cases (imports, relocations, loader quirks), so some DLLs may not load correctly.
Technical Focus: - Custom PE/DLL loading (manual mapping-style loader) - Loading modules from memory buffers vs filesystem - PEB module list linking/unlinking (stealth loading) - Evasion of image-load monitoring / kernel callback telemetry - Windows loader behavior and edge-case handling
Use Cases: - In-memory loading of post-exploitation capability DLLs without dropping to disk - Reducing module visibility by avoiding PEB linkage (`NO_LINK`) - Testing/validating defensive detections around image-load events and module enumeration - Building custom loaders for implants where standard `LoadLibrary` is too observable
Keywords: Windows, DLL loader, LoadLibrary, manual mapping, PE loading, in-memory DLL, reflective loading, PEB, module list, CreateFileW, image load callbacks, PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine, EDR evasion, user-mode telemetry, offensive tooling, C, Windows internals

entry.title: TitanLdr
entry.summary:
Title: TitanLdr (Titan) – Reflective Loader for Cobalt Strike with DNS Beacon over DoH
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): TitanLdr is a Windows reflective loader written in C and assembly intended for use with Cobalt Strike payloads. It focuses on in-memory PE loading (reflective DLL/BOF-style loading concepts) and includes logic to redirect Cobalt Strike’s DNS Beacon traffic over DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH) to blend C2 into HTTPS. The repository contains PE/PEB parsing code, hashing-based API resolution, and loader components typical of custom in-memory execution chains. It is primarily useful for red team operators and malware developers experimenting with custom loaders and alternate C2 transport paths. The interesting aspect is the combination of a bespoke reflective loader with DoH-based DNS beacon redirection, which can complicate network-based detection and egress controls.
Technical Focus: - Reflective PE loading (manual mapping, relocations, import resolution) - PEB walking / module enumeration for API resolution - Hashed function name resolution - C/assembly loader stubs and position-independent code - DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH) transport for DNS Beacon redirection - Cobalt Strike integration (Aggressor script .cna)
Use Cases: - Custom in-memory execution of Cobalt Strike payloads without standard loaders - Experimenting with DoH as an alternative egress path for DNS-based C2 - Loader tradecraft R&D (API hashing, PEB-based resolution, minimal imports) - Building test cases for EDR/network detections around reflective loading + DoH
Keywords: TitanLdr, reflective loader, manual mapping, PE parsing, PEB, API hashing, GetProcAddress, LoadLibrary, position-independent code, assembly stub, in-memory execution, Cobalt Strike, Aggressor Script, DNS Beacon, DNS-over-HTTPS, DoH, C2 evasion, Windows internals, import resolution, relocations

entry.title: BokuLoader
entry.summary:
Title: BokuLoader: Cobalt Strike User-Defined Reflective Loader (UDRL)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - BokuLoader is a proof-of-concept x64 Cobalt Strike User-Defined Reflective Loader intended to recreate and extend Cobalt Strike’s reflective loading and Malleable PE evasion behaviors. - It focuses on stealthy in-memory loading of Beacon with enhancements like reflective call stack spoofing (synthetic frames), custom API resolution (avoiding `GetProcAddress`/`LoadLibraryA`), and string/import obfuscation. - The loader supports multiple allocation strategies (HeapAlloc, MapViewOfFile, VirtualAlloc) and integrates/implements several Malleable PE options (e.g., `module_x64`, `entry_point`, `userwx`, `sleep_mask`, `obfuscate` with limitations). - It implements indirect NT syscalls (HellsGate/HalosGate) for memory operations (notably `NtProtectVirtualMemory`), aiming to reduce exposure to userland API hooks. - The repo also includes detection guidance describing signatureable strings, allocator artifacts, syscall patterns, and header/PE anomalies (e.g., first 0x1000 bytes zeroed). - Useful primarily for red teams and offensive tool developers building in-house CS loaders, and for blue teams researching detection opportunities around custom reflective loaders.
Technical Focus: - User-Defined Reflective Loader (UDRL) for Cobalt Strike Beacon - Reflective call stack spoofing (synthetic frames) - Indirect/direct syscall techniques (HellsGate, HalosGate) - Custom API/DLL resolution via PEB/TEB traversal (no `GetProcAddress`/`LoadLibraryA`) - Malleable C2 “Malleable PE” evasion feature support (allocator, module stomping, sleepmask, userwx) - Memory forensics/detection artifacts (header wiping, RWX/RX regions, syscall stubs)
Use Cases: - Build and test custom Cobalt Strike reflective loaders for controlled red team operations - Experiment with call stack spoofing and syscall-based memory protection changes - Evaluate EDR detection gaps vs. userland-hook evasion approaches - Develop defensive detections/signatures for UDRLs (strings, memory layout, syscall stub patterns) - Research tradeoffs of different Beacon memory allocators and module stomping behaviors
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, Beacon, UDRL, reflective loader, ReflectiveDLLInjection, Malleable C2, Malleable PE, module stomping, PEB traversal, TEB, InMemoryOrderModuleList, GetProcAddress bypass, LoadLibrary bypass, indirect syscalls, HellsGate, HalosGate, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, NtProtectVirtualMemory, sleep mask, userwx, call stack spoofing, synthetic frames, IAT hooking, pe-sieve, EDR evasion

entry.title: CallWindowProcW ShellcodeLoader
entry.summary:
Title: CallWindowProcW-ShellcodeLoader
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - A minimal Windows shellcode loader written in C that executes payloads via the WinAPI function `CallWindowProcW` instead of creating a new thread. - It allocates memory for shellcode, copies it into the buffer, flips permissions to executable, then invokes the buffer as a window procedure callback. - The key idea is “callback-based execution” on an existing GUI thread, which can reduce telemetry associated with common patterns like `CreateThread`/`NtCreateThreadEx`. - This is primarily useful for red teamers, pentesters, and malware developers exploring alternative execution primitives and EDR evasion tradeoffs. - It’s interesting as a simple example of abusing legitimate GUI/message-processing APIs to blend execution into normal-looking control flow.
Technical Focus: - WinAPI callback execution via `CallWindowProcW` - Shellcode loading (allocate, copy, execute) - Memory permission changes (RW → RX) - GUI thread / message loop execution context - EDR evasion via avoiding thread creation telemetry
Use Cases: - Proof-of-concept callback-based shellcode execution on Windows - Testing EDR detections focused on thread creation vs. callback execution - Building blocks for custom loaders that execute within existing threads - Training/demo code for Windows user32 callback abuse patterns
Keywords: CallWindowProcW, user32.dll, WinAPI, shellcode loader, callback execution, window procedure, GUI thread, CreateThread bypass, VirtualAlloc, VirtualProtect, RWX, memory permissions, code injection, in-process execution, EDR evasion, Windows internals, message processing

entry.title: CopyReadProcessMemory
entry.summary:
Title: CopyReadProcessMemory
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository provides a Windows PoC/tool that abuses the `ReadProcessMemory` API’s `lpNumberOfBytesRead` out-parameter as a write primitive to modify memory without using typical copy/write routines (e.g., `memcpy` / `WriteProcessMemory`). - The goal is to support payload staging/injection workflows while reducing common AV/EDR detections that focus on standard memory-writing APIs and hooked paths (notably `NtReadProcessMemory`). - It supports loading payloads from a local file, a raw string, or a remote URL, and can optionally execute the processed payload. - The project also includes XOR de-obfuscation (`-x`) and a helper Python script to XOR payloads before delivery. - It’s primarily useful for red teams, pentesters, and offensive security researchers exploring alternative memory manipulation primitives and EDR evasion tradecraft.
Technical Focus: - Windows `ReadProcessMemory` semantics and out-parameter abuse (`lpNumberOfBytesRead`) - Memory write/injection primitives without `WriteProcessMemory`/`memcpy` - User-mode API hooking/EDR evasion considerations (e.g., `NtReadProcessMemory` hooks) - Payload staging: local file, command-line string, remote HTTP retrieval - In-memory XOR de-obfuscation and execution flow
Use Cases: - Proof-of-concept demonstrating a nonstandard process-memory write technique via `ReadProcessMemory` - Payload staging/injection experiments designed to avoid common API-based detections - Red-team tradecraft testing against EDR user-mode hooks and telemetry - Lab research into Windows API misuses and defensive detection gaps - Obfuscated shellcode delivery and in-memory decode (XOR) prior to execution
