Keywords: ReadProcessMemory, NtReadProcessMemory, lpNumberOfBytesRead, out-parameter, process memory, memory injection, shellcode, in-memory execution, EDR evasion, AV evasion, API hooking, user-mode hooks, Windows API, payload staging, remote payload, HTTP download, XOR obfuscation, deobfuscation, C++ PoC, unprotect.it

entry.title: Ghost
entry.summary:
Title: Ghost (Evasive Shellcode Loader)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Ghost is a Windows shellcode loader focused on evading common EDR telemetry and in-memory detection. It targets three main detection areas: kernel callback visibility of thread/process execution, stack-unwinding/callstack-based anomaly detection, and memory scanning for known shellcode patterns. To reduce kernel-observable execution artifacts, it executes via Windows Fibers (user-mode scheduling) and uses fiber switching to help hide a beacon’s call stack during sleep. For callstack evasion, it implements return-address spoofing and a hook-based approach to swap fibers around sleep to obscure the beacon’s stack frames. For memory scanning resistance, it uses a “plain sight” technique: allocate a large region, fill with random data via SystemFunction036 (RtlGenRandom), and place shellcode at a random offset among the noise; it also supports embedding shellcode in PE resources. It additionally includes ETW patching, indirect syscalls, unhooking, and custom API hashing/SSN resolution, making it primarily useful for red teams and offensive security researchers evaluating EDR resilience.
Technical Focus: - Windows Fibers for user-mode execution and evasion of kernel callbacks - Call stack spoofing (return address spoofing) and stack-unwinding evasion - Sleep masking via function hooking + fiber switching - Shellcode concealment in large randomized memory regions (“plain sight”) - ETW patching/disablement in-memory - Indirect syscalls, unhooking, API hashing, SSN resolution
Use Cases: - Building/experimenting with evasive in-process shellcode loaders on Windows - Testing EDR detections around callstack integrity, sleep masking, and syscall telemetry - Researching fiber-based execution tradeoffs vs. thread-based injection/execution - Prototyping beacon sleep obfuscation by hooking the beacon’s sleep API - Evaluating memory-scanning robustness against “noise + random placement” techniques
Keywords: Windows shellcode loader, EDR evasion, Fibers, ConvertThreadToFiber, fiber switching, kernel callbacks, stack unwinding, call stack spoofing, return address spoofing, sleep masking, function hooking, indirect syscalls, SSN resolution, API hashing, ETW patching, SystemFunction036, RtlGenRandom, memory scanning evasion, PE resources, Cobalt Strike beacon

entry.title: Dirty Vanity
entry.summary:
Title: Dirty Vanity (Windows Fork API Injection / EDR Evasion PoC)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Dirty-Vanity is a proof-of-concept code injection technique that abuses the Windows “fork”/process cloning capability to evade EDR detections commonly tied to classic remote-thread injection patterns. The tool writes position-independent shellcode into a target process, forks (clones) that process into a new process, and then sets the forked process’s start address to the cloned shellcode so execution occurs in the child. The included shellcode is designed to rely on ntdll APIs and is generated via an embedded shellcode template project, with instructions for customizing and extracting the shellcode bytes. This is primarily useful for red teamers, pentesters, and malware researchers exploring alternative injection primitives and telemetry gaps around process creation/cloning. It’s interesting because it shifts execution to a forked process and can bypass detections focused on CreateRemoteThread/WriteProcessMemory-style sequences. The repo references the Black Hat Europe 2022 talk “Dirty Vanity: A New Approach to Code Injection & EDR Bypass.”
Technical Focus: - Windows process forking / process cloning (fork API) - Shellcode reflection into a remote process - Start address manipulation in a forked child process - ntdll-based shellcode and low-level Windows APIs - Injection tradecraft aimed at EDR telemetry evasion
Use Cases: - Testing EDR visibility/detections around process cloning and non-traditional injection flows - Building/validating alternative injection chains that avoid common remote-thread indicators - Researching defensive telemetry opportunities for fork/clone + start-address tampering - Rapid prototyping custom ntdll-based shellcode and reflecting it via a cloned process
Keywords: Dirty Vanity, Windows fork API, process cloning, code injection, EDR bypass, shellcode reflection, NtCreateUserProcess, ntdll, start address, process fork, remote memory allocation, shellcode template, position-independent code, Windows internals, Black Hat EU 2022, injection technique, telemetry evasion, C++ PoC

entry.title: obex
entry.summary:
Title: Obex – DLL Blocking (Blocking unwanted DLLs in user mode)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Obex is a proof-of-concept Windows user-mode technique/tool to prevent specific DLLs (e.g., EDR/monitoring components) from being loaded into a target process. It spawns an arbitrary process under debug control and blocks a configurable list of module names during early process initialization and also for later dynamic loads via `LoadLibrary*`. The default blocklist includes `amsi.dll`, demonstrating how to start PowerShell without AMSI being loaded. The project is implemented in plain C with no external dependencies and is intended as a practical reference for module-load interference from user mode. It’s primarily useful for red teams, pentesters, and malware researchers exploring defensive evasion and process initialization behavior, and for blue teams to understand and detect such tampering.
Technical Focus: - Windows debugging APIs / debug-controlled process creation - User-mode DLL load interception and blocking - Process initialization and module load order - `LoadLibrary*`-based dynamic module loading control - Module enumeration/verification (loaded module list)
Use Cases: - Launching a process while preventing specific security DLLs from loading (e.g., AMSI/EDR user-mode components) - Researching and prototyping user-mode module-load blocking techniques - Testing defensive detections around suspicious debug usage and missing expected modules - Building controlled lab PoCs for process initialization and DLL load behavior
Keywords: Windows, user-mode, DLL blocking, module load, process initialization, debug control, Debug API, CreateProcess, LoadLibrary, AMSI, amsi.dll, EDR evasion, monitoring DLL, module enumeration, C, PoC, blocklist, PowerShell

entry.title: Kharon
entry.summary:
Title: Kharon v0.1 (AdaptixC2 Agent & HTTP Listener Extender)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Kharon is a custom agent for AdaptixC2 designed to emphasize evasion, capability, and malleability. It is implemented as fully position-independent code (PIC) and explicitly avoids using a reflective loader. The project includes multiple stealth features such as sleep obfuscation, heap obfuscation during sleep, stack spoofing combined with indirect syscalls, and built-in AMSI/ETW bypasses. It also provides a “BOF API proxy” to execute Beacon Object Files with spoofed/indirect API usage, and implements a sizable subset of the Cobalt Strike Beacon API for compatibility. The repo ships both the agent extender and an HTTP listener extender, intended to be integrated into AdaptixServer via Go workspaces and built as extenders. It’s primarily useful for red teams/pentesters building AdaptixC2 operations and for defenders studying modern in-memory tradecraft patterns.
Technical Focus: - Position-independent (PIC) in-memory agent design without reflective loading - Sleep/heap obfuscation techniques - Stack spoofing and indirect syscalls - AMSI and ETW bypass methods - Beacon Object File (BOF) execution via API proxying - C2 integration via AdaptixC2 extenders (agent + HTTP listener)
Use Cases: - Deploying an evasive AdaptixC2 implant with reduced user-mode telemetry - Running BOFs while proxying sensitive Win32/NT APIs to alter call patterns - Testing EDR detections around sleep masking, stack spoofing, and syscall indirection - Extending AdaptixC2 with custom agent/listener components and module ecosystems - Researching Beacon API re-implementations and BOF interoperability
Keywords: AdaptixC2, C2 agent, HTTP listener, PIC, position-independent code, reflective loader avoidance, sleep obfuscation, heap obfuscation, stack spoofing, indirect syscalls, AMSI bypass, ETW bypass, BOF, Beacon Object File, Beacon API, VirtualAlloc, WriteProcessMemory, VirtualProtect, NtGetContextThread, NtSetContextThread, CLRCreateInstance, CoInitializeEx

entry.title: ldrgen
entry.summary:
Title: ldrgen — Template-based generation of shellcode loaders
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - ldrgen is a generator that produces Windows shellcode loader projects from predefined C templates and operator-defined profiles. - It aims to reduce the repetitive work of building new loader variants by templating source files, build artifacts, and configurable placeholders (e.g., encryption keys). - Templates are described in a `templates/config.yaml` that maps a “token” to required source files and substitution variables; profiles (JSON) select a template, set substitutions, choose architecture, and optionally compile automatically. - Example templates include a `CreateThread`-based loader that decrypts XOR-encrypted shellcode before allocating memory and executing it. - It’s primarily useful for red teamers/pentesters who maintain multiple loader implementations and want a consistent, automatable way to generate and compile them (e.g., via mingw-w64). - The interesting aspect is the separation of “loader engineering” (templates) from “operator configuration” (profiles), enabling fast iteration without making the tool a full framework.
Technical Focus: - Template-driven C source generation with placeholder substitution - Profile-based configuration (JSON) and template metadata (YAML) - Windows shellcode loading primitives (VirtualAlloc, memcpy, CreateThread, WaitForSingleObject) - Simple payload obfuscation/encryption workflow (XOR) and key injection - Cross-compilation/build automation (make, x86_64-w64-mingw32-gcc, i686-w64-mingw32-gcc, strip)
Use Cases: - Generate repeatable Windows shellcode loader projects from a standard template library - Rapidly produce loader variants by swapping templates, keys, and build settings - Automate embedding/encrypting shellcode into a stageless loader build pipeline - Maintain a personal catalog of loader implementations and compile them consistently for x86/x64
Keywords: shellcode loader, loader generator, Windows, C, Go, templates, profiles, YAML, JSON, placeholder substitution, XOR encryption, CreateThread, VirtualAlloc, memcpy, WaitForSingleObject, mingw-w64, x86_64-w64-mingw32-gcc, i686-w64-mingw32-gcc, makefile, strip

entry.title: Chimera
entry.summary:
Title: Chimera (Chimera Unlea$ed) — Automated DLL Sideloading Tool With EDR Evasion Capabilities
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Chimera is a Python-based automation tool for generating malicious proxy DLL projects to perform DLL sideloading against specific legitimate applications (currently Microsoft Teams and OneDrive). It takes raw shellcode, encrypts/obfuscates it (XOR with random key or AES), and outputs Visual Studio-ready C/ASM templates that compile into a sideloadable DLL with the required exports. The project focuses on evading EDR/AV by using modified SysWhispers3-style dynamic syscalls, polymorphic code changes (e.g., NOP sleds/register shuffling), timing delays via waitable timers, and sandbox/debugging checks. It also supports process injection via Early Bird APC injection (with “Module Stomping” planned/placeholder). Useful primarily for red teams, pentesters, and malware developers building tradecraft around proxy DLL sideloading and syscall-based evasion; it’s interesting because it packages multiple evasion layers into a repeatable build workflow tied to real-world binaries and missing-DLL scenarios.
Technical Focus: - DLL sideloading / proxy DLL generation (export forwarding) - Shellcode encryption/obfuscation (XOR, AES) - Dynamic syscalls (SysWhispers3) and syscall stub/ASM modification - Early Bird APC injection (process injection) - Sandbox/debugger evasion (disk checks, anti-debug) - Execution delay via waitable timers (timing evasion)
Use Cases: - Automate creation of sideloadable proxy DLLs for Teams/OneDrive-style missing DLL loads - Generate encrypted shellcode loaders embedded in DLL templates for controlled execution - Test EDR detection gaps around dynamic syscalls, polymorphism, and delayed execution - Build and iterate on injection techniques (swap Early Bird with custom methods) - Create reproducible lab PoCs for defensive validation of DLL search order abuse
Keywords: DLL sideloading, proxy DLL, export forwarding, userenv.dll, version.dll, Microsoft Teams, OneDrive, shellcode loader, AES, XOR, polymorphic code, SysWhispers3, dynamic syscalls, syscall stubs, MASM, Early Bird injection, APC queue, anti-debug, sandbox evasion, waitable timers, EDR bypass, Visual Studio template, Windows internals

entry.title: hookchain
entry.summary:
Title: HookChain: A new perspective for Bypassing EDR Solutions
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - HookChain is a Windows user-mode evasion technique/toolchain that combines multiple well-known primitives to bypass EDR telemetry focused on `ntdll.dll` syscall stubs. - It redirects execution flow by using IAT hooking to intercept Win32/NT API usage, then performs dynamic System Service Number (SSN) resolution and executes indirect system calls. - The key idea is to avoid the common EDR hook points in `ntdll.dll` by shifting where and how syscalls are invoked, without requiring source-code changes in the target application/malware. - The repo includes code (C + assembly) plus a whitepaper (PDF) and links to public talks (DEFCON 32, Sector/BlackHat Toronto, MindTheSec). - It is primarily useful for red teams, pentesters, and offensive security researchers evaluating EDR coverage and designing user-mode evasion tradecraft; it is also relevant for blue teams to understand and detect non-`ntdll`-centric syscall paths.
Technical Focus: - Import Address Table (IAT) hooking - Dynamic syscall number (SSN) resolution - Indirect system calls (syscall invocation without standard `ntdll` stubs) - Windows user-mode API interception and control-flow redirection - EDR user-mode hooking assumptions and bypass strategies
Use Cases: - Building/validating EDR bypass PoCs that avoid `ntdll.dll` hooks - Executing sensitive actions (e.g., process access, memory operations) with reduced user-mode telemetry - Researching detection gaps between Win32 APIs, `ntdll`, and syscall dispatch - Developing alternative syscall invocation frameworks for implants/loaders - Creating defensive detections for IAT tampering and anomalous syscall patterns
Keywords: HookChain, EDR bypass, evasion, Windows internals, IAT hooking, Import Address Table, ntdll.dll, syscalls, indirect syscall, SSN resolution, system service number, user-mode hooks, API hooking, assembly, C, endpoint detection and response, telemetry bypass, DEFCON 32, arXiv:2404.16856

entry.title: process cloning
entry.summary:
Title: The Definitive Guide To Process Cloning on Windows (huntandhackett/process-cloning)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository is a long-form technical guide plus C demos/library explaining Windows “process cloning” (fork-like behavior) and how it works internally. - It contrasts cloning via `NtCreateUserProcess`, `NtCreateProcessEx`, `RtlCloneUserProcess`, and remote cloning via `RtlCreateProcessReflection`, including practical limitations and stability pitfalls. - The write-up details what is and isn’t inherited (address space, handles, tokens, jobs), why `NtCreateProcessEx` clones can’t execute threads on modern Windows, and why `RtlCloneUserProcess` is safer due to ntdll state synchronization. - It evaluates offensive applicability, concluding clone-based code injection is generally noisy/detectable (remote thread + handle access + ETW/driver callbacks), while clone-based memory dumping is more viable and maps to documented PSS snapshotting APIs. - It also highlights an under-discussed angle: writable shared/mapped regions inherited by clones can create limited cross-process tampering primitives. - Useful for red teamers, malware researchers, and defenders who need to understand cloning/snapshot telemetry, dumping tradeoffs, and edge-case inheritance behavior.
Technical Focus: - Windows Native API process creation: `NtCreateUserProcess` vs `NtCreateProcessEx` - `RtlCloneUserProcess` synchronization/loader/heap lock considerations - Remote cloning via `RtlCreateProcessReflection` (remote thread + shared memory + events) - Copy-on-write semantics, mapped sections inheritance (`ViewShare`/`ViewUnmap`) - Stealthy memory dumping and Process Snapshotting (Pss*/NtPss*) - Detection surface: Ob callbacks, Ps process/thread notify, ETW/EtwTi
Use Cases: - Clone-based minidump creation of sensitive processes (e.g., LSASS) without suspending the target - Researching/detecting process snapshotting and clone-based dumping tradecraft - Building reliable “fork-like” execution in the current process (with caveat management) - Auditing inherited handles/memory mappings to find writable shared-region tampering opportunities - Developing tooling to compare parent vs clone memory layouts for troubleshooting crashes
Keywords: Windows process cloning, fork, NtCreateUserProcess, NtCreateProcessEx, RtlCloneUserProcess, RtlCreateProcessReflection, PEB, TEB, STATUS_PROCESS_CLONED, PROCESS_CREATE_PROCESS, handle inheritance, job objects, copy-on-write, NtMapViewOfSection, ViewShare, ViewUnmap, MiniDumpWriteDump, LSASS dumping, Process Snapshotting, PssCaptureSnapshot, NtPss, ETW, EtwTi, ObRegisterCallbacks, PsSetCreateThreadNotifyRoutineEx, PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutineEx, remote thread injection

entry.title: LatLoader
entry.summary:
Title: LatLoader — Automated Lateral Movement PoC Module for Havoc C2 (with Elastic EDR rule evasion notes)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): LatLoader is a proof-of-concept Havoc C2 module demonstrating automated lateral movement workflows and BOF/module development. It provides subcommands to upload payloads over SMB and execute commands remotely via WMI, plus combined “one-shot” movement primitives (`load`, `xorload`, `sideload`). The most feature-complete path (`sideload`) performs DLL sideloading of a shellcode loader and documents specific bypasses for multiple default Elastic EDR behavioral rules (e.g., avoiding direct execution of SMB-dropped binaries, leveraging excluded paths, and signing a DLL with an expired certificate). The repo also includes standalone (non-BOF) equivalents of the WMI exec and SMB write utilities to help learners compare implementations. It’s primarily useful for red teamers/pentesters learning Havoc + BOF tradecraft and for defenders validating/understanding detection logic gaps and rule assumptions. Notably, the author highlights that Elastic updated rules shortly after release, making it a good case study in detection iteration and attacker adaptation.
Technical Focus: - Havoc C2 module development and BOF (Beacon Object File) tradecraft - SMB-based remote file transfer and remote tool staging - WMI remote process creation/execution (WBEM/COM) - DLL sideloading for remote execution - EDR behavioral rule evasion patterns (Elastic rules) and detection engineering feedback loop - Shellcode loading and memory permission changes (e.g., VirtualProtect / NtAllocateVirtualMemory, HWBP syscalls PoC)
Use Cases: - Automate lateral movement steps (stage over SMB, execute via WMI) from Havoc - Learn/teach BOF development by comparing BOFs to standalone binaries - Reproduce and study Elastic EDR behavioral detections and bypass conditions - Build lab exercises for purple teaming around lateral movement telemetry - Prototype alternate loaders (XOR shellcode loader, DLL sideloader variants)
Keywords: Havoc C2, BOF, Beacon Object File, lateral movement, SMB, WMI, WBEM, COM, ProcessCreate, DLL sideloading, shellcode loader, VirtualProtect, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, HWSyscalls, hardware breakpoints, Elastic EDR, behavioral rules, YARA, osslsigncode, mingw-w64, code signing, expired certificate, defense evasion

entry.title: hypnus
entry.summary:
Title: hypnus — Sleep Obfuscation in Rust
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Hypnus is a Rust library for “execution/sleep obfuscation” on Windows that encrypts selected memory regions while a program is inactive (sleep window) and restores them afterward. - It aims to reduce in-memory exposure of payloads (e.g., shellcode buffers or even the full PE image) and to make the execution flow harder to analyze by combining memory protection with dynamic call stack spoofing. - The library implements multiple sleep mechanisms using Windows thread pool primitives (`TpSetTimer`, `TpSetWait`) and an APC-based approach (`NtQueueApcThread`), chaining spoofed callbacks rather than duplicating threads. - It optionally supports heap obfuscation via a custom allocator (`HypnusHeap`) and can register spoofed call targets with Control Flow Guard (CFG) when enabled. - It supports `#[no_std]` (with `alloc`) and notes that `no_std` is recommended when obfuscating the entire process image to avoid `std`/TLS-related access violations. - Useful primarily for red teams, offensive tool developers, and malware researchers building stealthier Windows implants/loaders; also relevant to blue teams for understanding modern sleep/memory evasion patterns.
Technical Focus: - Windows sleep obfuscation (timer/wait/APC-based) - Memory region encryption/decryption during inactivity - Dynamic call stack spoofing / stack trace evasion - Windows Thread Pool APIs (`TpSetTimer`, `TpSetWait`) - APC execution (`NtQueueApcThread`) - Heap obfuscation via custom allocator + CFG considerations
Use Cases: - Implement encrypted “sleep” for in-memory payload buffers to reduce scanable dwell time - Add stack-spoofed API chains around sensitive operations (encrypt/decrypt/protect) - Build `no_std` Rust loaders that can obfuscate large memory regions (including PE image) - Experiment with thread-pool–driven callback execution patterns for evasion research - Evaluate/detect threadpool/APC-based sleep obfuscation behaviors in EDR testing
Keywords: Rust, Windows, sleep obfuscation, memory encryption, in-memory evasion, call stack spoofing, Thread Pool, TpSetTimer, TpSetWait, wait objects, APC, NtQueueApcThread, Control Flow Guard, CFG, no_std, alloc, HypnusHeap, custom allocator, RWX protection, Ekko, Foliage, UWD

entry.title: shadowstep
entry.summary:
Title: ShadowStep — In-memory Encrypted Shellcode Execution Suite
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - ShadowStep is a Windows x64 research tool that executes encrypted shellcode such that the payload is never fully decrypted in memory; only a single instruction is briefly plaintext at any time. - It targets memory-scanning and signature-based detections by decrypting, executing, and immediately re-encrypting instructions in a custom single-step execution engine. - The approach avoids common single-step implementations that rely on trap flags, SEH/VEH registration, or breakpoint/debug-like artifacts, aiming to reduce observable indicators. - The repo includes a “Compiler” that transforms a raw shellcode blob into a custom executable and a “Runtime” project that implements the execution engine. - It uses Capstone for disassembly and currently supports XOR and RC4 encryption, with a design choice of x64-only shellcode. - Useful for red teamers, offensive security researchers, and defenders studying evasive in-memory execution and detection opportunities.
Technical Focus: - Instruction-by-instruction (single-step) encrypted shellcode execution - Windows process execution context swapping / virtualized context - In-memory anti-scanning / reduced plaintext exposure - Shellcode loader generation (compiler + runtime build pipeline via MSBuild) - Capstone-based disassembly for instruction handling - XOR/RC4 payload encryption
Use Cases: - Wrap known shellcode to reduce static memory signature exposure during execution - Integrate per-instruction decryption into a custom Windows loader - Research and benchmark EDR memory-scanning resilience vs. “never-fully-decrypted” payloads - Develop detections focused on behavioral artifacts rather than plaintext payload scanning - Create controlled PoCs for reverse-engineering resistance experiments
Keywords: Windows x64, shellcode, in-memory execution, per-instruction decryption, single-step engine, context switching, encrypted payload, XOR, RC4, Capstone, disassembly, loader, MSBuild, Visual Studio, vcpkg, EDR evasion research, memory scanning, runtime stub, compiler toolchain

entry.title: Proxy DLL Loads
entry.summary:
Title: Proxy DLL Loads
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository provides proof-of-concept code for “DLL-load proxying” on Windows to make module loads appear to originate from alternative call paths, commonly to evade ETW-TI stack-based visibility and callstack-based detections. - It demonstrates two main approaches: using a Vectored Exception Handler (VEH) to redirect execution into `LoadLibraryA`, and using undocumented NTDLL threadpool (`Tp*`) APIs to execute `LoadLibrary` via callback stubs (reducing exposure to userland API hooks). - The README walks through “hunting” undocumented `Tp*` exports by reversing `kernel32.dll` implementations and mapping them to underlying `ntdll!Tp*` routines, with Process Hacker headers as a reference. - A third PoC variant adds a “call gadget” from a legitimate DLL (`AuthenticateFAM_SecureFP.dll`) to insert arbitrary frames into the call stack during module load, targeting bypass of Elastic’s callstack signature rule for callback-based library loads. - It includes debugging guidance (register setup for callback stubs) and OPSEC notes (custom `GetModuleHandleA`/`GetProcAddress`, avoid `VirtualProtect` as a VEH trigger). - Useful for red teams, malware developers, and defenders researching detection gaps around callback-based execution and callstack spoofing.
Technical Focus: - DLL-load proxying / callstack manipulation during `LoadLibrary*` - Vectored Exception Handling (VEH) with `PAGE_GUARD` / `STATUS_GUARD_PAGE_VIOLATION` - Undocumented NTDLL threadpool APIs (`TpAlloc*`, `TpSet*`, cleanup groups, timers/waits) - Callback stub construction and register/context manipulation (RIP/RCX) - Call-gadget insertion to break callstack-based detection signatures - Reversing Windows DLL exports (kernel32 → ntdll) with IDA/x64dbg
Use Cases: - Proxying DLL loads to reduce ETW-TI stack-trace fidelity during module load events - Executing `LoadLibraryA` via threadpool callbacks using lower-level/undocumented APIs - Testing and bypassing callstack-based detection rules (e.g., Elastic callback-load rule) - Researching/replicating undocumented `Tp*` interfaces for custom loaders - Building defensive detections for anomalous threadpool callback-based module loads
Keywords: Windows, DLL load proxying, LoadLibraryA, VEH, Vectored Exception Handler, PAGE_GUARD, STATUS_GUARD_PAGE_VIOLATION, VirtualProtect, ntdll, kernel32, undocumented syscalls, threadpool, TpAllocPool, TpAllocTimer, TpAllocWait, TpSetTimer, TpReleaseCleanupGroupMembers, callback function, call stack spoofing, call gadget, ETW-TI, Elastic detection rule, IDA, x64dbg

entry.title: SilentMoonwalk
entry.summary:
Title: SilentMoonwalk — Fully Dynamic Call Stack Spoofer (PoC)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - SilentMoonwalk is a proof-of-concept Windows x64 call stack spoofer that hides the real caller by manipulating stack unwinding metadata/behavior rather than only control flow. - It uses a ROP-based approach to desynchronize the unwinder’s view of the stack from the actual execution path, removing the original EXE image base from the visible call stack and restoring the original stack on return. - The repo demonstrates two approaches: a “Synthetic” mode that inserts fake frames (less robust) and a “Desync stack” mode that replaces unsuitable unwind frames with suitable ones to keep the stack looking legitimate and unwindable. - It targets scenarios where EDR/AV/anti-cheat products inspect user-mode call stacks to detect suspicious API invocation chains. - Includes an UnwindInspector utility to parse and display PE runtime function/unwind info (RUNTIME_FUNCTION / UNWIND_INFO) for selecting usable frames/gadgets. - Useful primarily for red team operators, offensive tool developers, and researchers studying call stack integrity, unwind-based detections, and evasion tradeoffs.
Technical Focus: - Windows x64 stack unwinding (RUNTIME_FUNCTION, UNWIND_INFO, unwind codes) - ROP chains and stack pivoting for control-flow/unwind desynchronization - Call stack spoofing / return address spoofing variants - Frame selection based on unwind semantics (e.g., POP RBP frames) - User-mode telemetry evasion against call-stack-based detections - PE exception handling / unwind metadata inspection
Use Cases: - Spoofing call stacks for sensitive Windows API calls during post-exploitation - Researching and testing EDR/AV call-stack validation heuristics - Building/validating custom indirect syscall or API-call wrappers with plausible stacks - Analyzing module unwind info to find viable gadgets/frames (via UnwindInspector) - Demonstrating limitations and stability constraints of unwind/ROP-based spoofing
Keywords: Windows x64, call stack spoofing, stack spoofing, ROP, return address spoofing, stack pivot, unwinding, RUNTIME_FUNCTION, UNWIND_INFO, unwind codes, POP RBP, PE exception handling, kernelbase.dll, MessageBoxExA, EDR evasion, AV evasion, anti-cheat bypass, gadget discovery, control-flow vs unwind desync, UnwindInspector

entry.title: AceLdr
entry.summary:
Title: AceLdr – Avoid Memory Scanners
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): AceLdr is a position-independent reflective loader (UDRL) for Cobalt Strike Beacon designed to evade common in-memory scanners and signature-based hunting tools. It focuses on reducing or eliminating recognizable Beacon artifacts in process memory, including during sleep, to defeat tools like Moneta, PE-sieve, BeaconEye, and Hunt-Sleeping-Beacons. The loader implements dynamic heap usage with encryption of Beacon allocations before sleep, and encrypts/obfuscates Beacon code pages while marking them non-executable (inspired by FOLIAGE). It also includes return-address spoofing for selected WinAPI/NT calls and a “sleep without sleep” approach using wait APIs rather than a direct Sleep pattern. Encryption is performed via RC4 using SystemFunction032. It’s primarily useful for red teams and offensive operators customizing Cobalt Strike tradecraft, and for defenders studying modern memory-evasion patterns.
Technical Focus: - Position-independent reflective loading (UDRL) for Cobalt Strike Beacon - Memory artifact reduction: heap isolation, page permission changes, code/data encryption - Sleep obfuscation (“sleep without sleep”) via wait primitives - Return address spoofing for API call stack evasion - RC4 via SystemFunction032 (advapi32/crypt routines) - Evasion of memory scanners (Moneta, PE-sieve, Beacon-focused hunters)
Use Cases: - Deploying Cobalt Strike Beacon with reduced in-memory signatures during sleep/idle - Testing and benchmarking EDR/memory-scanner detections against encrypted/hidden Beacon memory - Researching defensive detections for heap encryption, RX→RW/NX transitions, and wait-based sleep patterns - Building custom loaders that integrate with Malleable C2 transformations to remove string artifacts
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, Beacon, UDRL, reflective loader, position-independent code, in-memory evasion, memory scanner evasion, Moneta, PE-sieve, Hunt-Sleeping-Beacons, BeaconEye, BeaconHunter, heap encryption, FOLIAGE, page permissions, NX, return address spoofing, NtWaitForSingleObject, WaitForSingleObjectEx, RtlAllocateHeap, InternetConnectA, SystemFunction032, RC4, Malleable C2, shellcode loader

entry.title: ShellGhost
entry.summary:
Title: ShellGhost
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): ShellGhost is a Windows shellcode self-injection PoC demonstrating a memory-evasion technique intended to keep shellcode “invisible” to common memory scanners throughout execution. It uses Vectored Exception Handling (VEH) and software breakpoints to single-step execution: each breakpoint is replaced on-demand with a small RC4-decrypted instruction chunk, executed, then re-hidden by restoring breakpoints and toggling page protections. A companion “shellcode mapping” Python script disassembles shellcode into per-instruction blobs, encrypts them individually, and generates C arrays plus metadata (RVA + opcode quota) to support correct RC4 decryption lengths. The approach aims to defeat scanners like Moneta and PE-sieve by avoiding long-lived plaintext shellcode in RX private memory, though it acknowledges detectability via breakpoint-filled pages and the inherent need for executable private memory at times. It includes special handling for Metasploit x64 shellcodes where API parameter strings reside inside the shellcode region (e.g., RCX pointing into the allocation during `JMP RAX`). Useful primarily for red teamers and offensive security researchers studying in-memory evasion tradeoffs and detection opportunities.
Technical Focus: - Vectored Exception Handling (AddVectoredExceptionHandler) for execution control - Software breakpoints (INT3 / EXCEPTION_BREAKPOINT) as an execution “skeleton” - Per-instruction shellcode mapping (RIP→RVA resolution) and metadata (quota/RVA) - RC4 decryption via SystemFunction032 (advapi32) with variable-length buffers - Memory protection flipping (RW ↔ RX) and private executable memory artifacts - Metasploit x64 shellcode quirks (register-based API calls, embedded parameter pointers)
Use Cases: - Researching breakpoint/VEH-driven “just-in-time” shellcode decryption/execution - Building PoCs to evaluate memory-scanner heuristics (Moneta, PE-sieve) and YARA ideas - Developing/benchmarking defensive detections for breakpoint-heavy RX private pages - Adapting shellcode loaders to reduce plaintext dwell time in executable memory
Keywords: Windows, shellcode, in-memory evasion, Vectored Exception Handling, VEH, EXCEPTION_BREAKPOINT, INT3, software breakpoints, RIP, RVA, RC4, SystemFunction032, USTRING, VirtualProtect, RWX, RX private memory, Metasploit x64, JMP RAX, Moneta, PE-sieve, YARA detection

entry.title: SentinelBruh
entry.summary:
Title: SentinelBruh — abusing SentinelOne’s VEH for vectored syscalls and local execution (PoC)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): SentinelBruh is an archived, “dirty” proof-of-concept demonstrating how SentinelOne’s use of Vectored Exception Handling (VEH) can be abused to perform vectored syscalls and enable local code execution techniques. The core idea is to leverage exception-driven control flow (VEH handlers) to transition into syscall execution paths in a way that can evade or interfere with user-mode monitoring/hooking approaches. It references prior work on dumping VEH handler chains, implementing syscalls via VEH, and detecting anomalous VEH handlers. The repo is explicitly labeled outdated and not recommended for reuse, but it documents a technique class relevant to EDR evasion research. Useful primarily for red team tradecraft R&D, malware dev experimentation, and blue team detection engineering around VEH abuse.
Technical Focus: - Windows Vectored Exception Handling (VEH) internals and handler chaining - Syscalls via exception-driven control flow (“vectored syscalls”) - User-mode EDR/AV hooking and bypass surface - VEH handler discovery/dumping and anomaly detection concepts - Local execution primitives leveraging exception handlers
Use Cases: - Prototype research into EDR user-mode monitoring bypass via VEH-mediated syscalls - Build detections for suspicious VEH registration/handler patterns in endpoints - Study how security products’ exception handlers can be repurposed by attackers - Reproduce and validate “vectored syscalls” techniques in a lab environment
Keywords: SentinelOne, S1, VEH, Vectored Exception Handling, AddVectoredExceptionHandler, exception handler chain, vectored syscalls, Windows syscalls, Nt* APIs, user-mode hooking, EDR bypass, local execution, anti-analysis, handler dumping, anomaly detection, Windows internals, C, assembly

entry.title: ShellcodeFluctuation
entry.summary:
Title: Shellcode Fluctuation PoC (ShellcodeFluctuation)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository provides a proof-of-concept Windows shellcode loader implementing “shellcode fluctuation”: periodically encrypting shellcode in memory and toggling its page protections between RX and RW or PAGE_NOACCESS. - The goal is to reduce exposure to memory scanners (e.g., Moneta, pe-sieve) that look for abnormal private executable pages and can dump implants for analysis. - The PoC targets Cobalt Strike Beacon specifically by inline-hooking `kernel32!Sleep` to detect when the Beacon sleeps, then encrypting/decrypting the shellcode region and flipping protections around the sleep interval. - A PAGE_NOACCESS mode uses a Vectored Exception Handler (VEH) to catch access violations when execution resumes, restoring RX and decrypting on-demand. - The README also discusses residual IOCs (e.g., “modified code in kernel32.dll” due to private working-set pages) and tradeoffs of hooking/unhooking to minimize obvious trampolines. - Useful for red team tool developers and malware researchers building custom loaders and studying in-memory evasion vs. scanner heuristics.
Technical Focus: - Memory protection flipping (`VirtualProtect`, RX↔RW / RX↔PAGE_NOACCESS) - In-memory payload encryption/decryption (XOR32) - Inline hooking / trampoline hooking of `kernel32!Sleep` - Vectored Exception Handling (VEH) for execution resumption - Self-injection loader pattern (`VirtualAlloc` + `memcpy` + `CreateThread`) - Memory scanner IOC mechanics (MEM_PRIVATE within MEM_IMAGE, working set analysis)
Use Cases: - Implement sleep-time memory masking for Beacon-like implants - Reduce “private executable memory” findings in memory triage tools - Experiment with RW vs. NOACCESS fluctuation strategies and their artifacts - Study how Moneta/pe-sieve detect modified modules and injected regions - Build/validate custom shellcode loaders with periodic in-memory obfuscation
Keywords: Shellcode fluctuation, Cobalt Strike Beacon, sleep masking, VirtualProtect, VirtualAlloc, CreateThread, kernel32!Sleep hook, inline hook, trampoline, XOR encryption, PAGE_READWRITE, PAGE_NOACCESS, Vectored Exception Handler, access violation, Moneta, pe-sieve, MEM_PRIVATE, MEM_IMAGE, working set, in-memory evasion

entry.title: ThreadStackSpoofer
entry.summary:
Title: Thread Stack Spoofing / Call Stack Spoofing PoC (ThreadStackSpoofer)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): ThreadStackSpoofer is a C++ proof-of-concept demonstrating “thread/call stack spoofing” as an in-memory evasion technique for injected shellcode. It targets scanners/analysts that walk thread stacks and flag return addresses that point into non-image, privately allocated memory (e.g., VirtualAlloc’d shellcode regions). The PoC hooks `kernel32!Sleep` so that when a Beacon sleeps, a callback (`MySleep`) temporarily overwrites a controlled return address on the stack with `0`, effectively truncating/terminating stack unwinding and hiding shellcode-related frames. The repo documents both a newer, simpler approach (return-address overwrite) and an older approach using `StackWalk64` (via dbghelp). It’s primarily useful for red team tool authors and malware researchers building custom loaders, and it highlights limitations: the resulting stack can be “unwindable”/anomalous and is not a full fake-frame stack spoofer. The README also outlines what a “true” spoofer would require on x64: reverse-emulating Windows unwind metadata (`.pdata`, `RtlVirtualUnwind`) to craft believable frames.
Technical Focus: - Thread call stack inspection evasion (stack/call stack spoofing) - API hooking of `kernel32!Sleep` / `SleepEx` for implant “sleep” interception - Return address manipulation via `_AddressOfReturnAddress()` and frame pointers (RBP/EBP) - Windows x64 stack unwinding internals (`RtlLookupFunctionEntry`, `RtlVirtualUnwind`, UNWIND_INFO/UWOP) - Shellcode loading/execution (`VirtualAlloc`, `memcpy`, `CreateThread`) - dbghelp-based stack walking (`SymInitialize`, `StackWalk64`) in older implementation
Use Cases: - Integrate call-stack hiding into custom shellcode loaders (especially Cobalt Strike Beacon-style sleep loops) - Evaluate/replicate commercial C2 in-memory evasion behaviors in lab environments - Research and develop detections for anomalous/unwind-broken thread stacks and Sleep-hooking patterns - Prototype advanced x64 fake-frame construction using `.pdata` unwind metadata
Keywords: ThreadStackSpoofer, call stack spoofing, thread stack spoofing, in-memory evasion, shellcode loader, Cobalt Strike Beacon, kernel32 Sleep hook, SleepEx, return address overwrite, _AddressOfReturnAddress, RBP, EBP, VirtualAlloc, CreateThread, dbghelp.dll, StackWalk64, SymInitialize, x64 unwinding, RtlVirtualUnwind, RtlLookupFunctionEntry, UNWIND_INFO, UWOP, .pdata, IMAGE_DIRECTORY_ENTRY_EXCEPTION, SEC_PRIVATE

entry.title: CTFPacker
entry.summary:
Title: CTFPacker
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): CTFPacker is a cross-platform (Linux/Windows) shellcode packer/loader generator intended for CTFs and pentest/red team exams where basic AV evasion is needed. It produces Windows EXE or DLL loaders that either embed shellcode (stageless) or download it over HTTP (staged). The generated loader combines multiple common evasion primitives such as indirect syscalls (SysWhispers-style), API hashing, custom module/API resolution, and NTDLL unhooking via the KnownDlls technique. Payloads can be AES-128-CBC encrypted and the loader can be “scrambled” to introduce polymorphic-like variation. Execution uses an injection technique (EarlyBird APC injection) and supports optional Authenticode signing via a provided PFX. It’s primarily useful for red teamers/pentesters and CTF players who want a repeatable way to wrap shellcode into a loader without hand-assembling these components.
Technical Focus: - Indirect syscalls (SysWhispers-derived stubs, NASM-compatible) - API hashing + custom GetProcAddress/GetModuleHandle implementations - NTDLL unhooking via KnownDlls mapping - AES-128-CBC payload encryption/decryption - EarlyBird APC injection - Staged (HTTP download via WinHTTP) vs stageless payload delivery; EXE/DLL output and signing
Use Cases: - Generate a basic AV-evasive Windows shellcode loader for CTFs/exams - Produce staged loaders that fetch shellcode from an operator-controlled HTTP server - Produce stageless loaders with encrypted embedded payloads to reduce static signatures - Create DLL loaders callable via `rundll32` (exported `ctf` function) - Rapidly iterate loader variants using scrambling/polymorphism and optional signing
Keywords: shellcode, packer, loader generator, AV evasion, EDR evasion, Windows internals, SysWhispers, indirect syscalls, NTDLL unhooking, KnownDlls, API hashing, GetProcAddress, GetModuleHandle, AES-128-CBC, EarlyBird APC injection, WinHTTP, staged payload, stageless payload, Authenticode signing, MinGW-w64, clang, NASM

entry.title: MochiLdr
entry.summary:
Title: MochiLdr (Reflective x64 Loader written in C/ASM)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - MochiLdr is a Windows x64 reflective loader that maps and executes a DLL from memory without relying on the normal OS loader path. - It targets stealthier in-memory execution by being CRT-free, position-independent, and by erasing the DOS header after allocating new memory. - The project includes custom implementations of GetProcAddress and GetModuleHandle, plus API hashing (Jenkins one-at-a-time) to reduce obvious imports/strings. - It supports TLS callbacks and registers exception handlers via RtlAddFunctionTable, improving compatibility with modern PE features and x64 unwind metadata. - The repo intentionally does not include an injector; users are expected to pair it with an external injector (e.g., KaynLdr’s) or write their own. - Useful primarily for red team operators, pentesters, and malware developers researching reflective loading tradecraft and PE manual mapping.
Technical Focus: - Reflective DLL loading / manual PE mapping (x64) - Position-independent code (PIC) and CRT-free payloads - API hashing (Jenkins OAAT) and custom export resolution - TLS callback handling during manual load - x64 exception handling / unwind registration (RtlAddFunctionTable) - Direct/low-level memory management via NTDLL syscalls
Use Cases: - Building custom in-memory loaders for red team payload execution - Researching/benchmarking reflective loading implementations (vs. KaynLdr/BokuLoader) - Developing stealthier loaders by minimizing imports and removing PE artifacts (e.g., DOS header) - Testing EDR detection logic around manual mapping, TLS execution, and unwind registration
Keywords: reflective loader, reflective DLL, manual mapping, PE parsing, x64, Windows internals, PIC, CRT-free, API hashing, Jenkins OAAT, GetProcAddress, GetModuleHandle, TLS callbacks, RtlAddFunctionTable, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, NtProtectVirtualMemory, NtFlushInstructionCache, LdrLoadDll, ntdll.dll, kernel32.dll

entry.title: MochiMapper
entry.summary:
Title: MochiMapper — Minimal in-memory manual PE loader (resource-embedded)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): MochiMapper is a minimal Windows manual PE loader that maps a payload PE from the host binary’s `.rsrc` section into memory and emulates key parts of the Windows loader. It solves the problem of executing an EXE/DLL without using the normal on-disk load path by performing relocation processing, import resolution (reading INT/ILT and writing the IAT), TLS callback execution, and x64 unwind/exception support via `.pdata` registration. Payloads can be optionally AES-128-CBC encrypted, with a companion Python utility (ObfusX) to encrypt PEs/shellcode for embedding. The loader also includes optional IAT-level hooks to spoof/hide command-line and related CRT APIs without modifying the PEB, and can target exported DLL functions instead of `DllMain`. It’s primarily useful for red team operators, pentesters, and malware researchers studying or prototyping in-memory execution and loader behavior. The project explicitly notes limited OPSEC and suggests enhancements like API hashing, indirect syscalls, improved key/IV handling, CRT-free builds, or converting to a reflective DLL loader.
Technical Focus: - Manual PE mapping from memory (resource section payload) - Base relocations and bounds-checked relocation walking - Import resolution (INT/ILT parsing, IAT patching) - TLS callbacks and loader emulation - x64 SEH/unwind support via `.pdata` registration - IAT hooking for command-line/CRT/exit API interception
Use Cases: - Execute embedded EXE/DLL payloads from memory without dropping to disk - Prototype/customize a manual mapper for red team tradecraft experiments - Study Windows loader mechanics (relocations, imports, TLS, unwind metadata) - Implement command-line spoofing via IAT patching for specific payloads - Encrypt and embed payloads (AES-CBC) to reduce static visibility of raw PE bytes
Keywords: manual mapping, PE loader, in-memory execution, .rsrc, AES-128-CBC, relocation table, base relocations, INT, ILT, IAT, import address table, TLS callbacks, .pdata, x64 unwind, SEH, export resolver, forwarder exports, IAT hooking, GetCommandLineW, __getmainargs, ExitProcess, mimikatz demo

entry.title: TrampoLatte
entry.summary:
Title: TrampoLatté (TrampoLatte) — AMSI & ETW bypass PoC via trampoline hooking
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - TrampoLatté is a proof-of-concept that bypasses AMSI scanning and suppresses ETW telemetry by trampoline-hooking key Windows functions and altering control flow. - It targets `AmsiScanBuffer` to force a clean result (e.g., returning `AMSI_RESULT_CLEAN`) and targets ETW by hooking `EtwpEventWriteFull` to short-circuit event emission (a simple `ret` stub). - The core technique is inline patching with a small jump stub (documented as a 13-byte `jmp r10`) that redirects execution into proxy/stub code, then returns to the caller with modified behavior. - The repo includes C and a small amount of assembly, and demonstrates the effect using a custom ETW consumer monitoring .NET runtime events. - It’s primarily useful for red teamers, offensive tool developers, and defenders studying common user-mode telemetry bypass patterns; the author notes it is not OPSEC-safe.
Technical Focus: - Trampoline/inline hooking and control-flow redirection - AMSI bypass by manipulating `AmsiScanBuffer` return semantics - ETW suppression by hooking `EtwpEventWriteFull` - Context saving/restoring in hook proxies - User-mode telemetry evasion against .NET runtime ETW consumers
Use Cases: - Building or studying AMSI bypass primitives for in-process execution - Disabling/suppressing ETW-based detections during .NET assembly execution - Researching detection opportunities for inline patching/trampoline hooks - Creating lab PoCs to validate EDR/ETW/AMSI monitoring coverage
Keywords: AMSI, AmsiScanBuffer, AMSI_RESULT_CLEAN, ETW, EtwpEventWriteFull, trampoline hooking, inline hook, function patching, jmp stub, ret stub, user-mode evasion, .NET runtime ETW, ETW consumer, Windows internals, C, x64 assembly, control-flow hijack, Havoc C2

entry.title: Koppeling
entry.summary:
Title: Koppeling — Adaptive DLL Hijacking / Dynamic Export Forwarding (Demo + Export Cloning)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Koppeling is a demonstration project for advanced Windows DLL hijacking where a malicious DLL gains execution while still proxying all expected exports to the legitimate library. - It addresses the common problem of “entry point not found” crashes when a hijacked DLL does not correctly implement/forward the victim’s required exports, especially in dynamic import scenarios. - The repo includes a vulnerable harness app, a legitimate DLL, an “evil” DLL, and tooling to clone or rebuild export tables so the malicious DLL can seamlessly forward calls. - It showcases multiple proxying approaches: static forwarding via linker directives, post-build export cloning (C# and Python), and a more dynamic technique that rebuilds exports and patches import tables at runtime. - Useful for red teamers, pentesters, and malware developers studying reliable DLL proxying; also valuable for blue teams to understand realistic hijack implementations beyond trivial PoCs. - Interesting because it operationalizes “adaptive” hijacking—handling both static and dynamic sinks—rather than only demonstrating basic DLL search order abuse.
Technical Focus: - DLL hijacking and proxy DLL design - Export table cloning and export forwarding - PE (Portable Executable) parsing/modification - Import Address Table (IAT) patching / runtime import repair - Static vs dynamic linking “sink” behaviors - Tooling in C++, C#, and Python for PE manipulation
Use Cases: - Build crash-free proxy DLLs for hijackable applications/services - Test and validate DLL search order hijacking scenarios in lab environments - Generate forwarder/proxy exports from a reference DLL automatically - Research detection opportunities around export-forwarding and PE tampering - Create realistic training examples for defenders (proxy DLL tradecraft)
Keywords: DLL hijacking, proxy DLL, export forwarding, export table cloning, PE file format, IAT patching, dynamic imports, static imports, linker directives, LoadLibrary, GetProcAddress, Windows internals, C++, C#, Python, NetClone, PyClone, adaptive DLL hijacking, import table, whoami.exe example

entry.title: sRDI
entry.summary:
Title: sRDI – Shellcode Reflective DLL Injection
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - sRDI is a toolchain for converting Windows DLLs into position-independent shellcode that performs Reflective DLL Injection (a self-contained in-memory PE loader + embedded DLL). - It solves the problem of executing a DLL purely from memory without using the normal Windows loader, enabling “fileless” style execution and custom injection workflows. - The loader implements key PE-loading behaviors such as section mapping with correct permissions, import resolution, TLS callback handling, and basic sanity checks. - The repo includes multiple front-ends (native EXE, C#/.NET, Python, PowerShell) to generate and/or run the produced shellcode, plus utilities to statically embed precompiled loader blobs. - It exposes flags for operational tradeoffs like wiping PE headers, clearing DLL memory post-execution, import obfuscation (randomization + optional delays), and passing the shellcode base to exports. - Useful primarily for red teams, pentesters, and malware developers building custom in-memory execution/injection chains; also valuable for defenders studying reflective loading tradecraft.
Technical Focus: - Reflective DLL Injection / in-memory PE loading - Position-independent code (PIC) shellcode generation - PE parsing: sections, relocations, IAT/import resolution - TLS callbacks and DllMain/export invocation - Anti-analysis/OPSEC options (header wiping, import obfuscation, memory clearing)
Use Cases: - Convert an existing DLL payload into shellcode for in-memory execution - Build custom loaders/injectors in C/C#/PowerShell/Python for post-exploitation - Embed a reflective loader blob into other tooling for staged execution - Experiment with PE loader behaviors (TLS, permissions, import handling) for research - Apply basic evasion tradeoffs (clear headers, obfuscate imports, self-clean patterns)
Keywords: Reflective DLL Injection, sRDI, ShellcodeRDI, PE loader, position-independent shellcode, PIC, in-memory execution, DLL to shellcode, IAT patching, import resolution, relocations, section permissions, TLS callbacks, DllMain, GetProcAddress, VirtualAlloc, Windows internals, PowerShell loader, C# loader, NativeLoader

entry.title: KhimairaLdr
entry.summary:
Title: KhimairaLdr
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): KhimairaLdr is a Windows evasive shellcode loader implemented as a DLL intended to be sideloaded by a dropped executable (example flow uses `java-rmi.exe` to load a renamed `jli.dll`). The DLL stages RC4-encrypted shellcode from a web server, decrypts it using `SystemFunction032`, and executes it via local injection using `Nt*` APIs with “threadless” execution (`jmp rcx`). It emphasizes stealth by avoiding static imports through CRC32 API hashing, using stacked strings, removing CRT, and stripping common loader APIs like `LoadLibrary/GetProcAddress`. It also includes anti-EDR measures such as `ntdll.dll` unhooking via indirect syscalls and WinINet cache cleaning to reduce disk artifacts. Persistence options include HKCU Run key and Startup folder LNK-based persistence. It’s primarily useful for red teams/pentesters building staged payload delivery with common evasion patterns and basic persistence.
Technical Focus: - DLL sideloading / proxy DLL-style loader (`jli.dll` via `java-rmi.exe`) - API hashing (CRC32) and import table minimization - Indirect syscalls and `ntdll` unhooking - WinINet staging + cache cleaning (artifact reduction) - RC4 decryption via `SystemFunction032` (advapi32/crypt routine) - Local shellcode injection and threadless execution
Use Cases: - Build a staged shellcode loader that retrieves payloads over HTTP(S) and executes in-memory - Test EDR detections around indirect syscalls, unhooking, and import-less loaders - Simulate DLL sideloading tradecraft using a benign-looking host executable - Establish simple user-level persistence via Run keys or Startup LNKs - Evaluate forensic artifacts from WinINet usage and cache-cleaning behavior
Keywords: KhimairaLdr, shellcode loader, DLL sideloading, jli.dll, java-rmi.exe, WinINet, cache cleaning, API hashing, CRC32, indirect syscalls, ntdll unhooking, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, NtProtectVirtualMemory, NtCreateThreadEx, in-memory execution, threadless execution, jmp rcx, SystemFunction032, RC4, persistence, HKCU Run, Startup folder, LNK persistence, CRT removal, stacked strings

entry.title: DojoLoader
entry.summary:
Title: DojoLoader — Generic PE loader for fast prototyping evasion techniques
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - DojoLoader is a modular in-memory PE loader designed to quickly prototype and test evasion techniques, originally built around running Cobalt Strike “UDRL-less” raw Beacon payloads. - It solves the problem of iterating on loader/sleep-obfuscation ideas without relying on UDRLs, reducing debugging and integration overhead. - The project extends a MemoryModule-style loader (inspired by ORCA000’s DynamicDllLoader) and adds payload retrieval (HTTP/file) with optional XOR encoding plus dynamic IAT hooking of `Sleep`. - It implements three sleep obfuscation approaches: classic RW→RX encryption/decryption, “MemoryBouncing” (freeing the mapped image during sleep and restoring it), and “MemoryHopping” (remapping at a new address and fixing execution via relocations/return-address adjustment). - It’s primarily useful for red teamers, pentesters, and malware researchers evaluating EDR detection gaps around RX→RW and sleep-based heuristics. - The interesting aspect is the focus on avoiding common telemetry (e.g., `VirtualProtect` RX↔RW transitions) by removing or moving the payload during sleep and restoring execution safely.
Technical Focus: - In-memory PE loading (MemoryModule-style mapping) - Sleep obfuscation via IAT hooking (`Sleep`/`SleepEx`) - RX→RW / RW→RX transition evasion strategies - Payload staging over HTTP and local file with XOR encoding - Memory remapping, relocations, and return-address adjustment
Use Cases: - Rapid prototyping of loader-based EDR evasion concepts - Testing sleep obfuscation variants against endpoint detections - Executing UDRL-less Cobalt Strike Beacon payloads in-memory - Researching detection opportunities around memory free/remap patterns - Building custom loaders for controlled red team tradecraft experiments
Keywords: PE loader, in-memory execution, MemoryModule, DynamicDllLoader, Cobalt Strike, Beacon, UDRL-less, sleep obfuscation, IAT hooking, Sleep, SleepEx, VirtualProtect evasion, RWX, RW->RX, RX->RW detection, MemoryBouncing, MemoryHopping, relocations, HTTP staging, XOR encoding

entry.title: GregsBestFriend
entry.summary:
Title: GregsBestFriend – Bypassing AV/EDR and Building with Different Compilers
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - GregsBestFriend is a C++ offensive development sample/tool from the White Knight Labs course that demonstrates AV/EDR evasion concepts alongside process injection-oriented code. - The main idea is that compiling the same source with different toolchains (MSVC/cl.exe, GCC/g++, Clang variants) produces materially different binaries, which can change static signatures and detection outcomes. - The repository includes build setups for multiple compilers and highlights how compiler choice/flags can influence executable uniqueness and optimization. - It also implements basic sandbox-evasion heuristics such as wasting CPU cycles via a large loop and checking the executable name for an expected string (to detect sandbox renaming). - It’s primarily useful for red teamers, pentesters, and offensive tool developers experimenting with build pipelines and basic evasion tradecraft; it’s also relevant to blue teams for understanding how trivial build changes can affect detections. - The project emphasizes operational considerations like avoiding VirusTotal submissions to reduce signature burn.
Technical Focus: - Windows offensive C++ development and process injection scaffolding - AV/EDR evasion via compiler/toolchain diversity (MSVC vs GCC vs Clang) - Binary variance through compiler optimizations and flags - Basic sandbox/analysis evasion (sleep/CPU burn, filename checks) - Build engineering for multiple Windows compiler environments
Use Cases: - Generate multiple functionally equivalent payload binaries with different compiler fingerprints - Test how EDR detections vary across MSVC/Clang/GCC builds and optimization settings - Use as a starter template for custom injection loaders in C++ - Evaluate sandbox behavior against simple anti-analysis checks (renaming, time/CPU constraints) - Blue-team validation: build-based evasion awareness and detection engineering exercises
Keywords: C++, Windows, process injection, AV evasion, EDR evasion, MSVC, cl.exe, GCC, g++, Clang, LLVM, compiler flags, optimization, binary diversification, sandbox evasion, anti-analysis, CPU cycle wasting, executable name check, offensive development, White Knight Labs

entry.title: ModuleShifting
entry.summary:
Title: ModuleShifting
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - ModuleShifting is a Windows in-process injection technique/tool that modifies a loaded legitimate DLL’s section to host shellcode or a PE payload, aiming to reduce common memory forensics indicators compared to Module Stomping/Overloading. - It avoids allocating new memory (e.g., VirtualAlloc) by reusing an existing module’s mapped image sections, changing protections, writing the payload, executing it, and (for shellcode) restoring the original DLL bytes afterward to erase the modified artifact. - The implementation is in Python using ctypes, designed to run fully in-memory via a Python interpreter and the author’s Pyramid framework (reducing reliance on compiled loaders). - For PE payloads it includes manual mapping steps such as section copying, relocations, import resolution, TLS callback execution, and restoring native section protections to avoid RWX regions. - It’s primarily useful for red teams/pentesters and EDR evasion testing, and is interesting because the “restore original content” cleanup step can significantly reduce post-execution memory IoCs in offline scanners (with the caveat that real-time inspection may still catch the transient modification).
Technical Focus: - Module stomping / module overloading variants (image section overwrite) - Memory protection changes (RW → RX) and avoiding RWX - Shellcode execution via function pointer / callbacks / CreateThread - PE manual mapping (relocations, import resolution, TLS callbacks) - In-memory execution via Python ctypes (no compiled loader) - Memory forensics/IoC reduction vs tools like Moneta and PE-sieve
Use Cases: - Local process injection for red team tradecraft without VirtualAlloc-based artifacts - EDR/AV evaluation of transient module-section overwrites and cleanup behavior - Running staged loaders where initial shellcode is disposable after spawning a self-sustaining payload - Researching detection strategies for module-section tampering and short-lived memory modifications
Keywords: ModuleShifting, module stomping, module overloading, DLL section overwrite, LoadLibrary, VirtualProtect, shellcode, manual mapping, PE relocation, import resolution, TLS callbacks, function pointer execution, CreateThread, ctypes, Python in-memory execution, Pyramid, Moneta, PE-sieve, memory IoCs, EDR evasion

entry.title: ProcessStomping
entry.summary:
Title: ProcessStomping
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - ProcessStomping is a proof-of-concept Windows process injection technique described as a variation of hasherezade’s Process Overwriting. - Instead of overwriting an entire PE image in the target process, it writes shellcode into a chosen executable section and redirects execution to it. - The core flow is: create a target process suspended (CreateProcess + CREATE_SUSPENDED), write the payload into the target (WriteProcessMemory), change the primary thread’s start context to the new code (SetThreadContext), then resume execution (ResumeThread). - The repo demonstrates pairing the technique with sRDI to execute a reflective-loaderless DLL (e.g., a beacon) as shellcode, including optional XOR encoding and network delivery of the blob. - It’s primarily useful for red teamers, pentesters, and malware developers exploring user-mode injection tradecraft and section-based payload placement. - It’s interesting because it highlights a practical alternative to full image replacement and calls out detection opportunities around suspended process creation + thread context manipulation.
Technical Focus: - Process Overwriting variant (“Process Stomping”) and section-based shellcode placement - Suspended process creation and primary thread hijacking - Remote memory writing (WriteProcessMemory) and entrypoint redirection (SetThreadContext) - sRDI (Shellcode Reflective DLL Injection) / reflective-loaderless DLL execution - Payload encoding/obfuscation (XOR) and simple network staging - Detection surface: CREATE_SUSPENDED + context change + rapid ResumeThread sequence
Use Cases: - Execute shellcode from a specific mapped section of a legitimate process - Stage and run sRDI-converted DLL payloads (e.g., C2 beacons) without a custom reflective loader - Experiment with injection tradeoffs vs. classic process hollowing/overwriting - Build detections for suspicious suspended-process + thread-context modification patterns
Keywords: Process Stomping, Process Overwriting, CreateProcess, CREATE_SUSPENDED, WriteProcessMemory, SetThreadContext, ResumeThread, thread hijacking, entrypoint redirection, shellcode injection, PE sections, RWX section, sRDI, reflective DLL injection, loaderless, beacon, XOR encoding, netcat staging, Windows API, EDR evasion

entry.title: PythonMemoryModule
entry.summary:
Title: PythonMemoryModule
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - PythonMemoryModule is a pure-Python (ctypes-based) implementation of the MemoryModule technique for loading PE payloads (DLLs and unmanaged EXEs) directly from a memory buffer without writing them to disk. - It ports Joachim Bauch’s MemoryModule approach and uses `pefile` for PE parsing plus PythonForWindows for interacting with process parameters. - The project targets “living-off-the-land” style execution by running payloads inside the signed `python.exe` process, avoiding `LoadLibrary` on a file path and avoiding dropping custom loader binaries or `.pyd` extensions. - It includes partial command-line argument support for in-memory EXE execution by stomping the process command line in the PEB, with notes on limitations across compiler/runtime implementations. - It’s primarily useful for red teams/pentesters and malware developers experimenting with in-memory PE loading and tradecraft; defenders can use it to understand MemoryModule artifacts. - Detection notes highlight a common tell: private committed memory regions containing mapped PE sections not backed by an on-disk module.
Technical Focus: - In-memory PE loading (MemoryModule / reflective-style loading) - PE header parsing and manual mapping (sections, imports, relocations) - Python `ctypes` Windows API interop - PEB manipulation (command line stomping) - AV/EDR evasion tradeoffs and artifacts (non-file-backed mappings)
Use Cases: - Load and execute a DLL payload (e.g., stageless beacon) from an in-memory download buffer - Run certain unmanaged EXEs in-memory with best-effort argument passing - Prototype fileless loaders in Python without compiling custom binaries or shipping `.pyd` modules - Research/detection engineering for MemoryModule-style manual mapping indicators
Keywords: MemoryModule, manual mapping, in-memory DLL loading, in-memory EXE execution, PE parsing, pefile, ctypes, python.exe, Windows API, PEB, Process Environment Block, command line stomping, GetCommandLineA, GetCommandLineW, __getmainargs, relocations, import resolution, private committed memory, fileless execution, EDR evasion

entry.title: dll proxy generator
entry.summary:
Title: DLL Proxy Generator (namazso/dll-proxy-generator)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - A Rust CLI tool that generates a Windows “proxy DLL” for an arbitrary target DLL, forwarding exports to the real library while also loading a user-specified secondary DLL. - It solves the tedious/fragile work of manually creating export-forwarding stubs (including ordinal-based exports) needed for DLL proxying and side-loading scenarios. - The tool supports specifying extra imports (by name or ordinal), choosing the proxy target path (defaulting to the same DLL name in System32), and selecting the COFF machine type (e.g., x64). - This is useful for red teams/pentesters and malware developers for DLL search order hijacking / DLL sideloading tradecraft, and for defenders to reproduce and test these behaviors in a controlled way. - It’s notable because it automates correct export forwarding (including ordinals) and incorporates a known “target DLL trick” referenced from perfect-dll-proxy.
Technical Focus: - Windows DLL proxying / export forwarding - PE/COFF export tables and ordinal handling - DLL search order hijacking / sideloading mechanics - Import table manipulation (name/ordinal imports) - Architecture targeting via COFF machine values (x86/x64)
Use Cases: - Generate a proxy DLL to intercept/forward calls while executing custom code via a secondary DLL - Build reproducible DLL sideloading test cases for application/EDR validation - Rapidly create “drop-in” proxy replacements for System32 DLLs (lab environments) - Validate handling of ordinal-only exports when crafting proxy libraries
Keywords: DLL proxy, export forwarding, ordinal exports, PE format, COFF machine, Windows loader, DLL sideloading, DLL search order hijacking, import table, System32, Rust, proxy-target, perfect-dll-proxy, LoadLibrary, Windows internals, red team tooling, defensive testing, CLI generator

entry.title: nimvoke
entry.summary:
Title: nimvoke — Indirect syscalls + DInvoke made simple (Nim)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - nimvoke is a Nim library intended to be imported into offensive tooling to simplify DInvoke-style dynamic API resolution and indirect system calls on Windows. - It abstracts delegate declarations and syscall invocation via Nim macros, aiming to reduce static imports and improve operational security. - The project hashes function/library names at compile time, parses ntdll’s Export Address Table (EAT) at runtime to enumerate syscalls, and derives System Service Numbers (SSNs) by sorting syscall stub addresses to remain resilient to userland hooks. - Syscalls are executed through a single “arg-shifting” trampoline (inspired by FreshyCalls) that jumps to the correct `syscall` instruction inside `ntdll.dll`, with the trampoline allocated on a private heap. - It’s primarily useful for red teamers, pentesters, and malware developers building Nim-based loaders/injectors who want cleaner syscall/DInvoke plumbing without re-implementing internals. - The repo also documents important limitations (e.g., Nim runtime core imports remain) and notes future work like x86 support and stack-frame spoofing.
Technical Focus: - DInvoke-style dynamic API resolution (LoadLibraryA/GetProcAddress via Nim `dynlib`) - Indirect syscalls via ntdll EAT parsing and syscall stub discovery - SSN derivation by sorting syscall addresses (hook-resilient enumeration) - Compile-time hashing of API/module names - Syscall trampoline (“arg shifting”) and private heap allocation - Nim macros for generating delegates and syscall wrappers
Use Cases: - Building Nim-based shellcode loaders/injectors using `NtAllocateVirtualMemory`/related syscalls - Reducing static Win32 imports in custom tooling via runtime API resolution - Bypassing/mitigating userland API hooks by jumping to clean `syscall` instructions in ntdll - Rapid prototyping of syscall-based tradecraft in Nim without hand-rolling stubs - Embedding op-sec friendly syscall initialization into larger implants
Keywords: Nim, Windows, DInvoke, indirect syscalls, ntdll.dll, EAT parsing, Export Address Table, SSN, System Service Number, syscall trampoline, FreshyCalls, GetProcAddress, LoadLibraryA, dynlib, ZwAllocateVirtualMemory, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, API hashing, userland hooks, EDR evasion, winim

entry.title: shellsilo
entry.summary:
Title: SHELLSILO — C-to-syscall assembly and shellcode translator
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - SHELLSILO is a Python tool that parses a constrained C-like syntax and translates it into Windows syscall-focused assembly (NASM or MASM) and corresponding shellcode/binaries. - It aims to reduce the manual effort of writing low-level shellcode by handling structure definitions/usage, variable assignments, control flow (if/while), and embedding strings/UNICODE_STRING initialization. - The tool resolves syscall numbers by mapping the host Windows build/model (queried via PEB inspection) to a bundled syscall table derived from j00ru’s syscall listings, and only looks up syscalls actually used in the input code. - The repo includes practical examples (e.g., `ntwrt.c`) demonstrating direct-syscall process injection flow: enumerate/find a target process, open it, allocate memory, write a payload buffer, and spawn a remote thread via `NtCreateThreadEx`. - Useful for red teamers, exploit devs, and malware researchers building direct-syscall payloads or prototyping shellcode without writing all assembly by hand. - Interesting because it combines a higher-level authoring experience with build-aware syscall resolution and can generate binaries directly, supporting x64 and multiple assembler syntaxes.
Technical Focus: - Windows direct syscalls (NTAPI) and syscall number resolution by OS build - Parsing/compiling C-like syntax into assembly and shellcode - PEB-based environment/build identification - UNICODE_STRING and string embedding for shellcode - NASM/MASM x64 assembly generation - Process injection primitives (alloc/write/remote thread)
Use Cases: - Rapid prototyping of Windows x64 direct-syscall shellcode - Generating syscall-based process injection stubs (NtOpenProcess/NtAllocateVirtualMemory/NtWriteVirtualMemory/NtCreateThreadEx) - Building payloads that avoid Win32 API imports by using NT syscalls - Creating reusable shellcode templates with structured data and Unicode handling - Producing NASM/MASM-compatible assembly for further manual tuning
Keywords: Windows x64, direct syscalls, NTAPI, syscall numbers, PEB, Process Environment Block, NASM, MASM, keystone-engine, shellcode generation, UNICODE_STRING, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, NtQuerySystemInformation, NtOpenProcess, NtWriteVirtualMemory, NtCreateThreadEx, process injection, syscallslist.csv, j00ru syscalls table, assembly codegen

entry.title: Bytecode Jiu Jitsu
entry.summary:
Title: Bytecode Jiu-Jitsu (Choking Interpreters to Force Execution of Malicious Bytecode) – PoC Tools
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository contains proof-of-concept tooling from NTT ZeroLab’s Black Hat USA 2024 briefing on “Bytecode Jiu-Jitsu,” a technique for coercing language interpreters into executing attacker-controlled bytecode. - It addresses the problem of reliably crafting and injecting malicious bytecode by reverse-engineering interpreter internals and extracting the necessary metadata (e.g., symbol tables) from real targets. - The toolchain is organized as a workflow: Tracer-BJJ collects runtime traces, STAGER-BJJ derives interpreter internal structures, Extractor-BJJ pulls bytecode and symbol tables, and Injector-BJJ performs the final injection. - The focus is on interpreter-aware manipulation rather than generic shellcode injection, emphasizing how bytecode formats and VM/runtime structures can be abused. - It is primarily useful for red teams, exploit developers, and security researchers studying interpreter internals and bytecode-level tradecraft; it also provides defenders insight into a less-monitored execution layer. - It’s interesting because it operationalizes a repeatable pipeline for bytecode injection that depends on understanding and leveraging interpreter implementation details.
Technical Focus: - Interpreter/VM internal structure discovery via tracing - Bytecode extraction and reconstruction (including symbol tables/metadata) - Bytecode injection pipeline (trace → structure → extract → inject) - Reverse engineering of language runtimes/interpreters - PoC tooling for Black Hat research reproduction
Use Cases: - Build a target-specific bytecode injection workflow against a chosen interpreter - Extract and analyze bytecode/symbol metadata from a running interpreter instance - Reproduce and validate “Bytecode Jiu-Jitsu” research claims in a lab - Develop detections by understanding required artifacts/steps (tracing, metadata access, injection behavior) - Train red/blue teams on interpreter-layer attack surfaces
Keywords: bytecode, interpreter, virtual machine, runtime internals, tracing, reverse engineering, bytecode injection, symbol table, metadata extraction, Black Hat USA 2024, NTT ZeroLab, Tracer-BJJ, STAGER-BJJ, Extractor-BJJ, Injector-BJJ, PoC, malicious bytecode, dynamic analysis, C++, Python

entry.title: RflDllOb
entry.summary:
Title: RflDllOb (Bambini / Next Gen) — Reflective DLL Injection Made Bella
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository contains an R&D implementation of reflective DLL loading plus a custom injector, with two variants: “Bambini” (baseline) and “Next Gen” (evasion-focused). - It targets common detection points in reflective loaders (static signatures, userland hooks, and in-memory scanners) by modifying how the loader code executes and how the mapped image appears in memory. - Next Gen introduces “YOLO” (encrypt/decrypt and discard the ReflectiveLoader after first use), indirect syscalls to reduce exposure to user-mode API hooks, and “SWAPPALA” to swap a private malicious mapping with a legitimate on-disk DLL mapping at the same address. - It also implements a custom sleep/masking approach (“SLE(A)PING”) using timer worker threads and suspended threads with crafted contexts, plus timer callback spoofing to make the sleep orchestration less suspicious. - Useful primarily for red team operators, pentesters, and malware researchers studying reflective loading tradecraft and memory-evasion techniques; also valuable for blue teams to understand what to hunt for in memory and timer/thread artifacts.
Technical Focus: - Reflective DLL loading (PIC loader, PE parsing, relocation/import resolution) - Indirect syscalls / SSN enumeration (FreshyCalls, SysWhispers-style approaches) - Loader self-protection (encrypt/decrypt loader, discard after use — “YOLO”) - Memory evasion against in-memory scanners (private vs image-backed mappings, swapping) - Sleep masking via timers/worker threads (SLE(A)PING) and timer callback spoofing - Handle/section manipulation (ZwClose interference, section/view mapping mechanics)
