Use Cases: - Build and study a reflective DLL + dedicated injector workflow - Prototype userland-hook bypass via indirect syscalls during loading - Experiment with in-memory scanner evasion by mapping/swap techniques (SWAPPALA) - Implement sleep masking that avoids common Ekko limitations (argument count/stack sharing) - Develop detections around suspicious timer usage, thread context crafting, and mapping swaps
Keywords: reflective DLL injection, reflective loader, PIC, PE parsing, relocations, IAT resolution, indirect syscalls, SSN enumeration, FreshyCalls, SysWhispers, ZwClose, NtContinue, ResumeThread, Control Flow Guard, stack cookie, MapViewOfFileEx, section mapping, private mapping, image-backed mapping, sleep mask, Windows timers, timer callback spoofing, in-memory scanners, Moneta, pe-sieve, Hunt-Sleeping-Beacons, SWAPPALA, SLE(A)PING, YOLO loader encryption

entry.title: BYOSI
entry.summary:
Title: BYOSI (Bring Your Own Scripting Interpreter)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - BYOSI is a PowerShell-based technique/toolchain to evade EDR by avoiding commonly hooked Windows APIs and instead executing payload logic through a trusted, code-signed scripting interpreter (PHP for Windows). - It targets an EDR blind spot where some products treat script files as inert text and focus detection on PE binaries, memory injection, and high-entropy sections rather than interpreter-driven script execution. - The workflow downloads and extracts a PHP Windows archive to `C:\Temp\php`, fetches a PHP “implant”/shell script, and runs it using the downloaded `php.exe` (signed by the vendor), leveraging trust/allowlisting that often applies to signed binaries. - The repo includes a PowerShell loader (`byosi.ps1`) and a PHP PoC (`poc.php`) demonstrating outbound connectivity/shell behavior under EDR monitoring. - It’s primarily useful for red teams and pentesters testing EDR coverage gaps around script scanning, interpreter abuse, and trusted download sources (e.g., GitHub). - It’s interesting because it reframes “living off the land” into “bring your own signed interpreter,” shifting detection from classic injection to process + script-content inspection and policy controls.
Technical Focus: - Bring Your Own Interpreter (BYOI/BYOSI) execution model - EDR user-mode API hooking avoidance via interpreter execution - PowerShell staging/downloading and archive extraction - Signed binary trust/allowlisting abuse (php.exe) - Script file type scanning gaps (PHP) and AMSI/Defender visibility limitations - Payload delivery via trusted hosting (GitHub) and network egress
Use Cases: - Validate whether an EDR inspects script content executed by third-party interpreters - Establish a foothold using a vendor-signed interpreter when unsigned binaries are blocked - Simulate adversary tradecraft for “trusted binary + script payload” execution chains - Purple-team detection engineering for interpreter spawning, unusual child processes, and script telemetry gaps - Assess controls like AppLocker/WDAC, network egress filtering, and script scanning coverage
Keywords: BYOSI, Bring Your Own Interpreter, PowerShell, PHP, php.exe, EDR evasion, API hooking, signed binary, allowlisting, script scanning, AMSI, Microsoft Defender, CrowdStrike, Trellix, SentinelOne, GitHub staging, payload download, C:\Temp, outbound connection, interpreter abuse

entry.title: Proxy Function Calls For ETwTI
entry.summary:
Title: Proxy-Function-Calls-For-ETwTI
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository provides a small C/ASM proof-of-concept that “proxies” Windows API calls to influence what Microsoft Defender for Endpoint (MDE/MDATP) sees in ETW Threat Intelligence (ETwTI) stack traces. - It focuses on hiding or obfuscating the call stack attribution for sensitive operations (notably memory allocation patterns) that are commonly surfaced via ETW/ETwTI-based telemetry. - The implementation uses an assembly helper to perform indirect/proxied invocation so the recorded stack frames differ from straightforward direct calls (per the referenced Dark Vortex research). - It’s primarily useful for red teamers, pentesters, and malware developers studying EDR telemetry evasion and how stack-walking-based detections can be degraded. - It’s interesting because it demonstrates a practical, low-level method to manipulate stack-trace visibility without necessarily patching ETW providers, highlighting a detection gap for stack-based heuristics.
Technical Focus: - ETW Threat Intelligence (ETwTI) telemetry and stack tracing - Call stack spoofing / stack trace manipulation via proxy calls - Indirect syscalls vs indirect API invocation tradeoffs - Windows memory allocation telemetry (e.g., VirtualAlloc-family patterns) - x64 Windows calling conventions and assembly stubs
Use Cases: - Build PoCs to test how MDE/ETwTI attributes memory allocations to modules/functions - Evaluate robustness of stack-walk-based detections in EDR products - Develop/compare evasion primitives (proxy calls vs ETW patching vs direct syscalls) - Create defensive test cases to validate detections for suspicious allocation chains
Keywords: ETW, ETwTI, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, MDATP, stack tracing, stack walking, call stack spoofing, proxy function calls, Windows internals, VirtualAlloc, memory allocation telemetry, x64 assembly, calling convention, nasm, mingw-w64, EDR evasion, user-mode telemetry, Dark Vortex

entry.title: Self Cleaning PICO Loader
entry.summary:
Title: Self-Cleaning PICO Loader for Crystal Palace
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This repository provides a position-independent code (PICO) loader variant for the Crystal Palace framework that “self-cleans” by erasing and freeing its own in-memory region after executing a payload. It addresses the common forensic/EDR artifact where injected loaders and RWX regions remain resident in a process after execution. The loader dynamically discovers its own start/end boundaries by using the entrypoint address for the base and an appended empty object/section (`pic_end.o`) as an end marker. It adapts sleep-obfuscation style techniques (Ekko-like timer/ROP-based flow) not to hide during sleep, but to change protections, zero the loader memory, and release it so the process continues without the loader region. The README includes Moneta screenshots comparing default vs self-cleaning behavior to demonstrate reduced in-memory traces. It’s primarily useful for red teams, pentesters, and offensive tool developers working on stealthy in-memory execution; defenders can use it to understand and hunt for self-deleting loader patterns. CFG-enabled targets are noted as requiring a CFG bypass for these ROP/timer-based techniques.
Technical Focus: - Position-Independent Code (PICO) loaders / Crystal Palace loader spec - Dynamic self-boundary discovery (start/end address calculation) - In-memory self-erasure (zeroing + freeing loader region) - Ekko-inspired timer-based ROP / sleep-obfuscation adaptation - Memory protection changes (RW/RWX transitions) and artifact reduction - Control Flow Guard (CFG) constraints and bypass requirement
Use Cases: - Building in-memory loaders that remove their own footprint post-execution - Reducing detectable RWX/RW regions left behind after payload run - Researching/replicating Ekko-style timer/ROP primitives for cleanup actions - Testing memory forensics tools (e.g., Moneta) against self-deleting implants - Developing detections for self-modifying/self-freeing injected code patterns
Keywords: Crystal Palace, PICO loader, position-independent code, in-memory execution, self-cleaning, memory erasure, VirtualProtect, RWX, Moneta, Ekko, sleep obfuscation, timer-based ROP, ROP gadgets, shellcode loader, process injection, CFG, Control Flow Guard bypass, artifact removal, evasive tradecraft

entry.title: VEHNetLoader
entry.summary:
Title: VEHNetLoader
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - VEHNetLoader is a Windows .NET assembly loader that focuses on evasion by combining CLR hosting with ETW and AMSI bypass techniques. - It implements “vectored syscalls” using Vectored Exception Handling (VEH) to execute Native API syscalls indirectly by intentionally triggering an access violation and reconstructing the syscall context in the exception handler. - For ETW suppression, it patches the syscall number (SSN) used by `NtTraceEvent` to a dummy value so ETW event emission fails. - For AMSI bypass, it modifies the `AmsiScanBuffer` string in the `.rdata` section of `clr.dll` so `GetProcAddress` cannot resolve the AMSI export, preventing scanning during reflective/hosted assembly loads. - Payloads can be RC4-encrypted on disk (helper script included) and decrypted at runtime before being loaded and invoked via `Load_3`/`Invoke_3` with `SAFEARRAY` arguments. - Useful for red teams/pentesters and malware researchers studying modern .NET loader tradecraft and common telemetry bypass patterns; also relevant to defenders for understanding detection opportunities (e.g., anomalous call stacks from vectored syscalls, in-memory patching of `clr.dll`/ETW paths).
Technical Focus: - Vectored Exception Handling (VEH) for indirect syscalls (“vectored syscalls”) - ETW bypass via patching `NtTraceEvent` syscall number (SSN) - AMSI bypass by tampering `clr.dll` `.rdata` (`AmsiScanBuffer` resolution failure) - CLR hosting to load and execute .NET assemblies (`Load_3`, `Invoke_3`, `SAFEARRAY`) - On-disk payload encryption/decryption (RC4)
Use Cases: - Load and execute a .NET payload from a native loader while attempting to reduce ETW/AMSI visibility - Research and prototype syscall indirection via VEH and its detection artifacts - Test EDR detections for in-memory patching of ETW/AMSI-related code/data - Deliver encrypted .NET assemblies and pass user-controlled arguments to the managed EntryPoint
Keywords: VEH, Vectored Exception Handling, vectored syscalls, indirect syscalls, Native API, syscall stub, SSN, NtTraceEvent, ETW, EtwEventWrite, AMSI, AmsiScanBuffer, clr.dll, GetProcAddress, CLR hosting, Load_3, Invoke_3, SAFEARRAY, RC4, .NET assembly loader

entry.title: Crystal Kit
entry.summary:
Title: Crystal Kit (Crystal-Kit)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Crystal Kit is an evasion-focused add-on for Cobalt Strike that experiments with replacing Cobalt Strike’s built-in evasion primitives (Sleepmask/BeaconGate) with a Crystal Palace PICO implementation. The goal is to evaluate whether alternative in-memory concealment and execution “primitives” provide better resilience against modern EDR detections than stock Beacon mechanisms. It integrates into a Cobalt Strike workflow via an Aggressor script (`crystalkit.cna`) and requires placing `crystalpalace.jar` in the client directory, with Malleable C2 settings adjusted to disable default sleep mask and stage obfuscation. The repo includes multiple loaders (standard, local, and post-ex) and low-level components (C/assembly) consistent with custom in-process execution and API interception. It’s primarily useful for red teams and security researchers testing advanced Beacon evasion tradeoffs and operational stability. It’s interesting because it explicitly treats evasion as swappable “primitives” and explores Crystal Palace PICO as a drop-in alternative to common Cobalt Strike patterns.
Technical Focus: - Cobalt Strike evasion primitives (Sleepmask, BeaconGate) replacement - Crystal Palace PICO integration (`crystalpalace.jar`) - Loader development (in-memory loading / post-execution loaders) - API hooking/interception (e.g., WinINet `HttpSendRequestA`) - Malleable C2 configuration changes for staging/obfuscation behavior
Use Cases: - Swap out default Beacon sleep masking with alternative PICO-based evasion for testing - Evaluate EDR detection differences between stock BeaconGate/Sleepmask and Crystal Palace primitives - Build/modify custom loaders for Beacon/post-ex DLL execution paths - Research and prototype userland API-hooking strategies around Beacon network I/O - Develop repeatable lab experiments for “evasion primitive” A/B comparisons
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, Beacon, Sleepmask, BeaconGate, Crystal Palace, PICO, Aggressor Script, crystalkit.cna, Malleable C2, in-memory loader, post-ex DLL, userland hooking, WinINet, HttpSendRequestA, EDR evasion, staging, obfuscation, Windows internals, C, assembly

entry.title: process inject kit
entry.summary:
Title: Process Inject Kit (Cobalt Strike Process Inject Kit port to C++ BOF)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository ports Cobalt Strike’s Process Inject Kit from C into a C++ Beacon Object File (BOF) template. - It provides reference implementations of Cobalt Strike’s built-in “fork&run/spawn” and “explicit” process injection techniques, intended to be swapped out via Aggressor Script hooks. - The key problem it solves is enabling operators to customize or replace Cobalt Strike’s default injection behavior while keeping compatibility with CS’s injection workflow. - It includes BOF source code, an Aggressor script (`process-inject.cna`) that implements the `PROCESS_INJECT_SPAWN` and `PROCESS_INJECT_EXPLICIT` hooks, and a Visual Studio solution to build x86/x64 artifacts. - Useful primarily for red teamers and tool developers who need controlled injection tradecraft (e.g., to adjust target processes, memory allocation/write/execute patterns, or operational constraints). - Interesting because it exposes a practical, modifiable injection framework aligned with Cobalt Strike 4.5+ injection updates and hook-based extensibility.
Technical Focus: - Cobalt Strike Aggressor Script resource hooks (`PROCESS_INJECT_SPAWN`, `PROCESS_INJECT_EXPLICIT`) - Beacon Object Files (BOF) in C/C++ - Windows process injection tradecraft (spawn vs explicit injection paths) - x86/x64 build and distribution workflow (Visual Studio solution) - Customization of CS “fork and run” injection pipeline
Use Cases: - Replace/modify Cobalt Strike default process injection to match an engagement’s OPSEC constraints - Prototype and test alternative injection implementations behind CS hooks - Build consistent x86/x64 injection modules for Beacon workflows - Research/diff injection behavior aligned with Cobalt Strike 4.5 process injection changes
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, Process Inject Kit, BOF, Beacon Object File, Aggressor Script, process-inject.cna, PROCESS_INJECT_SPAWN, PROCESS_INJECT_EXPLICIT, fork and run, spawn injection, explicit injection, Windows process injection, x86, x64, Visual Studio solution, red team tooling, tradecraft customization, injection hooks

entry.title: DLLirant
entry.summary:
Title: DLLirant
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): DLLirant is a Windows-focused tool that automates discovery of DLL search-order hijacking opportunities and assists with DLL proxying against a specified PE (EXE or DLL). It addresses the manual effort of enumerating imported DLLs/exports and repeatedly crafting test DLLs to confirm whether a target binary will load attacker-controlled libraries. The tool parses PE imports (via PeNet), generates custom DLLs exporting required functions, and iteratively tests them to detect execution paths that confirm hijacking viability (dropping a marker file at `C:\DLLirant\output.txt`). When a candidate is found, it generates proof-of-concept artifacts and stores them (e.g., under a `dll-hijacks` directory) alongside an output directory containing the staged target binary and dependencies. It also supports generating proxy DLLs that forward exports to a legitimate DLL while adding custom code. This is primarily useful for red teams and pentesters performing local privilege escalation/persistence tradecraft validation, and for defenders/researchers auditing software for unsafe DLL loading behavior.
Technical Focus: - DLL search order hijacking (load-order abuse) on Windows - PE import table parsing and dependency enumeration (PeNet) - Automated DLL generation with matching exported functions - DLL proxying / export forwarding stubs - PoC generation and execution confirmation via filesystem marker - Handling missing dependencies via staged `import`/`output` directories
Use Cases: - Rapidly triage third-party executables for DLL hijacking candidates - Generate working PoCs for confirmed hijackable DLL names/exports - Build proxy DLLs to wrap legitimate libraries while adding payload logic - Validate application hardening (SafeDllSearchMode, known DLLs, proper paths) - Support software supply-chain/security reviews of Windows applications
Keywords: DLL hijacking, DLL search order, DLL proxying, export forwarding, Windows loader, LoadLibrary, PE imports, Import Address Table, PeNet, C#, WPF, LLVM for Windows, proof-of-concept, persistence, local privilege escalation, binary dependency analysis, csrss.exe, SetErrorMode, staged dependencies

entry.title: ShellcodeEncrypt2DLL
entry.summary:
Title: ShellcodeEncrypt2DLL
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - ShellcodeEncrypt2DLL is a Python-based generator that produces a Windows DLL loader which decrypts and executes embedded shellcode at runtime. - It aims to reduce static AV detections by encrypting both the shellcode and suspicious API/function-name strings, and by resolving APIs dynamically. - The tool supports two build modes: “standalone” (AES key stored in the DLL for self-decryption) and “non-standalone” (key supplied at runtime, e.g., via rundll32 export argument) to hinder offline recovery if the DLL is captured. - It uses a C++ loader template compiled via mingw-w64 on Linux, with Python (pycryptodome) generating headers and encrypted blobs. - An optional patching step is included to tweak the produced DLL for specific AV products. - Useful primarily for red teams/pentesters and malware developers experimenting with encrypted payload loaders and DLL execution tradecraft (sideloading/hijacking/rundll32 execution).
Technical Focus: - AES-CBC encryption of shellcode and strings - Dynamic API resolution / string obfuscation - DLL export-based execution via rundll32 (EPoint) - Loader generation pipeline (Python → C++ template → mingw-w64 build) - Standalone vs external-key decryption design - Optional binary patching for AV-specific bypasses
Use Cases: - Generate an encrypted DLL shellcode loader for controlled red team operations - Test static detection resilience against AV/EDR with encrypted strings and payloads - Build DLLs for sideloading/hijacking scenarios where an exported entrypoint is invoked - Create “keyless-in-binary” loaders requiring an operator-supplied decryption key at runtime - Rapidly iterate loader variants by modifying template/patch steps
Keywords: DLL loader, shellcode, AES-CBC, pycryptodome, dynamic API loading, string encryption, VirtualAlloc, CreateThread, rundll32, export function, EPoint, DLL sideloading, DLL hijacking, mingw-w64, x86_64-w64-mingw32-g++, C++ template, patching, static AV evasion, msfvenom

entry.title: pyobject inject
entry.summary:
Title: pyobject_inject — executing shellcode directly from a Python variable
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository demonstrates a technique to execute shellcode directly from a Python bytes-like object without allocating a separate buffer. - It leverages CPython object layout by taking the object’s address via `id()` and adding a fixed offset (~32 bytes) to skip the `PyObject` header and reach the raw byte payload. - The code then changes memory protections with `VirtualProtect` to mark the region executable and runs it by casting the address to a callable (`ctypes.CFUNCTYPE`) and optionally executing it in a new thread. - It’s primarily a proof-of-concept “toy” for understanding CPython internals and in-memory execution tradeoffs, tested on Python 3.10–3.12 with varying reliability. - Useful for red teamers, exploit devs, and malware researchers exploring Python-based in-memory execution and detection implications.
Technical Focus: - CPython `PyObject` memory layout and header offsets - `id()` as an address leak / pointer primitive in CPython - `ctypes` function pointer casting (`CFUNCTYPE`) - Windows memory permission changes (`VirtualProtect`) - Threaded shellcode execution from managed runtime memory
Use Cases: - Prototype in-memory shellcode execution from Python without explicit `VirtualAlloc` - Research CPython object layout stability across versions (3.10–3.12) - Explore EDR/telemetry differences between Python-managed memory execution vs classic RWX allocation - Build minimal PoCs for payload staging inside Python processes
Keywords: Python, CPython, PyObject, id(), bytes object, memory offset, ctypes, CFUNCTYPE, shellcode, in-memory execution, VirtualProtect, Windows API, page permissions, executable memory, thread creation, code injection, runtime internals, pointer arithmetic

entry.title: RustVEHSyscalls
entry.summary:
Title: RustVEHSyscalls
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - RustVEHSyscalls is a Rust port of White Knight Labs’ LayeredSyscall technique for performing indirect Windows syscalls while preserving “legitimate-looking” user-mode call stacks. - It targets user-land EDR/API hooking by making syscalls appear to originate from normal Win32 API call paths rather than direct syscall stubs. - The project abuses Vectored Exception Handling (VEH) plus hardware breakpoints to capture a real API call stack frame and then redirect execution into an `ntdll.dll` syscall wrapper. - It includes setup/teardown routines to register vectored exception handlers and a `syscall!` macro to resolve syscall addresses and System Service Numbers (SSNs). - SSN and syscall stub resolution is done by walking the PEB to find `ntdll.dll` and parsing PE structures (Exception Directory and Export Address Table). - Useful for red teamers, pentesters, and offensive tool developers researching syscall-based evasion and call stack spoofing; also relevant for blue teams studying detection gaps around VEH/hardware breakpoint abuse.
Technical Focus: - Vectored Exception Handling (VEH) abuse for control-flow redirection - Hardware breakpoints (debug registers) for syscall interception - Indirect syscalls via `ntdll.dll` syscall wrappers - PEB walking and `ntdll.dll` discovery - PE parsing: Exception Directory + Export Address Table for SSN resolution - Call stack frame “legitimization” / stack spoofing concepts
Use Cases: - Building syscall-capable loaders/implants in Rust that avoid common user-mode hooks - Researching call stack–based EDR detections and bypass strategies - Prototyping stealthier invocation of sensitive NT syscalls (e.g., process creation) - Developing and testing defensive detections for VEH registration and debug-register breakpoint patterns
Keywords: Rust, Windows, Vectored Exception Handling, VEH, hardware breakpoint, debug registers, indirect syscall, ntdll.dll, syscall stub, System Service Number, SSN, PEB, PE parsing, Exception Directory, Export Address Table, userland hooks, EDR bypass, call stack spoofing, NtCreateUserProcess, LayeredSyscall

entry.title: InflativeLoading
entry.summary:
Title: InflativeLoading
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - InflativeLoading is a Windows x64 toolchain that converts unmanaged EXE/DLL payloads into position-independent “shellcode” by dumping the PE as it exists in memory and prepending a custom loader stub. - It aims to improve on classic reflective loading by using an in-memory dump (reducing disk-vs-memory discrepancies), avoiding extra manual mapping work, and enabling more controlled memory permissions (avoiding RWX). - The generated stub walks the PEB to resolve kernel32 exports (LoadLibraryA/GetProcAddress), patches PE structures (imports, relocations, delayed imports), optionally obfuscates PE header signatures, then transfers execution to the PE entry point and exits cleanly. - Recent updates add PE header signature obfuscation, NOP-like padding sequences (instead of long 0x90 runs), improved relocation/import handling for edge cases (e.g., malformed relocation entries in some payloads), and support for unmanaged DLLs. - Includes a companion C++ utility (DumpPEFromMemory.exe) that creates a suspended process (EXE) or maps a DLL to dump the main module and suggests memory allocation layout for the final shellcode blob. - Useful for red teams, implant developers, and malware researchers experimenting with PE-to-shellcode conversion, loader tradecraft, and evasive in-memory execution; also relevant for blue teams studying detection opportunities around custom loaders and PE header tampering.
Technical Focus: - PEB walking and dynamic API resolution (kernel32 export parsing) - In-memory PE dumping (suspended process / file mapping) and module reconstruction - Import Address Table (IAT) fixing and delayed import handling - Base relocation processing and edge-case relocation validation - PE header/signature obfuscation (e_lfanew and directory RVAs) and post-load wiping - Memory layout/permissions strategy (separate regions for stub/header/.text/other sections; avoiding RWX)
Use Cases: - Convert unmanaged EXE tools (e.g., console utilities) into PIC-like shellcode blobs for custom loaders - Convert unmanaged DLL payloads into shellcode for in-memory execution without requiring a reflective export - Experiment with PE header obfuscation and reduced static signatures in memory - Build/test alternative staging formats for C2 payloads (e.g., Havoc/Cobalt Strike stageless EXE/DLL) - Research/detect reflective-loading variants and PE-structure patching behaviors
Keywords: Windows x64, PE, PIC shellcode, reflective loading, PEB walk, kernel32.dll, LoadLibraryA, GetProcAddress, IAT fixing, delayed imports, base relocations, PE header obfuscation, e_lfanew, VirtualAlloc, memory permissions, in-memory dumping, suspended process, DLL mapping, UPX packed EXE, Cobalt Strike, Havoc

entry.title: COFFLoader
entry.summary:
Title: COFFLoader (Beacon Object File / COFF Loader)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - COFFLoader is a standalone loader for COFF object files used as Cobalt Strike Beacon Object Files (BOFs), allowing operators to execute unmodified BOFs without running a Cobalt Strike agent. - It implements a subset of Beacon’s internal APIs via a “beacon_compatibility” layer so BOFs expecting Beacon functions can run in this harness. - The project includes a Python helper (beacon_generate.py) to build Beacon-compatible argument buffers (hex-encoded) for BOFs that parse parameters via BeaconDataParse/BeaconDataExtract. - Injection-related Beacon compatibility functions are stubbed/empty, so BOFs relying on those features won’t fully work. - It’s primarily useful for red teamers/pentesters and BOF developers to test, debug, and validate BOFs in isolation and to understand BOF argument packing and execution flow. - Interesting because it provides a minimal, inspectable reference implementation of BOF loading/execution and Beacon API shims outside of Cobalt Strike.
Technical Focus: - COFF/BOF parsing and in-memory execution - Beacon API compatibility layer (BeaconDataParse/Extract, formatting/output helpers) - BOF argument packing/serialization (datap buffers) - Windows x64 BOF execution workflow (go() entrypoint) - Cross-platform build/testing harness (Makefile, test object)
Use Cases: - Execute and test Cobalt Strike BOFs without a Beacon session - Debug BOF argument parsing and buffer layouts using generated hex arguments - Validate BOF behavior in a controlled harness during development - Port/adapt BOFs to other frameworks by reusing/understanding Beacon API shims - Build CI-style regression tests for BOFs using the included test target
Keywords: COFF, Beacon Object File, BOF, Cobalt Strike, COFF loader, go() entrypoint, BeaconDataParse, BeaconDataExtract, beacon_compatibility, argument packing, hex arguments, Windows x64, object file parsing, TrustedSec, CS-Situational-Awareness-BOF, red team tooling, Sliver Armory

entry.title: ShellCodeLoader Indirect Syscalls
entry.summary:
Title: ShellCodeLoader_Indirect_Syscalls (Shellcode Loader using indirect syscalls)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This repository contains a Windows shellcode loader that executes payloads via indirect syscalls to reduce exposure to user-mode EDR API hooks. It re-implements the HellsGate approach for resolving syscall numbers/stubs, but uses an indirect syscall technique to make the call stack appear less anomalous than classic direct-syscall loaders. The project is explicitly inspired by BokuLoader and HellsGate and references MalwareTech’s write-up on bypassing user-mode hooks. It was built as an educational/CRTO2 preparation project and positioned as a stepping stone toward a User-Defined Reflective Loader (UDRL) style loader. The repo also includes tooling for API hashing (Python script) and notes planned work like custom GetProcAddress/GetModuleHandle and potentially manual mapping of ntdll. Useful primarily for red teamers, exploit devs, and malware researchers studying syscall-based evasion and loader tradecraft.
Technical Focus: - Indirect syscalls vs direct syscalls (call stack evasion) - HellsGate-style syscall stub/SSN resolution from ntdll - Shellcode loading and in-memory execution on Windows - User-mode EDR hook bypass concepts - API hashing and custom API resolution (planned/partial) - (Planned) ntdll manual mapping to avoid hooked exports
Use Cases: - Build/test a standalone shellcode loader for research and debugging - Experiment with bypassing user-mode API hooks by avoiding Win32 API call paths - Prototype syscall-based execution primitives for custom loaders/UDRL concepts - Study call stack artifacts and how indirect syscalls change telemetry - Generate and use API hashes for stealthier import resolution
Keywords: Windows, shellcode loader, indirect syscalls, direct syscalls, HellsGate, BokuLoader, ntdll.dll, syscall number (SSN), syscall stub, EDR bypass, user-mode hooks, call stack spoofing, GetProcAddress, GetModuleHandle, API hashing, reflective loader, UDRL, in-memory execution, x64 assembly, red team tooling

entry.title: Readme.md
entry.summary:
Title: ReflectiveDLLInjection (Stephen Fewer) – Reflective DLL Injection / In-Memory PE Loader
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository implements “Reflective DLL Injection,” a technique where a DLL loads itself from an arbitrary memory location inside a target process by embedding a minimal PE loader (ReflectiveLoader). - It solves the problem of executing a DLL in a remote process without relying on the normal Windows loader to load the DLL from disk, by manually mapping headers/sections, resolving imports, and applying relocations. - The workflow assumes the DLL bytes are already written into the target process, then execution is transferred (e.g., via CreateRemoteThread or bootstrap shellcode) to the exported ReflectiveLoader. - ReflectiveLoader locates its own image in memory, finds kernel32 exports to resolve LoadLibraryA/GetProcAddress/VirtualAlloc, allocates memory, maps the image, fixes imports/relocations, and calls DllMain(DLL_PROCESS_ATTACH). - Useful primarily for red teams, pentesters, and malware developers as a foundational building block for in-memory module loading and process injection tradecraft. - It’s notable as a canonical reference implementation of reflective loading/manual mapping used widely in offensive tooling.
Technical Focus: - Reflective DLL loading (manual PE mapping) - Remote process injection (CreateRemoteThread / bootstrap shellcode) - PE parsing (headers, sections, export/import tables) - Import resolution via LoadLibraryA/GetProcAddress - Relocations (base relocation processing) - Kernel32 export table parsing / dynamic API resolution
Use Cases: - Inject a DLL into a remote process without standard LoadLibrary-from-disk semantics - Build custom in-memory loaders for post-exploitation payloads - Research and prototype EDR-evasion-adjacent module loading techniques (manual mapping) - Develop and test detection logic for reflective/manual-mapped modules
Keywords: Reflective DLL Injection, ReflectiveLoader, manual mapping, PE loader, Portable Executable, CreateRemoteThread, shellcode bootstrap, VirtualAlloc, LoadLibraryA, GetProcAddress, kernel32.dll, export table parsing, import table, relocation table, DllMain, DLL_PROCESS_ATTACH, process injection, in-memory execution, Windows internals

entry.title: GhostStrike
entry.summary:
Title: GhostStrike
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - GhostStrike is a Windows C++ red-team tool that executes a payload via process hollowing to obtain a stealthy reverse shell/C2 execution path. - It focuses on evasion by avoiding static imports (dynamic API resolution via hashing), obscuring payload material (Base64 + XOR + Windows Crypto API–derived keys), and complicating analysis (control-flow flattening). - The workflow in the README shows generating a Sliver implant, exposing a listener via ngrok, converting the implant to shellcode with Donut, and embedding it into the C++ project for injection. - The core technique is running encrypted shellcode inside a legitimate target process to blend execution and reduce straightforward signature-based detection. - It’s primarily useful for Red Teamers and offensive security researchers experimenting with Windows injection/evasion tradecraft and for Blue Teamers who want a compact sample to build detections around.
Technical Focus: - Process hollowing on Windows - Dynamic API resolution (hash-based) - Shellcode obfuscation (Base64, XOR) - Windows Cryptography APIs for key generation/encryption - Control-flow flattening / code obfuscation - Payload generation pipeline (Sliver C2, Donut)
Use Cases: - Execute a C2 implant by hollowing a benign Windows process - Test EDR/AV detection coverage for API-hashing and hollowing behaviors - Build and validate defensive telemetry/detections for injection chains - Research payload protection layers (encoding + encryption) in memory-injection tooling
Keywords: process hollowing, Windows injection, C++, dynamic API resolution, API hashing, shellcode, Base64, XOR encryption, Windows CryptoAPI, key generation, control flow flattening, Sliver C2, ngrok, Donut, reverse shell, in-memory execution, evasion, malware tradecraft, Windows API, red team tool

entry.title: LoudSunRun
entry.summary:
Title: LoudSunRun — Stack Spoofing with Synthetic Frames (namazso / SilentMoonWalk / VulcanRaven-inspired)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - LoudSunRun is a Windows x64 C/ASM proof-of-concept for call stack spoofing by constructing synthetic stack frames and spoofing return addresses. - It aims to make sensitive API/syscall execution appear to originate from benign-looking call stacks (e.g., thread start wrappers), complicating EDR/user-mode stack-walk based detections. - The implementation allocates stack arguments, generates fake frames, prepares (optionally indirect) syscalls via SSNs, and uses a `jmp rbx` trampoline to pivot execution into a `fixup` routine that restores registers/stack and returns to the original caller. - A parameter struct drives the spoofing (trampoline gadget address, original return address, per-frame stack sizes, desired “shown” return addresses, and optional syscall number). - Examples include spoofed `NtAllocateVirtualMemory`, `memcpy`, and `NtCreateThreadEx` to perform local shellcode injection/execution. - Useful for red teams, implant developers, and researchers evaluating or bypassing user-mode telemetry that relies on call stack integrity.
Technical Focus: - Synthetic stack frame construction (stack spoofing / stack walking deception) - Return address spoofing with trampoline gadgets (`jmp rbx`) - Indirect syscalls using SSNs (Nt* invocation patterns) - Windows x64 calling convention and stack argument layout - Stack “cutoff” technique (pushing 0 to terminate stack walk) - Fixup/epilogue logic to restore stack pointer and registers
Use Cases: - EDR evasion research against user-mode call stack inspection - Building stealthier syscall/API invocation wrappers in implants - Testing detection logic for anomalous stack frames / ROP-like trampolines - Demonstrating synthetic-frame techniques for training and PoC development - Local process injection chains with spoofed call stacks
Keywords: Windows x64, stack spoofing, synthetic frames, return address spoofing, call stack spoofing, stack walking, EDR evasion, indirect syscall, SSN, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, NtCreateThreadEx, RtlUserThreadStart, BaseThreadInitThunk, trampoline gadget, jmp rbx, ROP, calling convention, shellcode injection, ntdll, kernel32

entry.title: DLHell
entry.summary:
Title: DLHell — Local & Remote Windows DLL Proxying (DCOM-assisted)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - DLHell is a Python tool from Synacktiv that automates crafting “proxy” DLLs and deploying them locally or to remote Windows hosts to achieve DLL hijacking/proxying. - It solves the operational friction of building a forwarding DLL, renaming/staging the original DLL, and placing both in a target directory so a chosen process loads the attacker-controlled proxy. - The tool supports remote operations over SMB (C$ admin share) and can optionally trigger execution via DCOM activation using a supplied CLSID/ProgID list (from `dcom.json`). - It compiles C++ proxy DLLs from a template (`template.tpe`) using MinGW-w64 toolchains and embeds a user-specified command to execute (e.g., launching a payload). - Authentication options mirror Impacket-style targeting, including NTLM hashes and Kerberos (ccache/AES key). - Useful for red teams and pentesters who already have admin-equivalent access and want repeatable DLL proxying/hijack deployment and optional DCOM-based triggering.
Technical Focus: - Windows DLL proxying / forwarding DLL generation - Remote DLL hijacking via SMB admin shares (C$) - DCOM activation via CLSID/ProgID (COM/DCOM) - Automated retrieval/renaming/staging of original DLLs - Kerberos/NTLM authentication flows (Impacket-style) - Cross-compilation of Windows DLLs with MinGW-w64
Use Cases: - Automate creation of a proxy DLL that forwards exports while executing a command/payload - Remote staging of hijacked DLL + original DLL on a Windows host via SMB (requires admin) - Trigger execution by activating a vulnerable DCOM class (ProgID/CLSID-driven) - Enumerate and extend known DCOM hijack targets via `dcom.json` - Rapid testing of DLL search-order/hijack opportunities in controlled assessments
Keywords: Windows, DLL hijacking, DLL proxying, forwarding DLL, DCOM, COM, CLSID, ProgID, SMB, C$ admin share, Impacket, Kerberos, NTLM, MinGW-w64, cross-compilation, lateral movement, remote code execution, netutils.dll, PROPSYS.dll

entry.title: Invoke SPSI
entry.summary:
Title: Invoke-SPSI – Simple PowerShell Shellcode Injector
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Invoke-SPSI is a PowerShell-based shellcode injector that decrypts an XOR-encrypted payload in memory and injects it into a remote target process. - It uses a .NET D/Invoke approach to call Win32 APIs without traditional P/Invoke signatures, aiming to reduce static detections in the script itself. - The repo includes a small Python helper (xor.py) to XOR-encode raw shellcode and a PowerShell script (Invoke-SPSI.ps1) that fetches the encrypted payload from disk or URL, decrypts it, and performs remote process injection. - The author positions it as a CTF/lightweight lab tool demonstrating that Windows Defender can sometimes be bypassed even with highly signatured payloads (e.g., Meterpreter) using simple obfuscation + injection. - It explicitly does not claim to bypass modern EDR or paid AV, and warns against real engagement use. - Useful primarily for red teamers/pentesters in controlled environments and for defenders studying common injection tradecraft and detection opportunities.
Technical Focus: - XOR payload obfuscation and in-memory decryption - PowerShell loader patterns (IEX/IWR, local/remote payload retrieval) - .NET D/Invoke to access Win32 APIs - Remote process injection workflow (allocate/write/execute in target process) - Basic AV (Windows Defender) evasion via trivial encoding + API indirection
Use Cases: - Demonstrating process injection fundamentals in Windows labs/CTFs - Testing Defender-only environments against simple in-memory loaders - Building detection content for script-based injection + remote memory operations - Rapid prototyping of PowerShell tradecraft (payload staging, decryption, injection)
Keywords: PowerShell, shellcode injection, process injection, D/Invoke, .NET, Win32 API, XOR encryption, in-memory decryption, remote process, VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, CreateRemoteThread, Meterpreter, Windows Defender, IEX, IWR, payload staging, CTF tooling, AV evasion

entry.title: DeepSleep
entry.summary:
Title: DeepSleep
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): DeepSleep is an x64 “Gargoyle”-style proof-of-concept that hides in-memory artifacts by changing its own memory protections while the implant is sleeping. It builds a ROP chain (VirtualProtect → Sleep → VirtualProtect) to mark the payload’s pages as inaccessible (N/A / PAGE_NOACCESS) during idle time, then restores executable permissions when active. The implementation is position-independent code (PIC) and avoids APC-based execution, aiming to reduce common injection/sleep-hook artifacts and make page enumeration simpler. The repo demonstrates bypassing memory scanners like Moneta (at the time of writing) when injected such that the executing thread’s base address doesn’t point to private committed memory. It’s primarily useful for red teams, implant developers, and researchers studying in-memory evasion and “sleep masking” tradeoffs. It also documents a detection angle: suspicious call stacks for threads in DelayExecution that include VirtualProtect and unknown/tampered regions.
Technical Focus: - Sleep masking via memory protection flipping (VirtualProtect + Sleep) - ROP-only execution flow (x64 ROP chains, gadget selection in ntdll) - Position-independent code (PIC) payload design - Memory scanner evasion (Moneta / PE-sieve-style heuristics) - Injection validation using Early Bird APC injection - Detection via thread call stack analysis during DelayExecution
Use Cases: - Implementing/experimenting with sleep-masking in custom implants - Evaluating how memory scanners detect “sleeping beacons” and protection changes - Researching ROP-based control flow to avoid direct API call patterns - Building test cases for defensive tooling that inspects thread stacks and memory protections
Keywords: DeepSleep, Gargoyle, sleep masking, ROP chain, x64, PIC, VirtualProtect, Sleep, PAGE_NOACCESS, ntdll.dll, ROP gadgets, Moneta, PE-sieve, memory artifact evasion, Early Bird APC, process injection, DelayExecution, call stack inspection, Hunt-Sleeping-Beacons, Windows 10 19044

entry.title: Hunt Sleeping Beacons
entry.summary:
Title: Hunt-Sleeping-Beacons
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Hunt-Sleeping-Beacons is a Windows userland callstack-scanning tool designed to identify “sleeping” C2 beacons by analyzing why threads are blocked/idle between callbacks. - It looks for indicators of injected/unpacked agents and common sleepmask implementations by inspecting thread callstacks for suspicious frames and memory characteristics. - The tool flags IOCs such as unbacked private RX/RWX memory in stacks, non-executable pages (permission-flipping sleepmasks), and module stomping via working set attributes. - It also detects APC- and timer-based sleepmasks by spotting relevant ntdll dispatcher/callback frames and, notably, enumerates threadpool timers and their callback addresses from userland. - Additional heuristics include abnormal intermodular call sequences consistent with module proxying and patterns suggestive of return address spoofing. - Results are heuristic (false positives expected, especially module stomping); by default it ignores .NET and 32-bit processes. - Useful for Blue Teams/EDR engineers and threat hunters validating suspicious processes, and for Red Teams to understand detectable artifacts of sleepmasking and stack evasion.
Technical Focus: - Thread callstack inspection for blocked threads (sleeping beacon detection) - Memory forensics heuristics (unbacked pages, RX/RWX, NX permission flips) - Module stomping detection via MEMORY_WORKING_SET_EX_INFORMATION / SharedOriginal - APC-based sleepmask detection (KiUserApcDispatcher, NtContinue, wait APIs) - Threadpool timer queue enumeration via NtQueryInformationWorkerFactory / WORKER_FACTORY_BASIC_INFORMATION - Stack evasion detection (module proxying patterns, return address spoofing/jmp-gadget returns)
Use Cases: - Hunt for in-memory C2 agents that “sleep” between callbacks - Triage suspicious processes by correlating idle threads with stack/memory IOCs - Detect timer/APC-based sleepmasking and enumerate suspicious timer callbacks - Validate or tune defensive detections around module stomping and stack manipulation - Red-team self-assessment of beacon OPSEC against callstack/timer-based hunting
Keywords: Windows, callstack scanning, sleeping beacons, C2 agent, in-memory injection, unpacked payload, sleepmask, APC, KiUserApcDispatcher, NtContinue, WaitForSingleObject, threadpool timers, TimerQueue, NtQueryInformationWorkerFactory, WORKER_FACTORY_BASIC_INFORMATION, FULL_TP_POOL, module stomping, MEMORY_WORKING_SET_EX_INFORMATION, SharedOriginal, copy-on-write, module proxying, return address spoofing, jmp gadget, ntdll, kernel32

entry.title: BOAZ beta
entry.summary:
Title: BOAZ (Bypass, Obfuscate, Adapt, Zero-trace) — Multilayered AV/EDR Evasion Framework (beta)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): BOAZ is a Windows-focused evasion framework/packer that generates polymorphic PE/DLL/CPL outputs from x64 PE files or raw payloads, aiming to bypass signature, heuristic, and behavioral detections across AV/EDR products. It provides a “defense-in-depth but offensive” layered pipeline combining payload/PIC conversion (e.g., Donut/PE2SH/Stardust), multiple encoders/encryptors, LLVM IR obfuscation (Pluto/Akira), and extensive loader/process-injection templates. The project emphasizes syscall-centric execution (direct/indirect syscalls, Halo’s Gate/TartarusGate), API unhooking, call-stack spoofing/proxy syscalls, and “threadless” execution primitives using VEH/VCH, page guards, and vtable-pointer based techniques. It also includes ETW bypass options (patching and a patchless HWBP+VEH redirection approach), anti-emulation checks, sleep/stack encryption, entropy reduction, signing/metadata cloning, and post-execution anti-forensics/self-deletion. BOAZ is primarily useful for red teams, pentesters, and offensive security researchers evaluating EDR resilience and testing detection coverage; defenders can use it to understand modern evasion tradecraft and build detections around behaviors rather than static indicators. It’s notable for the breadth of modular evasion combinations and the large catalog of loader variants targeting memory scanning and userland hooking.
Technical Focus: - Multi-layered AV/EDR evasion pipeline (signature/heuristic/behavioral) - PIC/shellcode generation and payload encoding/encryption (Donut, PE2SH, Stardust; AES/RC4/DES/ChaCha20, UUID/MAC/IP encodings) - Process injection & code execution loaders (APC, remote/local injection, thread hijacking, DLL overloading, threadless primitives) - Syscall evasion and unhooking (direct/indirect syscalls, Halo’s Gate/TartarusGate, hook detection, custom call stacks) - ETW bypass (NtTraceEvent patching and patchless VEH/HWBP-based bypass) - Anti-forensics and trace removal (AmCache/ShimCache cleanup, registry timestamp tampering, self-deletion)
Use Cases: - Generate obfuscated/encoded Windows payload wrappers for controlled red-team simulations - Compare AV/EDR detection efficacy across different loader + obfuscation + encoding combinations - Prototype and test syscall-based injection and hook-evasion techniques in a repeatable framework - Build DLL/CPL sideloading artifacts for execution-chain testing (rundll32/control.exe) - Research ETW bypass tradeoffs (patching vs patchless) and memory-scanner evasion patterns
Keywords: BOAZ, AV evasion, EDR bypass, Windows 10, Windows 11, Windows Server 2022, PE packer, polymorphic loader, process injection, T1055, direct syscalls, indirect syscalls, SysWhispers2, Halo’s Gate, TartarusGate, ntdll unhooking, IAT/EAT hooking detection, VEH, VCH, PAGE_GUARD, hardware breakpoints, ETW bypass, NtTraceEvent, EtwEventWrite, NtContinue, RtlCaptureContext, call stack spoofing, Donut, PE2SH, Stardust, LLVM obfuscation, control flow flattening, string encryption, RC4 SystemFunction032/033, DES SystemFunction002, AmCache, ShimCache, PPID spoofing

entry.title: dittobytes
entry.summary:
Title: Dittobytes — Metamorphic cross-compilation of C/C++ to PIC, BOF & EXE
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Dittobytes is a build system and custom LLVM-based toolchain that compiles C/C++ into truly position-independent code (PIC) shellcode, Beacon Object Files (BOF/COFF), and minimal executables across Windows, Linux, and macOS for AMD64 and ARM64. - It solves the common need for small, loader-less shellcode by compiling code directly to PIC without relying on reflective loaders (e.g., Donut/sRDI) or polymorphic decrypt stubs. - The project includes a metamorphic engine implemented as LLVM passes that introduce randomized, functionally equivalent instruction-level transformations so each build produces unique shellcode. - An intermediate LLVM Function Pass inlines constants to avoid `.rodata` dependencies, and a MachineFunction pass performs metamorphic transformations like register randomization and instruction substitution. - It ships example payload code, per-platform loaders for testing, and a feature-test suite to validate transformations. - Useful for red teams, offensive tool developers, and malware researchers interested in cross-platform PIC generation and evasion-oriented compilation techniques.
Technical Focus: - Position Independent Code (PIC) generation from C/C++ - LLVM pass development (Function Pass, MachineFunction Pass) - Metamorphic transformations (register allocation randomization, instruction substitution, nullification transforms) - Cross-compilation toolchains (Windows/Linux/macOS; AMD64/ARM64) - BOF/COFF artifact generation and `.text` segment extraction - API self-resolution constraints for shellcode-style payloads
Use Cases: - Generate loader-less PIC shellcode from custom C payloads for multiple OS/architectures - Produce Cobalt Strike-compatible BOFs from the same codebase - Create minimal “PIC-only” executables with constants inlined (reduced non-`.text` dependencies) - Build metamorphic variants of payloads to diversify static signatures across compilations - Research/benchmark metamorphic compilation and detection resilience using provided tests/loaders
Keywords: Dittobytes, LLVM 18, Clang, position-independent code, PIC shellcode, metamorphic code, polymorphism, LLVM Function Pass, LLVM MachineFunction Pass, instruction substitution, register allocation randomization, `.text` segment extraction, `.rodata` inlining, BOF, COFF, Cobalt Strike, cross-compilation, Windows, macOS, Linux, AMD64, ARM64, shellcode loader, evasion

entry.title: kong loader
entry.summary:
Title: Kong Loader — JIT Instruction Decryption Shellcode Loader (“rolling” per-instruction decryption)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Kong Loader is a Windows shellcode loader that uses Just-In-Time (JIT) per-instruction decryption so only the currently executing instruction is ever present in decrypted form in memory. It targets EDR memory-scanning detections that can catch traditional shellcode or even “sleep-masked” payloads during their decrypted execution windows. The loader disassembles/decrypts one instruction at a time, executes it, then re-encrypts it, trading significant performance and debuggability for reduced in-memory visibility. The project documents practical caveats (self-modifying shellcode issues, extreme slowness, no multithreading) and acknowledges the loader’s native stub is currently signatureable. It includes a YARA rule to detect the loader’s static native code and outlines future work like basic-block decryption and polymorphism. Useful primarily for red teamers/malware researchers exploring advanced in-memory evasion concepts, and for blue teamers studying detection opportunities around such loaders.
Technical Focus: - Per-instruction JIT decryption / rolling shellcode decryption - In-memory evasion vs EDR memory scanning - Shellcode loading/execution on Windows (x64) - Disassembly-driven execution (Zydis) - Detection engineering (YARA signatures) - PIC/position-independent first-stage constraints
Use Cases: - Build tiny first-stage payloads that minimize decrypted memory footprint during execution - Research/benchmark EDR memory scanning limits against per-instruction visibility - Develop and test detections (YARA/static signatures, behavioral heuristics) for JIT-decrypting loaders - Explore tradeoffs of instruction-level emulation/decryption vs sleep-masking approaches
Keywords: shellcode loader, JIT decryption, rolling decryption, per-instruction decrypt, EDR evasion, memory scanning, in-memory execution, sleep mask, Windows x64, PIC, position-independent code, Zydis, disassembler, YARA, signature detection, basic block decryption, polymorphic engine, red team, malware loader, Cobalt Strike

entry.title: dll hijack by proxying
entry.summary:
Title: Exploiting DLL Hijacking by DLL Proxying Super Easily (dll-hijack-by-proxying)
Type: GitHub Tool (tutorial + PoC code)
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository demonstrates how to exploit Windows DLL hijacking reliably using “DLL proxying” (a proxy DLL that forwards exports to the real DLL while running attacker code). - It addresses the common problem where naive replacement DLLs crash the target application due to missing/incorrect exports or behavior. - The technique uses a generated `.def` file to forward all exported functions (including ordinals) to a renamed legitimate DLL (e.g., `version_orig.dll`), while executing a payload from `DllMain()`. - It includes a Python script (`gen_def.py`) using `pefile` to extract exports from a legitimate DLL and emit the forwarding `.def` file automatically. - The build workflow emphasizes cross-compiling Windows proxy DLLs from Linux using `mingw-w64`. - Useful for red teamers/pentesters developing stable DLL hijack payloads and for defenders understanding how hijacks can be made non-disruptive and stealthier.
Technical Focus: - Windows DLL search order / DLL hijacking mechanics - Proxy DLL export forwarding via Module-Definition (`.def`) files - PE export table parsing with Python `pefile` - `DllMain()` execution and payload staging - Cross-compilation with `mingw-w64` (i686-w64-mingw32-gcc) - Discovery/validation with Sysinternals Process Monitor
Use Cases: - Build non-crashing hijack DLLs for vulnerable portable/side-loaded Windows apps - Rapidly generate export-forwarding stubs for arbitrary target DLLs - Create PoCs for DLL search order issues found during assessments - Reproduce and validate DLL hijack findings with realistic “works normally” behavior - Blue-team testing: simulate proxy-hijack tradecraft for detections/hardening
Keywords: DLL hijacking, DLL proxying, side-loading, Windows loader, DLL search order, export forwarding, .def file, PE exports, ordinal exports, pefile, mingw-w64, cross-compilation, DllMain, CreateProcess, Sysinternals, Process Monitor, KeePassXC, version.dll, persistence, privilege escalation

entry.title: CS Situational Awareness BOF
entry.summary:
Title: CS-Situational-Awareness-BOF (Situational Awareness BOF)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository provides a collection of “situational awareness” commands implemented as Cobalt Strike Beacon Object Files (BOFs) to run host and domain reconnaissance with a smaller operational footprint than spawning full processes. - It solves the need for quick pre-op checks (host, network, AD/LDAP, services, sessions, firewall, etc.) directly inside Beacon, reducing reliance on noisy built-in commands or external binaries. - The repo also serves as a BOF development reference, including a template/workflow (Makefiles, CNA integration) for building and adding new BOFs. - Techniques include Win32 API usage, COM-based ADCS enumeration, WMI querying, remote registry access, and LDAP security configuration testing (signing/channel binding). - It’s primarily useful for Red Teams and pentesters using Cobalt Strike, and secondarily for defenders studying common post-exploitation enumeration tradecraft. - Interesting due to breadth of practical enumeration coverage (including ADCS and LDAP security checks) and its role as a canonical BOF codebase/workflow.
Technical Focus: - Cobalt Strike BOF development and CNA integration - Windows Win32 API-based enumeration (processes, modules, services, sessions) - Active Directory / LDAP querying and security configuration checks - ADCS (Certificate Services) CA/template enumeration via Win32 and COM (ICertConfig, IX509PolicyServerListManager) - Remote host enumeration (SMB sessions/shares, remote registry, scheduled tasks) - DPAPI key material retrieval (DPAPI_SYSTEM, bootkey)
Use Cases: - Pre-execution host triage (whoami, uptime, env, resources, firewall rules) - Domain recon via LDAP searches and group/user enumeration - Identify ADCS attack surface by enumerating CAs and certificate templates - Validate LDAP hardening (LDAP signing / LDAPS channel binding) on DCs - Detect potential EDR/AV presence via driver/service image path signature checks and loaded module inspection - Remote enumeration of sessions, shares, services, scheduled tasks, and registry keys

entry.title: CallStackSpoof
entry.summary:
Title: Windows x64 Call Stack Spoofing (CallStackSpoof)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository is an educational Windows x64 call stack spoofing implementation in C++ and x64 assembly that constructs synthetic stack frames to make API calls appear to originate from legitimate call chains. - It solves the common fragility of “fake stack” approaches by parsing real PE exception/unwind metadata (Exception Directory / RUNTIME_FUNCTION / UNWIND_INFO) and building unwind-consistent frames. - The project dynamically discovers and validates a safe jump gadget (e.g., `jmp qword ptr [rbx]`) by checking the gadget’s enclosing function unwind characteristics and rejecting unsafe cases (e.g., frame-pointer based functions). - An assembly trampoline pivots to the synthetic stack, invokes the target API without a normal `call`, then restores registers/stack to return cleanly. - A PoC demonstrates spoofed calls to `NtAllocateVirtualMemory`, `WriteProcessMemory`, and `CreateThread`, producing a stack that includes `RtlUserThreadStart` and `BaseThreadInitThunk`. - Useful for red teamers, malware developers, and Windows internals researchers; also relevant to blue teams studying stack-walk-based detections and their evasion limits.
Technical Focus: - Windows x64 unwind metadata parsing (IMAGE_DIRECTORY_ENTRY_EXCEPTION, RUNTIME_FUNCTION, UNWIND_INFO/UNWIND_CODE) - Synthetic stack frame construction aligned with unwinding rules - Gadget discovery and “unwind-safe” gadget validation - Assembly trampoline: stack pivot/restore, non-volatile register preservation - Spoofed API invocation with variadic argument marshaling (x64 calling convention) - Limitations around UWOP_SET_FPREG, exception-safe unwinding, CFG/CET
Use Cases: - Building PoCs for stack-walk evasion during indirect syscalls / API execution - Researching how debuggers/EDRs reconstruct and validate call stacks on x64 Windows - Creating more realistic execution traces for in-memory loaders (allocation/write/execute chains) - Testing defensive detections that rely on suspicious call stacks or thread start provenance
Keywords: Windows x64, call stack spoofing, stack pivot, synthetic frames, PE Exception Directory, IMAGE_DIRECTORY_ENTRY_EXCEPTION, RUNTIME_FUNCTION, UNWIND_INFO, UNWIND_CODE, x64 calling convention, non-volatile registers, RtlUserThreadStart, BaseThreadInitThunk, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, WriteProcessMemory, CreateThread, jump gadget, unwind validation, CFG, CET

entry.title: win shellcode dev
entry.summary:
Title: Windows x64 shellcode to load DLL from memory (win-shellcode-dev)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository provides a Windows x64 reflective DLL loader implemented as position-independent x64 assembly shellcode that loads a DLL directly from memory (no on-disk DLL required). - It demonstrates the minimal PE-loading steps needed to map an in-memory DLL: resolve required kernel exports, allocate/copy sections, fix imports, apply relocations, and call the DLL entry point. - The approach is explicitly based on Stephen Fewer’s ReflectiveDLLInjection methodology, adapted into standalone shellcode. - A Python script builds a combined payload (shellcode + embedded target.dll) into a single `shellcode.bin`, and a simple `loader.exe` executes it for testing. - Useful for red teamers, exploit developers, and malware researchers studying reflective loading mechanics and custom in-memory module mapping. - Interesting because it’s a compact, educational reference implementation of manual PE loading logic in shellcode form.
Technical Focus: - Reflective DLL injection / in-memory PE loading - Kernel32 export parsing and API resolution - Manual mapping: section copying, import resolution, relocation processing - Position-independent x64 assembly shellcode - Payload building/packing (shellcode + DLL) via Python - DLL entry point invocation (DllMain)
Use Cases: - Build and test custom reflective loaders for implants - Study PE format loading steps for offensive tooling or research - Prototype in-memory execution techniques without dropping DLLs to disk - Create a minimal lab PoC for manual mapping and import/reloc handling
Keywords: Windows x64, shellcode, reflective DLL injection, manual mapping, PE loader, in-memory DLL, kernel32 exports, export table parsing, import table, IAT resolution, base relocations, DllMain, position-independent code, x64 assembly, loader.exe, target.dll, Stephen Fewer, ReflectiveDLLInjection, Python packer

entry.title: Voidmaw
entry.summary:
Title: Voidmaw (Improved Voidgate) — Memory Scanner Bypass via On-Demand Instruction Unmasking
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Voidmaw is a Windows userland technique/toolchain to evade memory scanners and YARA-style in-memory signatures by ensuring only a minimal amount of “real” payload code is visible at any time. It consists of two components: **Dismantle** (records which instructions are executed in a payload under PAGE_GUARD/VEH control, then encrypts them and produces a header) and **Voidmaw** (executes a copy of the payload where recorded instructions are replaced with INT3 and dynamically restored on breakpoint exceptions). The approach uses vectored exception handling to trap execution (guard page violations, single-step, and breakpoint/illegal-instruction exceptions) and re-mask instructions after they run, effectively making code transient in memory. It supports multithreaded payloads without hooking thread-creation APIs and is positioned as compatible with common C2 beacons and shellcode-converted PE payloads (e.g., mimikatz via pe_to_shellcode). It’s primarily useful for red team operators, offensive tool developers, and malware researchers studying in-memory evasion and exception-driven execution. The key interest is the “record then replay with per-instruction reveal” model that reduces static in-memory exposure of known-bad code sequences.
Technical Focus: - Vectored Exception Handler (VEH)–driven execution control - PAGE_GUARD + STATUS_GUARD_PAGE_VIOLATION instrumentation - Trap Flag (TF) + EXCEPTION_SINGLE_STEP for re-arming guard pages - INT3 (0xCC) masking and EXCEPTION_BREAKPOINT handling (or illegal-instruction workaround) - Per-offset instruction mapping, encryption (XOR), and runtime patching - Multithreaded payload support without NtCreateThread hooks
Use Cases: - Generate “transient code” loaders to reduce in-memory signature exposure of beacons/reflective loaders - Execute known-flagged native payloads (e.g., mimikatz shellcode) with reduced memory-scanner visibility - Research/PoC development for exception-based unpacking and anti-memory-scanning techniques - Test and validate defensive memory-scanning/detonation logic against on-demand code restoration
Keywords: Voidmaw, Voidgate, memory scanner bypass, YARA evasion, Windows, VEH, VectoredExceptionHandler, PAGE_GUARD, STATUS_GUARD_PAGE_VIOLATION, EXCEPTION_SINGLE_STEP, Trap Flag, INT3, EXCEPTION_BREAKPOINT, runtime patching, shellcode, reflective loader, Cobalt Strike beacon, mimikatz, Zydis, Zycore

entry.title: sickle pdk
entry.summary:
Title: Sickle – Payload Development Kit (sickle-pdk)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Sickle is a modular payload development kit focused on building, analyzing, formatting, and testing shellcode/bytecode across multiple architectures and platforms. - It streamlines common shellcode workflows by converting assembly to opcodes, extracting/formatting bytecode for many target languages, and providing a runner that auto-wraps and executes payloads on Windows and Unix. - The toolkit includes linear disassembly (Capstone), interactive assembly/disassembly and shellcode generation (Keystone), bad character identification with instruction-level pinpointing, and multiple diff modes for comparing shellcode stubs/binaries. - It ships with a growing library of payload modules (e.g., reverse shells, staged stagers, reflective PE/ELF loaders, egghunters, and some Windows kernel ring-0 primitives). - Useful primarily for red teamers, exploit developers, and malware researchers who need rapid iteration and validation of shellcode and small payload stubs. - Interesting because it combines “msfvenom-like” formatting/extraction with analysis (diff/disasm) and execution tooling in a single, extensible module-based framework.
Technical Focus: - Assembly ↔ machine code conversion (Keystone) - Linear disassembly and opcode extraction (Capstone) - Shellcode execution via auto-generated wrappers (Windows/Unix) - Bad character detection and instruction attribution (“pinpoint”) - Bytecode/binary diffing (opcode + asm-level) - Modular payload/stager framework (multi-arch, multi-platform)
Use Cases: - Rapidly assemble and format shellcode for C/C#/PowerShell/JS/Python/etc. wrappers - Test shellcode locally with minimal boilerplate using the runner module - Identify which instructions introduce restricted bytes in exploit constraints - Diff and analyze changes between shellcode stubs or packed/unpacked bytecode - Generate and customize common payloads (reverse shells, stagers, reflective loaders) - Extract opcodes from raw binaries and review linear disassembly for analysis
Keywords: shellcode, payload development kit, Keystone Engine, Capstone, assembler, disassembler, opcodes, bytecode execution, bad characters, egghunter, Hell’s Gate, NtProtectVirtualMemory, VirtualAlloc, reflective PE loader, memfd, reflective ELF loader, reverse shell, stager, diffing, Windows x64, Linux aarch64

entry.title: funcshenanigans
entry.summary:
Title: FuncShenanigans
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - FuncShenanigans is a Windows-focused C research/PoC repo exploring “function fluctuation”: keeping a single function’s code encrypted/garbled in memory until execution time, then restoring it briefly. - It solves the problem of minimizing in-memory exposure of sensitive code by confining a target function to a dedicated 4KB page and toggling page protections to control access. - The repo demonstrates two main experiments: (1) using a Vectored Exception Handler (VEH) to replace a function’s body with shellcode on an access violation, and (2) encrypting/decrypting the function on-demand via VEH plus timer-queue callbacks. - Core techniques include page-aligned function bounding, `VirtualProtect` permission flips (RW/RX/NOACCESS), XOR-based transformation, and exception-driven control flow (`EXCEPTION_ACCESS_VIOLATION`). - Useful for red teamers, malware developers, and security researchers studying evasive in-memory execution patterns and Windows exception/protection mechanics. - Interesting because it applies “fluctuation” at function granularity (not whole shellcode/PE), highlighting practical constraints like page boundaries and side effects of tampering code pages.
Technical Focus: - Function/page bounding via alignment and boundary marker functions - Vectored Exception Handling (AddVectoredExceptionHandler) for execution gating - Memory protection manipulation with `VirtualProtect` (PAGE_NOACCESS, PAGE_EXECUTE_READ, PAGE_READWRITE) - Self-modifying code / runtime code replacement (`WriteProcessMemory`) - Timer-queue based re-encryption (`CreateTimerQueue`, `CreateTimerQueueTimer`) - Basic in-memory encryption/obfuscation (XOR transform)
Use Cases: - Implementing on-demand decryption of a sensitive routine to reduce static memory scanning exposure - Experimenting with exception-driven execution flow and code-page permission traps - Prototyping function-level code swapping (benign-to-payload) at runtime - Studying detection opportunities around frequent `VirtualProtect` flips, VEH registration, and timer-queue callbacks
Keywords: Windows, C, MinGW, Vectored Exception Handler, VEH, AddVectoredExceptionHandler, EXCEPTION_ACCESS_VIOLATION, VirtualProtect, PAGE_NOACCESS, PAGE_EXECUTE_READ, PAGE_READWRITE, WriteProcessMemory, VirtualQuery, CreateTimerQueue, CreateTimerQueueTimer, self-modifying code, code encryption, XOR, page alignment, memory pages, shellcode fluctuation

entry.title: lordran.polymorphic.shellcode
entry.summary:
Title: lordran.polymorphic.shellcode
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository is a small PoC demonstrating “polymorphic”/self-modifying shellcode behavior on Windows. - It generates/executes shellcode that performs normal execution, then overwrites the previously executed stub to hinder post-execution forensic recovery, and reuses the same memory region to run a second-stage shellcode blob. - The demo shows two stages via MessageBox popups (“Bonfire” then “Hello World”), illustrating staged execution and in-place code replacement. - Implementation details include controlling function placement in the PE `.text` section using GCC attributes (mingw) and handling data placement issues (avoiding `.rdata` for the shellcode buffer). - It’s primarily useful for red teamers, exploit devs, and malware researchers exploring self-overwriting stubs, staged payload execution, and memory reuse tradeoffs. - The author notes it is not OPSEC-safe and is intended as a weekend PoC rather than production-grade tradecraft.
Technical Focus: - Self-modifying / self-overwriting shellcode stubs - In-memory staged payload execution and memory region reuse - PE section layout control (`.text` placement) with GCC `__attribute__((section))` - Windows shellcode execution patterns (RWX/RX memory, function-pointer execution) - mingw toolchain quirks (`.rdata` vs `.text`/`.data`) and shellcode embedding - Simple shellcode runner script (Python)
Use Cases: - Prototype self-erasing shellcode stubs to reduce recoverable in-memory artifacts - Experiment with multi-stage shellcode where stage-2 replaces stage-1 in-place - Study PE layout/section placement effects on shellcode extraction and execution - Build test cases for EDR/forensics detection of self-modifying code behaviors
Keywords: polymorphic shellcode, self-modifying code, self-overwriting stub, staged payload, in-memory execution, PE .text section, mingw, GCC section attribute, .rdata, shellcode runner, Windows API, MessageBox, memory reuse, code cave, forensic evasion, overwrite.exe, C, Python, proof of concept
