Keywords: Rust, Windows kernel, kernel-mode driver, WDK, DriverEntry, Windows driver development, Sanctum EDR, driver signing, test signing, build pipeline, cargo, FFI, Windows internals, kernel development, EDR engineering

entry.title: sanctum edr intro
entry.summary:
Title: Intro and plan for the Sanctum EDR
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post outlines the architecture and milestone plan for “Sanctum,” a Windows AV/EDR project implemented in Rust with both a kernel driver and user-mode components. - It focuses on building an EDR that can detect/prevent common malware tradecraft (process injection variants, syscall evasion, ETW/AMSI patching, persistence, DLL sideloading) while also serving as a learning/research vehicle. - The design includes a Rust kernel driver for telemetry and enforcement (process/thread/image-load callbacks, ETW Threat Intelligence monitoring, WriteProcessMemory monitoring, potential kill/contain actions) plus an injected per-process DLL for tamper detection and user-mode syscall/hook logic. - A user-mode “engine” handles scanning (IOCs, IAT analysis, entropy/PEB/syscall heuristics), GUI (Tauri), and future SIEM export and cloud IOC updates. - It’s useful for defenders and EDR engineers building telemetry/enforcement pipelines, and for red teamers/malware researchers to understand what an EDR can realistically observe and how detections might be implemented. - Interesting points include committing to Rust for Windows driver development, explicit mapping of offensive techniques to defensive telemetry, and a concrete callback/ETW-centric kernel plan.
Technical Focus: - Windows kernel driver development in Rust (WDK crates, no_std/libcore, unsafe boundaries) - Kernel callbacks: PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutineEx, PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine, PsSetCreateThreadNotifyRoutine - ETW Threat Intelligence monitoring and anti-ETW/AMSI patching strategies - Process injection detection signals (WriteProcessMemory, remote threads/APCs, suspicious stacks) - User-mode scanning heuristics (IOCs, IAT analysis, entropy, PEB access, direct syscalls) - Driver ↔ user-mode communication and telemetry/enforcement architecture
Use Cases: - Build a learning/reference EDR architecture with kernel + user-mode components - Prototype detections for injection, tampering, and persistence techniques on Windows - Collect process/thread/image-load telemetry and correlate it in a user-mode service - Implement anti-tamper checks for ETW/AMSI patching and NTDLL remapping - Develop an AV-style scanner with IOC feeds, disk scans, and optional sandboxing
Keywords: Sanctum EDR, Rust, Windows kernel driver, WDK crates, no_std, IOCTL, user-mode service, PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutineEx, PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine, PsSetCreateThreadNotifyRoutine, ETW Threat Intelligence, AMSI patching, ETW patching, WriteProcessMemory, DLL injection, reflective DLL injection, APC queue hijacking, Hell’s Gate, direct syscalls, NTDLL remapping, DLL sideloading, persistence detection, Tauri GUI

entry.title: sanctum
entry.summary:
Title: Sanctum EDR (Rust Kernel Driver EDR PoC)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Sanctum is an experimental proof-of-concept Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR) stack for Windows 11, implemented primarily in Rust with a kernel driver plus user-mode components. - It focuses on detecting “modern malware techniques” beyond traditional AV by monitoring low-level behavior such as syscall activity, NTDLL tampering, and kernel/rootkit manipulation. - The project explores kernel-side syscall interception via “Alt Syscalls,” ETW Threat Intelligence telemetry (partially deprecated/transitioning), and “Ghost Hunting” style detection for suspicious API/syscall patterns. - It includes supporting infrastructure like a GUI (Tauri), a user-mode engine that communicates with the driver, and legacy process-wide DLL injection/hooking components (now largely replaced by kernel hooking). - Deployment is intentionally VM-only due to kernel tampering risk and involves driver build/signing, optional ELAM/PPL service components, and specific file placement (e.g., sanctum.dll in System32). - Useful for defensive engineers, malware analysts, and red teamers researching EDR internals, Windows kernel telemetry, and how detection can be implemented (and potentially evaded).
Technical Focus: - Windows kernel driver EDR architecture (Rust, windows-drivers-rs toolchain) - Kernel-side syscall interception (“Alt Syscalls”) and its constraints under HVCI/VBS - ETW Threat Intelligence provider consumption (legacy/deprecated path) - Detection of NTDLL/user-mode tampering and syscall-hook evasion signals (“Ghost Hunting”) - File-system minifilter for ransomware-style file write detection - Protected Process Light (PPL) / ELAM-related service deployment and signing workflow
Use Cases: - Build a lab EDR to study Windows 11 kernel telemetry and detection engineering - Research syscall interception approaches and their interaction with HVCI/PatchGuard/HyperGuard - Prototype detections for injection, NTDLL patching, and rootkit-style kernel tampering - Develop and test ransomware-like file activity detection via minifilter signals - Red team validation of EDR detection surfaces and potential bypass strategies (in a VM)
Keywords: Rust, Windows 11, EDR, kernel driver, syscall interception, Alt Syscalls, PspServiceDescriptorGroupTable, HVCI, VBS, PatchGuard, HyperGuard, ETW, Threat Intelligence provider, PPL, ELAM, NTDLL tampering, Ghost Hunting, filesystem minifilter, IOCTL, Tauri GUI

entry.title: SylantStrike
entry.summary:
Title: SylantStrike – Simple EDR implementation to demonstrate bypass
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): SylantStrike is a minimal, educational EDR-like implementation created as part of a two-part blog series on building and then bypassing an EDR. The repository provides a reference design for basic endpoint monitoring/detection logic and a companion injector component (SylantStrikeInject) to exercise or evade those detections. It focuses on demonstrating common EDR building blocks and the practical weaknesses attackers can leverage when detections are simplistic or rely on easily-hooked userland telemetry. The codebase (primarily C and C#) is intended for learning and experimentation rather than production defense. It is useful for red teamers and malware researchers studying evasion, and for blue teamers wanting to understand how naive EDR approaches fail. The associated blog post provides the conceptual walkthrough and rationale behind the implementation.
Technical Focus: - Userland EDR design patterns and telemetry collection - API hooking / monitoring of suspicious Windows behaviors - Process injection concepts (via companion injector project) - Detection logic limitations and bypass strategies - Windows internals relevant to endpoint monitoring
Use Cases: - Build a lab-grade “toy EDR” to understand common detection approaches - Develop and test EDR bypass/evasion techniques against simplistic monitoring - Teach defenders how attacker tradecraft targets usermode hooks and weak telemetry - Create proof-of-concept injection activity to validate detection coverage
Keywords: EDR, endpoint monitoring, Windows internals, userland hooks, API hooking, process injection, telemetry, detection engineering, evasion, bypass, C#, C, Windows API, Sysmon-like concepts, red team, blue team, proof of concept, injector, EthicalChaos blog

entry.title: openedr
entry.summary:
Title: OpenEDR (ComodoSecurity/openedr)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - OpenEDR is an open-source Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) agent and telemetry pipeline intended to provide deep endpoint visibility and detection at “base security event” granularity. - It solves the problem of collecting and correlating endpoint telemetry (process, file, registry, and network activity) to support investigation, root-cause analysis, and alerting. - The repo describes a multi-component architecture including a Windows service, kernel-mode monitoring (minifilter and callback-based process/registry monitoring), network filtering, and user-mode API hooking via injected DLLs. - Telemetry is stored locally and forwarded to self-hosted or cloud ElasticSearch/ELK stacks (with setup docs for Elasticsearch, Kibana, Logstash, and Filebeat). - It’s useful for Blue Teams/SOC engineers building detections and for security researchers/red teamers who want to study EDR collection methods, coverage, and potential blind spots. - It’s interesting because it exposes a relatively complete EDR implementation (user-mode + kernel-mode sensors plus self-protection) that can be audited, extended, and used for defensive engineering.
Technical Focus: - Windows kernel telemetry collection (minifilter, system callbacks) - User-mode API hooking via injected DLL + driver-assisted loading - Process hierarchy tracking and event correlation - Network activity monitoring via filtering components - ELK/ElasticSearch-based telemetry ingestion and visualization - Self-protection mechanisms for agent integrity
Use Cases: - Deploy an open EDR sensor feeding ElasticSearch/Kibana for endpoint visibility - Build and test detection/alerting policies on rich endpoint telemetry - Research EDR sensor design (kernel callbacks, minifilters, injection-based hooks) - Validate attacker TTP visibility gaps (process, file, registry, network) in lab environments - Create custom integrations/dashboards around OpenEDR event schemas
Keywords: OpenEDR, EDR, Windows internals, kernel driver, file-system minifilter, PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutine, registry callbacks, API hooking, DLL injection, process monitoring, process tree, telemetry, self-protection, network filter, ElasticSearch, ELK, Kibana, Logstash, Filebeat, MITRE ATT&CK

entry.title: BamExtensionTableHook
entry.summary:
Title: BamExtensionTableHook
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This repository provides a proof-of-concept Windows kernel driver that hooks the kernel “Extension Table” callback mechanism used by `bam.sys` (Background Activity Moderator) to receive process create/exit notifications. It addresses a common evasion technique where attackers with kernel read/write zero out entries in `nt!PspCreateProcessNotifyRoutine` to disable standard `PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutine(Ex|Ex2)` callbacks used by security products. The PoC locates `nt!PspBamExtensionHost` and overwrites the pointer to `bam!BampCreateProcessCallback`, redirecting execution to a custom callback so process monitoring persists even if the standard notify array is cleared. It highlights that PatchGuard does not appear to monitor this extension-table modification (per the author’s observation), making it a potentially stealthy persistence/visibility mechanism. It’s useful for kernel security researchers, EDR/AV engineers, and red teamers studying callback tampering and alternative kernel notification paths. It also notes a “complete disable” option for attackers: writing `0` to `nt!PspNotifyEnableMask` to disable all process notifications including extension-table ones.
Technical Focus: - Windows process notify callbacks (`PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutine(Ex|Ex2)`, `nt!PspCreateProcessNotifyRoutine`) - Kernel Extension Table mechanism (`nt!ExRegisterExtension`, extension hosts) - Hooking `nt!PspBamExtensionHost` / `bam!BampCreateProcessCallback` - Kernel memory tampering / DKOM-style pointer replacement - Callback evasion and resilience against callback clearing - PatchGuard coverage gaps (as observed)
Use Cases: - Build resilient kernel process-monitoring callbacks that survive standard callback array wiping - Research/validate attacker techniques for disabling EDR kernel callbacks and corresponding mitigations - Develop detections for extension-table callback tampering (integrity checks on `PspBamExtensionHost`) - Red-team simulation of advanced kernel-level telemetry suppression and defender countermeasures
Keywords: Windows kernel, kernel driver, process notify callback, PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutine, PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutineEx, PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutineEx2, PspCreateProcessNotifyRoutine, PspCallProcessNotifyRoutines, Extension Table, ExRegisterExtension, PspBamExtensionHost, bam.sys, BampCreateProcessCallback, PspNotifyEnableMask, DKOM, kernel R/W primitive, callback tampering, PatchGuard, Windows 11 24H2

entry.title: CrimsonEDR
entry.summary:
Title: CrimsonEDR
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): CrimsonEDR is an open-source Windows DLL + controller panel that simulates common AV/EDR detections to help malware developers and red teamers test evasion techniques in a controlled way. It focuses on identifying behavioral patterns such as direct syscalls, NTDLL unhooking, AMSI/ETW patching, reflective PE loading, PE stomping, and suspicious thread origins/start addresses from unbacked memory. The project also demonstrates user-mode API monitoring by hooking `NtWriteVirtualMemory` to observe memory modifications associated with injection. It supports custom byte-pattern/IOC matching via a JSON file (default patterns include msfvenom-related sequences). Usage involves injecting the CrimsonEDR DLL into a target process using `CrimsonEDRPanel.exe` with a PID and DLL path. It’s useful for red team operators, malware dev training, and defenders who want a lightweight lab tool to understand what common EDR heuristics look for.
Technical Focus: - Direct syscall detection and syscall-evasion heuristics - NTDLL unhooking detection - AMSI patch detection (byte-level integrity checks) - ETW patch detection (byte-level integrity checks) - Reflective PE loading / in-memory execution detection - API hooking of `NtWriteVirtualMemory` and memory-modification monitoring
Use Cases: - Validate whether a loader/injector triggers common EDR-style heuristics - Train on AMSI/ETW bypass detection and counter-detection concepts in a lab - Test reflective loaders, PE stomping, and unbacked thread techniques against simulated telemetry - Build and iterate custom IOC byte-pattern signatures via `ioc.json` - Demonstrate user-mode hooking and process injection mechanics for education
Keywords: Windows EDR simulation, AV heuristics, direct syscalls, NTDLL unhooking, AMSI, ETW, patch detection, byte signature, IOC JSON, reflective PE loading, PE stomping, unbacked memory, thread start address, code injection, NtWriteVirtualMemory, API hooking, DLL injection, msfvenom, Windows internals, malware development training

entry.title: CobaltSentry
entry.summary:
Title: Cobalt Sentry (CobaltSentry)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): CobaltSentry is a Windows process memory scanner written in Go aimed at detecting Cobalt Strike beacons and related stealth techniques in live processes. It targets common in-memory tradecraft such as SleepMask (high-entropy regions), XOR-masked payloads/configs, Userland Reflective Loader (UDRL) artifacts, and API redirection/proxying patterns associated with “BeaconGate.” It also attempts to identify syscall evasion patterns like Hell’s Gate and Heaven’s Gate. The tool can scan all processes or a specific PID and supports multi-threaded scanning for speed, requiring admin rights to read process memory. It’s primarily useful for Blue Teams/DFIR and threat hunters validating beacon presence, but also valuable for red teams to test OPSEC and measure detectability of in-memory implants.
Technical Focus: - Windows process memory enumeration and scanning - Cobalt Strike beacon artifact detection (SleepMask, config patterns) - Entropy-based detection of encrypted/masked regions - XOR decoding / masked payload identification - UDRL / reflective loading indicators (PE header anomalies) - Syscall evasion detection (Hell’s Gate / Heaven’s Gate) and API redirection
Use Cases: - Hunt for Cobalt Strike beacons across endpoints by scanning running processes - Triage a suspicious PID to identify in-memory loader/beacon artifacts - Validate whether SleepMask/XOR masking is leaving detectable memory patterns - Detect potential syscall-stomping/gating techniques used to evade EDR telemetry - Support incident response by quickly flagging likely beacon-infected processes
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, beacon, SleepMask, entropy scanning, XOR mask, beacon configuration, UDRL, Userland Reflective Loader, reflective loading, process memory scanning, Windows, PID scanning, API redirection, BeaconGate, Hell’s Gate, Heaven’s Gate, syscalls, evasion, Go, DFIR

entry.title: BestEdrOfTheMarket
entry.summary:
Title: Best EDR Of The Market (BEOTM) V3
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - BEOTM is an open-source Windows EDR “lab” that implements common endpoint detection techniques to help understand how commercial EDRs work and how attackers try to bypass them. - Version 3 focuses on kernel-mode telemetry and interception, providing a driver (`beotm.sys`) plus a userland controller (`beotm.exe`) that also loads and compiles YARA rules (via a vendored Elastic protection-artifacts ruleset). - It demonstrates detection approaches such as kernel callbacks for process/thread/image/registry/object events, syscall interception via alternative syscall handlers, VAD-based image integrity checks, and call stack integrity validation (including Shadow Stack usage). - The project maps these detections to real-world TTPs like PPID spoofing, credential dumping, process hollowing/ghosting/tampering, APC/thread-hijack injection, abnormal syscalls, and registry persistence. - It’s primarily useful for EDR engineers, defenders, malware analysts, and red teamers who want a controlled environment to study detection surfaces and evasion constraints at low level. - Notable operational detail: it requires a test VM with Windows in TESTSIGNING mode and is built with VS2022 + WDK (C++20).
Technical Focus: - Windows kernel driver development (WDK) for EDR-style telemetry - Kernel callbacks (PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutineEx, PsSetCreateThreadNotifyRoutine, PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine, ObRegisterCallbacks, CmRegisterCallbackEx) - System call interception via alternative system call handlers + syscall integrity checking - VAD tree inspection for image/in-memory integrity validation - Thread call stack integrity checks (including Shadow Stack) - YARA-based scanning of memory buffers/files (Elastic protection-artifacts rules)
Use Cases: - Build and test kernel-mode detections for common Windows attack techniques - Validate how specific injection/persistence TTPs appear in kernel telemetry - Experiment with syscall monitoring/interception and integrity verification concepts - Develop and tune YARA rules against in-memory/file artifacts in a lab EDR pipeline - Reproduce and debug false positives/BSODs in a controlled VM with WinDbg
Keywords: Windows kernel, EDR, kernel driver, WDK, Visual Studio 2022, TESTSIGNING, PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutineEx, PsSetCreateThreadNotifyRoutine, PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine, ObRegisterCallbacks, CmRegisterCallbackEx, system call interception, alternative syscall handler, syscall integrity, VAD tree, Shadow Stack, call stack validation, code injection detection, YARA, Elastic protection-artifacts, PPID spoofing, process hollowing, APC injection, thread hijacking, credential dumping, registry persistence, WinDbg, NDIS, fwpkclnt.lib

entry.title: InjectAll
entry.summary:
Title: InjectAll — Coding a Windows Kernel Driver to Inject a DLL into All Running Processes
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): InjectAll is a tutorial-style source repository demonstrating how to build a Windows kernel-mode driver that injects a custom DLL into all running processes. It includes three components: a kernel driver, an injected DLL, and a user-mode C++ test console, implemented with C++ plus x86/x64 assembly in Visual Studio. The project focuses on end-to-end implementation details for system-wide DLL injection from kernel context rather than providing a polished “tool.” It’s primarily useful for red teamers, malware researchers, and Windows internals learners who want to understand kernel-assisted code injection mechanics and the engineering required to deploy it. The accompanying blog post and long-form YouTube playlist provide the step-by-step build and coding walkthrough. It’s interesting because it bridges user-mode injection concepts with kernel driver development and cross-architecture (x86/x64) considerations.
Technical Focus: - Windows kernel-mode driver development (WDK) and user-mode coordination - System-wide DLL injection mechanics across processes - x86/x64 assembly integration in injection stubs/loader logic - Windows process/thread internals relevant to injection - Visual Studio build pipeline for mixed driver + user-mode projects
Use Cases: - Building a lab-grade kernel-assisted DLL injection PoC - Learning Windows driver development patterns for process manipulation - Developing/understanding offensive tradecraft around global user-mode instrumentation - Creating test harnesses for EDR/self-defense research in controlled environments
Keywords: Windows kernel driver, WDK, Windows 10 SDK, Visual Studio 2019, DLL injection, kernel-mode injection, process injection, x86, x64, assembly, C++, user-mode loader, driver development, Windows internals, system-wide injection, test console, PoC, rbmm INJECT, low-level programming

entry.title: API To ETW
entry.summary:
Title: API-To-ETW (DumpEtwWrites Ghidra script)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository provides a Ghidra Java script that reverse-engineers a PE file to map Windows APIs/functions to the ETW events they emit. - It extracts ETW write metadata (e.g., EVENT_DESCRIPTOR symbols/IDs and call paths) and leverages public symbols (PDBs) to improve accuracy, addressing the lack of documentation for many ETW providers (notably kernel providers like `Kernel-Audit-API-Calls` where events appear as `task_nn`). - The tool automates what was previously a manual reversing workflow by scanning decompiled code for ETW registration/write patterns and outputting results suitable for grep/analysis (example CSV for `ntoskrnl.exe`). - It also discusses undocumented-by-design tracing ecosystems such as TraceLogging and WPP, highlighting that these can still be valuable for security telemetry. - Useful for detection engineers and blue teams building ETW-based analytics, and for red teams/malware analysts who want to understand what actions generate which ETW signals. - Interesting because it bridges Windows internals reversing with practical telemetry engineering, enabling faster discovery of high-signal ETW events tied to sensitive APIs.
Technical Focus: - ETW internals: providers, EVENT_DESCRIPTORs, event IDs, metadata extraction - Ghidra decompiler scripting (Java) and p-code/decompilation-driven analysis - Windows kernel/user-mode PE analysis with PDB symbol loading - Mapping API call graphs to ETW write sites (call-path reconstruction) - TraceLogging and WPP provider discovery (undocumented telemetry) - Handling edge cases in provider handle storage (e.g., GenericTableAvl/AVL patterns)
Use Cases: - Build a mapping of Windows kernel syscalls/APIs to ETW events for detection content - Identify which ETW providers/events fire for specific sensitive operations (process/thread, image load, ALPC, symlinks) - Triage undocumented `task_nn` ETW events by resolving them to concrete APIs - Reverse-engineer telemetry coverage of a target binary (kernel or user-mode) to inform evasion or monitoring - Generate reference CSVs for threat hunting and SIEM/EDR ETW pipelines
Keywords: ETW, Event Tracing for Windows, Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-Audit-API-Calls, EVENT_DESCRIPTOR, TraceLogging, WPP, Ghidra, decompiler, p-code, PDB symbols, ntoskrnl.exe, PE analysis, syscall, call graph, PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine, NtOpenProcess, NtTerminateProcess, ALPC, Windows internals, telemetry engineering

entry.title: Etw SyscallMonitor
entry.summary:
Title: Etw-SyscallMonitor — Hunting hidden shellcode via syscall summaries
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Etw-SyscallMonitor is a C# proof-of-concept that uses kernel ETW telemetry to build per-process “syscall summaries” (a set of security-relevant syscalls and selected parameters) as a runtime analogue to static import/capability profiling. The goal is to detect hidden shellcode or injected code by spotting anomalous syscall sets for a process, even when user-mode anti-analysis techniques are used. It periodically writes a whole-system `SyscallSummary.json` plus per-process profiles keyed by executable and a startup hash. The approach emphasizes robustness (malware must invoke syscalls to do work) but trades off event-level granularity, so it’s better for behavioral fingerprinting than precise timelines. The repo notes it works best on Windows 10 and uses BYOVD to enable PPL in order to collect `Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence` ETW events. It’s useful for threat hunters/blue teams building detections and for red teams/malware researchers evaluating syscall-level footprints and detection surfaces.
Technical Focus: - Windows ETW collection (kernel + Threat Intelligence provider) - Syscall set/profiling per process (behavioral fingerprinting) - Detection of injected/hidden shellcode via syscall anomalies - PPL enablement and telemetry access (BYOVD-assisted) - Periodic JSON export of system and per-process summaries
Use Cases: - Hunt for process injection/shellcode by comparing syscall profiles against baselines - Build lightweight behavioral fingerprints for executables without full tracing - Validate whether evasive payloads still expose distinctive syscall patterns - Generate artifacts (`SyscallSummary.json`, per-process JSON) for offline analysis/correlation
Keywords: ETW, Event Tracing for Windows, syscalls, Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence, kernel telemetry, syscall profiling, behavioral fingerprinting, shellcode detection, process injection, anomaly detection, PPL, Protected Process Light, BYOVD, Windows 10, C#, JSON telemetry, threat hunting, Black Hat Asia 2023, runtime capability analysis

entry.title: EtwTi FluctuationMonitor
entry.summary:
Title: EtwTi-FluctuationMonitor — Detecting hidden shellcode via ETW Threat-Intelligence PROTECTVM_LOCAL fluctuations
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository provides a Windows monitoring tool that uses Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence ETW events to detect “hidden shellcode” techniques that repeatedly toggle memory protections to evade scanners. - It focuses on violations of the “immutable code page” principle: executable pages should generally transition RW→RX once, not repeatedly flip between executable/non-executable or writable/non-writable states. - The tool consumes Threat-Intelligence PROTECTVM_LOCAL events to observe VirtualProtect-style protection changes and flags suspicious regions when the same allocation’s protections fluctuate more than once. - It explicitly accounts for false positives from JIT engines (memory reuse) and legitimate API hooking by using frequency-based heuristics rather than single-event alerting. - This is useful for defenders/EDR engineers and threat hunters building telemetry-based detections, and for red teams to understand what ETW TI can reveal about memory-hiding tradecraft. - The approach is tied to the Black Hat Asia 2023 talk “You Can Run, but You Can’t Hide - Finding the Footprints of Hidden Shellcode.”
Technical Focus: - ETW Threat-Intelligence provider (Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence) - PROTECTVM_LOCAL events and memory protection telemetry - Detecting shellcode hiding via VirtualProtect/NtProtectVirtualMemory flipping - Immutable code page principle (RW→RX) and violation detection - False-positive reduction via frequency/behavioral heuristics - Windows memory protections (RW/RX, W^X concepts) and private executable memory (JIT)
Use Cases: - Detect in-memory payloads that “hide” by repeatedly changing page protections - Build/validate EDR detections around ETW TI PROTECTVM_LOCAL telemetry - Threat hunting for suspicious executable memory behavior in user-mode processes - Researching evasion techniques that attempt to defeat periodic memory scanning - Baseline and tune JIT/hooking-related protection changes to reduce noise
Keywords: ETW, Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence, PROTECTVM_LOCAL, VirtualProtect, NtProtectVirtualMemory, memory protection flipping, hidden shellcode, in-memory evasion, executable pages, RWX, RW-to-RX, W^X, JIT, API hooking, behavioral detection, Windows internals, threat hunting, EDR telemetry, Black Hat Asia 2023

entry.title: Windows10EtwEvents
entry.summary:
Title: Windows 10 ETW Events
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository is a curated dataset of Event Tracing for Windows (ETW) event metadata collected from all manifest-based and MOF-based ETW providers across multiple Windows 10 releases (and includes Windows 11 21H2 updates). - It addresses the practical problem of enumerating and tracking ETW provider/event coverage and changes between OS versions, which is otherwise scattered across manifests, MOF classes, and undocumented/unknown providers. - The repo organizes provider definitions into `manifest/`, `mof/`, and `unknown/` categories and provides counts per Windows version (events and provider totals), enabling version-to-version diffing and telemetry planning. - It’s useful for Blue Teams building detections from ETW, Red Teams assessing telemetry exposure and potential ETW-based monitoring, and researchers doing Windows internals/telemetry analysis. - It’s interesting because it provides broad, versioned visibility into ETW’s available event surface area, which is foundational for both detection engineering and understanding what security products can observe.
Technical Focus: - ETW provider enumeration (manifest-based vs MOF-based) - Event metadata extraction and cataloging across Windows versions - Provider/event surface comparison (version diffs) - Windows telemetry and instrumentation (WPP/TraceLogging context via references) - ETW tooling ecosystem (PerfView, KrabsETW, SilkETW references)
Use Cases: - Build/validate ETW-based detection logic and data dictionaries - Identify which ETW providers/events exist on specific Windows 10 builds - Compare telemetry changes across Windows versions for regression testing - Support threat hunting by mapping suspicious behaviors to ETW event sources - Red team recon on likely host telemetry sources used by EDR/SIEM pipelines
Keywords: ETW, Event Tracing for Windows, manifest-based providers, MOF providers, event metadata, Windows 10 versions, Windows 11 21H2, provider GUID, WPP, TraceLogging, telemetry, PerfView, KrabsETW, SilkETW, OSSEM, detection engineering, Windows internals, event catalog, provider enumeration

entry.title: JonMon
entry.summary:
Title: JonMon v2.0
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - JonMon is a Windows telemetry research project that implements multiple open-source “sensors” to capture system activity for testing and verification. - It combines a kernel-mode driver (for low-level events like process creation, registry operations, and file create activity) with user-mode collection (covering .NET, RPC, network activity, and other events). - Collected telemetry is surfaced through the Windows Event Log to make querying and analysis straightforward. - The project is explicitly positioned as non-production research code intended to help understand and validate telemetry mechanisms (similar to how EDRs gather signals). - It’s useful for defenders and detection engineers validating event coverage, and for red teamers/pentesters who want to understand what actions generate which telemetry. - The repo includes configuration (JSON) and a wiki with installation guidance and event mapping.
Technical Focus: - Windows kernel driver telemetry collection (minifilter-style file/registry/process monitoring) - User-mode instrumentation for .NET, RPC, and network events - Windows Event Log as a telemetry output pipeline - Telemetry validation / event-to-activity mapping - Config-driven sensor behavior (JSON configuration)
Use Cases: - Build a lab telemetry sensor to understand what Windows actions generate which events - Validate detection logic by generating and observing ground-truth event streams - Compare kernel vs user-mode visibility gaps for specific attacker behaviors - Prototype custom telemetry sources before implementing production-grade sensors - Create event mappings for threat hunting and detection engineering
Keywords: Windows telemetry, kernel driver, minifilter, FltGetRequestorProcessId, process creation, registry monitoring, file create, user-mode sensor, .NET telemetry, RPC monitoring, network telemetry, Windows Event Log, event mapping, detection engineering, threat hunting, Sysmon alternative, ETW (conceptual), JSON configuration, Windows internals, security research

entry.title: Microsoft Windows Threat Intelligence
entry.summary:
Title: Windows APIs To Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence events (TelemetrySource)
Type: GitHub Tool / Documentation
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository section documents and maps Windows API/NT operations to ETW events emitted by the `Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence` provider. - It focuses on identifying which user-mode and kernel-mode actions trigger specific Threat Intelligence telemetry, especially around process injection primitives and memory manipulation. - Covered event types include remote/local virtual memory allocation/protection/mapping, cross-process read/write, APC queuing, thread context changes, process/thread suspend/resume/freeze/thaw, and driver/device object load/unload. - The author frames the mapping in terms of “operational functions” that perform the action, undocumented “event processing functions” that initiate auditing, and the final emission via `nt!EtwWrite`. - Useful for red teams and malware researchers to understand what behaviors are likely to generate TI ETW signals, and for blue teams to build detections tied to concrete API call patterns. - Includes a public Google Sheet intended as a living mapping reference.
Technical Focus: - ETW provider `Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence` event taxonomy - API-to-telemetry mapping for injection/memory-tampering primitives - Undocumented event processing/auditing functions in Windows - `nt!EtwWrite` event emission path - Kernel-caller vs user-mode caller distinctions for TI events - Driver/device object load/unload telemetry
Use Cases: - Build or validate detections for process injection and memory tampering based on TI ETW events - Assess which injection techniques are most likely to trigger Threat Intelligence telemetry - Correlate suspicious API sequences (e.g., alloc/protect/write + APC/context) to specific TI event IDs - Research Windows internal auditing hooks and ETW emission paths - Create lab tests to reproduce and baseline TI provider events across Windows versions
Keywords: ETW, Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence, ThreatInt, nt!EtwWrite, process injection, VirtualAllocEx, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, NtProtectVirtualMemory, NtMapViewOfSection, ReadProcessMemory, WriteProcessMemory, QueueUserAPC, SetThreadContext, thread suspend, process freeze, kernel caller, driver load, device object, Windows internals

entry.title: Event Mapping
entry.summary:
Title: JonMon Wiki – Event Mapping
Type: Documentation
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This page documents how JonMon maps its internal EventIds to specific Windows telemetry sources and kernel/user-mode collection mechanisms. - It solves the “what generated this event?” problem by explicitly tying each event type (process/thread/image, handle access, registry, file/pipe/mailslot, .NET, WMI, RPC, DPAPI, network, AMSI, and TI injection signals) to the underlying callback/ETW provider used. - Techniques covered include kernel notify routines (process/thread/image), Object Manager callbacks for handle operations, registry callbacks, minifilter IRP monitoring, and multiple ETW providers (DotNETRuntime, RPC, DPAPI, Kernel-Network, AMSI, Threat-Intelligence). - It’s useful for defenders building detections, red teamers validating what activity is observable, and researchers comparing EDR-like telemetry coverage. - The inclusion of RPC interface-focused collection (via MSRPC-to-ATTACK) and ETW Threat-Intelligence events for common injection primitives highlights a focus on high-signal behavioral telemetry.
Technical Focus: - Kernel callbacks: PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutine(Ex), PsSetCreateThreadNotifyRoutine, PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine - Object Manager handle telemetry: ObRegisterCallbacks (handle create/duplicate) - Registry monitoring: CmRegisterCallbackEx - File system minifilter callbacks: FltRegisterCallback (IRP_MJ_CREATE, named pipe/mailslot) - ETW providers: DotNETRuntime, WMI-Activity, RPC, Crypto-DPAPI, Kernel-Network, AMSI, Threat-Intelligence - Injection/abuse signals: Read/WriteProcessMemory, VirtualAlloc, QueueUserAPC, token impersonation heuristics
Use Cases: - Validate which attacker behaviors will be logged by JonMon (process injection, handle abuse, registry persistence, WMI persistence, RPC activity). - Build or tune detections by mapping alerts to the exact Windows callback/ETW source. - Compare JonMon telemetry coverage against Sysmon/EDR data sources for gaps and overlaps. - Research RPC-based tradecraft by focusing on monitored interfaces (MSRPC-to-ATTACK alignment). - Investigate credential/data access via DPAPI (CryptUnprotectData) and AMSI scanning visibility.
Keywords: JonMon, EventId mapping, ETW, PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutineEx, PsSetCreateThreadNotifyRoutine, PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine, ObRegisterCallbacks, CmRegisterCallbackEx, FltRegisterCallback, IRP_MJ_CREATE, named pipes, mailslots, WMI permanent subscription, Microsoft-Windows-RPC, MSRPC-to-ATTACK, Microsoft-Windows-Crypto-DPAPI, CryptUnprotectData, Microsoft-Antimalware-Scan-Interface, Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence, QueueUserAPC, ReadProcessMemory, WriteProcessMemory, VirtualAlloc, token impersonation

entry.title: TelemetrySource
entry.summary:
Title: TelemetrySource
Type: GitHub Tool (research/dataset)
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - TelemetrySource is a mapping project that links Windows telemetry events back to the specific functions/APIs responsible for generating them. - It focuses on understanding how common defensive data sources (Sysmon, Windows Security Auditing events, and Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence ETW) are triggered at the code/API level. - The repo provides per-source documentation plus visual overviews, and references a central Google Sheet used as the main event-to-function mapping index. - The methodology is documented via a series of blog posts describing how the author traces events “down to APIs.” - It’s useful for defenders building higher-fidelity detections and for red teams/malware researchers who want to understand what behaviors generate which logs (and where evasion might be possible). - The key value is making telemetry provenance explicit, enabling more informed detection engineering and validation.
Technical Focus: - Windows event generation provenance (event → API/function mapping) - Sysmon event triggers and underlying Windows APIs - Microsoft-Windows-Security-Auditing event sources - ETW providers, specifically Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence - Telemetry research methodology and reverse mapping workflows
Use Cases: - Validate which Windows APIs/behaviors will trigger specific Sysmon or Security log events - Build or tune detections based on known event-generation paths - Identify logging blind spots and potential evasion opportunities for adversary simulation - Create test cases to reproduce specific telemetry for purple-team exercises - Improve incident response triage by understanding event origin and prerequisites
Keywords: Windows telemetry, Sysmon, Microsoft-Windows-Security-Auditing, Windows Security Event Log, ETW, Event Tracing for Windows, Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence, event provider, API mapping, Windows internals, detection engineering, log provenance, telemetry generation, blue team, red team, threat hunting, Windows APIs, auditing events, Sysmon events, telemetry research

entry.title: SilkETW
entry.summary:
Title: SilkETW & SilkService
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): SilkETW and SilkService are C#/.NET wrappers around Windows Event Tracing for Windows (ETW) designed to simplify collecting and consuming ETW telemetry for research and introspection. They abstract ETW session/provider complexity and emit normalized JSON that can be written to disk, Windows Event Log, or shipped to external systems (e.g., Elasticsearch/HELK). SilkService runs SilkETW “headless” as a Windows service and supports multiple concurrent collectors via an XML configuration (user and kernel sessions, provider names/guids, keywords, output targets). The project also integrates YARA scanning to tag/filter collected event data, enabling detection of behaviors like in-memory .NET execution (e.g., execute-assembly) based on ETW payloads. It’s useful to both Red Teams (telemetry research, understanding detection surfaces) and Blue Teams (threat hunting, custom telemetry pipelines). It’s interesting because it operationalizes “free” high-fidelity Windows telemetry with flexible output and automation, while remaining relatively lightweight to deploy.
Technical Focus: - Windows ETW user vs kernel trace sessions (providers, keywords) - C#/.NET ETW consumption via Microsoft.Diagnostics.Tracing.TraceEvent - JSON event normalization/serialization for downstream analytics - Windows service-based continuous collection (SilkService) with XML config - Output sinks: file, Windows Event Log, HTTP/URL to ELK-style endpoints - YARA-based tagging/filtering of ETW event content (notably .NET runtime events)
Use Cases: - Threat hunting pipelines: collect ETW and forward to ELK/HELK for analytics - Researching Windows telemetry coverage for specific attacker behaviors/tools - Detecting suspicious .NET activity (JIT/Loader/Interop) and in-memory execution via YARA on ETW - Collecting kernel process/image-load telemetry to identify tool execution patterns - Building/validating detections based on specific ETW providers and event fields - Running multi-provider ETW collection at scale using a configurable Windows service
Keywords: ETW, Event Tracing for Windows, SilkETW, SilkService, C#, .NET Framework 4.5, Microsoft.Diagnostics.Tracing.TraceEvent, PerfView, kernel trace session, user trace session, ETW provider, provider GUID, keywords, Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-Process, Microsoft-Windows-DotNETRuntime, Microsoft-Windows-DNS-Client, JSON telemetry, Windows Event Log, Elasticsearch, HELK, YARA, logman, PowerShell, execute-assembly, Cobalt Strike, Mimikatz, Seatbelt

entry.title: krabsetw
entry.summary:
Title: microsoft/krabsetw (KrabsETW)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - KrabsETW is a modern C++ library that wraps low-level Windows Event Tracing for Windows (ETW) trace consumption APIs to simplify enabling providers, managing traces, and receiving event callbacks. - It addresses the complexity of directly using ETW consumer functions by providing higher-level abstractions for sessions, providers, and event handling, plus helpers to parse generic ETW payloads into strongly typed fields. - The repo also includes a C++/CLI-based .NET wrapper (Microsoft.O365.Security.Native.ETW, “Lobsters”) used in production by the Office 365 Security team, enabling ETW consumption from .NET applications. - Documentation includes an ETW primer and examples for both native and managed usage, including guidance on discovering ETW sources. - Useful for Blue Teams building telemetry/detections, EDR/security engineers, and Red Teams needing custom ETW-based visibility or research tooling. - Interesting because it provides a practical, production-backed ETW consumer framework with both native and .NET support (x64/ARM64) and NuGet distribution.
Technical Focus: - ETW trace consumption (user-mode consumers) - Provider/session management and event callback pipelines - Parsing EVENT_RECORD payloads into typed schemas - C++ wrapper abstractions over Windows ETW APIs - C++/CLI and .NET (NuGet) interop for ETW - Windows x64/ARM64 support considerations and threading/blocking trace start
Use Cases: - Build custom Windows telemetry collectors for detections and hunting - Prototype ETW-based sensors for process/network/file activity providers - Create .NET security tooling that subscribes to ETW providers in real time - Research and validate what ETW events are emitted by specific components/providers - Develop internal diagnostics/performance/security monitoring agents
Keywords: ETW, Event Tracing for Windows, trace consumer, EVENT_RECORD, PTRACE_EVENT_INFO, provider GUID, trace session, callback handler, schema parsing, Windows internals, C++, C++/CLI, .NET, NuGet, Office 365 Security, telemetry collection, x64, ARM64, Message Analyzer

entry.title: main
entry.summary:
Title: EVTX/ETW Resources (EVTX-ETW-Resources)
Type: GitHub Tool (Resource Repository / Dataset)
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository curates datasets and references to help practitioners learn and operationalize Windows Event Tracing for Windows (ETW) and EVTX logging. - It provides large collections of ETW provider manifests (raw XML) per Windows version, plus generated CSVs enumerating providers, event IDs, and message templates (split into internal vs third-party providers). - It also includes per-OS “all events” CSVs that aggregate event metadata across providers, enabling fast searching and mapping of telemetry to behaviors. - The repo links to key ETW research, talks, and tooling for interacting with ETW (e.g., logman, TraceEvent, PerfView, SilkETW) and includes example scripts (e.g., pywintrace). - Useful for Blue Teams and DFIR to build detections and understand telemetry coverage, and for Red Teams/researchers to assess what actions generate which ETW signals and where sensors may exist. - Interesting because it turns ETW’s fragmented provider ecosystem into searchable, versioned artifacts that can be used for engineering detections and telemetry-driven research.
Technical Focus: - ETW provider manifests (instrumentation XML) and event schema extraction - EVTX/ETW event ID and message cataloging across Windows versions - Provider inventorying (Windows-native vs third-party, e.g., Sysmon/AV/Office) - ETW collection/consumption tooling (logman, TraceEvent, PerfView, SilkETW, KrabsETW) - Telemetry research for detection/forensics and ETW sensor tampering awareness
Use Cases: - Build/validate detection logic by mapping behaviors to ETW providers and event IDs - Identify high-value ETW providers for threat hunting and forensic triage - Compare ETW telemetry changes across Windows versions (e.g., Windows 10 vs 11) - Generate allowlists/blocklists or coverage matrices for EDR/ETW-based sensors - Rapidly search event metadata when reverse engineering suspicious activity or tooling

entry.title: SealighterTI
entry.summary:
Title: Sealighter-TI (SealighterTI) — Run Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence ETW Provider via PPL abuse
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Sealighter-TI is a Windows research tool that combines the Sealighter ETW logging project with PPLDump’s Protected Process Light (PPL) abuse technique to start consuming the `Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence` ETW provider without using a Microsoft-signed driver. The problem it addresses is that subscribing to the Threat-Intelligence provider typically requires an Anti-Malware (or higher) PPL process, which is difficult to obtain legitimately outside of vendor workflows. It leverages an (historically) unpatched technique to coerce a PPL `services.exe` instance into loading an arbitrary DLL, then uses that privileged context to run the ETW trace and forward events into the Windows Event Log under a custom “Sealighter/Operational” channel. The repo includes build/run instructions using `wevtutil` to install an ETW manifest and emphasizes Release builds for reliable injection. It’s useful for defenders and security researchers who want high-fidelity injection/attack telemetry, and for red teamers who want to understand/validate PPL boundaries and ETW-based detections. Note: the underlying PPLDump exploit path is documented as patched on newer Windows 10 builds (21H2 19044.1826+), with alternatives suggested (e.g., vulnerable drivers).
Technical Focus: - ETW subscription to `Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence` - Protected Process Light (PPL) / Anti-Malware protection level - PPL abuse via DLL loading into PPL `services.exe` (PPLDump technique) - Event forwarding into Windows Event Log via custom provider manifest (`wevtutil im`) - Windows injection/Detours-style hooking and DLL-based payload execution
Use Cases: - Collect Threat-Intelligence ETW telemetry on systems where you can’t deploy a signed kernel driver - Validate/benchmark detections for process injection using TI ETW events - Research PPL trust boundaries and historical code-integrity/PPL bypass techniques - Lab reproduction of PPLDump-style primitives with an ETW logging payload instead of LSASS dumping - Generate Windows Event Log artifacts for SIEM ingestion and detection engineering
Keywords: ETW, Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence, Threat Intelligence provider, Protected Process Light, PPL, Anti-Malware PPL, services.exe, DLL injection, PPLDump, LSASS, code integrity, wevtutil, ETW manifest, Windows Event Log, Detours, injection detection, Windows 10 x64, vulnerable driver, test signing mode

Use Cases: - Build and run behavioral detections for common attacker TTPs on Windows endpoints - Threat hunting using high-fidelity system event streams and rule matches - Memory scanning for in-memory artifacts using YARA rules - Capture/record endpoint activity for incident response and post-compromise forensics - Extend detection/response workflows with custom Python filaments - Red-team detection testing (e.g., validate whether actions like credential discovery trigger alerts)

entry.title: mydumbedr

entry.title: wazuh

entry.title: injdrv
entry.summary:
Title: injdrv — PoC Windows kernel driver for DLL injection via APC
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): injdrv is a proof-of-concept Windows kernel-mode driver that injects a DLL into newly created user-mode processes using APC (Asynchronous Procedure Call) delivery during early process initialization. It targets Windows 7–10 across x86/x64/ARM32/ARM64 and includes specific handling for Wow64 processes, including injecting either same-arch DLLs or native (x64) DLLs into Wow64 on x64 systems. The repo documents multiple injection strategies: a classic “thunk” APC that calls `LdrLoadDll`, a “thunkless” technique that leverages `KiUserApcDispatcher`/calling convention quirks to call x64 `LdrLoadDll` directly (mitigating CFG issues), and a Wow64-specific `wow64log.dll` path reparse via a minifilter. It uses `PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutineEx` and `PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine` to time injection after key loader DLLs are mapped/initialized, and discusses pitfalls like deadlocks around `AddressCreationLock` and forced APC delivery. A demo DLL hooks a few `ntdll` functions (via DetoursNT) and logs events using ETW APIs available in `ntdll` to avoid `advapi32` dependencies. Useful for red teamers, malware researchers, and defenders studying kernel-assisted injection, early-bird injection timing, and Wow64/CFG edge cases.
Technical Focus: - Kernel-to-user DLL injection via user-mode APCs (`KeInitializeApc`, `KeTestAlertThread`) - Process/image load callbacks (`PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutineEx`, `PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine`) - Early process initialization / loader timing (`ntdll`, Wow64 DLL load order) - Memory mapping with sections to avoid RWX (`ZwCreateSection`, `ZwMapViewOfSection`, `ZwUnmapViewOfSection`) - Wow64 injection mechanics (`PsWrapApcWow64Thread`, `KiUserApcDispatcher`, CFG bitmap constraints) - Minifilter reparse-based redirection (`IRP_MJ_CREATE`, `IoReplaceFileObjectName`, `STATUS_REPARSE`)
Use Cases: - Implementing/understanding early-bird DLL injection from kernel mode - Researching Wow64 injection (x86 target) with native x64 payload constraints and CFG interactions - Building PoCs for EDR telemetry evaluation around APC injection and image-load callbacks - Studying safe memory permission transitions (section remap) and deadlock avoidance in callbacks - Demonstrating user-mode API hooking limited to `ntdll`-only dependencies plus ETW tracing
Keywords: Windows kernel driver, DLL injection, APC, user-mode APC, kernel-mode APC, PsSetLoadImageNotifyRoutine, PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutineEx, KeTestAlertThread, KeInitializeApc, LdrLoadDll, ntdll.dll, Wow64, PsWrapApcWow64Thread, KiUserApcDispatcher, Control Flow Guard, CFG bitmap, ZwCreateSection, ZwMapViewOfSection, AddressCreationLock, minifilter, IRP_MJ_CREATE, IoReplaceFileObjectName, STATUS_REPARSE, ETW, Detours, DetoursNT, protected process, PsIsProtectedProcess

entry.title: TiEtwAgent
entry.summary:
Title: TiEtwAgent – ETW-based process injection detection
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): TiEtwAgent is a proof-of-concept Windows detection agent that monitors and detects process/memory injection activity using Event Tracing for Windows (ETW), specifically the Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence provider. It aims to provide a more stable, kernel-visible alternative to userland API hooking approaches commonly used by EDRs. The repo is designed for researching both detection logic and potential bypass techniques, with detections implemented in code (e.g., `DetectionLogic.cpp`) and extensible event-field parsing via a generic event abstraction. It leverages the `microsoft/krabsetw` library to set up and consume ETW sessions. Deployment is oriented around running as a Protected Process Light (PPL) service, with guidance for test-signing and using an ELAM-capable certificate/driver. It’s useful for red teams and security researchers to understand what TI ETW can surface, and for blue teams to prototype injection detections without relying on fragile hooks.

Use Cases: - Prototype detections for common injection techniques (e.g., remote thread, memory mapping, RWX transitions) - Validate what Microsoft Threat Intelligence ETW events expose on specific Windows builds - Build a lab agent to compare ETW-based visibility vs userland-hook telemetry - Research and test evasion/bypass strategies against TI-provider-driven detections - Develop/extend custom detection logic by adding new event fields and handlers
