Use Cases: - Build BOFs using normal Windows API names, then convert imports to DFR automatically in CI/build steps - Reduce risk of incorrect DFR prototypes and simplify code reviews/maintenance - Maintain a centralized `imports.txt` mapping for large BOF projects - Enable easier debugging by compiling the same source as an EXE with mocked Beacon APIs - Create custom BOF build tooling that manipulates COFF symbols directly
Keywords: Beacon Object Files, BOF, Cobalt Strike, Dynamic Function Resolution, DFR, COFF, symbol table, string table, relocations, objcopy, --redefine-sym, --redefine-syms, llvm-objcopy, MinGW-w64, GCC, TrustedSec COFFLoader, BeaconPrintf, Windows API, kernel32.dll, advapi32.dll

entry.title: thievingfox
entry.summary:
Title: ThievingFox – Remotely retrieving credentials from password managers and Windows utilities
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post introduces ThievingFox, a post-exploitation toolkit for capturing credentials from common admin tools (password managers and RDP clients) by forcing target applications to load a malicious library and then hooking credential-handling code in-memory. - It addresses limitations of tools like KeeThief and mimikatz (app coverage and the need to “catch” apps while running) by using “passive” injection techniques that trigger when the victim app starts, avoiding noisy polling and common remote-thread injection APIs. - The author focuses on more robust hooking by targeting stable exported symbols or COM vtable method addresses rather than fragile byte-pattern signatures. - Captured secrets are written to disk encrypted using libsodium sealed boxes (public key embedded in the DLL; private key kept by the operator) and later collected over SMB. - Techniques covered include DLL proxying/sideloading (KeePassXC), COM hijacking (mstsc/RDCMan/MobaXTerm/LogonUI/consent.exe), and .NET AppDomainManager injection plus managed-method pointer swapping (KeePass). - It’s primarily useful for red teams and pentesters doing workstation/server post-exploitation, and it’s notable for credential capture paths that avoid LSASS dumping and reduce common EDR telemetry.
Technical Focus: - Passive injection via DLL proxying/sideloading and COM hijacking - In-memory API/function hooking (MinHook, byte patching) - COM in-proc servers, vtable inspection, and method hooking (e.g., IMsRdpClient*) - .NET Framework AppDomainManager injection and JIT/native method pointer swapping - Credential capture from LogonUI/RDP clients without LSASS access - Encrypted on-disk staging using libsodium sealed boxes + SMB collection
Use Cases: - Capture KeePass/KeePassXC master passwords (and keyfile-related material) when databases are unlocked - Harvest cleartext RDP credentials from mstsc/RDCMan/MobaXTerm during authentication - Collect credentials entered at the Windows logon/unlock screen (LogonUI) on “poisoned” hosts - Reduce detection risk versus OpenProcess/WriteProcessMemory/CreateRemoteThread-style injection - Stage and later retrieve captured secrets in environments where outbound HTTP listeners are impractical
Keywords: ThievingFox, post-exploitation, credential harvesting, DLL proxying, DLL sideloading, COM hijacking, CLSID, in-proc COM server, vtable hooking, MinHook, byte patching, AppDomainManager, .NET Framework, JIT compilation, method pointer swapping, KeePass, KeePassXC, mstsc.exe, RDCMan, MobaXTerm, LogonUI.exe, consent.exe, IMsRdpClient, put_ClearTextPassword, libsodium, sealed boxes, SMB exfiltration, PROCESS_MITIGATION_BINARY_SIGNATURE_POLICY, SeDebugPrivilege

entry.title: all about cli4bofs tool.html
entry.summary:
Title: All about cli4bofs tool
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - The post introduces **cli4bofs**, a command-line interface for documenting and executing Beacon Object Files (BOFs) directly from the filesystem without running Cobalt Strike, Sliver, or Metasploit. - It proposes a **universal YAML metadata schema** (name, description, OS, entrypoint/API exports, arguments, errors, examples, sources) to standardize BOF “manuals” similar to Cobalt Strike `.cna` scripts or Sliver extension manifests. - Metadata is embedded into BOF source files using `///`-prefixed lines and aggregated at build time into a single `BOF-collection.yaml` consumed by cli4bofs. - The tool provides `list`, `info <BOF>` (pretty help/usage), and `exec <BOF>` (run BOFs locally) and uses a typed-argument format compatible with Meterpreter `execute_bof` and Sliver COFF loader conventions. - Under the hood it relies on **Z-Labs bof-launcher** and supports Windows/Linux across multiple architectures with Zig-based cross-compilation. - It also includes a workflow to **fetch and build third-party BOFs** from `sources:` URLs and then use the resulting artifacts with Meterpreter’s `execute_bof`. - Useful for red teamers/BOF developers who need repeatable testing, consistent documentation, and easier BOF reuse across frameworks.
Technical Focus: - BOF metadata standardization via YAML (arguments, errors, examples, sources) - Local BOF execution (COFF/ELF object loading) via bof-launcher - Typed BOF argument encoding (int/short/str/wstr/file buffer) - Zig build system and cross-compilation for multi-arch payload tooling - Integrating third-party BOF collections (fetch/build pipeline) - Interop with Cobalt Strike/Sliver/Meterpreter BOF ecosystems
Use Cases: - Quickly view BOF usage/help outside of Cobalt Strike `.cna` scripts - Execute and debug BOFs locally in a lab without a C2 framework - Maintain a curated BOF catalog with consistent argument/error documentation - Fetch/build BOFs from public repos and produce ready-to-run COFF/ELF artifacts - Prepare BOFs for Meterpreter `execute_bof` usage and automation
Keywords: BOF, Beacon Object File, cli4bofs, bof-launcher, Cobalt Strike, Sliver, Meterpreter, execute_bof, COFF, ELF, YAML manifest, Zig, cross-compilation, argument marshaling, wstr, file buffer argument, BOF-collection.yaml, fetch-3rdparty-BOFs.py, Windows x86/x64, Linux ARM/AArch64

entry.title: learning sliver c2 01 installation
entry.summary:
Title: Learning Sliver C2 (01) – Installation on Kali Linux
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post walks through installing BishopFox’s Sliver C2 framework on a fresh Kali Linux server as the first entry in a beginner-focused series. - It covers both installing from official release binaries and building Sliver from source for easier customization. - The author explains Sliver’s client-server (multi-operator) architecture, including running the server as a systemd service and verifying it is listening on TCP/31337. - It details operator onboarding via mutual TLS: generating operator configs on the server, placing them in `~/.sliver-client/configs`, and connecting with the `sliver` client. - The post also notes Sliver’s use of a bundled Go toolchain and garble for building/obfuscating Go implants, and recommends installing MinGW-w64 for cross-compilation. - Useful for red teamers/pentesters setting up an open-source C2 lab environment and wanting a reproducible, non-“curl|bash” installation path.
Technical Focus: - Sliver client-server architecture and multi-operator setup - systemd service deployment on Linux - mTLS operator authentication (certs, keys, token) - Cross-compilation toolchains (MinGW-w64) for Windows payloads - Building Go projects from source (make, assets download) - Sliver asset unpacking and embedded Go toolchain/garble
Use Cases: - Stand up a Sliver C2 server on Kali for a home lab or test range - Create and distribute operator configs for collaborative operations - Run Sliver persistently via systemd and validate network exposure/ports - Build customized Sliver binaries from source for experimentation - Prepare a Linux build environment for generating Windows implants (via MinGW-w64)
Keywords: Sliver, BishopFox, C2 framework, Kali Linux, systemd, sliver-server, sliver-client, mutual TLS, mTLS, operator config, client certificates, token authentication, Go toolchain, garble, MinGW-w64, cross-compilation, TCP 31337, go-assets.sh, make, implants

entry.title: EV code signing with pfx in 2024
entry.summary:
Title: EV code signing with PFX in 2024 (bypassing USB token pickup workflow)
Type: Blog Post
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This post documents the author’s attempt to obtain and use an EV Code Signing certificate for Windows driver signing, and the friction caused by mandatory hardware token (FIPS) “certificate pickup” workflows. - It covers vendor vetting, token shipping, and the practical failure modes of SafeNet/eToken tooling (including lockout and buggy “initialize token” behavior), leading the author to reverse the pickup process instead of relying on the token software. - The author intercepts and modifies legacy IE-compat certificate pickup pages/JavaScript with mitmproxy, inspects hidden form fields (CN, keyLength=4096, token), and attempts to submit a custom CSR. - After discovering constraints (e.g., RSA4096 requirement and YubiKey limitations for certain configurations), they generate a compliant CSR with OpenSSL, inject it into the pickup flow, and recover the server’s PKCS#7 response from process memory. - Finally, they convert the PKCS#7 output into a usable PFX and successfully sign a binary, demonstrating that the “token-required” flow can be worked around at the web/app layer. - Useful for red teamers, malware devs, and researchers interested in code-signing supply chain abuse, legacy enrollment flows, and practical reverse engineering of certificate issuance tooling.
Technical Focus: - EV code signing & Windows driver signing prerequisites - Legacy certificate enrollment/pickup flows (IE compatibility, form-based PKCS#10/PKCS#7) - CSR generation and formats (PKCS#10, PKCS#7, PFX/PKCS#12) - TLS interception and client-side JS tampering (mitmproxy/mitmdump) - Hardware token ecosystems (SafeNet/eToken, FIPS 140-2 constraints, YubiKey PIV) - Process memory inspection/debugging (CheatEngine, x64dbg)
Use Cases: - Reproducing EV certificate pickup without vendor token middleware - Auditing/assessing certificate issuance and enrollment workflow weaknesses - Building automation around CSR submission and cert retrieval - Researching code-signing abuse paths and operational bottlenecks for driver signing - Troubleshooting token lockouts and broken vendor tooling during cert enrollment
Keywords: EV code signing, Windows driver signing, Microsoft driver signing, GlobalSign, certificate pickup, hardware token, SafeNet Authentication Client, eToken, FIPS 140-2, YubiKey PIV, RSA4096, PKCS#10, CSR, PKCS#7, PFX, PKCS#12, OpenSSL, mitmproxy, mitmdump, JavaScript injection, IE compatibility mode, x64dbg, CheatEngine

entry.title: ColdWer
entry.summary:
Title: ColdWer — Cobalt Strike BOF to freeze EDR/AV and dump LSASS via WerFaultSecure.exe PPL bypass
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - ColdWer is a Cobalt Strike Beacon Object File (BOF) that uses a WerFaultSecure.exe Protected Process Light (PPL) bypass to temporarily “freeze” EDR/AV user-mode processes and to dump LSASS memory on modern Windows systems. - It operationalizes prior research (EDR-Freeze and WSASS) by launching WerFaultSecure.exe at WinTcb PPL, attaching to a target process, and leveraging MiniDumpWriteDump behavior that suspends target threads. - By suspending WerFaultSecure after it has suspended the target, the target process remains effectively paused, creating a window to run post-exploitation actions with reduced user-mode monitoring. - For LSASS dumping, it relies on using a Windows 8.1 WerFaultSecure binary to produce an unencrypted/raw dump (Windows 10/11 versions produce encrypted dumps). - The workflow includes restoring the dump header (PNG → MDMP) before parsing with tools like Mimikatz. - It’s primarily useful for red teams and offensive security researchers; it explicitly notes it won’t work against EDRs with kernel-mode self-protection (e.g., CrowdStrike, SentinelOne).
Technical Focus: - WerFaultSecure.exe abuse for PPL (WinTcb) bypass - EDR “freeze” via thread suspension side-effects of MiniDumpWriteDump - LSASS memory dumping under PPL constraints - Cobalt Strike BOF + Aggressor script integration - Dump format/header manipulation (PNG magic → MDMP) - Limitations vs kernel-mode self-protection / tamper protection
Use Cases: - Freeze Microsoft Defender (MsMpEng.exe) or other user-mode protected processes during an operation - Dump LSASS on hosts where standard handle access is blocked by PPL - Create a short-lived window to run credential theft tooling (e.g., Mimikatz/Rubeus) with reduced interference - Research/validation of PPL bypass and user-mode EDR suspension techniques in lab environments
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, BOF, Beacon Object File, Aggressor Script, WerFaultSecure.exe, PPL, Protected Process Light, WinTcb, LSASS, credential dumping, MiniDumpWriteDump, thread suspension, EDR freeze, Windows Defender, MsMpEng.exe, tamper protection, kernel-mode self-protection, minidump, Mimikatz, Rubeus

entry.title: Carseat
entry.summary:
Title: Carseat – A Junior Seatbelt (Python implementation of GhostPack Seatbelt)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Carseat is a Python reimplementation of GhostPack’s Seatbelt focused on “situational awareness” enumeration of Windows hosts. - It aims to provide most Seatbelt modules that support remote execution, enabling operators to query security posture, configuration, and user/artifact data over the network. - The tool leverages Impacket-style authentication options (passwords, NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets) and uses WMI/registry/event log querying to collect results. - It includes a large set of modules covering AV/Defender, AppLocker, UAC, firewall rules, Sysmon, logon/process creation events, scheduled tasks, shares, LAPS, WSUS/SCCM, browser artifacts, and cloud credential file discovery. - Useful primarily for red teams and pentesters doing post-compromise host triage and remote enumeration; also relevant to blue teams for understanding what can be remotely collected and how. - Interesting because it brings Seatbelt-like coverage to Python/Impacket workflows, making it easier to integrate into existing offensive automation and remote ops.
Technical Focus: - Remote Windows enumeration via Impacket (SMB/RPC/WMI) - Registry and WMI-based configuration discovery - Windows Event Log parsing (e.g., 4624/4648/4688/4104, Sysmon EID 1) - Security control posture checks (Defender, AppLocker, AMSI, UAC, CredGuard, Secure Boot) - Artifact collection (browser history/bookmarks, RDP/Putty/KeePass/FileZilla, PowerShell history) - Enterprise management surface enumeration (SCCM, WSUS, WEF, OneDrive/Cloud sync)
Use Cases: - Remote host triage to identify defensive tooling, logging coverage, and hardening gaps - Enumerate credential/artifact sources (DPAPI keys, browser data, PowerShell history, cloud creds) - Assess lateral movement constraints (firewall rules, RDP settings, NTLM settings, local groups/users) - Identify persistence opportunities (scheduled tasks, autoruns) and monitoring exposure (Sysmon/WEF) - Collect recent security-relevant activity from event logs (logons, explicit creds, process creation)
Keywords: Carseat, Seatbelt, GhostPack, Python, Impacket, WMI, Windows Registry, Kerberos, NTLM hash, SMB, RPC, Event Log, Sysmon, PowerShell 4104, 4624, 4648, 4688, AppLocker, AMSI, Windows Defender, UAC, CredGuard, LAPS, SCCM, WSUS, WEF, DPAPI, autoruns, scheduled tasks, firewall rules

entry.title: Torpedo
entry.summary:
Title: Torpedo — “PsExec on Steroids” (C# SMB/SCCM Remote Execution Utility)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Torpedo is a C#-implemented PsExec-like remote execution tool for Windows that uses SMB (admin$) and SCCM-style service execution to run commands on remote hosts. - It focuses on stealthier tradecraft than classic PsExec by deploying an obfuscated/packed service, using named pipes for C2-style communication, and supporting encrypted, fileless DLL payload delivery executed in-memory via .NET reflection. - The tool includes token manipulation features: duplicating tokens from a target process by PID or creating tokens from supplied credentials (via P/Invoke), enabling execution under different security contexts. - It also performs basic target enumeration by remotely detecting the installed .NET Framework version over SMB to select compatible service payloads (net3.5/net4.5). - Post-execution cleanup is emphasized (service removal, binary deletion/shredding) to reduce artifacts. - Useful primarily for red teams and penetration testers needing lateral movement/remote execution in Windows environments while attempting to reduce common PsExec detections.
Technical Focus: - SMB admin$ service deployment (PsExec-style lateral movement) - Remote service creation/execution (SCCM-assisted execution model) - Windows access token manipulation (token duplication / logon with creds) - Named pipe IPC for payload transfer and output - Fileless in-memory .NET DLL loading (reflection) - Runtime encryption/polymorphism and artifact cleanup
Use Cases: - Remote command execution on Windows hosts where admin$ access is available - Lateral movement using service-based execution with token impersonation - Fileless delivery/execution of .NET payloads over named pipes - Running tools under stolen/duplicated tokens from specific PIDs - Post-exploitation automation with cleanup to reduce forensic residue
Keywords: PsExec, SMB, admin$, SCCM, remote service, lateral movement, C#, .NET Framework, token impersonation, token duplication, P/Invoke, named pipes, in-memory execution, reflection, encrypted payload, polymorphic encryption, service deployment, artifact cleanup, red team tooling, Windows internals

entry.title: Spartacus
entry.summary:
Title: Spartacus DLL/COM Hijacking Toolkit
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Spartacus is a Windows-focused toolkit that automates discovery and exploitation workflow for DLL hijacking (sideloading) and local COM hijacking opportunities. - It solves the manual effort of hunting missing DLL loads / registry lookups by generating ProcMon filters, capturing long-running traces, parsing large PML logs, and exporting findings to CSV. - For DLL hijacking, it identifies NAME_NOT_FOUND/PATH_NOT_FOUND DLL load attempts, correlates which DLLs were ultimately loaded, and can auto-generate Visual Studio proxy DLL solutions for the targets. - For COM hijacking, it detects missing `InprocServer32` registry keys from ProcMon traces and can also enumerate the registry to find misconfigured COM entries (missing binaries and weak ACLs). - It includes a proxy-generation mode that can leverage Ghidra headless analysis to extract export signatures and execute payloads via proxied exports (not only `DllMain`). - Additional functionality includes generating self-signed certificates and signing DLLs, plus a basic “detect” mode to flag suspicious proxying based on module path heuristics. - Useful primarily for red teams and pentesters (tradecraft automation) and secondarily for defenders validating exposure and hunting for hijacking-in-progress patterns.
Technical Focus: - Sysinternals ProcMon automation (PMC generation) and PML parsing at scale - DLL search order abuse / DLL sideloading and proxy DLL generation - COM hijacking via `InprocServer32` resolution and registry enumeration - Export forwarding and function-level proxying using Ghidra analyzeHeadless - Windows code signing: self-signed cert (PFX) generation and Authenticode signing - Module enumeration–based detection heuristics for suspicious DLL locations
Use Cases: - Identify DLL hijacking candidates from long ProcMon captures (including multi-level dependency loads) - Generate proxy DLL Visual Studio projects for rapid weaponization/testing - Build function-proxy DLLs to trigger payloads from specific exports instead of `DllMain` - Discover local COM hijack persistence/escalation paths via missing keys or weak ACLs - Sign test payload DLLs with lookalike certificate metadata for lab/assessment scenarios - Triage endpoints for potential active DLL proxying based on loaded module paths
Keywords: Windows, DLL hijacking, DLL sideloading, search order hijacking, proxy DLL, export forwarding, DllMain, COM hijacking, InprocServer32, RegOpenKey, HKCU, HKCR, HKLM, Sysinternals ProcMon, PMC, PML parsing, NAME_NOT_FOUND, PATH_NOT_FOUND, Ghidra analyzeHeadless, Visual Studio solution generation, Authenticode, self-signed certificate, PFX, SHA256 signing, module enumeration, ACL misconfiguration

entry.title: PhantomTask
entry.summary:
Title: PhantomTask
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): PhantomTask is a Windows CLI utility written in Rust that creates and immediately executes Scheduled Tasks while targeting a specific Terminal Services (RDP/console) session. It solves the problem of reliably launching a process in a chosen user session (including elevated “run with highest privileges”) using the Task Scheduler COM API rather than ad-hoc service/process tricks. The tool includes session enumeration to map session IDs to users, and supports both interactive-token style execution and password-based credentials for task principals. Tasks are created hidden by default and are scheduled with a near-future trigger then manually run right after registration. It’s useful for red teams and pentesters for post-exploitation execution in the correct desktop/session context, and for defenders/researchers to understand and test Task Scheduler–based tradecraft and telemetry. Notable limitation: session 0 / SYSTEM handling is localization-dependent and requires code adjustment.
Technical Focus: - Windows Task Scheduler COM interfaces (task definition, registration, run) - Terminal Services / session enumeration and session-to-user resolution - Task principal configuration (logon type, “run with highest privileges”) - Hidden scheduled task creation and immediate triggering - Rust Windows API bindings (`windows` crate), COM initialization (MTA)
Use Cases: - Execute payloads/tools in a specific logged-on user’s session (RDP or console) - Create hidden scheduled tasks for persistence or one-shot execution during ops - Validate EDR/SIEM detections around task creation/registration/run events - Automate session-aware tasking on multi-user Windows servers (jump boxes, RDS)
Keywords: Windows Task Scheduler, COM, ITaskService, ITaskDefinition, RegisteredTask, TaskFolder, scheduled task, hidden task, run with highest privileges, session ID, Terminal Services, Remote Desktop Services, RDP, WTS APIs, session enumeration, SYSTEM session 0, Rust, windows crate, clap, post-exploitation execution, persistence

entry.title: NyxInvoke
entry.summary:
Title: NyxInvoke
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): NyxInvoke is a Rust-based Windows execution wrapper that runs .NET assemblies, PowerShell commands/scripts, Beacon Object Files (BOFs), and PE files. It targets common defensive controls by including “patchless” AMSI and ETW bypasses plus an NTDLL unhooking routine designed to avoid triggering process creation notify callbacks (PspCreateProcessNotifyRoutine). Payloads can be provided from disk, URL, or compiled into the binary, and can be AES-encrypted with runtime decryption. The project supports dual builds: a standalone EXE CLI or a DLL callable via rundll32 with the same execution modes. It’s primarily useful for red teams and pentesters needing flexible in-memory execution and tradecraft experimentation around telemetry/scan evasion. It’s interesting because it consolidates multiple execution primitives (CLR/PS/BOF/PE) with built-in evasion and encrypted staging in a Rust implementation.
Technical Focus: - Patchless AMSI bypass - Patchless ETW bypass / telemetry suppression - NTDLL unhooking and user-mode hook evasion - In-memory execution of .NET (CLR hosting), BOFs, and PE loading - AES-encrypted payload staging (key/IV handling) and remote retrieval - Dual-mode packaging (EXE vs rundll32-invoked DLL)
Use Cases: - Execute encrypted .NET tooling (e.g., Seatbelt) with reduced AMSI/ETW visibility - Run PowerShell scripts/commands without standard script-block scanning paths - Execute BOFs outside of Cobalt Strike for post-exploitation tasks - Load and run PE payloads from disk/URL/embedded resources - Build a DLL-based launcher for LOLBin-style execution via rundll32
Keywords: Rust, Windows, CLI, DLL, rundll32, .NET assembly execution, CLR hosting, PowerShell, BOF, Beacon Object File, PE loader, AMSI bypass, ETW bypass, NTDLL unhooking, user-mode hooks, PspCreateProcessNotifyRoutine, AES encryption, payload decryption, remote payload staging, inline assembly

entry.title: Phantom
entry.summary:
Title: Phantom (Crybat/Jlaive Rewrite) – AV Evasion Batch Crypter/Stub Builder
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Phantom is an antivirus evasion/crypter-style tool that transforms executables into batch-based droppers/loaders intended to reduce static detection (with limited guarantees for .NET/native payloads). - It focuses on packaging and protecting payloads via AES encryption and compression, then executing through a stub with multiple anti-analysis features. - The project advertises defenses against common Windows telemetry and scanning surfaces, including AMSI and ETW bypasses, plus anti-debug and anti-VM checks. - It also includes post-execution behaviors such as self-deletion (“melt”), file binding, persistence (startup), and a UAC bypass option. - This is primarily useful for red team operators, malware analysts, and defenders studying commodity evasion tradecraft and how batch/stub-based loaders are assembled. - It’s interesting as a consolidated example of multiple evasion/persistence techniques in a C#-centric builder workflow, with accompanying technical documentation PDF.
Technical Focus: - Payload encryption (AES) and compression in droppers/stubs - Batch file wrapping / executable-to-BAT conversion techniques - AMSI bypass and script/content scanning evasion - ETW bypass / telemetry suppression - Anti-debugging and anti-VM/sandbox checks - Persistence and UAC bypass tradecraft
Use Cases: - Building test payload wrappers to evaluate AV/EDR detection and response - Studying common loader/crypter implementation patterns (builder + stub) - Reproducing and validating AMSI/ETW bypass detections in a lab - Generating samples for defensive research (static/dynamic signatures, behavior rules) - Assessing how encryption/compression and self-delete affect forensic visibility
Keywords: Phantom, crypter, loader, dropper, batch file, BAT, stub, AES, compression, anti-debug, anti-VM, sandbox evasion, AMSI bypass, ETW bypass, UAC bypass, persistence, startup folder, self-delete, file binding, C#

entry.title: Cobaltstrike BOFLoader
entry.summary:
Title: Cobaltstrike_BOFLoader (Open-source reimplementation of the Cobalt Strike BOF Loader)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): This repository provides an open-source port/reimplementation of Cobalt Strike’s Beacon BOF (Beacon Object File) loader, intentionally mirroring the original behavior “as is.” It targets a common BOF development pain point: BOFs that run in other COFF loaders (e.g., more robust open-source loaders) but fail in Cobalt Strike due to loader quirks and incomplete COFF feature handling (notably COMDAT/section handling). The project also replicates the undocumented Teamserver-side preprocessing that converts a COFF object into a smaller “blob” format delivered to Beacon. Included tooling packs a COFF (.o) into the expected blob and a standalone loader executes it, enabling reproducible debugging outside of Beacon. It’s primarily useful for red team operators and BOF developers who need fidelity with Cobalt Strike’s loader to diagnose edge cases and compatibility issues. Its value is in behavioral parity and debuggability rather than improved stealth or correctness.
Technical Focus: - Cobalt Strike Beacon BOF loading internals - COFF object parsing and relocation/application - Section/COMDAT handling edge cases - Teamserver-side BOF preprocessing and blob format - Standalone BOF execution workflow (pack + load) - Debugging BOF compatibility issues across loaders
Use Cases: - Reproduce Cobalt Strike-specific BOF failures outside of Beacon for debugging - Validate whether a BOF incompatibility is due to COFF features (e.g., COMDAT/sections) - Compare behavior against other COFF loaders to isolate loader implementation differences - Develop and test BOFs with a loader that matches Cobalt Strike’s quirks - Reverse/understand the Teamserver “COFF-to-blob” transformation pipeline
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, Beacon, BOF, Beacon Object File, COFF, COFF loader, bofloader, COMDAT, sections, relocations, symbol resolution, object file parsing, Teamserver preprocessing, blob format, Windows, red team tooling, BOF development, TrustedSec COFFLoader, debugging, compatibility testing

entry.title: OpenMalleableC2
entry.summary:
Title: OpenMalleableC2
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - OpenMalleableC2 is an open-source, framework-agnostic library that implements Cobalt Strike’s Malleable C2 profile format specifically for HTTP request/response transformations. - It solves the gap in many open-source C2 frameworks where HTTP traffic customization is less expressive than Cobalt Strike’s ability to embed beacon data into “legitimate-looking” web traffic. - The project enables reuse of existing Malleable C2 profiles (and related ecosystem tools like profile generators) to encode/decode arbitrary data over HTTP in a consistent way. - It includes example code (a “ping-pong” agent/server) demonstrating typical beacon-style GET check-ins and POST task output flows driven by a chosen profile (e.g., a gmail-themed profile). - It’s primarily useful for red teams and security researchers building custom agents/C2s, and secondarily for defenders who want to understand and emulate malleable HTTP patterns for testing detections. - The author notes it is “mostly working” but not guaranteed stable, implying ongoing validation and edge-case handling may be needed.
Technical Focus: - Cobalt Strike Malleable C2 profile parsing and execution - HTTP transformation pipelines (encoding/embedding callback data) - Beacon-like callback patterns (GET tasking / POST results) - Profile-driven request shaping (headers, URIs, body structure) - Interoperability with existing Malleable C2 profile repositories/tools
Use Cases: - Integrate Malleable C2-style HTTP shaping into a custom C2 framework or agent - Rapidly prototype “legitimate-looking” HTTP C2 traffic using existing profiles - Build test harnesses to evaluate proxy/IDS/EDR detections against malleable HTTP patterns - Reproduce and study Cobalt Strike-like network behaviors without using Cobalt Strike
Keywords: OpenMalleableC2, Cobalt Strike, Malleable C2, HTTP C2, profile format, traffic shaping, HTTP transformations, beacon callback, GET check-in, POST exfil/task output, C2 profiles, network evasion, red team tooling, custom agent, Mythic, Havoc, Adaptix, profile repository, Python, C library

entry.title: goexec
entry.summary:
Title: GoExec – Remote Execution Multitool
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - GoExec is a Go-based Windows remote execution multitool that consolidates multiple lateral-movement execution primitives behind a single CLI. - It targets common Windows remote management surfaces (WMI, DCOM, Task Scheduler, and SCMR) and emphasizes “OPSEC improvements” plus alternative RPC transports to better handle monitoring and firewall constraints. - The tool supports multiple authentication options including plaintext credentials, NTLM hash (pass-the-hash), Kerberos (including AES keys and ccache), and certificate-based auth (PFX). - It provides optional remote output collection by redirecting command output to a temp file and retrieving it over SMB (not recommended in monitored environments). - DCOM execution includes several COM object techniques (MMC20.Application, ShellWindows/ShellBrowserWindow, HTAFile URL monikers, and app-specific objects like Visual Studio DTE and Excel macro/XLL loading). - Task Scheduler methods include creating tasks with timed triggers, demanding immediate start, and temporarily modifying existing task definitions; SCMR supports create/change/delete service-based execution with multiple RPC binding options. - Useful primarily for red teams and pentesters needing flexible Windows remote execution paths and transport/auth variations for constrained environments.
Technical Focus: - MSRPC/DCERPC transports, endpoint mapper (EPM) discovery, custom string bindings (ncacn_np, ncacn_ip_tcp) - Remote execution via MS-WMI (Win32_Process.Create) and generic WMI method invocation - DCOM-based execution via specific COM objects (MMC20.Application, ShellWindows, HTAFile/IPersistMoniker, VisualStudio.DTE, Excel.Application) - MS-TSCH task registration/run/retrieve/modify (SchRpcRegisterTask, SchRpcRun, SchRpcRetrieveTask, SchRpcDelete) - MS-SCMR service creation/modification (RCreateServiceW, RChangeServiceConfigW) for execution - Authentication: NTLM (PTH), Kerberos (AES key/ccache), certificate (PFX), optional SMB output retrieval
Use Cases: - Execute commands remotely over WMI/DCOM/SCMR/TSCH during lateral movement - Operate in environments where SMB-based exec is blocked by using alternate RPC transports/bindings - Run “living-off-the-land” commands and optionally collect output for triage (whoami, netstat, sc query, etc.) - Leverage application-installed DCOM surfaces (Excel/Visual Studio) for niche execution paths - Modify existing scheduled tasks or services for temporary execution and revert changes afterward
Keywords: Go, Windows, lateral movement, remote execution, WMI, DCOM, MSRPC, DCERPC, Endpoint Mapper (EPM), SCMR, SVCCTL, Task Scheduler, MS-TSCH, SchRpcRegisterTask, SchRpcRun, Kerberos, NTLM, pass-the-hash, SMB, COM objects, MMC20.Application, ShellWindows, IPersistMoniker, mshta, Excel 4.0 macros (XLM), XLL, VisualStudio.DTE

entry.title: cookie monster
entry.summary:
Title: Cookie-Monster-BOF (cookie-monster)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Cookie-Monster is a Cobalt Strike Beacon Object File (BOF) that steals browser cookies and saved credentials from Chromium-based browsers (Chrome/Edge) and Firefox. It extracts Chromium’s WebKit Master Key and the newer App-Bound Encryption Key, then locates a running browser process that already has handles open to the Cookies/Login Data SQLite files, duplicates those handles, and performs “fileless” retrieval of the databases. A companion Python script decrypts the downloaded databases into usable cookies/passwords and can export to formats compatible with tools like Cookie-Editor and CuddlePhish. The repo tracks Chromium changes (e.g., v20 cookies and Chrome/Edge 127+ / Chrome 137+ decryption changes) and includes a mode to decrypt app-bound keys as SYSTEM without injecting into the browser (using Local State + PID impersonation). It’s primarily useful for red teams and pentesters performing post-compromise session hijacking and credential harvesting, and it’s interesting because it operationalizes modern Chromium cookie encryption/key retrieval constraints and handle-based file acquisition.
Technical Focus: - Cobalt Strike BOF development (C, beacon.h) - Chromium cookie/password encryption (WebKit Master Key, App-Bound Encryption Key, v20 cookies) - Handle enumeration/duplication to access locked SQLite DBs (Cookies, Login Data) - Fileless file acquisition techniques (handle-based copying) - DPAPI/Windows key protection and impersonation for decryption contexts - Firefox profile discovery (profiles.ini) and artifact collection (logins.json, key4.db)
Use Cases: - Post-exploitation extraction of Chrome/Edge cookies for session takeover - Dumping Chromium “Login Data” to recover saved passwords offline - Collecting Firefox credential artifacts for offline decryption workflows - Generating cookie exports for Cookie-Editor import or CuddlePhish automation - Operating in environments where browser DB files are locked (live handle duplication)
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, BOF, Beacon Object File, Chrome, Microsoft Edge, Firefox, Chromium, cookies v20, App-Bound Encryption Key, WebKit Master Key, Local State, Login Data, Cookies SQLite, handle duplication, fileless download, DPAPI, impersonation, PostProcessData, key4.db, logins.json

entry.title: WhoIsWho
entry.summary:
Title: WhoIsWho — Alternatives to the Windows `whoami` command
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository is a collection of proof-of-concept “whoami alternatives” for Windows, implemented in C++. - It addresses situations where calling `whoami.exe` is undesirable (e.g., restricted environments, application allowlisting, or wanting different telemetry/detection surfaces) by retrieving the current user identity through multiple APIs and subsystems. - Each folder demonstrates a different technique to obtain username/domain/SID context, ranging from simple Win32 calls (e.g., `GetUserNameW`, environment variables) to LSA/SID resolution and COM/WMI-based approaches. - The project is useful for red teamers and malware developers exploring low-friction host identity discovery and for blue teamers mapping which API paths generate which logs/EDR signals. - It’s interesting as a compact reference of diverse identity-discovery primitives across Win32, LSA, WMI, Terminal Services, and directory-related interfaces.
Technical Focus: - Win32 identity APIs (`GetUserNameW`, `GetUserProfileDirectory`, `GetEnvironmentVariableW`) - SID-to-name resolution (`LookupAccountSidW`, `LsaLookupSids`) - Process environment/PEB structures (`RTL_USER_PROCESS_PARAMETERS`) - WMI/COM enumeration (`IEnumWbemClassObject`) - Terminal Services session queries (`WTSQuerySessionInformation`) - AD/Directory/management interfaces (e.g., `ADSystemInfo`, `ADsComputer`, `IWSMan`)
Use Cases: - Replace `whoami.exe` in implants/tools to reduce dependency on external binaries - Compare detection/telemetry differences between identity-discovery methods - Host/user context checks before privilege escalation or lateral movement steps - Build minimal “situational awareness” modules for Windows payloads - Defensive testing: create detections for suspicious identity enumeration via uncommon APIs
Keywords: Windows, whoami, GetUserNameW, GetEnvironmentVariableW, LookupAccountSidW, LsaLookupSids, SID, LSA, RTL_USER_PROCESS_PARAMETERS, PEB, WMI, COM, IEnumWbemClassObject, WTSQuerySessionInformation, ADSystemInfo, ADSI, ADsComputer, WinRM, IWSMan, C++

entry.title: main
entry.summary:
Title: SCCMDecryptor BOF
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository provides a Cobalt Strike Beacon Object File (BOF) that decrypts Microsoft SCCM/MECM encrypted credential blobs extracted from the SCCM site database. - It reimplements Adam Chester (@_xpn_)’s original C# SCCM decryptor as an in-memory BOF for use during operations where dropping binaries is undesirable. - The BOF must be executed on an SCCM server that has the “Microsoft Systems Management Server” Cryptographic Service Provider (CSP), leveraging the local SCCM crypto material to recover plaintext credentials. - Operators supply a hex-encoded encrypted blob (from the DB) and the BOF returns the decrypted secret. - It is primarily useful for red teams and pentesters targeting SCCM environments, especially in post-compromise credential access and lateral movement workflows. - It maps to known SCCM credential exposure/misconfiguration tradecraft (referenced as Misconfiguration Manager Cred-5).
Technical Focus: - SCCM/MECM credential blob decryption - Microsoft Systems Management Server CSP usage - Cobalt Strike BOF (in-memory execution) - Site database credential extraction workflow (offline-to-online decryption) - Cross-compilation with MinGW-w64 (x86/x64)
Use Cases: - Decrypt credentials harvested from SCCM site DB tables/records during post-exploitation - Convert SCCM “encrypted password blob” artifacts into usable plaintext creds for lateral movement - Run credential decryption capability inside Beacon without writing a standalone executable to disk - Validate SCCM credential exposure paths during internal assessments
Keywords: SCCM, MECM, ConfigMgr, Cobalt Strike, BOF, Beacon Object File, credential decryption, encrypted password blob, site database, Microsoft Systems Management Server, CSP, Cryptographic Service Provider, @_xpn_, Adam Chester, Misconfiguration Manager, Cred-5, MinGW-w64, x86, x64, post-exploitation

entry.title: BOF RunPe
entry.summary:
Title: BOF_RunPE — In-memory PE execution inside Cobalt Strike Beacon without console creation
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): BOF_RunPE is a Cobalt Strike Beacon Object File (BOF) that maps and executes a Windows PE entirely in-memory inside the existing Beacon process, avoiding fork&run behavior. It solves common OPSEC issues by not spawning a child process, not creating a console window, and not using named pipes for output; instead it captures output by IAT-hooking common CRT/console APIs and redirects it to the Beacon console. The project implements multiple memory allocation and loading strategies (heap, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, and module stomping) and supports “proxy” execution paths such as timer queue or RegisterWaitForSingleObject callbacks. It includes evasion-oriented features like indirect syscalls, optional ntdll unhooking from disk, stack spoofing (“Draugr”), and thread start address spoofing via RIP/context manipulation. The README also documents likely detection vectors (ETW-TI, context/thread manipulation, RX transitions, stomped module integrity mismatches). It’s primarily useful for red teams and pentesters operating in Windows environments where EDR telemetry around process creation and console/pipe artifacts is heavily monitored.
Technical Focus: - In-memory PE loading/mapping (sections, relocations, IAT patching) - Output redirection via IAT hooking (printf/WriteConsole/UCRT) - Indirect syscalls and syscall resolution (userland hook bypass) - Stack spoofing and thread start address spoofing (RIP hijack via context) - Module stomping and alternative allocation strategies (heap vs VirtualAlloc) - NTDLL unhooking by restoring .text from disk
Use Cases: - Execute native x64 tooling (e.g., credential dumping utilities) inline within Beacon without spawning a new process - Reduce telemetry tied to fork&run, console creation, and named pipe output capture - Test/benchmark EDR detections around ETW-TI, thread context manipulation, RX transitions, and module stomping - Run payloads with configurable allocation/proxy methods to vary behavioral signatures - Research tradeoffs/limitations of inline execution (e.g., CET impact, lack of .NET support)
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, Beacon Object File, BOF, inline execution, RunPE, in-memory PE, reflective loading, IAT hooking, printf hook, WriteConsoleA, __getmainargs, NtAllocateVirtualMemory, NtProtectVirtualMemory, indirect syscalls, syscall resolution, ntdll unhooking, module stomping, stack spoofing, thread start spoofing, NtGetContextThread, NtSetContextThread, ETW-TI, RIP hijacking, Timer Queue, RegisterWaitForSingleObject, x64 Windows

entry.title: NetExec
entry.summary:
Title: NetExec (nxc) – The Network Execution Tool
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): NetExec is a community-maintained fork/continuation of CrackMapExec, focused on automating network-based enumeration and remote execution workflows commonly used in Windows/Active Directory assessments. It streamlines validating credentials (plaintext, hashes, Kerberos), enumerating services and domain information, and executing actions over common enterprise protocols. The project is Python-based and heavily leverages Impacket-style protocol implementations to interact with SMB/RPC/WinRM and related Windows services at scale. It’s primarily useful for red teams and pentesters performing lateral movement, credential spraying/validation, and post-compromise enumeration across many hosts. It’s interesting because it consolidates a large set of AD tradecraft into a single CLI with modular functionality and active community development after the original CME maintenance changes.
Technical Focus: - SMB/RPC-based remote operations and enumeration - Active Directory authentication (NTLM, Kerberos) and credential validation - Lateral movement primitives (remote command execution via Windows management protocols) - Network-wide scanning/orchestration and target batching - Impacket-based protocol interaction and tooling ecosystem integration
Use Cases: - Validate domain/local credentials or NTLM hashes across many hosts (spray/verify) - Enumerate SMB shares, sessions, local admins, and domain-related host info - Execute remote commands/actions over SMB/WMI/WinRM-style channels (where permitted) - Identify reachable services and prioritize lateral movement paths in AD environments - Automate repeatable post-compromise checks during internal engagements
Keywords: NetExec, nxc, CrackMapExec, Active Directory, Windows, SMB, MSRPC, NTLM, Kerberos, Pass-the-Hash, credential spraying, lateral movement, remote execution, Impacket, WinRM, WMI, enumeration, pentesting, red team, Python

entry.title: GraphSpy
entry.summary:
Title: GraphSpy — Initial Access and Post-Exploitation Tool for AAD/Entra ID and Microsoft 365 (O365) with a Browser GUI
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): GraphSpy is a browser-based GUI tool for initial access and post-exploitation against Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) and Microsoft 365 using OAuth tokens and Microsoft Graph–adjacent APIs. It centralizes storage and reuse of access/refresh tokens, supports device code flows (including polling and auto-actions), and enables interactive abuse of M365 services like OneDrive/SharePoint, Outlook, and Teams. A key focus is token-based persistence and session hijacking via Primary Refresh Tokens (PRTs) and generation of browser-importable PRT cookies for Entra SSO. It also includes functionality to enumerate/modify MFA methods (including adding TOTP/FIDO/Authenticator/phones) and to enroll Windows Hello for Business keys to regain PRTs even after password resets. The tool provides Microsoft Search-based keyword hunting across M365 data and a “custom requests” feature for arbitrary API calls with stored tokens. It’s primarily useful for red teams and pentesters performing Entra/M365 compromise simulation and post-compromise operations, and it highlights how powerful token/PRT/WHfB artifacts are for persistence in cloud identity environments.
Technical Focus: - OAuth2 token operations (access tokens, refresh tokens) and token management - Device Code authentication flow (phishing-style workflows, polling, automation) - Primary Refresh Tokens (PRT), PRT cookies, and Entra ID SSO session access - Windows Hello for Business (WHfB) key enrollment and persistence - Microsoft Graph / Outlook/Teams/OneDrive/SharePoint API interaction - MFA method enumeration/modification for persistence (TOTP, FIDO2, Authenticator, SMS/voice)
Use Cases: - Token-based post-exploitation against M365 (mail, files, Teams messaging) - Device code flow campaigns and automated follow-on actions after user auth - Establishing persistence via MFA method addition and/or WHfB/PRT mechanisms - Generating browser sessions via PRT cookies for interactive access to Entra-integrated apps - Searching tenant data (emails/files) for sensitive keywords using Microsoft Search API - Rapid prototyping/testing of arbitrary Graph/API calls with reusable request templates
Keywords: Microsoft Entra ID, Azure AD, Microsoft 365, Office 365, Microsoft Graph, OAuth2, access token, refresh token, device code flow, device code phishing, Primary Refresh Token, PRT, PRT cookie, SSO, Windows Hello for Business, WHfB, MFA methods, FIDO2, TOTP, Microsoft Authenticator, OneDrive, SharePoint, Outlook, Microsoft Teams, Microsoft Search API, token persistence, post-exploitation

entry.title: dcshadow
entry.summary:
Title: ShutdownRepo/dcshadow (Python alternative to Mimikatz lsadump::dcshadow)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository implements the Active Directory DCShadow technique in pure Python as an alternative to Mimikatz’s `lsadump::dcshadow`. - It stands up a “rogue DC” that temporarily registers as a domain controller and uses AD replication (DRSUAPI) to push crafted attribute changes into a legitimate DC, enabling stealthy persistence. - The project ships with a custom/modified Impacket (notably `crypto`, `gssapi`, `drsuapi`) to support the required Kerberos/RPC replication flows. - Workflow includes creating a machine account, adding a DNS record for reachability, providing Kerberos keys for the rogue DC account, and executing replication changes via a JSON plan or inline parameters. - It’s primarily useful for red teams and AD security researchers to reproduce DCShadow behavior, and for blue teams to generate telemetry/IoCs and validate detections in lab environments (explicitly not for production). - Interesting because it makes DCShadow experimentation more accessible outside Windows/Mimikatz and highlights replication-based persistence as a “transport” for other AD abuses (SIDHistory, DACL/ACE abuse, KRBTGT/RBCD-related scenarios).
Technical Focus: - DCShadow / rogue domain controller registration and unregistration - Active Directory replication via DRSUAPI (e.g., DRSReplicaAdd) over MSRPC - Kerberos authentication (RC4/AES keys) and GSSAPI/SPNEGO handling - Impacket modifications for replication and auth edge-cases - AD object/attribute manipulation via replication (JSON-driven changes) - Supporting steps: machine account creation (LDAP/LDAPS) and DNS record injection
Use Cases: - Lab simulation of DCShadow persistence to test SOC detections and incident response playbooks - Red team AD persistence by replicating malicious attribute changes into a DC - Researching/validating replication abuse primitives (SIDHistory injection, DACL changes, delegation-related persistence) - Building or extending an AD persistence framework around replication “transport” - Generating network/host telemetry for detection engineering (RPC endpoint mapper, DRSUAPI patterns)
Keywords: DCShadow, Active Directory, DRSUAPI, MSRPC, DRSReplicaAdd, replication abuse, rogue DC, Impacket, Kerberos, GSSAPI, SPNEGO, LDAP, LDAPS, DNS record, addcomputer.py, dnstool.py, DCSync, SIDHistory, DACL, ACE abuse, RBCD

entry.title: TopazTerminator
entry.summary:
Title: TopazTerminator (wsftprm.sys Topaz Antifraud BYOVD process terminator)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): TopazTerminator is a C proof-of-concept that abuses the signed-but-vulnerable `wsftprm.sys` (Topaz Antifraud kernel driver) to terminate arbitrary processes on Windows, including protected/PPL security processes. It demonstrates a BYOVD-style technique where an attacker loads a legitimate signed driver and triggers a dangerous IOCTL path that ultimately calls `ZwOpenProcess` and `ZwTerminateProcess`. The README focuses on reversing the driver’s non-standard IOCTL dispatch logic (a subtraction chain instead of a switch) to derive the required IOCTL (`0x22201C`) and input buffer length (1036 bytes). As of Jan 2026, the referenced driver hash is claimed to not yet be on Microsoft’s Vulnerable Driver Blocklist, making it operationally relevant until blocked. This is primarily useful for red teams, pentesters, and malware developers evaluating EDR/AV tamper resistance and driver-blocking controls. It’s interesting because it provides a clear, minimal implementation and reverse-engineering notes for reaching a kernel-assisted process kill primitive.
Technical Focus: - BYOVD (Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver) for security product tampering - Windows kernel driver IOCTL reverse engineering and dispatch logic - IRP_MJ_DEVICE_CONTROL handling and DeviceIoControl triggering - Process termination via `ZwOpenProcess` + `ZwTerminateProcess` - PPL (Protected Process Light) termination implications - Microsoft Vulnerable Driver Blocklist / driver reputation controls
Use Cases: - Terminate EDR/AV agents during post-exploitation to enable follow-on actions - Validate effectiveness of WDAC/HVCI/driver blocklist enforcement in an environment - Reproduce and study a kernel-assisted process-kill primitive for detection engineering - Assess monitoring opportunities around suspicious driver loading + IOCTL usage
Keywords: BYOVD, wsftprm.sys, Topaz Antifraud, vulnerable signed driver, IOCTL, IRP_MJ_DEVICE_CONTROL, DeviceIoControl, ZwOpenProcess, ZwTerminateProcess, PPL, protected process, EDR killer, kernel driver abuse, Windows 25H2, Microsoft Vulnerable Driver Blocklist, SHA-256, process tampering, driver loading, reverse engineering

entry.title: Loki
entry.summary:
Title: Loki Command & Control (Node.js C2 for Script-Jacking Vulnerable Electron Apps)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Loki is a stage-1 C2 framework written in Node.js designed to “script-jack” Electron applications by modifying runtime-loaded JavaScript to execute arbitrary Node code (MITRE ATT&CK T1218.015). - It targets trusted, signed Electron apps to evade controls and bypass application allowlisting (notably WDAC) by living inside an allowed binary’s execution context without breaking the app’s code-signing. - The framework supports “hollowing” (replacing the app’s resources/app payload) and “backdooring” approaches that can keep the legitimate app functioning while spawning Loki in the background. - C2 is implemented via Azure Storage Blobs using SAS tokens, with AES-encrypted messages and a “teamserver-less” design where clients/agents poll the same datastore. - Agents are proxy-aware by leveraging Chromium renderer child processes, and include post-exploitation primitives like file ops, process spawn, TCP scanning, shellcode execution, .NET assembly execution, and COFF/BOF execution. - It’s primarily useful for red teams and adversary simulation focused on application control bypass and stealthy initial footholds via common Electron software; defenders can use the included detection guidance and Sigma references to hunt for this tradecraft.
Technical Focus: - Electron application script-jacking (resources/app modification, ASAR/integrity check considerations) - WDAC / application allowlisting bypass via trusted signed Electron binaries - Azure Blob Storage C2 (SAS tokens) + AES message encryption - Proxy-aware execution via Chromium/Electron child processes - In-process execution: shellcode, .NET inline execution, COFF/BOF loading - Operational tradecraft: backdooring while preserving app functionality, opsec/detections
Use Cases: - Establishing an initial foothold by trojanizing a vulnerable Electron desktop app (e.g., Teams/VS Code/Cursor) - Bypassing Windows Defender Application Control / allowlisting by executing within a trusted signed app - Covert C2 over common cloud infrastructure (Azure Blob) with encrypted tasking/results - Post-exploitation tasking: file management, process spawning, internal TCP scanning, DNS lookups - Running shellcode, .NET assemblies, and BOFs from a Node/Electron context for modular capability
Keywords: Loki C2, Electron, script-jacking, T1218.015, WDAC bypass, application allowlisting, code signing, resources/app, ASAR, integrity checks, Node.js agent, Azure Blob Storage, SAS token, AES encryption, proxy-aware, Chromium renderer, shellcode execution, InlineExecute-Assembly, COFFLoader, BOF execution, Sigma rules, LOLBAS Teams

entry.title: awesome bof
entry.summary:
Title: Awesome BOF (chryzsh/awesome-bof)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository is a curated catalog of Beacon Object Files (BOFs) with a fast, searchable web index. - It solves the problem of discovering and quickly locating relevant BOFs across many GitHub projects by maintaining a centralized markdown catalog (BOF-CATALOG.md) and a generated JSON search index (bof-index.json). - The repo includes automation/scripts to discover, parse, and index BOF source repositories, plus a GitHub Pages site for interactive search. - It’s primarily useful for red teamers and penetration testers building Cobalt Strike/BOF-based post-exploitation workflows, and for BOF developers looking for examples and prior art. - It’s interesting because it operationalizes BOF discovery at scale (including periodic/automated updates) and provides reproducible tooling to keep the index current.
Technical Focus: - Beacon Object Files (Cobalt Strike BOF ecosystem) - Automated BOF discovery and repository parsing/indexing - Search index generation (JSON) + static web search (GitHub Pages) - Contribution workflow automation (row generation, catalog updates) - Python-based tooling and CI-style update workflows
Use Cases: - Quickly find BOFs for specific post-exploitation tasks (credential access, AD abuse, token theft, etc.) - Build/extend a BOF toolkit by referencing existing implementations and APIs used - Maintain an internal BOF catalog by reusing the indexer and site-generation scripts - Track new BOF releases via periodic discovery and catalog updates
Keywords: Beacon Object Files, BOF, Cobalt Strike, post-exploitation, offensive tooling, GitHub Pages, searchable index, bof-index.json, BOF-CATALOG.md, Python, repository parsing, automation, GitHub Actions, static site, red team, pentest, catalog, indexer, generate_md.py

entry.title: PipeViewer
entry.summary:
Title: PipeViewer (CyberArk) — Windows Named Pipe Permission & Metadata Viewer
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): PipeViewer is a Windows GUI tool for enumerating named pipes and displaying detailed metadata and security permissions (DACL/SD) to identify weak or misconfigured access controls. It helps security researchers and operators quickly spot pipes that are writable/connectable by low-privileged users, which can enable local privilege escalation, impersonation, or service abuse. The project was released alongside CyberArk research on Docker Desktop named-pipe attack surface and related privilege escalation paths. It includes filtering/highlighting, row marking, JSON export/import for offline analysis, and a “PipeChat” feature to connect to available pipes for basic interaction/testing. The tool relies on NtApiDotNet/NtObjectManager-style APIs for NT object inspection, which may trigger Microsoft Defender alerts due to common dual-use tooling signatures. Useful for red teams/pentesters validating escalation primitives and for blue teams auditing endpoints for insecure pipe ACLs.
Technical Focus: - Windows Named Pipes enumeration and inspection - Security descriptors, DACLs, and permission analysis on pipe objects - NT object manager / NtApiDotNet-based querying - Discovery of privilege escalation primitives via IPC misconfigurations - JSON export/import for pipe inventory and triage - Interactive pipe connectivity/testing (“PipeChat”)
Use Cases: - Audit endpoints for named pipes with overly permissive ACLs (e.g., Everyone/Users write access) - Triage Docker Desktop / developer tooling IPC surfaces for escalation opportunities - Validate and reproduce named-pipe abuse scenarios during internal assessments - Build a baseline inventory of named pipes and diff across hosts/builds via JSON export - Support defensive hardening by identifying services exposing risky pipe permissions
Keywords: Windows, named pipes, IPC, DACL, SDDL, security descriptor, ACL, NtApiDotNet, NT Object Manager, NtObjectManager, privilege escalation, Docker Desktop, pipe impersonation, service abuse, enumeration, C#, PowerShell, JSON export, endpoint hardening, CyberArk Labs

entry.title: NomadScanner
entry.summary:
Title: NomadScanner – Stealth Portscanner for Red Teams
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): NomadScanner is a Windows-based, C-written “memory-only” port scanner designed for stealthy reconnaissance during red team operations. It focuses on minimizing host artifacts (no file output) and blending network traffic by sending randomized HTTP probes (method, User-Agent, padding) with configurable jitter. The tool supports IPv4/IPv6, multithreaded scanning, banner grabbing, port range parsing with exclusions, and optional domain fronting via a custom `Host:` header. It also exposes network-level evasion knobs such as randomized ephemeral source ports, TTL/hop-limit variation, TOS/traffic-class changes, DF/DONTFRAG control, TCP window tuning, and `TCP_NODELAY`. It’s primarily useful for red teamers and penetration testers who need OPSEC-conscious service discovery on Windows. The interesting aspect is the combination of application-layer probe randomization with low-level socket option tuning to reduce simple signature- and heuristic-based detections.
Technical Focus: - Windows Winsock networking (`ws2_32`), socket option tuning - Stealthy port scanning via HTTP-based probes and banner grabbing - Traffic randomization (jitter, headers, padding, User-Agent rotation) - IPv4/IPv6 dual-stack scanning using `getaddrinfo` - Domain fronting / `Host:` header override - Concurrency (multithreading) and in-memory logging/output buffering
Use Cases: - Stealthy internal network port/service discovery from a Windows foothold - Identifying HTTP-speaking services and collecting response banners - Blending recon traffic into “normal” web-like patterns using randomized probes - Scanning dual-stack (IPv4/IPv6) environments with per-family socket behavior - Domain-fronted HTTP probing where SNI/Host-based routing is relevant (Host header override)
Keywords: NomadScanner, Windows, C, port scanner, stealth scanning, OPSEC, Winsock, ws2_32, getaddrinfo, IPv6, IPv4, banner grabbing, HTTP probes, User-Agent randomization, jitter, TCP_NODELAY, TTL, hop limit, TOS, traffic class, DONTFRAG, ephemeral source port, domain fronting, Host header

entry.title: MoveEdr
entry.summary:
Title: MoveEDR (MoveEdr.ps1)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): MoveEDR is a PowerShell script that disables AV/EDR products by scheduling file/folder moves or deletions to occur at next boot, before the target services and drivers start. It leverages Windows Session Manager’s `PendingFileRenameOperations` mechanism (the same underlying behavior as Sysinternals PendMoves / `MoveFileEx`) to rename/move EDR binaries and directories so they cannot load. The repo documents how the registry value is formatted (`REG_MULTI_SZ` with NULL-terminated src/dst strings) and automates generating correct entries for multiple vendors, with options to customize paths, suffixes, and to undo changes. It includes operational flags for dumping/loading the registry state, printing human-readable entries, and more aggressive Defender handling (moving the whole Defender folder). This is primarily useful for red teams/pentesters with local admin who need to neutralize endpoint controls without using vendor tamper-protection passwords. Notably, it warns of potential CrowdStrike-induced boot loops (“INACCESSIBLE_BOOT_DEVICE”) under some conditions, highlighting real-world stability risk.
Technical Focus: - `MoveFileEx` / boot-time rename-delete semantics - `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\PendingFileRenameOperations` - `REG_MULTI_SZ` NULL-terminated string formatting for pending operations - Pre-service boot execution order (Session Manager behavior) - PowerShell automation for mass path moves + rollback (undo) - EDR/AV tamper-resistance bypass via offline/early-boot file displacement
Use Cases: - Disable EDR/AV components on next reboot when local admin cannot uninstall/stop them normally - Temporarily “move aside” Defender/EDR to run tooling, then restore via `-Undo` - Test resilience of EDR deployments against boot-time file displacement - Incident response / lab reproduction of EDR failure modes caused by missing binaries - Validate defensive monitoring around Session Manager pending rename operations
Keywords: PowerShell, MoveFileEx, PendingFileRenameOperations, Session Manager, REG_MULTI_SZ, HKLM\SYSTEM, boot-time rename, pre-boot persistence, tamper protection bypass, EDR disablement, Windows Defender, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, CrowdStrike, Elastic Agent, Trellix, McAfee, Trend Micro, Sysinternals PendMoves, INACCESSIBLE_BOOT_DEVICE

entry.title: no defender
entry.summary:
Title: no-defender
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - no-defender is a Windows tool intended to disable Microsoft Defender and optionally the Windows Firewall by abusing the Windows Security Center (WSC) mechanism used by third‑party AV products. - It works by registering a fake/alternate antivirus via an undocumented WSC API so Windows believes another AV is present and turns off Defender accordingly. - The repository notes the WSC API is not publicly documented and typically requires an NDA with Microsoft, implying reverse engineering or private interface usage. - Persistence across reboot is achieved by adding the tool to autorun, requiring the binaries to remain on disk. - It’s primarily useful for red teams, pentesters, and malware developers evaluating defensive gaps and bypass paths; it’s also relevant to blue teams for understanding WSC-based tampering. - The repo was archived and its contents/releases were wiped following a DMCA complaint, leaving mainly the README and usage details.
Technical Focus: - Windows Security Center (WSC) API abuse (undocumented interfaces) - Defender disablement via third-party AV registration semantics - Windows Firewall disablement toggling - Persistence via autorun (startup registration) - Security product tampering / defense evasion on Windows
Use Cases: - Disabling Defender during offensive security testing to run tooling without AV interference - Simulating “installed third-party AV” conditions to study Defender/WSC behavior - Testing blue-team detections for WSC registration changes and AV/firewall state transitions - Evaluating persistence and on-disk artifact risks of WSC-based bypasses
Keywords: Windows Defender, Windows Firewall, Windows Security Center, WSC, undocumented API, AV registration, security product spoofing, defense evasion, autorun, persistence, tamper, endpoint security, red team, pentest, Windows internals, startup entry, service interaction, DMCA takedown

entry.title: No Consolation
entry.summary:
Title: No-Consolation
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - No-Consolation is a Cobalt Strike Beacon Object File (BOF) that executes unmanaged Windows PE files (EXEs/DLLs) inline inside the Beacon process and captures their output without allocating a console (avoids spawning `conhost.exe`). - It solves the problem of running native tooling while minimizing common telemetry such as new processes/threads and console allocation artifacts. - The project implements an in-memory PE loader with options to link the loaded module into the PEB, overwrite PE headers, and custom-load dependencies to reduce image load events. - It supports x86/x64, EXE and DLL execution (including selecting an exported function), and can cache/encrypt previously run binaries in memory for reuse without re-uploading. - Useful primarily for red teams and pentesters operating in Cobalt Strike who need inline execution tradecraft and controlled output handling. - Interesting due to its focus on “no new process/thread” execution, dependency loading control, and operational features like in-memory PE inventory/unload.
Technical Focus: - Inline PE loading/execution inside an existing process (BOF) - Console-less output capture via pipes/handle management - PEB module linking and PE header stomping - Custom dependency resolution/loading (ApiSet, manual LoadLibrary logic) - In-memory encrypted PE caching and lifecycle management (list/unload) - DLL export invocation and (x64) C++ exception support
Use Cases: - Execute native Windows utilities (e.g., `cmd.exe`, `powershell.exe`) without spawning `conhost.exe` - Run third-party EXEs (e.g., credential tools) inline to reduce process-creation telemetry - Execute DLL payloads by calling `DllMain` or a specific exported function - Preload and reuse binaries from encrypted in-memory storage to reduce network transfers - Reduce image load events by custom-loading dependencies and offloading them after execution
Keywords: Cobalt Strike, Beacon Object File, BOF, inline execution, in-memory PE loader, unmanaged PE, EXE, DLL, PEB, conhost.exe, console allocation, named pipes, handle inheritance, PE header overwrite, manual mapping, ApiSet, DarkLoadLibrary, donut, dependency resolution, image load events

entry.title: pipe intercept
entry.summary:
Title: pipe-intercept — Intercept Windows Named Pipes communication using Burp (via WebSockets)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - A Windows-only Python tool that man-in-the-middles local Windows Named Pipe traffic by standing up a proxy pipe server/client and bridging messages over WebSockets. - It enables inspection and modification of named-pipe IPC using common HTTP proxy tooling (e.g., Burp Suite) by tunneling the pipe data through a WebSocket connection that Burp can observe/intercept. - The approach works by creating additional pipe server instances so that target clients connect to the proxy instance (FIFO behavior and permissions can affect which clients get intercepted). - The README highlights practical limitations: must run on the same Windows machine as the pipe server and proxy, may break apps using pipe endpoint identity checks, and can interfere with servers using `FILE_FLAG_FIRST_PIPE_INSTANCE`. - Useful for red teams/pentesters and security researchers assessing local IPC attack surface, authZ/authN logic, and message-level vulnerabilities in applications using named pipes (including RPC-like patterns). - Interesting because it brings “Burp-style” interactive interception to a non-HTTP Windows IPC channel that is commonly opaque during testing.
Technical Focus: - Windows Named Pipes (Win32 API) proxying - MITM via pipe server instance hijacking / FIFO connection behavior - WebSocket bridge tunneled through an HTTP proxy (Burp) - IPC message inspection and modification workflows - Operational constraints: permissions, endpoint identity checks, `FILE_FLAG_FIRST_PIPE_INSTANCE`
Use Cases: - Intercept and tamper with Docker Desktop’s `\\.\pipe\docker_engine` traffic for security testing - Reverse engineer proprietary local IPC protocols carried over named pipes - Identify auth bypasses or insecure message handling in pipe-based client/server apps - Build reproducible test cases by capturing and replaying pipe messages through Burp - Validate defensive controls that rely on pipe peer identity (PID/user) assumptions
Keywords: Windows named pipes, Win32 API, IPC, MITM, Burp Suite, HTTP proxy, WebSocket, websockets Python, pipe server instance, FIFO connection order, FILE_FLAG_FIRST_PIPE_INSTANCE, RPC over named pipes, message tampering, protocol reverse engineering, local privilege boundaries, Python 3.13, pytest, CyberArk MITM_Intercept

entry.title: better sliver
entry.summary:
Title: Better-Sliver (fork of BishopFox Sliver)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - Better-Sliver is an archived fork of the Sliver adversary emulation/C2 framework focused on reducing detectability by changing default fingerprints and breaking static signatures. - It targets common defensive detections by modifying implant/server TLS artifacts (custom CAs), altering command strings that appear in YARA/behavior rules, and adding protocol fingerprint randomization. - The repo highlights how brittle static signature-based detections can be against minor source-level changes in a widely used open-source C2. - Notable changes include JARM randomization (later added upstream in Sliver v1.6 dev), adjustments to Donut/go-donut behavior (e.g., not attempting AMSI bypass by default), and attempts at beacon sleepmasking (removed due to bugs). - Useful for red teams and security researchers evaluating EDR/network detection resilience and for blue teams validating detection logic against variant builds.
Technical Focus: - Sliver C2/implant customization and rebuilds (Go) - Static signature evasion (YARA/byte-pattern breaking) - TLS/mTLS fingerprint changes (custom CA material) - JARM fingerprint randomization - Donut/go-donut shellcode generation and related detection surfaces - Beacon OPSEC features (sleepmasking experimentation)
Use Cases: - Build Sliver variants to test EDR and network detection robustness - Validate/strengthen YARA and behavioral detections against trivial string/fingerprint changes - Research protocol fingerprinting (JARM) and countermeasures - Compare upstream Sliver vs modified forks to identify stable detection anchors
Keywords: Sliver, Better-Sliver, C2 framework, adversary emulation, Go, implant, beacon, mTLS, TLS certificates, CA fingerprint, JARM, fingerprint randomization, YARA, static signatures, Donut, go-donut, AMSI, sleepmasking, network detection, EDR evasion

entry.title: defender acl blocker
entry.summary:
Title: Defender ACL Blocker
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): Defender ACL Blocker is a Go-based tool that prevents Microsoft Defender (and optionally other security services) from starting by manipulating file permissions. It impersonates high-privilege identities (SYSTEM and TrustedInstaller) to modify the DACL on a chosen system DLL (default: `C:\Windows\System32\kernel32.dll`). The tool computes each target service’s Service SID (S-1-5-80-…) and prepends explicit DENY ACEs for those SIDs onto the file’s ACL. Because DENY ACEs take precedence, the Windows loader blocks the service from loading the DLL, causing the user-mode components to fail initialization after reboot. It includes a removal mode to revoke the applied ACL entries and supports custom target files and service lists. This is primarily useful for red teams/malware research to study service identity, ACL enforcement, and a non-traditional Defender disruption technique.
Technical Focus: - Windows DACL/ACE manipulation (prepend DENY entries) - Service SIDs (S-1-5-80-*) derivation and targeting - Token theft/impersonation (SYSTEM via `winlogon.exe`, TrustedInstaller) - Privilege enabling (`SeDebugPrivilege`) - Windows loader behavior and DLL load authorization - Go implementation and optional binary obfuscation (Garble)
Use Cases: - Disable/impair Defender user-mode services by blocking DLL loads via ACLs - Research how Service SIDs interact with filesystem ACL enforcement - Simulate attacker tradecraft for defensive testing (hardening/detections) - Validate monitoring around DACL changes on protected system binaries - Build/ship an obfuscated PoC binary for controlled lab exercises
Keywords: Microsoft Defender, WinDefend, Sense, mpssvc, Windows ACL, DACL, ACE, DENY ACE, Service SID, S-1-5-80, TrustedInstaller, SYSTEM token, token impersonation, SeDebugPrivilege, winlogon.exe, trustedinstaller.exe, kernel32.dll, Windows loader, Go, Garble

entry.title: AntimalwareBlight
entry.summary:
Title: AntimalwareBlight
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - AntimalwareBlight is a PowerShell module that demonstrates executing PowerShell code at the “antimalware-light” protection level on Windows. - It targets weaknesses in the Early Launch Anti-Malware (ELAM) ecosystem, showing that anti-tampering guarantees depend on the least restrictive third-party ELAM driver. - The repo accompanies the REcon 2022 / Black Hat USA 2022 research (“Living Off the Walled Garden”) and is intended as a proof-of-concept rather than a fully weaponized bypass. - The module must be run elevated and relies on the user finding an overly permissive ELAM driver that will allow Microsoft-signed code (a specific TBS hash is referenced). - It’s useful for red teamers and security researchers to understand and validate ELAM/anti-tamper trust assumptions, and for defenders/vendors to assess supply-chain and driver policy risk.
Technical Focus: - Windows ELAM (Early Launch Anti-Malware) architecture and trust model - Antimalware-light / anti-tamper protection level concepts - Driver policy weaknesses and “weakest vendor” dependency - PowerShell module-based execution and controlled PoC design - TBS (Trusted Boot/Code integrity-related) hash allowlisting implications
Use Cases: - Evaluate whether endpoint anti-tamper can be undermined via permissive ELAM drivers - Reproduce and study the “antimalware-light” execution context for research/testing - Assess third-party ELAM driver policy quality in an enterprise environment - Inform defensive hardening and vendor risk management around boot-start drivers
Keywords: AntimalwareBlight, PowerShell, ELAM, Early Launch Anti-Malware, antimalware-light, anti-tamper, Windows internals, boot-start drivers, driver trust, Microsoft-signed code, TBS hash, code integrity, Defender research, REcon 2022, Black Hat USA 2022, EDR bypass research, kernel driver ecosystem, supply-chain risk

entry.title: C2TeamServer
entry.summary:
Title: Exploration C2 Framework (C2TeamServer)
Type: GitHub Tool
Short Summary (4–8 sentences max): - This repository provides the TeamServer and operator client for “Exploration,” a modular command-and-control (C2) framework aimed at red team operations. - The backend TeamServer is a standalone C++ application that manages listeners and active implant sessions, while the Python client connects to it over gRPC for operator workflows. - Implants (“beacons”) from companion projects (C2Implant for Windows and C2LinuxImplant for Linux) call back to the TeamServer to establish interactive sessions for tasking and output retrieval. - It supports multiple C2 transport channels including TCP, SMB, HTTP, and HTTPS, with TLS certificates used for gRPC/HTTP(S) communications. - The project includes Docker-based deployment guidance and a build pipeline using CMake and Conan, plus a linked blog series detailing design/implementation decisions. - Useful primarily for red teamers and C2 developers who want a reference implementation of a modern, extensible C2 stack with a separable operator UI and transport/listener architecture.
Technical Focus: - C2 architecture (TeamServer/client separation) - gRPC-based operator control plane + authentication (token-based) - Multi-transport listener design (TCP/SMB/HTTP/HTTPS) - Beacon/session management and tasking model - TLS certificate handling for secure channels - Build/dependency tooling (CMake, Conan) and Docker deployment
Use Cases: - Stand up a lab C2 for red team tradecraft development and testing - Develop/extend custom listeners, modules, or operator workflows - Evaluate gRPC-based C2 control-plane design and auth patterns - Run multi-protocol C2 infrastructure (HTTP(S), SMB, raw TCP) for exercises - Use as a learning reference alongside the “Building a Modern C2” blog series
Keywords: C2, TeamServer, beacon, implant, red team, gRPC, TLS, token authentication, listener, session management, HTTP, HTTPS, TCP, SMB, Docker, CMake, Conan, Python client, C++ backend, operator console

entry.title: wmiq
entry.summary:
Title: wmiq — Remote WMI query command-line utility (DCOM/DCERPC)
Type: GitHub Tool
